I hope, however, that Adamu's cutting insults get to the people of
the South-South and the Southeast: that they have no minds of their
own; that they can be teleguided by the Southwest. It is more cutting
to say they do not know their interest: Can the South-South see its
despoliation; its neglect; the carnage of Ogoni; of Odi; the curse of
Jesse? Or can they not?
The bottomline is: what is the interest the Adamu North is protecting
in Nigeria? Adamu's North has ruled for at least three- quarters of
Nigeria's independence. What has happened has been the heightening of
tribal tension; the ruination of the economy; the routine massacre of
Nigerians.
Let us admit, all of us, our mistakes and come down from our high
horses. We may still be able to salvage what is left.
What can we make of a people who are talking of avenging the so-
called wrongs of the last mayhem in Lagos? Who do they want to avenge
and on whose behalf - Nigerians or Northerners? What do you make of
people using religion for politics? Do they think that people have
forgotten Akaluka whose head was severed and paraded on the streets
of Kano? Do they think the people of Kogi, supposed to be in the
North, whose young priest was killed in the last Kaduna riots have
forgotten?
Surely Nigerians want to forget and move on but utterances such as
those of Adamu and the so-called Sharia body would not help matters.
Until we can agree on the need to come together and how to come
together it is going to be to "thy tents oh Israel". To loosen guard
is to be forced into a false sense of security. Until then, I shall
remain a Yoruba first, second and last in this unreliable contraption
called Federal Nigeria.
Abdullahi Adamu spoke of the Northern numerical strength, that
strength is suspect. That suspicion was what the Yoruba leader, Pa
Abraham Adesanya, codified as goats and sheep voting in elections.
Until a true and reliable census is conducted the numeral strength
will remain suspect.
The blackmailers of the North have tried to sow discord among the
people of the South-South and Southwest. The Southwest cannot
appropriate the resources of the South-South; the Southwest is an
implacable advocate of owners of resources controlling and enjoying
them.
Practically, every part of this country can survive on its own. It
takes just hard work, dedication, good faith and the will to do so.
Wealth creation has defied the conventional economic alchemy of land,
labour, capital. Land may still have something to do with it, but
technology and capital have more to do with it now.
Why must we share a consumption tax with those who do not consume? To
do so is what Adamu wants. But our amiable Vice-President, Abubakar
Atiku, did prescribe for the arrogant Northerner and I hope he took
the point at the same reception.
He said: "Selfishness, self-centredness and parochialism had never
led to progress and development, rather they dwarf societal vision,
stunt development and undermine the cohesive strength of societies,
leaving them stuck and vulnerable".
People are warned not to start what they cannot finish.

Original_Search_Title: Portraits Of Women In Achebean Literature
Headline: Nigeria: Portraits Of Women In Achebean Literature
Body_Text:
Lagos — At the just concluded ANA convention which spared a fractious
part of its programme on the doyen of African Prose Literature, one
of the numerous issues that cropped up was Achebe's portrayal of
women in literature.
In this piece written by Rose Ure Mezu, who teaches francophone and
anglophone feminist literature and theory at Baltimore's Morgan State
University, and the author of Songs of the Hearth, we learn that
Achebe's portrayal of women characters became progressive with each
novel he wrote.
When literary activities marking the sixtieth birthday of Chinua
Achebe reached fever-pitch in 1990, the greatest accolade given him
was summed up in one metaphor: the eagle on the iroko. Now, anybody
familiar with the African landscape knows that the iroko is the
tallest, strongest tree in the forest and that the eagle is, of
course, the king of the birds. It is not an easy feat to scale the
tree; that is why the Igbo proverb insists: "One does not climb the
iroko twice." Having succeeded in climbing the iroko, the climber
should appropriate all that he finds there: he may not be able to do
so again.The eagle, however, can both scale and soar above the tree
over and over.
In this metaphor the iroko then represents the field of African
literature; the eagle, Chinua Achebe.
Achebe has, of course, literally climbed and soared above the iroko
several times. More than those of any other African writer, his
writings have helped to develop what is known as African literature
today. And the single book which has helped him to launch his
"revolution" is the slim, classic volume called Things Fall Apart
(1958). Having been the first, so to speak, to scale the top of the
iroko, this eagle Achebe, and other male eaglets after him, arguably
have appropriated all that they have found there.
This paper will explore what is left for female eagles. The focus of
my study includes: (1) Achebe's portraiture of women in his fictional
universe, the existing sociocultural situation of the period he is
depicting, and the factors in it that condition male attitudes
towards women; (2) the consequences of the absence of a moderating
female principle in his fictions; (3) Achebe's progressively changing
attitude towards women's roles; and (4) feminist prospects for
African women. In the context of this study, the Igbo people whom
Achebe describes will represent the rest of Nigeria - and a great
many of the nations of Africa.
Were Nigeria and Africa oppressively masculinist? The answer is,
"Yes." Ghana was known to have some matrilineal societies, such as
the Akans; but Nigeria's traditional culture, Muslim as well as
non-Muslim, had been masculine-based even before the advent of the
white man. The source, nature, and extent of female subordination and
oppression have constituted a vexed problem in African literary
debates. Writers such as Ama Ata Aidoo of Ghana and the late Flora
Nwapa of Nigeria have insisted that the image of the helpless,
dependent, unproductive African woman was one ushered in by European
imperialists whose women lived that way. On the other hand, the
Nigerian-born, expatriate writer Buchi Emecheta, along with other
critics, maintains that African women were traditionally subordinated
to sexist cultural mores. I ally myself to the latter camp. I believe
that, in creating a masculine-based society, Achebe was merely
putting literature to mimetic use, reflecting existing traditional
mores. Colonial rule merely aggravated the situation by introducing a
lopsided system in which African men received a well-rounded
education while, like their European counterparts before the
mid-nineteenth century, African women received only utilitarian,
cosmetic skills in Domestic Science Centers - the kinds of skills
that only could prepare them to be useful helpmates of educated,
premier nationalists and professionals such as Nnamdi Azikiwe,
Nigeria's first President, and the late Obafemi Awolowo, the Yoruba
tribalist leader.
Things Fall Apart is significant because it began the vogue of
African novels of cultural contact and conflict. It has been
translated into over twenty major world languages. Commensurate with
its popularity, images of women receive attention. In a style that is
expository rather than prescriptive, Achebe's novel mirrors the
sociocultural organisation existing in the Africa of the era he
describes.
Like Zora Neale Hurston's Janie Mae Crawford (when married to Jody
Starks), Achebe's women are voiceless. But where even Janie is highly
visible, his women are virtually inconsequential.
In Of Woman Born (1977), Adrienne Rich unwittingly captures all the
nuances of the African traditional social milieu when she describes
patriarchy as : the powers the fathers: a familial, social,
ideological? and political system in which, by direct pressure - or
through tradition, law and Language, customs, etiquette, education,
and division of labour - men determine what parts women shall or
shall not play, and the female is everywhere subsumed by the male.
(57-58).
The world in Things Fall Apart is one in which patriarchy intrudes
oppressively into every sphere of existence. It is an androcentric
world where the man is everything and the woman nothing. In domestic
terms, women are quantified as part of men's acquisitions. As wives,
women come in multiple numbers, sandwiched between yam barns and
titles. These three - wives, yam barns, social titles - are the
highest accolades for the successful farmer, warrior, and man of
worth. These determine a man's social status, as illustrated by
Nwakibie who has three huge barns, nine wives and thirty children,
and the highest but one title which a man can take in the clan (21).
The society that Achebe is describing (1850-1900) is an agrarian one
in which the crop - the yam - is synonymous with virility. Achebe
explains that this all-important crop [stands] for manliness, and he
who [can] feed his family on yams from one harvest to another [is] a
very great man indeed .... Yam, the king of crops, (is) a very
exacting king (34-35). Consequently, to produce an abundant harvest,
the traditional farmer needs a good workforce. Women constitute (and
still do) the core of the rural workforce - farming, tending animals,
nurturing children, among other activities. To echo the Nigerian
critic Juliet Okonkwo, Achebe's cultural universe is one in which
women [are] to be seen not heard, coming and going, with mounds of
foofoo, pots of water, market baskets, fetching kola, being scolded
and beaten before they disappear behind the huts of their compound
(36). It would not be out of place to ally the existence of such
women to that of other diasporic black women described by Zora Neale
Hurston's metaphor "mule(s) uh de world" (14). Indeed, Zora's Janie
is robbed, of her voice by her own husband Jody, who, like Okonkwo,
chauvinistically believes that women's place is in the home (4l),
lumps together women and chillun and chickens and cows (67), and
wants to be a big voice" (27) in the affairs of the community.
A similar near-invisibility of women in Things Fall Apart is
acknowledged by the omniscient narrator. Describing a communal
ceremony, he confesses, "It was clear from the way the crowd stood or
sat that the ceremony was for men. There were many women, but they
looked on from the fringe like outsiders" (85). For centuries,
African women languished on the fringe of their universe - neglected,
exploited, degenerated, and indeed made to feel like outsiders. They
were not invited to stay when men were engaged in any discussion;
they were not included in councils of war; they did not form part of
the masquerades representing the judiciary and ancestral spirits.
Achebe's sexist attitude is unabashed and without apology. Unoka,
Okonkwo's father, is considered an untitled man, connoting femininity
(20). Coco-yam, of smaller size and lesser value than other yams, is
regarded as, female. Osugo has taken no title; and so, in a gathering
of his peers, Okonkwo unkindly tells him, This meeting is for men
(28). Guilt-ridden after murdering Ikemefuna, his surrogate son,
Okonkwo sternly reprimands himself not to "become like a shivering
old woman" (72) - this he considers the worst insult. Fleeing after
the murder, Okonkwo has no other refuge than his mother's town,
which, of course, has to be called Mbanta - "small town" - which I
read as being opposed in Okonkwo's thinking to the rugged, wild,
violent, strong, masculine connotations of his Umuofia (meaning
"children of the forest"). Such excessive emphasis on virility,
sex-role stereotyping, gender discrimination, and violence create an
imbalance, a resultant denigration of the female principle.
Such denigration brings Okonkwo to ruin just as much as it presages
the demise of his society's way of life. Okonkwo largely embodies
"all the virtues and some of the excesses of this society .... (for)
around (him are) heard the rhythmic beats of Umuofia's heart"
(Awoonor 253). One gets the impression of a strangle hold on
individuals: especially on the weak; the untitled, considered as
efulefu or "worthless"; and the outcast, embittered mothers of twins.
Even designed to break the weak and the women are the welcoming arms
of Christianity - an alien religion which steals quietly into the
clan, gathering adherents from those oppressed by Umuofia's rigid
insistence on allegiance to gods, customs, and laws.
Things Fall Apart is redolent of violent conflicts occasioned by the
utter lack of a moderating female influence. One example of this
absence can be found in Achebe's employment of the folktale narrating
the conflict between Earth, representing fertility or the female
principle, and Sky, representing the male principle. Donald Weinstock
and Cathy Ramadan argue that "the (folktales] initial quarrel between
Earth and Sky represents the struggle between masculine and female
powers and principles" (127). They assert that Okonkwo, who:
occasionally but reluctantly yields his tender emotions most often
expressed perversely towards Ikemefuna and Nwoye, is a paradigm for
(S)ky who withholds rain but releases it reluctantly and perversely,
since rain (falls) as it (has) never fallen before, preventing
vulture, who represents the female principle, from returning to
deliver his message, just as Nwoye, with his effeminate nature,
(does) not return to Okonkwo's compound. (20-21).
In the manner of the tragic hero, Okonkwo's consequent despair and
fall represent the despair and break-up of the Igbo clan before the
inexorable, invincible forces of the white man's religions and
political organisations, all because of the absence of that female
principle that could have maintained balance and sanity. This is
echoed by Chikwenye Okonjo Ogunyemi's postulation that present-day
Nigeria finds itself in the same quagmire as Umuofia of old because
of a similar degree of machismo: Is it any wonder that the country is
in shambles when it has failed to solicit the help of its better half
(women) . . . for pacific pursuits, for the betterment of the
country?" 60).
Achebe's female characters are generally stunted individuals as
above, or they are idealised as mothers in the manner of such
Negritude writings as Camara Laye's Dark Child . The latter, matemal
valorisation is indicated by the meaning of Nneka - "mother is
supreme" - as provided by Okonkwo's uncle Uchendu:
It is true that a child belongs to its father. But when a father
beats his child, it seeks sympathy in its mother's hut. A man belongs
to his fatherland when things are good and life is sweet. But when
there is sorrow and bitterness, he finds refuge in his motherland.
Your mother is there to protect you. (124)
The only women respected in Umuofia are those like Chielo, the
priestess of the Oracle of the Hills and Caves, who is removed from
the pale of normalcy. Clothed in the mystic mantle of the divinity
she serves, Chielo transforms from the ordinary; she can reprimand
Okonkwo and even scream curses at him: "Beware of exchanging words
with Agbala (the name of the Oracle of the Hills and Caves). Does a
man speak when a god speaks? Beware!" (95). Yet if Okonkwo is
powerless before a goddess's priestess, he can, at least, control his
own women. So, when Nwoye's mother asks if Ikemefuna will be staying
long with them, Okonkwo bellows to her: "Do what you are told woman.
When did you become one of the ndichie (clan elders)?" (18).
Perhaps Umuofia's shabby and degrading treatment of women and wives
stems from unconscious fear of, rather than reverence for, the
ubiquitous and capricious Earth goddess Ani or Ala, who wreaks such
havoc on the townspeople's lives. She is the goddess of fertility.
She also gives or withholds children; she spurns twin children who
must be thrown away; she prohibits anyone inflicted with shameful
diseases from burial in her soil. To the men of Umuofia, she must
seem the embodiment of the two-faced Greek furies and Scylla and
Charybdis joined together - vengeful, unavoidable, and
incomprehensible. Umuofia's men can compare to the ancient Greeks who
were noted for similar female images such as Pandora, Circe, Medea,
and Clytemnestra. In helpless, mortal dread of a fearsome divine
female principle, they come down heavily indeed on ordinary women
whose lives they can control as they like.
A cursory look at the place of women in Achebe's other works will
confirm a diachronic development. In No Longer at Ease (1963), there
is a discernible change in the style of Achebe's female portraiture.
At the end of the novel, Obi Okonkwo yields to the implacable force
of traditional ethos when choosing between his mother (representing
traditionalism), who threatens to kill herself if he marries an
outcast or osu, and the outcast protagonist Clara (representing the
modern female). The pregnant Clara gets an abortion and fades out of
the story. But at least she is cast as an educated, financially
independent woman. She has the makings of a spirited, independent
character, by virtue of her overseas education and profession as a
nurse. She can afford to do without Obi Okonkwo.
In A Man of the People (1966), there are images of women playing
traditional roles such as singers and dancers, or women adoring rich
politicians like Chief the Honourable M.A. Nanga. Mrs. Eleano John, a
tough party woman and board member - rich, independent, and assertive
- lamentably is cast as a semiliterate businesswoman with no
noteworthy role. We see Chief Nanga's wife, a beneficiary of the
colonial, utilitarian education, dissatisfied with her husband's
extramarital relationship and impending marriage to the young Edna.
Mrs. Nanga complains to Odili, but when the latter sets out to unseat
her husband, she reverts to her traditional role of helpmate fighting
to retain her precarious social and economic position. Consequently,
she remains a dependent, peripheral figure, deriving validity as a
human being only from her husband.
University of London, projects Achebe's new vision of woman's roles
and clarifies Ikem's hazy thoughts on the issue. Ikem accepts that
his former attitude towards women has been too respectful, too
idealistic. In the best Negritudinal manner, he has reverently put
every woman on a pedestal as a Nneka, where she is just as irrelevant
to the practical decisions of running the world as she was in the old
days (98). Beatrice gives Ikem insight into a feminist concept of
womanhood. She is articulate, independent, and self-realised, and she
re-evaluates women's position, asserting, "(I)t is not enough that
women should be the court of last resort because the last resort is a
damn sight too far and too late!" (91-92). In Beatrice, Achebe now
strives to affirm the moral strength and intellectual integrity of
African women, especially since the social conditions which have kept
women down in the past are now largely absent. Urbanisation and
education have combined to broaden women's horizons. Therefore, Ikem
tells Beatrice, I can't tell you what the new role for Woman will be.
I don't know. I should never presume to know. You have to tell us
(98). Achebe's newly envisioned female roles are to be expounded,
articulated, and secured by woman herself; and the modern African
woman is doing just that.
It is insufficient that Achebe the icon merely acknowledge the
injustice of his earlier treatments of women. Feminist ideology lays
the task of self-actualisation on women ourselves. Like Ngugi wa
Thiong O's female characters Wanja (Petals of Blood 1977) and
Wariinga (Devil on the Cross,1982), African women are playing active
rores in their nation's histories by resisting "being pushed or
tempted into accepting subservient or degrading or decorative roles"
(Evans 134). They are
developing what I have termed "the will to change" (Mezu 217).
In 1966, Flora Nwapa published Efuru. Significant in African feminist
scholarship, it signals a long-awaited departure from the
stereotypical female portraiture in male-authored African literature.
The eponymous Efuru chooses her own husband and marries without his
paying a dowry. She decisively deals with conflicts, radically
departing from the script of the traditional African woman "in the
peripheral, tangential role of a passive victim of a masculine-based
cultural universe" (Mezu 27-28).
But Efuru is plagued by infertility, polygyny, infidelity, and
abandonment by two undistinguished husbands. She finally abjures
marriage, opting for meaningful singlehood as priestess of the
goddess of the river, Uhamiri, vindicator of victimised womanhood.
In Idu (1970), Nwapa again embarked on a revisionist course, now
making a man responsible for infertility. Though in a similar vein
the Ghanaian writer Ama Ata Aidoo published a play, Dilemma of a
Ghost (1965), Nwapa was for a long time the lone African female
novelist s voice lamenting patriarchy. The prolific Buchi Emecheta
joined the fray with The Joys of Motherhood (1980). As the female
Nigerian critic, Chikwenye Okonjo Ogunyemi, writes:
If Nwapa is the challenger, Buchi Emeche a is the fighter. . For the
first time, female readers though female characters are aware of
their subjugation by their fathers, uncles, husbands, brothers and
sons. (62)
All of Emecheta1s novels expound "the theme of female oppression, the
slave girl becoming her leitmotif - the archetypal African woman
buried alive under the heavy yoke of traditional mores and customs"
(Ogunyemi 62). This list of African feminist novelists, dramatists,
poets, and literary critic is growing. African women feature equally
in publishing - Nwapa with her Tana Press and Emecheta with Ogwugwu
Afor.
African women must acknowledge gratitude to women and men - to
mothers, fathers, uncles, and brothers - who, disregarding patriarchy
and traditionalism, ensured them educations. It is only thought such
enlightenment that African women writers have been able to dismantle
the myth of female irrelevance by challenging such archetypal roles
as witches, faithless women, femmes fateles, viragos, and playthings
of capricious gods. In achieving this, women writers have been
supported by some male writers, labelled gynandrists: Isidore
Okpewho, Ousmane Sembene, Ngugi Thiong'o, Mongo Beti, Henri Lopes.
Given the intensely patriarchal nature of traditional African
cultures, African feminism cannot be considered radical. For white
European and American women, feminism has predicated itself on ending
gender discrimination and demanding equal job opportunities and
voting and property rights.
For African and African-American women, feminist ideology reflects
specificities of race, class, an culture. It is for this reason that
the former has failed to make any lasting appeal to Africa and its
diaspora. Because African women do not wish to alienate men, because
African women do not wish to alienate the bulk of their
tradition-based sisters, because many traditional African customs and
mores are worth preserving, most African feminists espouse womanism,
which Alice Walker defines as a philosophy that celebrates black
roots, the ideals of black life, while giving a balanced presentation
of black womanhood.... (I)ts aim is the dynamism of wholeness and
self-healing.
The iroko is there for women to climb, after all. Educated African
women, and those African women and men in exalted, decision-making
bodies, must and do realise their duty to make society an equitable
place for their less-privileged sisters. Equipped with education,
resilience, and the will to survive, female eagles can scale and even
soar over irokos, placing no limitations on their capabilities.
African women are making meaningful contributions: as lecturers,
professors, and presidents of universities; as commissioners and
ministers, senators and governors, and chairpersons of political
parties; as directors and others involved in literacy movements and
campaigns against forced marriages, clitoridectomies, and obsolete
widowhood practices. African women can outstrip their fictive
counterparts to be partners with men in national progress and
development, and to gain individual self-realisation and fulfilment.

Original_Search_Title: Express Fisheries Comes Under New Management
Headline: Nigeria: Express Fisheries Comes Under New Management
Body_Text:
Lagos — As the Christmas season sets in, the culinary sector is
exploring all avenues to keep Nigerians busy with assorted dishes to
make the period truly memorable.
One such move was recorded recently following the opening of business
at the popular Express Fisheries on Ajao/Olufemi junction Surulere,
under a new management Ugoli's Restaurant and bar.
The managing director, Ugolis restaurant and bar, Mr. Jonice Ogonuwe
said while speaking at the opening ceremony in Lagos that the public
was certainly in for good times beyond the Christmas period.
He said "good things have happened in Surulere, at your door
particularly to those living around Ajao and Olufemi streets, who now
have the opportunity to savour our dishes."
He implored would-be customers to expect the best in African cuisine
and local dishes including pounded yam, fried rice, jollof rice,
salad, semovita, amala, fufu and eba, with assorted soups to go with
every choice.
Speaking further, he urged prospective customers to come in and have
a taste of their daily menu adding, the taste of the pudding is in
the eating."
Ogonuwe explained that he found succour in restaurant management in
order to demonstrate what he has learnt all these years and have
creditably done well in it since he ventured into the industry of
over a decade now.
The managing director who disclosed that Express Fisheries brought to
three, the fast food outlet managed by his company, said the fourth
would be an upbeat fast food shop located at Okonlawon which would
commence operations soon.
He spoke of plans to increase the staff strength but commended the 15
workers who had been responsible for packaging these fast foods at
what he described as moderate prices, saying their level of
productivity coupled with the hardwork of his wife over a period of
four years brought the company to its present enviable position.

Original_Search_Title: New Around the Nation
Headline: Nigeria: New Around the Nation
Body_Text:
Lagos — Abia: Stanley Ohajuruka, speaker of the Abia State House of
Assembly said the leadership tussle that engulfed the state
legislature in the after-math of the June 26, crisis produced "no
victor and no vanquished".
He said the leadership tussle in the house should be seen as exercise
of the constitutional rights of each member. Ohajuruka stated that
the new leadership is set to chart a new course and has rededicated
itself to the service of God through a weekly prayer session.
He enjoined his colleagues to close ranks to restore the integrity of
the House, so that the people of the state can enjoy the fruits of
good legislation.
Abuja
French Agency for Development will soon open an office in Abuja as a
sign of France's support of Nigeria's new democracy. Antoine
Pauilieute, chief executive of the agency, would arrive Nigeria to
actualise the French dream of building a strong business presence in
Nigeria.A press release from the French embassy said the first office
of the agency will start in Abuja and may later operate an
independent office separate from the embassy later.
The agency will, among other things, fund job-creating projects and
grant subsidies. The agency will also guarantee a 15-year loans for
financing public enterprises with a six-year grace period.
Nassarawa
Nassarawa State government has donated relief materials worth N2
million to displaced persons in the recent ethnic clashes in parts of
Lagos metropolis. Musa Ibrahim, special adviser to Governor Abdullahi
Adamu, who led an eight-man delegation to Lagos, said apart from the
cash gift of N363,970 for Nassarawa indigenes, the other relief
materials would be extended to other people.
The materials included yam tubers, bags of rice, beans, sugar,
cartons of fish and cans of palm oil. Ibrahim said the government was
ready to assist its indigenes to put behind them the ugly incident
that claimed many lives. He urged other states of the federation to
emulate Nassarawa by assisting the displaced persons. He advised
Nigerians to learn to build and not to destroy.
Ogun
Olusegun Osoba, Ogun State governor, last week commissioned the N70
million general hospital built by the state government to boost
health services in the state. The hospital is the third to be
commissioned out of the 12 rehabilitated through the assistance of
African Development Bank, ADB.It is located in Owode Egba in Obafemi
Owode local government of the state.
Osoba said the rehabilitation of the hospital was part of the
dividend of democracy, which the people should enjoy. He promised
that his government would leave no stone unturned in its efforts to
bring development to the people of the state. He also said the people
would be enjoying uninterrupted water supply soon, as his
administration was going to spend another N15 million on the Owode
water scheme.
He, however, decried hooliganism in politics and urged politicians to
devote their time to the service of the people rather than engaging
in acts capable of distracting the attention of the government.
Ondo
Seven transformers worth N600 million have been installed in Ondo
State within the last six months. This is part of the federal
government's effort to boost electricity supply in the country.
Samuel Akabogu, district manager of the National Electric Power
Authority, NEPA, Akure, said the transformers would soon be energised
to remove the problem of load shedding and power outage. He pointed
out that with the new transformers, electricity consumers in the
entire state would enjoy uninterrupted power supply. He condemned
constant harassment of NEPA officials by irate customers and advised
them to desist from such acts capable of undermining the progress of
the state.
Akwa Ibom
Akwa Ibom State government has been told to take immediate steps
towards the establishment of state and local government offices of
the presidential committee on the review of the 1999 constitution.
Clement Ebri, co-ordinator of the programme in charge of Cross River
and Akwa Ibom States, said the establishment of the offices has
become necessary in order to articulate the views of the people on
the aspects of the constitution they want amendment.
He stated that the essence of the local government and the state
offices of the presidential committee on the review of the
constitution was to serve as contact places for people to register
their views instead of travelling to Abuja.
Ebri said the committee has responded to the spate of criticisms that
trailed the last visitation of the committee to parts of Cross River
and Akwa Ibom States. He noted that major developments not previously
envisaged have taken place since the committee commenced its
assignment few months ago. Ebri, former governor of Cross River
State, appealed to all the local government chairmen in the two
states to put in place all logistics to ensure that the mission of
the committee was accomplished.
Bauchi
Abdulmalik Muhammed, deputy governor of Bauchi State, has tasked
local government chairmen to make the security of lives and property
in their various domains a priority. He raised the security alarm
while addressing a security meeting of the council bosses at the
formal presentation of the national security scheme jeeps donated by
the National Association of Local Governments of Nigeria, ALGON to
the police. He warned against undue pressure on the divisional police
officers, DPOs, in whose care the vehicles were placed. He enjoined
the chairmen to co-operate with the DPOs in maintaining vigilance
within and outside the state in order to guarantee effective flow of
business.
Adamawa
Adamawa State requires no fewer than 150 tractors to meet the needs
of farmers in the state. Usman Aji, general manager of the state
Agricultural Mechanisation Authority, said the state had already
repaired 60 tractors which were used during the last rainy season. He
explained that the state being predominantly agrarian needed more
tractors to meet the demands of farmers in its 21 local government
areas.
He explained that revenue from tractor hiring has increased as
against three million naira collected last year because supervision
of the tractors was affective and diversion of funds minimised. He
said that the government has approved the sum of N4 million for the
repair of two of the six bulldozers owned by the authority.
Kano
The Kano zone of the National Drug Law Enforcement Agency, NDLEA,
arrested 317 persons with 2,035 kilogrammes of hard drugs between
January and October this year. Fredrick Ezeorah, zonal commander in
charge of the zone said 80 percent of the drugs recovered was
cannabis. He stated that 96 of the 317 persons arrested were
convicted, while 101 cases were pending before courts and 70 were
still under investigation.
The zonal commander claimed that the agency had succeeded in curbing
the menace of drug trafficking at the popular Kofan Ruwa area in the
state capital. He stated that the command was incapacitated in terms
of manpower and logistics to curb the rampant cases of smoking of
cannabis at the Sani Abacha and Kano Pillars stadia in Kano during
football matches.
Rivers State
Four persons were confirmed dead, while 23 others were declared
missing in a boat mishap along the Port Harcourt - Buguma sea route
last week. Eye witnesses said the out-board engine with 35 passengers
on board collided with a sea truck.
The boat was said to be carrying people travelling to old Bakana to
attend a burial ceremony. The ill-fated vessel was said to have been
over loaded with relatives of the dead person when the incident
occurred. Bakana is a settlement in the Asan Toru local government of
Rivers State.

Original_Search_Title: Second Burial Is Dying
Headline: Nigeria: Second Burial Is Dying
Body_Text:
Kaduna — Civilisation and poverty are working in opposition to the
expression of a people's age-old tradition.
For the Akweya or Akpa people, when a dead body is "laid to rest",
the soul is not yet deemed to be at rest. The traditional belief is
that the soul remains in atfuora, which is something in between limbo
and purgatory. Therefore they believe that a lavish ceremony is
required to usher the soul into obankwu (or eternal rest; heaven). It
is this ceremony that has erroneously been branded "second burial".
A second burial rite held recently on Saturday the 3rd and Sunday 4th
of November at Ejor-Akpa in Otukpo Local Government Area of Benue
State showed that this aspect of the people's culture is in for
perilous times.
As expected, the celebrants rolled out drums and greeted them with
dances, ate and drank aplenty in age groups. Late Mama Olodu
Onyaloka, who was interred about three years ago preparatory for
eternal rest, was expected to be turning joyously in her grave. Goats
and cows were slaughtered for meat to go with mountains of pounded
yam. People came from far and near to grace the event.
Usually, cannons are fired about three days before the two-day
ceremony, to give assurance to all and sundry that the occasion
remains on track. On the eve (usually Fridays, because weekends are
choice) more cannon shots are heard. Also, by now the families of the
deceased put finishing touches to the entertainment package. A
'summit' of the extended family is called.
At the court, members who have been levied various items, ranging
from cash to yams and livestock, are asked if they are ready for the
continuation of the rites. The answers seldom come in straight yes or
no; it's often "Let's see till tomorrow".
The following day, masquerades representing ancestral spirits file
out.
They make incantations to the ancestors, begging for eternal repose
of the soul of their beloved relation, supplicating that without the
ancestors' mercy such eternal rest would never be realised. Foremost
among these masquerades that are believed to represent ancestral
spirits is Ikwukwura, an Akweya domestication of mainstream Idoma
Alekwuafia. [Akweya dialect is a variant of Idoma with tinges of
Etulo (in Benue State) and Yeche (in Cross River State) traditions.
Even the Jukun masquerade known as Atsuku appears at an Akweya
ceremony as Ikpachuku.] Soon, youths whose torsos look like seed yams
gather with drums, beating syncopation to the delight of cheering
crowds. Elders, children and the middle-aged do their dances to these
drum sequences with various verve and piquancy. Women also hold their
own dances amid wood- and handclaps.
A second-burial ceremony is festive, acquiring its own economy, even
if short-lived. Aside from hawkers who peddle akara (bean cakes),
palm wine and other consumables, age groups seat through the days
deliberating on matters that affect them and their members. Socially,
they impart norms on the younger ones.
In one of the age groups, a young man was caught drinking with his
left hand. He was fined a carton of beer. A lame protest ensued, but
witnesses sprang up among the peer group, and the punishment was
going to take its course. However, an elder came and pleaded on
behalf of the transgressor, that the lad was just ignorant of the
law, having grown up in the city. A small amount was used to appease
the gathering and the punishment went back to rest. If the offender
hadn't the fine, Weekly Trust learnt, he would have been made to pay
on credit.
Nonetheless, the episode is of great significance in forecasting the
future of such burial rites. Firstly, several youths are proselytes
of the Christian faith, which, they feel, kicks against many aspects
of African tradition. A church official at the village particularly
used the word "heathen" to qualify the ceremony. Secondly, there is
growing disenchantment with the huge financial outlay of each second
burial, and the fact that it is done at the detriment of other
developmental project like health care and better infrastructure. So,
the opponents of second-burials argue, more people are dying, and the
more second- burials are required to be performed. Thirdly, in the
words of one John Scotty Anyebe, "the cost also makes certain special
masquerades, like one known as Iglo, nearly extinct because almost no
one knows when they were last seen."
Mr. Akpallah Okenyodo of the Benue State Ministry of Information
said, "The burial rites of chiefs and other elevated people in Akweya
society take place after about three years of the chief's death, and
because village heads rule until they die, it normally takes a long
time to see the grand second-burial rites." He corroborated Anyebe's
assertion that much of the activities associated with the rites of
passage of nobles are endangered. Okenyodo suggested that the events
be opened for video-recording as against the present prohibitions
placed on any form of record-keeping on the ceremonies: no
photographs, nor audio and video. He also includes in his proposal
the need for central committees to regulate schedules of
second-burials, perhaps again lending a voice to another assertion by
Anyebe: "It is rare for ordinary second- burials [like Mama Olodu's]
to take place at this time of the year, because it is believed that
this is a period of food scarcity. Therefore a village head whose
second burial holds at such time feeds his people. This is good
tiding."
Date of Publication: November 17, 2000

Original_Search_Title: Udenwa, Taking Giant Strides in Imo State
Headline: Nigeria: Udenwa, Taking Giant Strides in Imo State
Body_Text:
Lagos — Imo State may be backward in terms of federal presence. She
has no federal industry to show as being a part of the federal
Republic of Nigeria.
She may not have a digital telephone system, which has stagnated her
growth economically when compared with other states created alongside
her some 24 years ago.
She may be saddled with dilapidated industries and infrastrucural
facilities as well as a mind boggling N10 billion debt profile. In
fact she may be whatever people choose to identify her with. But
certainly, there is one thing synonymous with her and that is peace,
hospitality and appreciation.
Such was the case on Thursday, September 21 when Achike Udenwa
returned from his mission abroad. The welcome was tumultuous
befitting a man who has confronted the many seemingly intractable
problems bedevilling him.
Right from the Port Harcourt airport in Rivers State to the
Government multi- purpose Hall, the air was rent with songs of
praise, appreciation and goodwill. The faces of the people were one
with hopes and longings to touch the man who had promised last year
to transcend the Olympian height and take them to the 'promised
land.'
Imolites may not have seen the 'promised land' but there is the great
hope that four years is still a long way and with the giant strides
taken so far, there is no doubt that she will get there.
At the airport, all the state executive council members were there.
So also were traditional rulers, party stalwarts, industrialists,
opinion leaders, women leaders, town and village union executives and
the Omenimo dance troupe.
Chief Achike Udenwa alighted from the Chanchangi airliner at exactly
11 am in company of the culture and Tourism Minister, Chief Tonye
Graham-Douglas to witness the array of dignitaries waiting to say
'welcome back home.'
With the pumping of hands, for the first time extended to 'gentlemen
of the press' over, he entered the presidential waiting room where he
fielded questions from the newsmen.
Udenwa who travelled with the President and Commander in-Chief
Olusegun Obasanjo to attend the gathering of the Presidents and heads
of government tagged the 'millennium summit' organised by the United
nations looked refreshed, robust and younger. Spotting his usual
traditional attire with the 'Onwa' cap to match, he answered
questions posed to him ranging from the trip to London and Israel and
the economic benefit to the people of Imo State.
He also answered questions on the land policy, the acquisition of
shares in trust for Imo indigenes from the federal government sale of
some companies, the pension for primary school teachers,
privatisation of state-owned companies, implementation of the
Government white paper on probe panels. Others included the
revitalisation of Adapalm, Cabinet reshuffle, Imo airport, road
construction and rehabilitation, derivation fund among others.
The entourage then proceeded to Owerri, the state capital in what
became the longest motorcade in recent history.
Unknown to him, he was ambushed by a dance group led by the Women of
the People's Democratic Party in front of the Nigerian Union of
Journalists, (NUJ) building along the Port Harcourt/Owerri
Expressway. The members of the motorcycle union popular known as
'Inaga' also joined the convoy at the junction to the Federal
University of Technology, Owerri.
Alighting from his brand new 504 Peugeot Bestline Sallon car, which
is now spotting a new coat of arm, Udenwa danced to the rhythm of the
drums inspite of the protestations of his security personnel and
protocol officers.
In a rare show of oneness with the people, he undertook the remaining
two kilometres journey to Government House on foot.
Waving, cheering, acknowledging the greeting and smiling to the
appreciation of the teeming crowd of enthusiasts who had abandoned
their work to line up the streets to catch a glimpse of the man of
the moment.
This time the crowd was uncontrollable as they surged forward to
shake his hands, to ask after his health and even occupiers of the
Assumpta Cathedral could not help joining the masses to thank God for
his safe return and to express delight that he could alight from his
air-conditioned car to identify with the people.
At the multi-purpose Hall, it was then time to sing praises to God
for His mercy and love for Imo state, her people, leaders and the
country.
Finally, in appreciation of this show of love, Udenwa had this to say
to them, "I must thank you all very much for this show of warmness,
this show of solidarity. I least expected that these number of people
will turn out to welcome me on my return. I thank you very much. Even
for the Omenimo dance troupe to be here shows me that my people are
all well."
Overwhelmed by what he has witnessed, he promised to undertake
people- oriented programmes which will further endear him to the
people of Imo State. Well, as an adage says, "when the vegetable
becomes sweet, we add yams," or when the sleep becomes sweet, snoring
begins." Indeed the journey has not being bad at all.
Contractors are on site in the three Senatorial zones constructing
and rehabilitating 12 roads expected to be completed before the end
of December. Already, seven of the 10 roads awarded in the Owerri
capital city have been completed. Three General Hospitals have been
rehabilitated and over N250million worth of hospital equipment are
being expected from the United States.
Contract for the digitalisation of telephone has been signed by the
federal government, public utilities have been enhanced, arrears,
pensions and gratuities as well as backlog of salaries have been
cleared. The new minimum wage is being paid to public servants and
employment opportunities are been created.
Perhaps, these and many more are what the people see and are happy.
Hence the 'millennium welcome.'

Original_Search_Title: Ghana, Equatorial Guinea Discuss Cooperation
Headline: Equatorial Guinea: Ghana, Equatorial Guinea Discuss Cooperation
Body_Text:
Accra — A permanent Joint Commission of Ghana
and Equatorial Guinea is drawing up a legal framework in Accra
for economic and bilateral Co-operation aimed at improving living
standards in both countries.
Equatorial Guinea, endowed with oil, tropical wood and eco-
tourism resources, is represented by a 19-member delegation led
by Don Lucas Esono Mbang, minister of Information, Culture and
Tourism.
Ghana, which boasts of a relatively developed industrial
base for production and processing of cocoa, gold, oil and other
mineral resources, has 30 members, led by Yaw Adjei, supervising
director of Economic and Political Department of the Foreign
Ministry.
Ghana Communications Minister, John Mahama, minister of
Communications, who inaugurated the Commission Tuesday, said
economic integration of the sub-region holds the key to
development, especially in the era of globalisation.
"The so-called third world countries, especially those in
Africa, have been increasingly marginalised in the new
international, political and economic order because of the
inherent weaknesses in our economies," he said.
"Globalisation and liberalisation pose tough challenges for
us. We must face those challenges and overcome them to operate
effectively in a highly competitive and harsh environment where
the developed countries jealously guard their advantages in their
unending quest for wealth and power."
Mahama, who emphasised Ghana's commitment to promote
regional and sub-regional integration, urged African countries to
draw lessons from Europe where integration at various levels has
expanded trade and improved the well being of citizens.
"It, therefore, behoves us also not to be complacent about
our modest individual achievements, but to pursue our efforts at
integration more diligently and pool our resources in more
cohesive and coherent collaborative efforts," he said.
He recalled the exchange of visits by Presidents Obiang
Guema Mbasogo and Jerry John Rawlings in 1997 and August 2000
respectively, saying these visits culminated in the inauguration
of the Joint Commission.
The minister urged members of the commission from various
sectors, including petroleum, banking, environment, health and
industry, to consider co-operation in other areas such as science
and technology, trade and investment and education.
Adjei recalled that Ghana's relations with Equatorial Guinea
date back to the last century when Tetteh Quarshie brought cocoa
from Fernando Po (now Bioko), the largest of the islands in
Equatorial Guinea, back to Ghana.
Cocoa is Ghana's most important export crop.
"The similarities in our agricultural products should enable
us to exchange information on the techniques of production and
processing of these products," he said.
He said Ghana hopes that its efforts at achieving sub-
regional integration and economic union with a single monetary
unit would strengthen South-South Co-operation.
Esono Mbang said South-South Co-operation would help meet
challenges of globalisation, confront poverty and promote
sustainable development.
He called for coherent and harmonised efforts and the
sharing of resources to achieve these goals.
The framework will be completed on Wednesday.
Equatorial Guinea is in the Gulf of Guinea off Western
Africa. It consists of several islands and a mainland, covering
an area of 10,831 square miles.
Its main crops are cocoa, coffee, banana, rice and yam. It
has seven provinces with Malabo as the capital.

Original_Search_Title: The Second Cunning Of The Generals
Headline: Nigeria: The Second Cunning Of The Generals
Body_Text:
Lagos — The General is coming again, from the idleness of retirement.
The Generals are coming again, to eat the send yams after devastating
the nation's barns. The Generals are coming again because they found
life foul after the state house. The Generals are cunning again to
dig the grave for a nation they had seriously wounded. It is their
second cunning of their second coming.
Well, you were ear-witnesses to their dry run attempt to get back
into political prominence before the House of Reps mess and show of
shame of last week. Babangida bank rolled a seminar in Jos, some say
for ten million naira, to launder his self-battered political image.
He got a lot of media attention for that image laundering bash, no
thanks to us journalists, many of whom are still bewitched by
Badamosi's maradonic spells. A veteran journalist has put that down
to a lack of professional maturity. I put it down to existential
struggles. Of course, as a bright member of the fourth estate of the
realm pointed out to me, they were in good company of the Hausa
service of the "Bee Bee See" which saw nothing wrong in fanning
ethnic embers in Nigeria through the promotion of Hausa to the
neglect of Yoruba and Igbo. We must do something about that by and
by.
You are also aware that good old jack, the very Dr. Yakuba Gowon (not
fake doctor mind you) has recently rankshifted his civil war slogan,
Go On With One Nigeria into Go On With One North. Dr. Shehu
perspicacity is acknowledged for this battle of diminishing acronyms
in an attempt to boost a blighted political image. What are we
talking about? About the time IBB was putting together his plans for
removing the permanent stains of ruining the economy and the leprosy
of June 12, Jack the scion of those adorable Wusasa missionaries was
busy being persuaded to become the leader of the anti-Christian,
anti-Nigeria sharia politicians. What a wonderful piece of apostasy?
But my very beloved Jack who, with former Senegalese President,
Leopold Senghor, was initiated in 1972, in Awolowo Hall of the now
Obafemi Awolowo University into hthe PALM - WINE DRINKERS CLUB and
now wishes to be known as the head of the Sharia politicians, was not
alone in the metamorphosis of the tokunbo generals. They are all
fairly used, aren't they? With IBB convoking a conference of out-of
job politicians and hunger-harrassed academics, old rivalries were
rudely reawakened. Gen. Buhari, bristling with a mixture of anger and
envy, too decided it was time to stake a claim. The General put aside
the barrage of criticisms that, under him, Abacha's infamous
Petroleum Theft Fund or Petroleum Trust Fraud became an instrument to
develop oiless sharia states at the expense of the oilful Christian
states, and decided to join the fray. With his half-kinsman, Idiagbon
not available in this realm any more for Gestapo style assaults, he
corner Marwa (buba, sokoto and all) and stormed the Oyo State
Government House, Agodi, at the head, he alleged, of nomadic Fulanis
whose cattle had been devastating the local government's in Oyo
State's Oke - Ogun. Chief herdsman will do, he must have thought, if
nothing else affords him the opportunity of a political come-back.
The duo, fully armed with verbal cattle sticks, confronted Lam
Adesina, the willy Oyo State Governor, with allegations of Fulani
marginalisation, oppression, extermination etc. etc. in the most
aggressive military manner they could recreate. But the police and
the SS disarmed the former soldiers. Their allegations were unworthy
fictionalisations! Uncharacteristically, Gen. Buhari had to climb
down from his high horse but not before Uncle Lam had given him and
poor Buba Marwa, one or two slams. If you understand military
mentality, you will know that the man might try again. He has only
tactically withdrawn.
Buba Marwa of course lost more. Since the incident, tongues have been
wagging and dangerous questions such as how much did Marwa earn all
his working life to become the proud owner of an airline on reluctant
retirement are being seriously raised. His enemies are already
whispering maliciously, I think, that the man's airline might turn
out to belong to Lagos State.
Marwa, to keep his Buba over his sokoto in peace, should choose
between leading his airline or leading an imaginarily aggrieved
Fulani herdsmen.
So, in various ways and in various guises the generals are trying to
come again after the solitude of retirement.
What does that portend for Nigeria? Specifically, for democracy and
economic progress of the slumbering giant?
The second coming of the tokunbo generals into the nation's politics
portends disaster, nothing more, nothing less. Reasons? There are
several. First, our tokunbo generals' brand of politics is
authoritarianism which they disguised under the cloak of military
discipline. They can only thrive in a climate of intrigues,
conspiracies and sponsored violence.
Compelled to abandon the bullet far the ballot box, these men who are
used to commanding and being obeyed can only corrupt the system.
Besides, as we can now see clearly after the deed had been done, the
generals, to a man, are agents of feudalism, anti-federalists and
religious biggots. For them, Nigeria is equal to the North and the
North is equal to a polity looted blind, pauperised and dispossessed.
These men engineered and implemented all the woeful policies tearing
Nigeria apart today and they still deceive themselves that they would
be welcome back. That is, in their favourite expression, "is
inordinate ambition."
Their involvement in politics can only sow more seeds of rancour and
discord. As I write, I have before me Buhari's post-State House
interview in which he obliquely linked Babangida with drug smuggling.
And the mutual recriminations would go on ad infinitum.
Now the economic performance of the tokunbo generals is one reason
why they should do penance and observe silence throughout their
lives. Collectively, these generals turned Nigeria's paradise into
the poorest of the poor nations on earth. Each came in promising
miracles; each left the State House with a legacy of disasters. You
may not know that the Naira was once exchanging for two dollars.
Well, may be they want to come back to make it exchange at the rate
of one thousand Naira to one dollar.
Of course, they might be counting on the fact that since, as they are
now claiming, imposed Obasanjo on us, they can take their turns.
Well, now that they've revealed their tricks they have to think of
something else. Besides, they must first go to jail each for a
minimum of five years to get mellowed enough for democratic
governance. Uncle Segee did that and he is still flogging security
men all over the place.
Gowon remains for me incomprehensible among our politicking tokunbo
generals. Our only official meeting during his reign made me admire
him. Olu Adegboro, present Ondo State commissioner had filled a space
in our student journal with GOWN The TRAVELLER and his under employed
men forced us to Dodan Barracks. Gowon was most amiable when he met
us (we were not thrown into the gulag, mind you). He explained why he
had to travel and sent us back to Ife. I also know he lost his first
love to the civil war. The feudalists used him and dumped him. Now
they retrieved him like a rag again. Wonderful. I think he is being
bewitched by Dr. Adegbite, the shariaist who sits on the board of his
IGI. Let Jack be ware. Dr. Adegbite was with MKO in the Council for
Islamic affairs and we all know the result.
Now, let nobody misunderstand me. Soldiers are welcome into politics
if they seek to be law-makers, like my good friend Ike and not so
good friend David Mark. But the Presidency? They must forget it. By
the way let IBB know that it is we, Nigerian voters who would decide
his political future not God. God is not a politician and if he
wishes to go and contest elections in heaven, Good luck Maradona.

Original_Search_Title: Duke And The Poverty Question
Headline: Nigeria: Duke And The Poverty Question
Body_Text:
Lagos — The former United States' president, Lyndon B. Johnson told
the American people that poverty means, "coming home each night empty
handed to look at the expectant faces of your children who lack the
things they need. It means a lonely battle to maintain pride and
self- respect in a family that you cannot provide for".
But the Cross River State Governor, Mr. Donald Duke views poverty as
a manifestation which consigns the people to low income level,
unproductive resources, hunger, disease, lack of access to education
and health care, lack of sustainable livelihood as well as
deprivation of access to basic social services.
Though Cross River State has abundant human and natura1 resources
with rich flora and fauna, these resources, have overtime, remained
largely untapped and underdeveloped. Though it has large expanse of
arable land that supports the cultivation of both cash and food crops
with a total population of three million people and a projected
600,000 families, the people have remained poor.
Perhaps it was the down slide and overbearing effect of poverty on
the people that goaded Governor Donald Duke after taking his oath of
office, to declare unconditional war against poverty in the State.
The essence is to ameliorate the burden of the people as well as
raise the annua1 average income of these families from the present
N10, 000 to N100, 000.00 in about 10 years.
The Donald Duke led administration then resolved to confront the
programme in phases using select target communities as experimental
grounds. In this connection, Uyanga Community in Akamkpa local
Government Area was chosen as a model for the implementation of the
project. Uyanga was a typical local community before the inception of
the current Community based Development initiative. The community
sustains a population of 25,000 Inhabitants. About 65 percent of this
population are believed to have migrated from neighbouring States,
Akwa Ibom and Abia for petty trade and exploitation of timber. The
indigenous inhabitants are said to be principally subsistent farming.
Though Uyanga has remained a call station for travelers since the
colonial period, its economy is still primitive and the general
living condition deplorable. Today theirs is a success story.
This poverty, amelioration programme which mainly employs the
Community Development Initiative (CDI) requires the identification of
priority projects by each benefiting and participating community as
well as their active involvement in such projects through the supply
of labour, while the State Government and donor agencies provide the
much needed Finances, other inputs and technical know-how.
The United Nations, European Union, World Bank among others are part
of the crew expected to support the State Government's committed
fight to alleviate the high rate of poverty among its people. In this
regard, the Cross River State Donors Conference slated for 8th
through 10th November, 2000 is a welcome development in view of the
fact that it will afford the donor agencies and the State Governments
ample opportunity to re-appraise the implementation of the Uyanga
Poverty Alleviation Programme, using the Community Development
initiative to which all are stake holders. It will also rekindle and
strengthen the confidence of the donors, in the ability of the Cross
River State Government to make good use of their contribution.
Aside from that, the donors conference committee on Poverty
Alleviation Programme of the State Government is designed principally
as a facilitator to actualise the dream of providing basic
infrastucture for development.
The Uyanga model Community is essentially the effort of the State
Government Development and Poverty Alleviation Agency to show case
its concept of Community based poverty reduction programme.
So far the State government has constructed the following facilities.
Feeder roads to farms and plantations to ease evacuation of farm
produce, expansion of street roads for improved accessibility.
Establishment of an Ultra Modern Health Care the up-grading of St.
Paul's Primary School facilities to meet the needs of the teeming
enrollment of pupils for Primary Education and the establishment of a
Secondary School to stem rural-urban drift among the youths in the
area. A mini-water scheme with full reticulation, a magistrate court,
a police post, a model market and a fire station are among the lot.
The State Government has also gone ahead to establish a civic centre
at Uyanga to enhance social mobility, public enlightenment campaigns
especially on Government's activities, policies and programmes. Also,
Agro-based Industries for processing of Agricultural Primary produce,
to add value to farm produce has been established in the area.
Again, Uyanga has now has a technica1 centre where basic skills can
be acquired in the maintainance of facilities that are being provided
for the Community.
When the president announced a couple of weeks ago that the poverty
alleviation programme is to be replaced by National Youth Empowerment
scheme (NYES), to curb the teeming unemployment rate among the
youths, observers would have wondered how the Cross River State
Government would cope given that it has thrown its weight into
poverty alleviation. Interestingly, it had the fore sight of
introducing an employment scheme so it may not have much to worry
about. In this regard, the State Government has embarked on the large
scale cultivation, of pineapple, castor, cocoa, oil palm and cashew.
But Uyanga, cassava and yam tubers are the major food crops grown.
However, while these crops are grown in the area, the farmers lack
the requisite capital, credit facilities, necessary inputs and
storage facilities among others to boost the large scale production
of such crops. Much of the labour is supplied by the families.
Therefore it is expected that the Intervention of the Government, and
donor agencies as stake holders will help show case Uyanga as a model
for other communities to emulate.
It is hoped that the donors conference will come up with helpful
suggestions for the successful implementation of the programme in the
state.

Original_Search_Title: The Price Mongers
Headline: Nigeria: The Price Mongers
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigerians live at the mercy of price mongers who capitalise
on minor dislocation to arbitrarily hike prices Arbitrary price hike
on the pretext of slight crisis in the country is increasingly
becoming endemic.
Consumers are left to dance to the whims and caprices of importers,
manufacturers, retailers, service providers and transporters. Any
slight upset in the economy, no matter how marginal, immediately
attracts higher prices to goods and services.
However, it takes only one day to get pushed to the wall and you
start to fight back. One day you find out you have been kicked so
much and so hard that you cannot take any more.
Early in the week, I went to Oshodi market in Lagos to buy meat but
found out that a piece of meat which otherwise sold for N500 now sold
for N1,500. Reason? the butchers said fighting broke out between
members of the Oodua People's Congress (OPC) and Hausa butchers at
the Oko-Oba abattoir, Agege, Lagos. About one week after normalcy was
returned to the troubled Abattoir, meat sellers still using the fight
as an excuse to hike prices.
A survey at the various cold stores in Ikeja, Apapa and Balogun
market in Ogba, revealed that, a kilogram of chicken/turkey parts
which hitherto sold for N170, now attracts N300. "Most of the frozen
chicken/turkey parts are imported so, what has it got to do with the
mayhem at the Abattoir?" I asked the traders. "Madam, everything don
cost" replied one of the traders.
At the Ketu/Mile 12 market, Lagos, the prices of every item from
fresh tomatoes, yam and beans had all attracted 50 to 100 per cent
price increase few days after the OPC/Hausa skirmishes had subsided.
A tin of brown beans which sold for N500 about three weeks ago, now
sells for N700. Of course, the transporters had their own field day.
Transport fare went up as high as 200 per cent. Last week,
transporters going to Obalende/CMS bus stop in Lagos Island, charged
as high as N150 instead of the normal fare of N40. Mr. Johnson Jimoh,
a member of the Road Transport Union explained that, they bought a
four litre fuel for N500 instead of N110.
But then, does that justify the almost N200 increment? There are 29
Industrial Unions in the country, each one protecting the interest of
its members but none protecting consumers. According to Comrade Ola
Ojedokun, Deputy General-Secretary, National Union of Food, Beverage
and Tobacco Employees, "apart from the Nigerian Labour Congress
(NLC), no other body has been championing the cause of consumers."
Blaming government for the arbitrary price hike, he said "it is their
duty to control the prices of commodities and keep inflation in check
but they are handicapped as they are even too weak to control the
manufacturers."
Stressing the need for a consumer association, Comrade Ojedokun said
"regrettably, the level of our awareness may not make it possible
because, every one is after his pocket. We have had a situation where
Government tried to control house rent but selfish landlords tried to
jeopadise it."
Godwin Eluwa, the Managing Director of Godax Nigeria Ltd, Allen
Avenue Ikeja, also stressed the need for a consumer association and
called on the Federal Government to organise itself by working
towards a stable economy, higher naira value before the private
entrepreneurs can follow suit.
A consumer association, said Mr. Eluwa, "will mean a pressure group
that can reject sub-standard products, bring prices of products down,
draw Government's attention to the influx of sub-standard imported
food products flooding our markets just like you have in other
developed countries." In July, a seminar tagged: "National Workshop
on Consumer Protection" was organised by a Consumer Protection
Council in Abuja and the Nigerian Institute of Legal Studies Lagos.
The Consumer Protection Council went into hiding as soon as the
seminar ended. Consumers are yet to feel their impact while majority
do not even seem to be aware of its existence.
In Nigeria, there is no known democratic legislation regarding
consumer protection. The consumer protection laws available, can be
classified according to Federal and state laws. The Federal laws
include, the Hire-Purchase Act of 1990, the Food and Drugs Act 1990,
the Price Control Act 1990. The Merchandise Marks Act 1990, the
Standards Organisation of Nigeria Act 1990, the NAFDAC Decree 1993,
the Consumer Protection Council Decree 1992 and the Trade
Malpractices (Miscellaneous Offences) Decree 1992.
The state laws include the Sale of Goods, the Carriers, the Inn
Keepers and Hotel Proprietors Laws. But none of these has in the real
sense of it, offered any protection to the Nigerian consumers.
Publication date: November 6, 2000

Original_Search_Title: Study Says Roots, Grains of Bean-Yams Edible
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Study Says Roots, Grains of Bean-Yams Edible
Body_Text:
Kinshasa, DR Congo — Roots and grains of the
bean-yam (sphenostylis stenocarpa) are edible, according to a
study made by the University of Lubumbashi in the Democratic
Republic of Congo recently.
The university's faculty of agronomic science is conducting
a study on the leguminous plant (bean-yam) which grows in Africa
and in all provinces in Congo.
With regard to its nutritional value, the study shows that
fresh sphenostylis stenocarpa tubers contain 5 percent proteins
compared to less than 1 percent for cassava, around 2 percent for
potatoes and 3 percent for sweet potatoes.
When dry, the tubers contain 10 to 19 percent proteins
compared to 2 percent for cassava and 4 to 4.5 percent for
potatoes and sweet potatoes.
"These tubers contain five to 10 times more protein than
those of cassava. Consumed as chips, they taste like potatoes,"
the research concludes.

Original_Search_Title: Leader of Nation's First Coup To be Exhumed and Reburied
Headline: Nigeria: Oshimili Confers Posthumous Title On Nzeogwu, May Exhume Corpse
Body_Text:
Okpanam, Delta — Thirty-four years after the death of the leader of
Nigeria's first military coup, the late Major Chukwuma Kaduna
Nzeogwu, his kinsmen are poised to exhume his body and rebury him at
Okpanam in Oshimili North local government of the state.
The late Nzeogwu had also been conferred with a posthumous
chieftaincy title of Dike Omu of Okpanam, meaning "the strong one,
the one who leads in time of war."
The Oza-Omu of Okpanam, Chief (Mrs.) Martha Dunkwu, announced this
weekend at Okpanam on the occasion of her first New Yam Festival,
first coronation anniversary and 45th birthday celebrations.
She appealed to the federal, state and local governments "to name
streets, institutions and monuments after Major (Chief) Chukwuma
Kaduna Nzeogwu (of blessed memory), a courageous and great son of
Africa.
"Government", she further suggested, "should also institute an
Nzeogwu memorial lecture, which will eventually reveal whether this
motives were selfish or for a genuine progressive national purpose.
"At the appropriate time, the people of Okpanam will demand for the
body of Chief Nzeogwu to be exhumed and reburied at Okpanam," she
said, explaining that the anti-corruption drive of the present
administration "has only cosmetic value at the moment as its proper
application has not been achieved", while the Niger Delta Development
Commission (NDDC) Act was yet to be fully set on course.

Original_Search_Title: Eyewitness Account of Harare Riots
Headline: Zimbabwe: We Are Prepared to Die Rioters
Body_Text:
Harare — Eyes and nose itchy and streaming, I forced my way into the thickening
crowd just to catch what some of the agitated young men and women were
trying to say.
But suddenly the atmosphere was shattered once again by a volley of
teargas fired into houses and straight into the growing crowd. This scenario
of upheaval had been played over and over again early this Monday morning in
Harare's high-density suburbs of Mabvuku and Tafara.
Some of the teargas canisters were shot right into the schools in the
two suburbs where Grade Seven and Ordinary Level students sitting their
final exams were rudely interrupted. They had to contend with struggling
with the right answers as well as breathing.
Residents, citing unbearably high costs of living and the political
crisis in the country, said they had reached breaking point and could not
carry on anymore.
"We have reached a point where we can't carry this Robert Mugabe
government anymore. Mugabe must go now because we have had enough
problems. We have no jobs and don't know where our next meal will come
from. Those of us who still have jobs can't even afford to go to work
because of high transport costs," said one Mabvuku man.
He applied a wet pink towel to his eyes to relieve the sting of the
teargas and went on: "Instead of the government doing something to alleviate
our problems, they are sending their police force to intimidate us by
teargassing our families.
"We are prepared to die if it means Mugabe and his government will go as
they must go."
Around him, several young men and women chanted "Mugabe must go" and
vowed that some of them would have to die to make this possible.
One young woman, hair askew and eyes red and streaming, said she and
other poor residents in areas such as these were now used to teargas and
strong-arm treatment by the police.
"They can throw as many teargas canisters as possible but there's no
going back. We are hungry, unemployed and have absolutely no hope for a
better future as long as Mugabe is still in power," she said.
"We are not scared of dying because as long as Mugabe is in power, we
are actually dead."
One middle-aged woman sporting a tattered brown blouse scoffed at the
suggestion by the Consumer Council of Zimbabwe that consumers should boycott
bread and instead feed their families with sweet potatoes or yams.
"How many people in this country have fields of yams and sweet potatoes?
You still need money to buy enough to feed a family. These people are so far
removed from reality that they don't seem to realise that our major problem
is lack of money, not alternative food," the woman said.
Another woman, wiping mucus and tears from a toddler's face, pointed out
that the suggestion would only work when the sweet potatoes and yams were
still in season.
"What then do we do when they are no longer in season? But even then, we
have no disposable incomes to talk of so I think it's very unfair for well
fed and rich people to suggest alternatives for us when they are free to
make their own choices," she said.
The prices of basic food commodities such as mealie-meal, cooking oil,
sugar and bread have all become a luxury for most families.
A standard loaf of bread is now selling for anything between $22 and $27
and most urban families depend on bread for their breakfast and lunch.
Roads leading into both suburbs were barricaded with heavy rocks, car
wrecks and burning tyres.
The riot police, determined to put an end to the growing unrest managed
to round up some of the young men and women and forced them to remove the
barricades but no sooner had the police passed than another group of young
people came and put back the blockades in scenes reminiscent of South
Africa's 1980s struggle against apartheid.
Even in other parts of the suburbs where the streets were deserted,
truckloads of riot police willy-nilly fired teargas into houses as if to
draw out the people.
As if prepared to do war, some of the residents made their way onto the
streets armed with wet towels, a sure sign of seasoned teargas victims, and
threw stones and any other missile at the law enforcement agents.
As some of the rioters marched towards the police, they chanted that the
teargas was mild and would not kill them.
Business came to a standstill as shops were forced to close and market
stalls stood dusty and deserted. Those who had earlier in the morning dared
to buy bread were forced to crumble it and throw it away.
A Kukura Kurerwa bus that had tried to defy orders not to take workers
into town was burnt down and commuter omnibuses were forced to pick up and
drop off passengers on the out-skirts of the two suburbs.
By midday, the situation was growing more tense as school children
joined in the chanting of slogans denouncing the government and Mugabe.
The running battles between the people and the police intensified but
the residents refused to be cowed. As we continued on the teargas trail, one
of the three police trucks suddenly stopped.
About 15 police officers spilled out, moved menacingly towards the young
people, under orders from other policemen to remove yet another barricade
across the road separating Mabvuku and Tafara.
Wielding baton sticks, the police officers made for the youths and
started whipping them and telling them off for blaming the government for
their problems. By now several members of the foreign media were busy
filming the events as they unfolded.
The leader of the force who had led his officers in whipping the youths
suddenly turned onto the media and demanded their Press cards.
Forced to retreat, Chris Mazivanhanga, a cameraman with Associated Press
(AP), was manhandled and forced to retreat. Rob Cooper, also of AP, was told
rudely to leave before the officer turned on the Financial Gazette crew and
demanded that they too leave.
Only the state-controlled news agency ZIANA was allowed to go on with
its work.
"All of you should leave because you are disturbing our programme," said
the officer, adding to his fellow officers: "The ZIANA guys can go on
because they are our people."
As we drove out of Mabvuku, a soft drink delivery truck was being turned
away by the police who said if the driver valued his security and that of
his goods he should put as much distance as possible between himself and the
two suburbs.
I could not help but notice that the police could have had a tip- off
about the impending disturbances.
A few metres from the junction that joins the Mabvuku and Mutare roads
were the remains of a fire and police coats draped on a shrub and an
assortment of cups.
On the other side of the capital, in the high-density suburb of Mufakose
and in the nearby town of Chitungwiza, a few skirmishes pitting residents
and tuck-shop owners had been quickly quelled down by the police.
By Tuesday morning, the riots had spread like wild fire to the capital's
other high- density suburbs of Mbare, Kuwadzana, Highfield, where once again
police fought running battles with residents.
One was left with the feeling that neither side was willing to
compromise.
Alfred Nhema, a political scientist at the University of Zimbabwe, said
continuous hikes in the price of basic goods had reached unacceptably high
levels that were not matched by salary increases.
"In most countries, food riots are symptomatic of the underlying
political situation in the country and such demonstrations usually serve as
a wake-up call to the political leadership and the elite in the country," he
said.
"This problem has to be resolved because it does not help to simply
adopt the ostrich approach of burying your head in the sand. In most cases,
food riots are a precursor to political instability so they need to be
addressed or they will spread."

Original_Search_Title: Strike Will Persist Unless Government Disciplines Itself-Akinlaja
Headline: Nigeria: Strike Will Persist Unless Government Disciplines Itself-Akinlaja
Body_Text:
His predecessor was a firebrand and has remained so ever since. And
he has proved as daring since he succeeded Chief Frank Kokori, the
man that stood on the side of the masses during the June 12
imbroglio. Joseph Akinlaja, the new helmsman at NUPENG does not speak
with hot yam in his mouth. He calls a spade a spade. In this no holds
barred chat with Asst. Features Editor, Leonard Nzenwa the man had
warned that more shock treatment may come the way of government if it
does not discipline itself.
EARLY last week, Lagos the commercial nerve centre of the nation
almost witnessed another round of fuel scarcity following threats by
your members that they were going to go on strike. How was this
misunderstanding resolved.
Yes, when we heard that the tanker drivers and the Lagos State
Government were going to be on collision course, we took pains in
this office to contact the authorities, that is the director of State
Security Services (SSS) and the Lagos State Government. Everybody
