played a wonderful role. The executive governor of Lagos State,
Senator Bola Tinubu, even invited me and officials of PTD in the zone
and resolved the matter. He asked that the vehicles be released and
also said in future if they have disagreement with any of his
functionaries, at least before they do anything they should give him
the opportunity to intervene which we like very much. Myself and
Senator Tinubu resolved many problems quietly. But the problem also
is that it is difficult also to see authorities without problems.
They say he is not around, which does not help issues at all. People
say that there are state matters, and really there are state matters,
but it seems to me too that there are some that are more urgent than
the others. If you hear that certain officers call and you are not
there I think it is better to return that called to know how serious
the matter is. Especially, we in NUPENG since we won't call on
anything frivolous. So we have managed to resolve it and there was
really no strike.
Sir, your members seem to be a heady lot. They park their vehicles
recklessly and indiscriminately on major highways obstructing
traffic. They also disregard traffic rules and regulations in the
country. Why is this so, and how is the association addressing this
issue?
Well, in some cases it is not their fault. When our members are at
fault we try to eliminate that through education of their leaders.
That they should first of all realise that the vehicles they are
driving are big, and so they are to give consideration first to other
road users and that they should not park indiscriminately. The other
one that is not their fault is if you go to Apapa area, you see a lot
of parking even on the highway, which is dangerous. That problem is
as a result of two things, one insufficiency of fuel in the northern
parts of the country, so everybody would have to come to the coastal
area to lift petroleum products for the hinterland. Unfortunately in
this country people talk about symptom without discussing about the
cause of those things. If it is in Europe, immediately they
anticipate this kind of thing, they will open up a place for them to
park. They link it up with computer gadget and then call people to
come and load. So during Adisa',s time, we had this kind of problem
that lead to strike. And they gave a place at Orile-Iganmu, an
expanse of land, sand filled place. To enter the place during rainy
season is difficult, because it is a difficult terrain and the people
having towed their vehicles many times abandoned the place and came
to the street. It is like when you say you are evacuating beggars to
a rehabilitation home and you don't give them food there, they will
come back to town. That is exactly what happens.
What is your opinion about the proposed plan by the government to
impose special tax on oil products considering that fears are being
expressed that it may be a grand ploy by government to hike the price
of fuel and petroleum products.
Well, I will say that the government should not make the mistake it
made the last time. You see from my own point of view it is not that
people cannot pay for fuel because if the price is not really
appropriate you cannot kill the black market. Black market will
thrive and people will sell at arbitrary price. The last quarter of
1998 and the first and second quarter of 1999, fuel was surplus in
this country. Black marketers were put out of business. Definitely,
they will not be happy about it and so they may be part of the
saboteurs, pipeline vandals and in the end you find out that it is
the same people who say they cannot pay certain amount that now pay
N300 per gallon for kerosine. It is painful really, but there's
nothing you can really do about it.
Also, your members have been accused of aiding and abetting smuggling
of petroleum products abroad. Is there anything your association has
done or has been doing to checkmate members from indulging in this
act.
Yes, I mentioned something which I want you to note. During the third
quarter of 1998 and the first and second quarters of 1999 fuel was
surplus in this country. So much so that even when government fixed
diesel price at N19, people were buying at N18 and N17.
It seems at the slightest excuse, your association threatens that you
are going on strike. Is this not a blackmail to perpetually impose
hardship on Nigerians. Are you giving any assurance that this will
not reoccur?
Authorities should be sensitive to issues. You see most of the things
you see in NUPENG, all those strike you see is to sensitise the
conscience of the authorities because our union is critical and if
you disband it by law you did not disband the human being any more.
So they already know what to do. So the question of disbanding it
does not arise, because it is going to be ineffective. We are using
some issues to send some signals that government should be sensitive.
Governments in this country are not sensitive. In Europe, if you go
on hunger strike people will panic. But here if you go on hunger
strike, you will die. That is a way of not punishing the public but
still sending a signal across to the government. So here, your right
is your right. Your right in Nigeria is a function of your influence
or your power. So there are certain things we do that look odd but
sensitising the conscience of the government of this country. So the
fact that somebody does not go on strike does not mean that he has no
genuine case, so listen to him. If you know that you are going to
listen to him when he goes on strike why don't you listen to him
before he goes on strike. Until we get there, we will continue to
have this problem. You know in the oil industry, we came to get
recognition through this shock treatment. Before, they will ask
workers to sign forms that never belong to unions. And workers will
sign. They need an organisation which will say, it is right to
associate, to collectively bargain. Look at the case of teachers and
the way they have been humiliated. So if we in NUPENG dont' send
shock wave till they ask question, they will not ask question. And if
they ask question, we then tell them that is not our intention to
punish the public. People came out enmasse in Belgrade last week to
sack Milosovic. If it is in Nigeria, they will roll out the tanks to
kill everybody. Everybody has a right of protest. So we must have an
organisation that must talk to the government.

Original_Search_Title: What Value There is In Groundnut
Headline: Nigeria: Groundnut: What Value?
Body_Text:
Oh how I love groundnut! It is my favourite nut. When I perceive the
smell of groundnut, my head turns. When I see groundnut, I must
surely buy for myself . When I hear people talk about groundnut I
must surely contribute. Why? Because I have seen the benefits of
eating groundnut on my skin, my hair, and my sight.
Some people have stopped eating groundnut, while some select the kind
they eat, either boiled, roasted or the dry fresh one because it
gives them pimples. But is it really groundnut that causes these
pimples? I have a friend that always develops pimples when under
stress or pressure from the environment. I also have friends who
develop pimples for a short while during their menstrual period.
There are also some people who react to external product that are
incompatible wuith their skin types by developing pimples! Now
whatever it is that causes your pimples, there is a whole lot to gain
from eating groundnut.
Groundnut, scientifically known as Arachis hypogea L., belongs to the
leguminosae family. In this family, you have plants such as beans,
cowpea, peas, clover, Iru (Parkia biglobosa), ugba (Pentaclethra
microphylla). They are highly rich in proteins. The plant groundnut
is a small annual crop grown beneath perennial tree crops such as
coconut, oilpalm or rubber. It is grown principally for its protein
rich seeds (kernels), the oil and the meal derived from them and the
vegetable residues. The seed are borne in fruits (pods) which develop
below the soil surface. The dry pericarp of the crop when mature is
known as the shell or husk.
About two thirds of the world crop, groundnut, is crushed for oil.
The oil is used in cooking most of our food. In the home, it is used
to fry eggs, plantain (dodo), stew or sauce, pancake, yam etc.
Groundnut oil releases the aroma in whatever it is you are cooking.
It gives a fine finish to the food you are cooking. Groundnut seeds
can be eaten fresh, dried, boiled, roasted or ground into a paste to
make this local snack, the Hausas call Kulikuli. It is a fine snack
that goes down well with Ijebu garri. Groundnut is also appreciated
with Booli(Roasted plantain), Guguru (Popcorn) and banana. For those
of you who eat groundnut with all of the above you would agree with
me that groundnut is enjoyable with these and it goes down well into
the system, occupying your system after taking enough water.
Groundnut gives energy for strength and warmth. The body utilizes it
to produce and maintain a glowing skin, healthy hair and good eye
sight. The wrong notion about groundnut not being good for the eyes,
should be corrected because it is very good for the eyes, especially
for growing children.
The residue after the oil has been extracted (press cake) contains
forty to fifty percent of protein and provides valuable food and
feeds. Groundnut flour produced from the cake is used in many food
and food product. Do you have a local dish in your place that is made
of groundnut? You can write to share this with us.
Groundnut oil delays spoilage in food, that is why the local fish
seller cleverly uses oil to preserve the smoked or dry fish. Even the
food industry, especially the canning industry, uses oil to preserve
minced meat or fish and sauce in cans e.g. Sardine. Groundnut is also
used for margarine and vegetable ghee, for pastries and bread. It is
also used in making soaps and cosmetic products for the skin and
hair, lubricant and emulsions for insecticides and as a fuel for
diesel engines. In the rural dwellings, the locals use the dry shell
to ignite fire because it burns easily. In the U.S.A about half of
the groundnut harvest is used to make peanut butter, salted groundnut
and confectionery products are also important.
Groundnut haulum is nutritionally comparable to grass hay and is used
as feed in many countries. The shell may be used for fuel, as a soil
conditioner, as a filler in feeds and as a source of furfural,
processed as a substitute for cork or for hard board or composted
with the aid of lignin- decomposing bacteria. Come to think of it ,
have you ever wondered why groundnut would be planted beneath tree
crops? Do you know that they contribute to the enhanced
sustainability of intensified cropping systems by improving soil
fertility through Nitrogen fixation, permitting a longer duration of
groundcover in the cropping sequence?
There is big financial gain in the trading of groundnut oil and its
product and this is one reason why many are abusing the oil by
selling adulterated ones. The genuine groundnut oil does not solidify
at room temperature, its smell is typically the odour of fresh
groundnut and the color is golden or faint bronze not white as you
have it in many solidified oils in bottles displayed for sale in the
market.
The best way to enjoy the benefit of groundnut is to take them fresh,
they do a lot of good to the system . Take advantage of them by
eating them as often as possible, the Food Industries are doing the
same by processing them into finished goods that have undergone
rigorous processing in machines, reducing its nutritional value and
they still make their gain. DonÂt settle for less, go for the best of
it, go natural!

Original_Search_Title: Then Where Was Agbonma?
Headline: Nigeria: Then Where Was Agbonma?
Body_Text:
Lagos — Perhaps she decided to take a stroll. But how can that be? It
is so unusual for her to think of taking a stroll in the morning
hours and without informing anybody?
Okunnu has just taken his seat among his friends not knowing what to
do when his in-law, Ajuluchukwu walked in. He got up and shook his
hands.
"Welcome", he said, trying to appear as cheerful as possible, but he
could hardly hide his anxiety. Without even waiting for him to sit
down Okunnu asked: "Did Agbonma meet you in the house?"
"Agbonma?" No! It was your neighbour who came to inform me that you
wanted to see me. I would have come earlier, but I had some visitors.
But come to think of it, tell me, why should Agbonma be at Umulumgbe
instead of here?"
Okunnu realizing that he had broached the subject without preparing
the ground for it, searched Okeleke Anadi's face for help as to how
to handle the the situation from that point onward.
Always equal to the task Anadi cleared his throat and began:
"In-law do not mind your in-law Okunnu, there is no problem at all.
Perhaps the kolanut he gave us earlier is the last piece in the house
and that may explain why he does not know that an in-law should be
received like a king.
Okunnu without saying a word, got up and went into his inner chamber
and then came out with two big kolanuts and presented them before
Ajuluchukwu, with these people: "my people and particularly, my
in-law forgive me because it is only the brainless man who does not
know that his brother is a guest and should be well treated."
Very curious, Ajuluchukwu did not want to concentrate on kolanut . He
thanked his in-laws for the kolanuts, took one of them and put into
his pocket saying "when the kolanut gets home it will tell its own
history". He then blessed the remaining one and while the youngest of
the men in the assembly was bearing the plate of broken nuts round
the room he returned the issue at hand.
Okunnu still worried but not willing to be careless anymore said
"there is no problem at all." It is just that I came in and I did not
see her and since she had been saying that she wanted to visit her
father's home, I just thought that must have been it." Of course,
Okunnu was telling a lie, but he really had no option.
Trying to be as relaxed as possible he continued, "after all someone
who has two ripe fruits on a tree is entitled to take them all. Your
sister may have decided to drink from the famous Umulumgbe Ngene
early this morning. In fact, you may have taken different routes and
may have missed yourselves.
"We were eating and drinking before your arrival. Here is your cup,
you may wish to join us. "
Ehe ! that is what you should have said before going ahead to worry
about a grown up woman.
Almost convinced now that nothing was amiss Ajuluchukwu joined the
party.
Before departing Ajuluchukwu went to see Mgbeoye and looking at her,
he said "I know that it is a matter of market weeks now. Your mother
should be here by now. With her and Agbonma helping you will be able
to cope. Mgbeoye nodded not wanting to say much and in the process
betray anything for she was not sure what the men discussed. Taking
that as a final conclusion that there was no cause for alarm,
Ajuluchukwu thanked her for the delicious onugbu soup and pounded yam
and then accompanied by Okunnu, and his friends, left for Umulumgbe.
Okunnu was still wondering whether to leave for Itulu to go fetch his
mother-in-law, but the thought that anything may go wrong if he left
kept him to his seat.
He brooded all day. It was almost dusk when he was roused from a
drowsy reverie by a woman's frail voice.
"Okunnu Melifonwu, Emeni Okwugo has arrived." The voice was very
distinct. It belonged to Mgbeoye's mother. She had away of speaking.
She pronounced her own name and announced her presence as though she
needed to inform those around her again and again about herself.
Someone once said it was because of her dimunitive stature which she
believed she needed to call attention to severally or otherwise
escape recognition.
Okunnu wiped his face with hands as if to ensure that he saw right
and heard right. He stood up and met Emeni at the door of the Obi.
His relief was unmistakable. At least that was one problem off his
shoulder so that he would now have the time to solve the riddle
concerning Agbonma's whereabouts.
These thoughts coursed through his mind as he watched Emeni pick up a
stool.
Shortly she sat beside him wanting to know all that had been
happening. Emeni stood out among women. She was a very peaceful
woman. She made peace, set forth suggestions to family problems
without appearing to intrude.
She did not seek to accord more respect than she deserved and for
that reason Okunnu went out of his way to please her. During
festivities he always took gifts to her.
"Has Mgbeoye been troubling you?" she inquired playfully.
"No!" he replied with mock consternation and then played along: "Even
if she meant to, she is no longer in a position to do so. But of
course you know that she is peace herself".
That was exactly what Emeni was angling to hear. She had been
wondering why Okunnu did not come to fetch her.
"Now Okunnu," she continued shortly, "I saw Mgbeoye's friend Ezinne
Okolocha of Itulu Akpa. She was the one who told me that she saw
Aduaka, you know that one that lives seven houses from you, who had
come to sell yam.
"She then inquired about Mgbeoye her childhood friend from him and he
then told her that she would soon put to bed.
"After what Mgbeoye has gone through in life, you know she deserves
all the care she can get. What am I suggesting in effect? I am saying
that I have been expecting your emissary or you to call on me. After
waiting for a long time, I decided to come. I know you cannot send me
away. Mgbeoye is my daughter and I also believe you cannot say that I
have come too early. From what I have seen of Mgbeoye since I came, I
should have come earlier."
Okunnu, now shamefaced managed to explain, "no, no Emeni Okwugo, all
is not as it seems. All is not as it seems. You are not another
person that I should lie to you or play games with you. Our gods
forbid. You see, we have been having problems with my second wife
Agbonma. In fact, she left this morning without saying a word to any
of us. I am only praying that she does not come to any harm. I have
been finding it difficult to do anything with a straight mind since
her strange ways began. Now she has stepped into a clear brook and
muddied it. Her brother, you know him now, Ajuluchukwu left here
around midday and he said that she had not been to Umulumgbe. It has
become a pain".
Emeni listened attentively and replied: " May the gods of the land
forbid that she fall into any danger."
"Ami Oh!"
It must have been midnight. The family of Okunnu was asleep and the
entire village was very quiet . Only the shrill sounds of night
insects broke the dead silence.
On her floor bed Emeni turned on her side and wondered what woke her
up. She could not immediately tell. Initially she thought it was her
newly found insomnia that was at work. But hardly had she readjusted
herself ready to sleep off again when she heard voices.
They sounded so close. The first voice, obviously that of a man
inquired of his companion, " why did I not see you at the appointed
time that day?"
"You mean the day before yesterday", the second voice wanted
clarification.
"Yes, did we not agree to meet here ?" the first voice asked,
sounding very impatient.
"You will never believe what happened that day. We had gone to fetch
water from the Ogbanku. We were having a good time, I mean all of us
who went to fetch water. There was no sign of trouble and in fact, we
had almost got back to the village when Achalaugo the sharp mouthed
one turned the discussion to child bearing."
Unimpressed, the first voice asked: "And what did that have to do
with our appointment?"
"Listen!" the other continued. "Achalaugo unknown to us had been
looking for an opportunity to tell Agbonma where her god burnt her
umbilical cord.
"Everybody was talking but immediately Agbonma said "it was a matter
for God to decide", Achalaugo retorted: "Go and sit down. What do you
know about it anyway. You may never be able to discuss this issue
with a voice of experience in your life. What I know is that it is
the atrocities that some families have committed that have made the
gods to seal up the wombs of their children from generation to
generation.
"Agbonma did not speak again. I did not know what to say. Achalaugo
having achieved her aim was enjoying herself. It was really a bad
day. It was such a callous thing to say and to say it so directly.
"When I got home, I just went to bed and was too restless just
imagining what Agbonma was going through.
"I kept wondering what if I got married and did not get my own
children, would I have to go through all that from my fellow women.
Perhaps I should never really get married. It is very scaring to
think of what horrors and evil the human mind and tongue are capable
of visiting on people!"
"God forbid! Don't be silly! You will get married and to me. People
will say what they feel but there is no one that agrees not to be
happy because of the wickedness of others. I mean, what perverted
logic are you getting carried away with. In fact, you are yet to tell
the whole truth."
Emeni lay still awhile. What? Agbonma? Which Agbonma? My daughter's
co-wife? No it cannot be?
To be continued next week.

Original_Search_Title: Consumer Price Index On Increase
Headline: Nigeria: Consumer Price Index On Increase
Body_Text:
THERE has been an increease in the measure of changes in the average
level of retail prices paid by Consumers in both Urban and Rural
areas.
The federal office of statistics revealed that increase stood at
3656.1 as against 3673.6 witnessed the previous month.
The above figure represented 0.5 percent decline below the 3673.6
observed in June, 2000. The drop is caused by the increase in the
prices of stables and kerosene during the period under review.
The All item Index remained stability in the urban area while the
rural All item Index dropped by 0.6 per cent.
Between July, 1999 and July, 2000 the average consumer price level
witnessed an increase by 6.7 percent compared to 5.9 percent recorded
in the preceding month. The rural and urban indices rose by 6.6 and
6.9 percent respectively, during the period under review.
The average annual rate of rise of consumer retail prices or the
percentage change in average composite Consumer Price Index (CPI) for
the twelve month period ending July, 2000 was 1.2.
The Implication of this is that a fixed market basket of consumer
goods and services purchased with N100.00 during the twelve month
period ending July, 1999 sold for N101.2 on the average during a
corresponding period ending July, 2000.
The food index remained the same in July 2000. This resulted from the
increased in the prices of edible oil being offset by decreased in
the prices of yam tuber. July, 1999 and July 2000, witnessed an
increase in the composite food index by 1.7.
The change in percentage is the average of he index for the period of
the twelve months period ending July, 2000 was a decline of 1.0
percent.
The All item excluding farm produce" Index declined by 0.8 percent in
July 2000. In the twelve months of July 2000, the index increase by
14.1 percent and the percentage change in the average of the Index
for the twelve month period ending July, 2000 was 4.9.

Original_Search_Title: Nigerian Independence Anniversary Celebration: Past In The Future
Headline: Nigerian Independence Anniversary Celebration: Past In The Future
Body_Text:
Lagos — Contemporary Nigeria is a nation of paradoxes - the worst has
delayed its departure while the best is yet to come. Perhaps given
the historical antecedents of the creation of the Nigerian State, the
persistence of the past as a drag on the future is not altogether
unexpected or unhistorical.
Yet Nigeria remains a nation of immense possibilities. Howbeit after
forty years of independent existence - the period that presages the
onset of middle age in an individual's life - possibilities and
potential should have given way to reality defined by a hopeful
anticipation of the future embedded in concrete achievements of the
present.
There is a sense in which it could be suggested that our forty years
of chequered independence mirrors the Jewish story of forty years in
the wilderness. Nigerians could therefore be forgiven for their
impatient but unanswered question: when will we get to the promised
land?
Symbols and myths are important indicators in human life and
experience. They summarise and they define. They can be powerful
tools in the apprehension of stubborn reality or the projection of a
desirable vision of the expected. What symbols then can best
approximate and summarise the Nigerian story - forty years down the
road? Is it the phoenix - that symbols of re-emergence from
unwholesome disaster and against the odds?
On is it Prometheus - that symbol of awesome power, forever
unrealised and unfulfilled. Neither would be appropriated in our
circumstance. There is a sense in which the continued existence of
the Nigerian state is a reaffirmation of the continuing relevance of
the miracles in human experience. Given the unabetting and inexorable
push of the forces of globalisation in the contemporary world, if
Nigeria did not exist, the necessity for co-operation and interaction
across our ethnic and micro-national enclaves would have enforced on
us the emergence of a co-operative arrangement with the hallmarks of
the Nigerian state as a conscious tool of supra-national coexistence.
The wisdom of the Igbo elders suggests that there is always in the
human psyche and experience the need to undertake an unremitting
quest: never able to value or appreciate fully that which we have. In
a sense it is also a matter of perception: human perception has often
proven a more potent instrument of history than the reality as
summarised by the extant facts. This perhaps explains the current
paradox that while the international business community has consigned
us to the dog-house as the most corrupt nation on Transparency
International's corruption index, the same community has put us on
top in the optimism index as the nation that embodies most the
potentials for desirable change. So contemporary Nigeria is its own
justification for existence. No apologies are expected and none
should be given.
October 1, 1960 was an unforgettable experience. The euphoria could
be touched with bare hands. It marked for most Nigerians of that
period the end of an era that called their dignity and humanity to
question. It was the beginning of a new dawn of unimaginable
potentials. Six short years down the road the unexpected happened -
the men on horse back arrived, never to depart except for short
intermissions - until last year. What went wrong?
Given the benefit of hindsight, it is possible to suggest that we
never really came to terms with the magnitude of problems which
colonialism chose to mask. We never understood the awesome problems
that needed to be understood and mastered. We always underrated the
demands of responsibility and self-governance. We underestimated the
complexity and intricacies implied in the interactive dynamism of
competing and coexisting nationalities, sub-nationalities and
cultural entities defined by their own history and world-view. Above
all, we ignored because we could not envision the awesome power for
good that could be unleashed when diversities and differences are
creatively channelled to produce new flavours and new colours of
coexisting realities in the human family. We lost in the process the
catalytic creativity of what President Clinton, a product of a
multicultural, dynamic and future-focussed society has called
creative tension. It is the prerogative of youths to idealise and to
oversimplify. So did the young soldiers who came to recreate Nigeria
in their own image driven by the exuberance and sheer force of their
inchoate idealism. The fly in the ointment is that forty years down
the road we may not have understood where the rain began to beat us -
to borrow an Achebean aphorism. What then is the score-sheet on the
road we have taken?
There is the apocryphal story of the meeting of the two great
founding fathers of our putative nation-hood - Nnamdi Azikiwe and
Ahmadu Bello. One would have us forget our differences while the
other would prefer we understand our differences. The reality is that
we have chosen neither to forget nor to understand. The result is
that because we cannot forget we have been unable to reach out from
our fortresses of sectional exclusivity to join forces with other
compatriots to build a nation, indeed a civilisation, of creative,
responsive and enterprising people. Is it not an unacceptable paradox
that men who have presided over the affairs of Nigeria - the largest
collection of African peoples anywhere on earth - are more
comfortable to be counted as champions of their "sections" of Nigeria
rather than being seen as the embodiment and symbols of an inclusive
Nigerian nationality? Conversely, we also find that because we cannot
understand, we are impatient with and perhaps even intolerant of, the
view that patience in understanding is the constructive basis for
co-operative and integrative coexistence. It is the basis for
cumulative, consolidative and reconciliatory potential in human
experience which over time is the only basis for the emergence of
that which can endure. The alternative vision of a conformist and
uniformist social reality is doomed to failure - as the collapse of
communism and fascism constantly reminds mankind. We need to create
in the Nigerian community an environment that harnesses the potential
of our diverse cultures and diverse competencies for a constructive
edifice that emerges from a shared vision of a dynamic nationhood.
The odds remain daunting but achievable. We must, therefore, come to
terms with the demands of a shared destiny which can only be willed
into being through a shared vision.
There are two stories that Nigerians love to retell to each other.
The first deals with how the Malaysians came to Nigeria, picked a few
seedlings of the oil palm and proceeded thereafter to build an entire
industry on it. The Malaysians are now not only leaders in the
production and exportation of the products derived from the oil palm
but are the industrial leaders on the various intermediate and final
industrial products derivable from the oil palm. This circumstance is
the result of their mastery of applied science and technology
harnessed to the industrial domestication of the oil palm. This story
is illustrative as a contrast to our inability to utilise to the
optimum the opportunities that are inherent in our rich resource
endowments. It is perhaps pertinent to indicate also that as of 1964,
Malaysia was behind Nigeria in all the indicators of economic or
human development of relevance. In addition, Malaysia is a
multi-ethnic and multi- religious society that, like us, fought a
civil war that degenerated for them into a long running guerrilla war
- we were luckier. Yet they were able to put that disruptive and
violent interregnum behind them. What and how made their miracle
possible.
Now to the second story. The story has to do with how the Japanese
came to the University of Ife and picked up the prototype of the yam
ponder machine and took this contraption back to Japan, cleaned it up
technically and produced an elegantly designed version. The resulting
commercial machines are said to be on sale in Nigeria at prices
unaffordable to most, although it has become a status symbol for rich
Nigerians including, I hear, former Ministers and Heads of state.
This second story confirms that we are capable of innovative thinking
and can apply our minds to the problems and tasks thrown up by the
circumstances of our peculiar environment as do the thousands of
Nigerian scientists and technologists in the diaspora. We need only
add that Japan does not produce any yams and the Japanese do not eat
any derivative of yams. But they recognise a potential market when
they see one. We must not forget also that in the immediate
post-world war era, made in Japan was the symbol of poor quality
manufacture. Now the reverse is the case. This story illustrates then
our failure to exploit the rewards of creativity, initiative and
innovation in the Nigerian system as it exists presently.
The most dramatic demonstration of our collective failure is the fact
that in 2000 AD, the quality of life of our people is worse than it
was in 1964! Indeed, the GDP per capita which had progressively risen
to the equivalent of USD $1000 per capita has now plummeted to a
little over USD $300 - one third of the value in 1980. The obvious
reason for this situation has been our inability to mobilise science,
engineering and technology (SET) into the economic development and
wealth creation process. For experience in the post-world war era of
the 20th century demonstrated a necessary linkage between value
addition through productive endeavours embodied in SET and the
take-off of break-through economic growth in economies as different
as the countries of Latin America and South East Asia. The size of
financial disbursements of SET activities in any given country is now
an important indicator of SET's role in the economic growth and
industrial growth of such a nation. Empirical data suggests 2.5 - 3.0
per and GDP as the threshold. Nigeria's expenditure on SET is
presently 0.1 per cent of GDP. It was SET that Malaysia called into
service as the instrument of choice for the development first of its
oil palm industry and now of its petrochemical industry. The build-up
of national capacity in these basic technologies has been the launch
pad that she has utilised to build the high technology industries
based on computer chips and integrated circuits in which Malaysia is
now a leading exporter. This example dictates that the priorities for
our economic development in the new century should be to engineer a
paradigm shift in the definition of our national priorities such that
we can focus on the following tasks:-
* work for a ten-fold increase in the provision of funds for SET
activities;
* realign SET activities to be in synergy with our economic
priorities - our competitive economic advantage should be anchored on
our SET capacity and capabilities at each given period and in each
sector;
* ensure the permeation of all areas of national life by SET content
and concept;
* reorganise SET organs to serve the needs of our economy and to
serve as the co-ordinative nerve centre for our economic and socio-
political goals.
The question of what we need to do has been impliedly answered by the
example of Malaysia, Indonesia, South Korea and Taiwan. The question
of how requires further thought. As the experience of these countries
demonstrate, industrial development is the obvious strategy of
choice. But in the post-industrial age of high technology, what
strategies are appropriate to our situation? Each country's situation
and unique capabilities, competence and endowments in resources and
skills determine the appropriate mix of incentives and initiatives.
In our particular circumstances, given the inexorable push of and
prebendalism. We have experienced the progressive degeneration of our
society to this level in the last fifteen years. The hope and the
expectation is that in the democratic environment in which we have
chosen to pursue our life together as a nation, the objectives of
transparency and accountability will become welcome attributes of
governance and political interaction and hence, the essential
lubricant for our new entry into an era of equity and social justice.
There is yet another story that Nigerians regale themselves with. The
story is that God had been so liberal in His endowment of Nigeria
that the ruler of a less endowed nations complained about the implied
partiality indicated in the distribution of such wealth and such
other desirable natural resources. At which complaint God had
replied: "wait until you see the people that I will put there."
Self-denigration can be mistaken for modesty or humility but it is
not a desirable character in an individual or in a nation. Nigeria
has indeed been used and abused by her leaders but this cannot
explain or excuse the fact that negative sentiments and opinions
about our country come naturally to the average Nigerian than the
positive. It is true that in the office, on the roads, in the farms
and in the factories, Nigerians do not show sufficient care and
compassion for each other. Indeed we can in some circumstances be
regarded as a callous nation. But the negative opinions about the
country and about each other that is the currency of social exchange
amongst the citizens of an otherwise great nation is not an admirable
or constructive trait. We have not always been that way. The violence
of the war years and after, the dog-eat-dog mind-set of the post-war
years of rebuilding and reconstruction and the materialism of the
oil-boom years have all left their scars on the national psyche. We
need to rediscover the memories and echoes of our age of innocence.
This is where the quality and style of our leaders becomes the
philosopher's stone, the panacea for all ills. For the truism is
instructive: our actions rather than our words are the truest
testimonies and signals of who we are. That is the challenge before
the Nigerian leadership and the Nigerian people at middle age. May we
as we search for our future in our past, reconnect with our essential
core being as a nation and in the process rediscover a lost world of
glory and innocence through God's grace. May the Good Lord bless our
nation especially on its 40th Birthday.

Original_Search_Title: Why Igbos Are Not United - Prof Nwankwo
Headline: Nigeria: Why Igbos Are Not United - Prof Nwankwo
Body_Text:
Distinguished banker, author and former Dean, Faculty of Business
 Administration, University of Lagos (UNILAG), Professor Green .O. Nwankwo
 has admitted that Igbos are not united  in the pursuit of their legitimate
 demands from the Federal Government.
The reasons for the sad development, he said, is the preponderance of
youths in the Igbo population which according to him, "is very high".
Speaking in Surulere, Lagos during the Ndiakeme -Arondizuogu New Yam
Festival, Professor Nwankwo said "Igbo population is high just as the
youths are many. While elders sit behind watching and mapping out
ways of forging ahead, the young ones are easily agitated and
restive." Referring to the lukewarm attitude of the Igbos to the
Movement for the Actualisation of the Sovereign State of Biafra
(MASSOB), Prof. Nwankwo said "that does not mean elders are not
supporting them." In his remarks, the organiser of the festival,
Chief Innocent M. Okoro tasked Ohaneze Ndigbo on Igbo unity saying
"it has a role to play in the unity or disunity of Igbo people."
According to him, Igbo elders were not in the forefront of the
struggle by the Chief Ralph Uwazuruike-led MASSOB because "we do not
want to derail the new democracy in which we all have a stake." Chief
Okoro said this year's New Yam Festival was also organised in
remembrance of the great nationalist, politician and acolyte of the
"Great Zik of Africa," late Chief Mbonu Ojike "whom the government
has left unsung." Late Chief Ojike he said, in collaboration with
late Mbadiwe, established the only NITEL exchange in Arondizuogu
which has long collapsed. Chief Okoro enjoined state governors in the
former Easter Region to live in peace and harmony with their subjects
saying "in spite of the balkanisation of the former Eastern Region in
the name of state creation, we still remain one."

Original_Search_Title: The Needle Has Fallen From the Leper
Headline: Nigeria: The Needle Has Fallen From the Leper
Body_Text:
Lagos — It would be unnatural to have expected those who have been
idly feeding fat on the nation since independence, eating the
precious seed yams after ravaging the nation's barn, not to attempt a
come-back.
What else does the nation expect from millionaires who own neither
special skills, nor farms nor factories when it is mounting an anti-
corruption crusade, probing looters and asking for probity and
accountability? The needle of exploitation and internal colonialism
has fallen from the leper, its attempted retrieval cannot but be
dramatic and it can only be restored if those of us whose blood it
was used to suck turn round to give assistance at our own peril.
That sums up for me the latest attempt by members of the oligarchy
who successfully ruined Nigeria in the last three or four decades to
regain power through foul or fair means. The sudden emergence of the
association of former military ruiners of the nation as critics of
the Obasanjo government which, they are not tired of telling every
bird on the wings, was made possible by them shows how fragile our
democracy remains.
But the old game is up, at least for most Nigerians. What did the old
war horses achieve when they had power legitimately and
illegitimately? The northern masses, in spite and because of the
Arewa mafia's virtual monopoly of power, remain further back than
their southern counterparts. Is that what they want us to accept
again? At the national level, it was the same tale of woes. The
economy was destroyed wilfully.
Nigerians' self-reliance at group and individual levels were
destroyed. Corruption was enthroned as the means of governance and
the nation witnessed retarded growth. They must have taken Nigerians
for fools to have started another Maradona move. May be the diversion
is to make us forget the 12.3 billion dollar scam?
But it is not enough to bandy words with those who have ruined the
nation's dreams. It is important that we realise that they have been
clever enough to have laid down a structure that could assist them to
make a come back. So long as the unitary system they imposed on the
federation remains, so long would they be tempted to stage a
come-back. It is the distortion of the federation into a unitary
state that made possible the virtual internal colonialisation of the
entire nation by a clique. That must be reversed in order to ensure
that it will never happen again.
In this regard, President Obasanjo appears to be in the classical
dilemma of Pela. In contrast with the position of his Yoruba kinsmen,
he appears to be a believer in unitarism which he appears to confuse
with unity. He also appears to be comfortable with a big
paternalistic federal government. It seems to me that he believes
that once the leader of government is honest all other things will
fall into place. That is an error since the existing structure
determines the form and functions of its elements. Until the
President cures himself of this error he would continue to encourage
his grave digger friends.
By the way, upon what logic is the Arewa mafia operating by thinking
that the presidency must return to the North after Obasanjo. What
about the East, South-south and the Middle Belt? Until Nigeria's
distorted federation is restored we would continue to have attempts
at political revisionism. That is the reality.

Original_Search_Title: Local Government Boss to Make Peace for Traditional Rulers
Headline: Nigeria: Local Government Boss to Make Peace for Traditional Rulers
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Executive Chairman of Oyi Local Government Area of
Anambra State, Mr. O.C Ilozue has pledged to resolve the disharmony
inherent in the leadership of the council of traditional rulers in
Oyi Local Government.
According to him, the councils constitution favours the succession of
leadership by the communities in alphabetical sequence.
Ilozue maintained that his government would work in accordance with
the dictates of the constitution.
Speaking at this year's New Yam Festival at Awkuzu, he pledged to
work towards the development of the council. The festival was graced
by indigenes, friends and well wishers of the community who converged
to celebrate the traditional rite.
Among the dignitaries that graced the occasion include His Royal
Highness, Igwe C.A. Ibegbunam of Nkpor, Igwe J.N. Nwegwu of Logu, the
entire Igwe in- Council led by Chief Emeka Obi Oduenyi, the Onosoleze
Awkuzu, and top government officials including the local government
chairman and his entourage among others.
The traditional ruler of Awkuzu, Igwe John Nebolisa also used the
occasion to further clarify issues about some allegations recently
levelled against him in the media. He dismissed them as the handwork
of his detractors who are envious of his contributions to the
development of the area.
The royal father accused the Chief Security Adviser to Governor
Mbadinuju of Anambra State, Mr. Chukwuma Nzeribe, of using his access
to the state's information machinery to run him down.
The traditional ruler cited the several revolts witnessed in the
state in the last few weeks against the security fees and other
excesses of the Bakassi Boys as an instrument of vendetta by the
public as ways of registering their discontent over the state
administration.
He, however, praised the State Governor Dr. Chinwoke Mbadinuju for
suspending the criminal fees upon return from his foreign trips. He
also called on governor to listen to the voice of the people and
terminate without further delay the appointment of the chief security
officer immediately.

Original_Search_Title: Item People Paint Lagos Red With New Yam Festival
Headline: Nigeria: Item People Paint Lagos Red With New Yam Festival
Body_Text:
Lagos — The use of town - unions and socio-cultural organisations as
instruments for the revival of a community's cultural heritage was
amply demonstrated on September 16 when the Item Union, Lagos branch,
celebrated its new Yam Festival (Ekembo).
Tagged, "millennium new yam festival," it was conceived by the union
to celebrate the cultural and traditional values of the town. The
importance of the event was underscored by the massive attendance of
both the town's indigenes and their guests who had defied that day's
rain and fuel scarcity to grace the occasion.
No doubt, unhappy with the sad displacement of the African culture by
the western one, the union invited Professor Onwukwe Alaezi of the
Abia State University, as guest speaker, to educate, particularly,
the younger ones, on the origin, significance and cultural content of
the new yam festival.
The eminent professor, in his scholarly paper, traced the origin of
the new yam ceremony to the festival of the first front or feast of
weeks as commanded by Moses, in line with God's law which was handed
over to him (Lev. 23:9-22). Using anthropological, sociological,
historical and religious empirical evidence, Prof. Alaezi proved that
the Igbos of Nigeria (including Item people), are of the same stock
as the Hebrews of Israel.
According to him, the Igbos of Nigeria practice the laws of Moses
regarding farming; they leave the land fallow for seven years before
recultivating it; they do not take home all the crops after harvest,
but leave some for the poor who may wish to go scavaging the
remnants; and they observe, strictly, the festival of the first fruit
in the form of new yam festival.
He noted that the new yam ceremony in Item is usually a process
lasting over a period of time within a three-month planting and
harvest season.
"Item as a whole observes certain pre-new yam ceremonies with special
socio- cultural significance attached to each ceremony." These
ceremonies usually occur week after week of the Igbo four-day-a-week
format.
The ceremonies are: Isuofia (bush clearing), Igwa Mai (appeasing the
gods of the land), Iku Odudu (planting of African beans), Ita Achicha
(symbolic of the Jewish unleavened bread), Iwa Ndi Ama (sharing of
the remaining old years amongst relations before the arrival of the
new ones), Afo Agwu (two days to the eating of the new yam,
sacrifices are made at the shrine of Agwu and other gods), Nkwo Ikpa
(the day preceding the actual eating of the new yam when a vigil of
songs is observed at the village Square), Eke Mbo (the new yam
celebration day), Orie - Ikpata Okuku (the day all wives cook for
their lineages and men, old and young eat from the same pot and drink
from the same gourd in a covenant - like communion) and Igba Izu (the
day of celebration for warriors and big hunters who dance to the
beating of a big wooden drum).
The meticulous observance of these stages, resulting to the new yam
festival is, significant: it marks the end of the old year and the
beginning of the new one in Igbo calendar of four days a week which
tallies with the Jewish calendar.
Besides, Professor Alaezi submitted that the celebration emphasises
the promotion of positive aspects of the people evident in unity,
progress, development, mutual respect and help for one another.
He further argued that instead of completely rejecting the new yam
celebration because of its fetish aspects, it should be repackaged by
culture practitioners to conform with the will of God.
It was more than enough provision to make it take the form of the
celebration of the first fruit as commanded by Moses either through
the already existing ceremonies devoid of sacrifice and offering to
idols or through a total overhaul of the entire process with inputs
of the true worshippers of God," he said.
Earlier, Eze Mba Ajah, Inikiribada of Akpa Na Agbo Item, had, in
breaking the Kolanut at the occasion, thanked God for the reality of
the day, and prayed for long life and prosperity to his subjects in
Lagos whom he enjoined to uphold their tradition always.
Another remarkable aspect of the occasion was the Ikwasa Okwa by the
Item women which was received by the Item Union President, Mr. Theo
Egesi Eke, and handed over to Eze Ajah. It was at this point that the
event climaxed with the cutting of a new yam into nine parts,
representing the nine component villages of Item by Eze Ajah and
distributed to the women. The distribution naturally followed the
order of village seniority.
Mr. Eke, in his welcome address, had told the audience that since
farming is the mainstay of Item people, it was germane that the
Town's union, under his leadership, should take the initiative of
celebrating the new yam festival for the first time in Lagos so that
people will become better informed on this vital aspect of their
cultural heritage.
He also used the occasion to solicit the assistance of the
governments at the various levels in the rehabilitation of their
roads and electricity.
A colour was added to the event with the abundance display of
cultural dances: Ikpirikpo Ogu, Ite Akwukwa, Udu Nkulu, Isi Nwebulu,
Inyom Item Lagos Cultural Group, Inyom Item Badagry Dance Group, etc.
The Chairman of the occasion, Chief Gospel Nwabuko Ugwa, commended
the efforts of the organisers of the programme, and called on Abia
State government to extend democracy dividends to the populace.
At the end of the day, it was a grand occasion, as in-laws, friends
and well wishers turned up to celebrate with Item Union, Lagos
Branch, on the occasion of Item millennium new yam festival.

Original_Search_Title: Abidjan Residents Prepare for the Unknown
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Abidjan Residents Prepare for the Unknown
Body_Text:
Abidjan, CÃ´te d'Ivoire — Abidjan residents began to
restock their food supplies Wednesday, 48 hours ahead of the
publication by the Supreme Court of the list of candidates for
Cote d'Ivoire's 22 October presidential election.
"We have come to the supermarket to get our food supplies,
as tension escalates in the conflict between the ruling military
junta and the numerous supporters of political parties, to the
extent that this makes people afraid", said Marie-Andrea Kouame,
a housewife pushing a trolley full of food items at a supermarket
at the Plateau area of Abidjan.
She has not forgotten the 4-5 July mutiny by soldiers,
which prevented people in Abidjan from going about their day-to-
day chores.
"During the mutiny by soldiers last July, my children and
myself spent two days without showing our faces outside. We
had all sorts of difficulties finding what to eat", she recalls.
The mutiny officially led to the death of three people
and eight others who sustained injuries.
Ten commissioned and non-commissioned officers were
arrested and detained in the Abidjan military prison.
Another resident, Alfonso Gonzales, an Italian national who
has spent three years in Cote d'Ivoire said he needed to buy more
food as a precautionary measure ahead of the announcement of the
D-day.
He said people whose candidates fail to make the list could
raise hell.
At the Treichville market, several women said they would
buy food that would last for at least two weeks.
"You can see all these baskets filled with bananas, yams,
rice and 'couscous'. We have enough supplies for 10 or so
days. The precaution is worth taking, for with the military,
anything can happen", another resident said.
Banks too have also been busy with an influx of customers
coming to withdraw money, in the face of the uncertainty
that lies ahead in the coming days.
"Since eight o'clock, the number of clients keeps growing.
They rush in either to get the salary of last month or to
withdraw money in anticipation of possible social unrest",
Zacharie Aka, a bank employee, said.
"It is normal that we all find ourselves at the bank.
Nobody wants to run into trouble with nothing to eat during hot
days", Anatole Zago said.
Supporters of the Rally of Republicans (MDR) have
threatened to make Cote d'Ivoire ungovernable, if their leader,
Alassane Dramane Ouattara, is barred from the presidential
race.
To "avoid confusion", the ruling junta in Cote d'Ivoire,
has imposed a state of emergency and a curfew from Friday 6
October to Monday 9 October 2000.
"These measures are aimed at "preventing serious threats to
the security of people and goods in this pre-electoral period
likely to destabilise the normal operation of State
institutions", the government said in a statement announcing the
state of emergency.

Original_Search_Title: Re-inventing the Investment Climate
Headline: Nigeria: Re-inventing the Investment Climate
Body_Text:
Lagos — The heightening realisation that foreign investment in the
nation's socio-economic renaissance is imperative sends President
Olusegun Obasanjo tripping around the globe and the Minister of
Industries preaching outlines of the new wisdom. Agha Ibiam reports
Since the successful enthronement of a democratic order in Nigeria,
the government of President Olusegun Obasanjo has been making frantic
efforts to lure foreign investors. In apparent linkage to this
purpose, Obasanjo in 16 months, has made about 34 foreign trips,
cutting across 25 countries since assumption of office. Although
these frequent trips have been widely criticized.
Against this background when the Minister of Industry, Mr. Steven
Akiga, at the recent official opening of Sharp Showroom and Service
Centre, in Lagos, promised that foreign investors will henceforth
enjoy tax holidays as well as export expansion grants, it elicited
little surprise. He promised that foreign investments in the country
would be guaranteed through the Investment Promotion and Protection
Agreement (IPPA) with countries doing business with Nigeria.
The Industries Minister also revealed that the current economic
reforms in the country were designed to increase the attractiveness
of Nigeria's investment environment and foster confidence in the
economy. "The reforms have resulted in the adoption of liberal and
market oriented economic policies, the stimulation of increased
private sector participation and the elimination of bureaucratic
bottlenecks which hinders private-sector investment and long-term
profitable business operations in Nigeria," he added.
Further, he also noted that industrialization provided the brightest
hope for sustained growth, employment generation improved savings,
poverty alleviation and indeed economic development.
Mr. Akiga explained that government's plan for industrialization has
been to address effectively, the problems of deteriorating
infrastructures, poor inter and intra-sectarian linkages, lack of
patronage for domestic products, unfavorable tariff regime, dumping
and the inability of Nigerian manufactured goods to compete favorably
internationally."
Speaking with THISDAY recently, a Director of the Lagos Business
School (LBS) Dr. Pat Utomi, described the idea of inviting foreign
investors to Nigeria as a wonderful one. "That kind of incentive is a
wonderful one. It is normal in many countries that are looking
forward to attract investments. Nigeria in the past had introduced
tax holidays, export support, duty drawback scheme. But what
happened. Just go and look at the ones we had implemented in the
past," he asked.
Though not completely agreeing to the wonders foreign investors and
related policies will achieve in Nigeria, Dr. Utomi argued that there
are still problems to be tackled. Hear him: "The problems I have with
policies in Nigeria is that people think that one decision made in
isolation of so many critical variables will solve all the problems
that have been waiting to be solved and in six months later not much
has happened, everybody is frustrated.
We must come to terms with the fact that we are dealing with a basket
of variables that will have impact and effect on foreign investors."
Utomi further explored why foreign investors may refuse to come and
invest in a country where there are no requisite facilities. Besides
the export incentives, manufacturing incentives like tax holidays are
mouth-watering. He fingered local security situation as a
disincentive. Identifying constraints ranging from bad
infrastructures, which will be impossible for him to get his product
from where he has located his factory to the seaport, Utomi noted
that these were drawbacks to the prospective investor.
"Suppose he is producing for export and each time he gets to the
ports, custom officials blackmail him to the point that he gets
thoroughly frustrated. What do you expect?" he asked
Utomi also said that everything he has stated has specific problems
of specific enterprises that have failed and that he knows.
In a recent trip to London, according to him, he felt bad for not
seeing any Nigerian product like pineapples, unlike Ghana whose goods
flood the market. He attributed this scenario to lapses in
operational efficiency and dubious attitudes of some Nigerian Customs
officials.
Peeved by his observation in London, on return to Nigeria he went
into research on why the country does not export those things. His
efforts revealed that each time fresh produce, like pineapples or
yams get to the ports, Customs will round them up and run around
until the produce gets bad, all in trying to extort money from the
products' exporters.
Not unnaturally few prospective exporters of such delicate products
would like putting themselves to such inconvenience and loosing
millions on investment to boot.
Utomi who cannot figure out why people indulge in corruption,
described that as abnormal adding that such is not a matter of
morality. "The point is, there are lots of factors that impact upon
investment. Productivity increases, which will count to development.
Is a wonderful thing that we are addressing them, but the sooner we
learn that we have to address the entire basket before Nigeria is
attractive, the better it will be."
On the energy front, it is common knowledge that Nigeria is a country
that is flaring so much gas. One of the competitive advantage areas
should be cheap energy. Many analysts believe it would be cheaper to
have energy in Nigeria than elsewhere because of the country's
endowment in gas.
Utomi posited that if Nigeria can manage all the factors he had
identified income would grow at more than 12% per annum and people
will be falling over each other to invest in Nigeria. "We should deal
with them. Ghana has dealt with those issues. You arrive Accra
airport, you feel welcome to the country. The reverse is the case in
Nigeria. If you are a foreign investor, you won't leave Accra for
Nigeria," he said.
According to Utomi merely talking about foreign investment would
yield little, but when the appropriate investment climate is created
both domestic and foreigners will come.
Along the lines of this thinking, foreign investors may not begin to
move in any major way, until they see Nigeria moving. "They know how
much of Nigerian money is abroad. They don't see Nigerians bringing
back their money. If the indigenes are putting their money away,
foreigners can't bring their money," he reasoned.
For Mr. Sunday Ahaiwe, a Lagos-based professional banker, for Nigeria
to attract foreign investors, there are some fundamental issues that
would have to be addressed. "It is not only attracting such
investors, but to sustain them. The problem of made-in- Nigeria goods
attracting very low patronage, is not that Nigeria is not interested
in patronizing home made goods. The problem is that such goods sell
at a cost higher than their imported counter-parts," he told THISDAY.
And moreÂ."Industries today in Nigeria are run at a very formidable
cost in terms of energy. Many conglomerates use almost 100%
self-generated power energy. And when this is the case, it will
translate to a very high cost. He stated that it is only an
irrational consumer that buys good at a higher cost when they are
similar products having similar satisfaction that can be bought at a
lower cost.
"There is no enough reason to go abroad and invite these people. They
are not blind people. Apart from what they are hearing about Nigeria,
they would want to come and investigate the available infrastructures
on the ground," he observed.
Citing an instance that the recent August visitor, Mr. Bill Clinton
of the United States came to Nigeria with his own generator and
virtually everything he needed to use in Nigeria, he saw this as an
insult and a let down to the country, he added.
Experience has shown that foreigners develop a place. Ahaiwe who did
not doubt that investors certainly would enhance economic reform and
bring technology down to us when they come. In this vein it will
reduce the prices of goods for the common man, provide job that will
translate into community development by way of social responsibility.
Going by this reasoning, he disclosed that investors are people who
don't waste their resources unnecessarily. "Before you invest, you
analyze your investment and look at the returns on them. The cost and
then the capital outlay. You look at the possible profit and loss of
the investment, before considering whether there will be tax holidays
or not," he noted.
Tax holidays, to him makes him laugh. He wondered why grant tax
holidays to foreigners, when the indigenous manufacturers don't have
any incentive. Charity starts at home, he told THISDAY. "That is why
the cost of goods in Nigeria are galloping everyday and foreign goods
are evidently cheaper and people don't patronize home made goods. Our
local industries are manufacturing at very high cost. For them to
make profit, are to sell at a profitable price and such profitable
prices always translated into higher prices? Grant tax holidays to
the local manufacturers and let people buy goods at a cheaper price.
If they can now extend it to foreign investors because all are
working towards development. But if tax holidays are extended to
foreigners, when local industrialist are stifled and dying that means
the economy will be import oriented," he elaborated.
On the other hand, if there are incentives and infrastructures aiding
the local manufacturers, Ahaiwe further observed, foreigners will be
attracted to come, but when the government is brandishing these and
it is common knowledge they are not obtainable, prospective foreign
investors will not take them serious. "They will think they just want
to attract them, when they come, they will be trapped," he said.
He argued that any policy that is introduced, which does not
translate into the welfare of the common man is an unwelcome policy,
and that with the high cost of production in the industries many of
them are retrenching because they want to cut down cost. When a
company spends N2 million a month, to generate power, then labour
will not be sustained because they want to stay on board. He felt
this was as a result of certain micro economic policies, which have
not favored the local manufacturers.
Whatever the case may be, the Industries Minister remains unfazed by
the complex maze he necessarily must navigate to break through. He
captures this mind-set in another dimension of the game-plan he had
put out: there will be "unhindered and timely reparation of capital
and dividends when declared, through the Inter-bank Foreign Exchange
Market (IFEM)."
Observers believe that with the new minister of industry that is on
board, investors should be looking forward to having a better
investment climate that will favor both the local and international
investors. But the sooner the underpinning variables are tackled, the
better for the country.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Consumer Items On the Rise
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Consumer Items On the Rise
Body_Text:
Lagos — Prices of consumer items, particularly foodstuff, experienced
upward shifts in prices in the week. While demand remained high,
supply was affected by the energy crises of the previous week which
crippled transportation across the country.
Items like beverages maintained a relative stability in most cities,
while foodstuffs went a little bit high. The Post Express went to
most conventional markets in the metropolis to monitor the heart
beats of retailers and consumers alike. Most sellers complained that
the high cost of transportation occasioned by scarcity of fuel and
diesel affected grossly the prices of most foodstuffs. Consumers on
the other hand, are not finding it easy. Majority of them that spoke
with us said they have tightened their consumption instinct to enable
them meet up with other financial obligations particularly
transportation cost.
A breakdown of the composite consumer price index indicates that
edible items take greater part of the disposable income of consumers,
thus out of 1000 weight components of the composite consumer price
index, food price exhausts 690.9, all items less farm produce stand
at 362.3, drinks, tobacco and kola consume 47.4, clothing and
footwear stand at 46.9, while accommodation, fuel, kerosene and light
stand at 118.7
Consumer items that witnessed a relative increase in price include
kerosene, fuel, gas and diesel. Kerosene formerly sold at N140 per 5
litres moved up to N250 and presently sells at N400. Fuel and diesel
are scarce and basically sold at black market rate, while gas is
unaffordable.
The energy crisis however affected the prices of staples including,
garri, yam, fresh tomatoes, stockfish, crayfish, fish and palm oil.
A bag of garri formerly sold at N1800 now goes for N2100, a tin
formerly at N280 now sells at N380 while one olodo of garri sells at
N90. A tuber of yam depending on the size sells at an average price
range of N65, N130 and N200, for small, medium and big sizes
respectively.
A small basket of fresh tomatoes costs N250, medium size costs N650
while a big basket stands at N1,200. Analysts said farmers
experienced bumper harvest but high cost of transportation from the
rural areas where they are produced to the cities has influenced the
upward price movement of these staples.
A mudu of crayfish costs N500, a bottle of palm oil costs N60, 3
litres of vegetable oil costs N350 while a carton containing 6
bottles stands at N2100. Rice, beans, Ogbolo, egwusi, onions, maggi
and knorr, drinks, millet and maize maintain relative stability
within the period under review.
The composite consumer price index was 3673.6 in June 2000
representing an increase of 4.3 per cent above 3521.7 recorded in
May, 2000. The rise in the index was largely attributable to
substantial increase in the price of kerosene. The prices of staples,
kolanut and detergent also went up during the period. The urban and
rural all item indices rose by 3.3 and 4.5 per cent respectively.
The average consumer price level between June 1999 and June 2000
advanced by 5.9 per cent compared to 2.2 per cent observed in the
preceding month of May. At that same period the urban and rural
indices rose by 5.8 and 5.9 per cent respectively.
The composite food index rose by 1.7 per cent in June 2000. The
upward movement in the index was largely caused by increases in the
prices of garri and yam tuber. The price of edible oil dropped in
many centres. Between June 1999 and June 2000 the composite food
index scarcely showed any change. The percentage change in the
average of the index for the twelve month period ending June 2000 was
a decline of 1.4 per cent. All items excluding farm produce index
went up by 7.5 per cent in June 2000. In the twelve months to June
2000, the index rose by 13.9 per cent and the percentage change in
the average of the index for the twelve month period ending June 2000
was 5.0
The average annual rate of rise of consumer retail prices for the 12
month period ending June 2000 was 0.9, indicating that a fixed market
basket of consumer goods and services purchased with N100 during the
12 month period ending June 1999 sold for N100.9 on the average
during a corresponding period ending June, 2000.

Original_Search_Title: For Power to Go East
Headline: Nigeria: For Power to Go East
Body_Text:
Lagos — Now that the potential of the East Electorally is no longer
in doubt, it may be useful to explore the likely pattern of
activities that will presage the coming of political power.
Probably, the most important assignment will be the breaking down of
walls of suspicion and misunderstanding. For this purpose the East
ought to encompass willing and supportive elements across the old
Bendel, parts of the Middle Belt and of course, the former Eastern
region. Ethnographically, these people share a good deal in common
even if variations in dialects or tongues are evident.
They are all of the Bantu stock and were not conquered by the
jihadists of the early nineteenth century. As independent minded
people, they place a lot of premium on achievement rather than
ascription. They also share a great pride in their culture and
traditions of good neighbourliness, self reliance, and hard work.
These are, the bonds that have united these peoples even if not much
has been made of their coincidence of interests.
To revive these bonds, there is need for increased mutual
interactions of these peoples on the social economic and political
front. May be, a good starting point will be the resolve last year of
the Youth groups of these areas to forge a common platform. Even if
the youth begin with the realisation of the immense wealth and
opportunities in the area, they surely will have enough agenda to
host and co -host consciousness raising meetings across the length
and breath of the area. A focus on the removal of the artificial
enmity foisted on them by the former leaders of the ancient regime
will have the effect of self rediscovery and the perception of a
common cause.
As soon as it becomes clear that there is indeed a pattern of mutual
interdependence in the area, the present level of commercial exchange
within the area will receive a boost. Food products from the Old
Bendel areas like, yam, garri, cocoyam, okro will find ready markets
in the East. While rice, egusi and vegetables from the East will
circulate freely in the other areas. These will also engender the
need to rebuild the road networks within and without the area.
Nothing brings out cooperation and understanding between people more
than cultural exchanges. Inter-state sporting and cultural events can
easily be inaugurated within the zone. Imagine what interest a
football match between Edo College, Benin and Emuda College, Abriba
in Abia State will engender. Or another friendly between Oron Boys
High School in Akwa Ibom and St. Patrick's College, Asaba. Is it not
possible for an Urhobo cultural troupe to be hosted in Uyo in the
spirit of new understanding. Nothing, of course, will stop a school
in Idah from going on an excursion to Opobo. These are possibilities
that must be translated into reality.
But to do these require maximum political will. The Isoko man may
have to accommodate the views of the Ibibio, Igbo and Efik. Similarly
the Igbo will have to reinvent the brotherly ties with the Kalabari
and the Ogoja people. This will call for new perspectives among
politicians in the area. Any gain by one group should not be seen as
a loss to the others. At the political level, all hands ought to be
on deck for the actualization of a more beneficial derivative formula
in revenue sharing. In the past, it was fashionable for people from
other areas to parasite on oil revenue to the detriment of the
indigenous peoples. A common front could end this injustice once and
for all.
Therefore as the political ferment spreads in the country, the East
and its neighbour cannot afford to be complacent. The West is
battling with itself over their proper political identification.
There is no reason to believe that their Alliance for democracy will
emerge from its present trauma with its cohesive capability intact.
Moreover, the debilitating debate of the place of Afenifere in the
political equation of the West seems to point in the direction of a
realignment of forces. So far, they have managed to appear like a
team just because of the Obasanjo's incumbency.
The North, of course, is nursing its grudges over its "decision to
concede power." It appears to be sending dubious feelers to the East.
On no account must this be taken seriously as the northern
protagonists are back to their old habit of scouting for our
politically deaf and dumb as surrogates. Having held power, by their
own admission, for five out of one, 40 years of independence, it will
be inconceivable for the North to want to have yet another go. But
since nature abhors a vacuum, unless the East is up and doing, nobody
will be surprised to see a desperate North staking out again for new
alliances or coalitions.
As a matter of urgency, the consultations should begin in the various
localities to prepare the minds of the people to this new enterprise.
Just as the council of Unity and Understanding was able to recreate
the political East, then reached out to the political West and Middle
Belt to set the stage for an Abiola victory at the polls. So also
would this new proactive East, cashing in on the favourable political
tide of the present, push for, grab and exercise political power for
the good of all Nigerians.

Original_Search_Title: Bash Ali's Phoney Cruiserweight Title
Headline: Nigeria: Bash Ali's Phoney Cruiserweight Title
Body_Text:
Except for the discredited transition programme manipulated by self
acclaimed evil genius, General Ibrahim Badamosi Babangida, no other
programme has suffered postponement or cancellation than the
so-called world boxing title bout packaged by loquacious and burly
boxer, Bash Ali.
Like General Babangida, Bash Ali had the yam and the knife in his
boxing match against Terry Ray. In the publicity hype that preceded
the fight, the Edo-born boxer was the piper and was doing the paying
too.
He visited most media houses announcing that he wanted to do what no
other person on earth has done, winning a world title at an age (56)
even his late mother could not defend.
It got to the a point that the Journalists got fed up with his
assurances on the fight while the average man on the street waved off
Bash Ali with the left hand each time his name or the purported fight
was mentioned.
But last Saturday, the much postponed fight came to pass and Bash Ali
is shouting to high heavens that he has permanently shut his critics
up with not only staging the fight but becoming the "oldest' man ever
to win a world boxing title.
Surprisingly however, the media world seem to have been hoodwinked
into believing him and joined in the celebration of a victory that I
believe has no other name but a "fluke."
Even his ex-school mate, Tayo Balogun who knows all the truth about
Bash Ali is keeping mute, allowing Nigerians to be deceived or making
the international media to take Nigeria as an unserious country.
Kudos must be given to Emeka Odikpo, a former chairman of the Lagos
State chapter of the Sports Writers Association of Nigeria (SWAN) who
asked some pertinent question during his sports programme on Radio
Nigeria 2 in Lagos. If those questions could be answered by Bash Ali,
then his so-called victory could be seen in another light.
Odikpo wanted to know why a boxing contest of that magnitude was
nowhere in the Internet, not mentioned in any radio or television
network around the world except by Lagos-based newspaper, radios and
televisions. Even at that, the mention was made after unnecessary
pressure from the boxer himself who has friends all over the place.
Anyway such trash can never find its way into the pages of Sports
Vanguard.
Whether Bash Ali answers Odikpo's questions or not, authenticity can
never be given to the so-called fight in which the name of the
President, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo was smuggled in as the chairman of
the occasion. What an insult. Name dropping, you would say, all in
attempt to give the fight some credibility.
From the beginning of the boxer's hype to last Saturday, real bout,
the questions that bothered me were, why was Bash Ali the arranger,
the organiser as well as the promoter of the fight. When boxer's like
Mike Tyson, Frank Bruno or even our Heavy Akinwande had a fight, they
were not involved in the organising or promotion of their own fight
talkless of organising their own publicity.
Another dimension in the so-called cruiserweight fight was the
concern of Bash Ali in the fight that brings Terry Ray here, the
hotel he will stay and even the type of food he should eat. To worsen
the matter, the loudmouth turned master of ceremony of any occasion
he arranged for the fight.
But whether the fight is fraudulent or not, Bash Ali has beaten a
Terry Ray that is more flesh than muscle for a boxer.
It was obvious the former champion was not in shape or has been out
of the punching business for a very long time.
For those who believe in Bash Ali, he is now a world boxing
cruiserweight champion and the country can have some quiet.
The only area I would quarrel with Bash Ali is in the attempt to
sneak into the Guinness Book of Records as the oldest boxer to win a
world title. It is not honourable to inflate your age to claim a
credit that you don't deserve.
Though I am not the boxer's father to know his true age, but those
who are close to him have said Bash Ali cannot be 56 or 57 years old
that he is claiming. One such friend is Tayo Balogun whom I learnt
was the boxer's senior by one year in secondary school. Mr. Balogun
has said that he is senior to Bash and not yet 50, so how can the
boxer claim he is 57.
Bash Ali's elder sister was reported to have once said that the
boxer's claim could throw her into some kind of problem in getting a
suitor because men who hear that her younger brother is already above
50 years would do some calculations with their legs.
World records do not come by wishful thinking. You don't work towards
it, it happens while one is struggling to achieve one thing or the
other.
A good example is the story of Senator David Dafinone who qualified
as a chattered accountant and thought his children should follow his
footsteps. Today his family has made a name for itself and the
Guinness Book of Records has recognised it. Would anyone say the
former Senator planned towards this? It just happened as the family
worked along.
Bash Ali should contend himself with the so-called World title he has
won. Of course these are so many world title belts in the United
