States that could rival the ones we have here at the great 'Oluwole'
in Lagos.
If you look closely at the Jankara market also in Lagos, you can get
such belts there because I'm told there is nothing you can't get
there.
But Mr. Balogun who has a popular Sunday phone-in programme on Star
FM radio should save some die-hard believers of Bash Ali's claim that
he is the oldest boxer to have won a world title, the trouble of
spreading false information to the public especially the growing
kids, by telling Nigerians the whole truth on Bash Ali's age.

Original_Search_Title: What Is Retirement (2)?
Headline: Nigeria: What Is Retirement (2)?
Body_Text:
These groups had special names which distinguished them one from
other. The groups on the right and left sides of the pathway
constituted the flanges of the spear", and all three converged
simultaneously on the enemy village.
The ages of the warriors determined which group took the one or the
other of the three routes, just as it was the age-range that
determined who was to be in the fighting squad. Retirement from the
squad took place as each set in the group attained an average age of
about fifty years. But in the case of the regular occupations such as
farming, hunting, black-smiting, basket-making, weaving, pottery and
others, the retirement age was indefinite and varied markedly with
the circumstances of the individual, including age, health condition
and personal dedication to the occupation. Most people, on account of
their personal circumstances, we were told, did not retire from their
chosen vocations until they became too old or too fragile to continue
working. It was not uncommon to find that there was no narrow
specialisation; that is, a farmer could at the same time be a basket
weaver or a hunter. Only in a few cases was the occupation absolutely
exclusive.
Another instance of retirement from active public function in our
traditional setting or ancestral life was in the case of village
guards - a sort of "vigilante" group.
In this connection, youths of about thirty or more years - all
indigenous of the village and by virtue of native custom or tradition
- were initiated and entrusted with the responsibility and duty of
guarding the village. These youths kept vigil over the village night
and day, interrogated strange visitors, detained suspects and brought
them before the village head and elders for further interrogation and
trial where applicable and, in general, ensured the safety of life
and property in the village during their tenure. In cases where there
was evidence of a sinister motive of a strange visitor, jungle
justice was administered on the visitor even before a report reached
the village head and elders.
For purposes of performing their duties, members of the vigilante
group assembled daily in a hut, which was built for the purpose in
the centre of the village. Where there was no hut, the assemblage
took place in an open space in a shade under a tree which was
strategically located. Here they kept watch and carried out their
other functions under the leadership of the oldest person in the
group. Change of the group or set which corresponded to "retirement:
of the group from their civic duties occurred every three years. That
is, after three years in office", a new set took over, after the
usual initiation. These new initiates then became recognised, as
"coming of age:" that is, they now became recognised as adult members
of the village community. While the vigilante group performed the
duties associated with their "office," the rest of the village
community went about their normal day-to-day business.
Compulsory active participation in the age-related vigilante group
and other similar cultural functions such as communal work, like
clearing the bush path that led to the farming area, building of
market stalls and so on, from which individuals became exempted at a
certain age, could be likened to retirement from community service.
The procedures and formalities were quite varied in different tribes
or cultural settings.
The practice of organised age-related vigilante groups is still in
vogue in some villages in Edo State up till the present-day, although
the custom has been eroded to varying degrees in most parts by
education, modernity and taking over of security matters generally by
government. For example, the establishment of police posts in various
localities has greatly reduced the need for our forefathers' type of
village vigilante groups.
There were no retirement benefits as such for the retirees of the
village vigilante or other groups. Members of the groups with the
exception of the physically handicapped were still virile and capable
of carrying on any individual occupation of their choice. But in
other areas of the social fabric, the elders (particularly those who
had retired from their vocations) were given special concessions in
certain matters. For example, they were exempted from communal work;
when someone in the family slaughtered an animal, particularly
portions of the animal such as one of the legs (including the thigh)
must be given to the oldest man in the family; similarly, in the case
of farm harvest, some of the harvested products especially yams must
be given to the oldest man in the family. This was a form of social
welfarism - a social obligation as well as a form of recognition that
the old man who was no longer capable of active farming (the retired
farmer) required some social support. These and other traditional
ways of recognising the elderly in the society more or less
corresponded to "a retirement benefit" albeit unofficial whereby
there was a realisation of the need to cater for the elderly. Thus
the aged (corresponding to the retiree) subsisted on these charities
and the support of their biological children and relations.
Retirement from public service nowadays is governed by statute just
as it was by tradition in the days of our forefathers. But whereas in
the case of our forefathers, the tradition was intristic to each
tribe or ethnic group, policies concerning retirement from public
service apply countrywide. Nevertheless, in some areas, some of the
customs mentioned above still persist such as age-group initiation
and age-group duty allocation. In the modern or current public
service (which is mostly government service), service in government
parastatals and private organisations, retirement takes place usually
at the age of fifty-five, he or she is expected to submit an
application which goes to the Establishment Division of the Office of
The Head of Service of the federation through the Head of Department
of the officer concerned (in the case of a federal officer) or to the
Director - General of his State Ministry of establishment (in the
case of Civil Service Commission. The Establishment checks the claim
of the officer by reference to his Record of Service. If this is
authentic and certified by the Head of Department, the establishment
signifies its approval, notifies the Civil Service Commission and
then proceeds to calculate the officer's retirement entitlements
using the laid down formula prescribed in the conditions of service.
The retirement benefits are usually the pension and gratuity; the
parameters used for the necessary calculations being the officer's
age, length of service, officer who properly retires is entitled to
receiving 70 percent of his terminal salary monthly for life and a
once-for-all gratuity which is a lump sum grant of 3-months terminal
salary of the officer. Details of these and of their mode of
computation are available from the Ministry of Establishment,
appropriate gazettes and or relevant Federal Establishment Circulars.
To qualify for a gratuity and pension, an officer on a pensionable
appointment in the public service must have completed a minimum of
fifteen years of service and be at least forty years of age as at the
date of retirement. Any officer on a pensionable appointment who has
served for less than fifteen years and less than forty-five years of
age on retirement has to wait until he is forty-five years of age
before he becomes eligible to claim any retirement benefits due to
him in compliance with the appropriate Section of the Pension Act
(Section 4(2). Any officer who serves for less than 10 years has no
entitlement; similarly, an officer who is on temporary, daily-paid
non-pensionable appointment.
In the private sector, individual organisations have their respective
pension schemes or superannuation; the latter being a scheme whereby
some fraction of the monthly or annual income is set aside by an
employer for his worker against his retirement. Since these schemes
vary with the size, nature, well-being and several other factors
concerning organisations in general, we cannot go into their detailed
descriptions here, particularly, as our thrust is, how to maximise
our well-being on retirement. It is noteworthy that the money that
accrues to the worker over a number of years, like the gratuity and
pension of a public servant, constitutes a substantial amount with
which the worker begins a new life on retirement. Other ways in which
some private organisations assist their employees towards "retirement
at its best" include training schemes for children of their
employees, housing schemes whereby employees could take loans to
build own houses and having for their workers insurance schemes of
various kinds. It is important that before joining a firm or an
organisation, a prospective employee finds out about these and other
administrative policies of the organisation. The advantages of such a
step are obvious.
Whereas the criteria for retirement from service in private
organisations are flexible, those in the case of public (or
Government) ministries are rigid. For example, age had hitherto been
used as a primary criterion for retirement. Nowadays, however, more
emphasis is being place on length of service. Thus, if an individual
has gone 30 or more years and he is below the stipulated age of 55 or
60, he must retire, whereas in earlier days, he would be allowed to
continued working until he clocks 55 or 60. Consequently, in recent
years many relatively young individuals have been retired or are
being retired. Although, the idea is to minimise cost or make room
for the masses of unemployed youths in the society, the policy on the
longrun could be counter productive as knowledge and experience which
most of the young recruits have not got are some of the crucial
factors for an efficient progressive and productive service.
Experience has to be acquired; it cannot be bought and it has no
substitute. Both groups of individuals (the new recruits and the
working group) in the society as well as others should therefore be
catered for or accommodated in the service if satisfactory progress
is to be made.
The argument that the young retiree is still within the economic
system and should therefore be able to continue to make his
contribution seems unrealistic, although this has been the argument
proffered by many administrators. It seems therefore that an
alternative to retirement of the young seasoned workers is necessary.
This alternative approach should be multipronged and should include
the following: deliberate job creation, diversification of
ministries' functions to enable the ministries accommodate more
personnel, creating more ministries; taking deliberate and positive
steps to strengthen the economy, diversification and widening of the
economic base through improved application of the existing
technological knowledge and "more."
It should be emphasised that sight must not be lost of the vitality,
experience and knowledge of a young officer who has served 25 or more
years vis-a-vis those of a young recruit and that in view of their
importance in society every effort should be made to create in the
service room for both the raw recruit and the young knowledgeable
officer who after 30 years or so of service is marked for retirement
simply because he has done 30 years.
Quite often, some individuals in the public service and also some in
the private sector do not wish, for one reason or the other, to
proceed on retirement even though they are statutorily qualified to
do so. This may be considered quite natural and is due to several
factors most of, which are personal to the individuals, concerned.
Chief of these factors is the unpreparedness which had earlier been
mentioned. Furthermore, it will be recalled that when a human being
becomes used to a pattern of life or a routine, he or she finds it
difficult to effect a change in that pattern. Generally speaking,
there is usually a resistance to change of any sort. Hence one of the
causes of the difficulty experienced by a public servant to change to
retirement life. In addition there is also fear of the unknown. Apart
from his awareness that he will cease to go to work and hence no
income as a retiree, a potential retiree is uncertain of the full
implication of not going to work as usual. Consequently most workers
do not look forward to retirement; some, however, do. These latter
are those who have surveyed the terrain and fixed their eyes on
something more interesting and more profitable to do on retirement.
One other reason why some folks do not look forward to retiring from
service or their regular occupation is a feeling of insecurity. This
is in connection with where to get the wherewithal to maintain the
standard of living to which they have become accustomed, and
wherewithal to meet the financial obligations to their individual
nuclear and extended families and others. In some cases, prospective
retirees who are not yet too old to work have no knowledge of any
other job than the one they were doing prior to their retirement.
Consequently, they find it difficult to get something else to do when
they retire.
In anticipation of these and other problems, some of which are
imaginary, many potential retirees fear to go on retirement.
Consequently, some of them try to dodge or avoid it even when they
know that it is due, or that they are statutorily overdue for it.
This they do by falsification of their age. Further apparent reasons
for fear to go on retirement include the gross inadequacy of the
retirement benefits to sustain the accustomed life-style, a feeling
of insufficiency, a lack of self-fulfillment and, in the case of a
worker who feels he has not attained his ambition in the service, a
feeling of non-self-actualisation. These reasons and more, either
singly or collectively, result in some officers' dis-inclination to
go on retirement.
Probably as a result of this and for purposes of proper
administration to monitor staff situation, the Establishment Division
of the Office of the head of Service of The Federation (in the case
of federal officers) and the Ministry of Establishment (in the case
of officers in the State Service) send out periodic circular letters
as mentioned above to elicit the necessary information concerning an
officer's career and his/her progression in the Service.
Retirement Before Due Date
An officer could be retired before his due date. This happens as a
result of (I) a punitive measure arising from gross misconduct, (ii)
pruning or rationalisation of the Service, (iii) redundancy, and (iv)
invalidation. The first is consequent on disciplinary action and does
not attract any retirement benefits; the second and third take place
according to the normal Civil Service rules and regulations and are
applicable only in circumstances of over-establishment; while the
fourth takes place when an officer gets involved in an accident or a
protracted illness certified medically by a fully qualified medical
doctor, acting on behalf of a properly constituted Medical Board.
Cases of individuals coming under categories (ii) to (iv) are treated
according to their own merits in consideration of whether or not they
should benefit from the provisions of the Retirement Rules and
Regulations.
Retirement in all cases including that arising from attainment of the
statutory age is compulsory. Officers who feel dissatisfied or
depressed that they have to go on the compulsory retirement should
try to remember that they cannot remain in the service forever, they
should not forget that younger generations of individuals are coming
behind them, and that fresh blood, vitality and other attributes are
in constant demand in the Service. In an advanced state of ageing
these qualities in most cases invariably decline resulting in decline
in efficiency and productivity.
Potential retirees (i.e. those nearing the retirement age) should
also note that "go home" is not a curse. They should note that
retirement, which is bound to occur sooner or later, offers
interesting and incredible challenges, which though sometimes
appearing formidable and insurmountable, can prove interestingly
pleasurable, if well prepared for, well planned and properly managed.
Officers with tendencies towards depression on the eve of their
retirement or in the course of their retirement should pick up
courage and face the situation squarely, courageously and with a
strong determination to enjoy their retirement. They should remember
that where there is a will, there is a way. Success does not lie only
in the public service, it can also be achieved in various other
aspects of life, including retirement.
Retirement from Spheres of Activity Other Than The Public Service Or
working Life
Retirement takes place not only in the public or private service or
in working conditions but also in other spheres of human activity.
Take for instance, sex life. This has been known, in most cases to
decline with advancing age, although it does not completely peter
out. In normal circumstances, unilateral retirement without proper
prior discussion with or understanding of the sex partner has been
known to cause serious disharmony, breakdown of the relationship and
other disastrous consequences; especially in a marriage situation
which include child-bearing. Unilateral decision of one of the
partners to go into a state of permanent sexual abstinence without
expert medical advice or opinion especially during child-bearing age
or during a period of life when both partners are expected to be
sexually active, could be inimical to a marriage relationship. For,
it could reduce the marriage to a hollow shell of its real self, as
the decision may not be acceptable to the other marriage partner.
Thus the unilateral decision in this regard could undermine the
interpersonal relationship between the married couple as well as that
within the family unit. The latter (i.e. the family unit) is
universally recognised as the basic institutional unit of a nation.
And, characteristics like harmony, love, loyalty, discipline and
others in the family units are believed to be usually reflected in
the life of a nation.
In business it is also important for a business partner to notify
others concerned of his wish to retire, if he has the intention of
doing so. For a sudden breakaway by a member of the association or
partnership could in one way or the other, spell doom to the interest
of the other or others in the association or the business as a whole.
A sudden approach therefore would not be fair.
Other examples of spheres of activity where retirement takes place
include sports and politics. In both cases, the decision to retire is
entirely personal and does not necessarily require prior consultation
with a partner or an associate in the business unless the prospective
retiree so desires. Instances could be multiplied "ad infinitum".
Cases should, however, be considered on their own merit.
The important thing to note is that in contemplating voluntary
retirement, where other people would be affected one way or the
other, the prospective retiree should take such persons into
confidence. These persons may include or could be the prospective
retiree's spouse, his children (if these are mature enough to
appreciate what is going to be at stake), his business partners or
associates, his friend and well-wishers. This is particularly
necessary in the case of a family if the prospective retiree is the
breadwinner. This does not necessarily mean seeking prior approval of
the persons who will be affected by the decision to retire, but
simply to make them aware of and be prepared in their own ways for
the eventual new situation which will arise if and when the
contemplated retirement takes place. Also for one thing, as most
retirees know, proper approach to decision-making requires that those
who will be affected by a decision, except in secret or highly
confidential official policy matters, should be allowed to
participate in the decision-making process. This is crucial for
purposes of its implementation if and when the decision is taken.
For, the success of implementation of a decision depends largely on
participation of all the parties concerned.
Retirement In The Case of Special Vocations
'Special vocations' here in our context may be defined as those
specialised jobs that require special knowledge, special training and
skill for their effective performance. They are therefore beyond the
competence or capability of the ordinary average individual and hence
not jobs for all and sundry. They include Priesthood (orthodox and
traditional), Medicine (also orthodox and traditional), Engineering,
Law, Technical jobs, teaching and others.
People in these vocational calling hardly ever go on full retirement
from their jobs. For owing to their highly specialized knowledge and
professional skill, they are always in demand. Thus even after
leaving the public service on attainment of their statutory
retirement age, a great deal of them continue to serve in their
private establishments or Companies, as Consultants, managing
Directors or other posts in their vocational callings; some are
employed on part-time (or even full-time) basis by large private
organisations. Some who are advanced in age and too old to work
employ young professionals in their field to work for them or to
carry on with the administration of their firms. In this way, the
heavy burden of the day-to-day running of the business is taken off
their shoulders.
We may describe the professionals in this category as "semi-retired."
With further advancement in age, the ability to perform effectively
declines. Even if the will, knowledge and skill are there, the
physical disability with consequent reduction in energy output
precludes effective performance. Thus there is in many cases, a
general decline in productivity alongside age. At this stage the
business is handed over to a competent junior colleague who is
trusted. The elderly professional, now fully retired, diverts his or
her attention to activities, which will further enhance his enjoyment
of his well-earned retirement in a relaxed mood. Activities at this
stage include intensification of hobbies, like writing, reading for
pleasure, playing and listening to music walking as an exercise,
helping to look after siblings and grandchildren, participation in
community activities and many others.
To be continued
Co-ordinated by Dr. Dele Sobowale

Original_Search_Title: Cadbury Commended Over Retention of Nigeria Management
Headline: Nigeria: Cadbury Commended Over Retention of Nigeria Management
Body_Text:
Lagos — The president of Institute of Directors (IOD) Nigeria, Mr.
Emmanuel Ijewere, has commended Cadbury Nigeria Plc for maintaining
its 100 per cent Nigeria management team inspite of 40 per cent
ownership of the company by Cadbury Schweppes (its foreign partners).
Mr. Ijewere was speaking when a delegation of the governing council
of the institute paid a courtesy visit to Cadbury recently, in
continuation of its reach-out programme to blue-chip companies.
Mr. Ijewere lauded the calibre of human resources in the company and
stressed that this showed the amount of confidence the international
business community had and still has in the competence of the
directors. He said unlike other organisations which are either
organisation based, industry-based, the IOD is human based because it
is involved in preparing those at the top and others on the way up,
for the art of corporate governance which can only be performed by
human beings.
Meanwhile, the Managing Director of Cadbury Nigeria Plc, Mr. Bunmi
Oni, has said that import substitution and a preference for local raw
materials are Cadbury's strategies for long term growth and survival.
Mr. Oni was addressing a delegation of the institute of directors,
IOD, Nigeria led by its President, Mr. Emmanuel Ijewere, which paid a
courtesy visit to Cadbury Nigeria Plc recently.
He said policy makers in Cadbury had foreseen, many decades ago, that
manufacturing companies would soon run into trouble if they did not
get local materials to substitute imported ones. Since then, he said,
local substitutes had been sources for each possible product.
Long before import substitution and local processing of raw materials
became a fad," Mr. Oni said: "Our company had put money into
developing new products using local substitutes. It has also
continued to search out ways to reduce cost through the use of local
raw materials." According to him, many of Cadbury's products as they
presently are, including the 30 year old Tom Tom; Tomapep and Poundo
Yam, are the fruit of that deliberate policy.
Another long term strategy which has benefited the company is an
early export orientation. Mr. Oni explained that much effort had gone
into producing goods which would be useful to other companies and
those which would be sold in other countries of the world.
To demonstrate belief in the creation of a 21st Century team, the
company, he revealed, had spent about 23 million naira in the last
two years on training and development of staff. About 10 million
dollars had also been spent in the last decade to upgrade processes
and plants.
On hand to receive the IOD delegation along with Mr. Oni were Mr.
Biodun Jaji, sales and marketing director, Dr. Teju Bogunjoko,
operations director, Mr. Dayo Olawaiye, human resources director, Mr.
Ayo Akadiri, finance director, Mr. Segen Oyewale, managing director
of Stanmark Coca Processing Limited and Mr. Gbenga Adebija, corporate
affairs manager.
Present at the Cadbury visit along with Mr. Ijewere were the second
vice president, Ms. Benedikter C. Molokwu; Mr. Femi Ekundayo,
treasurer of the institute; Dr. Ebosele Okoduwa; Mr. Muyiwa Odejayi,
Mr. Yemi Dada, Mr. Olushola Obaddimu, director-general of the
institute and Mr. Akin Iroko, senior manager.

Original_Search_Title: Going Down Under
Headline: Nigeria: Going Down Under
Body_Text:
Lagos — The problem with being on the color pages of TEMPO is that I
have to submit each article one clear week ahead of its publication
date so that it can meet the production. That usually is not a
problem when I am not commenting on a burning, juicy national issue
or when I am on my way to Australia!
Yeah baby! Yours truly is writing this on Thursday the 7th and by the
time you read it I shall Insha Allah be in the beautiful habour city
called Sydney. Yes right, envy me if you must, I do love the
attention.
Now, the down side is this one week gap between me writing and you
reading, yep, I will e-mail the stories but it will lack the
immediacy that sending the story two days before publication would
have.
So what do you think? Should I swallow my vanity and go back to my
good old black and white page? Well then you might as well cut off my
right hand and feed it to the dogs. I have come to like my "sisi"
picture on this page and the glamorous look of the color page so
going back to that mug shot and the guerilla journalist-like black
and white pages may just be asking a tad too much for a freshly vain
me.
So color pages and delayed articles it shall be then. Incidentally
the primary purpose of this trip to the bottom of the world is
ostensibly to go and experience and report the Olympic games which I
shall do on my sport column in the weekend edition of PM News but the
real reason baby is to have fun, period! I am going to have so much
fun I will be sick of it. I really do wish you could go with me but
hey? There is no plane big enough (oh the arrogance of Funmi) for all
the jisters so I ask you to come along on this page as I experience
for us all and write about this trip down under. I knew I had to be
at this Olympic games. First, it is the only major sporting
tournament in the world and the only one I had never been to. Then,
it is of course the first in this millennium (na you know if you are
waiting for your own millennium to start next year, free will and all
you know) and it is to be held in Australia! Australia had always
sounded to me like Siberia, one of those places you just do not take
off and go to. Now, mind you, I inherited my father's Ajala spirit I
intend to go around the entirety of the earth, all countries at least
once before the good Lord calls me. I have oft said it that if He
calls me before then, I shall vex and return as an airplane, at least
then I shall get to fulfill my dream. So, I knew I must see the games
in Sydney for if I miss this chance, how am I sure that I shall get
another opportunity to go there. We after all are not talking about
Europe or the Americas, which are a reasonable distance away, this is
another world entirely. I am told that I shall fly for two days and
almost twenty-two hours through Brussels and Zurich, which is the
route I have chosen.
I have no idea what Australia is like except what I have gathered
from the internet but of course any seasoned wakabout will tell you
that all those brochure pieces are never exactly as it seems also the
places that interest me most are hardly the cosmetic tourist
attractions of any city. For one, I intend to find a local Nigerian
food joint as soon as I can. What do you mean I can't? Do you not
realize that Nigerians can be found in such unbelievable places as
Afghanistan and unpronounceable, thus unspellable little cities in
impoverished Russian enclaves? Why would there not be an appreciable
Nigerian population in a big cosmopolitan albeit dead far city like
Sydney. I know there must be a joint were Nigerians hang out and were
on can get a decent plate of Ogbono, Edikanikong, Ewedu with pounded
yam. At the least, there will be a pepper soup joint, trust me on
that. However if against all expectations, there are no such places
in Sydney, I can bet my last Zuma (sorry, no name rock) that some
smart Nigerian business woman would open one whilst the game lasts.
That was my experience during the world cup in France two years ago.
Even with all the gastronomical delights the city of Paris had to
offer, we the chosen people of God from Nigeria began to hunger for
some real food, a few swallows here and there and a plate of soup
with some soul.
On frantic enquiries, we were told that there was in fact one place
in town where we could get "Nijer" food served without any
pretensions. I cannot remember anymore what part of town it was we
went, all I recall is that we took the tube and came out into this
nice prosperous looking neighborhood and walked a few blocks to a
little alley which turned out to be the living room of an apartment.
The apartment was crawling with carpenters, mason and such who were
making such a din as they knocked the place into shape.
At the end of the alley was a little backyard like place with chairs
and tables set in typical Nigerian food joint manner. There was
makossa music playing in the background above the cacophony of voices
speaking in various Nigerian languages as the aroma of food wafted
through the air. I knew I was at home. I recognized quite a few of
the other customers as officials and journalists from Nigeria who had
also come for the World Cup. How come they discovered this place so
quickly? I wondered. Then I found out that the woman was just setting
up the place. She came with the Nigerian contingent with a mobile
kitchen and rented the apartment just for the duration of the
championship! She realized there would be a great demand for Nigerian
food and decided to open a joint in Paris whilst the world cup was
on. I asked if the investment was a worthwhile one and was assure
that she would make enough money to cover her cost, make some profit
and get a holiday and shopping in Paris to booth. Her food was very
pricey but we were too glad to have it to care.
I never found out if she did come back home after the world cup. If
she did I wonder if she would be in Sydney to give us her pricey, not
very great but glad to have it anyway brand of home cooking.
So, it is that I leave you in peace for a working holiday for the
next three weeks. I will be free of the Lagos traffic and drivers. I
will be free of NITEL, NEP, the word nascent and the sight of burnt
bodies on the road. I will not be afraid to park or go out in the
evening for fear of armed robbers. I shall have a ball. However, I
shall miss the madness, the drama from Abuja and my TV show, which
has run me, ragged around the edges. I shall miss Nigeria for you
know what? Even in all of the madness, insecurity and want, I find
comfort and an identity. The most dreadful thing about being outside
Nigeria is that people look at you but they do not see you.
You are completely unimportant, just one of the crowd, a mostly
unwelcome part of the crowd, it is an unsettling eerie feeling.
I discovered how truly essentially Nigerian I am when once in Harare,
as we drove around town I wondered unconsciously but loudly why there
were no street hawker so I can buy a snack to munch on in the car.
The biggest "Nijer dey for blood" joke occurred in Chicago when one
morning I woke up and began to iron my clothes. I had ironed half of
the clothes in my box when my girlfriend asked in alarm if I was
going away, why was I ironing so many clothes at the same time. It
was then it dawned on me that NEPA (sweeter than NEP) was not about
to take the light so I had no cause to iron clothes in "advance"! May
God keep you too.

Original_Search_Title: The Menace of Surface Mining
Headline: Ghana: The Menace of Surface Mining
Body_Text:
Accra — If there is one problem about which much has been said and
written, but about which very little has been done, it is the havoc
being caused by surface mining to Ghanaians.
Even, at the risk of sounding hyperbolic, one could say that not a
single day passes without some town or village or whole community
complaining about the destruction of their farmlands, or the
pollution of their water courses, or the failure of some mining
company to pay them compensation.
From Kwabeng to Osino, in the Eastern Region, to Obuasi, Dunkwa,
Prestea and Tarkwa, there have been incessant protestations by these
communities over the menace being caused to them by surface mining.
In fact, a couple of years ago, the chiefs of one of the towns in the
Western Region lost their cool, donned their mourning clothes and
took to the streets to demonstrate against one such mining firm.
Recent newspaper reports reaching the Ghanaian Chronicle indicate
that tension is mounting between the residents of Kenyasi No.1 and 2
in the Asutifi District of the Brong Ahafo Region and some surface
mining companies.
According to the Public Agenda, the complaints of the people center
as usual around issues like resettlement, relocation, payment of
compensation, and destruction of farmlands and open fields.
According to the people, farm crops are being destroyed by the two
mining companies - Rank Gold and Normandy Goldfields Ltd.
The paper reports that out of about fifty affected farmers
interviewed, only three had sat in any meeting to discuss the
problems that have arisen out of the forcible takeover of their
farms.
The usual features of the problem that is becoming a nationwide
phenomenon are present in the Kenyasi scenario; some simple rural
folks are told their lands have been taken over by some foreign
mining companies, they are then resettled somewhere and paid some
pittance as their compensation for all that had happened to them. In
all these transactions, the affected people are never consulted.
One of the unfortunate by-products of the IMF-sponsored Structural
Adjustment Programmes, in developing countries, has been the wanton
exploitation of their natural resources to, as it has often been
argued, maximise exports and increase foreign exchange earnings.
This over-exploitation of natural resources, especially in the timber
and mining sectors, goes on without due regard to the destruction
being caused to the environment and its adverse effects on the
livelihood of the people in such areas.
The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) in its annual report for
1998 came out with some guidelines to be followed by such mining
concerns.
These include the submission of an Environmental Impact Assessment
(EIA) report prior to the commencement of operations, the involvement
of the affected persons and communities in negotiations about all
aspects of the seizure of the lands and the payment of compensation.
Unfortunately, it appears these guidelines are not being adhered to
by majority of the mining companies operating in the country now,
especially those engaged in surface mining operations. The result is
that these mining companies pollute the sources of drinking water of
those communities within which they operate, destroy their farmlands
and sometimes even relocate them without giving such aggrieved
persons the opportunity to express their views on such issues.
What is more, these affected and aggrieved persons are not allowed to
participate directly [or even indirectly] in determining how much
compensation should be paid to them.
The Chronicle strongly believes that if the EPA guidelines were
strictly adhered to by the mining companies, most of the troubles
that have arisen between some of them and the communities in which
they operate would not arise.
We are also of the view that the basis for assessing the quantum of
compensation to be paid to each farmer is defective. For instance,
certain crops like plantain, cassava, cocoyams and yams, which are
erroneously classified as food crops, do not attract any compensation
when they are destroyed by the mining firms, even though it is clear
today that, for most people in Ghana today, these crops constitute
their major sources of income.
The EPA report referred to above, also raised the issue of some
people like, herbalists, who might not own any parcels of land and
would, therefore, not be paid any compensation, even though they
would lose their sources of livelihood if the farmlands of the
landowners were destroyed during mining operations.
The operations of these surface mining companies are causing grave
havoc to some residents in this country of ours and unless and until
appropriate measures are put in place to ensure that they receive
justice, we shall always experience tensions and confrontations in
the communities in which these miners operate.

Original_Search_Title: News In Brief
Headline: Africa: News In Brief
Body_Text:
ABIDJAN - Calm returned to Abidjan, Cote d'Ivoire's business capital
Tuesday, after the tension that followed the shootings early Monday near the
residence of military ruler, Gen. Robert Guei, by armed dissidents who
killed two of his body guards.  There were long queues Tuesday morning as
nervous motorists filled their tanks from the petrol stations, which had
been closed throughout Monday because of the shooting incident.  Shops,
supermarkets, and especially banks were bustling with activity once again,
after remaining closed during the entire day Monday.  An exciting atmosphere
prevailed in the Plateau area, Abidjan's business centre, giving the false
impression that this was the first day after the weekend.
ADDIS ABABA - The UN Security Council has said that demining the
Ethiopia-Eritrea border areas would be an urgent task for the 4,200- member
UN Mission in Ethiopia and Eritrea or UNMEE besides monitoring the
cease-fire between the two countries, which fought a two-year war that ended
in June.  The UN peacekeepers are expected to be in place by November to
ensure peace prevails within the "Temporary Security Zone," 25 km inside
Eritrea along the entire 1,000 km-long common border.  Authorising the
deployment of the mission Monday, the Security Council mandated the force to
carry out a range of verification tasks, including monitoring the cease-fire
between the two countries.
ZORZOR - More than 13,000 internally displaced persons in embattled
northern Liberia are facing severe food shortages as fighting between
dissidents and government troops continue.  The majority of the displaced
are surviving on wild yams from the forest, leaves prepared with palm oil
and cassava from farms of host villages that they have to labour to obtain.
Rice, the Liberian staple, is almost non-existent in many areas, including
Zorzor, a provincial headquarters, 210 km from Monrovia, and the major
buffer for people en route to or from the war zone.
CAPE TOWN - South Africa's military force is riddled with racism,
according to an interim report presented Monday by Defence Minister Mosiuoa
Lekota.  The interim report, commissioned following an attack by a black
soldier which left eight white people - seven soldiers and a civilian woman
- dead at the Tempe military base near Bloemfontein one year ago, will be
followed by a final report and recommendations by the end of the year.  The
inquiry found that racism in the defence force manifests itself in a number
of forms, including harassment against black members, indifference by white
officers, abusive language, discrimination, poor accommodation, lack of
promotion of black members and the assault and intimidation of black members
to ensure that they do not report the cases to the police.
CAPE TOWN - Africa stole the limelight at New York Fashion Week Monday
when a collection of five African designers brought the more than 600 guests
to their feet in a spontaneous show of elation.  They were Bongiwe Walaza
(South Africa), Deola Sagoe (Nigeria), Bonga Bengu (South Africa), Tracey
Lee (South Africa) and Julian Smith (South Africa).  In a turnout usually
reserved for the world's established designers, more than 100 of the most
important international and US news and fashion media witnessed Africa's New
York fashion debut.  Among them were New York Times, Elle, Harper's Bazaar,
Vogue, ABC, CBS, NBC, Fox and CNN.  In a coup for the show, the most
important voice in international fashion, Andre Leon Tally, the editor of
American Vogue, chose Sagoe as the winner of Africa Designs.

Original_Search_Title: Thousands of Displaced People Face Starvation
Headline: Liberia: Thousands of Displaced People Face Starvation
Body_Text:
Zorzor, Liberia — More than 13,000 internally
displaced persons in embattled northern Liberia are facing
severe food shortages as fighting between dissidents and
government troops continue.
The majority of the displaced are surviving on wild
yams from the forest, leaves prepared with palm oil and
cassava from farms of host villages that they have to labour
to obtain.
Rice, the Liberian staple, is almost non-existent in
many areas, including Zorzor, a provincial headquarters, 210
km from Monrovia, and the major buffer for people en route to
or from the war zone.
The influx of displaced persons into nearly 14 towns
and villages in Zorzor and Salayea districts has impacted on
food security and availability, triggering a triple hike in
the cost of food and other items in the area.
The severe food scarcity has compelled adults and
children to venture into war zones to fetch food on farms
abandoned by fleeing villagers.
"We have to weed farms, pound rice, cut wood or brush
farms to even find little food for our wives and children to
eat daily," T. Jomah Kamara, a displaced teacher, told PANA.
But others are going to bed without food on many days,
while a sizeable number of civilians are still in the forest
living on wild fruits and fearing to come to town, Varlee
Yarmah, one of the displaced, said.
Physician assistant Augustine Daniels of the Konia
health centre said the wild yams the displaced are surviving
on were a source of diarrhoea and other health related
problems.
He identified diseases plaguing the displaced persons
as malaria, acute respiratory infection, diarrhoea, scabies,
worms and wounds sustained while foraging the bush for food.
Shelter for the displaced constitutes one of their
major problems as the bulk of them are sleeping in open-air
and crammed huts, schools and market buildings.
The influx of displaced into the towns and villages
have resulted into an acute shortage of safe drinking water,
as there are insufficient hand pumps while the absence of
public latrines for the growing population is degenerating
into a sanitation disaster.
Unlike the past, the displaced are not fleeing into
neighbouring Guinea which has closed its border with Liberia,
and because the Liberian dissidents are reportedly attacking
from that country.
Meanwhile, about half a dozen international NGOs have
completed a three-day assessment mission to northern Lofa
County to assess the state of the internally displaced
persons and the security situation in the area.
The visit was the first in over two months since
fighting between dissidents and government troops broke out
in July in the provincial town of Voinjama, 260 km from
Monrovia.

Original_Search_Title: Tension At Itu Bridge Head, AKS
Headline: Nigeria: Tension At Itu Bridge Head, AKS
Body_Text:
Socio-economic activities came to an abrupt halt at Mbak Itam, the
secretariat of Itu Local Government Council in Akwa Ibom, Tuesday
September 5. It was not a public holiday. But work was stopped as
news filtered through Mbak Itam that the whereabouts of the
Vice-Chairman, Mr. Bassey Edet Ekanem was not known. The number two
citizen of the local government council had disappeared in less than
tidy circumstance, a development which heightened fear and tension in
the area.
His story of sorrow began Saturday September 2, when he was abducted
and taken to an unknown place. A resident of Atai told Sunday
Vanguard, the council officer was accosted by a group of men who
stormed a shopping centre where he was doing his purchases at about 8
p.m. that fateful day. Described as a very huge man in his mid 40s,
he was said to have been subdued mysteriously.
Perhaps most heart-rending is that despite his massive frame, he was
dumped in the boot of his Peugeot 505 official car and it sped out of
sight. His war thirsty abductors numbering about 18 stormed the place
on motorbikes and immediately sealed off the area. Everybody watching
the scene was left dazed. Before Ekanem could realise what was going
on he had been intimidated.
Nobody could raise a voice and nobody braved the situation. In a
split second, their mission was accomplished and the car with the
convoy of motorcycles zoomed off in a maddening speed. That was the
last time Ekanem was seen. The same applied to his official car with
registration number LG 02 TTU.
Although his abductors were still at large as at press time,
suspicion was high that the incident was not unconnected with the
long-standing Itu bridge boundary dispute with their Cross River
State neighbours. As at last Thursday, palpable tension ruled the
area. Residents of Mbiabo in Itu had since the incident fled for fear
of their lives. The indigenes who are mainly fishermen had abandoned
their occupation to seek refuge in adjoining villages for fear of
further attacks.
When recently, trouble sparked off, the villagers were sacked, a
market by the foot of the bridge known as Volvo was razed. At normal
time, it was in this market that their women sold fish caught by the
men. Indeed, they eked out living therein. It is a different story
today. Those spoken to told Sunday Vanguard they were in deep misery.
They said they were like a fish cast out of the sea.
Without shelter over their heads, the women who have turned to cocoa
yam farmers put up in ramshackle houses. Truly, they were in despair.
But they were also in dilemma as their attackers had not come into
full picture. There were claims, Sunday Vanguard learnt, the
attackers are from Ikot Offiong side of the contending forces. A
counter claim had it that the attackers came from Akwa Ibom. They are
confused. The kidnapped vice-chairman hails from Ikot Offiong.
A fisherman in Ayadeghe community, Effiong Okon Asuquo narrated his
agony thus: "When fresh crisis erupted on Wednesday my engine, canoe
and fishing net were seized by unknown persons who threatened us with
dangerous weapons."
Another fisherman, Edet Okon Edet, told Sunday Vanguard, they
experience hostility every passing day. "We have abandoned our means
of livelihood because of the enemies who would not announce their
arrival", he said.
Both men were worried that violence might erupt soon in the area
notwithstanding the presence of the police and armoured personnel
carriers positioned at the Itu toll plaza.

Original_Search_Title: What Is Retirement?
Headline: Nigeria: What Is Retirement?
Body_Text:
INTRODUCTION
Retirement is something to which most people look forward late in
their working life either in pleasant anticipation or with a great
deal of trepidation. For those who have taken the trouble to plan it,
retirement can be a wonderful experience, for those who fail to
anticipate and plan retirement can be a most painful experience. The
sudden realisation that one is no longer useful to society takes its
toll on body and soul. People living in unplanned retirement age die
early and there is a growing body of research to support the
conclusion that they fall sick more often and die earlier.
Mr. M. O. Imana from Owan West Local Government of Edo State, an
accomplished educationist and past tutor at Kings College, Lagos in
the book RETIREMENT AT ITS BEST to be serialised over the next few
weeks explains how to achieve a fulfilling retirement. Read on.
RETIREMENT means different things to different people. Typically and
like most English words its meaning depends on the context in which
the word is used. Thus, among its common dictionary meanings are "to
withdraw," "to retreat," "to go to bed", "to go to rest" and a number
of others. Take for example the word "complete holiday". To some this
means rest", that is, "complete or total abstention from work, either
physical or mental, for a period of time." To others it means
"temporary break from normal routine"; that is, doing something of a
different nature from the normal routine during the period designated
"holiday"; or "deviation" so to say, "from normal routine activity",
such as in a school situation wherein pupils are compelled to stay
away from attendance of normal classes during certain periods of the
calendar year, or in an office where an officer in compliance with
statutory regulations legitimately stays away from work and is said
to be on "holiday" or "leave." In this case a holiday may be
considered synonymous with leave. For when an officer is "on leave,"
he is expected to take a break from going to work. Although he or she
during the period of leave is entitled to his or her salary,
allowances where applicable and other benefits attached to his level
in his occupation. Both holidays and leave are expected to be periods
of rest. But they could be converted into period of rest combined
with pleasurable activities other than normal routine work. These
activities include games, sports, reading and others which are of
recreational nature as well as work which is different from the one
which leads to the "leave". Most workers also use their leave periods
to catch or deal with domestic matters which they could not see to
during the working months.
In view of the above, it becomes necessary for us to define our frame
of reference. Thus the context in which we wish to treat the subject
"retirement" here is one which concerns "giving up a regular job or
regular activity with consequent cessation of the enjoyment of the
fringe benefits or other benefits associated therewith"....
Although the phenomenon of retirement, especially as far the public
service is concerned, has gained much prominence and publicity here
in Nigeria in recent years as a result of pruning of the Civil
Service due to the on-going adverse economic conditions of the
country, it is by no means something new. For instance, some of us
were told by our parents and grandparents before they passed on to
the great beyond that, prior to the advent of the white man and his
white collar job, people, as a result of their ages and other
debilitating conditions retired from their regular occupations such
as farming, hunting, black-smithing and others. There were in the
community in their time, certain age groups whose duty it was, among
others, to fight and defend the village against any external
aggressor. In this regard, the duty, which was mostly part-time, was
performed by able-bodied male youths. The older members of the
community who were generally referred to as "the Elders" sat home
while men of the ages between thirty-five and fifty either singly or
in groups, depending on the magnitude of the conflict, were sent to
fight the enemy or to attack the village against which there was a
grouse. The elders' homestay was not to passively sit and await the
return of the warriors or the outcome of battle but to carry out in
the process and on behalf of all concerned, certain traditional
rituals including sacrifices to their "gods of war" and other
activities which were all aimed at protecting the youths that had
gone out to fight and to ensure their victory just as in the present
day when during a war, religious leaders and other groups hold
special services and prayers to propitiate God, Allah and or their
other gods.
Quite often in a formal attack on a village, the invaders went in
three groups according to age; each group functioning under a leader.
One group took the main pathway leaving directly to the village which
was under siege and constituted the spearhead attackers, another
through the bush on the right-hand side of the pathway and the third
the bush on the left.
These groups had special names, which distinguished them one from
other. The groups on the right and left sides of the pathway
constituted the flanges of the "spear", and all three converged
simultaneously on the enemy village.
The ages of the warriors determined which group took the one or the
other of the three routes, just as it was the age-range that
determined who was to be in the fighting squad. Retirement from the
squad took place as each set in the group attained an average age of
about fifty years. But in the case of the regular occupations such as
farming, hunting, black-smithing, basket-making, weaving, pottery and
others, the retirement age was indefinite and varied markedly with
the circumstances of the individual, including age, health condition
and personal dedication to the occupation. Most people, on account of
their personal circumstances, we were told, did not retire from their
chosen vocations until they became too old or too fragile to continue
working. It was not uncommon to find that there was no narrow
specialisation; that is, a farmer could at the same time be a basket
weaver or a hunter. Only in a few cases was the occupation absolutely
exclusive.
Another instance of retirement from active public function in our
traditional setting or ancestral life was in the case of village
guards - a sort of "vigilante" group.
In this connection, youths of about thirty or more years - all
indigenous of the village and by virtue of native custom or tradition
- were initiated and entrusted with the responsibility and duty of
guarding the village. These youths kept vigil over the village night
and day, interrogated strange visitors, detained suspects and brought
them before the village head and elders for further interrogation and
trial where applicable and , in general, ensured the safety of life
and property in the village during their tenure. In cases where there
was evidence of a sinister motive of a strange visitor, jungle
justice was administered on the visitor even before a report reached
the village head and elders.
For purposes of performing their duties, members of the vigilante
group assembled daily in a hut, which was built for the purpose in
the centre of the village. Where there was no hut the assemblage took
place in an open space in a shade under a tree, which was
strategically located. Here they kept watch and carried out their
other functions under the leadership of the oldest person in the
group. Change of the group or set, which corresponded to "retirement:
of the group from their civic duties occurred every three years. That
is, after three years in off", a new set took over, after the usual
initiation. These new initiates then became recognised as "coming of
age:" that is, they now became recognised as adult members of the
village community. While the vigilante group performed the duties
associated with their "office," the rest of the village community
went about their normal day-to-day business.
Compulsory active participation in the age-related vigilante group
and other similar cultural functions such as communal work, like
clearing the bush path that led to the farming area, building of
market stalls and so on, from which individuals became exemption at a
certain ages, could be likened to retirement from the community
service. The procedures and formalities were quite varied in
different tribes or cultural settings.
The practice of organised age-related vigilante groups is still in
vogue in some villages in Edo State up till the present day, although
the custom has been eroded to varying degrees in most parts by
education, modernity and taking over of security matters generally by
government. For example, the establishment of police posts in various
localities has greatly reduced the need for our forefathers' type of
village vigilante groups.
There were no retirement benefits as such for the retirees of the
village vigilante or other groups. Members of the groups with the
exception of the physical handicapped were still virile and capable
of carrying on any individual occupation of their choice. But in the
other areas of the social fabric, the elders (particularly those who
had retired from their vocations) were given special concessions in
certain matters. For example, they were exempted from communal work;
when someone in the family slaughtered an animal, particularly
portions of the animal such as one of the legs (including the thigh)
must be given to the oldest man in the family; similarly in the case
of farm harvest, some of the harvested products especially yams must
be given to the oldest man in the family. This was a form of social
welfarism - a social obligation as well as a form of recognition that
the old man who was no longer capable of active farming (the retired
farmer) required some social support. These and other traditional
ways of recognising the elderly in the society more or less
corresponded to "a retirement benefits" albeit unofficial whereby
there was a realisation of he need to cater for the elderly. Thus the
aged (corresponding to the retiree) subsisted on these charities and
the support of their biological children and relations.
Retirement from public service nowadays is governed by statute just
as it was by tradition in the days of our forefathers. But whereas in
the case of our forefathers, the tradition was intristic to each
tribe or ethnic group, policies concerning retirement from public
service apply countrywide. Nevertheless, in some areas some of the
customs mentioned above still persist such as age-group initiation
and age-group duty allocation. In the modern or current public
service (which is mostly government service), service in government
parastatals and private organisations, retirement takes place usually
at the age of fifty-five, he or she is expected to submit an
application goes to the Establishment Division of the Office of The
Head of Service of The Federation through the Head of Department of
the officer concerned (in the case of a federal officer) or to the
Director - General of his State Ministry of Establishment ( in the
case of Civil Service Commission. The Establishment checks the claim
of the officer by reference to his Record of Service. If this is
authentic and certified by the Head of Department, the Establishment
signifies its approval, notifies the Civil Service Commission and
then proceeds to calculate the officer's retirement entitlements
using the laid down formula prescribed in the conditions of service.
The retirement benefits are usually the pension and gratuity; the
parameters used from the necessary calculations being the officer's
age, length of service, officer who properly retires is entitled to
receiving 70% of his terminal salary monthly for life and a
once-for-all gratuity which is a lump sum grant of 3-months terminal
salary of the officer. Details of these and of their mode of
computation are available from the Ministry of Establishment,
appropriate gazettes and or relevant Federal Establishment Circulars.
To qualify for a gratuity and pension, and officer on a pensionable
appointment in the public service must completed a minimum of fifteen
years of service and be at least forty years of age as at the date of
retirement. Any officer on a pensionable appointment who has served
for less than fifteen years and less than forty-five years of age on
retirement has to wait until he is forty-five years of age before he
becomes eligible to claim any retirement benefits due to him in
compliance with the appropriate Section of the Pension Act (Section
4(2). Any officer who serves for less than 10 years has no
entitlement; similarly, an officer who is on temporary, daily-paid
non-pensionable appointment.
In he private sector, individual organisations have their respective
pension schemes or superannuation; the latter being a scheme whereby
some fraction of the monthly or annual income is set aside by an
employer for his worker against his retirement. Since these schemes
vary with the size, nature, well being and several other factors
concerning organisations in general, we cannot to into their detailed
descriptions here particularly as our thrust is, how to maximise our
well being on retirement. It is noteworthy that the money that
accrues to the worker over a number of years, like the gratuity and
pension of a public servant, constitutes a substantial amount with
which the worker begins a new life on retirement. Other ways in which
some private organisations assist their employees towards "retirement
at its best" include training schemes for children of their
employees, housing schemes whereby employees could take loans to
build own houses and having for their workers insurance schemes of
various kinds. It is important that before joining a firm or an
organisation a prospective employee finds out about these and other
administrative policies of the organisation. The advantages of such a
step are obvious.
Whereas the criteria for retirement from service in private
organisations are flexible, those in the case of public (or
Government) ministries are rigid. For example, age had hitherto been
used as a primary criterion for retirement. Nowadays, however, more
emphasis is being place on length of service. Thus if an individual
has gone 30 or more years and he is below the stipulated age of 55 or
60 he must retire whereas in earlier days, he would be allowed to
continued working until he clocks 55 or 60. Consequently, in recent
years many relatively young individuals have been retired or are
being retired. Although the idea is to minimise cost or make room for
the masses of unemployed youths in the society, the policy on the
long run could be counter productive s as knowledge and experience
which most of the young recruits have not got are some of the crucial
factors for an efficient progressive and productive service.

Original_Search_Title: Generalising the 13 Per Cent Derivation
Headline: Nigeria: Generalising the 13 Per Cent Derivation
Body_Text:
Lagos — Entertaining representations from some Solid Minerals
Producing Communities recently, the Federal Government has been
reported as saying that the 13 per cent derivation payable to oil
mineral-producing states will equally be due to every state producing
natural resources.
This is a very serious policy statement because it touches on one of
the most contentious issues in our land at the moment, and that is
the question of revenue allocation.
Thus, there is need for what constitutes natural resources for the
sake of the application of the 13 per cent derivation formula to be
explicitly defined. Nigeria is a country endowed with immense natural
resources. These natural resources are countless, so much so that if
their exploitation were to have viable potentials, this country would
not have continued in her difficult economic circumstances today.
Thus, the generalisation of the application of the 13 per cent
derivation would naturally compel the assessment of the economic
viability of the said natural resources. Natural resources, for this
purpose, would then not be defined in themselves, but in their
potentials as sources of foreign exchange earnings for the country.
This clarification is important because as natural resources go,
yams, cassava, groundnut, maize, cocoa, among others, are all
inclusive. But for purposes of revenue earnings for this country,
they are clearly out of reckoning. The quantity in which these
categories of natural resources that do not earn revenue for the
country is produced is also immaterial here. What should be of
paramount importance to their inclusion or otherwise in the
application of the 13 per cent derivation formula is their relevance
to the country's foreign exchange earnings.
Another matter that is thrown up by this Federal Government's talk
about generalising the 13 per cent derivation is that of
environmental hazards. A natural resource may be significant to the
country's foreign exchange earnings, but the environmental
unfriendliness that its exploitation poses to the inhabitants of the
environment in which it is located may be comparatively and even
relatively less than others. The level of the environmental hazard
that attends the exploration of natural resources must, therefore, be
factored in the determination of its place in the 13 per cent
derivation.
We are not strangers to the fact that one of the nightmares of the
oil- producing communities in this country is the environmental
degradation that oil exploration has caused their areas. Farmlands
have been destroyed, and rivers have been polluted, including many
sources of drinking water. This environmental problem has been the
main source of confrontation between the oil- producing communities
and the government. It has equally been the chief source of the oil
communities' persistent agitation against the 13 per cent derivation,
which they have derided as being paltry. It is, therefore, important
that the government does not loose sight of this subdued rejection of
the 13 per cent by the oil communities on the ground of not being
enough compensation for environmental hazards they suffer in its
inclusion of the production of natural resources that are relevant to
Nigeria's foreign exchange earnings, but which do not carry
environment hazards comparable to that of oil production. To
generalise the 13 per cent derivation without being sensitive to the
aforementioned issues will create more problems than it can solve.
The Federal Government must be conscious of this danger and guard
against it.

Original_Search_Title: New Anti Crime Outfit Underway in Delta
Headline: Nigeria: New Anti Crime Outfit Underway in Delta
Body_Text:
Asaba, Delta — A new anti-crime outfit coded "operation storm" is to
be launched soon in the Delta State. Vanguard gathered that the new
outfit will replace the existing one known as "Operation Wipe,"
launched by the former police commissioner in the state, Mr. Morris
Evanson, a few years ago.
Addressing top officers of the state police command at the end of a
two-week training programme in Asaba, the police commissioner, Mr.
John Ahmadu, said the training was meant to prepare the officers and
men for the execution of the new police offensive code-named
"Operation Storm."
He explained that officers and men that would execute the new
offensive needed to be groomed to prepare them for great challenges
ahead. He advised them to live above board to be able to restore the
confidence of the public on the police. Said he: "You have to be
polite to the people on the road. Greet them good morning sir, good
afternoon sir; where are you going, can you identify yourself, please
do you have an ID card for me to identify. If he brings it and after
checking whatever, you say "thank you sir, have it back sir, bye,
sir". And I can assure you that if people see you behaving like this,
you would not be surprised if somebody brings you one motor load of
yams.
Close sources hinted that the setting up of the new offensive was at
the instance of the governor, James Ibori, who had on several
occassions expressed concern over the rising crime wave in the state.
Already, no fewer than 25 pick-up vans have been procured by the
state government in readiness for the take-off of the new outfit in
the state.

Original_Search_Title: Arguments of "The Rice Import Liberalization Agenda in Liberia"
Headline: Liberia: Arguments of "The Rice Import Liberalization Agenda in Liberia"
Body_Text:
Atlanta — The need for economic research and analysis as prerequisite
for policy decisions is an elementary and obvious fact, which we
should waste no time to debate. And yet, some people have
superfluously stressed this need for policy research, as if others
were not aware of it.
Every Liberian economic policy, not only agricultural policy,
requires research for its successful formulation and implementation.
This is a given, and we do not have time to waste on debating whether
or not Liberia should carry out agricultural policy research. Project
feasibility studies and cost/benefit analyses are routine procedures
that have to be conducted before instituting any economic policies
and programs. No serious Liberian government will embark upon an
economic policy or project without the appropriate research to
determine its implications for resource allocations and economic and
social returns. Unlike our critic, Harry Greaves, I have never heard
any serious Liberian economist advocating rice self-sufficiency as a
policy option without the need for research. And I, for one, have
never in any of my writings, especially those carried in this
magazine, proposed this policy option without the need for
appropriate research. So, why is he still insinuating that I am
calling for rice self-sufficiency without research, when in fact he
has been adequately informed that this is not my position?
Why is it that instead of debating the main issues raised in my
original submission, "The Rice Import Liberalization Agenda: Some
Critical Policy Issues", our critic continues to avoid them, but
quibble over elementary and obvious matters? And although such
diversionary attitude and sophistry was brought to his attention in a
previous rebuttal, "Blinded by Free Trade and Comparative Advantage
Dogma", he has neither acknowledged nor amended it in his latest
rejoinder, "The Continuing Debate about Self-Sufficiency in Rice
Production for Liberia". Not only did he refuse to address the
critical policy questions raised in the original piece, he was also
unable to identify where in that article I expressed "emotion-laden
cant, hype and theology about the need for Liberia to be
self-sufficient in rice", and where I advocated "policy prescriptions
purely on the basis of emotion", as he claimed in his earlier
reaction. Instead, he continues to perpetuate the distortion,
insinuation, and falsehood that I am advocating for rice
self-sufficiency without research.
Let it be known that I have nothing against our critic for being "an
advocate of free trade", as he professes. What I have problem with is
the apparent severity of his ideological zealousness. It is still
believed that his reaction to my original article stemmed more from
his free trade ideological convictions than from any genuine desire
to discuss the important policy issues raised in that article. In his
first reaction, "What's the Case for Self-Sufficiency in Rice
Production?", he was clearly suggesting that the policy implications
of import liberalization discussed in the original article should not
have been raised in the first place because Liberia does not need
rice self- sufficiency since, according to him, the country can have
its rice consumption needs met through importation. Such is the kind
of ideological arrogance that I detest. Because of one's belief in
free trade he does not want others to have the freedom to criticize
it! In essence, he is precluding all other policy options, except
those in line with his free trade ideology. Thus, he was the one
passing policy judgement without research. If this was not the case,
why then has he persistently evaded the central arguments of the
original article? Why has he reduced the whole debate to the trivial
issue of how Liberia needs agricultural policy research? This is not
an issue to debate; it is an obvious and elementary requirement of
any economic policy making. My original article did not advocate rice
self-sufficiency without the need for research. Again, can our critic
show me where?
The main objective of the original article (and let me emphasize) was
to expose some of the policy contradictions and problems inherent in
the Government of Liberia's longstanding flirtation with rice self-
sufficiency and the present government's new policy of rice import
liberalization. Upon coming to power, Taylor had decried Liberians
for being too "lazy" to produce their own rice and had threatened
them that they would pay higher prices for imported rice, if they did
not become self-sufficient. Few months later, this same government
started a rice import liberalization program aimed at removing all
necessary barriers to importation of rice to Liberia. Not only was
such a shift self- contradictory, but the new policy of rice import
liberalization also contains serious negative implications for
macroeconomic stability and the government's own dream of rice
self-sufficiency. My article was designed to expose some of these
policy implications. Let me put these questions to our critic: Isn't
it true that a broader policy of import liberalization, involving
rice, petroleum, and possibly other sectors of the economy (as
envisaged by the government), could have a destabilizing effect on
the government's own objective of fiscal restraint, in terms of
revenue loss? In spite of its commitment to balanced budgets, the
government has been running deficits since it came to power (about
0.5 percent of GDP for 1999). Broad-based import liberalization could
lead to the loss of government revenue and, if this happens, then
balancing the budget would be effected further from the expenditure
side, possibly exerting greater pressure on the already thin public
and social sectors of the economy. Isn't it true that cheaper
importation and influx of foreign rice and other commodities on to
the Liberian market could have a negative impact on Liberia's balance
of payment, and since the country now faces a binding external
financing constraint (huge external debt and so forth), government
would most likely resort to a fast devaluation of the Liberian dollar
in order to diminish such influx? Wouldn't this devaluation of the
dollar, in turn, possibly lead to increases in rice prices and
inflation in general? Finally, isn't it possible that if import
liberalization involves more economic sectors, then the overall level
of import restriction in the economy will substantially fall, and as
the economy grows and export earnings (foreign exchange) increase,
government could be tempted to allow more importation of foreign rice
and other commodities? And if such inflow of rice were so strong that
it penetrated rural households, wouldn't this have a detrimental
effect on rural subsistence farming?
Those were the policy issues that the original article was about. Are
these issues not worth discussing, just because international trade
is "free" and Liberia is assured of getting its rice supply from
foreign countries? Thank Allah, our critic has now agreed with me
that Liberia cannot rely solely on world markets to deliver all of
its rice consumption needs. In his latest rejoinder, he wrote: "So,
what are the alternatives between the extremes of 100 percent
importation and 100 percent self-sufficiency in domestic production?
The reality is that there are many people in rural Liberia who are
engaged in subsistence farming, including rice production. Clearly
they need help and some resources should be directed at helping them.
But devoting some resources to assisting subsistence farmers to
expand rice production is a far cry from deploying very significant
resources to attain 100 percent self-sufficiency in rice production."
First, this statement is one of the generalized insinuations by our
critic that I detest. Have I ever written anywhere that Liberia
should strive for 100 percent self- sufficiency in rice production?
Second, by this statement, our critic has finally agreed with me that
Liberia cannot depend solely on the importation of rice, but the
country can also assist its rural farmers to contribute to rice
production. But if he agrees to this point, why then has he accused
me of "emotion-laden cant" just for discussing the implications of
rice import liberalization for rural subsistence farmers? Okay, let
us suppose, for the sake of argument, that the country has agreed to
import 80 percent of its rice consumption, and the remaining 20
percent from rural subsistence farmers. Shouldn't we still be
concerned about the possible negative implications of an economic
policy that promotes unrestricted importation of foreign rice? The
only time the country may afford to ignore such policy implications
is perhaps when it imports all of its rice consumption needs from
abroad. So, you see, our critic cannot have it both ways. He cannot
be saying that rural farmers should be encouraged to contribute to
domestic rice production and then crucify those who are concerned
with the negative implications of a policy that may endanger such
rural economic contribution. This is self-contradiction at its
maximum!
With respect to Harry's continuous renditions about comparative
advantage and free trade, I have already provided an in-depth
analysis of the international operation of these theories in my last
rebuttal, "Blinded by Free Trade and Comparative Advantage Dogma".
Most types of comparative advantage are deliberately constructed by
nations. They are not handed down to countries on silver platters. In
fact, a careful examination of the history of global economic
relations indicates that the dominant powers of the world economy had
deliberately influenced the development of present-day patterns of
international trade. It is about time that we stop viewing
comparative advantage in static terms. Even if Liberia does not have
comparative advantage now in rice production, does it mean that she
can never have such an advantage in the future? Did we have
comparative advantage in rubber plantation before Firestone? The
acquisition of comparative advantage is a dynamic and developmental
process. Hence, to believe that Liberia should abandon rice
