production once a cost/benefit analysis rules that it has no
comparative advantage is not only shortsighted but also self-
defeatist.
In previous analysis, it was made sufficiently clear that I am not
opposed to international trade. It is the extreme inequalities
underlying international trade and capital movement that annoy me. Is
it economically fair, or can we even call it free trade, when some
countries are pressured into opening their economies wide to the
consumer products of countries which restrict similar flows to their
economies? Can we say that there is a leveled playing field in
international trade when some countries are spending billions of
dollars in subsidies to restrict entry to their markets while
enforcing their entry into the markets of others? Our critic appears
to be less concerned about such inequalities. In his latest
submission, he writes: "There are many great and noble economic
practices in the West (to use a generalization) which we in Liberia
would do well to emulate. Broad- based price supports for special
interests, such as is accorded textiles and sugar in the United
States, butter and wine in some European countries, and, yes, rice in
Japan, are not amongst the practices which I think we would be wise
to emulate. The reasons these industries are protected in the West
often have more to do with politics than sound macro-economic policy
making." This conclusion that agricultural industries in the West are
protected more for political than macroeconomic reasons is highly
refutable. But let us agree for the benefit of our critic that this
is true. Isn't it still unfair to ask Liberia not to emulate
provision of price support to its industries when countries with
which she trades are providing such support to theirs? When some
countries are allowed to provide price support to their industries
and others are prevented from doing so, can we say that the patterns
of trade established among those countries are truly based on
comparative advantage and free trade? I do not believe so.
Finally, in the context of the Liberian economy, as explained in my
last rebuttal, there is a strong link between food self-sufficiency
and food security. The exact rates of self-sufficiency that Liberia
could strive for, say 35 percent, 50 percent, or 75 Percent, or
whatever, are to be determined from economic studies and research.
Nevertheless, in order for the country to be secure of its rice and
other major food supplies, there are very compelling economic reasons
why she should strive for such food security through raising its
overall self- sufficiency rates in the production of not only rice
but also other major food commodities, such as cassava, sweet
potatoes, and yams. Paramount among these reasons is the fact that
Liberia's current sources of export earnings and foreign capital
inflows are not reliable, stable, and everlasting. Our critic's
statement that "...if Liberia did not produce one grain of rice...,
the country's food security would still not be in jeopardy" can only
be true if these sources of foreign exchange were dependable, stable
and sufficient. In order for the country to resign itself to
importing most of its major food supplies, it must not only have a
lasting and sufficient source of other resources to export to world
markets, but such resources must also be valued at adequate and
stable world prices. Do we have a lasting source of such nonrenewable
mineral resources as iron ore? Are we managing our tropical forests
the right way so that they provide a continuous source of exports?
Can we continuously depend upon rubber export to pay for our food?
From the existing knowledge and past experience, these export sources
are not only exhaustible, but they have been insufficient and
unreliable. Even if the answer to each of the above questions were
'yes', does Liberia have any control over how these traditional
exports are valued on world markets? There is a great deal of
volatility in these markets, and a small country, like ours, has
virtually no influence on how its exports should be priced on world
markets. Moreover, Liberia's unsustainable external debt burdens
(e.g., a debt service-to-export ratio of 70.8 percent) now render
most foreign capital flow to the country uncertain.
It is in light of the above and other economic reasons that Liberia
cannot solely rely on importation of its major foods. We do not have
industrial products to sell to world markets, and the export base we
have is very narrow. From such a narrow base we have been unable to
derive all of the financial resources needed to pay for our food
imports and other foreign inputs required for economic development.
And partly because of this, we are now fatally indebted to foreign
countries. Now, should we still continue to rely on this dwindling
base which may soon collapse under us, or should we strive to do
something different and possibly improve our chance of survival? If
we agree that the external sector of our economy is deficient, then
doesn't it make sense that we should supplement it with a strong
domestic sector capable of seriously contributing to the production
of our major foods? Finally, to our friends who are concerned,
rightfully so, about comparative advantage and resource allocation,
which of these two activities is Liberia, with all its fertile and
adequately watered land and no flooding, capable of achieving with
the least technical, financial, institutional, and international
political barriers: Establishing and owning a car manufacturing plant
in Monrovia, or providing a rice-processing mill to rural farmers in
Tappita? A food for thought.
Geepu Nah Tiepoh is a development economist and consultant with ACLAD
Development, Canada.
For subscription information, go to: www.theperspective.org or send
e-mail to: editor@theperspective.org

Original_Search_Title: Attah's Deal to Revive Akwa Ibom's Industrial Base
Headline: Nigeria: Attah's Deal to Revive Akwa Ibom's Industrial Base
Body_Text:
Lagos — Akwa Ibom State has been one of the leading players in
Nigeria's economic power bloc. Apart from being the second highest
producer of crude oil and natural gas in the country, she is rich in
a variety of agrarian products, including palm oil, rubber, cocoa,
kola nut, coconut, cassava, yam, maize, rice, cowpeas, plantain,
banana, pineapple and gmelina. She also has an array of tropical
livestock in abundance.
Apparently, she has all the potentials that should make her one of
the richest states among the comity of states in the federation
considering that she is endowed with enormous reserve of solid
minerals such as limestone, clay, gold, salt, coal, silver nitrate
and glass sand.
Strategically located to reap the benefits from the Calabar and Ikot
Abasi Export Processing Zones as well as the Port Harcourt Oil and
Gas free zone, Akwa Ibom possesses all the unique advantages other
states in the federation eagerly yearn for.
Ironically, since her creation in 1987, Akwa Ibom is yet to attain
her full potentials. At inception, the state inherited ten ailing
industries-Sunshine Batteries, Essien Udim; Peacock Paints, Etinan;
Quality Ceramics Industries, Itu Steel Products Limited, Eket;
Asbestonit Industries, Oron, Plasto Crown Industries, Uyo;
International Biscuits Limited, Ikot Ekpene; Champion Breweries
Limited, Uyo; Pamil Industries, Abak and Sea State Seafood Limited,
Eket.
Spread across various parts of the state, these institutions held the
promise of a strong, virile industrial economic base for the people.
But today, most of these establishments have either closed shop or
constitute a conduit through which government scarce resources are
drained.
Efforts by successive administrations in the state to revive them and
restore the confidence that hitherto existed have remained largely a
pipe dream, as there has hardly been any noticeable change in them.
Though claims have always been made to a programme of resuscitating
infrastructure in these industries and bring Akwa Ibom back to work,
the establishment of Akwa Ibom Investment and Industrial Promotion
Council (AKIIPOC), by the Gorvernor Victor Attah administration, has
been one of the remarkable achievements of the democratic
dispensation in the state.
One of the headaches of that administration since inception has been
how to harness the strategic advantages of the state and secure
viable economic partners and core investors with the right muscle to
help in reviving these ailing industries.
As part of the preliminary efforts to achieve these objectives, a
core investor has not only been secured for the Champion Breweries,
Uyo, brewers of the international award- winning larger, an interim
board has been constituted to supervise the early resumption of
operations there.
AKIIPOC is also spearheading the mobilisation towards getting core
investors involved in the resuscitation of other ailing companies.
However, more significantly, Governor Attah believes the most
effective way to get things back to work again in the state is not
for government to be the propelling force of the process. Being
largely a person with a private-sector background, he believes the
best approach to the state's industrialisation is through a
co-ordinated privatisation programme.
Apparently, that is why he has relentlessly been pursuing the
campaign to attract foreign investors with the right financial and
technical expertise to come in and take over the running of these
industries as strategic partners with the state government.
Last year, the governor undertook an economic tour of the United
States where he held several meetings with a number of potential
investors and sold the states' rich economic potentials to them.
Though the trip was then criticised by some persons who thought the
problem could have been approached differently, the dividends the
trip has so far yielded has changed such perception and strengthened
the resolve of the state government to continue its pursuit as the
most practical way of getting out of the woods.
Since the trip, there has been a swirl of interests from potential
investors from the United States, Japan, Taiwan and China.
To consolidate the gains of the investment drive, early this month,
the state governor also led a high- powere delegation to Houston,
Texas on another economic tour of the United States. The objective
was primarily to brief Akwa Ibom citizens, resident in that country
on efforts by the government to bring her economy back to work as
well as reinforcing the confidence and interest of investors to agree
to come to do business in the state.
During the tour, the governor held consultations with such groups as
Global Energy, Simons and Company; Dynergy Services; Axial Group;
Evergreen Gas; Energy Exchange Incorporated, among several others.
The purpose, generally,was to intimate the investors about the agenda
of the state government to develop the oil and gas sectors of her
economy as well power generation and supply, telecommunications and
agricultural sectors.
Consequently, the governor spoke of the incorporation of the Akwa
Ibom Petroleum and Energy Limited (APEL), a state-funded holding
company to facilitate the active participation of the state and its
indigenes in the process to control and manage the country's oil and
gas sector.
To achieve this objective, APEL has already established three main
subsidiaries-APEL Exploration and Production Company Limited; APEL
Refining and Petrochemical Company Limited, and APEL Gas Company
Limited as well as APEL Power Limited .
APEL production and Exploration company which will focus on
investments in the upstream sector of the petroleum industry is
already bidding for one of the oil blocks located in the marginal
production fields in Akwa Ibom.
APEL Refining and Petrochemical Company was established to spearhead
the states' plans to set up an export-oriented refinery. The state
has already secured the Federal Government's nod to execute a 120,000
barrels per day topping plant in the state.
Though the plant is not expected to refine petrol, it will have the
capacity to produce diesel, kerosene and naptihla, a basic ingredient
for the production of aviation fuel.
On the other hand, APEL Gas Company which will focus on efforts to
develop infrastructure towards the effective utilisation of gas
resources has already started discussions with the Department of
Petroleum Resources (DPR) as well as other relevant agencies on the
possibility of establishing a $1.6 billion gas plant in the state.
The company which has already received the approval of the management
of Exxon/Mobil Corporation to work together on the gas plant project
as partners is exploring the possibility of opening negotiations with
Shell Petroleum Development Company (SPDC), for a gas sales and
purchase agreement (SPA) to ensure the supply of the commodity for
its operations.
Following the perennial problem of power supply in the state, APEL
power company limitedwas esterblished with the aim of harnessing the
huge gas resources in the state towards the construction of a
reliable power sector in the state.
Though the state has secured the approval of the federal government
for the construction of a 330 KVA power plant in the area, plans are
in advanced stage to establish her own Independent Power Plant (IPP).
One of the most potent draw backs to investments in the state has
been that of poor communication.
Consequently, the state has already applied and received approval for
the operation of a full-flegged telecommunication exchange system to
facilitate easy communication within and outside the state.
What is more? The moribund agricultural sector in the state is also
receiving attention from the state government, as a fertiliser plant
will soon be established to boost the effective operation of the
agricultural sector.This is in recognitiong of the fact that prior to
the advent of oil in the country, the state was one of the most
viable agricultural regions in the country. If she regains her lost
glory, the solution to her poor employment situation would have been
found, while her poor economic base will receive tremendous boost.
Though many express reservations about the state of intra-city
infrastructure like roads in the state capital, indications are that
if the current efforts to resuscitate the ailing industrial sector of
the states' economy are sustained, Governor Victor would have
succeeded in putting the state back to work and inscribing his names
in gold for posterity. That is when his desire to "re-engineer the
states' processes so that her public service can run with a private
sector mentality, delivering unparalleled values within the Nigerian
environment" will have much meaning. It is a deal that must work.

Original_Search_Title: Demand for Consumer Items Low, Supply High
Headline: Nigeria: Demand for Consumer Items Low, Supply High
Body_Text:
Lagos — Consumers' prices for edible items during the week
experienced some changes in most markets as a result of forces of
demand and supply. While supply for consumer items is high, demand
for them is low.
A breakdown of composite consumer price index shows that edible items
gulped up to 690.9 out of 1000 weight components. All items less farm
produce stand at 362.3. Drinks, tobacco and kola stand at 47.4,
clothing and footwear stand at 46.9, while accommodation, fuel and
light stand at 118.7, indicating that food stuff demand ranks
highest.
In most Lagos markets, prices of some staples went up during the week
while some maintained a level of stability. Consumer items that
experienced rise in prices during the week include, yam, fresh
tomatoes, crayfish, garri and kerosine.
Analysts attribute this rise to prevalent energy crisis that has
affected transportation of such items from the rural areas to the
cities.
A tuber of yam which formerly stood at an average cost of N120, N90,
and N60 depending on the size, now goes for between N200, N150 and
N100. Expectation is that with the bumper harvest of farmers in the
rural areas and the arrival of new yams, that prices of tubers of yam
will go down drastically.
A basket of fresh tomatoes which formerly sold for N700 now sells at
N1,200. A mudu of crayfish formerly sold at N300 now goes for N500,
while a tin of garri formerly sold at N280 now sells for N320.
Kerosene which is one of the most essential items in most homes has
skyrocketed from N90 for five litres to N140. This is basically
caused by the prevailing energy crisis that has affected diesel and
kerosene mainly.
Analysts ascribe this development to the survival instinct which is
very high particularly in a developing economy like ours where
greater percentage of consumers' disposable incomes are spent on
food, unlike developed economies where greater parts of consumers'
incomes go into things like recreation, entertainment, education and
cultural services. An analyst commented that "anything that affects
edible items affects inflation rate and that is why the inflation
rate has continued to glide downwards to a single digit in recent
months."
Recent report has it that the large supply of consumer items has to
do with the bumper harvest that farmers in the rural areas
experienced within the year, coupled with the fact that the Federal
Government early in the year released about $500 million for
importation of food items.
This however, has not helped situation as the imported consumer items
have pushed down the demand for local stuffs.
Edible oil, particularly, is mostly affected and has compelled
indigenous vegetable oil industries to record low sales, culminating
in high level of inventory. The situation is said to have impacted
heavily on the inflation rate of the economy, and inflation rate is
decreasing at an increasing rate, and it is no more news that the
inflation rate has fallen to a single digit of 1.1. Government should
revisit tariff on food items, and all the consumer items that could
be produced in Nigeria should be protected using optimal tariff
regime

Original_Search_Title: Ipele Demands Own Local Government in Owo
Headline: Nigeria: Ipele Demands Own Local Government in Owo
Body_Text:
Lagos — As the clamour for self-government, and enhanced development
at grassroots level grows louder across the country, a call has gone
out to Ondo State government to split Owo Local government into three
to ensure even development.
This call, contained in a memorandum submitted by the people of Ipele
and its surrounding villages, communities and settlements to Ondo
State Committee on Creation of more Local Governments, is to break
the present Owo Local government into Owo West, Owo Central and Ipele
(or Owo East) local governments with the headquarters at Ipele.
The memorandum, signed by 15 representatives of Ipele headed by Chief
Titus Olajide Olupitan as chairman, pointed out that the present Owo
local government is too large and unwieldy and its split would bring
government nearer the people and ensure justice and even development.
The memorandum traced the struggle of Ipele, the second largest town
in Owo local government, for its own council area to 1953 and pointed
out that proposals for the same purpose were submitted, in turns, to
the Federal Government in 1991, the Committee on creation of States
and Local Governments in Nigeria in 1995 and the Mbanefo panel in
1996 to no avail.
The proposed Ipele Local government, according to the memorandum,
meets such criteria for consideration as a viable population,
agrarian economy, large land mass as well as natural and human
resources that could be rapidly deployed for the take-off of the
council when created.
The proposed council area, says the memorandum said, has 12 wards, 36
towns and villages and, in terms of geographical contiguity, the
towns and villages, hamlets and farmsteads with a total population of
more than 75,000 spread out in different directions with Ipele town
at the centre.
The memorandum argued that, although Ipele accounts for about 50
percent of internally generated revenue in Owo Local government, the
total collection from the entire area had progressively fallen in
recent time due largely to popular resentment against age-old
official neglect and the inability of revenue officials to cover the
entire area.
As the gateway to Abuja, the nation's capital, Ipele is rapidly
developing into a major centre of social and commercial activities
due to the convergence of an efficient road network from Lagos, Oyo,
Ogun, Osun and Ondo States.
The proposed Ipele Local government, the memorandum also said, has an
extensive fertile land which favours the mass production of food and
cash crops such as cocoa, cashew, kola nut, cassava. The industries
on ground include, sawmill, aggregate quarry, yam flour,
block-making, weaving and dyeing, industrial starch and pot-making.
Ipele and environs, the memorandum continues, is host to 12 primary
schools, two secondary schools, a comprehensive heal centre, five
private hospitals, three petrol stations, a bank and a number of
cinema and other recreational facilities as well as 36 churches of
different denominations and the headquarters of a local Anglican
Archideaconry.
Politically, according to the memorandum, Ipele accounts for 50
percent of all the votes in any election in Owo local government.
Chief Olupitan, chairman of Ipele local government creation committee
said: "given to sheer size, population and potentiality of the
proposed local government area, the approval by Ondo State government
would mean a demonstration of its avowed commitment to justice and
fair play".

Original_Search_Title: Ghana Gets $4.8m From Yams
Headline: Ghana Gets $4.8m From Yams
Body_Text:
Kumasi — Ghana Recieved a total of 4.8 million dollars from the
export of yam, cocoyam and cassava production.
This figure represents 6.1% of the national agricultural export.
Mr. Samuel Nuamah Donkor, the Regional Minister, disclosed this at a
day's seminar on the improvement of root and tuber crops in Ghana.
The aim of the seminar organised by the Ministry of Agriculture in
conjunction with IFAD was to sensitize the knowledge base of farmers
on the production of root and tuber crops.
Mr. Nuamah Donkor noted in a speech read on his behalf that, even
though Ghana receives a lot of money from the exportation of root and
tuber crops, much has not been done to improve their quality.
The regional minister also disclosed that between 1992 and 1998,
cassava production ranged between 800,000 to 1.2 metric tonnes
depicting an average yield of 10 metric tonnes per hectare.
He could not discern why there was low productivity level for root
and tuber crops in the country in spite of its great importance.
"Good management practices would improve this situation", he advised.
He expressed the government's commitment to ensure adequate food
production in the country and hoped farmers would complement
government's efforts with the knowledge acquired at the seminar.
The national programme co-ordinator of the Root and Tuber Improvement
programme, Mr. Akwasi Agyei Adjekum, disclosed that root and tuber
production alone contributes 42% of the national gross domestic
product.

Original_Search_Title: I'm Fighting for the Youths of Niger-Delta - Rita Lori
Headline: Nigeria: I'm Fighting for the Youths of Niger-Delta - Rita Lori
Body_Text:
Lagos — Chief (Mrs.) Rita Lori is an Iteskiri respected opinion
leader. Recently she filed a suit at the Abuja High Court seeking an
injunction to restrain the governor of Delta State, James Ibori from
further spending of the 13% derivation fund accruing to the state
from the fund made available to the state by the federal government.
Not long after she got the court injunction that she alleged that her
life is being threatened. In this interview with our staff writer,
Mcphilips Nwachukwu, the community leader spoke on the threat to her
life, and the oil politics in Niger Delta. According to her, "my
commitment is to the youths of the Niger Delta..."
Q: Chief, we learnt that your life is being threatened since you got
the court order restraining Delta State government from further use
of the 13% oil derivation fund paid to the State. Can you tell us
exactly what happened?
A: You know I've been a strong advocate of the proper use of the
13%derivation fund due to Delta State. I've spoken on many occasions
that the money derivable from the 13% derivation should be used
essentially on the areas that have been badly exploited by the
explorations of oil. Despite the fact that I've spoken many times ; a
lot of people and groups have spoken to the governor of Delta State,
he still believes that the money should be used by the state; and of
course I went to court; and I got a court order retraining him; and
ever since I took that action and even before I took that action,
there have been threats to my life. Well, this day, it is very easy
in a hungry society where people have lost their conscience, it is
very easy to feed these hungry people with lies. And equip them to
assassinate others. Hence I'm making my report to my country for them
to know that my life is being threatened. I've got personal messages
and people come to me and tell me that I should leave this case or
that I am endangering my life. I've got phone calls dissuading me
from prosecuting the case...You know that there is a similar court
action here in Lagos by one Mr. Odili? Do you know that his people,
the traditional ruler and his chiefs stampeded to disassociate
themselves from him, he was driven virtually from the court, he was
virtually trampled upon morally so that he was no longer able to
pursue that court case. Well, they are attempting to do that to me,
but I can tell anybody that they are joking because my commitment is
to the youths of the Niger Delta that have been impoverished morally
and physically. They have no rooms over their heads . They have no
means of livelihood. Their water has been polluted. They have no hope
for the future. The swamp which they inhabit has been badly exploited
that theirs amounts to a hopeless case. So I'm committed to seeing
that whatever revenue that is due to them should be properly used.
What I'm saying is that there should be a blueprint of what has to be
done. The area has to be systematically developed. There must be a
blueprint. The way these governors are going about it is totally
wrong. For instance, you say that you are going to build a stadium so
that the people will see their money; but that is nothing. The
Iteskiri man or the Ijaw man or the Isoko man, when he is inside the
sea suffering does not know what is going on in the stadium... even
if he has the chance to go to the stadium, he is too hungry to kick
any ball. What has stadium got to do with his life? Whereas what
should first be done is to provide the basic necessities of life for
the people like food. It is true that these areas are very swampy but
the Ijaws for instance, plant such food crops like: plantain,
potatoes and sugar cane. They feed themselves. And they have a season
: a season for the flood and a season for the recess of the flood.
During the season for the recess of the flood they plant plantain,
they plant water yam; these are things that they plant with which
they feed themselves. So, God himself, has made it possible that
these people even in the environment in which they live can still
practice agriculture to sustain themselves. So the fish which is no
longer there because of the activities of the exploration companies I
thought, our leaders should look into and put strong effort at
breeding fishes and improving the technology of breeding good fishes;
and developing fish farms. All over the world today, that's what
people do, people no longer depend solely on wide fishes any more. So
this should be done for our people...I learnt that they have
registered a company to build a refinery. The federal government has
built refineries that are not working, at the end of it what are they
doing with the federal government ? They want to privatize it...for
me this arrangement is a gang-up where the wealth of the people and
place will fall into the hands of a few people. So what we are
complaining about the North in terms of marginalisation is going to
happen in Delta state and even in a worse scale; a situation where
the minority has gone to take hold of the wealth, oil that does not
come from their area. They are going to take the money and use it at
the detriment of the micro- minorities....
Q: Let us get one thing straight. Are you saying that the 13%
derivation should be used only in the development of the micro-
minorities as you said without regard to other constituting units of
the state?
A: I'm not saying that.
Q: What are you saying?
A: What I am saying is that emphasis should be laid on the areas that
have been badly exploited. Any area at all that has been badly
exploited should be developed. The formula of disbursement should be
based on quota system. It should take into consideration how much of
the peoples land that has been taken, the amount of oil that has been
taken from that area, the amount of money paid to each area should be
commensurate with the level of destruction that it suffers. However,
what I'm saying is, even if that has to be done it has to be done
constitutionally. That is why I went to court so that the court can
give it's order and we can go back to the National Assembly for
ratification of the process of sharing the 13% derivation so that
there won't be any blood shade, there won't be ant trouble, it is in
the constitution; so that when this money comes, it goes to these
areas for development; and the people of these areas should be fully
responsible for the development of their area. They should be
involved so that if they embezzle the money the youths know them,
after all they are not far from them and they can easily hold them
responsible for lack of development in their areas.
Q: Going back to the threat you alleged to your life, do you suspect
any person or group of persons as being responsible for this heinous
act?
A: Well, I don't know and can not say. But I suspect all those that
who are interested in the matter. There are so many people who are
interested in the matter and they are the people. Those people and
groups that are interested in taking the money that belongs to my
people, that belongs to the poor; who want be rich at the detriment
of the poverty stricken people of the swamp. I suspect all of them.
Q: Like which people and which group?
A: No.. no I think it will be wrong to mention any name.
Q: Okay what steps are you taking to safeguard your life?
A: Well I'm telling my country. And of course I'm reporting the
matter to the government... I've reported the matter to the
government, to the SSS, to the Police. I've reported the matter to
all the powers that are responsible for taking care of the lives of
Nigerians.
Q: Since after the court injunction granted you, do you know if the
Delta state government still went ahead spending the money?
A: Well, I don't know if they are using the money. When the final
judgment is given we shall look into that . The Accountant- General
has been put on notice, the Attorney- General is on notice, the
mobilization commission is on notice ; all the people who should
safeguard the money are on notice, so at the end, we will find out
where the money is and how much it is; because the injunction
stipulates that how much that is paid before and the money paid after
the injunction shouldn't be touched.
Q: Despite the threats coming through letters and telephone calls,
has there been any physical confrontation... is there any kind of
movement in the form of movement trailing or something like that?
A: Yes, people are trailing me. Yes, I know people are trailing me.
But you see one thing, when God asks you to do something, you will do
it. I've the courage to do what I've to do for the poor.
Q: Recently, there have been about two incidents of fire out-burst
resulting from busting of oil flow pipes, can we connect these
incidences to the youths reaction to the state government's use of
the 13% derivation fund?
A: You see the truth of the matter is that when a man has seen his
wife, his own children killed and burnt to death ; and has seen his
father and mother burnt to death; and yet the next day he picks up
his bucket to scoop for oil, it means that, that person has no value
for his life. He is pushed to the wall and has no other choice. These
people have to exist, it is a trade, a dangerous venture for that
matter, but they must do it because they have to lives. This is what
I see about the situation; and it is a pity, and that's why I am
saying that the first thing any of these governments should do for
the people should be to give value to lives. Look at me, I do not
think that I will go or my brothers and sisters should go and use
bucket to go and scoop oil; because we know the danger inherent in
it. They can not go because they have values for their lives, they
can afford three square meals a day; but these other ones who can not
afford a meal a day, even if the inferno burnt out a hand off them,
they will still use the remaining hand to scoop fuel.. because they
are hungry.
Q: In your opinion, what do you consider the solution to the problems
of the people of the Niger- Delta?
A: The problem of the people of the Niger-Delta is essentially one
that is connected to poverty; but still a lot can be done for them by
any sensitive government. For instance, Burutu, Bomadi Ijaw and
Patani are the homelands of the Ijaw people and they are islands.
These islands can be sandfilled and a school of fishery built since
fishery is the occupation of the people. Fishery occupation in this
part of the country can be fully developed like it is done in other
parts of the world. Today, people do not rely on wild fishes only,
but on developed fishes that are cured and developed through high
breed technology. Thailand lives on swampy rice, they also export to
other countries. In like manner, all the Ijaw, Iteskiri areas, swamp
rice cultivation should be given attention and encouraged as well as
the cultivation of mangrove rice. If there is employment for the
youths, and they are made to believe that if they work very hard that
one day they can bring Abuja to Burutu, they will definitely not
waste their lives and those of others by killing and robbery.

Original_Search_Title: The Abalaka Bogey
Headline: Nigeria: The Abalaka Bogey
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Federal Government wields the big stick banning all
medical practitioners who claim cures for HIV/AIDS from using them
until it gives the go-ahead. It is feared that a conspiracy is afoot.
Of all the established laws of nature, consistency of change has
proved to be the most immutable. Right from the ages, man has lived
in an unbroken chain of change but ironically, events in the history
of man have shown that he is generally rebellious against this
reality. Change is only tolerable when it creeps on him like a
chameleon. Perhaps this age-long rebellion against change could well
explain the suspicion and strong animosity and, indeed, conspiracy
against the world's greatest thinkers and inventors.
Jeremiah Ojeneme Abalaka, medical director of Medicrest Specialist
Hospital, Abuja whose claim to the cure of HIV, the virus that causes
AIDS is one of such. The Kogi State-born medical doctor and
immunologist's breakthrough vaccine and the proclamation of same has
put him in the relevant health authorities' black book. They
lambasted him for publishing his findings in the media rather than
going through the normal laid down procedures for such announcements
in medical practice. The imbroglio got to a head when the Minister of
Health, Dr. Tim Menakaya, was reported to have vowed to resign if
Abalaka's claims were true. Although, the Minister denied ever saying
such, Abalaka and his allies insist, the minister didn't speak tongue
in cheek.
The crisis, however, got to a head 20 July when in Abuja, the Federal
Government banned the use of all drugs and vaccines their inventors
claimed prevent or cure the disease. Briefing the media on this
development, Menakaya announced that President Olusegun Obasanjo has
directed the "suspension of the use of all drugs/vaccines claimed to
prevent or cure HIV/AIDS in the country until the completion of the
work of the committee set up to investigate the claims". This
announcement, according to observers, only seems to have reinforced
the belief that there is a conspiracy with a political undertone to
stifle Abalaka's claims.
While the controversy raged, Abalaka, in a bid to prove the efficacy
of his preventive vaccine against HIV, by his own admission, injected
himself with his vaccine and also introduced HIV infected blood into
his system. "I have done this up to six times to prove my point", he
told The News. Abalaka believes that the fact that he used himself as
a guninea pig to test his vaccine is the supreme test any scientist
could run. But his colleagues still wanted him to go through the
medical journal and publish his findings to which Abalaka replied:
"The Atomic bomb discovered 55 years ago; have they made it public
for everyone to know the formula they used? The Coke we are drinking,
is it not a scientific formulation, have they made it public?" The
battle was so fierce that Abalaka cried out to Nigerians that "they
want to kill me. There have been two attempts on my life".
Abalaka's claim, however, received a major boost in April this year
when the Chief of Air-Staff (CAS), Air-Vice Marshal Isaac Mohammed
Alfa, said that 30 Nigerian ECOMOG soldiers who had earlier tested
positive to the HIV infection before they were sent to Abalaka have
now reverted to negative after undergoing the treatment. A month
after this public pronouncement, the Chief of Army Staff, General
Victor Malu and a top army medical personnel also publicly
acknowledged the efficacy of Abalaka's vaccine treatment: "Soldiers
infected with HIV got better after five weeks of treatment by
Abalaka," Malu stressed. The report of a preliminary investigation by
the Nigerian Academy of Science headed by Professor Anya. O. Anya
noted that Abalaka's vaccine needed to be investigated further
because: "It is something worth investigating" but health authorities
adamantly maintained that there is no cure for AIDS. At best, health
authorities showcased the 'plight' of a young man on TV who claimed
he was HIV positive before going to Abalaka but he got worse after
treatment at Abalaka's Medicrest Hospital.
The immunologist said he was shocked at the level to which the
Ministry of Health's resentment for his noble work had gone because,
according to him," I have never set my eyes on that young man. I
can't tell him from Adam. This is pure set up. If they are sure of
what they are saying, let them produce the boy's registration card."
Yet, people living with HIV/AIDS would not stop trooping to his
Medicrest Hospital and indeed to other hospitals which lay claims to
such cures.
There is no gainsaying the fact that there is a fire of reawakening
blowing across the world. This is resurgence of the efficacy of herbs
and roots. There is a renewed clamour for herbal products and
medicine. Many medical conditions which had hitherto defied orthodox
medical solutions are being solved with herbs and roots and as a
pharmacist with Braxton Chemicals in Ogba, Lagos put, it, "any
pharmacist who is worth his salt, will go back to the roots and not
solely depend on the orthodox means of cure". But this awareness,
according to Dr. Paul Olisa-Ojeih, Medical Director of Iris Medical
Foundations, Lagos," will send orthodox medicine out of business and
this is why they are kicking against us". The National Secretary of
the Pharmaceutical Society of Nigeria, Azubuike Okuor, however,
lambasts the alternative medical practitioners for "starting a
controversy and sustaining it." He says that any person making a
claim to a cure of AIDS should avail himself of the vast experience
of agencies like the World Health Organisation (WHO). But Dr. Bode
Tawak, of BODDELL Clinics, Lagos, asked his professional colleagues,
"what is medicine, it is the act of relieving pain or totally curing
an ailment? The patient must feel better. The patients receiving
treatment from Abalaka and Co. claim they feel better and even post-
treatment lab tests show their HIV sero- prevalence had changed from
positive to negative, so what are we talking about? It is pure
politics. Let them find a way of improving on what sick people say
cures them".
Dr. Bola Olaosebikan, publisher of Healthcare magazine said Abalaka
and his likes should go to the National Assembly to protest their
ban. He is surprised that Nigeria does not encourage its talents and
inventors like Uganda and South Africa, are now doing but "the
military men that he has cured should come out and help him". The
News investigations show that they are already doing that, as armed
soldiers now guard his office and accompany him when he has to go
out.
Nigeria, indeed, showcases fatricide of talents. Ayodele Makanjuola,
a professor of Agricultural Engineering at the Obafemi Awolowo
University, (OAU), Ile Ife, had invented the human yam pounder about
31 years ago. He went through hell to get patent for that product. He
told The News, "it was difficult to obtain a local patent for the
machine. In fact, Britain and Japan granted me patent before Nigeria
did." The absence of an enabling environment and high level politics
have prevented two other products of Makanjuola from seeing the light
of the day.
But above these, there seems to be an international conspiracy
against the African inventor. This international conspiracy is
generously watered by the support enjoyed from local proxies. Ojeih
believes the three pharmaceutical companies in the forefront of
manufacturing HIV/AIDS drugs, Roche, GlaxoWellcome and Pfizer are in
an unholy alliance to discredit anybody with an alternative cure for
the disease from which they are making several billions of dollars.
One of such drugs which President Obasanjo incidentally recommends,
is the AZT, which according to Christine Maggiore's book, 'What if
everything you thought you knew about AIDS was wrong?,' is a drug
which originally was not created for the treatment of AIDS. AZT a
chemical compound, she said, was developed and abandoned over 30
years ago as a potential chemotherapy treatment for cancer. Prior to
the drug trials in 1986, AZT had never been administered on humans.
In 1964, experiments with AZT on mice with cancer showed that the
drug was so effective in destroying healthy growing cells that the
mice died of extreme toxicity. As a result, AZT, she claims, was
shelved and no patent was ever filed. Twenty years after, the
Pharmaceutical Company Burroughs Wellcome (now Glaxo Wellcome) began
a campaign to remarket AZT as an anti-HIV drug. The pharmaceutical
company won approval for AZT as an AIDS treatment drug after one
highly flawed study of only four months duration. Following
recommendation for "early detection," one-third to one-half of HIV
positives who develop AIDS do so only after taking AZT! Studies,
Maggiore stressed, have shown that AZT actually accelerates decline
and decreases quality of life, at times even causing death before any
AIDS - defining illnesses appear. AZT indeed, research has shown,
destroys the kidneys, liver, intestines, muscle tissue and the
central nervous system. AZT is thought by some to be a drug in search
of a disease. He accused unnamed foreign critics of waging a
"campaign of intellectual intimidation and terrorism" akin to the
"racist apartheid tyranny we opposed."
The just-concluded World AIDS Conference in Durban, South Africa was
almost entirely devoted to the President Thabo Mbeki declaration that
AIDS may not yet be the biggest threat to Africa where poverty,
hunger and lack of other basic necessities of life take a far greater
human toll. Indeed, Mbeki's subscription to the 'alternative theory
underscores a strong commitment to home-brewed remedies. The AIDS
issue is one it seems President Obasanjo will not reason on the same
plane with his South African soulmate. Meanwhile, HIV/AIDS spreads,
and Nigerians who have anyway never trusted official pronouncements
and actions where their health is concerned, continue to troop to
where they are convinced the cure is. The only difference now, is
that Abalaka may not grant interviews to say he is treating so many.

Original_Search_Title: Olusegun Obasanjo Takes On Poverty Alleviation With His PAP Programme
Headline: Nigeria: Olusegun Obasanjo Takes On Poverty Alleviation With His PAP Programme
Body_Text:
Lagos — Life is some what tough for Akpan Effiong, 40, a palm wine
tapper who lives on Edem Street, Old Cala- bar - a sprawling slum in
Calabar, Cross River State. Each day, at 5.30 a.m. Adiaha, his wife,
treks a quarter of a kilometre up the street to fetch water from a
borehole in a relation's compound. But if she could afford N2.00 per
bucket, she would save herself the effort. Her family cannot even
afford one good meal in a day.
None of their four children is in school. While their eldest son was
withdrawn from school in primary five over non-payment of fees, the
others never even went to school. The family resorts to herbal
medicine for treatment each time a member of the household falls
sick.
Effiong's case is just one out of thousands in Old Calabar. This part
of Calabar where the colonial masters once lived has since
degenerated into a slum and a symbol of abject poverty. It is dotted
with ancient and rickety houses that could collapse anytime.
A common feature of life in Old Calabar is that all the houses have
no toilet and potable water. Its residents are mostly traders with an
average daily income of between N100 and N150.00. That amounts to a
monthly wage of N3, 000 and N4, 500, or between N36, 000 and N54, 000
per annum.
This is short of the 1990 World Bank World Development Report, WDR,
which drew up a cut-off living standard below which a person is
classified poor. The report said any person whose income fell below
$370 (that is N40, 700 at prevailing exchange rate of N110 to the
dollar) is poor.
Another World Bank report titled "2000 World Bank Atlas" has ranked
Nigeria as the 12th poorest country in the world with a GNP per
capita of $740 as at 1998. The World Bank study used the purchasing
power parity method to arrive at its conclusion. By this, the GNP per
capita is converted into the dollar. The publication also ranked
Nigeria as the 21st poorest nation in 1998 with a GNP per capita of
$300, using what it called the Atlas Method. By the method, the GNP
per capita of the country is converted from its national currency
into dollar at the prevailing exchange rate.
Using the World Bank Atlas method, it means that Nigeria with a per
capita income of $300 falls into the category of absolute poverty.
The federal office of statistics, FOS, report for 1980 to 1996
confirmed that 67 million Nigerians live below the poverty line.
President Olusegun Obasanjo is not unaware of the level of poverty in
Nigeria. In keeping with his campaign promise to alleviate it, he
announced a poverty alleviation programme, PAP, earlier this year.
The scheme which is to cost the federal government N10 billion was
designed to offer micro-credit to semi-skilled and unskilled
Nigerians as part of government's plan to turn-around the prostrate
economy. It was also aimed at creating 200,000 jobs for unemployed
persons thereby stimulating production within one year. At least
5,000 jobs were meant to be created in each state of the federation
and Abuja.
The Federal government immediately constituted a poverty alleviation
committee in each state of the federation to supervise the
implementation of PAP. The committee's mandate was to issue out forms
to applicants, device PAP schemes at the state level and disburse
funds to successful applicants.
PAP was soon faced with several implementation problems. Top on the
list is corruption on the part of the officials charged with the
implementation of the scheme. Newswatch learnt that the poverty
alleviation committees set up at the state levels to aid its
execution are packed full with PDP sympathisers. Originally,
membership of the committees was to be made up of staff of the
federal ministry of works and housing in the states. But the
politicians who have hijacked the committees in the states are said
to be extorting between N100 and N200 from thousands of prospective
job seekers before issuing them registration forms. In Ogun State, S.
A. Uchendu, chairman of the state poverty alleviation committee, had
to publicly denounce the practice.
"All we require from them are their names, addresses and states of
origin. Anybody that is telling them to bring money to facilitate the
job seekers' chances is purely a 419 case," said Uchendu. In Akwa
Ibom State, beneficiaries of PAP are yet to be paid their first
salaries because the money was allegedly cornered by some of the
officials.
the peoples' Democratic Party, PDP, the ruling party, is using the
programme for patronage of its members. Instead of offering micro-
credit to semi-skilled and unskilled Nigerians, PAP funds are now
used by PDP officials at the state level to dispense favour to party
loyalists. Siaka Alfa, a member of the All Peoples' Party, APP, in
Kogi State, told Newswatch that PAP had been abused in the state. He
said PDP leaders beginning with the wards right up to the state
executives were known to have forwarded lists of party faithfuls,
cronies and family relations for inclusion in PAP.
"At the end of the month, they are paid N3,500 for doing nothing.
There is no visible programme of training or employment for the
participants to justify their being paid that kind of money. I don't
think that was the intention of the people who planned the scheme,"
Alfa told Newswatch.
PAP is similarly abused in Osun State controlled by Alliance for
Democracy, AD. Officials of the poverty alleviation committee of the
state are said to have included an unspecified number of
praise-singers of Okunade Sijuade, the Ooni of Ife, as beneficiaries
of PAP. "For singing praises of the Ooni, they get N3,500 each at the
end of the month," claimed a frustrated applicant who did not want to
be named.
Hoodlums and shysters have also stepped into the scene to further
derail the objectives of the scheme. Con artistes have devised
different schemes to defraud unsuspecting job seekers under the guise
of PAP. For instance, one Queen V.N. Babalola has initiated what she
calls "operation farewell to poverty," OFP, with headquarters at
number 6, Aperin Close, Old Bodija Estate, Ibadan. The motto of the
organisation which claims to cover the whole of south-west zone is
"economic empowerment of the citizens through participatory
democracy."
Babalola lures job seekers through advertisements to register with
her organisation for all sorts of favours ranging from financial
hand- outs to micro-credit and training. Babalola's job is made
easier by a network of churches, which provides logistics support for
OFP. To qualify to participate in her scheme, applicants must pay
N500 to obtain registration forms and identity cards. They must,
however, apply through the pastor of any Christ Apostolic Church,
CAC, who, in turn, liaises with an agent appointed by Babalola. There
are 680 such registered applicants at the CAC Ibefun, Odogbolu, Ogun
State.
Joseph Okitikpi, a registered applicant with OFP, told Newswatch that
Babalola's agents meet once in a month with applicants in the church
premises to discuss their participation in the scheme. They are asked
to pay N20 each per sitting and N100 each as hospitality fee for the
agent. "I don't really understand what is going on. They said they
are supposed to give us loans since April. I applied for N250, 000
and up-till now nothing has happened," Okitikpi told Newswatch.
Okitikpi was under the impression that the scheme was aligned with
that of the federal government PAP. "Besides, there was a rumour that
the woman was related to President Obasanjo," said Okitikpi.
When Obasanjo presented PAP proposals to the national assembly, he
was greeted by criticisms. Esther Idris-Abba, a graduate and job-
seeker, told Newswatch that the president's effort came a little too
late. "Having known the problems, in this country, the president
ought to have brought this programme in the year 2000 budget and not
as an extra budgetary matter. Secondly, I believe that he should have
availed the public of the details of the programme. Thirdly, knowing
Nigeria too well, it is very easy to see that this will be another
avenue for some big-guns to make money," she said. She went on:
"Imagine the president sending his ministers to go and launch a
programme which the public knows nothing about? Imagine the amount of
touring advances and allowances that would go into that; they are
surely enough to pay some low-level staff for a year or two. Now that
the president is creating this ephemeral programme, how is he going
to sustain it?"
She is not alone in criticising the programme. Lekan Balogun,
chairman, senate committee on national planning, is equally critical
of PAP. In his contribution to the debate on the budget 2000,Balogun
said: "If government is to create an enduring impact in the job
creation drive and increase capacity utilisation, manufacturing
plants must be given renewed attention through the manipulation of
appropriate policy tools."
Balogun said that the executive must be made to pay attention to a
coherent policy formulation, build virile policy-making machinery and
follow normal budgetary process.
He criticised the "hole-and-corner" approach to policy-making which
the executive arm of government has adopted in fighting poverty. "By
how much shall we say that inflation has been brought down, or
unemployment reduced? These shortcomings might have been avoided if
the statutory budget meetings had been held, he said.
An expert, who requested anonymity, told Newswatch last week that
Obasanjo's programme is faulty in many ways. She said the
job-creation strategies would not work because the jobs to be created
by the scheme are non-productive and that the salaries to be paid to
beneficiaries are such that they will not guarantee elevation above
poverty line.
Another critic said that the money voted for the programme is only
sufficient to pay about 200,000 workers in the unskilled sub-sector
for only twelve months. "What would the government do after the
twelve months? Will it seek a new mandate for another N10 billion?",
he asked.
Critics say that a proper poverty alleviation programme must not
necessarily bloat the civil service. They argue that such a programme
must emphasise self-dependence by beneficiaries. They also said that
the ability to monitor and evaluate the progress of any such
programme is essential. The general expectation is for the Obasanjo
government to develop a detailed and sustainable blueprint for the
programme to cover all tiers of governments and to be multi-pronged
to achieve the desired effect.
The speaker of the house of representatives, Ghali Umar-Na'Abba, is
worried that PAP might not be sustained after the first year. In his
letter to Obasanjo, he said eventhough the house had granted approval
for the programme, it could not be sustained under the provisions of
the constitution. From the tone of Na'Abba's letter, it is clear
Obasanjo and the house of representatives are poised for a
confrontation over the implementation of the programme which
commenced without an enabling law.
In his letter to Obasanjo, Na'Abba expressed doubts as to whether the
N10 billion proposed disbursement without legislative approval could
be sustained by Section 82 of the constitution and therefore
recommended that the president forward a bill to provide for the N10
billion intended to kick-start the reflation of the economy as a
supplement to the appropriation bill for 2000 with details.
There are fears as to whether Nigeria's present crop of leaders has
the commitment and sincerity of purpose to address the issue of
poverty. Akintoye Rhodes, chairman, Democratic Advance Movement, DAM,
Lagos State, told Newswatch that the way Obasanjo went about the
scheme showed that he did not consider poverty alleviation as a
priority.
"President Obasanjo had to submit the poverty alleviation bill as a
supplementary bill to the 2000 budget. Does somebody forget his
priority?" asked Rhodes. His view is that the scheme is another
avenue for some people to enrich themselves.
But Rhodes has agreed that there was a need to strengthen the small
and medium-scale industries that employ between 50 and 100 people.
This way, he said, Nigeria could produce primary products for export.
He said the government must provide the enabling environment to boost
productive activities.
Economic analysts believe that it is not enough to have the intention
but that such intention must translate into positive action. The
general belief is that certain social infrastructures like water,
regular electricity supply, good roads and so on, must be provided
before poverty can be alleviated.
The government must also embark upon rigorous human development
efforts to make its poverty alleviation scheme meaningful. The World
Bank which works in partnership with countries with low HDIs believes
that no country can secure sustainable economic growth or reduce
poverty without healthy, well-nourished and well-educated people.
Such was the concern of Ogboneanya Nwankwo, a 1992 unemployed
graduate of the University of Lagos, about PAP. He told Newswatch the
programme was yet to impact positively on the standard of living of
Nigerians several months after it was flagged off. "Majority of the
poor are dying of abject poverty, children are malnourished. Many
families cannot afford the basic needs of life as a result of the
high cost of living in the economy. So, what poverty are they
alleviating?" asked Nwankwo.
Despite the high level of poverty in the coun-try, government
officials continue to claim that the rate of inflation has reduced
and that the economy had begun to grow. Joseph Sanusi, governor of
Central Bank of Nigeria, CBN, said recently that inflation had
dropped from 10.2 percent in 1998 to 6.6 percent as at last December.
"On the inflation front, fiscal discipline, accommodating monetary
policies contributed to bringing down the rate of inflation and
sustained stability", said Sanusi. But he acknowledged that other
economic indices of growth slumped during the same period. For
instance, he said capacity utilization of the manufacturing sector
fell from 33.7 percent in 1997 to 32.4 percent in 1998. Gross
Domestic Product, GDP, similarly dropped from 3.2 percent in 1997 to
2.4 percent in 1998.
Sanusi said that there was a noticeable improvement in the macro-
economic environment even though the level of manufacturing capacity
utilisation remained low. He said this was due to low effective
demand as a result of poverty, ageing machinery and equipment, lack
of working capital and inadequate infrastructure.
Contrary to Sanusi's claims, cost of living continues to rise
generally in Nigeria. A Newswatch survey of markets around Lagos
showed that prices of food items have risen sharply. For instance, at
Oshodi market, a bag of egusi which cost N6, 000.00 last year now
sells for N9, 000.00. A cup of egusi was N40.00 but is now N70.00.
Reuben Chukwudi, a trader at Oshodi market, told Newswatch that the
increase was as a result of the high cost of transportation. Egusi is
brought into Lagos mainly from the East.
The cost of beans has also risen sharply. Beans is regarded among
low-income earners as a substitute for meat. As at last year, a
measure of beans cost N20.00 but it now costs N50.00. Ismaila Idris,
a trader at Oke-Odo market, told Newswatch that the price of yam has
similarly gone up. He said the problem was with the cost of
transportation. He said to bring in a truck-load of yams from places
like Abuja and Lafia to Lagos in 1995 would have cost N10, 000.00 and
about N15, 000.00 last year. But it now costs between N23, 000.00 and
N25, 000.00 depending on where the yams are coming from.
Rice is, however, considered cheap at the moment. As at November last
year, a bag sold for between N2, 800 and N3,000 while a cup measure
sold for N60, but now a bag sells at between N2,300 and N2,400 and a
cup measure at N45.00. Newswatch learnt that this is attributable to
massive importation of rice by Obasanjo late last year up-till early
this year. Many traders who stocked rice last year for sale this year
could not do so as a result of surplus rice leading to a fall in
prices.
Paul Dalynton, a trader, told Newswatch at Abesan retail market,
Ipaja, last week that the food stuff business was witnessing a
downturn because there was no money in the economy. "You can easily
read poverty written on the faces of people now", he said.
Awal Zakariya, 23 year-old beggar, is also unimpressed with the
implementation of PAP. Zakariya lost his father after his primary
education. His father left behind seven of them. Zakariya fled from
his village, Rigasan, to Zaria, in order to escape hunger and
despondency. He had no western education, no capital to go into petty
trading and so he took to begging. Today, he is a very bitter man. "I
want to tell the government and those ruling our land that they
should know that they are like potatoes. They are the roots and we
are the leaves. Once the leaves are not looking good, then the root
is definitely not going to look good," Zakariya said.
He said if the government was sincere with its Universal Basic
Education, UBE, programme, it would go a long way in improving
people's lives. "If they give free education and I know that I will
get food I will definitely go to school, but even as it is, big men's
children who have good western education have no jobs. They become
liabilities on their parents, so what is the hope of the children of
the poor?" Zakariya asked.
The impact of UBE is yet to be felt in most public schools. For
instance, at a primary school in Ajeromi, Lagos, pupils sat on bare
floors and were exposed to direct rays of the sun coming through worn
out and perforated ceiling. With tears in her eyes, a teacher in the
school who spoke under anonymity told Newswatch: "I believe that the
government is not blind. This is one of the government schools in
Lagos. The situation in the school can speak for itself".
She said the school had long awaited promises from both the local
government and state primary education board, SPEB, which promised to
remedy the situation. As of last week nothing had been done to redeem
the promise.
Unemployment is yet to abate. Fidelis Osamor, public relations
officer of the federal civil service commission told Newswatch
recently that about 90,000 forms had been given to first-degree
holders at the Abuja headquarters of the commission and the states.
He said the commission had to adopt stringent criteria for collection
of forms to peg the number of applicants at 90,000.
But there seems to be no respite for the applicants as yet. Newswatch
learnt that the 52 ministries and extra-ministerial departments have
failed to declare their vacancies or those of their agencies as
directed by the commission. The ministry officials are also said to
be sidelining the civil service commission by recruiting staff
directly on ad-hoc basis initially and later regularising them. This
situation is believed to have made it difficult for the commission to
carry out its function of giving jobs to the applicants.
Nigeria has never fallen short of attempts at alleviating poverty.
Previous governments devised several schemes to alleviate poverty in
the land but virtually in all cases, the attempts fell short of
expectations. For instance, the establishment of the National
Directorate of Employment, NDE, by General Ibrahim Babangida, former
president, in 1986 was to promote skill acquisition and self-
employment. But the project failed to achieve the set objectives due
to inadequate funding and corruption on the part of its officials.
Other poverty alleviation programmes like the Peoples' Bank of
Nigeria, PBN, Nigeria Agricultural and Co-operative Bank, NACB,
Family Economic Advancement Programme, FEAP, and National
Agricultural Land Development Authority, NALDA, have similarly failed
to alleviate poverty in Nigeria. The organisations were all
rationalised by the present regime to form a National Poverty
Eradication Commission, NPEC.
Since 1980, poverty level has been on the rise in Nigeria. The
federal office of statistics, FOS, poverty profile of Nigeria
published last year, showed that the level of poverty rose from 28.1
percent in 1980 to 46.3 percent in 1985 but dropped slightly to 42.7
percent in 1992. It rose sharply to 65.6 percent in 1996.
According to the report, the estimated number of the poor rose from
18 million in 1980 to 35 million in 1985. It further rose to 39
million in 1992 and to 67 million in 1996. The report showed that
poverty was at a higher level in the rural areas than in the urban
areas because 70 percent of Nigerians live in the villages.
The introduction of the Structural Adjustment Programme, SAP, in 1986
is said to have complicated the poverty situation in the country. The
growth of the gross national product, continues to dip. From 3.0
percent in 1992, it dropped to 1.3 percent in 1994 and then rose to
2.2, 3.4, 3.8, and 2.4 percent in 1995,1996, 1997 and 1998
respectively. Going by the UNDP's human development index,, HDI,
Nigeria ranks 142nd with HDI of 0.400 among the 174 countries listed
in 1997.
By 1998, Nigeria dropped to 146th position, becoming one of the
world's 40 poorest nations. The clear import from the UNDP report is
that poverty has been on the increase in direct production to the
extent of the deterioration in the economy.
A report of the independent commission on population and quality of
life published in 1966, noted that half a century after the UN
pledged to abolish hunger, millions of people still live a life of
destitution. Poverty, says the report, is "a blatant attack on
(human) dignity."
Reported by Tunde Asaju, Obong Akpaekong, Nick Odeh, Geoffrey Ekenna
and Rosemary Isado.

Original_Search_Title: Farming Of Fish Spreading
Headline: Nigeria: Making Millions From Fish Hatchery
Body_Text:
Lagos — Aquaculture or fish farming is fast spreading all over
Nigeria. Experts believe that it will be one of the highest growth
industries in the country by the year 2010.
We all now the importance of fish as a food item and also as a cheap
source of animal protein. Nigeria with a population of over 100
million people is a huge market for food fish. The demand is high but
the supply is quite low.
Indeed, there are many fishermen in Nigeria - both in the artisanal
(canoe) industrial (trawl) sectors, but their wild catches and
supplies are not enough to satisfy the requirements of the teeming
population. Even when these productions from Nigeria's inland and
coastal waters are heavily supplemented with imported fish yet the
demand continues to out-strip the overall supply. And yet, the fish
prices remain prohibitive to many.
In Nigeria, I have met many people who don't eat meat but I'm yet to
meet a fellow who doesn't eat fish in any form - except, of cause,
the Hare Krishna devotees, and they are few in number. Thus, there is
real money in food fish production which is more affordable to the
people than say, chicken. So in order to make fish readily available
at affordable costs, farming it is the way forward.
But since a lot of people would like to venture into fish production
business without being fishermen, the only road to their ambitions
will be to build fish farms in their premises, farm estates or on
marginal lands not suitable for agricultural crops. Yet in their
localities streams and rivers and impoundments with good quality
water waste idly, untapped. Others have borehole water facilities in
their premises and ignorantly leave it only to serve domestic
purpose. But a wiser use would be to attach a couple of 8m2 concrete
tanks and make tens of thousands of Naira weekly on live fish sales
to neighbourhood customers and market women re-sellers.
It is a fact that the major problem of most people interested in
commercial fish farming is how to obtain the fingerlings for stocking
when required. Once there is a ready and constant availability of
fish fingerlings in a particular community or locale, many people
there are usually attracted to fish farming in a short time.
Indeed, there is real money to be made in commercial hatchery
business which has no season. It is ideal for anyone wishing to be
self-employed and making hundreds of thousands of naira monthly in
fingerling sales - depending on the scale of your operation in your
premises or farm. It is also ideal for young school leavers and
university graduates wishing to make big money early in life.
But the scarcity of commercial hatcheries in Nigeria is a big problem
both for fish farmers and intending ones. For example, in Lagos
State, fingerlings are purchased from not more than three hatcheries.
But the, one must have to place orders weeks or even months in
advance, and at the end you may not get up to the number you require.
In most cases, Lagos fish farmers purchase their fingerlings from as
far as Ibadan and Ogun State. A fish farmer from Edo State may have
to buy his fingerlings in Lagos, Warri or Ibadan. Farmers in the
eastern states may go to Port Harcourt or Warri for supplies. Those
in the northern states purchase theirs from New Bussa in Niger State.
Imagine the distances, and by the time you transport the fragile
fingerlings to your farm, most of them would have been dead. These
problems could be a business opportunity to any body or a company
wishing to diversify into a lucrative ready cash venture.
In view of this, many farms nor are trying to build their own
hatcheries both to escape the high cost of fingerlings and also to be
to have a ready stock of fingerlings when they wanted in order to
expand their operations. But unfortunately, they lack the technology
for mass fish seed production. Without a ready availability of
fingerlings at the right time and quantity, fish farms cannot expand
their operations or increase their productions.
Some multi-national oil companies in Nigeria have set up rural
community development projects in the oil producing areas. These
laudable programmes are aimed at assisting rural farmers to boost
food production. They, among other activities, engage in improved
seed multiplication programmes for fish, cassava and yams. That is
improved fingerlings and crop seedlings which they distribute to
farmers. AGIP has the Green River Project at Obrikom in Rivers State;
Shell runs the community development programme in all oil producing
areas and; ELF has its Elf in rural life project at Oboburu also in
Rivers state.
But go to any of them and request for say 30,000 fingerlings and if
you are lucky and patient enough the highest you can get is 10,000.
In short, they normally offer 10,000 because they have many other
farmers to supply.
In hatchery business venture, all you need to start is a borehole
facility with good quality water supply and a space of about one plot
of land or less. With some breeder fish (catfish, for example) in
stock in your farm or purpose-built hatchery, using our modern
technology the operator can mass-produce millions of fingerlings a
month - either for his farm needs or for sale. One fingerling of
about 3 weeks old sale for between N5-10. Those of 4-5 weeks old sale
for between N10-15.
Assuming you have a small hatchery and produced 300,000 early
fingerlings of catfish this week, with our technology 70 per cent or
more will survive to reach advanced fingerlings stage in three weeks.
Assuming also that only 100,000 survived and you sold each at a
minimum price of only N5 instead of N10. Your revenue for that month
should be N500,000 (five hundred thousand naira). Yet the cost of
micro feed (Sanders Artemia) for feeding them to achieve faster
growth will not be less than N10,000. But you will be hard pressed
supplying buyers from within and outside the state where your
hatchery is located. But then, your immediate community needs, too.
And for those already in the business, this is the lure of
aquaculture. Thus, for your fish farms and hatcheries, it pays to
appoint consultants who are leading the way. Those who look ahead and
involve themselves now, will be the leading players in this
development, and obviously also the first to benefit.
Mr. Sharon Anochie is a Lagos-based aquaculture technologist and
consultant.

Original_Search_Title: Assessing Benin's Economic Performance 40 Years After Independence
Headline: Benin: Assessing Benin's Economic Performance 40 Years After Independence
Body_Text:
COTONOU — On the whole, Benin has achieved little
economically 40 years after independence even though its budget
substantially increased from 5.402 billion CFA francs (recurrent and
capital expenditure) in 1960, to 231.338 billion (recurrent) in 2000
(700 CFA francs = 1 US dollar).
With the devaluation of the CFA franc in January 1994, the 2000
budget shows state expenditure rising to the tune of 375.800 billion
CFA francs, which includes a deficit of 144.462 billion CFA francs to
be filled by foreign partners.
During the 1980 and 1990 decades, Benin's budget has been
characterised by a substantial deficit more or less, according to
officials in Cotonou.
The population has increased from 2.106 million in 1961 to some 6.5
million this year. This shows a 2.6 percent growth rate in 1979-1980
as against 3.3 percent currently.
On the other hand, economic growth rate which increased from 3
percent in 1981-1985, to 5.7 percent currently, is not strong to
create enough wealth to improve the well-being of the people.
Thus, the people are increasingly impoverished. The situation was
particularly acute with the drastic conditions of the three
structural adjustment programmes (SAP) negotiated with the World Bank
and the International Monetary Fund (IMF), after the collapse of the
banking system in 1989. The economy recorded a negative growth rate
of 2.9 percent during the revolutionary and Marxist- Leninist era.
The State could not pay its 40,000 employees and even had to slash
this to 30,000 in order to create room for growth, based on the
recommendations of a 1998 national study on long-term development of
the country.
In the view of World Bank and IMF representatives in Cotonou,
improved economic performance arising from the implementation SAP has
enabled the two institutions to grant Benin debt cancellation of 460
million dollars, or some 322 billion CFA francs.
This "external debt cancellation decision comes with a national
programme aimed at reducing poverty in Benin", finance and economy
minister, Abdoulaye Bio Tchane, pointed out.
The country's outstanding debt is close to 800 billion CFA francs.
Inflation, which the government estimates at 2.8 percent, is eating
deep into household incomes, particularly after the recent increase
in the price of petroleum products by between 32 and 77 percent.
Furthermore, galloping inflation has weakened the purchasing power of
the average person in Benin, thus increasing poverty in most
households. The per capita income dropped from 203 dollars in 1985 to
192 dollars in 1995.
According to the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) in
1995-1996, 67 percent of the rural population and 55 percent of the
urban population of Benin lived below the poverty line.
The agency says "900,000 people of Benin, some of whom face
intermittent food shortages, are affected by extreme poverty".
However, Benin is generally self-sufficient in food with cereals
production (maize, millet, sorghum, and rice) that increased from
335,790 tons in 1978 to 700,000 tons presently.
Production of tubers (cassava, yams) has also risen constantly.
The government has even set aside one billion CFA francs in the 2000
budget to boost cassava production, part of which will be exported to
the European Union (EU).
Lack of access roads makes it difficult for food products and the
main cash crop, cotton (production of which increased from 335,000
tons in 1999 to 360,000 tons this year) to be transported from remote
areas.
Despite government's occasional maintenance efforts, many rural
access roads are not passable during the rainy season and for most of
the harvest period.
At the social level, Benin has one doctor for every 10,000
inhabitants. Mortality rate averaged 14.3 per 1,000 people between
1992 and 1997. The illiteracy rate fell from 68.8 percent in 1996 to
59.9 percent in 1992.
The national university has a student population of 12,000 while the
number of jobless people are close to 8,000 this year against 6,640
in 1988 and 7,180 in 1993.
At independence on 1 August 1960, former Dahomey, also referred to as
'Africa's Latin district' because of the number and quality of its
educated elite, was renamed Benin in 1975 under the Marxist-Leninist
revolution.
This small West African country covering 112,622 square kilometres
could have achieved more despite its limited natural resources, had
it not been completely destabilised by the spiral of military coups
that ensued just three years after independence.
Indeed, six military coups took place between 1963 and 1972, often
with the complicity of politicians engaged in bitter rivalry.
The last putsch, staged in 1972, brought the then Maj. Mathieu
Kerekou to power, for the first time, and ushered in a revolutionary
military rule.
The regime, which embraced the Marxist-Leninist ideology in 1974,
with the participation of civilians in the government, ruled the
country with an iron fist for 17 years.
It was supported by the highly politicised Benin army, which brutally
repressed the slightest inclination to challenge or contradict
official revolutionary ideas.
Many student protesters were imprisoned and sometimes tortured to
death. Former heads of state (Hubert Maga, Sourou Migan Apithy,
Justin Ahomadegbe and Emile- Derlin Zinsou) went into exile after
they were put under house arrest for over ten years.
Many other politicians and intellectuals followed suit.
Most of them returned home only to attend the historical February
1990 national conference, which granted amnesty to all the political
exiles.
Meanwhile, the national economy had collapsed since 1988. Western
donors, who could not stand both the nature of the regime and the
catastrophic manner in which the country's meagre resources were
being managed, abandoned the State.
In 1989, on the eve of the national conference that was to dissolve
all the revolutionary institutions and set up a democratic process,
the government abandoned the Marxist-Leninist option.
The conference also restored public freedom, particularly the freedom
of the press and a full multiparty system.
A one-year (1990-1991) transitional government was set up. A
transitional parliament granted President Kerekou personal amnesty
for all wrongdoings committed during the 17 years of his rule.
In March 1991, at the end of the second round of the first free
presidential elections, interim Prime Minister Nicephore Soglo was
elected for a five-year term beating Kerekou, his challenger.
In March 1996, Kerekou defeated Soglo in the second round of the
second presidential polls to make a come-back to power.
The next presidential election will be in March 2001.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Staple Foodstuffs Stabilise
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Staple Foodstuffs Stabilise
Body_Text:
Lagos — Prices of foodstuffs and other consumer items in the country
