higher education and allow the universities to fend for themselves,
which he said was impossible. He further lamented that it has been
the policy of IMF to discourage higher education in developing
countries so as to scuttle their growth and development, for, without
high level manpower, no country could reasonably grow.
He then warned that if President Obasanjo doesn't distance himself
from administering IMF prescriptions on Nigeria, Nigerian students
and workers would bring him down, adding that the Bretton Woods
institutions would be his greatest waterloo. I never believed that at
a time when millions of Nigerian parents cannot foot the bills of
their wards in universities, and at a time when the clamour is for
free education at all levels, any well-meaning Nigerian government
would venture into wholescale privatisation of higher education in
the country. So, while I duly noted the views of the ASUU president,
I honestly didn't give it a thought in the belief that ASUU is once
more plotting to send home our children to remain, as usual, out of
school for months doing nothing. In spite of my perception about the
Obasanjo administration and the fuel crisis it deliberately fuelled,
I was of the strong belief that they would never contemplate such a
thing that would bring doom to higher education and the nation in
general. Even military regimes, I reasoned, never dared to visit such
an evil on Nigerians and the educational system.
Painfully, ThisDay of 20/06/2000 proved me wrong when it reported
that the Federal Government is set to withdraw 85% of its financial
commitment to the universities beginning from next academic session,
adding for emphasis that, the move enjoys the support of IMF. As a
prelude to the granting of autonomy to universities JAMB would be
phased out beginning from the next academic session. I thought deeply
after reading this piece about the future of our great country. I
wondered what would become of the democratic order should government
go ahead with this deadly IMF plot against our people. I got very
concerned how a democratic regime could work towards the
disempowerment of the people in such a way as never contemplated by
all the past dictatorships.
After agonising over the plot, I took the New Nigerian of the same
day and read an article by one Tukur Umaru Mohammed. The area that
caught my attention says: The inner workings of IMF are succinctly
summarised by Mr. Davison Budhow who headed IMF missions to a number
of third world countries. His testimony is chilling.
In his letter of resignation, Mr. Budhow wrote: Today I resign from
the staff of the IMF after over 12 years and after 1000 days of
official fund work in the field, hawking your bag of tricks to
governments and peoples in Latin America, the Caribbean and Africa. I
have no illusions that the way we operate could be summed up this
way: devalue your currencies, tax the poor, remove all transfers and
subsidies to the under-privileged, fire the people, cut out social
services, let the children starve, let malnutrition of all in the
shanty towns be our performance criteria, increase prices of bread,
yams, soap, water, negate the constitution, kill all the hope and
aspiration from the heart of the down- trodden. Go back
again...begging in the streets, turn back the clock two hundred years
and let us again be the masters and you the slaves. These are the
words of a senior IMF official who worked there for over twelve
years! I examined these words very critically and all speak the
truth, as we know it, of the Obasanjo administration. I dare say that
the administration has no policy of its own except that dictated by
the Bretton Woods institutions. This was why in spite of the fuel
riots Obasanjo still told us that fuel subsidies would have to go
whether or not we like it and he knows the dangers, economically,
socially and politically. Yet, he is bent on going ahead in line with
instructions.
As the Nigerian Tribune reported recently, IMF has taken over Nigeria
with its officers supervising our strategic economic institutions.
Some sources, in fact, indicate that the so-called anti-corruption
bill, in its raw form, was designed by the body and anybody who reads
it then knew that once passed as designed, Nigerians would be made
slaves in the name of fighting corruption. All our rights would have
been violated with impunity Consider the so-called grey areas in the
budget. They are nothing but provisions by the National Assembly for
the supply of electricity, water, rural roads and health facilities
in rural areas. No sooner was the budget passed than the IMF said it
was not acceptable to it simply because it does not want the rural
people to benefit from such services. We Nigerians shouted at NASS,
not knowing that it was fighting a battle against the IMF on our
behalf. Don't be surprised to learn sometime to come that it is not
the Presidency as such that doesn't want to work with NASS but the
Bretton Woods institutions who are increasingly finding it difficult
to get their agenda through the National Assembly.
As we all know, they have got the Presidency in their pockets doing
as they dictate. No one can deny this fact. I feel very sorry to
observe that from all indications the Obasanjo administration is an
extension of the IMF. And this comes out the more when I reflect on
reports by his South-West newspapers that senior officials of the
administration have some substantial links with some foreign
intelligence agencies. This was perhaps a pre-election blackmail.
Unfortunately, however, the more we look and observe, the more the
accusation comes through.
There was first the plot for a military pact, which some thoughtless
people championed. Then the doors were opened to foreigners to buy
our national assets at auction rate. Fertilizer subsidy was
withdrawn, something that they do not apply back home as Chief Falae
told us recently. Withdrawal of subsidy in fuel was attempted and
still in the pipeline as the president told us in his latest national
broadcast. In fact, the broadcast was just to tell us that we might
as well go to hell and the plan would go ahead regardless. The
Vanguard captured this in one of its editorials. With these subsidies
gone, we can only say that the poor would gravely suffer as correctly
lamented by Mr. Budhow, and as captured in the piece by Tukur U.
Mohammed in the New Nigerian.
Now, subsidy in higher education would be withdrawn by 85%! No doubt,
the Obasanjo administration is still fishing in troubled waters and
for reasons we don't know. It is wrong to insinuate that the
government is interested in fuelling crises. No government would like
to do that. It is equally wrong to assume that they want us to be
economically enslaved to the West. No patriot would contemplate such
a thing. Nor is it correct to reason that they do not care about the
deep-rooted poverty in the land, which has given rise to various
violent tendencies. All governments care about the people. It is
absolutely wrong to say that, like the ASUU president said, they
don't bother if students and workers bring them down.
This would neither be in their interest nor ours. We want this regime
to be our road map to the haven of democracy, no matter its numerous
pitfalls which could be corrected over time. We cannot say that they
don't want democracy by their actions in the National Assembly. Then,
what is the great agenda and goal? Breaking up Nigeria? Certainly no.
Then what? We have had so many crises since the government came to
power and for it to engineer and fuel another one through withdrawing
subsidy on education is hard to understand.
Now another danger is looming that would devastate the polity no less
than the fuel crisis. Labour certainly would join, as also the entire
population. because when you talk of withdrawing subsidy on
education, you mean, by it, taxing poor parents or denying the
children of the poor fair access to higher education. What it further
means is that you are ruining their future in old age since they
invest in education so that their children could take care of them
when they are old. Since the creation of Nigeria, no worse policy has
ever been considered by any government as this one. This makes the
administration a dangerous right wing government of the extremist
brand; the Pinochet type of government which oppressed the people to
satisfy its masters in Washington only for them to dump him like an
unwanted over-ripe fruit in old age. So also Noriega and Shah of
Iran. They laboured for their masters who turned around to ditch them
later when they were no longer of any use. This is the danger in
enslaving oneself to these people. Their theory of use and dump is
too glaring for comfort.
But the greater fear is not this, but the possible use of brute force
to push unpopular theories down the throats of the people. All IMF
slavish countries have applied this method one way or the other.
Force had always been used to suppress the popular will of the
people. This was the case with the SAP riots though it was largely
confined to Lagos. This was also the case with the invasion of the
National Assembly quarters. And if they could be intimidated, we too
could. I fear for this occurring in the event of the withdrawal of
subsidy on higher education. Worse than this, the universities would
collapse. Parents would not be in the position to pay.
The private sectors wouldnt. The universities would only end up
opening sham outreach centres, and even then only a few would be able
to afford them. What this means ultimately is that higher education
would become available only to the children of a few. This would
seriously affect the middle class system and invariably the entire
system. The poor would revolt and the polity would be badly affected.
No advantage, of whatever nature, would accrue from this policy
except to make worse the poverty of the Nigerian poor and the
retardation of intellectual learning. Will Obasanjo in the name of
university autonomy see our universities closed down forever? I hate
to sound it that the IMF is digging the political grave of this right
wing government. Let this government not be deceived that some hidden
forces have the power and means to protect it when the greater
interest of the people is at stake.
Whatever IMF policies it wants to implement, it must dialogue with
the stakeholders, in this case, ASUU, NANS, representatives of
parents, etc.
If the Federal Government wants peace on the campuses, it has to
consult with these and other organisations; otherwise there would be
trouble.
Apparently the centre is not willing to take responsibilities for
university education. This being the case, it should hand them over
to the states with their corresponding allocations. To privatise
university education is to downgrade Nigeria to the status of an
illiterate nation which does not know what it wants. We can now see
the sense in the call for the devolution of power. If the centre can
no longer hold the universities, the states should; but certainly not
the IMF. We hope the administration would see reason and walk along
the path of sanity and caution in handling this explosive issue. We
sincerely hope so.
Finally, is this what the PDP promised us? If not, then the party has
a duty to Nigerians to tell the administration not to lead us to the
slaughter slab. If it doesn't, it is digging its own grave. The PDP
has completely derailed from the path it promised Nigerians and it is
in its own interest to retrace its steps before it crumbles along the
way. The party has been systematically killing itself by constantly
shooting itself in the foot, always walking one- legged. Right now,
there is little left of the PDP except as a unit in Aso Rock with a
special assistant in charge.
APP has just been discharged from hospital and is recovering fairly
well.
AD was rushed there in coma and may not survive the operation. For
long, PDP has been an out-patient and may be hospitalised soon. The
withdrawal of the education subsidy may spell death. INEC has a duty,
therefore, to allow the other six political organisations to operate
as political parties along with the APP, which shows evidence of
survival. In the alternative, new parties should be allowed to freely
operate. PDP has badly frightened us with its excessive pro- IMF
policies which are all geared towards undermining our collective
interest. And there are no signs that it would change.
News Story N1.m gift from Gov. splits Northerners in Rivers Amadi
Ruskin, Port Harcourt One-million naira gift from the Gov-ernor of
Rivers State, Sir (Dr.) Peter Odili, to the Northern community during
this years Id-El-Kabir celebrations in Port Harcourt has caused a
rift among the Northern community in the state, pitching the Sarkin
Hausawa of the state, Alhaji Isa Madaki, against Alhaji Balarabe
Mato, Chairman of the Northern Forum, Eastern zone.Alhaji Mato was to
said to have stormed the office of the Sarkin Hausawa upon his return
from Holy pilgrimage in Saudi Arabia, and accused him of diverting
the gift for his (Sarkis) personal use. Alhaji Mato was said have
even lambasted his vice, Alhaji Chizo Salihu (a member of the
delegation to Government House that warranted the N1m. dash) for not
effectively checking the financial recklessness of the Sarki. The
Alhaji was also credited with statements advising the government to
always distinguish between a traditional ruler and a gift for the
Northern Forum.
However, Alhaji Balarabe Ahmed, the Chairman of Kano State indigenes
in Rivers State, told Weekly Trust that the Northern Community in
Rivers State still have confidence in the leadership of Alhaji Isa
Madaki as the Sarki Hausawa of River State, saying that the N1.m gift
from the Rivers State Governor is purely a traditional one, and not a
political one. He said all over the world, it is customary for
traditional leaders to pay Sallah homages to people during which
gifts are usually presented. He said the Sarki had not misused the
gift and warned Alhaji Mato against doing things that are capable of
demeaning them before their hosts whom he said have shown tremendous
love for them.

Original_Search_Title: Kwashiorkor-A Disease of Ignorance
Headline: Africa: Kwashiorkor
Body_Text:
Lagos — Oh! Mother Africa, you are so blessed with abundance of
nature! Why then do your children suffer from disease that must have
been classified as stranger! Is it over-development or
under-development or misere or misconception? No!
It is ignorance of how to apply the law of simplicity; for there are
foods simple enough for an average peasants child to eat everyday
that offer nourishments sufficient to keep such a child alive without
any health problem-free from any disease till old age. Various groups
and different combinations will be referred to later in this article.
Kwashiorkor-what is kwashiorkor and who are those that suffer from
this disease? Kwashiorkor is a disease that is caused by dietary
deficiency of protein not only in the quality of food eaten but also
in the quantity of protein in each diet taken. Some of the
manifestations and symptoms of kwashiorkor are: 1) Enlarged liver due
to fat collecting in the peritoneal cavity and causing the child's
abdomen to protrude.
2) Diarrhea is common and usually leads to dehydration and salt
imbalance in the body.
3) Puff face and fineness of the hair (no life in the hair's growth).
Children of the poor areas of the world suffer from this disease
because of their diets and lifestyles.
4) This disease can be termed progressive.
After weaning a baby, the risk or possibility for a child becoming
anemic or malted are many. Also one of the reasons being that the
nutrients taken through the mother cease with weaning as the child is
then left to whatever nutrients he/she can directly obtain from what
he eats or takes daily. It is only with great care that nutritional
deficiency will not occur especially in a poor family set up in a
third world country, where the basic diet comprises mainly all types
of pro-ceased carbohydrates, e.g., Cassava products or garri, yam,
cocoa yams polished rice with very little vegetable, and in most
cases very small protein or none.
Occasionally some bean/legumes diets are given. No milk, (in actual
fact milk is not a must in an African child's diet because
genetically, the digestive system of a baby born in Africa is not
programmed to absorb milk as its European or American counterparts).
There are other fruits and vegetables etc., that can replace milk
when mother's milk is given from birth till a child is 6 months to
nine months. After this period the whole bony structures must have
been well established and the risk of a child becoming sickly will be
reduced.
The treatment of kwashiorkor takes the form of repairing and
re-storing with well-balanced diets of minerals and vitamins that the
body normally requires. Also fluid and salt in the body must be
balanced by taking enough vegetable fruits.
Parents must be educated on how best to feed their children in order
to prevent a disease such as kwashiorkor or similar ones.
Malnutrition is related to kwashiorkor as the poverty level of a
family may so affect the nutritional intake of a child that may lead
to diseases such as malnutrition and kwashiorkor. Unlike kwashiorkor,
malnutrition occurs in all parts of the world even where there are
plentiful to eat etc. Poverty is the majora use or factor. Some of
the other cause may be wars, natural disasters and some diseases
caused by malabsorption of foods taken. But basically, nutritional
factors can be mainly associated to a disease such as malnutrition.
Dietary consideration in kwashiorkor must include combinations of
well-balanced protein such as bean dish with vegetable. Meats are not
necessary with such a meal. Fruits in season to be added. Bananas or
pawpaw are nutritional and delicious. Children like them. Fish and
meats with fruits and lots of rash or cooked vegetables combined
provide some nutrition. Also, seeds and nuts plus grains must be
introduced to the diet. Millet is common here in Nigeria, not only in
the Northern parts but everywhere.
Delicious drinks can be made with fruits and vegetables. Fufu or
millet is taken with vegetables etc. Breakfast cereal may include
sorghum (grains Oka baba -Yoruba).
Eggs are taken with other meals. Also some oils, e.g., olive oil is
taken daily.
Below are some sources of nutrients in fruits, vegetables etc.
1) The family called Legumes (peas, beans and cloves) supply calcium,
potassium, phosphorous, etc, in non-poisonous form. They are called
lime plants and present us with materials to build bone, ligaments
and teeth.
2) Sources of Vitamins C in some fruits and vegetables are: Pawpaw
(Papaya), Cabbage, Green peas, Garlic, Lemons, Tomato, Orange,
Raspberries, Watermelon, Onions, Carrots, Cucumber, Potatoes, Radish,
Apples, Beetroot, Sweet potatoes, Banana, Orange peel, Pineapple,
Cherries, etc.
3) Some proteins can be obtained in eggs, milk, fish or chicken,
seeds as beniseeds (sesame seeds) cheese or cottage cheese.
The first stage of treatment to include mainly: - 1) Raw and fresh
vegetables and fruits first thing in the morning, carrots and bedroof
juice is essential.
2) The fruit of immature coconut is anemic food.
3) Pawpaw (papaya) and banana are highly delicious. Cleanse the bowel
of the child and introduce garlic or onions as worm expeller.
Massage is important.
Vitamin D is essential.
Second stage to include vitamins and minerals to rebuild the body.
Diarrhea must be then have been stopped.

Original_Search_Title: Consumers Experience Relative Stability in Foodstuff Prices
Headline: Nigeria: Consumers Experience Relative Stability in Foodstuff Prices
Body_Text:
Lagos — Consumers' prices for edible items particularly foodstuffs in
Lagos markets have maintained some level of stability, while they
exhibit a slight difference in other places.
Consumers attribute the level of price stability to weaken purchasing
power and inflation rate which has continued to go down to a single
digit since December 1999.
In most major markets in Lagos, a big tuber of yam costs an average
of N120, medium size tuber of yam sells for N90 while a small tuber
of yam sells at N60. A bag of rice sells at an average price of
N2,500, a tin is sold at between N650 - N750, while a mudu or olodo
sells at between N160 - N180, just as a derica cup of rice sells for
between N40 - N45.
A bag of beans in most contemporary Lagos markets sells at between
N4,500- N5,200, a tin sells at N600-N650, while a derica cup goes for
between N40-N45.
A bag of garri sells at between N2,000 - N2,100 while a tin sells at
N200. A medium size basket of fresh tomatoes sells at between N700 -
N900 while a mudu/olodo of crayfish sells at between N200 - N250.
Onions has gone down completely as a mudu of it sells at N50, this
has to do with its seasonal nature as the same quantity could not be
bought with N200 December last year.
Vegetable oil, especially the imported ones have forced the
indigenous ones to go down as both compete for consumers attention in
the market. A gallon of it sells at an average cost of N450. Palm
oil, instead of going down has continued to increase in price. A
bottle of palm oil now sells for N70 instead of N60 this time last
year.
A bag of Egusi sells for N6,700, a tin sells at N900, then mudu/olodo
sells for N70 while a cup is N20, In a like manner, ogbono/ogbolo
sells at N28,000 per bag, while a tin sells at N8,000, then a mudu
and a cup sell at N800 and N100 respectively.
An analysis of the items index from the federal office of statistics
in Lagos shows that the composite consumer price index which measures
changes in the average level of retail prices paid by consumers was
3347.2 in March 200. This represented an increase of 1.1 per cent
above 3317.6 recorded in February 2000. Some of the items that
experienced upward price movement included drinks, kerosine and
household goods. The urban and rural all item indices registered
increases of 0.7 and 1.2 per cent respectively.
Compared with the average consumer price level in March 1999, the
composite Consumer Price Index for March 2000 went down by 1.4 per
cent. The drop over the period, which means that retail prices of
consumer goods and services, on the average, were selling below their
corresponding level a year ago, were largely caused by items such as
millet, guinea corn, maize, kerosine (open market price) and cooking
gas. By making similar comparison, the urban and rural indices fell
by 1.4 per cent in each sectors.
The average annual rate of rise of consumer retail prices or the
percentage change in the average composite CPI for the twelve month
period ending March 2000 was 2.7. This means that a fixed collection
of consumer goods and services purchased with N100 on the average
during the twelve months period ending March 1999 sold for N102.7 on
the average during a corresponding period ending March, 2000.
Food Index
The composite food index advanced by 0.6 per cent in March, 2000.
Increases in the prices of staples, vegetable and fruits were largely
responsible for the rise in the index. Compared with the level of the
index in March, 1999, the Composite Food Index for March, 2000 went
down by 2.7 per cent and the percentage change in the average of the
index for the twelve months period ending March, 2000, was a drop of
0.6 per cent.
All Items less Farm Produce
The index for "All Items Less Farm Produce" which measures charges in
the level of retail prices consumers pay for services and
manufactured goods rose by 1.8 per cent in March, 2000. The level of
the index was zero per cent above the corresponding level in March,
1999. The percentage change in the average of the index for the
twelve months period, March, 2000 was 8.3.

Original_Search_Title: Yam Cultivation Causes Environmental Degradation
Headline: Ghana: Yam Cultivation Causes Environmental Degradation
Body_Text:
Accra — Yam cultivation leads to a high degree of environmental
degradation, a three-month field study in the Sekyere West and
Ejura-Sekyedumase Districts of Ashanti has established.
Presenting the findings at a day's workshop at Fumesua, near Kumasi,
at the weekend, Mr. Joseph Nketia Berchie, a Research Officer of the
Crop Research Institute (CRI) said this was so because yam depletes
the soil of nitrogen.
The study was part of year 2000 programme of the International Centre
for Development Oriented Research in Agriculture (ICRA 2000) based in
Holland.
He said that since yam would not give a good yield when cultivated on
the same field twice, farmers do a lot of shifting cultivation.
Also the barks of trees on the farms are peeled off and fire set to
them to destroy the foliage so that yams can have enough light and
the tendrils can climb them.
He said the situation could be avoided if yam farmers would use
fertiliser but they claim that the yams would lose their original
taste and cannot be stored for long which he said, is not true.
Unilever reduces water pollution
Unilever Ghana Limited (UGL) has taken measures to check
environmental pollution, Mr. Micheal Charamba, Technical Director of
UGL stated at the week- end.
Speaking at a durbar to climax activities marking the Safety, Health
and Environment week at Tema, he said the company has since 1995
reduced its water pollution by 96 percent, solid waste by 39 percent
and energy use by 21 percent.
The week was under the theme, "Achieving a sustainable growth with
cost effective quality products in a safe and healthy environment".
In view of this, a one billion cedis effluent treatment plant was
commissioned in 1988 to reduce the impact of palm oil in liquid
effluent on the Chemu Lagoon.
Mr. Charamba said UGL is commissioning a physical refinery plant that
will further help to reduce energy consumption and reduce the water
effluent load.
In order to achieve these, the Technical Director said the company is
training its employees to develop sustained interest in safety,
health and environmental activities and to reward performance.
Mr. Edwin Barnes, Chief Director of the Ministry of Environment
Science
and Technology (MEST) urged industries to observe pollution
prevention and sound environmental degradation control mechanisms as
well as safety in order to maximise productivity.
Senior Citizens plant trees to mark day
Senior Citizens in Akwapim North District last Saturday observed the
Senior Citizens Day with a tree planting exercise at Akyeremanteng
Tourist Complex near Akan Falls in the district.
The Senior Citizens and some holidaymakers, who visited the tourist
complex planted 120 seedlings including acacia and mahogany, which
were supplied by the District assembly and the District Forestry
Office.
In an address at a reception, Nana Otu Pebi, Akwapim-Mamponghene
called on the government to come out with far reaching policies that
would make farming economically attractive to the youth and help stop
their constant drift from the land to other sectors of the economy.
He explained that if the current trend is allowed to continue, very
soon most of the youth would leave their family farmlands to the big
towns only to return later to find such lands occupied by strangers
and rich commercial farmers.
NGO sponsors Hippo sanctuary
A British NGO is sponsoring three research teams to conduct an
ecological survey at the Wechiau Hippopotamus sanctuary in the Upper
West Region.
Apart from building office accommodation facilities, the survey is
aimed at developing the sanctuary as a tourist attraction. Mr.
William Agambire, Regional Manager of the Ghana Tourist Board (GTB),
said this during the Fourth Tourism Awards Night held at Wa last
Saturday.
The area, which has different types of Hippopotami and birds, was
among 17 sites, hotels, restaurants and individuals that received
awards.
Mr. Agambire said the people of Wechiau had demonstrated their
enthusiasm in the development of the sanctuary by constructing two
local lodges at different locations for overnight stays and
stopovers.
The region has 17 hotels with 260 rooms and 334 beds, he said, and
called on tourists to take advantage of the facilities to visit the
region.
Council Chairman expresses concern
In spite of a by-law by the Techiman District Assembly against
environmental pollution particularly that of water bodies, human
activities continue to endanger the Tano River and streams such as
Atweradaa and Afiaso. People deliberately throw human waste in the
drains, which end up in the River.
Speaking to the Ghana News Agency (GNA), Mr. Kwadwo Dekyi, Chairman
of Techiman Urban Council, expressed concern about the way people
construct places of convenience and bathrooms in lanes and behind
their buildings.
He said that apart from the dumping of human waste, the river and the
two streams have been littered with used polythene bags and
containers and advised hawkers to assist in ridding the area of items
which pollute the environment.
Mr. Dekyi appealed to the District Assembly to increase the number of
garbage containers in the town and to enforce environmental laws
strictly.
He warned that the Council would soon mount an exercise to track down
people who pollute the environment.

Original_Search_Title: Okpokwu Local Government Is Working Again
Headline: Nigeria: Okpokwu Local Government Is Working Again
Body_Text:
Lagos — Comrade Abba Moro is one of the victims of the Late General
Sani Abacha tyrannical rule. On three occasions, he was disqualified
from contesting in elections to the chairmanship seat in Okpokwu
Local Government of Benue State. However, in December last year, he
emerged as the Executive chairman of the council after a landslide
victory in a free and fair election "The man of the people," as he is
fondly called spoke to SUNDAY Orinya on his one year in office.
Excerpts
Q: After series of disqualifications on spurious grounds by the
Abacha Administration you were finally elected last year as a
chairman of Okpokwu Local Government. How has it been in the last one
year as Chief executive?
A: I feel very fine that I won the election. Particularly I feel good
that I have been able, in a very modest way, to touch the lives of
the people of the Local Government that stood steadfastly by me
during the period of my travails. The only problem is that I feel sad
that the system is not operating, seriously speaking, the way I
expected. All in all, I feel very glad that I have been elected by my
people and I have been able to touch their lives in terms of
infrastructural facilities.
Q: You talked of the system not working the way you had expected.
What do you mean by that.?
A: I believe that the process of governance is a collective process,
and it involves everybody. From the man on the executive seat to the
man in the legislative arm of government, and much more importantly,
the ordinary person in the streets. I had a feeling and I still have
that feeling that governance is supposed to be a process of
continuous consultation, so much so that every policy of government
should be informed from inputs from every segment of the society. I
feel very sad that the military mentality, the siege mentality under
which we had operated is still permeating the democratic set up. That
is why I'm very, very sad about it.
Q: To what level has this affected your operation in the last one
year?
A: At my own level, apart from the psychological frustration that I
feel once in a while it has not in anyway affected me much. I operate
in a manner that is open. All the key officials of my local
government and councillors come along with me. And in the siting and
the execution of projects, the people have been actively involved. At
my own level I feel very very fine. I don't have any problem at all.
Q: Your tenacity in achieving your ambition of becoming a Local
Government Chairman after series of disqualifications must have been
informed by a particular desire for the people. After one year in
office how far have you gone in meeting this desire?
A: As you have rightly pointed out, three times I was disqualified
from contesting election. And on each of these occasions, majority of
the people of the Local Government stood behind me. The last occasion
was like everybody had died in the Local Government. And so that
informed the tenacity with which I pursued my election the fourth
time when I eventually won. So like I said earlier, basically in
virtually all wards of the Local Government I have been able to
provide a level of health facilities. In areas of critical need we
have been able to provide water - surface water projects and bore
holes. In more than two third of the wards of the Local Government we
have been able to provide bridges and culverts to open the rural
areas to the centres. We have also been able to rehabilitate
virtually every vehicle that is in the Local Government. We have been
able to provide some recreational facilities for the people. In that
way, I am satisfied that the people are satisfied that the Local
Government is working. I feel fine and challenged to do more.
Q: If you look back to the performance of your predecessors how would
you say they operated?
A: When I came to the Local Government on assumption of office, I
discovered that everybody was talking about 'this is the way we do
it.' The way we do it in their own context is taking money from the
Federation account and plotting how to share it. As a matter of fact,
I discovered that 23 years after the creation of my Local government
there is nothing of substance on the ground to show for its old age.
And one of the first tasks that I embarked upon was the complete
re-orientation of the people, particularly the staff of the Local
Government, to see the need of doing things the correct way in terms
of erection of its physical structures in the Local Government and
meeting the basic needs of the people. I am happy that right now in
Okpokwu Local Government everybody is convinced that work has to be
done. There is a complete transformation from the human being to the
physical aspect in terms of rehabilitation, renovation and
reconstruction of the entire facilities. Virtually all abandoned
projects like water and health facilities have been completed.
Q: You must notice some particular areas that the Local Government is
deficient, how have you been able to address this?
A: Like I said earlier, Local Government Administration generally is
supposed to be a grassroot administration, and in that regard, I
discovered on assumption of office that due to lack of focus, nobody
appears to know where they were going and where they were coming
from. Of course, just like every other segment of the Nigerian
society, government has been a means of making quick money and
getting rich. Infact, because of the low industrial base of the
society government has become a veritable avenue for making people
millionaires overnight.
When I assumed office, I consciously put in place a policy of
re-focusing programme of administration. As the closest government to
the people, there was and there is still the need for us to focus on
projects that have direct bearing with the needs of the people. That
is essentially what we have been doing.
Q: As one of the oldest local Governments in the State, it is
disappointing that there is no industry in your local Government.
What have you done in this respect?
A: Since my assumption of office I have attended two trade fairs. On
each occasion, I have been rated highly by organisers of the trade
fairs. Having expended a lot of money at the trade fairs, I can't see
any thing to show for it. I will continue to announce to the people
of the world that if Benue State is the food basket of Nigeria, then
Okpokwu Local Government is the food basket of Benue State. There is
virtually no agricultural product that is not produced in abundance,
ranging from yam to cassava to rice and what have you. A lot of
mineral deposits abound in the Local Government. We have Gypsum in
Anmoda, large deposit of Salt in Ichama area. All these things abound
in the Local Government. I keep on insisting that people just have to
come forward. Somehow, I believe that especially in the areas of
trade fairs, the organisers are supposed to expose the potentials of
rural communities especially the Local Governments that participate.
On the part of the Local Government, we have been able to establish
some level of industrial layout and if anybody comes, we have
industrial plots free of charge. We also give tax holidays. One
transporter is already enjoying that facility . All that we can do at
the moment is expose the industrial potential of the Local Government
in terms of abundant agricultural raw materials and mineral deposits.
On our part we have invited an organisation that is basically
concerned with the establishment of agro based cottage industries.
They have sent in proposal for the establishment of a fruit juice
industry. We also intend to meet the basic needs of the people like
establishing a block industry. This is to meet the needs of the
people and also our modest contribution to the Federal Government
Poverty Alleviation Programme. We hope that it will reduce
unemployment and rural-urban drift. These things don't even cost
much. By the estimate we have, N1.4 million can give us a cottage
beverage industry. And I think the Local Government can afford this.
Q: Why are you adopting this multi-faceted approach in addressing
problems of the local government instead of tackling them one after
the other? Are you going to narrow down to specifies later?
A: I came to the inevitable conclusion of the multi-faceted approach
to the development of the area because I keep insisting that if you
go down to the grassroot every problem that is conceivable is in the
local government. The school system is collapsing, social facilities
are lacking, like road network and where they exist, they are in
deplorable conditions. The health system is in bad shape. I feel it
will be unfair to the ordinary person because if you move from one
community to the other, the problems reproduce and replicate
themselves. And so if you come to a place and you say let me choose
health, the roads are simply not there. It will be unfair to just
concentrate on uplifting the health system at the expense of the
road. Besides, the school system also cries for attention. This is
the reason why we have approached the provision of facilities in that
manner. So far we have made some modest extra effort. Probably after
24 months, we may be thinking of going to specifics.
Q: You seem to have invested much on building of market stalls. What
is this intended to achieve both in short and long term for the
council.
A: One of the factors that constitute serious drain to the revenue of
the local government both internally generated and the federal
allocation is the salary of workers. On assumption of office I
discovered that internal revenue of the local government was first on
average of N140,000 a month. Having realised that much is not
expected from the federation account as it was then operated, I
decided on a few areas that I think will be able to generate funds
for substantial part of the wages of workers so that we can put the
balance from the Federation Account on the development of rural
areas. It is partly for that reason, as you have rightly observed
that we have embarked upon the aggressive development of markets to
generate revenue principally.
Secondly, because of our rural nature and the poor road network we
want to avail the common man the avenue of disposing the abundant
agricultural produce in the market. We have also introduced a novel
mass transit system to help in the movement of goods and people using
trucks. We have ordered for a few trucks. The Local Government
filling station has also been reactivated to bring additional revenue
to the Local Government. All-in all, I have a target of one million
naira monthly as from the next few months. This will complement
whatever we get from Federation account which is not static. Since we
don't have any control over the Federal allocation, our policy is to
look inward to rake in additional revenue.
Q: From what you have seen in the last one year do you think the
present revenue formula is favourable to the third tier of
Government?
A: Speaking the minds of most of my colleagues, the allocation of
revenue to the local Governments is arbitrary. What we get from the
Federation Account is not known by anybody apart from Federal
Government officials, whatever acrues to the Federation account
nobody knows. When they give you your allocation they never tell you
how much accrued to the Federation account. They never tell you
anything. All you have to know is that you have been allocated this
amount this month. You have no way of knowing. Arbitrary deductions
for primary school education, whatever, for loans that were taken for
the Local Government without the knowledge of the Local Government.
At the end of the day, what they give to you is a mere pittance, and
the revenue allocation formula is grossly weighed against Local
Governments. Virtually every problem of the society is in the Local
Government. The health care system that needs urgent attention is in
the Local Government. The primary education sector- that industry
producing leaders of tomorrow is in the Local Government. The need to
move, road problems and all that, they are all in the Local
Government. Yet the Local Government takes the least of allocation. I
keep insisting that this money should go to where there are problems.
As a matter of fact, I'm of the opinion that the Local Government
should be given 30 percent from the Federal account. That is yet to
be actualised but I feel strongly that revenue allocation to the
local government is grossly inadequate to develop the rural areas
because more than 80 percent of the Nigerian population is in the
rural areas and in the Local Government.
Q: What have you noticed in terms of the level of autonomy enjoyed by
the Local Government under this democratic set up?
A: The most absurd document that I have ever seen in my life is the
1999 constitution as far as the Local Government System in a
democratic set up is concerned. It took a lot of resources of the
State to put that document together and, honestly in anticipation of
this democratic rule, I had expected a fantastic document. But
unfortunately, the most useless constitution we have ever had had is
the 1999 constitution as far as it relates to the Local Government.
It is a complete negation of the reformed Local Government System.
Local Government, was just mentioned in passing. Just section 7. And
at best what happened is that they just said the Local Government is
the property of the State. Under this arrangement, the Local
Government is at the mercy of the State and they can do whatever they
want to do with the Local Government. That is a very sad commentary
on the constitutional development of this country. But we hope that
when the national assembly gets down to business to review the
constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, they will remove
this stupid provision for the Local Government.
Here in Benue State, there is no serious tampering with the operation
of the Local Governments for now. For the moment there is some level
of autonomy here apart from a few hiccups here and there. That of
course, could be as a result of the kind of person who is in power.
If not there is every temptation for somebody to do the absurd by
encroaching on the finances and workings of the Local Government. You
may be aware that in some states some houses of assembly have picked
two years as tenure of councils. That is a stupid thing to do. I have
spent one year in office and I know what has accrued to the Local
Government in terms of finances. I know that you can not, seriously
speaking, start any serious business of administration in the Local
Government and complete it within the period they are contemplating.
If that happens and they say every Local Government council should go
after two years, I can assure you that every local government
landscape will be littered with abandoned projects. Because, as you
are all aware, only a few discerning persons tend to create new ideas
and new structures as soon as they assume office. The best thing that
will happen to this country is to allow elected officials enough time
to stay in office to execute projects.
Q: What period do you think is sufficient?
A: Well, there is no time that is sufficient for completing projects.
But like I said before, problems abound. But I think, sufficiently,
given the presidential system that we are operating, four years is an
ideal period, an ideal tenure for elected officials especially the
executive arm. My experience has shown that you use six months or two
years to learn the ropes, the next two years you put structures on
the ground and you use the last year, the fourth year to consolidate
and finish the projects and then you can pack your bags and baggages
and go.
Even if it means giving one tenure to elected officials, I personally
do not mind. But at least at any particular point, sufficient time
should be given to somebody to start something and be able to finish.
Q: Looking back to the last one year, would you say there are things
you have left undone or things you did which you shouldn't have done?
A: Like every human being, I'm not perfect. I know people may come
and say we should have done this and we shouldn't have done that. Of
course given the background that some of us have found ourselves, I
feel that one way or the other, we have put a few steps wrong. Since
it is a little early we are trying to adjust ourselves. In most cases
greater number of the people you come across are a little selfish.
Most of the things they will say you have done and not done are
things that will not favour them. But like in every human endeavour,
definitely there are hiccups here and there. Sometimes you wake up
from bed and even mere talking to people, you say things to people
that you think ordinarily you shouldn't have said and at the end of
the day, you are full of regrets.
Given the circumstances, the pressure under which we are working,
because everybody is excited about democracy, so somebody can even
walk to an elected officials bedroom and say this room is now longer
your own. I'm sure that there are a few things that some of us are
supposed to have done that we have not done, but we will try to
adjust.
Q: You are one of the worst victims of military dictatorship in Benue
State owing to your democratic belief. Do you still hold democracy in
high esteem?
A: First of all I have insisted that I'm a democrat. A democrat to
the extent that organisations and government are representatives of
the people. I have also served in the national assembly of the
Federal Republic of Nigeria as an aide to a prominent senator and I
have discovered that resources - financial, human, and whatever, -
abound in this country. Experience also has shown that few persons in
Government, in military arrangement, cornered these resources. And so
overnight you have millionaires and billionaires all over the place.
The majority of the Nigerian populace wallow in abject poverty. That
has virtually informed my agitation and struggle for a long time.
In democracy, because of the scope of representation, resources of
the country reach out to a greater number of the people. For
instance, you have 29 members of the Benue House of Assembly. We have
23 Local Government chairmen and you have an average of fifteen
councillors in each of the Local Governments. All these people
represent the people. At the end of the month, a single councillor
that has a salary of N20,000.00 is going to serve the purpose of over
forty people -dependants, parents, relations etc. In the military
arrangement, apart from members of the executive council, it is the
governor and his aides. And they say cut down the size of the exco
and the rest of them and he just cut it down leaving a very few to
partake in the sharing of the resources. And so, I have that strong
feeling that the military, definitely, is an aberration. It is an
institution that serves only the interest of a few cabals. It doesn't
serve the interest of the generality of the people. No matter how
democracy is, I have a string feeling that it accommodates the people
much more than military rule.
If for anything, democracy is synonymous with freedom. Freedom to
move, to approach, freedom to participate even by representation in
the process of Government. And so to that extent there is everything
that we can cheer about in democracy. And I want to insist that given
the process of a country like Nigeria, the learning process, the
process of imbibing the ethos of democracy normally takes a long
period. Don't mind that a few persons are a little selfish. They cry
for heavens to fall when their interests are affected only to peter
away when it is the turn of another person.

Original_Search_Title: Discovering Accra: The Unsung Heroes
Headline: Ghana: Discovering Accra: The Unsung Heroes
Body_Text:
Accra — When we talk about economic prime movers our minds readily go to the
mining sector, the industrial sector, the tourism sector and the agricultural
sector which seems to have been placed on the back burner. The finance sector
where the banks readily come to mind has also been identified as a great source
of economic activity.
Government seems to be hell-bent on divesting itself of participation in
most of the sectors, only concentrating on monitoring for effective tax
returns. Private sector investors are buying the divested enterprises; a sector
described as the engine of growth. Agreeably, centrally controlled economies
all over the world are giving way to open market forces and Ghana is no
exception.
The Chartered Institute of Marketing Ghana has instituted annual awards to
honour companies and individuals that perform very creditably to the growth of
the economy in one area or the other. This could come up for commendation as it
could serve as incentive enough to goad the stakeholders to lift up their
performance and enhance greater economic growth.
During the demonstration organised by the political parties outside of
government on November 25 last year, a very senior police officer told The
Accra Mail that he was touched by the activities of our compatriots who bring
foodstuff to the cities from the hinterland. According to him, when he and his
colleagues were detailed on patrol from about 2 o'clock in the morning, he saw
the number of women perched on top of trucks full of agricultural produce to
feed the millions of people in Accra. The number of young men who were carting
foodstuff to the markets and the drivers who had had to drive all night long to
bring food to the city dwellers touched him immensely.
The police officer was candid enough to point out that these people are the
heroes who society will always fail to honour. "This country honours the
farmer, but who gives a damn how the farm produce gets to the consumer? These
poor women have husbands and children, but they spend long nights carting food
to feed us and we fail to honour them," he lamented to The Accra Mail. He was
emphatic that there were names nominated for the much-hyped up Millennium
Awards that did not deserve mention at all.
Mentioning the tourism sector brings to mind the efforts being made by the
sector ministry and its affiliate agencies to upgrade facilities to boost the
tourism industry. These are the hotels, restaurants and fast-food joints and
what are traditionally known as chop-bars. Laudable as this may be, one area we
have all lost sight of include those who sell food items at all turns within
our capital.
Some of these people sell roast plantain, corn, yam and other items like tea
and bread. A great number of foreign tourists to the country prefer to identify
with the diet of the ordinary Ghanaians. They patronise things like tea and
bread, fufu and soup, kenkey and fish; a complete departure from what our
tourism experts perceive of these tourists. Every corner of Accra has one or
more of such facilities. Are these people recognised as contributing anything
to the economy of our country? If they are, what is there to show for it?
Every civil servant patronises these people for snacks each day of the
working week. Their bosses send for roast plantain and groundnuts each
lunchtime, yet the lot of these service providers hardly improves. Some
overzealous city officials go to the extent of driving these people away from
the very people who patronise their wares at the Ministries and Departments.
Who will sing their song for them? Who is the voice of these voiceless people,
one may ask?
Coupled with the harsh weather conditions under which some of them prepare
and sell their wares is the seasonal shortage of their raw materials. When
plantain is out of season, these women will either have to fold their business
up till the next season or look for alternatives to survive on. Others simply
close shop, period! But these are people who make the world of human survival
go on, insignificant as their contributions might seem.

Original_Search_Title: IRIN-WA Update of events in West Africa
Headline: West Africa: IRIN-WA Update of events in West Africa
Body_Text:
Abidjan — NIGER: Soldiers accused of attempted kidnapping - Eight soldiers
have been charged with the recent failed kidnap of Djibrilla Hamidou Hima, a
spokesman of the country's defunct military government, AFP reported their
lawyer as saying on Tuesday. They are charged with "arbitrary illegal
confinement and association with criminals," the lawyer said.
Four other soldiers allegedly involved in the kidnap on 10 June were
released on Saturday. Hima, too, was released by his captors a day after they
seized him.
NIGERIA: State carrier resumes Accra flight
Nigeria Airways resumed its Lagos-Accra route on Monday after three years of
suspension, AFP reported an airline spokesman as saying on Tuesday. The airline
has agreed with Ghana Airways that for three flights a week, passengers with a
Nigeria Airways ticket can board a Ghana Airways flight and vice-versa. Nigeria
Airways suspended the route in 1997 due to bad management, corruption and huge
foreign debts. The state carrier is currently under investigation by the
government in its bid to eliminate corruption, AFP reported.
GHANA: Corruption on the increase
Corruption is on the increase in Ghana, the country's Serious Fraud Office
said in its recently released report for 1999. The acting executive director of
the bureau, Brian Sapati, said "grand and petty" corruption and economic crimes
were increasing because people would always take advantage of Ghana's
liberalised economic system.
BENIN: IFAD cassava development programme
The UN International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD) and Benin have
signed a US $13.11-million loan agreement for the country's Roots and Tubers
Development Programme, IFAD said on Tuesday.
The programme, which aims to diversify government export earnings, will
concentrate on identifying commercial outlets for cassava. It also aims to help
alleviate rural povery, improve the food security of rural households and
reduce the marginalisation of small farmers, particularly women, IFAD said.
Cassava, yam and sweet potato are staples of household food security. The
country's production of roots and tubers, which acounts for two-thirds of all
food produced annually, is concentrated in small farming sectors.
The IFAD-initiated programme, which cost some US $19.28 million, will be co-
financed by the West African Development Bank, the Benin government and
beneficiaries.
This item is delivered by the UN's IRIN humanitarian information unit (e-
mail: irin@ocha.unon.org; fax: +254 2 622129; Web:
http://www.reliefweb.int/IRIN), but may not necessarily reflect the views of
the United Nations. If you re-print, copy, archive or re-post this item, please
retain this credit and disclaimer.

Original_Search_Title: BBC Management Opposes Cementia
Headline: Nigeria: BBC Management Opposes Cementia
Body_Text:
Lagos — The feud between the Benue State government and the Bureau
for Public Enterprises (BPE), following the choice of Dangote Group as a
core investor in the Benue Cement Company (BCC) has taken a dramatic
dimension as the management of BCC has petitioned the Federal Government
that it would resist the come back of Cementia/Lafarge to the company.
The Director-General of the BPE, Alhaji Nasir El-Rufai, made this
disclosure Saturday in a chat with the newsmen in Kaduna after the
Federal Radio Corporation of Nigeria (FRCN), hosted him as the Guest of
a popular Hausa programme, Hannu Dayawa (meaning too many hands).
Said he: "I have a nine-page letter from the management of Benue
Cement saying that they will never work with Cementia because Cemential
abandoned them and insulted them in 1985 and said that when they leave
they would not be able to produce cement but bake Gboko yams."
El-Rufai, therefore, stated that the brouhaha by the Benue State
people as a result of the sale of the 35 per cent Federal Government's
shares to Dangote remained sheer "hypocrisy and the reasons being given
by Benue State government is all a bundle of nonsense and chasing
shadows."
The director-general argued that the opponents of Dangote's core
investorship were doing so not out of genuine concern but because "he is
a Fulani Moslem, pure and simple," describing such people as
"ethnic/tribalists" who do not believe that "every Nigerian has an
opportunity to go and live anywhere in Nigeria and invest anywhere in
Nigeria."
He described Dangote as a whizkid who has the managerial capability
and expertise to turn around the moribund cement company.
"Dangote is qualified. He has come with sound technical partners. He
gave us highest price and in our view he is a young man that should be
encouraged to continue investing anywhere in Nigeria. He has more of his
investment and facilities in Lagos. He has done very well. He has
employed people there. He has created jobs. His activities in
commodities sector has brought down the prices of rice and sugar and we
hope he will use his acumen to bring down the prices of cement," he
enthused.
El-Rufai also declared that the entire transaction which favoured
Dangote Group was transparent.
"The people that oppose him are just being tribalistic. They are not
looking at the facts. What we have done was totally transparent and I
challenge anyone to tell us what we did wrong," adding, "the Federal
Government is free to sell its shares to anyone that it wants. We are
not going to sell shares to Cementia at N4.50 when we have a Nigerian
willing to buy it at N5.35. We are not mad," he stated tersely.

Original_Search_Title: Customer Service is Our Strength
Headline: Nigeria: Customer Service is Our Strength
Body_Text:
Lagos — Dr. Adebodun Sanyaolu, marketing director of Livestock Feeds
PLC says that excellent customer service is his company's weapon in the
highly competitive feeds production market.
Q: What is the current relationship between your company and
Neimeth?
A: Well, before the divestment of foreign partners, Livestock Feeds
and Pfizer (now Neimeth) were sister companies. Then, Pfizer Products
PLC was into the production of pharmaceuticals while Livestock Feeds
was responsible for animal feeds and care. In 1997 the Americans
divested their shares in the two companies to key staff and Nigerian
shareholders in a management buy out. Basically, we are both sister
companies under one management but now we are two separate companies
under different managements.
Q: Is there no more business links between the two firms?
A: We still have business links, because the Pfizer phamaceutical
plant is under Neimeth. We are in charge of animal drugs but Neimeth
manufactures for us.
Q: How has the business of livestock feeds been in the country?
A: Well, like any manufacturing concern we face a lot of problems. A
lot of policies affect us. All the policies that affect manufacturers
also affect us in terms of input and declining sales. Our business is
dependent on the population. Now we have a tiny market as a result of
diminishing purchasing power of the population. Consumers have little
money to purchase products.
Q: How has the importation of frozen foods such as chicken and
turkey affected livestock feeds in Nigeria?
A: Obviously, you find out that frozen chickens affect our business.
They are imported. Our farmers here cannot compete with the foreign
farmers. That is why the volume of business in livestock production is
declining. The volume of work in the farm is going down and this
affects our sales. Consumers want to buy cheap things not minding how
they are produced. So they buy imported frozen chicken that is cheaper,
not minding the health implications. If farmers cannot sell their
products, it means they too will not patronise us. That is why I'm
calling on the government to ban the importation of frozen foods
instead of the high tariff placed on them. The fact is that most of
these frozen foods don't come through our ports, they come in through
smuggling. So, it means whatever money government thinks of making from
the high tariff is being lost to smugglers. Nigerian farmers can
produce sufficiently for the populace if they are encouraged. The
facilities and infrastructure to do this are there.
Q: You mean our farmers can adequately provide the chickens and
turkey needed in the country?
A: Yes, I believe so. In fact, there was a time in the past that we
were able to do so. Most of the farms that used to have 100,000 fowls
now have between 10,000 and 20,000 because the cost of production is
too high while the money they make from it is small. In fact people are
not buying chicken. They believe it is more expensive than frozen fish.
You see because the poverty level is higher now, the disposable income
of an average consumer is low. He is looking at chicken as a luxury. So
he is not interested in it. Even people don't buy eggs again. They
either eat their yam with pepper or oil because they believe that the
cost of eggs is high. The cost is high because production cost is high.
If production cost is made cheaper by government through low interest
rates and easy access to loan facilities and other incentives, farmers
will be able to produce in large quantity at lower costs.
Now at high production cost if you produce more chickens there will
be a glut. So also if you are producing eggs you cannot keep them for
long when there is no favourable market. You just have to dispose of
them else they go bad. And there is no incentive to take care of the
loss that may arise. Hence, many poultry farms are under lock and key
though the infrastructure are still in place. You can be sure that if
there is a favourable environment people will produce.
Q: There has been an upsurge in the number of players in livestock
feeds production. How has this affected your company?
A: Well, competition is to make you more efficient and face
challenges. The only problem we have is that the market is shrinking.
We have a small market to chase. The competition is higher especially
with people trying to find short cuts and what you find out is that
there are smaller mills where farmers can go for milling at a cheaper
prices irrespective of the quality of the feed. When we saw that the
competition was getting keener with everybody trying to find a market
niche for their products, we made strategic plans so that farmers
continue to patronise us. We have an edge over our competitors through
strengthened customer service. We have animal health drugs and keep
professionals on the field to offer additional services to farmers.
Q: What is the relationship between your company and these other
feed millers?
A: Well, for the major players in the industry we have an
association which meets once in a month during which we discuss matters
affecting our operations. The only people we don't relate with are feed
millers who are not registered but produce feeds anyhow without
considering quality. We appeal to government that there is need to
register feed millers and that there must be a minimum standard of
quality.
Q: How do you think the current wage increase will affect livestock
feed mills?
A: Well, we believe that if there is more money in the system people
will be able to buy what they like instead of the 0-1-0 or 0-1-1 mode
of feeding to which many people are used. We expect that there will be
more demand for chicken and if there is more demand for livestock it
means there will be more demand for feeds by farmers.

Original_Search_Title: June 12 And Enemies Within Democracy
Headline: Nigeria: June 12 And Enemies Within Democracy
Body_Text:
Lagos — History is after all a very useful subject. No matter how
disguised, no matter the amount of secrecy, no matter the magnitude of
bribes at the point of committing an ignoble act, history unstoppably
catches up with agents of reprehensible deeds and passes its cruel
verdict.
That has been the lot of those who signed away in secret the June 12
Presidential victory and until each of the men {thank God there was no
woman except late Justice Ikpeme of doubtful memory} can produce
evidence to the contrary, they will remain in the Black Book of
Nigeria's history.
The scoop in The Guardian of June 11, 2000 was not entirely news to
many in the Nigerian media. Many of those who signed away Nigeria's
peace, progress and prosperity by signing away the expressed wishes of
over 14 million Nigerian citizens, were known. But now, the
comprehensive list of the makers of Abacha, the harbingers of woes for
millions, bringers of premature deaths for hundreds and false
imprisonments for a lucky few, have been named. Stand up for
identification please, Augustus Aikhomu Babangida's dupe 'civilian' No
2 and Shonekan of the illegal National Government (ING).
Alhaji Rimi, Dr. Olusola Saraki, Dr. Ahmed Kusamotu, Bushir Dalhatu,
Tom Ikimi, Joseph Toba, Joe Nwodo {a reluctant signatory}, Okey Uzoho,
Dr. Bawa Slka, Theo Nikire, Prof. Eyo Ita and Bola Afonja should also
join them. Also to stand up for identification are Y. Auka, Abba
Muntala Mohammed, Alhaji Ramalan, Alhaji Halilu Maina and Dr. Odunze.
The soldiers, Brig. Gen. Ukpo, Lt. Gen. Dongonyaro and Brig. Gen John
Sagaya are also members of the gang. May the good Lord forgive deceased
Musa Yar'Adua, Abdulrahman Okene and Amos Ikulade, whose names also
occur in the list. Also to stand for identification are Senators Jim
Nwobodo {make up box and all) and Gen. David Mark of former
Babangidaist. From Gen. Obasanjo's cabinet let the ministers Adamu
Chiroma {Finance}, Tony Anenih{Works & Housing} and Dapo Sarumi {Co-
operation in Africa or whatever} and Sule Lamido{Foreign Affairs} join
their colleagues. Patrick Dele Cole who advises the President on
Foreign Affairs and Babangida's National Security Adviser, Lt. Gen.
Aliyu Mohammed who is also President Obasanjo's National Security
Adviser should join his colleagues.
These men owe Nigerians lots and lots of explanations. We need to
know why each of them still living individually and severally put their
pens on paper to sign away June 12 and sign on corruption, death,
hunger, strife, what have you. What inducements were offered by the
self-confessed evil genius? Whose mandate were they exercising at the
point in time they took their actions, since there was no meeting of
any recognised organ of the parties at that time? To think of how many
innocent souls their signatures had sentenced to gruesome deaths, how
many homes have been destroyed, how much property damaged, how far back
we have been sent as a nation on the path to progress and prosperity,
is to confront the issue in rage. But we must give them room to explain
how the yam rotted and their knives still peeled them. Probably they
were blackmailed. Probably they were misled. Probably. Probably. The
world cannot wait to hear their explanations.
But what happens until those explanations come? For those who are
dead amongst the men, we can only leave them in the courts of the
Almighty God. But a question mark hangs on the suitability of those
living to continue, unrepentant, as public office holders. There is no
guarantee that given another opportunity they will not betray the
people again as they did in the closet of the past darkness. So, the
best they should do is to resign honourably and pray for the nation's
forgiveness. There are no two ways about it. They have repudiated
democracy and have no moral justification, whatsoever, for being key
players in the new democratic process.
Of course, being politicians they would likely hang on with such
traditional excuses like quota system and the marginalisation of the
constituencies they represent. Here, the Nigerian polity should become
wiser. The polity should ask itself whether hunger, disease and want
have ever disappeared because a particular son-of-the-soil has been
installed in a government job. Besides, and this is significant, there
is no part of Nigeria today which cannot boast of ten, if not better,
of least, equal replacements for any single office holder at any level.
It is a matter of conscience. May be those concerned have facts we
don't now know which will change their present public image. Let them
speak up and speak up very very loud. The nation is all ears.
Meanwhile, the press will be doing the nation a great service if it
spikes all reports on the enemies within democracy, unless the event
concerns their confessions and sincere apologies.

Original_Search_Title: Poverty Alleviation And Feeding The Nation
Headline: Nigeria: Poverty Alleviation And Feeding The Nation
Body_Text:
Lagos — Any Nation that cannot feed itself cannot really claim to be
free. Real independence is in self-sufficiency in food production and
the people living above poverty line. Nigeria has a very high potential
to achieve these noble objectives if only a number of issues are
aggressively addressed.
With an estimated population of around 100 million, Nigeria is
predominately an agrarian state. It has undulating landmass of
924,000km2 that spans the West African subregion. 75 per cent of
Nigerians live in rural areas thus farming accounts for 75 per cent of
all jobs with three-quarter of it practiced on patches of less than two
hectares on a small scale. Peasant farmers form about 95 per cent of
Nigeria's agricultural output. Integrated agriculture is the mainstay
of the people.
The hoe and the cutlass are agricultural trademarks in Nigeria, but
with the evolution of Operation Feed the Nation and the Green
Revolution, mechanized and scientific approaches are increasingly
involved. Paradoxically, OFN was launched in 1975, by the then Head of
State - General Olusegun Obasanjo (rtd). It was at a time when Nigeria
was intoxicated by the oil boom leading to mass importation and OFN
died a premature death.
Ironically, slightly more than two decades later, the same hoe is
the symbol of agricultural production in Nigeria. Coincidentally, the
President, the same person but now in a civilian government has
captured the slow pace of agricultural development in the country.
Realizing that this is a political dynamite, has come up with Poverty
Alleviation programme.
A glance at the mode of production used by the majority of our
peasant farmers indicates a future crisis in food production.
Hence the following problems must be addressed:
Unpredictable global climatic changes, low yields due to poor
production methods, poor means of conservation and preservation,
hoarding through market forces and problems of communication.
The rate at which the ozone layer and the biosphere is being
destroyed has an adverse effect on regular annual rainfall. Many
peasant farmers have little or no knowledge of whether forecasts, hence
a substantial amount of seed is wasted. Unfortunately agriculturist and
geographers can hardly lay claim to comprehensive statistically data on
retrospective rainfall patterns in the country.
Most peasant farmers have limited knowledge of land tenure,
acquisition and use. Hence, the same piece of land is tilled over the
years for mono cropping without being left fallow or having its crop
rotated. Bush burning has become prevalent thus destroying the
ecosystem and the misapplication of fertilizers has resulted in the
destruction of soil nutrients through acidification and toxification.
Some cases of peasant farmers diluting fertilizer and sprinkling it on
plants for an envisaged bumper harvest have been recorded and
documented. Peasant farmers now depend solely on commercial fertilizers
where manure made from compost of plant and animal waste could have
sufficed.
Moreover, centralized fertilizer distribution is detrimental to
effective food production in Nigeria because of the hijacking of sales
by unauthorized, greedy middlemen. Multitudes of ignorant peasant
farmers are deprived of a legitimate means of livelihood. Little
information on improved breeds is communicated to peasant livestock
farmers.
There is dearth of veterinary consulting centres and a shortage of
well-trained extension workers. Hence, wrong medication has led to a
high mortality rate in livestock.
Lack of information on modern methods of conservation and
preservation has led to substantial post-harvest losses. Perishable
agricultural commodities such as Mangoes, Tomatoes, Pineapples, Banana,
Pepper, Potatoes, Yam, Pawpaw, Kolanuts, Groundnuts, Meat, Fish, etc
rot away at the end of each harvesting season. Modern silos and large-
scale ovens are far out of the reach of peasant farmers and
paradoxically, they are located in urban centre. In addition, many
centres of agricultural production lack good roads.
Market forces have had a negative impact on the welfare of many
farmers. Inflation has infiltrated into society and greedy middlemen
have generated a great deal of confusion in the marketing processes of
our agricultural production and distribution.
Most peasant farmers have thus become lone rangers in the local
market scene instead of establishing farmer's co-operatives that can
stabilize the prices and sales of farm produce.
The majority of Nigeria farmers are illiterate peasant and TV, news
magazines, pamphlets etc, are far beyond their reach. New ideas on
agricultural techniques are not within easy reach of our numerous
peasant farmers. The ratio of agricultural extension workers to the
farming population, though not documented, is grossly inadequate.
Unfortunately, agricultural science, unlike mathematics and English
language, is not a compulsory subject in educational institutions.
Information dissemination is imperative in every areas of new
farming methods, instrumentation and implementation of agricultural
policies. The emphasis in communications in the US, China, Korea,
India, Israel, Canada, etc, has greatly boosted sustainable
agriculture.
Information is crucial to development; information should be
available to our programme, radio, exhibitions, photographs,
newspapers, magazines etc. They should be available in the local
language to peasant farmers through agricultural extension projects,
research institutes, universities, colleges, Ministry of information,
Libraries etc.
Information on global climatic conditions comes as a result of
scientific research, availability of data and sophisticated scientific
instruments. Making weather forecasts available to peasant farmers
would go a long way in preventing the waste of large amounts of grain
planted with the onset of the first disappointing rains. Moreover,
detection of underground water tables as developed in India, could be
done on a large scale in our rural farming areas.
Publicizing the effects of tree planting on farmland could also aid
farmers and the government in the promotion of afforestation projects
thus effecting positive climatic changes and boosting agricultural
production. Alley farming is another susbstainable agricultural system
which needs greater promotion.
Information on land decrees and policies which vest all lands in
government possession on behalf of the people is a welcome innovation
as this keeps land speculators at bay.
Emphasis should be placed on mixed farming and simple methods of
soil testing to establish the degree of acidity and alkalinity would go
a long way in preventing misapplication of fertilizers.
Information on improved systems of agriculture including hydro-
forestry and alley systems are considered to be relatively inexpensive.
Israel has been successful in the Negev desert and has extended this
dry-soil success to sorghum production in Botswana, Cameroon, Ethiopia
and Chad.
Agricultural co-operatives are valuable in recruiting peasant
farmers into groups which can then apply for government's assistance
and get easy access to cheap loans, inputs, simple implements etc.
In conclusion, the wealth of a country lies in its agricultural
production level. Abiola on the ideal agricultural policy believed in
according recognition to the millions of farmers who daily till the
land, harvest the farms enrich our dining tables and supply our
industries, cottage and otherwise, with their raw material
requirements. Indeed, we need a pragmatic Agricultural Policy to
optimize the agricultural potentials of Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Price War
Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Price War
Body_Text:
Lagos — Emeka Alex Duru, staff writer takes a look at the factors that gave
rise to the fuel price hike, bringing to the fore, the attendant consequences
and reaction from Nigerians.
By the close of work on Wednesday, it had become apparent that the Federal
Government of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, had failed in its last ditch efforts to
halt a scheduled nationwide strike ordered by the apex leadership of the
Nigerian Labour Congress (NLC). The indefinite sit-at-home exercise was billed
to commerce on Thursday, in the event of government delegation and the NLC
failing to reach an acceptable agreement on the outrageous new fuel prices,
unilaterally imposed by the government. The government through its major oil
marketing agency, the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation, (NNPC), had, late
last week, without prior negotiations with relevant and concerned bodies, jerked
up the prices of petroleum products by over fifty per cent. By the new price
regime, a litre of Premium Motor Spirit (P.M.S), commonly referred to as petrol,
was raised from N20 to N30. A litre of diesel was hiked to N29 from the previous
price of N19. While kerosine was jerked from N17 to N27 a litre.
Initially, not many look the rumoured price increase seriously especially as
it was not backed by any official statement from the NNPC. By Friday, a day
after the embarrassing decision was taken however, Mr. Jackson Gaius Obaseki,
the Group Managing Director (GMD) of NNPC, laboured to explain the reasons
behind the measure. Throwing up a combination of emotions and what senior
government officials referred to as the facts of the matter, the oil chief held
that the hike remained the only option for his corporation to stay afloat. He
claimed that the price increase would not have been contemplated but for the
fact that over 75 per cent of fuel consumed in the country is imported. While
this, he said, was not in his original agenda, it had to be resorted to
following the failure of the NNPC machinery. A barrel of imported crude he said,
sells at $20 instead of $10.
Given this regime of "massive importation to meet up", Obaseki said it would
only amount to self deceit for the pump price to remain at N20 per litre. "It
was obvious that if we continue the way we are going, it becomes a suicidal bid,
