he sated. Giving the impression that the government was even magnanimous in its
new price, he explained that "if we are to recover our cost today, you all know
we should be looking at a situation where you will be having between N35 and N40
per litre of petrol.
In a manner that apparently sought to replay previous governments' stated but
largely unkept pronouncements, Obaseki said the hike is temporary. According to
him, the old prices would be reverted to, sometime around August when the
nation's refineries would have been back on full stream. By the human face
approach, the NNPC obviously intended to mollify the visibly enraged Nigerians.
But this did not achieve his desired results. Shortly after the new prices
were unfolded, transports fares and costs of other basic needs hit the roof
tops. Within Lagos metropolis for instance, short distance journeys that
previously attracted fares of N5 and N10, were hiked to between N10 and N20
respectively. Between Oshodi and Apapa which used to cost N20, the fare was
jerked up to N40. Inter-city fares were also inflated. A journey from Lagos to
Ibadan that used to go for N80, was almost doubled, to between N130 and N150.
While the trunk journeys from Lagos to the East which in times of lull like now,
ordinarily cost between N600 and N1000 depending on the state of the buses,
attracted between N1000 and N1,500. As the transport fares jumped up, prices of
food stuffs and other household communities followed suit. At the popular mile
12 market in Kosofe Local Government Area, Lagos sates, tubers of yams that were
previously sold for about N50, went up to N70, while a small basket of tomatoes
that sold for N150, attracted N250. Mallam Ismaila Uman, a Hausa trader, who
attributed the price hike to the regime of fuel prices laboured in vain to hide
his frustration with the situation of things. "Oga", he referred to The Post
Express on Saturday, E no dey easy. You see this price, me, I no like am. But na
government (pronounced gwomant) e cause am. Before, we dey pay N15,000 to bring
tomato (lorry load) to Lagos, Now, the price (fare) e don go up. This one you
dey see, I bring am yesterday (Wednesday). I pay N23,000. Na him make me put
money. But (bat) I no like am, customer no dey buy", he lamented.
Elsewhere, the situation was the same. Tensions were visibly written on the
faces of Nigerians . Pockets of resistance and protests were reported in major
cities of the federation especially in the South-west. In Lagos, hoodlums and
street urchins commonly referred to as Area boys,had a field day. Apart from
joining genuine students in disrupting the sale of petroleum products, at
filling stations, they cashed in on the confusion brought by the situation, to
dispossessed innocent but stranded commuters of their belongings. All eyes
looked up to the government to take charge of the situation and rescind its
decision on the new fuel price. But nothing happened. Neither was anything
intended to happen.
So, the organised labour took up the gauntlet. An emergency meeting of the
Central Working Committee (C.W.C), of the NLC was hurriedly summoned in Lagos,
on Saturday. It was presided over by comrade Precious Kiri Kalio, the NLC vice-
president. At the meeting, the C.W.C reasoned that "the decision of the Federal
Government to increase the price of fuel was most insensitive, inconsiderate and
punitive to the citizens of the country especially the workers and the masses of
Nigerians". The increase, Labour observed was "inconsistent with the assurances
of the Federal Government that it will consult with the NLC on the very volatile
and sensitive issue of petroleum prices".
The C.W.C recalled the position of the organised labour that, should the
government effect any price increase unilaterally, the action should be resisted
with every conceivable means. It therefore regretted that the "government, by
its action has compelled organised labour to react accordingly." The union
consequently directed "all Nigerian workers to commence a sit at home strike by
Thursday, June 8 if the Federal Government (failed) to reverse the outrageous
increase".
The order was communicated to all the state branches of the congress, who, in
turn signified their intentions and resolve to abide by it. To give vent to the
directive, Adams Oshiomhole, the President of the NLC, hurriedly cut short his
visit to Geneva, Switzerland, where he was participating in the annual
International Labour Organisation (ILO) conference. By Monday, he had already
flown into the country to take charge of events. His arrival gave tremendous
impetus to the resolution of the C.W.C. True to expectation, he embarked on
fool-proof mobilisation campaign for the strike. His efforts instantly paid off.
By the close of work on Monday he had receive assurance from the NLC branches in
virtually all the states and Abuja that none was about to sabotage the strike.
Emboldened by the show of solidarity, Oshiomhole, fixed a rally at Abuja. The
Post Express on Saturday, learnt that the turnout was overwhelming. In a rare
demonstration of fraternity, workers at the Federal Government Secretariat,
abandoned their duty posts to take part in the exercise. They even asked for the
strike to commence a day earlier than scheduled.
With the tremendous support from Abuja, Oshiomhole scheduled a similar rally
for Lagos. One factor strongly worked in his favour. Majority of Nigerians were
emphatic in rejecting the new price hike. Even some of those that were thought
to be in the same corridors of power with Obasanjo, disowned him on the issue.
While the new price regime made the rounds, the National Assembly washed its
hands off the debacle. Both Dr. Chuba Okadigbo, President of the Senate and
Alhaji Ghali Na'Abba, Speaker of the House of Representatives, blamed the
Presidency for the unwarranted increase. At a deferent forum, they expressed
surprise that despite a provision of N104 billion made by the Assembly in the
year 2000 budget to subsidise fuel the Presidency went ahead to effect a price
hike.
Na'Abba was more emphatic on the topic. He said, in the 2000 budget, we
provided for N104 billion to ensure that government does not withdraw the so-
called subsidy. This, we note, was accepted by the executive.
Even during the harmonisation of the grey areas in the budget, the executive
did not raise any objection to it. We are therefore at a loss to understand the
need for the current price increase. This is nothing but a breach of trust".
Other commentators toed this line of thought. Even the Alliance for Democracy
(AD), which, of late, appeared to have assumed the ignoble role of defending
every of Obasanjo's actions, frowned at the increase. Through its national
Publicity Secretary, Valentine Ohams, the party descried the increase as a
devastating economic assault on the already impoverished Nigerians. The Academic
Staff Union of Universities (ASUU), added its informed voice to the floodgate of
criticisms. Its National President, Dr. Dipo Fashina, said that price hike
"contradicts everything that a government ought to do if it is serious about
combating poverty". Chief Gani Fawehinmi, the effervescent Lagos lawyer, simply
saw the act as Obasanjo's efforts at poverty elongation". Many also spoke in
this direction.
Thus, faced with robust opposition on all fronts, Obasanjo was literally left
in the colds. His tiny and insignificant band of supports included Chiefs Bola
Ige, Arttoney-General and Minister for Justice, Philip Asiodu, Chief Economic
Adviser and other top government functionaries who support him were informed
more by the sycophantic efforts at retaining their jobs than facing the
realities of the time. His party, the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), made
feeble attempt to defend him but its voice was drowned in a sea of opposition.
Even the governors deserted him. They were embarrassed that the President could
take such a fundamental decision without taking them into confidence. More so,
they were ill prepared for the likely fall-outs if the action. So they left him
on his own.
As a face-saving measure therefore, the President made a complete U-turn to
embrace dialogue that he had previously ignored. He called for a meeting with
the NLC on Tuesday, at his Abuja residence. At the meeting, The Post Express on
Saturday learnt, Obasanjo adopted various strategies to sway the NLC leadership
to his side of argument in raising the fuel price. When this did not yield the
desired results, he injected subtle threats of cutting down the size of Federal
Government work force if he was forced to slash the fuel price. Even this did
not work as the NLC emphatically told him that by contemplating this action, he
would be courting the anger of all workers in the country. At this juncture, our
source revealed, the government delegation asked to be allowed to reduce the
price regime to N25 per litre of petrol. Two strong considerations were said to
have informed this posture. The government asked for the new price to enable it
minimise the cost of subsidising the products. There was also the ego factor. In
this regard, the government reasoned that a total reversal to the status quo
ante would portray it as being lily livered. To its opponents, it assumed it
would appear as being unserious and inarticulate in formulating its policies.
This would therefore be used as an instrument of blackmail against it. To keep
its head therefore, it asked for the N25 price both as a concession on both
sides and as a factor of prestige in politics. However, this argument hit the
brick walls as the Oshiomhole - led NLC executive told the President point
blank, that the union would not accept anything other than a total return to the
old selling price of the petroleum products. At this stage, our reporter stated,
there was a stalemate and the meeting was adjourned, till the next day,
Wednesday.
At the resumed parley, nothing of substance was achieved, Obasanjo, goaded by
a military antecedent that brooked no opposition, stuck to his gun. On its part
the NLC, carrying the mandate of Nigerians, did not blink. By the close of the
day, no agreement was therefore reached. Thus the final stage was set for the
showdown.
And as programmed and anticipated by labour, the face-off was total. Even
non-government employees took part in the sit-at-home strike. Apparently for
fear of losing their wares to hoodlums, traders and shop owners did not turn up
for business. Private and commercial vehicles kept off the roads. Even the
nation's air spaces were closed to both in-coming and out-going flights.
Even with these tough measures, the Presidency, by Thursday afternoon had not
rescinded its decision on the fuel price hike. those that claim deep knowledge
of Obasanjo swore that given his stubborn background and the prodding of the
International Monetary Fund (IMF) officials, he might not be in a hurry to
reverse the price. Rather, if given the chance, he would seek a further hike.
Indications to this line of thought began to manifest themselves at the early
stage of the administration shortly after being installed last year, the
government floated the kite of the fuel price increase . However, it then, said
the appropriate pricing" or deregulation of the products would be handled by the
major oil marketing companies, in line with the market forces of demand and
supply. Soon after the government unfolded the agenda, public outcry ensued.
NLC, with benefit of hind sight suspected that the administration, like its
counterparts in the past, was up to a mischief. It promptly reacted. Oshiomhole
led a workers delegation to the National Assembly complex Abuja where they
registered their protest with the law makers. While the delegation sought to
carry its message to major streets in the metropolis, it was teargassed by anti-
riot police. The delegation was dispersed, but it had achieved its mission. The
government beat a temporary retreat from the policy.
Some months later, the fuel serpent began to emerge from its hibernation.
Chief Asiodu lifted the lid for its dangerous re-appearance shortly after the
yet- to-be implemented wage increase pronouncement last month, the presidential
adviser, gave hints of possible job cuts in the federal civil service unless
something drastic was done. Analysts then saw him groping towards fuel price
hike as the only route at shoring up the federally generated revenue. In
presenting his recipe, Asiodu, who in the past had presided over NEPA then known
as the Electricity Corporation of Nigeria (ECN), for over ten visionless years,
pretended to be guided by national sentiments.
However, unknown to many, he and the President were out to play the scripts
authored by the IMF and the Paris Club. These are made up of a group of leading
Western countries to which Nigeria is heavily indebted. The two bodies were said
to have provided a lot of inputs into the government's economic policies. IMF
specially insists that complete withdrawal of petroleum subsidies remains the
only viable route for the country to recover from its prostrate economy. Given
its fine text book postulations, Nigerians would not have been in a hurry to
doubt the IMF elixir. But its antecedents in the country had been catastrophic.
And so its presence in the nation's economic decisions is always seen as a bad
omen.
It made its ignoble incursion into the country's economy in 1981, when it
goaded the civilian regime of Alhaji Shehu Shagari to adopt austerity measures.
The hardship occasioned by the policy, provided the face-value reasons for
Major-General Muhammadu Buhari and his band of coupists to sack the regime in
1983. At inception, Buhari did not give the Fund much room to operate. So, he
threw open a nation-wide debate for its loan to be accepted or rejected. A
preponderance of opinions tended towards its rejection. But the government was
yet to call its bluff when Gen. Ibrahim Babangida struck and displaced Buhari.
Through the back door, Babangida embraced IMF and a Structural Adjustment
Programme (SAP), was imposed on the country. With the programme began a massive
inflation occasioned by persistent devaluation of the national currency, the
naira. The late Gen. Sanni Abacha's tenure 1993-1998, completed the circle of
IMF induced economic suicide for the country.
Based on these unsavory backgrounds, those that oppose the fuel price hike
may have a point. ASUU president believes that by the measure, " the government
appears to be more concerned with pleasing the IMF and the Paris Club and other
clubs of money lenders than with the welfare of Nigerians". Obasanjo and his
advisers are yet to debunk this allegation. He appears bent on his programme of
"poverty elongation". If he succeeds, he would after all be toeing the line
paved by his predecessors in office. Babangida did it in 1986 when he hiked the
price of petrol from 20k to 39.5k Two years later, the argument of "appropriate"
pricing and the need to check smuggling", was again used to increase the price
to 42k. Twice in 1989, it went up to 60k and 70k. Ernest Shonekan's Interim
National Government, raised it up to N5 with diesel going for N4.75k. Abacha
after his coup, temporarily brought it down to N3.25 only to hike it to N11.00
later in his regime. The Gen. Abdulsalami Abubakar regime which handed over to
Obasanjo, initially pushed up the prices of petrol to N25 per litre with diesel
and kerosine going for N23 each. However, following widespread demonstrations,
the government brought down the prices of petrol to N20, diesel N19 and kerosine
N17 a litre . Even at these prices, Nigerians have not been finding it funny.
But Obasanjo seems unperturbed. Close watchers of his tactics predict that in
raising the price of petrol to N30, he was only trying to act out what his
predecessors had done. The thinking here is that what he had in mind is N25 per
litre. So, by dangling the N30 option, the workers would, themselves ask for a
N25 per litre Abacha and Abubakar succeeded in the gamble, perhaps, due largely
to the environment under which they operated. Both were military dictators.
Obasanjo on his own, has a military background, In fact, he once called the
shots as a military Head of State. However, whether these credentials would work
for him in the current dispensation remains a question which only time can
answer.

Original_Search_Title: Bill for Review of Lagos State Laws Underway
Headline: Nigeria: Bill for Review of Lagos State Laws Underway
Body_Text:
Lagos — A bill to review Lagos State laws to make them relevant to present
day realities is underway, a member of the state House of Assembly, Hon.
Olarewanju Ope has said.
Ope who spoke to THISDAY on the menace of touts in the state said it was not
true that the state government had been reluctant in tackling the problem. But
explained that some existing laws of the state had become irrelevant in the
present realities in the country.
"It is a big problem that has to be tackled. During the Military, because
they had the power of force, they were able to take care of some of these
problems. But now some of these guys come as political thugs and what have you.
I think that there has to be a law in the state banning the activities of touts
in our parks because it is the business of government to ensure the protection
of lives and property in the state.
"I will not say that the state government has been foot dragging. We are
trying to review some of the existing laws not just for touts but for all the
other sectors in the state. We are trying to review them to see how we can amend
them where necessary in order to have good laws that is relevant to the present
needs of our people, that suits our present situation and circumstances. You
know some of these laws are out-dated, archaic, and not suitable for the present
situation. I thing a bill on that is on the way", he added.
Assessing the leadership of the Assembly in the last one year, Ope, who is
representing Lagos Island constituency, scored it high. According to him, the
Speaker of the Assembly, Dr. Olorunnimbe Mamora, has been very inspirational and
intelligent in handling the affairs of the House for the past year.
On the increase in prices of petroleum products by the Federal Government,
the lawmaker urged President Olusegun Obasanjo to revert the price to N20 per
litre as the rate of N30 per litre will only further pauperise the people.
He dismissed as merely standing logic on the head, the position of government
that it could no longer grant subsidy on fuel, claiming that government has
never granted any subsidy contrary to its claim.
"It is impossible for government to claim it has been subsidising fuel, it is
just not possible. Take for instance that you are a farmer who is producing yam.
Would you now claim that you have been granting yourself subsidy each time you
consume yam? In any case the product belongs to us as such saying that you are
bringing up the price to be at par with what obtains elsewhere is untenable".

Original_Search_Title: The Ekpili Take Over
Headline: Nigeria: The Ekpili Take Over
Body_Text:
Lagos — In Onitsha, music is food. Residents of this commercial
city, east  of the Niger, have a way of relating  to whatever they
consider music of the moment. Perhaps, this has to do with  the
physical and emotional vigour they expend in every human endeavour,
from merchandising which  is the most popular  to other less
conspicuous  activities.
One musical  genre that rocked the city for many years is Highlife.
For the typical  Onitsha man, it is a means of expression as well as an
expression itself. From its arrangement  to delivery, the music
identifies with the struggles, aspirations and travails of the Onitsha
trader in an intimate manner. Its inspirational lyrics that are highly
spiced with rhythmic renditions once filled every available air space
in Onitsha. And musicians such as Chief Stephen Osita  Osadebe, Ali
Chukwumah, Celestine Ukwu, Oriental Brothers and many others were
ceaselessly worshipped by their teeming  fans.
Today, Highlife is no longer as popular as in the days of yore.
Ekpili, a cultural musical form has taken over especially among the
youth. But Ekpili has always  existed in and around  Onitsha. As far
back as the  '40s, and right  into the  '60s before the  strong wind of
Highlife blew in, the genre was a  strong force in the neighbouring
Igbo-speaking parts of Delta State. It was  quite common for itinerant
Ekpili groups to travel on invitation from community to community
performing for many days before  moving on to the  next. However, this
was mostly done during new yam festivals and other  cultural
activities.
The civil war period, when there was little but  hunger and death to
celebrate, marked low tide for Ekpili. By the end of the war,  the
music had almost become  extinct. But  it has suddenly re-appeared and
re-invigorated in Onitsha. The new Ekpili returned without  completely
shedding its old garb. Local instruments that had been closely
associated with it such as the Igba (drum of a definite pitch), Ogwe,
Conga, Ogene and Ichaka, among  others, are still around. Ekpili also
retained an old hand in Prince Morocco Maduka who is the latest
sensation. Morocco, it was gathered, started playing the genre in 1963.
One interesting feature of Ekpili is that almost all its artistes
abandoned their different  trades for music after a period of musical
apprenticeship. Morocco who  leads the pack, was a businessman for many
years until 1963 when he branched out. Chief  Ikeanyi Nwoye was
formerly a motor mechanic, Chief Ifeanyi Okafor (Okafor Egwu-Egwu) was
a carpenter while Senator Nwizu left buying and selling for Ekpili.
Others are Chief Udemgba Onwuzulike who combines business and Ekpili
music, Odenjinji of Omor, a former rice  farmer and  Chief Akunwatta
Ozoemene  Nsugbe.
Interesting also is the fact that  most of Ekpili musicians are
passionately in love with the prefix 'Chief' before their  first names
even when it is obvious that some of them are too young to be real
traditional title holders. But Nwoye who spoke to The News defended his
chieftaincy claims simply by pointing to the beads on his left wrist.
He  also  explained away that he had not abandoned  his first
profession: "I have not left mechanic  completely. So, if tomorrow I
decide not to play music  again for one reason or the other, I will
return to where I came from."
Ekpili is a very physical  genre of music that  constantly explores
cultural and domestic themes. It is also unadulterated, according to
its  many devotees,  but the fact that modern instruments  such as the
keyboard which were not there before had been introduced questions the
validity of such a claim. While it  extols virtues  like honesty,
fairness and good neighbourliness, Ekpili themes condemn vices  and
other societal problems that retard development.
The  dominant features of the music are its story-like rendition,
praise  songs on personalities, a language  that is accessible to every
Igbo speaker and occasional historical excursions, among others. There
is also the presence  of a handful of gifted dancers who perform during
private engagements.
However,  the overall quality of Ekpili music in the market today
falls short of what could be said for a well-produced  and packaged
product. Apart from the fact that the various  works are mainly on
tape, obviously to reach  a wider audience, the fluctuation  and
unsteady record output is also a sore point. And from all indications,
the road to producing  digitally recorded Ekpili music in compact disc
is still far off. So for now, the artistes and producers are working
within what is available  and commercially expedient.
In Onitsha, one music  company that has recorded  many Ekpili acts
is Melody Music. According to its General Manager, Obinna Okpalo, their
support for the music  will continue for as long as the artistes are
prepared to work with them. "We are doing all these  as a recording
company because  of our desire to sustain our cultural values. This
helps  society a great deal because we will be leaving  something
behind for  future generations." Okpalo confidently told The News that
Ekpili music is a cultural revolution sweeping  through Onitsha and
most towns and villages of  eastern Nigeria.
The music is, indeed, widely accepted. It blares in luxury buses,
taxis, stores  and even from commercial motor cycles. And no gathering
or  event is  usually complete without  Ekpili music or live band
entertaining. This  music form is also moving gradually  to many
Nigerian cities with heavy concentration of Igbo especially in the
western and northern parts. This, for now, is the success story of
Ekpili.
But Onitsha, like most  fast moving  cities, is ever welcoming of
something  new and exciting. How many more years the music will live
depends largely on future  developments. But  for now, Ekpili is king.

Original_Search_Title: Confederation Works in Umuchu (Book Review)
Headline: Nigeria: Confederation Works in Umuchu (Book Review)
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Culture-History Of Umuchu, Leo Nnoli, Nolix Educational,
Enugu, 1999, 253 pp, UZO MAXIM UZOATU: Confederation is arguably the
most controversial subject in Nigeria today. The Sharia used to enjoy
the top controversy spot until it was quickly displaced when the South-
East Governors came up with the confederation idea.
Critics of confederation argue that it is a sure-fire route toward
the disintegration of Nigeria as a nation. When commentators talk of a
confederal state, they almost always travel to far away Switzerland to
find an example of such a constitution. Well, the book I have before me
now proves conclusively that there exists a place in Nigeria where
confederation works.
The culture-History of Umuchu by Leo Nnoli gives a compelling
account of how an effective confederal town emerged out of the
amalgamation of diverse peoples and interests. In his controversial
essay, "A source for Chinua Achebe's Arrow of God," Professor Charles
Nnolim had argued that Achebe's masterpiece amounted to a retelling of
the history of his hometown of Umuchu. At the heart of Arrow of God
lies the story that goes thus: "In the very distant past, when lizards
were still few and far between, the six villages-Umuachala, Umunneora,
Umuagu, Umuezeani, Umuogwugwu and Umuisiuzo-lived as different peoples,
and each worshipped its own deity. Then the hired soldiers of Abam used
to strike in the dead of night, set fire to the house and carry men,
women and children into slavery. Things were so bad for the six
villages that their leaders came together to save themselves. They
hired a strong team of medicine-men to install a common deity for them.
This deity which the fathers of the six villages made was called Ulu.
Half of the medicine was buried at a place which became Nkwo market and
the other half thrown into the stream which became Mili Ulu. The six
villages then took the name of Umuaro, and the priest of Ulu became
their Chief Priest. From that day they were never again beaten by an
enemy."
In the Culture-History of Umuchu, Leo Nnoli writes. "On the great
day of unification of Umuchu, all the elders representing the
constituent villages were said to have assembled at a common meeting
place, today called Nkwo-Uchu. When they had assembled, the medicine
man, after preparing the charm, announced to all that the medicine was
god-given and was to be called Ichu (Antidote) and that the signatory
villages should be collectively called Umu-Ichu. He also informed them
that the charm embodied the spirit of the whole villages of the union
and that the charm was a visible expression of their strength, unity
and survival which must be preserved and protected by all means, even
if it meant fighting. Thereafter, the diviner made two wraps of the
charm and buried one at the place where the assembly had gathered. This
is the place where the famous Nkwo-Uchu market stands today. The other
wrap of the charm was taken some distance away and thrown into a nearby
lake which is today called Uchu."
Incidentally, the central crisis in Achebe's Arrow of God revolves
around the Festival of the First Pumpkin Leaves (that is, Itu-onu-
Ugbogulu) and the eating of the new yam. In Nnoli's Culture History of
Umuchu, we read: The establishment and celebration of the Itu-Onu-
Ugbogulu festival which entails throwing of the pumpkin's tendril by
the Uchu Priest is a part fulfillment of the unification pact and is
celebrated by all the village-groups in the community. This festival
also announces the new year and permits the eating of new farm produce,
especially yam."
According to Leo Nnoli, "The confederation, ab initio, did not
recognise a centralised authority such as a chief showing vividly the
independent nature of the various villages before and after the
amalgamation."
The Nigeria nation has a lot of lessons to learn from the Umuchu
confederation. Like Nigeria, Umuchu is made up of peoples who traced
their origins from diverse places. Nnoli traces the origins of the
migrant villages of Umuchu thus: "Ihitenato from Amankwu Arochukwu, the
Ogu group which previously claimed autochthonous origin but have been
closely associated with Nri, the Ogwugwu (Imenano) group from Urualla;
Amihie from Achina, Umuagu-Ibughubu from Ufuma, Osete and other
Ibughubu groups from Umunoha, finally the Achalla group from Akokwa and
Ugwuakwu respectively."
It is in fact a miracle that such a diversity of origins could ever
forge together into a functional town. Umuchu happens to be a border
town in the Aguata Local Government of Anambra State, and the
confederation works beautifully today. The hardworking historian, Leo
Nnoli, has done a great job in researching and writing the history of
the very historic town. Nnoli who is the chief speech-writer of the
Executive Governor of Anambra State, Dr. Chinwoke Mbadinuju breaks bold
ground in his analyses of the history, culture; politics, religion,
colonisation and traditions of Umuchu. He had rich interviews with the
likes of the legendary Nze Obika Umetiti (1879-1999) before the grand
old man died.
The culture-History of Umuchu is a treasure house of knowledge. The
book lists the 24 sons of Umuchu who fought the Second World War as
well as the 159 soldiers who lost their lives during the Nigeria Civil
War.
Leo Nnoli has written a very remarkable book. Now that Nigerians are
talking of confederation, Biafra and the convocation of a Sovereign
National Conference, The Culture History of Umuchu is a must -read. I
commend it highly.

Original_Search_Title: Akume Like Eureka, Found His Political Linchpin
Headline: Nigeria: Akume Like Eureka, Found His Political Linchpin
Body_Text:
Lagos — Bbenue state is a mirror reflection of the good, the bad and
the ugly. The picture painted by the press is one of a state caught
napping and in the throes of inactivity suggestively trapped in a
despicable clamp of the immoral; it is projected that prostitution is a
way of life. Benue, perhaps is also one of the food baskets as the big
tubers of yams from there attest.
Politically, since the death of former senator and the champion of
the cause of the middle belt in 1982, Chief Joseph Tarka, party politics
had tended to revolve and follow the pattern fashioned by the maverick
politician. Tarka attained during his life time a cult status. The
simple reason was because he was reckoned to have stood up to the
Northern Oligarchy by challenging their hegemony. He rubbished their
quest to perpetuate and dominate them by forging an alliance with NCNC
along with his own party United Middle Belt Congress that resulted in
the United Progressive Grand Alliance (UPGA).
Since then, the Benue politics had thinned down to what an individual
can offer. At least to catch up with the larger than life image of
Tarka, from Aper Aku, Moses Adasu to George Akume all of them have
struggled to find relevance in a state where reticence seems to be the
order of the day.
In October 1999, a few months after the Akume administration took
oath of office, members of the Benue state House of Assembly did
something very symbolic. They unanimously threw out a motion, described
as barbaric and discriminatory, moved by one of their own. Hon. Godwin
Atser, representing Konslisha constituency. Atser, had in that motion
urged the state government to stop opening employment to non-indigenes
of the state. He had stunned the House when he moved the motion advising
the state government to send the non-indigenes packing from their jobs
as their continued stay according to him, amounted to depriving
qualified Benue indigenes from filling vacant positions.
Almost immediately another member representing Oturkpo/Ankpa
constituency Mr. Ralph Igbago countered the motion averring that it was
not only dis criminatory as it violated section 42 (1) (2) of 1999
constitution but also violated the right of freedom to live and work in
any state in Nigeria. He further stressed that the motion was not only
dictatorial and archaic, but barbaric. Igbago wondered why somebody will
ask a fellow Nigerian to stop offering employment to non indigenes. It
was unfair, he lamented.
Today, the executive arm and the legislature are caught pants down in
a hypocritical situation. The state as it were have pitched camps with a
foreign core investor, in the name of Lafarge/Cementia in preference
over a local investor, Dangote Ltd.
The State government's stand, contained in an eight-point resolution
at the end of one of its council meetings in Makurdi coincided with a
similar decision by the house and the Mbayion community (Benue cement
company's host) concerning what it tagged powerful Northern interests to
manipulate the sale of the cement plant in favour of Dangote Ltd.
Dangote's interest in BCC, according to the government and the people
of Benue, represents the determined commitment of the Northern
(Hausa/Fulani) Oligarchy to perpetuate its strong hold on the state and
indeed the Middle Belt by taking over the cement plant which is the sole
economic live wire of the people.
The state government's resolution signed by Mr. Igbarumun Igbudu, for
and on behalf of the secretary to the state government, read in part;
"apart from lack of technical competence, members (of the state Exco)
expressed tremendous apprehension that the Dangote group is incapable of
performing the role of core group investor" the statement reeled.
The government further said it was its cardinal responsibility to
protect the future generation of Benue citizens "since BCC Plc is only
the viable federal government industry in the state," that it would
ensure that the company's privatisation "should attract reputable
shareholders that can turn around the fortunes of BCC."
Justifying its decision to back Cementia /Lafarge, the state
government recalled that it was the foreign bidder who was the technical
partners that set up BCC in 1978 and managed the factory until 1984 when
they left.
Meanwhile, lending his voice and playing a mediator's role in the
raging and passionate controversy, former Governor of Kano state, Mr.
Kabiru Gaya called on the Benue state House of Assembly to be cautious
in dealing with the attempt by Aliko Dangote to secure a large share in
the BCC.
He cautioned that their stand could be interpreted as laying a
dangerous precedence against any Nigerian investor who is willing to aid
the ailing corporation. The former governor who presided over the
affairs of Kano state under the aegis of National Republican Convention
(NRC) argued that it was improper for the state to sideline bona fide
Nigerian citizens in preference over foreigners.
In addition, and apparently tugging at the senses of Akume, Gaya
reminded the state that BCC's status was that of the Federal
Government's property. He noted and reminded the Assembly men that many
Benue state indigenes were doing business in Kano without hindrance, so
"why would they want to stop a Kano indigene from doing a legitimate
business in Benue, he queried? At the core of the BCC matter is what is
really needed to revive the organisation. According to an expert's
opinion, over N2 billion is needed. And the National Council for
Privatisation (NCP) has said it was quite inconceivable that after the
sinking of such an amount that the investor will embark on a course of
action that will not contribute to the growth and development of BCC.
The NCP advised the Benue State indigenes to ensure that the select
core group investor has the necessary resources, technical capacity and
managerial know how, to provide BCC with the required leadership and
direction "That," the NCP, insists "should be the concern of all well
meaning persons from Benue state and not to issue the kind of futile
threats that have been flying all over the place" it stated.
Perhaps, everything about the BCC imbroglio and struggle for its soul
goes far beyond the buying and selling of the core investors share. It
is indeed the struggle for the heart of the masses who control the
votes; who have not experienced any change in their lives since Akume
assumed office. It is perhaps not the orchestrated threat to commit mass
suicide as suggested by Akume giving the struggle a tinge of agitation
for self determination. Tarka had proven the point, need Akume reinvent
the spirit of Tarka to find relevance in the politics that appears to
have slipped out of his hands? But who really is George Akume? What is
his politics? He is reported to be young, energetic patriotic and free
from bad antecedents. Chief Barnabas Gemade and Dr. Iyorchia Ayu are
said to be his ardent supporters.
Perhaps Akume has found the linchpin of his political life. Like
Eureka, the privatisation of BCC has provided him the launching pad to
write his name in the hearts of the Benue state indigenes.
Accused of forever complaining of lack of finance, those who were
swift to score him low in his performance over the past one year in a
comparative sense have said that what Reverend Father Moses Adasu did in
just three months in office far outstrips what he has done so far.
"It took only three months for the administration of Father Moses
Adasu in 1991 to establish the Benue state University, Makurdi, the
opening of Advanced Teachers College Oju, the Tarka foundation and the
establishment of a host of other cottage industries dotted all over the
state.
Rev. Father Adasu himself in a report credited to him, believes that
the state needs nothing but prayers and the intervention of God in the
affairs of the state. Adasu who revealed that Akume was his Director of
protocol during his time stated that he (Adasu) was indeed disappointed
in the things that Akume was doing.
When Akume mounted the rostrum to campaign for the people's votes, he
promised them among many things the completion of a fertilizer plant in
Makurdi and a 330 KV Electricity project. He also promised them the
adequate supply of water in Makurdi and other major towns. These things
are far from being yet fulfilled.
Meanwhile, in what appears as a monologue, no one has heard anything
emanating from the Dangote camp. Will Dangote against all these turn a
deaf ear to buy into the heart land of trouble? Meanwhile, the host
community has vowed to resist Dangote in its environs but promised to
welcome those they described as reputable cement companies with track
record of cement production world wide like the Blue circle,
Cementia/Lafarge among others.
"Dangote is put on notice that if our words are not heeded and
eventually they win the bid, they are investing their funds in a hostile
environment," they warned. Dangote can as well heed that threat! Flames!
Arson! Sabotage, Work to rule action and other very unwieldy industrial
actions can be employed to exact their pound of flesh.

Original_Search_Title: Hunger, Tension Increase In Besieged Kisangani
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Hunger, Tension Increase In Besieged Kisangani
Body_Text:
Kampala — Tension caused by renewed reinforcements, deployments and
arrival of big arms in Kisangani has created fears here of a fresh
outbreak of clashes between Ugandan and Rwandan armies, reports Emmy
Allio from Kisangani.
The civilian population trapped by the war is now unable to afford
basic necessities like salt which now costs US$2 (sh.3,200) per packet
of 500 grammes. A piece of pineapple now costs about a dollar (sh1600).
Around Kapalata, 14kms on the Kisangani-Buta road, civilians refused
to sell their fresh cassava tubers and yams over the weekend.
Ugandan soldiers said the Congolese now want to barter salt for
foodstuffs.
"I think we have no option but to fly in more salt to barter for
food," a UPDF soldier said.
The local population now blames "foreign armies" for the suffering
and destruction of property. They are demanding that the United Nations
plays a more active role in forcing the former allies-turned
adversaries out of the city.
Besides two UN flights last week, there have been no other flights
into Kisangani since May 4. Salt, sugar, petroleum products and
medicine are scarce as prices sky-rocket daily.
The rate of a dollar to Congolese Francs has dropped from 36 to 25
in the past fortnight.
Authorities in Kigali and Kampala have agreed to demilitarise the
ruined city of over a million people.
They have also agreed to withdraw their troops to a distance of over
200kms out of the city, and leave only companies of 200 soldiers.
In the agreement, the Rwanda-backed RCD-Goma rebels are also to
withdraw. But RCD-Goma's military commander, Jean Pierre Ondekane, on
Friday said his forces would not withdraw.
Col. Sula Semakula, the UPDF commander in Kisangani, said "What is
known as the RCD-Goma the army is in fact RPA. The Rwandans have never
recruited Congolese into army.
"The agreement to demilitarise is null and void if the Goma group
does not pull out."
Uganda and Rwanda army chiefs say the situation in Kisangani is
unavoidably hostile.
Attempts by Col. James Baxter, the chief of staff of the UN Observer
Mission in Congo (MONUC) to bring Col. Semakula and his Rwandan
counterpart, Lt. Col.
Steven Kalyango, to a round table flopped twice last week.
UPDF-controlled Bangoka International airport, about 4kms from La
Forestiere, is being besieged by RPA who have dug-in along the southern
and eastern sides of the runway. The rival armies are separated by a
runway and its apron measuring about 100metres. Sources said the RPA
has brought in two fresh battalions to Bangoka since both countries
agreed to cease fire. UPDF soldiers say RPA's control of the Kisangani
city centre is in violation of the agreements to end the clashes.
But the RPA are trapped in the city as they cannot get supplies.
The main road to their supply route is being held by the UPDF.
Ugandan soldiers said RPA now uses the River Congo to ferry supplies
from the Bafwasende-Lubutu junction.
Lt. Col. Patrick Kiyingi, commander of UPDF's 9th battalion whose
forces man the bridge, said the situation is tense and fighting may re-
erupt anytime.

Original_Search_Title: Mr. President, You Can Do More For The Judiciary (Editorial)
Headline: Ghana: Mr. President, You Can Do More For The Judiciary (Editorial)
Body_Text:
Accra — President Jerry Rawlings last Thursday expressed grave concern about
the quality of some aspects of justice being administered in the country. He
also spoke about the inadequacies in the judicial system. We are glad about the
President's frankness but The Dispatch thinks the President can do far more for
the Judicial Service than currently pertains.
Is he aware that quite a number of the newly-promoted judges do not have
court rooms? Is he also aware that some of the judges lack certain basic office
equipment? Has he had a look at their salary scales?
Has the President bothered to find out why many lawyers are not attracted to
the bench? Apart from the poor and unattractive salary structure, the lack of
proper accommodation is also a deterrent.
In addition to improving the lot of the Judiciary, the President must upgrade
the working conditions of the Police, since an efficient Police Service makes
the work of the Judiciary easier.
Regarding instances of what may appear to be lenient bail conditions for
certain crimes, the Attorney-General's Office must ensure the reform of some
laws which will make such things difficult to occur. Someone who has stolen
three tubers of yam may be denied bail and in the end, sentenced to a minimum
jail term of six months. The trial would be over within a month. People who may
have been in fraud totaling Â¢1 billion could be bailed for Â¢2 million. The trial
may even drag on for months.
Mr. President, we appreciate your frustration but you have the power to
improve the situation. The ball is in your court!

Original_Search_Title: Governors: Have They Delivered?
Headline: Nigeria: Governors: Have They Delivered?
Body_Text:
Lagos — Before the dawn of democracy almost a year ago,it had
appeared like we were all sentenced to an eternity of socio-economic
oddities. Nothing was working, except the mindless and wanton
corruption being perpetrated by our unformed rulers. Each day, the
masses prayed, but the ruling soldiers preyed harder on us all
Fright had been animated just as the detention camps were
overflowing, not with criminals, but with the bold and the critical.
Yet, the crafters of the script seemed to have only finished with
Act One, Scene One of the long-drawn drama which indeed was a cliff-
hanger. And there lied our collective apprehension But God's timely
intervention averted the programmed catrosphe.
This explains the exultant spirit of Nigerians when on May 29, last
year military rule was terminated and civil rule was ushered in. Given
the long years of mis-rule and governmental misdemeanour, the notion
was that with democracy all our problems will be gone and we will be
all ushered to El-Dorado. And from then on, we shall begin to savour
the milk and honey flowing abundantly in our land.
To many, democracy was the needed sesame for all our woes and
deprivations, But almost one year after, the people are examining the
books to see how balanced out. What is the dividend from the one-year
democracy in various states? What has changed? Is life better now? Or
is there the prospect of a better tomorrow? Have the guys we all filed
out to vote for on January 9, 1999 as governors been able to bring
Government nearer the people and to give hope to the hopeless?
Not a few state's chief executives have simply used the last one
year to undertake tutorials and rehearsals in the various Government
Houses. In several states,it is a running battle of nerves and politics
between the state governors and their deputies, as it is with Lagos and
Abia states. And while some of the governors undertake periodic
international jamborees in the name of investment drive, their wives
also indulge their passion at the expense of the state.
In the first part in a series, we assess the last one year in office
of state governors beginning with those of Abia, Ekiti, Jigawa, Plateau
and Rivers states. Eddy Odivwri, Chukwudi Nwabuko, Samuel Ajayi, Peter
Umar Omale in Jos and Ahamefula Ogbu did the assessment.
Governor Orji Uzor Kalu can be described as God's gift to Abia
State. Since coming to power one year ago, he has been able to
transform the state and give it a new lease.
In terms of provision of infrastucture such as roads, water and
healthcare facilities, few governors would contest with him for the
first position.
Very early in his tenure, Kalu recognised the need to open up Aba,
the commercial nerve centre of the entire south/east and began the
construction of roads. Less than four months in office Kalu became the
first governor to commission about four roads namely- Obohia, Umale,
Ohanku and Ama- Ogbonna. He followed this up by constructing some roads
in the state capital, Umuahia.
In all, no fewer than 23 road projects were embarked upon. Ten of
the roads have been completed while the others are nearing completion.
He has also provided six boreholes in each of the 17 local
government areas of the state. One such local government now with
potable water is Ukwa East local government. Until now, the local
government depended on a stream for water, with many residents of the
local government trekking several kilometres for this necessity of
life.
During his visit to the state last February, so overwhelmed was
President Olusegun Obasanjo with Kalu's performance that he declared
him 'Action Governor'. The name has stuck since then. A visit to 'God's
own state' shows the impact of Kalu who wants to go down in history as
one governor who succeeded where others failed. Since the creation of
the state, the amount of work done in the past one year could be said
to surpass what previous governors or administrations did for eight
years before the coming of Kalu.
Following the steps of the federal government, Kalu has launched war
against corruption. Two anti-corruption bills are currently with the
state House of Assembly. The bills spell out in details acts considered
as corrupt practices. Without mincing words, Kalu is keeping faith with
his electoral promises. At his inauguration, he promised to make a
difference within his first year in office. No less a person than
Obasanjo who cannot be said to be flippant has commended the Governor.
Same with Obasanjo's deputy, Atiku Abubakar who commissioned Obohia,
Ohunke, Omuma, Osusu, Umule and Ogbor hill roads within the governor's
first 100 days in office.
But what is defining Kalu's tenure more than anything else is his
penchant for stoking controversy. However he is not just controversial
for controversy sake. Rather, it is a product of his conviction and
worldview.
For instance, against the backdrop of the recent Kaduna mayhem where
many Igbos were massacred, Kalu was in the forefront of agitations for
a confederal arrangement. This call came in the wake of his stance that
he could no longer keep quiet if Igbos were being attacked. To many,
the controversial statements were unfortunate but that is vintage Kalu,
who as it seems has become the embodiment and rallying point of the
Igbo spirit. It is to his credit that Kaduna State Governor wooed him
to Kaduna to persuade Igbos to return to the troubled city after the
Sharia riots.
Another controversial act of Kalu was the way and manner he
dissolved his cabinet, shoved aside personal aides, in a move
unprecedented in the history of governance in the country. Although
many railed at him for this action, the truth is that as one with both
the yam and the knife, he was in order, since he has the power to hire
and fire. All in all, Orji Kalu has a focus and is poised to take Abia
to greater heights. However, to do this, it will be in his own interest
and enternal glory if he reconciles all sections of Abia, particularly
that section that feels it is being alienated in the scheme of things
in the state.
This is one governor whose focus has been centred mainly on the
disadvantaged rural dwellers. This pre-occupation informed the emphasis
he has placed right from the outset on agriculture
With a predominantly agrarian populace the state is exploring a
rural transformation through agriculture where it has comparative
advantage. The level of poverty in the state and the desire to
transform it rapidly according to Governor Turaki is being hampered by
the problem of lack of studies on the problems of the state for a
focused programme of action which would be easily sustainable.
One of such schemes which will yield its first fruit by the end of
the year is what could simply be called the sugarcane miracle of
Jigawa. It is a scheme whereby rural farmers are provided with high
yielding disease resistant industrial sugar cane to be planted in farms
clustered together so as to achieve assembly effect. The second reason
is to be able to establish small scale processing industries so that
when the sugarcane is harvested, they will be processed in the factory
built within the location of the farm.
One attraction of the scheme seem to be the satis approach whereby
the biomass of the sugar cane would be used to generate electricity for
the rural community where the processing factory is located.
The calculation is that a family which cultivates a hectare of
sugarcane which research has shown has one of the highest yields per
hectare in the world, will net N250,000.00. A pilot scheme of the
experiment from where it got materials for cultivating the farms proved
98 per cent successful hence the contagious enthusiasm with which it
has been embraced.
On its own, the government launched what it called the Millennium
Village where the sugarcane was planted in large scale with irrigation
facilities provided. This is in addition to mango, date palm of which a
hybrid with high yield that matures in two years has been developed.
Other crops being cultivated already are guava, tamarind, lemon and
another economic tree, the gum Arabic. Most of the crops are to be
targeted at foreign markets to yield foreign exchange for the state.
Governor Turaki has also introduced a novel administrative style in
the state. This is calculated to bring government closer to the people.
While the in-thing is the sitting of ministries and parastatals in
state capitals in other states, Jigawa spreads its own ministries
across the emirates while other ministries were dissolved into
departments and run by professionals. For example, the state ministry
of agriculture in overseen by the deputy governor and it is located in
his own native emirate.
Another innovation introduced by Governor Turaki in Jigawa State is
the Peoples' Forum called the Jigawa Peoples Congress. It serves as a
forum where all grievances, suggestions, and other knotty issues are
resolved or reported. It is also a link between the ruler and the ruled
where all immunity is removed and wrong done either by the governor
against the people or the people against the governor are tabled. It
serves a second function of acting as a liaison between non
governmental organisations and community development based
Organisations which helps to propagate government programmes as well as
protect the interest of the people.
The governor is also vigorously tackling the decay in educational
facilities and standard and to his administration has mapped out
special days, Fridays for members of the executive council to go to the
classrooms to teach the pupils. A more permanent arrangement is the
plan to hire teachers from some countries like India and China at a
cheaper rate to teach the children in both primary and secondary
schools. Again, all teachers in the state are to be retrained to bring
them up to modern standards. Another approach is the area of
remuneration where the state is trying to improve condition of service.
Other schemes in the pipeline include the provision of computer
education with plans to establish an Internet University with
professors from abroad to handle it so that the people of the state
will not lag behind in the world of information systems and technology.
Turaki's dream is to build a beautiful city like Port Harcourt,
small but well planned so that people would want to visit and stay.
Already, a team from Singapore has visited the state to conduct an on
site survey and would be coming back to commence the landscaping of the
state. This is to complement the tourist attraction development of the
state which already has a lot of salespoints in the Baturiya Wetland
birds sanctuary where birds from all over the world gather every
September to November yearly in what foreign tourists have branded 'the
international Birds conference'. There is also the Birninkudu ancient
rock painting said to have been ingrained in the rocks by early cave
men and the proposed Wawan Rafin Resort while Maigatari village, said
to be of importance to location development in tourism will be looked
into.
Just like some states are planning to generate their own power,
Jigawa state apart from calling for them to be linked to the national
grid plans to generate its own power at a cost lower than the current
8-10 cents per kilowatt as some companies have agreed to give them at
five cents although there is a proposal already in which indicates a
supply price of 2 cents if the state can supply its own gas to the
foreign firm that will supply the power.
Major achievements:
Transformation of rural areas; making the people get a feel of
government's activites in their lives.
Drawback:
Political disharmony in the state. Problem of lack of electricity in
several parts of the state.
Rating: Above average
The first six months of the Governor Joshua Chibi Dariye's
administration in Plateau State were basically low keyed especially in
project execution as the regime spent more time streamlining invincible
issues to reposition the work force in line with it's vision of re-
engineering.
Re-engineering, as the secretary to the state government, Ezekiel
will explain, is about refining and adding value to government and this
perhaps explains the retreat, the first of its kind in any state which
governor Dariye and his Executive Council attended for nearly seven
days at the Kent Academy, Miango- Jos.
The first six months were utilised basically used to effect payment
of the N3,000 minimum wage and arrears to workers, payment of
accumulated leave grants of N79 million, re-engaging with UNDP after
the suspicions placed on the state for not honouring its counterpart
funding of programmes of the world body; payment of scholarship
allowances to students as well as the release of N86 million as grants
to the voluntary agency schools. The government also established the
Dangin Contractors Commission along with similar bodies to restore
sanity in the civil service, assess all on- going projects in order to
streamline them into Dariye's re-emerging mission. Shortly after the
retreat at Kent Academy, the governor launched his administration's
projects implementation phase by flagging off the contracts for roads
in each of the three senatorial zones and at present, work has
progressed over fifty percent on the Bokkos/ Barkin Ladi road; Riyom-
Tahoss and Zarmko Ã Madubi roads.
In spite of these strides, the administration has come under
criticism for moving "slowly" and allegedly spending too much on
frequent foreign trips. The governor is also contending with the
problem posed by some power blocks in the state some power bed rocks
particularly old politicians including those close to former chairman
of the PDP, Chief Solomon Lar. Many in this camp believe Dariye "is a
small boy who did not pay much political dues before winning the
governorship". They also complain that the governor is too independent
for their liking especially as he has taken far-reaching decisions
without consulting with them. The recent face-off between Dariye and
Sports Minister Sango many say is an example of such ill-feeling.
An APP stalwart James Shapruk however told Thisday that the
governors trips had begun to yield fruits especially with the recent
decision by a U.S firm to take over and complete the abandoned Jos Rock
International Hotel and also build over 1000 houses for sale to people
of the state. The administration has constructed the Zia bridge in
Dengi, and is building three hospitals in each senatorial zone and
converting them to specialist hospitals. It has renovated 51 secondary
schools with at least three in each LGA; and is building five wells in
each district, a total of 208 wards and has re-awarded the contract for
the construction of the Jos- Zaria main stadium for N1.2 billion out of
which N400 million has been paid as take off sum.
The SSG revealed that to celebrate his one year in office, Governor
Dariye will commission twelve projects among which are the Jos Township
stadium, the multi-million Naira indoor theater which the government
completed and the Dadrin Kowa Youth center.
Even where it appears the regime has fared well in the past twelve
months, the issue of water supply and petroleum seem to be hard nuts to
crack, the multi-billion naira greater Bukuru-Jos ADB assisted water
scheme remains a major headache just as government appears to have run
out of ideas on how to solve the problem of acute fuel shortage which
is affecting socio-economic life as well as agricultural development.
The story of the Rivers state government in the last one year, has
uniquely posted some positive entries on the balance sheet at the end
of the first "trading season".
Governor Peter Odili of Rivers state has carved a niche for himself
in the way he has carried on with the business of governance.
Specifically, he has touched the lives of his people in several areas
like:
EDUCATION
Dr Odili has embarked on the renovation of several dilapidated
school buildings. He has also ensured the prompt payment of teachers
within the state. But his critics point out that he has concentrated
his efforts on schools within Port-Harcourt city alone.
HEALTH
Odili has approved free medical health services for school children
of age six and below as well as adults above 65 years of age. But not
much haas been said about stocking the government hospitals with drugs
and relevant equipment.
TRANSPORT
Perhaps it is in the area of transport that Odili has made the most
remarkable impact. He has affected all the means of transport in the
state. He provided 25 school buses for school children to carry them to
and from their schools free of charge. This is novel and unchallenged
in the nation.
More than that, the Odili Government has also provided train
services for the state's civil servants. This has been hailed as it has
reduced the gross cost of transport expenses of civil servants. It is
said that about five thousand passengers patronise the train on a daily
basis. The people in the riverine area of the state are not left out.
About ten out-board engines(ferries) have been provided to also service
their interest.
POWER GENERATION
In this area, Odili has also shown some good initiative, albeit
quietly. Recently, the state government also acquired some high-powered
generators to generate electricity in some communities. This provides
the basis for future privatisation scheme.
MICRO-CREDIT SCHEME
This is another area the state has provided the lead for others to
follow. The state already has some co-operative schemes which are aimed
at empowering the people economically. It is not certain how many
persons are already benefiting from this scheme, but that there is such
a scheme is on its own salutary.
HOUSING
Governor Odili at his inauguration had promised that within his
first 100 days in office, he would build one thousand housing units.
One year after, he has not delivered on this promise. He has however
initiated some housing programmes but the affordability to the masses
is another matter.
PEACE MANAGEMENT
Rivers state is a volatile state with several rounds of violence. It
is a veritable source of social unrest. Odili's capacity to rule has
been tried severally on account of the social upheavals. At one time or
the other, several communities have gone up in arms against one another
or even amongst themselves. Some of them include the Choba rage with
Wilbros resulting in the controversial rape story.
Similarly, the Okirika and Eleme war indeed upset the peace of the
state s several lives and property were lost.
And then again, there is the recurrent Ogoni uprising which has
resulted in several deaths too. But in all of these, Odili has managed
to contain the riotous tendency of the Rivers state youths.
Odili, a medical doctor has shown good sociological grasp in the
understanding of the Rivers people.
As he once noted, "peace is the fundamental platform on which any
development can occur. We appreciate this from the start and then we
first of all had to understand the sociological factors that can
militate against peace. And then ensure you keep your eye on them."
He has also demonstrated being a good politician by working to get
the headquarters of the proposed NDDC located in Port-Harcourt, against
the better favoured Yenagoa in Bayelsa state.
Major Achievement:
Provision of some basic infrastructure
Drawback:
Inability to deliver on all promises made.
Rating:
Above average.
As a state, the greatest handicap of Ekiti is funds. The state does
not seem to have enough to meet its obligations to its people and that
is the problem the governor of the state. Otunba Adeniyi Adebayo, has
had to face in the last twelve months. Apart from allocation from the
federation account, the state cannot boast of any meaningful source of
income. Nevertheless, the governor has been trying to justify his
election as the state's chief executive.
In a state where education is viewed as a veritable industry, the
governor seems to have scored a bull's eye when he decided to pay fifty
percent of the examination fees of all the students of the state's
origin sitting for the on-going West Africa Examinations Council, WAEC,
examinations. Many saw this as very good step on the part of the state
governor, especially in a state where the poverty level is very high.
This enabled many students to be able to sit for the exams. In the same
vein, the governor also supplied computers to some secondary schools in
the state. According to him, this measure was meant to aid computer
literacy in secondary schools in the state. For Adebayo, his priority
seems to be education. The state university, University of Ado-Ekiti
too has benefited from this direction. He is set complete another set
of hostels in addition to the one he had built as accommodation is the
greatest problem facing the students of the university. Above all,
right now, secondary students in the state do not pay school fees
again.
Another area where Adebayo did well is in the area of the state-
owned television station. The state's last military administrator,
Atanda Yusuf, a retired Navy Captain, had commissioned the station even
when there were not enough equipment to transmit signal from the
station. Adebayo had to close it down, re-award some contracts and now,
the station is functioning.
Despite paucity of funds, the governor has been trying to do
something in the area of health. Many health centres have been upgraded
to general hospital in an apparent move to make health care delivery
available to the people. These hospitals have in turn been supplied
with drugs.
It may not exactly be a plus for the Iyin-Ekiti-born lawyer, but
taking into consideration the situation in other states, the governor
deserves kudos for being one of the few governors who have maintained a
good relationship with their deputies since they assumed office almost
a year ago. His deputy, Mr. Paul Alabi, has been having it smooth with
his boss. Though some have attributed this to the difference in the
ages (the deputy is much more older), many will however give it to the
governor for his maturity and his ability to carry his number two man
along. In the same vein, the state has been relatively peaceful in the
last one year in a country where many states have had more than their
own fair share of crisis. In fact, it is a credit to Adebayo that there
has never been a single incident of communal clash since he assumed
office.
It has, however, not been song of victory all the way for the son of
retired General Adeyinka Adebayo. He has not been able to effectively
tacke the menace which bad raods constitute in the state. Coming from
Osun State, after Ijebu-Jesa, the smooth road terminates at Itawure and
the dancing begins. The road is still as it was long ago and there is
no sign it will change. And it is like that in most parts of the state.
Apart from this, siting of the Federal Government Girls College in the
state has almost become a battle of supremacy between him and education
Minister, Tunde Adeniran. Adebayo prefers Aramoko while Adeniran
prefers Efon Alaaye. Now the problem has not been resolved with the two
communities laying claim to the school.
Also the governor has not been able to shake off the toga of being a
socialite who will not bat an eyelid in leaving state matters and
coming to Lagos to attend parties.
Adebayo also kept tongues wagging because of his loyalty to the
former deputy governor of the old Ondo State, Nathaniel Aina, and also
an Alliance for Democracy, AD, chieftain in the state. Apart from being
the party boss, his son was given a plum government job and above all,
Aina was named the chairman of the Governing Council of the state
university. He has since declined because of the criticism that
followed the apparent lopsided appointments.
Major achievement:
In the area of education.
Drawback:
His apparent failure to grasp that apart from education, he has to
graple with other area as well
Rating:
Average

Original_Search_Title: HIV And The Abala Cure
Headline: Nigeria: HIV And The Abala Cure
Body_Text:
Lagos — Joseph Abalaka, M.D. has been in the news in recent times.
is much-trailed therapy for the cure of persons ailing from the Human
Immuno-deficiency Virus (HIV)/ Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome
(AIDS) has split public opinion down the middle.
In the event, very little about the debate is clarified by the
advocacy of the contending parties.
The Health minister's eagerness to resign if anything of substance
comes off Abalaka's claim is as unhelpful as the latter's professed
disposition to inject himself with the dreaded virus in a bid to prove
the value of his cure. In as far as Dr.
Abalaka claims for his therapy, the ability to reverse an HIV
sufferer's sero-prevalence, it ranks alongside the combination drug
therapies, based on protease inhibitors, that are currently popular in
the west. Thus far, it scarcely extends the frontiers of scientific
knowledge.
But it promises a great deal at another level: it challenges all
known therapies on price. Whereas current anti-HIV remedies cost
upwards of $12000 (about N1.2million) per annum, Dr. Abalaka claims for
his therapy the humble cost of N25,000 These obviously are the details
that matter.
For if this claim were true, it bespeaks some advance in the
compounding of drugs. Lamentably, the contrasting declarations made for
and against the likelihood of Dr. Abalaka having chanced on a nostrum
of such powerful import overshadow the central issues.
More than any other, both the ministry of Health and the claimant
should know that procedures exist for substantiating the veracity of
such claims. Indeed, a couple of years back, the countries of the OECD
agreed on common standards for conducting the clinical trials on which
these procedures are based.
Doubtless, such procedures have been expedited in the past
Â­especially for some of the present anti-HIV treatments. But it is
essential that Dr.
Abalaka's claim demonstrates fidelity to this process in order that
it be taken seriously. For starters, he ought to file a patent
application (describing both the idea behind his treatment and the
method for working it). It is then expected that Âpre-clinicalsÂ -
tests on laboratory animalsÂ­to ascertain the drug's toxicity (how much
collateral harm it does to the recipient) and bioavailability (how well
it is absorbed and delivered to the point of need) are conducted and
recorded. ÂPhase twoÂ and Âphase threeÂ trials (testing on humans)
follow from this.
The basic requirements for these Â­large numbers of HIV-infected
people, and a fair network of relatively sophisticated teaching
hospitals and related medical facilitiesÂ­are already in place.
Fundamentally, the task is to determine the category of patients to
administer the drug to, when and how, while all the time ensuring that
a statistically reliable result will be obtained therefrom.
Conducting these tests and arranging the result in a way that could
convince regulatory authorities to give the necessary approval for the
commercial development of the drug demands that it be given to a great
number of people who suffer from the disease the medication is designed
to cure. Similarly, some or any of the existing HIV intervention
therapies must be administered on an equal number of persons.
This latter set must not be aware of the fact that they are not
benefiting from the new drugs. It is for this reason that the search
for a commercial development of a new drug is a very costly process.
And about a third of this cost, which is put at around $500 million by
the leading pharmaceutical companies, is accounted for by Âclinical
trialsÂ.
Which is where the government comes in. It is improbable that Dr.
Abalaka can muster anything like the sums needed to go through this
process unaided. Thus once he demonstrates himself amenable to the
idea, it behoves the federal authorities to help speed things up, to
lend financial assistance and to guard the patent on this jealously.
Sadly, this prospect is soured by two traits local to the Nigerian.
The most off-putting is the penchant for not taking ourselves
seriously. On the other hand, those in charge of our healthcare system
have not handled the matter with the urgency it deserves. No one is
saying that Abalaka should be given a carte blanche.
But for crying out loud, a desperate disease calls for a desperate
cure. The danger ultimately is not that Abalaka might turn out to be a
quack.
Actually, this would give a rather desirable outcome. It does not
only settle the matter one way, it would be of immense relief to those
who argue that the euphoria over Dr. Abalaka's claim will deflect from
the larger message of the anti-AIDS campaign.
The possibility instead, if at all there is anything to Dr.
Abalaka's claim is that as it happened with the yam-pounder concept,
this idea might yet see better days outside this shores.
Publication Date: May 11, 2000

Original_Search_Title: End The HIV/AIDS Cure Controversy
Headline: Africa: End The HIV/AIDS Cure Controversy
Body_Text:
Lagos — It is about time that the claims by Dr. Abalaka that he has
developed an effective vaccine for reversing HIV infection be scientifically
ascertained and the raging controversy laid to rest in the overall interest of
the nation.
Claims on the discovery of cures for HIV and full blown AIDS are not strange
in Nigeria. Unconstrained by the rigid rules of scientific proof required of
orthodox western trained doctors and unaffected by their code of ethics that
punishes advertising with deregistration and expulsion by the Nigerian Medical
authorities, every roadside herbalist claims a cure for not only HIV but full
blown AIDS. Unfortunately the media, driven by the need to generate revenue,
have been less circumspect in airing such unverifiable and unverified claims.
Thus when Dr. Abalaka came on the scene his claims could not but evoke doubts.
But Dr. Abalaka is not another roadside herbalist opportunistically
exploiting the AIDS scourge to fleece off the public. He is a trained medical
doctor registered to practice in Nigeria. Thus, his claims to have developed an
effective vaccine against HIV infection does not fall into the hocus pocus
category of the herbalists. Besides, the corroboration of the effectiveness of
the ABALAKA VACCINE by very senior military officers who had sent soldiers
certified to have tested positive to HIV during their peace-keeping operations
for treatment which they claim had been successful, raises the level of
seriousness of the claims. In contrast, however, the Federal Minister of
Health, another trained medical doctor has emotionally refuted Abalaka's claims
and the mandarins in the Health Ministry have been generally sticking to the
official script that Dr. Abalaka's claims are frivolous.
It should be recalled that this is not the first time a Nigerian makes a
break-through in science or technology on the home turf only to be denied and
defamed by his colleagues and ignored by the government. It is on record that
in the mid-70s one student Fagoyinbo of The University of Ife (now Obafemi
Awolowo University) invented a yam pounding machine which was used to produce
good quality pounded yam for visiting General Obasanjo the then military Head
of State. There were encomiums and songs of praise. But there it all ended
until the Japanese smuggled out the technology and produced an improved version
of the machine which were massively imported into Nigeria and paid for in
foreign exchange. The same ineptitude and prodigal neglect were exhibited over
the first successful Nigerian test tube births and the invention of an
effective cholera vaccine by Nigerians in the private sector. This raises the
suspicion that professional rivalry borne out of the fact that those who
receive official funding only hold seminars and talk shows while those without
such motivations produce results. It could be surmised therefore that the
policy of one's rival never killing a full-grown game might be at work in these
matters.
A continuation of this trend is undesirable for the nation. It weakens the
intellectual development of the country, deepens the nations embarrassing
dependence on foreign scientific products and technological breakthroughs,
devalues the African image in the world and contributes largely to our poverty
in the midst of plenty. An effective made-in-Nigeria HIV vaccine, for instance,
could off set our national debt in a few years, beyond making Dr. Abalaka rich.
It is said that so far, no arm of government has shown special interest in this
matter. We therefore, call on Mr. President to first facilitate the patenting
of Abalaka's vaccine and second set up a high powered panel of neutral experts
to evaluate the claims on HIV vaccine. The on-going controversy must end
soonest and it must not become another yam-pounding machine experience.

Original_Search_Title: Workers Nationwide Mark May Day
Headline: Africa: Workers Nationwide Mark May Day
Body_Text:
Lagos — Workers nationwide will mark today's May Day amid expectation that
the Federal Government will announce a new minimum wage.
President Olusegun Obasanjo is expected to address workers at a rally by the
Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) at the Tafawa Balewa Square in Lagos. Also
expected at the event are the Senate President, Dr. Chuba Okadigbo, the Speaker
of the House of Representatives, Alhaji Ghali Na'Abba and Lagos State Governor,
Senator Ahmed Bola Tinubu.
It is expected that Obasanjo may use the occasion to announce a new enhanced
salary package for civil servants nationwide.
Speaking on a new salary package for workers yesterday, an NLC official
called for a review of the 1981 legislation pegging worker's minimum wage at
N250.
Mr. Chidi Nnorom, who is the Chairman of the National Union of Air Transport
Employees (NUATE) said in Lagos, shortly before a meeting of the NLC's Central
Working Committee (CWC) that the new minimum wage would serve as a benchmark
for future hikes.
Amid speculations of a raise of the minimum wage to N7,500 and allegations
of a sell-out by labour, Nnorom said whatever was agreed upon now would not
debar further negotiations. He said that the new rates, which would be backed
by laws, would serve only as benchmarks for unionized workers.
"When you talk of minimum wage, it is meant to protect only unionized people
and serve as a guide to individual unions in their negotiations with their
employers," he added.
On the N7,500 minimum wage being touted, Nnorom said he was not aware that
any amount had been fixed, saying that the NLC's CWC was meeting to get briefs
from its negotiating committee on the matter ahead of today's May Day
celebrations.
He urged workers to exercise restraint on their clamour for jumbo wages and
enjoined them not to allow their ranks to be broken.
The labour leader also urged extreme tact "in dealing with the person who
has the knife and the yam", noting that apart from increased wages, workers
