as a historical tourist monument, complete with a hotel and other friendly
amenities.
This is the Mutineers Park across All Saints Church, Nakasero. No one as of
now can imagine that that is a preserved green in the state it is. Therefore,
whereas the council is conscious of the environment, we at the same time would
not encourage bush, yams, potatoes or monkeys in the city in the name of
preserving the environment.
Which is why we find it interesting that some people have actually argued
that the Golf Course Apartments, one of the biggest private investment in
residential accommodation in the country, should not have been located where
they are.
But that is properly gazetted residential area. Should the periphery of the
Golf Course be left wild?
We are aware of course that a number of those talking loudest are members of
the Golf Club.
The club has just embarked on major extension (both vertical and horizontal)
to their existing club house.
What irony!
* The author is the Town Clerk of Kampala City Council.

Original_Search_Title: Bank of Ghana cheques bounce 10 times
Headline: Ghana: Bank of Ghana cheques bounce 10 times
Body_Text:
Accra — China State Construction Engineering Corporation Ghana Ltd has
presented a cheque valued at over Â¢240 million ten times for payment at the
Bank of Ghana. In as many times the cheque bounced.
The Chinese Government owned company has undertaken major construction
projects in Accra and is presently renovating State House, popularly referred
to as Job 600. It has also been awarded a contract to construct the Alajo
drainage works around Accra's Kwame Nkrumah Circle area.
Further Chronicle investigations at CAL Merchant Bank revealed that
ENERGOPROJECT, another European construction company based in Accra is facing
financial embarrassment following a similar experience of dishonoured cheques
drawn on Bank of Ghana cheque. A cheque for Â¢318 million it presented for
payment has had problems with Bank of Ghana tellers for more than a month
having been rejected on a number of occasions. The cheque, issued on the 24th
of February, 2000 and credited to the company's account at CAL Merchant Bank
has bounced and as at April 5, 2000 the cheque had been so defaced that there
was no space on it for the 'Return to drawer' stamps usually administered on
cheques by banks.
Incidentally, that particular Cheque is part of the payment of a multi-
billion cedi new office for the Ministry of Finance. Energoproject has also
undertaken major projects for government including the Accra International
Conference Centre, Cedi House and the ongoing high rise building opposite SSNIT
Accra headquarters offices.
Another company similarly hit by these recurrent incidents of bounced
cheques is Razel, a French Construction Company. Currently battling serious
cash flow problems which is threatening jobs of construction workers. There
were fears expressed by workers visited by Chronicle at some of their sites
that they will be laid off soon, and some of the expatriate staff confirmed
their precarious condition.
Perhaps the company that may be hardest hit is the one that has had a
chequered history of run-ins and dalliance with the Government, the originally
German owned Construction Pioneers, previously called Carl Ploetner.
Chronicle learn that they are owed in excess of Â¢40 billion and were
recently forced to lay off workers to stave off collapse. A management staff
confirmed this information to The Chronicle at their Korle Lagoon project site
in Accra last Friday.
The Director of Budget at The Ministry of Finance, Major Tara said his
Ministry does not issue cheques but only approves of Government expenditure
after approval by the Controller and Accountant General.
His Public Relations Office however said that Government revenue for the
first quarter of this year was so low that it was exhausted before March and so
the Ministry if now having to wait for Parliament to approve their estimates
before their budget could be approved, adding that it was their hope that the
problem would be solved.
He explained that the Governor of The Bank of Ghana was so bent on asserting
his independence to curb excessive Government spending.
Dr. Kwabena Duffour has established a reputation of doggedness since he
assumed office, and has a very tight monetary control policy.
The Controller and Accountant General Mr. Tuffuor refused to answer the
question put to him directly in a note slipped to him by The Chronicle through
his secretary last Friday. He asked that this reporter should go and write a
proper letter.
Bank of Ghana official Secretary Mr. Odei said he is not authorised to speak
on issues even though traditionally BoG Secretaries had acted as Spokesmen. He
said he was not going to deny that their cheques have been bouncing. He also
asked for formal letter.
This is not the first time that cheques issued by the State have been
dishonoured by Bank of Ghana.
Last year, cheques hurriedly issued to a horde of angry contractors who had
besieged the Ministry of Finance because of liquidity problems.
Dr. Duffour at the time was quoted to have told the contractors that 'There
is no money here. Go and ask The Minister of Finance whether one send people to
go and collect yams from a yam barn into which no yams have been sent'.

Original_Search_Title: Food Shortage Hit Kailahun
Headline: Sierra Leone: Food Shortage Hit Kailahun
Body_Text:
Freetown — The people in the Kailahun District are in desperate need
of food as hundreds are dying of starvation on daily basis. Concord
Times man who just returned from a trip to the district discovered that
most people are now merely walking skeletons.
The food shortage has affected both areas controlled by the
government and the rebels.
The people now forage for bush yams and bananas in a bid to survive.
Relief agencies have not made any appreciable impact in the district;
they have always complained of inaccessibility.

Original_Search_Title: The People Of Kong Demand Green Light For Ouattara
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Ivorian City of Kong Expect Ouattara
Body_Text:
Kong, Cote d'Ivoire — When the rains finally came, residents
of the northern Ivorian city of Kong acknowledged that they have been
awaiting the climate change for over two weeks.
The downpour came 22 December over Kong and heralded the morning
coolness of the Harmattan, which makes the body cold and appeases the
mind.
Modibo Toure, an octogenarian, born around 1921, suggested that the
downpour in Kong, about 700 km north of Abidjan, was a good omen.
Toure, who is considered as one of the most reputable marabouts of
the region shouts: "Nothing bad can befall our son Alassane."
Barely an hour after the rains ceased in Kong, a mythical city that
is well-known for its occult sciences and esoteric practices which
attract "clients" from all over the country and the sub-region, came the
announcement by the Electoral Commission approving Alassane Ouattara's
candidature in the forthcoming parliamentary elections.
"It is good news," Fabakary Ouattara, the local secretary
Republican Rally (RDR) sighed with ears glued to his radio.
Those words were filled a certain relief after the anxiety caused
by earlier rumours about the possible rejection of the candidature of the
man whom the whole region unanimously considers as its future MP in the
first Parliament of the Second Republic.
"If ADO (Alassane Dramane Ouattara) were not considered eligible,
there would have been no elections here and Kong would have ended up
without an MP", Ouattara said.
On 15 November, the association of traditional kings and chiefs had
warned the new Ivorian authorities about the consequences of the possible
elimination of RDR chairman and son of their region from the 10 December
parliamentary elections.
The kings and dignitaries of the "Grand North" further warned that
if that happened, there would be no elections in the whole savannah
region, thus leaving it with no representation in parliament.
Residents of Kong are not bothered about the presidential decree of
5 November, inviting any voter to contest the eligibility of any
candidate declared qualified by the Electoral Commission (EC).
The consider NEC as a new and futile pitfall in the path of the
"brave "tche" (warrior in Malinke).
They are already preparing to extend an enthusiastic and fervent
welcome to their standard-bearer.
It is a fact that ADO will certainly win the day in this stronghold
which is already his, according to the party's local secretary.
Kong boycotted the presidential elections by over 99 percent
on the orders of Ouattara, who was excluded from the race over
nationality question.
But, if the late President Felix Houphouet-Boigny's former Prime
Minister does not need to put up a fight to conquer a citadel that is all
his, he should, on the contrary, undertake rehabilitation effort which -
without exaggeration - is as challenging as Herculean.
Should he become the MP of the land of his ancestors - his great
grand-father, the emperor Sekou Ouattara founded the Kong Empire in the
17th Century - the task will devolve on him to lift Kong from the dust,
oblivion and the misery to modernity.
Residents of the district hardly believe they are in Cote d'Ivoire,
regarding the backwardness of their area in comparison to other parts of
the country.
A passenger of a wheezy "badjan" (twenty-two passenger mini-bus)
complains that such vehicles abandon them on several occasions on the
nearly impassable 104-km road linking Ferkessedougou with Kong.
The description illustrates of the state of dilapidation of the
small town, which has shut itself off from the rest of the country.
The home of the Ouattaras, with its 5,500 inhabitants, has not seen
an inch of tarmac on its roads since it became a sub-prefecture in ...
1961!
"If you compare the level of development of the other sub-
prefectures which were created at the time with Kong, you will see that
the latter was simply forgotten," the deputy mayor,
Gaoussou Ouattara, who is Alassane's elder brother, observes bitterly.
"Kong existed before Cote d'Ivoire", Fabakari Ouattara the "local"
Republican authority added.
But nothing was done here, during the colonial era and since
independence.
"The only favour that Houphouet-Boigny did us was to appoint
Alassane as Prime Minister" he added, critiquing "the ingratitude of a
man for whom our parents and grand-parents fought bitterly."
With a population of about 33,000, the department of Kong
has no more than an elementary school. And the only site being set up to
build a council high school was initiated with the financial assistance
of the wife of the future MP of the constituency, Dominique Ouattara. It
is expected to open in October 2001.
There is a single hospital in the area operating under difficult
conditions with only one doctor plus a nurse and a midwife, who has been
sick and were under treatment in Abidjan for the past three months.
To make matters worse, the old matron who helped the midwife lost
her husband recently in accordance with Muslim tradition.
The old driver, who regularly provides miracle solutions with a
jolting on ambulance, on the average transfers three critical patients to
Ferkessedougou per month.
He shares the famous bumpy, laterite road with only seven
of the eleven "badjans" which connect the two cities, once a day.
Dr. Joseph Nguessan who humorously considers himself "a bush
doctor," has the hearts of people because he works around the clock
without complain.
People who were initially hostile to the doctor have now become to
cherish him after nearly three years of dedicated service.
Residents of the area generally harbour suspicion about civil
servants and State administrative officials assigned in the area.
They pray in a small villa that was transformed into a church and
get together in a small bar run by the wife of a gendarme.
Kong thus presents the particularity of being, maybe, the only
commune in Cote d'Ivoire where there is no "marquis" or church worthy of
the name!
Ivorian Electricity Company technician, responsible for repairing
the generator that provides the town with electricity idles all day in
Mrs. Kouassi bar.
Kong however has two banco-built mosques, which date as far back as
the 13th and 17th centuries. The one built in the 17th century is the
biggest and also the most famous as Emperor Samory Toure prayed in it.
These two beautiful architectural works which have resisted fleeting
history and harsh weather, next to the house where
Governor Binger stayed at the end of the last century, within the
beautiful tiny library built through French co-operation, is worth the
journey to Kong.
But the deplorable state of the road discourages tour operators who
prefer sending tourists to Senoufo territory, in Korhogo, only about 153
km away.
"Asphalting this road is first among priorities because we are
virtually land-locked," the deputy mayor said.
In addition to tourism, which could have sustained the small
commune, the improvement of yam farming, an important source of revenue
in addition to cotton and cashew nuts, is currently facing serious
problems because of difficulties of evacuation.
But an issue to which residents of Kong attachÃ© particular
importance is Alassane Dramane Ouattara's residence, where works have
been suspended for nearly a year.
The "workers stayed here for over a month without being paid. They
did not work either because there were no materials.
They finally had to leave," explained an adolescent while manually
rewinding a dozo music cassette.
Meanwhile, unless something unpredictable happens, ADO should be in
the region of his ancestors next week to start his campaign.
That will be his third visit there since his birth in Dimbokro, in
Baoule territory.
But the pending visit, which will be in all respects different from
those before, will mean a lot to his brothers with Fabakary Ouattara
saying: "We place all our hopes on this descendant of Kong."
"We hope he will shake the dust which accumulated over ages
darkening the prestigious city."

Original_Search_Title: Mills Has Betrayed Ejura
Headline: Ghana: Mills Has Betrayed Ejura
Body_Text:
Ejura — Ashanti Vice President and NDC presidential candidate,
Professor J. E. A. Mills, has been described as a failure to the
people of Ejura in the Ashanti Region. An NDC activist, Kwasi Addo
Smith, at a public forum at Ejura last week said their leader had
failed them for not honouring earlier promises.
Addo said Prof. Mills instead of talking about how he could help them
improve the production of more yams and corn, he talked about the
increase in the producer price of cocoa.
The activist questioned how many people in Ejura can boast of a cocoa
farm, as the area is known to be a non-cocoa producing area. He made
this criticism when the flagbearer of the National Reform Party
(NRP), Goosie Tanoh, toured the Ejura constituency and asked for
questions after he had given his speech.
"With this, it is clear that our party leader has nothing good to
offer the people of Ejura and for that matter we must think twice,"
added Addo. The chief of Ejura, Nana Kwaku Safo II, asked for peace,
tolerance and patience before, during and after the elections and
advised all aspirants to accept the final results in good faith.

Original_Search_Title: Goatherdism And the Rise of Village Tyrants
Headline: Kenya: Goatherdism And the Rise of Village Tyrants
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Definitions: Goatherdism is an ideology characterised by
two main features. First, it is a system of appointing and
disappointing in defiance of merit, and on the basis of tribe,
sycophancy, illiteracy and inability.
Secondly, it is the application of rudimentary, subjective and
irrational principles of goat husbandry to the complexities of
bureaucracy, government and society in general.
Village tyrant: One who is guided by this reasoning. All hens belong
to the cockerel. I am the cockerel. Therefore, all hens belong to me.
One particularly goatherdistic cockerel, called Mobutu Sese Kuku wa
Sabanga, or something in that general category, had a long name that
meant: the cockerel that never pauses to catch its breath so long as
there is a hen still on its feet.
Having dispensed with that, let us get to specifics. There is a bunch
of hooligans, thugs, layabouts and idlers that calls itself Mungiki.
This thuggish Mungiki thinks that every woman on earth should follow
its wishes - the majority of which are illogical, harmful, atavistic,
others plain stupid.
For example, it thinks taking snuff is sexy and we should all be
pushing our forefingers up our noses. They also believe that all
women should be subjected to FGM. Equally, it entertains the stupid
notion that trousers are immoral and should not be worn. Well, if the
Mungiki are men enough (and women enough), why don't they take me on
rather than assaulting innocent women? I wear trousers, too.
These are goatherds, defying the law, attempting to impose their
contemptible wills on society and deserve nothing but the most
vitriolic vilification.
If the Mungiki can answer this question, then their dictates can be
put in the law and we shall henceforth conduct ourselves in
accordance with its wishes: What do they think qualifies them (a)
judge what is, and what is not, moral? and (b) what makes their
peripheral definition of morality generalisable on the rest of
society?
There you have it my snuff stuffing good people.
Here's another. Cabinet minister Isaac Ruto has accused the Press of
malice and distortion. He has denied, very strongly, that certain
things earlier reported and has protested that they were a figment of
the media's fevered imagination and have no relationship with the
truth whatever.
It had been reported that he had boasted of not only causing certain
civil servants to be transferred, but was also in the process of
transferring others who were not either lining his toe, or toeing his
line - I forget which.
His attitude, as reported in the Press, was not only ungentlemanly
and tribal, but it showed a person with little or no concept of the
objective workings of a government. One steeped in the belief that,
for example, because he is a minister, all civil servants in his
political principality of Bomet are his to command, fire, transfer
and brag about.
I cannot but wonder how a man of these qualities would end up in the
Cabinet and what possible contribution there is for him to make to
the nation when his world-view is so insular, so jingoistic.
Like I said, the minister has denied saying these things and the
argument is, therefore, academic.
All the same, I trust that Mr. Zakayo Cheruiyot, the Permanent
Secretary in the President's Office in charge of security and
provincial administration, as well as the Civil Service boss, Dr
Richard Leakey, understand the need to shield junior civil servants
from goatherdism.
Equally, were I a civil servant and were forced to choose between
committing allegiance to the whims to a man who will humiliate me and
brag about it in public, I'd choose to go and grow yams in Meru.
Here's yet a third goat driver, a fellow in Cote d'Ivoire who has
been refusing to grow up. He overthrew the legitimate government and
has been banning opponents from running for office. The Pretext? They
were not Ivorian. Then when this ka-man lost the election, he
declared himself winner and dissolved the electoral commission.
"Ivorian strongman steals election" was the headline in one news
service.
This man was behaving in accordance with an old script. He doesn't
seem to have heard of another goatherd called Slobodan Milosevic who
attempted similar tricks only a few days earlier. They didn't work.
Happily, the Ivorians have done something similar to this buffoon.
Wednesday was a great day for Africa.
Let's return home for another case. A member of Parliament, in a fit
of populistic exhuberance, tells a baraza (as a general rule,
politicians open their mouths without engaging their brains at
barazas) that he would take a Bill to the House seeking to make
lynching legal.
Then he denies he ever said anything like that and (a) accuses the
reporter of writing fiction and (b) me of maligning and impeaching
the dignity of Parliament.
Before starting an inadvisable press war, this gentleman, whom I
insist on not naming, needs to know two things. First, reporters
don't ever write fiction. Ever. They can misunderstand, they might
add salt (for which they lose their jobs) and they may mishandle, but
they never put words in politician's mouths. It is politicians who
eat their words of their own volition.
Number two, saying the quality of parliamentary debate is bogus and
sophomoric is not to affront the dignity of the House (if such a
thing exists) but to state a fact. To deny this fact is goatherdism.
We ran out of space, we'll pick up the theme later.

Original_Search_Title: Price of Consumer Items Normalise After Lagos Crisis
Headline: Nigeria: Price of Consumer Items Normalise After Lagos Crisis
Body_Text:
Lagos — Prices of most consumer items which shot up last week in the
wake of ethnic violence in Lagos have come down to pre-crisis level.
Prices took dramatic jumps last week as the violence in the Lagos
disrupted commercial activities and kept most traders indoors.
Items like beverages and foodstuff were trapped in some areas of the
state, Alaba/Suru, Mile Two and Yaba market, thus compounding the
situation. A small tin of Bournvita sold at N180 at Alaba/Suru and
Yaba before the crisis jumped up to N210 while Milo which was
formerly sold at the cost of N190 added N30 to its cost, selling at
N230.
Moreso, a bag of garri before the riots was sold for N1400, rice
N2600 but jumped to N1700 and N2800 during the crisis.
Beverages like minerals and malts also added N5 to N10 during the
fracas.
This was because the retailers' access to source of supply was cut
off by the obstacles, thereby making the commodities scarce.
However, The Post Express had early this week gone to most
conventional markets in the metropolis to know the views of retailers
and consumers.
Most retailers spoken to complained about high cost of transportation
occasioned by scarcity of fuel and diesel during the riots last week.
This, they said, contributed to the increase of some of the consumer
items.
One of the retailers spoken to who gave her name as simply Shola said
that some products/items which went up last week had come down to
their normal prices while some remained unchanged. On the other side,
most consumers spoken to, said that prices of goods had reduced this
week, compared to what they were last week.
Although consumers have not been finding the prices easy to pay, they
still are constrained by the indispensability of the items to
continue to buy within the limits of their resources.
Most consumers said that they have tightened their consumption
instincts to enable them make both ends meet.
However, a breakdown of the composite consumer price index as
released recently by Federal Office by Statistics (FOS) indicate that
edible items take greater part of the disposable income of consumers.
Out of 1000 weight components of composite consumer price index, food
price exhausts 690.9, drinks, tobacco and kola take 47.4, clothing
and footwear 46.9 while accommodation, fuel, kerosine and light stand
at 118.7.
Nevertheless, some consumer items this week witnessed a relative
increase in their prices. Such items include garri, rice and palm
oil.
Garri, which was formerly sold for N1,400 a bag now sells for N1700
per bag while olodo which was formerly sold at N40 now sells for
N120.
Moreso, a bag of rice which was formerly sold at the cost of N2600
now sells at the rate of N2800 while palm oil which about two weeks
ago sold at the rate of N50 per bottle now sells for N60 per bottle.
Meanwhile, some items like fresh tomatoes, onions, yams and egusi
witnessed decrease in their prices this week, as farmers experienced
bumper harvests. A basket of small-size tomatoes which sold at N300
now sells at N200, medium size which sold at N700 now sells at N550
and big size tomatoes of the same measure which sold N1300 now sells
at N1000.
A trader at Iddo market said that items like tomatoes, yam, onions
and vegetable will remain cheap throughout the dry season, because
for yam it is the harvest time while it is the favourable season for
tomatoes and onions.

Original_Search_Title: The Worries Of Exporters
Headline: Ghana: The Worries Of Exporters
Body_Text:
Accra — The Ghana Export Soiree, which the Federation of Associations
of Ghanaian Exporters held at the Labadi Beach Hotel last Thursday
succeeded in bringing to light the headache exporters in the country
are having over the unprecedented free-fall of the cedi.
The function which brought together exporters and various
stakeholders in the business of exports especially the
non-traditional was intended to refocus attention on the long-term
role of the NTE sector in stemming the recurring foreign exchange
problem in the country. No wonder government officials, diplomats and
bankers were all present to grace the occasion.
The function was the first to be organised by FAGE since a new Chief
Executive in the person of Augustine Adongo was appointed to manage
the affairs of the federation which is the umbrella organization for
the various product associations like the Yam Exporters Association,
Horticultural Association of Ghana and a dozen of others. The
federation has been supported by the USAID in recognition of the
potential of the private sector in enhancing the export sector of the
economy.
The federation's president, Nana Yeboa Kodie Asare II, who delivered
the key note address at the function did not mince words when he
painted a rather gloomy picture about the nation's ability to attain
the goals of the Vision 2020. The vision envisages that the country's
total export earnings by that year would hit some $16 million with
the non-traditional export sector raking in as much as $12 million.
To attain this, the president said something radical and innovative
would have to be done. Nana Yeboa was basing his position on the high
interest rate currently prevailing in the country and the free-fall
of the cedi. The unacceptable situation, he said, was subjecting
exporters to a high degree of stress.
Apart from the general constraints facing exporters in the country he
mentioned that our exports are unable to develop internationally
competitive capacities that are consistent with the requirements of
the market.
Another problem facing exporters in the country, according to him, is
the lack of access to information on international standards and
market opportunities. Even though much has been done to remove
bottlenecks to exports, he said there still are some regulatory
requirements that frustrate exporters and investors alike.
The federation on its own intends to embark on a programme to help
members surmount some of the aforementioned problems, he disclosed.
In the coming months, for instanc, he announced that the federation
would embark on a series of programmes to delve into the issues which
are militating against the smooth operations of exporting our non
traditional items. The ultimate aim of this, he said, is to
kick-start the development of a comprehensive and integrated export
development programme and strategy of implementation.
The sponsors of the soiree, Stanchart, also briefed exporters on the
advantages in doing business with their bank. The Executive Secretary
of the Ghana Export Promotion Council, Mr. Tawiah Akyea, who
represented the Trade Minister talked about government making the
business of exports easier and the achievements so far.

Original_Search_Title: Kudirat: Worshipped In Death
Headline: Nigeria: Kudirat: Worshipped In Death
Body_Text:
Lagos — On Sunday June 4, 2000, the Lagos State Government, led by Governor
Bola Ahmed Tinubu unveiled a N3m cenotaph in memory of Alhaji Kudirat Abiola.
Femi Adepoju writes on what transpired at the occasion and affirms that
Kudirat's image looms large in the hearts of Nigeria's democratic forces.
Alhaja Kudirat Abiola couldn't have got anything less in form of a post-
humous honour. To her credit, the struggle which consumed her was eventually
won. The calibre of people who thronged the unveiling ceremony of the memorial
cenotaph done in her memory by the Lagos State Government speaks volume about
the stature of the late crusader which has not been diminished even in death.
In attendance were relevant democracy mongers. Chiefs Ayo Opadokun, Abraham
Adesanya, Frank Ovie Kokori, Dr. Beko Ransome-Kuti, Air Comdr. Dan Suleiman,
Prof. Wole Soyinka, Dr. Fredrick Fasheun and a former Governor of Kwara State,
Cornelius Adebayo were in attendance aside from several others. Of immense
importance was the ceremony that the Canadian Ambassador to Nigeria came in
person.
It has since become common knowledge that Alhaja Kudirat Abiola, wife the
acclaimed winner of June 12 1993 (annulled) presidential election was on her way
to honour an appointment at the Canadian Embassy when she met her untimely
death. She was going to Lagos in her white Mercedez Benz car when she was
silenced by an Uzzi gunshot fired by Sergeant Rogers, a ready tool in the hand
of the late maximum ruler of Nigeria Gen. Sani Abacha. Abacha felt Alhaja
Kudirat's clamour and activities to ensure the actualisation of her husband's
June 12 mandate became a reality ought to be ended by killing her.
The event of last Sunday June 4 showed that the late tyrannical ruler only
succeeded in achieving one thing: making Alhaja Kudirat a matyr whose struggle,
Commodore Dan Suleiman said he was happy is being carried forward still.
Speaking at the unveiling ceremony at which Lagos State Governor, Bola Ahmed
Tinubu and her deputy, Senator Kofoworola Bucknor-Akerele were hosts, Suleiman
observed that Kudirat did not die in vain, because she died so that democracy
can survive in Nigeria. Sulaiman also observed that though it is only Lagos
State Government that seems to be honouring Kudirat, her struggle was for the
entire nation.
"Kudi did not die for Lagos or western region alone", Suleiman contended,
adding that she worked very closely with the late crusader and discovered that
she was not fighting for a section of the country.
"I worked very closely with her during the struggle and I knew (her mind),
that she was not working for a section of the nation but all," he stressed.
Obviously counting the gains of Kudirat's death, Dr. Frederick Fasheun, the
leader of the much-dreaded Oodua People's Congress said June 4 used to be for
mourning before this present dispensation.
However, he noted that the date has now become synonymous with celebration of
the death of Kudirat who transformed from "a house wife to a legend. A legend in
the defence of democracy." Full of praise for the Lagos State Governor, Bola
Ahmed Tinubu for the special way in which he has remembered the late Kudirat,
Dr. Fasehun declared that though the late crusader died for democracy, democracy
is yet to evolve in Nigeria. What is obtained for now, he opined is the
transition towards democracy.
"We have civil governance not democracy," he argued. Amplifying his claim,
Fasehun said people won't go to bed one night thinking of petrol as costing N20
per litre and 24 hours later wake up paying N30 for the same quantity of petrol.
Eulogising the late Alhaja Kudirat, Chief Frank Kokori who himself was tucked
away in detention for an appreciable length of time for his commitment to
democracy believes that she couldn't have died for a more noble course than for
her nation. Going down memory lane, Kokori informed that he went to Jos backing
Babagana Kingibe against M.K.O. Abiola at a Social Democratic Party convention.
After sometime, Kingibe joined the ticket. It was him who took me to Abiola
and told Abiola this is the only man you can trust in my national executive
council." Kokori further recalled that, that night he was sitting with M.K.O. in
his sitting room when he called Alhaja Kudirat Abiola at about 1.00 a.m. and
said "this man sitting here is Kokori, he is one of our reliable leaders please
respect him."
Capturing the second thought of Kudirat that struck him at the unveiling
ceremony, Kokori revealed that in the midnight of 11 June 1994,at about 1.00
a.m. his phone rang and alas, it was Kudirat at the other end of the phone. She
had reported to Kokori that policemen numbering about 500 had come to whisk away
her husband asking: "what do we do?" Replying, Kokori told her to cool down.
"Madam take it easy, Mandela was in prison, don't worry, we will do our
best," he remembered telling her. He observed that the unveiling ceremony was
indeed a celebration for Kudirat since the greatest honour anybody could have in
any struggle is to die as a heroine for her country." Dr. Beko Ransome-Kuti
worked in tandem with Alhaja Kudirat Abiola during the struggle for democracy in
Nigeria. He recalled that Kudirat fought against dictatorial rule and paid for
it.
"We mourn her because by her death we lost one of those who raised a voice
against tyranny, dictatorship, misgovernance in Nigeria", Beko declared, adding
that Goodness only knows what would have happened it more people like her had
followed her example. Beko described her as an icon of democratic principles and
ideas. He said she advocated and fought for these while alive.
"She will forever remain a source of inspiration for the future generation of
Nigeria," Beko remarked. Continuing, he said as a wife, Kudirat rose to fill the
shoes of her husband when the same forces of darkness detained him. More than
can be asked for, Beko posited, was the sacrifice made by the Abiola family.
Consoling them over the double loss suffered during the dark days of Abacha,
Kuti prayed that the future would reward them in several measures. He lent his
voice to the declaration that June 12 be made Nigeria's Freedom Day as against
May 29 declared by the Federal Government.
Perhaps the high point of the celebration was the unexpected appearance of
the Abia State Governor, Orji Kalu who breezed into the univeiling venue at
about 2.45 p.m. There were some initial moves by members of the Oodua groups who
wanted to protest the governor's movement to the hightable being the official
police at the occasion, but good reasoning prevailed. What could be said to be
the highest honour expected to be bestowed on the heroine came when the Nobel
Laureate, Prof. Wole Soyinka revealed that Radio Kudirat would be moving home to
operate.
The radio station, he announced, is meant to be in the class of the world
acclaimed broadcasting stations but hinted that the resources would not be
sourced abroad . Instead, he said funds would be sourced within the country to
make the station function. The Nobel Laureat lamented in the most critical
language that the essence of the memory of Late Kudirat was being insulted by
the gale of blood letting in the country.
Pleading for a change of heart on the part of Nigerians, Soyinka said the
ideals the heroine died for must not be betrayed. Governor Bola Ahmed Tinubu
also used the occasion to recapture how in Benin Republic he became the host of
his co-exiled compatriots passing them on to Europe until it became compulsory
for him to leave the country too because the search for him had become intense
and he was almost going to be caught. His sudden departure from the West African
country shortly before Cornelius Adebayo arrived there he said made the former
Kwara State Governor to suffer as he had to live in a dingy room for about a
year before the Canadian government came to his aid.
Governor Tinubu also described how he used to disguise to the market in Benin
Republic on a motor bike (Okada) to buy yam flour and fish. The occasion did not
go without the students anouncing their presence in a way that has now become
their regular style. Chief Ayo Opadokun was harrassed at the venue by the
students who jeered and booed him because of what was described as his support
for the Offa indigenes against the Offa Polytechnic students in the recent face
off between the indigenes of the town and the students. Opadokun later described
the experience as the price of leadership. He hails from Offa. Kudirat's
cenotaph which cost N3m was unveiled by Governor Tinubu, Chief Abraham Adesanya
and Prof.
Wole Soyinka whose name is expected to be engraved on the plaque by the
cenotaph later since he was not originally billed to be part of the unveiling.
Made of brass, the cenotaph erected in the toll gate area of the 7-Up Alausa
Junction depicts celebration of freedom, while the side overlooking the 7-Up
area shows agonising faces of a mother, students and citizens crushed by the
tyrannical rule of the military; with the portrait of Kudirat Abiola by the
side, depicting the window through which the wind of freedom blew in. Kudirat's
immediate family was ably represented by Kola Abiola at the epoch making event
event.

Original_Search_Title: Scorched To Death
Headline: Nigeria: Scorched To Death
Body_Text:
Lagos — Not a few people perish as a petrol tanker loses control and crashes
into the ever-busy Obodoukwu Road Market, Okpoko. Everybody has his palm
clasped to his nose as flies keep hovering around, attracted, of course, by the
foul smell emitting from the spot where the charred remains of the petrol
tanker still lies.
Scattered around the vicinity are what remain of yam tubers, tomatoes,
pepper, cassava and other assorted food items which belonged to the roadside
traders.
And close by, a white coffin lies in wait, with the attendants
intermittently wiping tears from their blood-shot eyes. Evidently, they have
wept to no end.
That was the poignant picture last Thursday, at the ever-busy Obodoukwu
Road, Okpoko, where traders have found a market. Indeed, the market is a
typical case of illegal street-trading. Nobody, however, seems to have ever
raised an eye-brow. So, the people must have taken the situation as normal,
hence nobody anticipated the event of Monday, 20 March. Eyewitness accounts
gave the time as "between 5.30 and 6pm." A petrol tanker whose driver obviously
lost control swept everything on its way as it descended the road. Exactly
opposite the NEPA service station, it rammed into the gutter and lay still.
In no time, sympathisers and rescuers alike were said to have gathered to
salvage those trapped therein. Then, the unexpected happened.
A deafening explosion occurred pushing a great quantity of petrol outwards
in different directions. The accompanying inferno "spread with rocket speed"
according to Rufus Maduekwe, a beans-shop keeper who told TheNEWS he saw it
all. No fewer than 50 persons were said to have been trapped.
Fire-fighters were said to have come from Asaba, the Delta State capital,
but before then, the damage had been done. The ailing landlord in front of
whose house the tanker crashed could not muster enough strength to vacate his
house which had gone up in flames. Consequently, he perished with it. The white
coffin was for the remains of a woman trader who could only be identified by
the colour of her wrapper which was not completely burnt.
Similarly, a six-month-old baby was discovered in the gutter the following
day having been washed there by the torrential rain that fell later that Monday
night. Its mother, as at last week, was still lying under critical condition at
Pieter Hospital, Owerri Road oblivious of what had become of her baby.
Some traders were lucky. Those whose "God did not sleep" on that day as
Onyeka Onyeanwuna put it were taken to Chibike Hospital and Maternity Home, on
Obodoukwu Road, Pieter Hospital, Njidechukwu Hospital and Graceland Hospital,
Okpoko. Onyeanwuna himself was one of those lucky few. The young, barrow-
pusher, told this magazine that he had gone to help rescue those initially
trapped under the tanker when the explosion occurred and entrapped him. But he
miraculously escaped death.
Nevertheless, he said he had already rescued one man, who though had one of
his legs broken, escaped the fatal explosion.
The same fate also befell Friday Ani and Joseph Eze, both of them barrow-
pushers too. Ani regretted that the man he was about rescuing went with the
fire. At Chibike Hospital, a nurse said a pregnant woman amongst others whose
condition were not very critical had been discharged.
As at last Thursday, nothing had been done to evacuate the trailer and those
trapped underneath it. Distended entrails were quite visible suggesting that a
whole lot of corpses were not washed away by the rain of that fateful Monday
night. Maduekwe said it was, in fact, the rain which finally put out the fire,
and "carried away many dead bodies."
Since the tragedy, Obodoukwu has become a Mecca of sorts, albeit an agony-
inducing one, for those whose relations and friends are no more. A NEPA staff
who pleaded anonymity told TheNEWS that a similar incident had occurred in the
same area in 1985, claiming over a hundred lives.
To avert more of such incidents in the future, he suggested that Ogbanu
Local Government Area where Okpoko belongs, should ban road-side trading. Until
the government hearkens to this advice, there may yet be more of such
unpleasant occurrences, since the traders are back to the roadside, displaying
their wares!
Publication date: April 3, 2000

Original_Search_Title: 58 Malian Child-Labourers Return Home
Headline: Mali: 58 Malian Child-Labourers Return Home
Body_Text:
Bamako, Mali (PANA) — Fifty-eight Malian youths illegally recruited from
various parts of the country to work in plantations in neighbouring Cote
d'Ivoire have returned home.
Officials said in Bamako Friday that the children aged between 12 and 17
years, arrived at the border town of Sikasso, some 380 kilometres from Bamako,
the Malian capital, on Thursday.
Nine of the children who returned were sick while one girl was pregnant, the
officials said, adding that they were retrieved from five camps in the district
of Mankono in Cote d'Ivoire, where they worked in cotton, corn and yam
plantations.
An official of the Malian consulate who accompanied the children said they
were removed from the plantations after Ivorian authorities decided to check the
trafficking of Malian children brought to work in the country.
Several sources stated that most of the children worked in plantations and
were forced to take drugs to improve their performance in farm work.
A recent publication of the United Nations Children's Fund whose regional
bureau for West Africa is headquartered in Abidjan, confirmed the existence of
networks for the trafficking of Malian children used as farm hands in Ivorian
plantations.

Original_Search_Title: We Are Battle Ready
Headline: Nigeria: We Are Battle Ready
Body_Text:
Lagos — Joe Igbokwe, an Igbo, a writer and a social critic, tackled
the Igbo question in the Nigerian polity. He spoke with ABIMBOLA
OGUNNAIKE.
Q: What is your view on the recent attack on Igbo in Kaduna and the
reprisal attacks in the East?
A: The attack on Igbo is not new in this nation. It started in Jos
in 1945, Kano in 1953, the entire North in 1966 to 1970 and that
culminated in the civil war in which millions of Igbo lives were lost.
Maitatsine religious riots in Kano which occurred in 1980 with very
dire consequences on the Igbo. In 1982, the riot came to Maiduguri. In
1984, it was in Jimeta, Yola. It was in Gombe in 1985. There, the Igbo
were killed, their property looted. The killing spree came to Kaduna
and Kafancha in 1987. In 1991, it was in Bauchi, Katsina and Kano. In
1992, in Zango-Kataf, Igbo were the primary targets because any time
you have a problem in the North, the Igboman is the primary target.
This is because of the type of business they do. They display their
spare parts, they display their building materials, they display the
electronics and so on. In 1993, the riot spread to Funtua. In 1994, you
would recall, Gideon Akaluka, an Igbo citizen, was murdered in Kano. He
was beheaded, his head was paraded in the streets of Kano. All these
attacks have very dire consequences for us, the Igbo. So if you push
somebody to the wall, he would have to react. It is becoming a
tradition that any time you have a crisis in the North, the Igbo bear
the brunt.
I think what happened in the East was pent up emotions of the sordid
past. Already, we have accepted, for 55 years, the senseless killings
of Igbo from 1945 to year 2000. But now, we will fight back. The
attacks will not be allowed again. It will not be condoned by Igbo
people. We are battle-ready. This is because no one section of the
country has a monopoly of violence. No one religion has a monopoly of
violence. I think what happened in the East, as a result of the recent
Kaduna riot, is a warning signal to anybody that Igbo nation of year
2000 is not going to accept this killing, this looting, this
destruction of lives and property. It is no longer acceptable. And if
any Igboman, I am telling you now, is attacked anywhere in this
country, we will respond as quickly as possible within 12 hours from
now on. What happened recently was just a signal. If it happens again,
it is going to be very devastating. We are not going to take it again.
Q: Don't you think this can lead to another civil war just like that
of 1967?
A: Yes. Why should people continue to repeat history. What happened
has shown that men have learnt nothing from history. Despite the
history of tragic dictators all over the world, Adolf Hitler and co.,
Idi Amin of Uganda, General Zia Ul-Haq of Pakistan, Nicolae Ceausescu
of Romania and even General Abacha too, men have learnt nothing from
history. We don't want to go along that line again because no one
nation fights civil a war and lives together. But the meaning of
Nigerian nation, just like my brother, Nkemba of Nnewi, has said,
should not be that of blood-letting. It should not be that of killing.
If the meaning of Nigerian nation is to kill one ethnic group any time
there is crisis, then the nation has no meaning. We had better return
to our respective areas.
Q: What is then going to be Igbo's gains if Nigeria breaks up?
A: We will survive.
Q: In what ways?
A: We have all the capacity to survive. We survived three years of
blockade during the civil war. We were able to manufacture our own
petroleum products. We were able to build our own rockets hitting their
targets with precision. We had our means of getting things done. We
refined petrol and brake fluids from coconut.
Q: (Cuts in). But you don't have access to the sea.
A: What happens to River Niger? Don't you know we have River Niger
at Onitsha? It can be dredged and it would link the Atlantic Ocean
through Calabar. President Shehu Shagari attempted to dredge the river
in 1983 as a gimmick for the election he won and he later abandoned the
project. Even if we don't have a standing river, Igboman can do two
things. We run the engine of this nation, without being immodest. I
want to tell you that Igbo run the engines of this nation. Most of our
people are scientists. Most of our people are engineers. Most of our
people are doctors. So, we have the wherewithal. We have the capacity,
we have the human resources, we have the material resources to give us
an edge. Eighty per cent of nations which have come from the former
Eastern Region are going by the grace of God. We are going with our
immediate brothers.
Q: Who are your immediate brothers?
A: The Akwa Ibom, the Cross River, the Rivers.
Q: But they believe that you have been marginalising them in the
scheme of things.
A: No. It is not true. We have allowed them. They thought we were
their enemies. If a woman feels that she is being cheated, she should
be allowed to marry another man. So, they thought we were cheating them
and we were their problems. So they tried to marry the other side. They
tried the other husband, that is their Northern brothers. They will go
with their immediate neighbours. If I have problem at 7, Igbokwe Street
here, people at No. 4, No. 9 will gather before you begin to look for
my brothers who are not even around here. Don't you know that recently,
South-South governors were to meet with the Eastern governors but that
was aborted. We have a cordial relationship with them. And I can tell
you, when we go to Igbo fora, we will tell a good number of them from
Calabar, from Rivers and others that we are brothers. In fact, the
emphasis will no longer be on Igbo nation but on the former Eastern
Region. That is the way it should be. All the time, we should not be
talking about three major ethnic groups. There are more ethnic groups
in this country that are not up to 100,000 people. If we break away, if
Igbo nation breaks away, if Yoruba and Hausa nations break away, what
happens to these small ethnic groups? Where will the Ogoni be? Where
will these small, small tribes that are not up to 50,000 people be? You
think they can be a nation? That is the tragedy.
Q: How do we relate with one another if Nigeria breaks?
A: I don't want to consider that possibility. I don't want to look
at that option. Nigeria will not break up because we have so many
things to gain by staying together.
Q: What are the gains?
A: Very many. When you go to Ketu in Lagos, people from the North
bringing tomatoes, bringing beans, bringing yams, bringing goats,
bringing cows and so on. That is something. And the East, they go with
their industries, they move to the North, they move to the West with
their spare parts and all that. And the Yoruba with their
intellectuals, have control over many of the financial institutions and
insurance companies. They are professionals. That is the benefit from
the Yoruba nation. There are so many things Nigeria will get from the
Igbo nation. There are so many things Nigeria will get from the North.
We have so many things to gain by remaining together.
Q: What is the Igbo agenda in the nation called Nigeria?
A: I can tell you, Igboman has the greatest stake in Nigeria. A
Yorubaman can move from here to Sao Tome, to Brazil, to Liberia to
Sierra-Leone. They have their kinsmen there. Even Togo there, they have
over three million people there. I have made this point clear in
several fora. The Hausaman has his kinsmen from here to Saudi Arabia to
Niger, so they can afford to go there any time there is crisis. Igboman
has no outlets anywhere in the world. There is no place in the world
where Igbo is spoken. That is why you see them they have accepted
Nigeria as their own. They reside in every nook and cranny of Nigeria.
Recently, they have invested about N8.5 billion in Lagos here, moving
out of Idumota auto spare parts market to another place near
International Trade Complex along Oshodi-Apapa expressway They are
investing N8.5 billion in that project.
Q: What are the problems of the North?
A: They don't want other people to succeed where they have failed.
President Olusegun Obasanjo has been able to correct all that in very a
short period and they are not happy about it. Look at how much money
Abacha looted. In fact, it is described as one of the biggest search
for stolen money in the history of the world. We are not happy that a
leader from the North is being portrayed as a looter, the worst leader
any nation has ever produced in the whole world. It is no longer
possible for them to beckon to the military any time they lose
elections. With their grip of the military, most of them were so blind
that they were pursuing career in the military that they forgot that
they have to send their children to school. They thought the military
would live forever, and they would control the machinery of power
forever. Since Nigeria gained independence in 1960, they have been at
the helm of the affairs. They have been there for so many years that
they don't even remember that they have other Nigerians to share power
with. They don't believe that one day, this leadership will swing to
other people. So, some people cannot manage life after power.
Publication date: March 27, 2000

Original_Search_Title: Census Not For Tax Purposes - DCE
Headline: Ghana: Census Not For Tax Purposes - DCE
Body_Text:
Accra — The BirIm North District Chief Executive, Prince Yaw Danso,
has dispelled the misconception prevailing in the district that census
figures will be used as a basis for the imposition of more property
rates and other taxes.
He said this when he addressed the chiefs and people of Akokoaso at
their annual celebration of Yam Festival at Akokoaso, recently.
Yaw Danso said since the formation of the Birim North district (BND)
in 1987, this is going to be their maiden census exercise, which will
help the district assembly to know their actual population figures,
adding that this in turn will help accelerate development in the
district.
He said with the expansion of Akokoaso township, the assembly is to
embark on a pipe-borne water project, which will take off during the
middle of this year, alongside their self-help electrification project.
In his welcome address, the chief of Akakoaso, Nana Danso Abeam II,
appealed to the Ministry of Health (MOH) through the District Chief
Executive to post a midwife to their clinic to help in the health
delivery system in the town.

Original_Search_Title: Midlands Tourney Set For Tomorrow
Headline: Africa: Midlands Tourney Set For Tomorrow
Body_Text:
Lusaka — The Midlands Pool Organisation (MPO) in conjunction with
Sams Sports Cafe have lined-up a pool championship tournament as part
of Youth Week celebrations.
The events organiser, Kabungo Kamanga yesterday said the tournament
which starts tomorrow at 16:00 hours will attract eight players in the
first round.
"On Saturday at 12:00hrs another eight players clash and these will
qualify to the semi-finals. The 16 will then battle it out until two
reach the finals the following day.
The champion will walk away with a trophy, tape recorder and
K200,000 cash while the runner-up will bag a gift voucher worth
K100,000, Kamanga said.
He said the competition was open to both male and female players as
long as they paid the K 15,000 participation fee at Sam's Sports Cafe.
He paid tribute to Yams Motors, Bolder, Axis Centre, Dunns, Dulys
Motors, Compudoc and Bonel Holdings for sponsoring the tournament.

Original_Search_Title: The Changing Face Of Ado Ekiti
Headline: Nigeria: The Changing Face Of Ado Ekiti
Body_Text:
Lagos — Before Ekiti State was created in October 1996 by the regime
of the late Gen. Sani Abacha, Ado Ekiti which became its capital, was a
rustic town. There was a plethora of old buildings which, given the
aesthetics of the time, were status symbols for cocoa and yam farmers.
These days, state-of-the-art structures, owned by government and high
networth private citizens, are springing up by the day.
The Government House, formerly owned by the local government, has
been given a face lift.
The Agricultural Credit Corporation building, opposite the Fajuyi
Memorial Park, Iyin-Ekiti road, has now been converted to the Ministry
of Justice. Beside it is the high court, the front of which wears the
structure of a giant letter "M", the handiwork of Architect Fola Alade,
the designer of all Federal secretariats in the country.
A spear throw from the Governor's Lodge is a block of four duplexes
with black gates and three stars in base relief etched on them. The
property is owned by Chief Jobi Fele, a past chairman of the Odu'a
Group of Companies. A source told TheNEWS Property that the structure
gulped about N5 million. It is yet to be occupied despite its
completion.
On a ridge at the back of Ola Oluwa Grammar School in Ilawe road, is
a group of buildings belonging to Otunba Reuben Famuyibo, a past
governorship aspirant in the state. The two buildings which look like
medieval castles, have the air of being deserted as moss and algae have
sprouted out of some portions of the fence, and the macadamised
entrance. The buildings lie almost fallow for the owner lives in
Ibadan. Famuyibo, according to a source, sunk N30 million into the
project. Another lone structure, a monument to envy, stands adjacent
Famuyibo's St. Peter's Castle. High heeled individuals now take a
liking to the ridge.
In other parts of the town, rich indigenes also have their country
homes. At Ureje (Ikere road), Chief Afe Babalola (a Senior Advocate of
Nigeria) has his glass house. With the massive gate and fence, however,
passers-by may not see the structures inside. There are also Bisi
Egbeyemi's law chambers near Total petrol station; and the Fajuyi Lodge
(built by the children of the late Lt.-Col. Adekunle Fajuyi, governor
of the Western Region).
A golf course at Erinfun, on the way to Ikare, is an addition to
efforts at bringing the atmosphere of Lagos, Ibadan or Port Harcourt to
Ado Ekiti.
Speaking generally on the new structures in the town, Architect
Alade told The News Property that on the government side, no building
could be called state-of-the art. He added, however, that this could be
because the state government wanted to cut its coat according to its
cloth. Alade attributed this to the "refusal of the Federal Government
to give our state a take-off grant when it was created".
Publication date: March 13, 2000

Original_Search_Title: Those days are gone (Opinion)
Headline: Sierra Leone: Those days are gone (Opinion)
Body_Text:
Freetown — Those who organized the just ended Youth Festival at the
National Stadium did this nation the kind of service that has been the
exclusive preserve of Mammy Dumbuya church namely, trauma healing.
If you were there at the stadium last Sunday and you saw the sea of
heads, and the way people let off steam with such casualness, you
certainly would have a picture of the customary Sierra Leonean penchant
for happiness, one that vanished since the war became a monster. And
you would ask why the rebels just wont let the people be happy.
There is something alluring about Sierra Leone to the extent that
those who normally come for a fleeting romance end up getting hooked
and finally taking the country to the altar. That was how James
Oguoguo, a successful editor of a Lagos tabloid, came from Nigeria and
has not been able to get back to his fatherland.
A reenactment of the quintessential Sierra Leone was what shone at
the stadium. It was a period when the latent power of happiness burst
and took a sweet revenge on the agony of bloodshed, arson, rape and the
choleric peace process. It was a period when Sierra Leoneans freed
themselves from physical and psychological anguish to become part of
humanity.
There they were the members of the Sierra Leone Police Force, my
real revelation, with shoulders high rather than heads bowed, selling
the image of the new-look police with such fervor and commitment you
would want to cry. And of course rivulets of tears strolled down my
cheeks seeing a much-maligned group telling the people the bad old days
were gone and that a new era had dawned. You needed to see Sergeant
Calker on stage singing sweet melodies with superb aplomb while
thousands, including yours truly, stomped feet in reciprocation to the
excellent rendition of the lyrics.
The thousands who thronged the stadium to mingle and shuffle slept
till perhaps midday yesterday and woke up to confront yet again today's
reality. What happened at the stadium therefore becomes a trap in a
trance, not unlike a quick dose of anesthetics whose effect has the
temporariness of the morning dew - which disappears as quickly as it
comes. I have spent the last hours ruminating on how to ensure a
continuity of the people's happiness the way they were last Sunday. I
felt I could speak with either YES Minister, Dr. Alpha Wurie or his
Deputy Abass Collier - both terrifically creative individuals - to
suggest a bi-monthly festival of the kind that's just ended. Again I am
thinking that will be a cosmetic solution.
When I first came to Sierra Leone in 1991, the government didn't
need to organize festivals to get people on the streets on weekends, as
if nothing tied them down, to make merry and live long. No, it didn't
happen that way; it came naturally: the economy had not nose-dived, war
was a faraway cry in neighbouring Liberia, and social life was upbeat.
Alas, those days are gone. Those who made merry in the early nineties
buoyed by the diamond boom now walk about with hunchbacks beaten by the
war doom.
They were there at the Stadium with smiles that didn't quite appear
natural. Those people woke up yesterday afternoon to discover that the
status quo is as constant as the cotton tree. They will not have food
to eat, they will not have money for taxi fares, they will not know
what is happening with the disarmament process, they will sleep today
with one eye open because the rebels could pay a visit, they will hear
stories of corruption, they will be told the rebels are digging their
diamonds, they will hear of a Peter Vandy and of a Foday Sankoh, they
will be told their President is Dr. Ahmad Tejan Kabbah, they will be
told of fresh obstacles to peace and they will sigh in resignation, at
the futility of pretending to be smiling.
Sierra Leone that used to cuddle and nestle new comers now repels.
The snaky Hill Station hills have lost their alluring power and now
look haggard like a window. The beautiful beaches seem like yam
tendrils gone astray because the farmer is too weak to attend to them.
The physical deterioration of the country finds a willing and uncanny
accomplice in the people's psyche, which now runs at zero altitude.
There is an alarming brain drain. Sierra Leone's best brains now
traverse the length and breath of the globe in search of something,
anything, to do to prevent the soul from divorcing the body. They have
no real country to call theirs. In their imagination what exists is
only a geographical expression.
The faces we see on the streets are no happy faces; they are faces
bated in misery. The question comes: what do we do to bring back happy
faces? The answer is simple: give the people peace.
Among the multitude at the National Stadium were scores of rebels.
They mixed freely with all including the one-legged lady in crutches
who hopped around happily dancing to the music of Lucky Dube. Her right
leg had been severed by rebels and what is left is a stomp that stops
just above the kneel level wrapped in white bandage.
This woman may never be able to do anything constructive again in
her life but she could still be happy if she feels secure. The
possibility that the remaining leg could go sometime is what must be
erased.
Foday Sankoh has a duty to bring back those happy faces; he has a
responsibility to ensure that this lady's other leg does not go in
future; that people are no longer dispatched in a pogrom in a grisly
spectacle. The days of merry making must come back.
They are gone but they can make a u-turn. Sankoh knows what to do to
ensure this.
It is his challenge.

Original_Search_Title: Igboland On Fire
Headline: Nigeria: Igboland On Fire
Body_Text:
Lagos — Sharia killings in Kaduna excites reprisal attacks in the
Igbo towns of Aba and Owerri. Ebelo Goodluck & Ben Eguzozie report
Like a yam seedling divested of its first shoot, Sharia is ramifying
across the land as Nigerians wake up to its daily horrors. In Aba and
Owerri, bodies of slain Igbomen and women arriving from the Kaduna
mayhem stoke a flame of pent up anger. Thirty-two lives were reportedly
lost on the first day of rioting. The figure is bound to rise as the
death toll is ascertained in the coming days.
Aba, a huge commercial centre with a large feisty population of
young traders was just the right spot of a reprisal attack. Given that
it's a terminus for much of road transportation from and to the East,
it naturally became port of disembarkment for corpse of Igbos felled by
another round of religion-inspired killings in Kaduna. The gory
spectacle incensed a restive population.
And from Aba's shops and shacks, thousands of youths poured into the
streets wielding guns, axes, daggers and other weapons of destruction.
Many roadblocks were mounted on the road from the North. Bodies
littered the streets of Aba, especially on the outskirts where the
Hausas reside. And like in Kaduna, the security forces were caught
flat-footed by the rioters who had a field day venting their spleen on
human and vehicles they could identify.
Reprisals in the East are rather rare. The Igbos and many other
southern ethnic groups have long borne the brunt of the fury of Hausa
and Fulani zealots. Only recently, a riot in Sagamu between alleged
Oodua Peoples' Congress (OPC) members of the Yoruba ethnic group had
sparked off a retaliatory killing of Yorubas in Kano. The Aba riots
seems to follow the Sagamu, Kano trend.
Since the inauguration of the Olusegun Obasanjo regime, the
implementation of Sharia-Islamic law, has generated heated exchange
between its proponents, and Christians and other Nigerians who feel it
violates the country's secular constitution. What has been in dispute
is the implementation of the criminal codes of the Sharia. The Zamfara
State government had already adopted the whole gamut of Sharia, so has
Niger State.
Attempts to impose Sharia in Kaduna-a state with almost equal
population of Christians and Muslims-had cost the country over 300
lives and millions of naira in charred property.
The regime itself has been slow to action, pleading a reluctance not
to be seen as a prosecutor and the judge in its own case. But the
recent Kaduna killings seem to have pried open Obasanjo's sealed lips.
Last week, he described the Zamfara and Niger state governments'
action as illegal. And in Kaduna city itself, to assess the damage,
Obasanjo was overwhelmed by the scale of the destruction: "I feel very
sad for what I have seen because I could not imagine the magnitude of
destruction. As somebody who had his early military life here in Kaduna
and developed here, you can imagine how I feel... ." Obasanjo posed
questions Nigerians are waiting for him to provide answers for. Was it
pre-planned or it was spontaneous? Was it the handiwork of hoodlums who
hijacked what would have been a peaceful demonstration? While Obasanjo
seemed marooned in his puzzles, Odumegwu Ojukwu is all clear of the
political motivation of the Kaduna attack." There can be no pretence
that this matter,... is religious.... I believe and I do stress that
this matter is political. I urge the authorities to take immediate and
appropriate steps to curb the political tension... ."
Ojukwu laments the brunt bearer role his people have had to play
over the years. "Our brothers and sisters and indeed, our co-
religionists is another episode in our unending accursed national saga.
We are now being forced again to ask, how much more sacrifice do we
have to make before we can be embraced as equal citizens of one
Nigeria."
Chief Uche Momah, president of the Igbo speaking community in Lagos
clearly miffed at the development wants a radical reorganisation of
Igbo commerce and politics. "It is time the Igbos thought of backward
integration. Let the Igbo rethink also their alignment."
The feeling in Igboland is that of complete outrage at the killings.
If anything, the Obasanjo administration would have to do more than
mere talking to douse the Sharia conflagration.
Additional report by Emeka Ibemere.
Publication Date: 9 March 2000

Original_Search_Title: IRIN-WA Update of events in West Africa
Headline: West Africa: IRIN-WA Update of events in West Africa
Body_Text:
Abidjan — CHAD: French grants 17.6 million euro for drainage systems -
Residents of two southern Chadian towns and the capital, Ndjamena, who often
must wade through their rain-flooded neighbourhoods may soon get some relief.
Chad and the French aid agency, Agence francaise de developpement (AFP),
signed a 17.6-million-euro (US $17.48 million) deal on Wednesday to build
drains in the southern towns of Moundou and Sarah, and in Ndjamena.
The official French news agency, AFP, reported that Moundou and Sarah will
get 3.9 million euro ($3.87 million) of this grant and Ndjamena 8.3 million
euro ($8.25 million). It said 5.4 million euros ($5.36 million) would go to
improving sanitation in the western provinces of Lac and Kanem.
This is the first time the AFD has given money for the health sector, in
line with its new duties following the reorganisation of French development
aid, AFP said.
SIERRA LEONE: Land mines pose limited problem, UN says
A United Nations investigation team in Sierra Leone has found that land
mines pose a "limited problem" in the country which can be dealt with by the UN
mission there, UNAMSIL, a spokeswoman for the Secretary-General said on
Wednesday.
The UN Mine Action Service completed a week-long technical assessment
mission on Sunday to determine the scope of the problem of land mines and
unexploded ordnance in Sierra Leone, Maria Okabe told reporters in New York.
The team held discussions with the government, the warring factions, the UN
mission and other parties. Access has not yet been secured to all areas of the
country and the team travelled to Kabala in the north of Sierra Leone and to
Kenema and Daru in the east to investigate mines and unexploded ordnance.
Okabe said the team recommended that a mine action information and
coordination centre be established as part of the peacekeeping operation.
According to Save Heritage and Rehabilitate the Environment (SHARE), a local
NGO, a 12-year-old lost an eye last week after picking up a land mine at Yams
Farm on the outskirts of Freetown. SHARE has repeatedly expressed concern over
the lack of information on the whereabouts of mines laid during the conflict in
Sierra Leone.
SIERRA LEONE: Limb-fitting equipment give to Handicap International
Handicap International (HI) has been given equipment to enable it to provide
artificial limbs for ex-combatants who have gone through the Disarmament,
Demobilisation and Reintegration (DDR) programme, according to an information
bulletin published on 10 February by the National Committee for Disarmament,
Demobilisation and Reintegration (NCDDR).
Valued at US $48,000 the equipment, which was donated by the British
Department for International Development (DFID), was handed over to HI on
Tuesday by Sierra Leone President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah on behalf of his
government.
NCDDR cites a representative of the amputees, Muctarr Jalloh, as saying they
needed help that would enable them to reduce their dependence on others.
SIERRA LEONE: DDR sensitisation efforts continue
Members of NCDDR's information unit travelled to Bo and Kenema last week in
their latest attempt to spread the word on the Disarmament, Demobilisation and
Reintegration (DDR) programme, according to NCDDR's latest information
bulletin.
"The decision to venture into the provinces, which is considered long
overdue, was taken in response to the uncomfortable realisation that many
people including ex-combatants and their commanders do not know much about the
DDR programme," NCDDR said.
During meetings with KISS 104 FM and SLBS 93.5, provincial radio stations in
Bo and Kenema respectively, the team provided broadcast material on the DDR
programme, including the newly recorded DDR song.
For the past five months NCDDR has been running a DDR radio programme on the
Sierra Leone Broadcasting Service which has only been heard in the Freetown
peninsula as it was not broadcast on shortwave.
SIERRA LEONE: Food aid to Freetown camps to phase down
Plans to phase out the provision of food aid to some displaced people living
in camps and around the Freetown peninsula are underway, the UN Humanitarian
Assistance Coordination Unit (HACU) said in its most recent situation report,
17-30 January.
HACU said that only displaced people from safe areas or those wishing to
return to their homes would not receive food.
"Resettlement rations, food for work, food for training and related projects
will be provided for those who wish to resettle," HACU said. "Those who live in
areas that are still not safe will remain in camps and continue to receive
support."
NIGERIA: Suspect arrested over oil fire
A boy has been arrested by police in connection with a petroleum fire which
began on Monday and killed 17 people in Abia State, 'The Guardian' newspaper of
Lagos reported.
The fire, which firefighters have put out, started in a pit dug by villagers
to collect petrol leaking from a burst oil pipe belonging to the Petroleum
Products Marketing Company (PPMC), the paper said on Thursday. The fire was
believed to have been ignited by a boy lighting a match in the air saturated
with petrol fumes. PPMC sources said that pipelines in the area had been
vandalised.
Villagers in Ogwe, Ukwa-West Council Area had been fetching the product from
the pit for two months and selling 60 litres of petrol for about 1,000 naira
(US $1), the daily said. In October 1998, at least 700 people died in a
pipeline fire near Warri, in the Niger Delta, while trying to siphon off petrol
from a burst pipeline.
NIGERIA: Judicial inquiry into corruption at state carrier
In its continuing battle against corruption the government has set up a
juridical commission of enquiry into the running of Nigeria Airways between
1983 to 1999, AFP reported the justice minister as saying on Wednesday.
Kanu Agabi, who inaugurated the seven-member commission on Tuesday, told
reporters it was under orders to "bring to book perpetrators of corruption and
economic crimes", according to AFP. A report by a government panel of inquiry
released in January said corruption and mismanagement at the airline, during
the period under review, cost Nigeria some US $180 million. The phenomenon also
left the carrier with $7.7 million in local debts and $71.4 million owed to
foreign creditors, AFP quoted the report as saying.
The government has pledged to privatise the airline and last week an
official said the government an had reserved rights to 10 top destinations for
up to five years from privatisation. This fact, AFP reported the head of the
Bureau of Public Enterprises as saying, would hopefully increase bidding for
the airline.
NIGERIA: Government probes use of cocoa funds
The federal government has set up a seven-member panel to investigate
alleged mismanagement of the cocoa levy and butter stock funds over the years,
'The Guardian' reported on Thursday.
Commerce Minister Mustapha Bello said a "critical appraisal" of the
situation was needed. He spoke of "erratic demands" ranging from the decision
to grant a US $8-million loan to the federal ministry of transport in 1995 and
another $15 million loan to cocoa operators.
President Olusegun Obasanjo pledged at his inauguration on 29 May to fight
corruption at levels of government. "I will give the forthright, purposeful,
committed, honest, and transparent leadership that the situation demands,"
Obasanjo said as he was being sworn in. He added that there would be "no sacred
cows".
NIGERIA: Jackson leads trade delegation
The US special envoy to Africa Jesse Jackson is in Lagos at the head of a
trade delegation to Nigeria, news organisations reported on Wednesday.
On arrival Jackson told reporters that the climate was ripe for business and
foreign investment. He described Nigeria as being blessed with markets, money,
location and talents, ingredients which made it attractive to a US investor.
The mission is made up of business representatives and experts in the fields
of energy, telecommunications, agriculture, transport and insurance.
NIGERIA: UNESCO highlights education in its cooperation
President Olusegun Obasanjo and UNESCO Director-General Koichiro Matsuura
signed a memorandum on Tuesday expressing their commitment to reinforced
cooperation between Nigeria and the organisation, the UN agency said.
Obasanjo spoke of education as a national priority for Nigeria and of the
importance the country attached to ongoing reform in this area. Matsuura
assured him of UNESCO's full support.
Nigeria is a member of the E-9 group. This is a group of nine highly
populated countries that account for half the world's people and 70 percent of
global illiteracy, UNESCO said on Wednesday.
LIBERIA: Human rights group wants bail for detainees
A human rights group has filed a motion for bail for five people arrested in
1998 and accused of treason, Star radio reported on Thursday,
The Centre for the Promotion of Human Rights said the accused, who are being
detained at Monrovia Central Prison, have not yet been indicted and maintain
that their prolonged detention violates their rights to due processes of the
law. The rights group said the detainees should be given bail until the
government proves a capital offence against them, Star reported.
LIBERIA: Telecommunications partly restored
The Liberia Telecommunications Corporation (LTC) has partly restored
services to Monrovia following a fire at the company's main facility on Sunday,
Star reported.
An LTC spokesman said that some lines on the national exchange are now
working and efforts were ongoing to return the situation to normal.
Investigations into the cause of the fire are being carried out by agents from
the Ministry of Justice, the National Security Agency, police and LTC
management, Star reported.
This item is delivered by the UN's IRIN humanitarian information unit (e-
mail: irin@ocha.unon.org; fax: +254 2 622129; Web:
http://www.reliefweb.int/IRIN), but may not necessarily reflect the views of
the United Nations. If you re-print, copy, archive or re-post this item, please
retain this credit and disclaimer.

Original_Search_Title: The African film making experience
Headline: Africa: The African film making experience
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — Gaston Kabore is an award winning film maker from
Burkina Faso. He completed his studies at the Sorbonne University,
France, and has been working in the film industry ever since. He has
worked as technical cinema advisor for the Ministry of Information and
Culture, as well as Director of the National Centre of Cinema.
From 1985-1997 Gaston Kabore served as General Secretary of the Pan-
African Film Directors Federation. He has also been a member of the
judging panel at the Cannel Film Festival.
His film repertoire include Wend Kuuni (the gift of God), 1982, which
won a Cesar award for Best French Speaking Film, Zan Boko 1988, winner
of the best Screenplay prize at Belgium's Namur Festival; Rabi, 1992;
and Buud Yam (Stallion of Yennenga) 1997, which won five awards at the
15th Fespaco Film Festival, 1997, including grand prize. Since 1979
Gaston Kabore has been working on the Executive Committee of the
Ouagadougou Film Festival.
Gaston Kabore was among the facilitators at M-Net's New Directions
Africa workshop that ended on the 22nd January, 2000. The workshop, held
off the coast of Senegal on the historic island of Goree, brought
together established and emerging script writers, directors and
producers from seven African countries for a series of workshops on
various aspects of film making, which ended with the production of a
short film-seven takes on the same script.
Gaston Kabore has been working with script writers from each country,
including Manyazewal Endashaw of Ethiopia, in an effort to identity and
promote film making talent throughout the continent. Indrias Getachew
from Ethiopia had the chance to interview the award winning film maker.
Excerpts follow:
Indrias Getachew: How did you get started in film?
Gaston Kabore: It happened in 1974. I was studying history in Paris
at the Sorbonne University and eventually for my Masters of Science I
did a study about the representation of Africa within the illustrated
press in France at the end of the 19th century after the Berlin
Conference in 1885, which was about the sharing of Africa among the
colonial powers.
By watching the pictures that they were drawing about Africa: savage
continent and the French being on a mission to civilize Africa, I saw
that the image of Africa has been disfigured since that time. They
