established a kind of exotic vision of Africa, and I realized how the
analysis was false and how they are hiding their secret desire of
dominating under the [guise] of bringing civilization to Africa.
That was my first contact with images, pictures. When I finished my
4th year of university I was about to start my Ph.
D in history, instead I decided to go to film school to learn the
language of images. I was realizing that all the cliches about Africa,
the lies about Africa were so consistent that already we can see the
continuation of those things in the television pieces.
I wanted to learn the language of moving pictures to be able to use
cinema in history but also to reportray, to repossess my continent
through images. I was not aiming to become a permanent filmmaker, but it
seemed to me that cinema was a major tool of expression where Africans
can fight to recover their own image.
After two years I understood that in fact I was more interested than
I had expected, not only to record history with cinema, but to tell
stories with cinema, because telling stories is also something that
Africans need, because they need to reflect their own reality-also to
invest the imagination in moving pictures. Of course, the study that I
made before in history, a little bit of sociology and anthropology, it
enabled me to understand what cinema could bring to my people and what
kind of films I should try to do.
- The term African Renaissance has been used a lot at this workshop.
How do you see it, what is driving this renaissance?
I think the term renaissance, to be precise it came from South Africa
and I can understand why.
They were living under Apartheid, and with the end of apartheid they
were a new nation. I think for them it is a renaissance because the
anti-apartheid people-they might be black or white-did not really have
access to the media, particularly the medium of cinema and television.
Now for South Africans it is a real renaissance because for the first
time they have access to present their imagination, their visions into
film. For the rest of Africa, at the Pan African Federation of Film
Makers we have been fighting to have a rooted cinema in Africa.
Today we are saying we have already done a lot with more or less
success; but we still need to rethink film making in Africa. We need to
face the challenge of television today, the satellites and everything.
In the past we were fighting to have access to the screen in the
cinema hall; but today people don't need to go to a theater. In their
homes they are watching 15-30 (if you have satellite) different
channels.
So I think that the new historical situation in South Africa is one
thing; but also in the rest of Africa we are trying to address new
issues. We have the need to think differently for the survival of Africa
in terms of images.
- What are your predictions for the next four years-will we be able
to meet this challenge?
Of course it will not be easy because of the concerns that you know-
the lack of means, of infrastructure, the absence of skills in some of
the countries-but I still think that each country cannot go further
separately. We need to cooperate.
We need to bridge and consider the market of two, three countries so
we can hope that the tremendous things can go ahead. In the Pan African
Federation of film Makes for three decades we tried and have succeeded
to establish dialogue with the Organization of African Unity based in
Addis Ababa.
We have succeeded to make them vote a lot of resolutions and
recommendations concerning cinema. I remember this conference in '86 at
Fort Louis in Mauritius when one of the leaders was saying that cinema
is a vital need for African people.
The problem is that this political awareness and consciousness about
cinema is not transferred into acts in the countries. So, I do see
reason to hope but I don't forget that it is a struggle not yet
finished.
- In your perspective, what is the role of film in the development
agenda of the continent. Why is it a vital need for Africans?
I think Africa is suffering more from a lack of memory than from a
lack of resources.
Africa is suffering more from the political blindness of politicians
than from a lack of resources. I do believe that cinema helps to wake
up, to open the eyes of the people.
They will recover the possibility to see through their own eyes the
reality. They will become actors in their own lives instead of being
just passive in front of all the disasters taking place in the country,
and also in front of the politicians completely preoccupied with staying
in power instead of trying to see to the long term development of their
countries.
If we are able to give our people confidence, to let them know more
about their history because when you know who you are, you have a better
chance to know where you are going. But if you are completely hopeless,
if you give up, it seems that you are useless to the entire world.
To me cinema is not alone-it is also up to people working in
communication-what are you bringing as a new vision? What kind of
consistent hope are you giving to the people to say well it is difficult
today but we do know that we are building our country for the coming
generations. To me this is really what cinema could bring.
To me it is as important as health, food, education, roads, and
transportation. But we need people to have vision; and this is the real
drama of Africa.
Most of the time our decision makers, our politicians, the ruling
class, is completely hopeless in front of those big questions. A nation
is not built in ten years time-the time that they are in power only,
stealing the nation's money and everything.
And they forget that these people already have a very long history.
We need just to give them the possibility to stand up and play their
role, and be the workers of their destiny.
- When you look back on your career, have you been able to achieve
your vision?
To me the times I have been most happy in my film life were when I
see my people watching one of my films and they even think that the
story written by me belongs to them. It is better than any other
congratulations.
- As you think back to the things that drew you to this profession,
what are your feelings on this workshop?
For most of the script writers it is the first time they are
confronted with script writing, but it is good because we need to
develop new skills within Africa. Some of them have a very good
potential-they are very motivated.
I am not saying that the seven writers here will become scriptwriters
for the rest of their lives. But at least I have seen them all ready to
go as far as possible in that experience, learning quickly and
understanding that it is a real serious business.
To write a story is not a joke-it is something that hurts you a lot;
it is painful because you are in the process of excavating in your own
life, giving your vision. To write a story is to expose yourself, and
each of us is trying to hide ourselves.
That is the first thing that you discover when you are writing
novels. You are penetrating the fibers of the human being and sometimes
you are discovering yourself.
We need this kind of thing to be enlarged. M-Net cannot be the only
one to do that.
We need the television in Ethiopia to say well I also have my role to
play. I need to give the professionals in my country the possibility to
make filMs. It is possible; it's not just a question of money, it is
also a question of commitment, a question of historical consciousness,
question of responsibility.
When you are informed you no longer act like before.

Original_Search_Title: The sights and sounds of Ho
Headline: Ghana: The sights and sounds of Ho
Body_Text:
Accra — Ho is the capital of the Volta Region. It is blessed with a
countless number of tourist attractions. They are both natural and cultural,
varied and spatially distributed.
MOUNTAIN RANGES/HILLS
The Ghana-Togo Mountain Range and the Ewe Mountains form the most dominant
relief features. The mountainous areas are places of outstanding scenic beauty.
The most classical being the AVATIME mountains, most especially Mt. GEMI at
AMDzofe. It stands well over 2,800 feet above sea level.
The immediate environs have a near temperate micro-climate characterized by
cool mornings, evenings and nights and a lot of foggy weather.
Condensation often takes place on the ground level making the Amedzofe area
an extraordinarily unique destination.
The fact that mosquitoes are not found at Amedzofe-Gbadzeme areas make the
area the number one choice in the country for siting a health resort
(sanatorium).
The Ewe Mountains, WETO for short, lies between Tsito and Peki. It is also a
very beautiful mountain. In Tsito and Abutia Agore can be found spots where
water oozes out of an aquifer.
The Kpere Hill is also one area that commands out standing panoramic view.
One can see an arm of the great Volta Lake in the distance.
The KABAKABA/GALENKUI Hills, the most dominant hill at Ho is one area which
is beyond description. Currently, half of the mountain is re-forested with teak
trees, which make the mountain green, especially during the rainy season.
WATERFALLS
Ho District has at least two waterfalls, the better known being the falls
between Amedzofe and Gbadzeme. It is a lovely fall that cascades down slope
thrice before forming a big pool. The water from the fall is pure, colourless,
odourless and extremely soft.
The other fall is said to be located near Ashanti Kpoeta. Incidentally, I am
yet to visit the site described by a trusted friend as being very lovely.
KALAKPA GAME RESERVE
This reserve comprises both Adaklu and Abatia Traditional Areas and covers
an estimated 125 square miles of gentle gradient which drains into various
streams.
The Kalakpa stream is the only kind in this reserve, but dries up in the dry
season. The main vegetation is savannah grassland interspersed with a few fire-
resistant trees.
ANIMALS
The main animals found in the reserve include, Kob (Kobus-Kob), Baboon
(Papio Anubis), and Green Monkey (Cercopithecus Aethiops).
These three animals are mostly seen in the dry season, especially after the
bushfires.
ZITOE (KOB HILLS) is an area where one can see the Kobs in herds running, at
times into several hundreds. Other animals found on the reserve include,
buffalo, bush buck, bush pig, maxwell duiker, water buck, patas monkey, genet
cat, and grass cutters.
BIRDS
The Kalakpa Game Production Reserve is said to be among the richest in the
West African sub-region. There are countless numbers of sparrows, swallows,
egrets, franocolins, weaver birds, etc.
ANCIENT GERMAN BUILDING/RELICS
Hidden among the mango trees on the side of a hill at Abutia Agore, can be
found disused old buildings made entirely of locally-found good quality clay
and initially roofed with thatch.
It used to house at one-time, Capt. Von Doulis, a German officer in charge
of the then Trans-Volta Togoland, who served under Alexander Foxe (Galenkui)
At the foot of the hill which housed Capt. Doulis' bungalow is a beautiful
aquifer. The water is said to have been used by all the people living far and
wide in the olden days.
All other German buildings were destroyed. The old church building at Ho
Kpodzi and the EP Schools are some of the relics. There are some old buildings
too at Biakpa and Amedzofe now used as Guest Houses.
At Ho and Adaklu can be found graves of some early Missionaries who lived in
there. On top of Mt. Gemi is a huge metal cross erected by the Germans.
HISTORICAL RELICS/MUSEUMS
A huge bell which was captured by the Ashantis during one of their wars with
the people of Ho is on display at the E.P. Church Headquarters at Ho. The
church bell was said to be taken to Kumasi and placed on a fetish grove and
later returned.
ARTS AND CRAFTS
Kente weaving is very important at Kpetoe and Afegame. The natives of these
two towns are Ga-Adangbes who migrated there during the great exodus. Other
craft items include basketry, pottery, etc.
Festivals
The main festival celebrated in the Ho District is the Yam Festival. It is
celebrated in September. The Rice Festival is also celebrated in the Amadezofe
areas.
HOTELS
Ho alone has over 17 hotels and Rest houses. The star-rated ones are Chances
Hotel 3-Star, Freedom Hotel 2-Star, Woezor Hotel 1-Star and Tauras Hotel 1-
Star.
The latest sensation in the hotel industry is Caravan Sarai, which is yet to
be commissioned.
SUGGESTIONS
There is the need for the many villages in the Kalakpa Game Reserve, whose
inhabitants are persistently poaching to be relocated.
Unless this is done, this very important tourist asset will be completely
destroyed.

Original_Search_Title: 'Because We No Cry...'
Headline: Ghana: 'Because We No Cry...'
Body_Text:
Accra — In these days when injunctions - whether interim or perpetual - fly
around our noses like mango season flies, I am surprised no one has gone for an
injunction order to restrain the callous harmattan weather that is making life
unbearable for us.
Or is it because there are some people who enjoy this harmattan weather? One
such person was Unoka, father of Okonkwo in Achebe's 'Things Fall Apart'. (My
former lecturer in English at Legon has already warned me I've done a critique
of all Achebe's books.)
Unoka, who was not known to be a hard worker, loved the harmattan season
when all harvests had been done and musicians moved from village to village
drinking and making merry.
It was this Unoka man who once went to the agbala priestess to complain
about his abrabo mu nsem.
Nana agbala, each year before I sow my crops I offer sacrifices to Ani, the
earth goddess, and my ancestors. After sowing the crops, I also offer
sacrifices. Yet, my harvests are poor. I don't knowÂ
Hold your breath, man, the priestess cut in. You haven't offended the gods
or your ancestors. And if a man is at peace with the gods and his ancestors,
his harvest will be good or bad depending on the strength of his arm.
You, Unoka, are known to be a man whose arms are weak. When your colleagues
cross seven streams and seven hills to cut down virgin forest with their axes,
you dump your yams in exhausted land around the outskirts that do not take hard
labour to clear. Go and work like a man.
The Son of Man, Efo Kokuvi, often reminds me of Unoka. It is clear now that
certain things are pinching the Son of Man in his chest - like Osofo abrobe.
Thus, each year, at the least opportunity, he apologises for what he
described as mistakes and excesses committed, during his revolution. 'Folks,
sorry o', he would say, reminding me of my Nigerian brothers.
But before the final phoneme leaves his lips, Kwabena Agyepong and a host of
others who suffered direct hits during those heady days fire back, you lie, we
no go forgive you lai lai.
The Son of Man might be thinking that he has dedicated enemies who are after
his blood, and who have sworn never to accept his apology.
But the truth is that like Unoka he is not getting the desired results
because he does not go far enough in his attempts to appease his victims by
showing remorse.
Ours is a society that places much emphasis on decorum in the conduct of our
daily affairs. Therefore, to achieve genuine reconciliation, society insists
that the mode of appeasement is determined by factors like the nature and
gravity of the injury suffered by the victim, and the relationship between the
parties.
The following toli was given to me by an honourable Member of Parliament who
is a friend of this column.
A rich cocoa farmer had several farm labourers all of whom had their wives.
It happened that one labourer was shelling the wife of another labourer. As
they say, all day for thief man, but one day for the owner. The trespasser was
caught one day as he was seriously shelling his colleague's positions.
The traditional court that was put together to try the offender found him
guilty and asked him to pacify the aggrieved husband with the sum of one
guinea, a bottle of schnapps and one fowl. This was accepted.
Three days later as this aggrieved husband was cutting thatch along the
village stream he saw the trespasser bathing naked in a pool. He saw
everything. Everything.
He sneaked back home and told their landlord and master that the items to be
given him as restitution were woefully inadequate. If this is the weapon his
colleague used in shelling his wife then considerable damage has been caused to
certain vital installations in an area not readily visible.
The elders agreed and made an upward adjustment to ensure peace and
reconciliation.
In some cases, merely saying 'sorry o, oga' can heal wounds and bring about
peace. But when the offence is serious the offender has to go with a delegation
of advocates to the house of the injured party to pacify himer (him/her).
And mind you, you don't go on such trips with five-star brokemen wearing
potholed charlie wote. They must be men of substance. And they have to go with
'something'.
Some people lost their wives, husbands, parents and other dear ones during
Efo Kokuvi's revolution. Today, he says they were excesses so he is sorry. Does
he think such victims can easily forget when he has not done anyth ing else to
show he is really remorseful?
Justice Cecelia Koranteng-Addo was nursing a two-week old baby when she was
abducted and murdered by agents of the PNDC. A few years later, out of grief,
her husband, Justice Koranteng-Addo, also died, leaving their daughter an
orphan. Does Efo know where the child is? Whether she goes to school or not?
Acheampong, Akuffo and Afrifa are still buried in unmarked graves at Nsawam.
Why can't Efo Kokuvi release their bodies to their families in the interest of
peace and reconciliation?
People are quiet, but they have not forgotten their ordeals. At the
appropriate time, they will speak. Because they no cry, ino be say ino pain
them.

Original_Search_Title: Gov. Nnamani Visits Umuode Refugees
Headline: Nigeria: Gov. Nnamani Visits Umuode Refugees
Body_Text:
Lagos — For the first time since February last year, the displaced people of
Umuode who have been living in refugee camps in Akpuoga Nike received some
succour from the Enugu State Government.
Governor Chimaroke Nnamani who visited the refugee camps declared
government's interest in resolving the age-long chieftaincy and land dispute
between the Umuode and Ozucku communities.
The Governor made donations of 250 tubers of yam; 20 bags of 250 kilogrammes
rice and fifteen bags of salt to the teeming refugees numbering over one
thousand.
However, according to an indigene of the Umuode clan, the donations only
constitute a drop in the ocean as it was grossly inadequate to the number of
people in the camps.
Meanwhile, the Igwe of Umuode, Moses Ugwu told P.M News in an interview that
the government's promise should be immediate rather than over time, stating
that his people are dying from starvation, sickness and ''severe psychological
trauma.''
The Igwe who himself is hibernating in Enugu said Governor Nnamani should
rather direct that the Umuode community be resettled in Aguefi land in Akpugo
rather than the marshy Abari land in Oruku, which is not secure and has been
declared 'inhabitable' by experts.
The Umuode-Onuku crisis dates back to 1985 over the rotation of the
traditional stool among the three communities of Umuode Umuchiani and Onogowo,
when it became the turn of Umuode to produce the next Igwe.
According to a 1976 constitution ratified by the then Anambra State
government, the other two clans (Umuchiani and Onogowo) allegedly ganged up to
forestall the enthronement of an Umuode Igwe. Since then, there has been
violent clashes between the communities leading to several hundreds of deaths,
especially on the side of Umuode.

Original_Search_Title: ADF ambush UPDF convoy
Headline: Uganda: ADF ambush UPDF convoy
Body_Text:
Kampala — 3 rebels killed By Souza Justus In Bundibugyo Rebels of the Allied
Democratic Forces (ADF) Jan. 31 ambushed a contingent of UPDF soldiers at Ngite
village Harugale sub county in Bundibugyo district.
The incident occurred at 7:30 a.m when the UPDF was on a search-and-destroy-
rebels operation.
The UPDF overpowered the rebels and killed three of them, and recovered from
them one SMG with 17 rounds of live ammunition.
Three UPDF soldiers suffered minor injuries. They are admitted at Bundibugyo
Hospital.
All the dead ADF rebels wore long beards, two of them looked healthy, but
the third was sickly.
The bodies which were clothed in torn civilian clothes had on them roasted
yams, ripe bananas, pieces of sugar-cane, three small saucepans, and pieces of
torn blankets.
The incident was confirmed by security sources.

Original_Search_Title: Wipe Out Hunger in Kenya - Build Good Roads!
Headline: Kenya: Wipe Out Hunger in Kenya - Build Good Roads!
Body_Text:
Nairobi — We have enough food in the country; the only problem is our
inability to move food from surplus areas to areas where there are deficits..."
It was Kenya's Minister of Agriculture, Chris Obure, who said this recently.
So, does Kenya have a famine or is it merely a question of poor distribution of
food? To a hungry person, the difference is the same.
Famine does not occur overnight. The signs are as clear as daylight. The
dark of the night is the famine.
The previous agriculture minister, Musalia Mudavadi, had warned there was
going to be food shortage nine months ago. In addition, there was the warning
of a looming "food crisis" issued by USAid's FEWS (Famine Early Warning
System). And, at the time of the El-Nino rains, we were warned that there was
an imminent drought. Obviously, this information is still available at the
ministry.
Many years ago, a Minister of State in the Office of the President, the late
Justus ole Tipis, advised parliament that beans from a previous harvest --
which formed our national food reserves -- had to be exported as they were
perishable!
The issue being discussed then was drought. Readers may remember how, three
years ago, the media highlighted the case of a hungry boy who dug up a dead dog
and ate it.
The first drought I can personally recall was in 1965. That was also the
first time I saw yellow maize, which we nicknamed "kaserikali" (the
government's maize).
Since then, a simple analysis shows certain climatic conditions recurring
every four to five years. Kenya's economy, like the climate, runs on a four-
year cycle too, which is not surprising because our economy is agro-based.
So our rainfall patterns are known, as is the fact that we do not receive
rainfall in all districts at the same time. So, why can't we plan around the
cycles to move surpluses to deficit areas as and when necessary?
Because we can't move food from one place to another. When it rains, farmers
cannot move food from the shamba to the market, so the food rots. If we manage
to move it, it is at a very high cost, which translates into high food prices.
My aunt in Nyandarua does not bother any more. The most succulent cabbages
and carrots are mashed as fodder for her cattle; she does not dilute her tea
with water, she uses only milk. After all, the milk will go to waste, as she
can't transport the milk to the dairy.
Now, would it not have been better for a vegetable processing factory and a
cheese factory to be built in her area so that her produce could be packed and
sent to other districts?
I was visiting a friend in Cherangani recently and was invited to pick as
many oranges as I could.
I collected a sackful free of charge. Why? My friend couldn't sell them,
because the roads were so bad he couldn't get them to the market.
All goods in Kenya belong to all of us collectively. The mere fact that
Nairobi, where I currently reside, does not grow oranges, bananas, yams and
sweet potatoes is not reason enough to deny me access to them.
Likewise, people in Nyandarua should be able to eat pineapples from Thika or
Taita and cashewnuts from Kilifi, while Wajir residents should enjoy sukuma
wiki, green maize and mihogo.
Yet our children and the general populace suffer from malnutrition while
food goes to waste. We cannot hope to develop this country with an unhealthy
population, yet Kenyans have been accused of being lazy, shunning work and so
on.
How do you work on an empty stomach or at best with only carbohydrates
obtained from relief maize? Comparing our work output with that of the Japanese
is unfair. Their food is easily available at a cost they can afford because
they have roads.
If we had enough food, we would work at two jobs for 14 hours like
Westerners. Currently, we spend all our physical and mental energy tracking
down the next meal.
Why don't we have roads? Or better still, why do we have the right roads in
the wrong places and vice versa?
Thirty six years of political independence dedicated to "development" should
have given us the appropriate infrastructure for our needs.
Maybe the problem is our population -- 30 million Kenyans! But we were not
all born at the same time. Our planners have been telling us over the years
what our numbers will be in the coming years, year in and year out.
Investors are prepared to put up the necessary processing factories and
transport food as the need arises, but they cannot be expected to build the
roads as well.
On the other hand, if the roads were privatised, they would build and manage
them. That should solve the problem of the government's "reluctance" to build
roads while taxing us for the same.
The red onions sold in Nairobi come from Arusha, not because we cannot grow
enough in Loitokitok, but because there is an all weather road between Nairobi
and Arusha, while there is none from Loitokitok. With roads in place, farmers
will be able to move their produce and produce more for export. We will then be
a Newly Industrialised Country long before the year 2020, with a healthy
population.
Build roads.

Original_Search_Title: Mind your language (Commentary)
Headline: Africa: Mind your language (Commentary)
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Only when Africans put their vernaculars first will they be able
to fully express their scientific genius, writes ALI MAZRUI in this article on
globalisation, language and gender.
What is development? How does it now relate to globalisation? Since among
fragile societies globalisation is a mixed blessing, we would have to redefine
development: Development equals globalisation minus dependency.
One strategy of transcending dependency is indigenisation. This includes
greater utilisation of indigenous techniques, personnel and approaches for
purposeful change. Indigenised approaches to globalisation would include
greater use of African languages in the pursuit of scientific, economic and
constitutional change.
No country has ascended to a first rank technological and economic power by
excessive dependence on foreign languages. Japan rose to dazzling industrial
heights by "scientificating" the Japanese language and making it the medium of
its own industrialisation. Korea has approximately scientificated the Korean
language and made it the medium of its own technological take-off.
Can Africa ever take off technologically if it remains so overwhelmingly
dependent on European languages for discourse on advanced learning? Can Africa
look to the future if it is not adequately sensitive to the cultural past?
Culture as communication and culture as production need to converge. Israel
resurrected a dead language, Hebrew, and made it a language of modern
efficiency.
This lingo-cultural gap may be disastrous for reducing dependency in
Africa's experience. This gap may also delay the full maturation of Africa's
scientific genius.
In the 20th century, no language is automatically a scientific language but
every language is automatically a poetic language. Two policies--of
scientification of African languages and support for African poets and writers-
-have to be jointly pursued as part of long-term national development. Culture
as communication and culture as identity should find a meeting point in
literature.
Three Africans have won the Nobel Prize for literature since 1986. It was
possible for an Egyptian Arab, Neguib Mahfuz, to win it for literature written
in his native Arabic. It was possible for a South African white, Nadine
Gordimer, to win the Nobel Prize for books written in her native European
language.
But it was not possible for the only Black laureate, Wole Soyinka, to win
the Nobel Prize for literature written in his native Yoruba. Soyinka could only
be in the running for the prize through the imperial language of the Other.
Are we waiting for modernisation to come? Or are we waiting for dependence
to leave? Other has precluded the linguistic Self from ever being noticed as
being of literary relevance.
Rabindranath Tagore won the Nobel Prize in 1913 when India was still a
British colony. The works for which he won the Nobel Prize were in Bengali. He
created literature from his linguistic womb.
When Tagore won the Nobel for literature written in Bengali before the first
intra-civilisational war (World War I) it must have looked like a major step
towards a concert of cultures, a step towards a partnership of civilisations.
The award signalled the potentialities of parity of esteem across cultural
divides. But progress in cultural parity since Tagore has been slow and
painful. The functions of culture in almost every society continue to feel the
hegemony of Westem power and the omnipresence of Western civilisation.
This, in the era of globalisation, means a linguistic revolution in Africa
will need to be reinforced by a gender revolution--for in every society women
are the trustees of indigenous languages. Mothers transmit the mother tongue
from generation to generation.
In most sub-Saharan traditional lifestyles, a woman was culturally supposed
to have a triple custodial role. As custodian of fire, she finds herself in
charge of rural Africa's most important source of domestic energy--firewood.
She treks long distances to collect it. As custodian of water, the African
woman ensures water supply for the home and for the extended family. Again she
will often walk a mile or two to a lake or river.
As for the woman's role as custodian of earth, this is linked to the concept
of dual fertility--the fertility of the womb (woman as mother) and the
fertility of the soil (woman as cultivator). In many African countries women
are the majority of the farmers--as well as being major suppliers of domestic
water and firewood.
What role does culture allocate to men? In societies of mixed husbandry,
males (both boys and men) are in charge of the larger domestic animals like
cattle or camels. Some cultures allocate responsibility for all domestic
mammals (goats, sheep, etc.) to males--leaving only poultry in the hands of
women.
Men fell trees so that women can use branches for firewood. Men go to cities
or mines to work while their womenfolk remain on the farm.
A new division of labour emerged with some colonial economies-- women
cultivated food-crops like yam and maize while men took charge of cash crops
like cocoa, cotton and coffee.
But in places like Kericho it is still women who pick the tea leaves at
harvest time. It is considerations such as these which make cultural awareness
indispensable for effective development planning.
In this age of globalisation, the role of women also needs to marry culture
with technology. Science itself needs to be androgynised. This is a process of
conferring both female and male characteristics to a phenomenon.
Only 67 per cent of girls of school age in sub-Saharan Africa are in school.
Only 23 per cent enter secondary school. And only three per cent find their way
into higher education.
Among the young women who finally do go to universities, only a small
fraction study science and technology--a mere three percent in a country like
Chad rising to 28 in South Africa (but a disproportionate number in South
Africa are non-black).
All over Africa girls are routinely discouraged from engineering and high
science. Scientific and technological careers are widely regarded as the
preserve of men. In the new century, it is time to dissolve this superstition.
Science and technology in Africa need to be androgynised.
The search continues for female excellence, partly as an answer to wider
problems of African societies which men have failed to solve.
In the sciences and technical professions in Africa, the dissemination and
instruction should initially focus on the four traditional custodial roles of
the African women. * Woman as custodian of fire: The science of energy
supplies, from firewood to petroleum and natural gas. * Woman as custodian of
earth: The science of agriculture with greater facilitation in credit access
and equipment for women. * Woman as custodian of water: The science of water-
management and the role of women in water supply. * Woman as home manager: A
new science needs to be developed to make home management more efficient, cost-
effective, and part of the wider computation of the Gross National Product of a
given society. Phase I: The candidates would be women and the voters for these
particular seats would also be exclusively women.
Phase II: The candidates for these seats would still be women but the voters
would be both men and women. The women candidates would thus have to address
the concerns of men as well as women.
Phase III: When female parliamentary candidates on the common electoral roll
would be in sufficient numbers to stand a chance of winning without special
gender- reservation of seats. Gender-reservation of seats would be abolished
and women would compete on the common electoral roll.
If development equals globalisation minus dependency, how is the dependency
to be subtracted from the globalisation? I have suggested two strategies for
reducing dependency - indigenisation and androgynisation. Language and science
are at stake. In both of them skills have interacted with values.
To paraphrase the English poet, Alexander Pope (1688-1744):
Excessive globalism is a dangerous thing? Drink softly, or taste not the
planetary spring.

Original_Search_Title: Voting for NDC not panacea for development
Headline: Ghana: Voting for NDC not panacea for development
Body_Text:
Accra — Impression gathered from a tour of three districts of the
Eastern Region is that people think only those who voted for the
National Democratic Congress deserve development projects which they
consider as gifts and projects they are entitled to.
On December 14 last year, at an Abirem durbar of the chiefs,
assembly members and opinion leaders of the Birim North District, the
chief of Ayirebi, Obrempong Gyapere Agyekum II, questioned the
propriety of the government's repairing roads in the Oda Constituency
which voted for the NPP and not the NDC. "We voted for the NDC,  where
the government stands, we stand, so how come that you repair Oda roads
and leave ours out?" the chief who was spokesman for the Association of
Chiefs in the district asked the Minister of Roads and Transport,
Edward Salia, who had been invited to the durbar.
At Okrakwadwo in the Akuapem North District the following day, the
chief of the town asked the Eastern Regional Minister why the street
and a feeder road leading to his town had not been repaired. The same
"we voted (massively)  for the NDC" was cited as reason behind the
question.
Even the supposedly  - non partisan chieftaincy affairs are now
being thrown onto the laps of NDC politicians. At Adukrom Palace, also
in the Akuapem North District, a spokesman for the Traditional Council,
Ankobeahene Osew Opeasiakwa II said his people stood where the NDC
stood. And he called on the government to recognize the paramountcies
Okere, Guan and Akuapem Anafo had created for themselves so that they
would, in turn, maintain their  support for the NDC.
On December 2 when the minister interacted with the chiefs and
people of the Afram Plains District at Donkokrom, she was implored by
one W.K. Lotsu to see to the liberalization of the laws pertaining to
tree - felling and sawing so that people living on the Dwarf Islands
would have enough lumber for their fishing boats.  "All of us on the
island and along the lake are behind the NDC government," he stated,
calling for rehabilitation of a feeder road leading to the lake in
addition to the timber issue.
A day earlier, the deputy Eastern Regional minister, Mr. Samuel
Ofosu Ampofo, had been told at Maame Krobo in the Afram Plains south
constituency that the government should create avenues for exporting
yams from the area to increase the farmers' earnings. A man who made
the appeal during the open forum claimed "we are all NDC".
The good news, however, is that party leaders in the region have
assured that the government is committed to developing all
constituencies, irrespective of what party they voted for.  At Maame
Krobo, for instance, Mr. Ofosu Ampofo said the government would develop
the nation "step by step", appealing that people should not demand
certain projects or favours before voting for any particular party.
The regional minister, Ms. Patience Adow herself explained to
another gathering at Asenema in Akuapem North that it is because
resources are limited that certain communities cannot have electricity,
roads, schools, water or other amenities at the same time as other
communities are supplied.  She, however, assured that eventually the
government would supply every community with basic necessities, calling
on the communities themselves to step up their self-help activities to
augment government efforts.
In all the areas where she toured, the regional minister assured
cocoa farmers that the government would continue to pay for their
produce, in spite of the setbacks on the World Market.  She appealed to
all to bear with the government as it takes steps to deal with the
current high cost of living, noting that it is a general problem
associated with the global economic recession.
For a reliable planning of the socio-economic development of Ghana,
Ms. Adow called on every body resident in the country to ensure that
they are counted in the next census.
She explained that the night of March 26, year 2000, has been
declared the national census night and wherever one sleeps that night
is where one will eventually be counted.

Original_Search_Title: Scientists Develop Vaccine From Modified Sweet Potato
Headline: Kenya: Scientists Develop Vaccine From Modified Sweet Potato
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Scientists have developed an edible vaccine against food
poisoning from a genetically modified sweet potato.
The immunity against Norwalk virus-responsible for causing food
poisoning from many poorly stored or handled foods, is triggered by
eating a potato that is genetically modified and equipped with
anti-Norwalk virus, according to researchers at Cornell University,
USA.
The invention, a joint research with the University of Maryland
School of Medicine, is capable of halting effects of Norwalk virus in
the human body incase of consumption of poisoned food. The
researchers add that eating the modified and vaccine inclusive sweet
potato successfully curtail food -borne illness.
The triggering of immunity against a virus by eating a sweet potato
is not the first invention.
The Thompson Institute developed a vaccine against the highly killer
Hepatitis B, still using a genetically modified sweet potato last
year.
The switch of vaccine into edible foods, says Dr Anthony Fauci,
director, United States National Institute of Allergy and Infectious
Diseases (NIAID) that funded both potato vaccine initiates is
precipitated by the realisation that: "Edible vaccine offer exciting
possibilities for significantly reducing the burden of disease like
hepatitis and diarrhoea, particularly in developing world where
storing and administrating of vaccine are often major problems."
Should this edible vaccine be successful in Africa, says Dr Grace
Akoth, a nutritionist, then Africa will term this as a gigantic
milestone because millions of African population face food-borne
illness every year depleting the little medical facilities available
in poorly equipped government hospitals."
"Unlike other forms of vaccine injected into the body or administered
through drops-often subjected to illogical controversies, fears and
safety queries like the recent countrywide polio vaccine falsely
rumoured as laced with Aids/ HIV, there will be little room for
hatching of such a rumour in this genetically modified sweet potato
that has already developed vaccine," says a doctor at Kenyatta"
National Hospital.
"An edible vaccine, " says Dr Carol Tacket, professor at the
University of Maryland School of Medicine, "could be easy to produce,
safe, affordable and effective."
Should this scientific venture materialize, as researchers says is
the position, the concept of edible vaccine could provide inexpensive
protection for most of the world's poorest population, many incapable
of affording the conventional vaccine, and where administered free,
lack means of reaching at the designated centres.
Many countries produce sweet potatoes in thousands of tons making it
their staple food. This means that the genetically modified sweet
potato could not only offer starch and carbohydrates to their
consumers but also guard against food-borne illness.
Kenya, through the Kenya Agricultural Research Institute (Kari)
recently started field trials of the first transgenic sweet potatoes
which lab tests and limited field trials at its research farms proved
successful.
"If the transgenic sweet potato fronted by KARI proves a success,
then it should not hesitate borrowing the vaccine theory from their
counterparts in America. This decade is an 'agricultural revolution'
and Kenya should not be left behind," says Dr Akoth.
'The percentage of poor quality milk from co-operative societies
processing milk is very high. This is despite the farmers supplying
the co-operatives with relatively good quality milk.'

Original_Search_Title: Libyans Observe Ramadan
Headline: Libyans Observe Ramadan
Body_Text:
Libyans Sunday started the holy
month of Ramadan, when Muslims fast from dawn to dusk with most
social activities suspended.
During the fast period Muslims are also obliged to abstain
from drinking, smoking and having sexual relations.
But most Arab and Muslim countries began their own fasting
Monday, while others will even wait to start Tuesday.
The fact that all countries do not start the fasting month
unanimously depends on the lunar calendar.
The beginning and the end of the month are fixed by the
appearance of the new moon, which might be seen in one place and
not in another at the same time.
In Libya, after the beginning of Ramadan was announced
Saturday night, the pace of life abruptly changed.
The 42-hour working week is also drastically reduced.
Shopping for foodstuffs has stepped up as crowds Sunday
thronged vegetable and fruit markets, triggering a marginal
increase in prices.
By Monday, a six-kg box of sweet potatoes had risen to 1.5
Libyan dinars (about three US dollars) compared to 1.2 dinars the
previous day. But the prices of imported fruits such as banana,
apples and pears, remained unchanged.
This is partly because, consumers are rather attracted to
the country's seasonal fruits, particularly oranges, which are
among the best and most juicy in the Mediterranean basin.
Meanwhile, Libyan television has started broadcasting a
series of special programmes for Ramadan, when most activities
are shifted to night time in the Arab and Muslim world.
In Libya, these includes shows, shopping in supermarkets,
football matches and even family visits.
But by the second week of the holy month, the emphasis
would shift from foodstuffs to the purchasing of clothes for the
Aid Al-Fitr, the end-of-Ramadan feast.
In line with the religious mood, fewer women are seen on
the streets of Tripoli during the day, while the working women
attend to their duties wearing long traditional attire.

Original_Search_Title: Plenty of Food At Kongowea Market
Headline: Kenya: Plenty of Food At Kongowea Market
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A glut of fresh food has seen prices tumbling in Mombasa,
while many parts of Kenya are still suffering from famine. Prices
have fallen by as much as 70 per cent - with tomatoes, for example,
down from Sh1,000 a box to Sh300 - triggering ill feeling between
farmers and traders at Kongowea wholesale market.
A 14-kg sack of onions was now going for Sh190 instead of Sh600, and
a bag of potatoes was now going for Sh1,200 instead of Sh3,000.
Cabbages had fallen in price from Sh35 to Sh10 a kilo.
The farmers accuse the traders of underpaying them - particularly for
potatoes, other vegetables and fruit - while the traders complain
that business is poor.
Among the most productive areas at the Coast are Shimba Hills, which
has realised a good harvest of maize, vegetables and oranges, parts
of Kilifi and Kwale districts which have high production of cassava
and sweet potatoes, and Tana River which has produced a lot of
mangoes.
Also benefiting from a food glut is Mpeketoni settlement scheme of
Lamu District.
In many parts, however, the produce has failed to reach the market
due to poor roads or lack of transport, raising fears that most of
the food might perish before reaching the market.
In Tana River and Lamu districts poor roads coupled with banditry
have been the main problems, while most parts of Kilifi have suffered
the same problem.
Orange farmers from Shimba Hills report that a lot of produce has
gone to waste after perishing at the market due to lack of buyers.
They accused middlemen of undercutting genuine brokers by negotiating
lower prices even before the oranges have reached the market.
The farmers said they have now elected a committee which would
control the amount of the oranges being sent to Kongowea market.
Their newly elected chairman, Mr. Saidi Shee Mwamamboge, and
Vice-Chairman John Sesi said that they would meet at the market on
Saturday to lobby for the reduction the fleet taking oranges to the
market.
"Most times we have as many as 17 lorries bringing oranges to the
market and traders take advantage of this by dictating prices. We
therefore want to reduce this number to about five lorries a day,"
they said.
Wholesalers said that the problem facing farmers was that the high
season for oranges and tomatoes had come and supply had outstripped
demand.
They said they were going so cheaply that some farmers were finding
it difficult to break even. Some middlemen had taken advantage of
this and were now taking the fruits directly from the farms.
The traders said that the farmers had themselves to blame after
failing to heed their advice that they each allocate themselves a
certain quota so as to control the amount entering the market.
They attributed high production to the low rainfall which they said
had limited the water-borne diseases which normally affect crops
during heavy rains.
They said the production costs had also gone down for farmers, since
they do not have to spend money buying expensive sprays to stop pests
and diseases.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Emergency Report No. 45 - Africa
Headline: Africa: WFP Emergency Report No. 45 - Africa
Body_Text:
Rome — (A) Ethiopia
(a) Since January, Ethiopia has received a total of 1,231,233 tons of
food aid. Around 76 percent of this total arrived via Djibouti port,
7 percent via Berbera port, 17 percent was purchased locally and 0.3
percent delivered by air. The majority of food aid, some 80 percent,
has been for relief operations. Some 484,763 tons of the food aid was
channelled through WFP.
(b) Emergency Needs Assessment (ENA) is expected to start in Tigray
and Borena at the end of the week. The Tigray mission was postponed
for a week to allow assessment of possible damages by recent heavy
rains. The ENA will involve 22 teams (over 100 team members), who
will cover 61 zones in all parts of the country. A two-day training
of assessment teams is on-going. In connection with the ENA, a
possible Somali Region aerial livestock assessment is being
discussed, to obtain better information on actual livestock
population in the region.
(c) Somali Region: The number of new admissions in supplementary
feeding centres in Korahe Zone is decreasing. This is attributed to
the fact that nomadic population is moving towards improved grazing
areas, following a much higher rainfall during the deyr rains than in
previous years. However, the rain is also causing problems for
storage of recently harvested sorghum. Rainfall situation in Shinile
zone is reported to be good, but there are reports of new IDPs in
urgent need of assistance, arriving daily to Dembel woreda from
surrounding areas. Excessive rainfall in Warder zone has led to
floods. In Gode, deyr rains are continuing and the number of
beneficiaries in therapeutic and supplementary feeding centres is
decreasing.
(d) There has been an increase in confrontations between the
Ethiopian Army and the Ogaden National Liberation Front in Somali
Region. Clan problems along Ethiopia-Kenya border continue and have
been caused displacement of Garre people across the border into
Ethiopia.
(e) SNNP Region: Unusually heavy rainfall has been reported from
Wolayta, resulting in rotting of maize crops and water-logging
affects. The farmers have not been able to harvest the crops. The
rain has some positive effects on the growth of sweet potato and
enset.
(f) WFP food has been dispatched to facilitate repatriation of 2,400
Kenyan refugees. Some 4,760 Kenyan refugees are expected and
scheduled to repatriate by the end of the year.
(g) Daily average discharge in the Port of Djibouti has been
4,000-4,500 tons. WFP special operation to upgrade the Port of
Djibouti is in its final stages. Berth 13 is fully operational, and a
mobile storage unit has been erected.
(B) Sudan
(a) Preliminary results from the first round of WFP's Annual Needs
Assessment (ANA) in southern Sudan show a 15-20 percent increase in
food needs in 2001 compared to this year. The first round of the ANA
suggests that the most food insecure areas next year will be Aweil
West County, Ruweng County, Leech, Phou and Bieh states, as well as
Torit and Kapoeta counties. Gogrial, Twic and Aweil East counties,
and Latjor State are expected to be moderately food insecure. It is
anticipated that pockets of food insecurity will be found in Bor and
Pibor counties, and in Abiey, Tonj, Rumbek, Yirol and Wau counties.
In contrast, it is expected that Yei and Mundri counties will be food
self-sufficient, and Yambio, Maridi and Tambura counties will be
surplus-producing areas.
(b) Preliminary findings from the southern Sudan leg of the FAO/WFP
Crop and Food Supply Assessment mission point to high crop production
in Western Equatoria including Yei and Kajo Keji counties in Eastern
Equatoria. However, food deficits are expected next year in northern
Bahr el Ghazal, Upper Nile and Jonglei, as well as parts of Eastern
Equatoria. The Lakes region is on the whole expected to be food
self-sufficient, though with pockets of food insecurity where, for
instance, there are high number of internally displaced persons. The
Sobat area is expected to have pockets of food insecurity. ANA and
FAO/WFP Assessment exercise are closely coordinated.
(c) Food aid provided by WFP represents 62 percent of the total food
aid destined for southern Sudan during the period January to October.
The remaining 38 percent of the relief food has been provided by CRS,
NAP, WVI and LWF, which operate in Equatoria and Lakes regions.
(d) Heavy rains washed away sections of the road between Lokichoggio
in north-western Kenya and Narus in Kapoeta County, Eastern
Equatoria, during October. This delayed food aid convoys destined for
distribution sites in Kapoeta County. WFP recently sent an engineer
to assess the extent of the damages, prior to carrying out emergency
repairs on the road. At present, it takes WFP-contracted trucks a
whole day to travel the 45 km stretch of road between Lokichoggio and
Narus, instead of the usual 2-3 hours.
Note: All tonnage figures in this report refer to metric tons
(End WFP Emergency Report No 45)

Original_Search_Title: Scientists Encourage Potato Planting
Headline: Mozambique: Scientists Encourage Potato Planting
Body_Text:
Maputo, Mozambique — The Mozambican scientific
community has called for the massive planting of a variety of
sweet potato with an orange pulp to eradicate malnutrition and to
supply its consumers with vitamin A.
The call was made Tuesday in Maputo during a national
seminar on the promotion of the planting of the orange pulp sweet
potato.
According to Isabel Andrade, the co-ordinator of the
regional research network (SARRNET) for root crops like sweet
potatoes and cassava, the promotion of orange pulp sweet potato
is an alternative aimed at reducing the high costs involved in
the purchase of vitamin A capsules - a strategy currently used to
deal with deficiency in the vitamin.
Andrade told the national news agency that the planting of
sweet potato in Mozambique "is very easy," and is well known to
the majority of the rural population. It is thus the "best
alternative" for reducing vitamin A deficiency.
Manuel Amade of the National Agricultural Research Institute
claimed that if the planting of this variety of sweet potato is
well received in the Mozambican countryside, it will be possible
to cut child deaths caused by diseases such as measles by about
50 percent.
The total land where the bulb is currently planted is just
55 hectares in the southern provinces of Maputo and Gaza.
The authorities are to distribute sweet potato seeds to
91,000 families in the two provinces, and a further 15,000
families in the central Sofala province by the first half of
November.
Vitamin A deficiency leads to blindness, and vulnerability
to diseases such as diarrhoea, measles, and respiratory
infections.

Original_Search_Title: And Now to the Notebook
Headline: Zimbabwe: And Now to the Notebook
Body_Text:
Harare — Let's get ready for a big win Cde In his interview with the
BBC last week, RGM carefully sought to do two things: tell senior
party members that he intends to run again as the party's candidate
in the 2002 state presidential ballot and that eventually, when he
decides to go, both his deputies would also have to clear the decks.
I think he succeeded in getting the message across, but whether he
will indeed be allowed to stand as ZANU PF's presidential candidate
is another thing altogether. But then, we won't have to wait much
longer because his party is scheduling what could turn out to be a
particularly defining internal power struggle in the December
congress. It's clear that all the unhappy voices which have hitherto
only privately complained about his policies in whispers will now
either have to come out into the open or shut up for a long time
indeed.
There are no two ways about it - the hour of decision has finally
arrived. FIM's attention has been drawn to an obituary on black
nationalist Michael Mawema written by ZANU PF's legal chief Eddison
Zvobgo in the Mirror two weeks ago. "It is a shame that he (Mawema)
was denied a Heroes' Acre funeral," Zvobgo wrote, referring to the
Politburo's decision to reject an application by Masvingo province
for hero status for a man whose contribution to Zimbabwe's 1970s
independence struggle has already been well-chronicled for anyone who
cares. "There is no doubt that his passion was the people and that
his life's purpose was also the people," Zvobgo said. He added: "It
is however comforting to know that many deserving heroes did not make
it to Heroes' Acre. Dr Mawema joins Sheba Tavarwisa, Lookout Masuku,
Jairos Jiri and numerous more who lie humbly elsewhere." Readers will
recall equally critical comments which Zvobgo, who is clearly upping
his criticism of the government, made at Mawema's private funeral in
Masvingo a few weeks ago. He told the mourners, most of them drawn
from his powerbase of Masvingo, that they should not worry too much
about the Politburo's decision because, after all, it had been made
by men and women who themselves "are not perfect". The Politburo had
merely voted by a show of hands which he said could never guarantee
anyone a place either in hell or heaven.
Mawema's deeds while on this our beautiful earth, he said, clearly
made him a people's hero. Looks like Zimbabwe's usual dull political
landscape is headed for some torrid times. Pity that it took so many
men and women of conscience this long to speak out against obvious
injustices, but then it is perhaps better late than never. Some of
the reasons given by government sympathisers for the seizure of
Capital Radio's broadcasting equipment are really outrageous,
notwithstanding the fact that these people have every right to say
what they are saying. Last week in Harare, there was Chenjerai
Hunzvi, leader of the lawless section of the war veterans, telling a
public meeting that the government's clampdown on the radio would
deny imperialist forces a voice to announce a coup which he said they
are plotting against the government. Really? What evidence does he
have and why has he not bothered to report it to the police, as he
should as a concerned and patriotic Zimbabwean? As for Jonathan Moyo,
the government's chief spin doctor, I have yet to hear a denial from
him that Capital Radio will only get a broadcasting licence over "my
dead body" - as he has been widely reported as saying. Surely how can
a government minister who is supposed to be fair to all (I can hear
you laughing at my irresponsible statement!) - and in this case the
final arbiter on who gets such a licence - speak out in this manner?
Capital Radio, owned and run by mostly Zimbabweans, has every right
to have its application for a broadcasting licence heard by an
impartial panel and not to be pre-judged by those who appear to have
already condemned it for whatever reasons, however valid. Certainly
such statements already disqualify Moyo from being an impartial judge
in this instance and could weigh heavily against him in any court
case that the radio station could bring against him.
Still on Moyo, he seemed to be at pains to tell state television at
the weekend that the presidential amnesty granted to people guilty of
offences related to the February referendum and the June ballot had
been a result of representations made by various groups which felt
that the action would help the cause of reconciliation. Really? It
would be interesting to know the groups that made the representations
and which political party will benefit most from the amnesty. What is
clear though is that the pardons, to benefit outright criminals who
abducted and even tortured opponents, will accentuate lawlessness in
Zimbabwe and increasingly push ordinary people into taking the law
into their own hands. When a government fails to protect its own
citizens and even pardons the guilty, no one is safe anymore and
there is no law and order, which Moyo says still exists in Zimbabwe.
As thousands of poor residents of Harare's Mabvuku and Tafara took to
the streets in protest against skyrocketing living costs earlier this
week, none other than the head of the Consumer Council of Zimbabwe
(CCZ) Elizabeth Nerwande denounced them. Nerwande, who is
increasingly alienating herself from hard-pressed consumers by her
frequent pro-establishment pronouncements, suddenly saw a hidden
political hand in the spontaneous riots. She then urged consumers to
boycott bread, whose price went up again last week, and to eat sweet
potatoes and madhumbe, forgetting that not only are these foodstuffs
seasonal but actually cost much more than bread these days. Just in
case the CCZ boss was unaware, a kg of both madhumbe and sweet
potatoes now costs no less than $30, higher than the $20 that one
spends to buy a small loaf of broad. And this, of course, assumes
that urban dwellers in cities such as Bulawayo and Harare can still
find madhumbe which I understand are only grown in far-away
Manicaland.
During my travels into rural areas in the past few weeks, I found an
interesting point worth sharing with readers: while urban areas
suffocate from constant fuel shortages, rural folks have never had it
better. In fact, fuel shortages are precisely the sort of things
which the rural folk only hear about on the radio.
I am sure this has nothing to do with the policies of ZANU PF
officials such as Ignatius Chombo and Obert Mpofu of punishing urban
dwellers for voting for the opposition during the June election.

Original_Search_Title: Irin-CEA Regional Update
Headline: Central Africa: GREAT LAKES: IRIN-CEA Update 1,035
Body_Text:
DRC: UN expert condemns detention of NGO workers
UN Special Rapporteur on the Situation of Human Rights in the DRC
Roberto Garreton has strongly condemned the detention of rights
activists and representatives of nongovernmental organisations (NGOs)
in South Kivu, in the east of the country. He also criticised the
maltreatment of the detainees, and attacks on their freedoms of
association and assembly. In a press release issued on Friday,
Garreton criticised the arrest of a group of 15 NGO representatives
and human rights defenders at the headquarters of the Jeremie Groupe
on 9 October, when a group of armed men reportedly forced their way
into the meeting room and roughly manhandled those present before
forcing them into vehicles which took them to a detention centre.
"With the exception of one or two, the detainees were tortured before
finally being set free around 19:00 hours the same day," the press
release stated. Garreton said that this attack had taken place just
days after the visit of UN High Commissioner for Human Rights Mary
Robinson to eastern DRC between 1-3 October, and was totally
contradictory to the commitments made by the rebel Rassemblement
congolais pour la democratie (RCD) during that visit on the need to
protect human rights defenders. He urged that the detainees should
not be pursued judicially or suffer from further intimidation. The
release noted that Garreton had presented this information to UN
Secretary-General Kofi Annan and would also be informing the UN
General Assembly of these events. [for full statement, go to:
http://www.unog.ch/news/]
DRC: Rwanda says nothing new in DRC troop withdrawal offer
President Paul Kagame of Rwanda said on Tuesday that DRC President
Laurent-Desire Kabila's promised withdrawal of troops to 15 km
distance from their current positions was nothing new. Kabila had
already agreed to this in a disengagement plan agreed in Kampala,
Uganda, but had never wanted to implement the clause, while Rwanda
had asked to effect the withdrawal over 200 km, Rwandan radio quoted
Kagame as saying. It remained to be seen if Kabila would have the
Forces armees congolaises (FAC) act on the latest withdrawal offer,
Kagame said. Despite violations of the Lusaka peace agreement, Rwanda
would continue to seek peace while safeguarding its security and
territorial integrity, he added.
Kabila agreed to have DRC troops withdraw 15 km during a meeting with
the heads of state of Zimbabwe, Namibia, Uganda and Rwanda - Robert
Mugabe, Sam Nujoma, Yoweri Museveni and Paul Kagame - in the
Mozambican capital Maputo on Monday. It was also agreed that the
country leaders would meet again in two weeks' time to assess the
progress that had been made in troop withdrawals.
BURUNDI: 8,000 displaced in and around Rutana
Approximately 1,600 families, or 8,000 people, have been displaced in
Rutana province in the southwest, and in areas closer to the border
with Tanzania, as a result of recent fighting between the Burundi
army and rebel groups, humanitarian sources told IRIN on Tuesday.
Burundi radio last week reported that 16,000 families had been
displaced "following the infiltration of terrorist gangs from
Tanzania." The 1,600 families displaced included affected populations
in Bihuhu and Kayero, Musongati, Gitaba and Rutana, an aid official
in Bujumbura stated. There were also unconfirmed reports of heavy
fighting and related displacement in the central province of Gitega,
which humanitarian agencies were following up, the official stated.
The provinces worst affected by continuing clashes between Hutu rebel
groups and the Burundi army are Makamba, Bujumbura Rural, Rutana,
Ruyigi and Bururi, where the rebels have strong bases, according to
an October emergency update from the nongovernmental organisation
Save the Children (UK). The eastern province of Cankuzo has also been
the scene of considerable conflict.
BURUNDI: Army believes Friday rebel ceasefire unlikely
Defence Minister Colonel Cyrille Ndayirukiye on Tuesday said he did
not believe Burundi rebel groups would cease fighting by Friday, 20
October, the deadline set by regional leaders for the armed groups to
begin a ceasefire. Ndayirukiye said the army would battle the rebels
with increased strength if the ceasefire deadline was not met, the
BBC's Kirundi/Kinyarwanda service reported. In his view, the Burundi
war was not over, Ndayirukiye added. The minister told a press
conference in Bujumbura that, in addition to armed Burundi groups,
the army was fighting Congolese Mayi Mayi and Rwandan Interahamwe
militias. Ndayirukiye also said the army was not retrenching soldiers
but would address that issue after some progress had been made with
implementation of the Arusha peace accord, signed on 28 August.
BURUNDI: Tracing of separated children continues
Two hundred and five once separated children have been reunited with
their families since January, according to an October emergency
update from Save the Children (UK), which coordinates all family
tracing and prevention of separation work in Burundi. While war and
population displacements were directly responsible for many
separations and left children "extremely vulnerable to separation",
children also ended up on their own because of family breakdown
arising from poverty and an environment of ongoing violence, Save the
Children (SCF) said. "Despite these factors, the actual number of
unaccompanied children in Burundi is much lower than circumstances
would suggest ... mainly because separated children are absorbed into
extended family networks," it added.
Bringing together the different groups involved in family tracing
work, including the Ministry of Social Action and the Promotion of
Women, had allowed SCF to form a detailed picture of the complex
causes of child separation, and to collaborate with other agencies in
implementing prevention and tracing programmes, the report said. [for
further details, go to Burundi page at: http://www.reliefweb.int/]
RWANDA: RPA says more than 44,000 repatriated from DRC
The Rwandan Patriotic Army (RPA) has repatriated some 210 Rwandan
refugees who had been rounded up by the Interahamwe and ex-FAR,
particularly in Gisha, Gito and Tongo, in North Kivu, eastern DRC,
Rwandan radio reported at the weekend. It quoted Rwandan army field
commander, Lt-Col Alexis Kagame, as saying that the aim of the army
was not only to fight the enemy in the region but also to free the
hostages and to let them return home. More than 10,000 civilians who
had been trapped by the Interahamwe and the ex-Forces armees
rwandaises (ex-FAR) in the forest of Nyamuragira, in Masisi, North
Kivu, have been rescued in all by the RPA, according to its
officials. Rwandan radio on Tuesday quoted Captain Frank Mutembe as
saying that the repatriation process had been a success due to
"commendable cooperation" between the RPA and residents of Masisi.
Colonel Laurent Munyakazi was quoted as saying that, since the
operation started last year, more than 44,000 Rwandans had been
"liberated and repatriated" to Rwanda.
Although UNHCR could not verify the repatriation or the figures
involved, an official in Nairobi said that refugees who had recently
returned to Rwanda had told UNHCR officials there that they had been
in contact with RPA soldiers in eastern DRC to find out about the
security situation back in Rwanda. The UNHCR, for its part, had
repatriated 11,268 Rwandan refugees from the DRC to Rwanda between
January and September, the official said. The agency repatriated some
31,982 Rwandan refugees from the DRC in 1999.
RWANDA: Rainfall predictions over-optimistic
An optimistic seasonal rainfall forecast for Rwanda, issued in August
by the Drought Monitoring Centre (DMC) in Nairobi, has been revised
downwards, suggesting that drought-affected areas in Kigali Rural,
Gitarama, Butare and Kibungo will face additional problems with
agricultural production to the end of the year, a monthly food
security update from USAID's Famine Early Warning System (FEWS)
reported on Sunday. The DMC now predicted that Rwanda had a 35
percent probability of below-normal rainfall for the October-December
period, the report said.
The price of major food items had increased by 13 percent, on
average, from early September to early October, according to market
price data cited by FEWS. While a 13 percent rise in the price of
beans was typical for the time of year, triggered by an increased
demand from farmers - for seeds - and boarding schools, price
increases of 25 percent for Irish potatoes and 17 percent for cassava
flour suggested smaller reserves than previously thought, the report
added. Informal imports from the DRC were helping to keep price
increased in check, especially for bananas and beans, the report
said. The price of bulky, perishable commodities (sweet potatoes,
cassava and green bananas) had increased more in Bugesera region,
worst hit by the continuing drought, than in the rest of the country,
it said.

Original_Search_Title: Irin Regional Update
Headline: Central Africa: GREAT LAKES: IRIN Update 1,034
Body_Text:
DRC: Leaders agree to pull back troops
South African President Thabo Mbeki said after a one-day summit in
the Mozambican capital, Maputo, on Monday that five leaders with
troops in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) had agreed to pull
back their troops close to the positions they held when they signed
the Lusaka ceasefire agreement in July last year. The summit was
attended by Presidents Laurent Desire Kabila of the DRC, Robert
Mugabe of Zimbabwe, Sam Nujoma of Namibia, Yoweri Museveni of Uganda
and Paul Kagame of Rwanda. Mbeki's spokesman, Nazeem Mahatey, told
reporters after the meeting: "The presidents agreed that forces be
withdrawn 15 km backwards, and Rwanda further offered to withdraw
some 200 km back from current positions."
Meanwhile, the BBC said on Tuesday that Kabila had reportedly agreed
to the deployment of UN peacekeepers in government-controlled areas
of the DRC.
DRC: Army denies launching "offensive on all fronts"
The Forces armees congolaises (FAC) on Monday denied that it was
engaged in any "generalised offensive" against rebels in the country,
and accused the Rassemblement congolais pour la democratie (RCD) of
making such allegations to undermine this week's political talks on
the DRC crisis in the Mozambican capital, Maputo. Colonel Eddy Kapend
denied what he called "unfounded charges" by the RCD first
vice-president, Jean-Pierre Ondekane, that the FAC had launched an
offensive on all fronts, AFP reported. Ondekane said on Sunday that
President Laurent-Desire Kabila had on 14 October launched "a general
offensive on all fronts" against the RCD and the rebel Mouvement de
liberation du Congo (MLC) of Jean-Pierre Bemba, the report added.
DRC: Government sending reinforcements to Mbandaka
Meanwhile, the DRC government is reportedly still sending military
reinforcements to the towns of Mbandaka and Imese in Equateur
Province, northwestern DRC. An official from the rebel Mouvement de
liberation du Congo (MLC) told IRIN on Tuesday that the fighting had
"calmed down" since Sunday. "Government troops bombarded our
positions at Konongo again on Saturday," he said. "The situation is
quiet but unpredictable." He confirmed that there were several POWs
at MLC camps in Angenga in Lisala and Gbadolite.
RWANDA: Lobby group condemns "villagisation"
A lobby group, the Rassemblement pour le retour des refugies et la
democratie au Rwanda (RDR), on Tuesday called for the dismantling of
the "rural townships" set up by the government, saying the programme
was alienating people from their livelihoods. "Under this programme,
millions of peasants are forcibly displaced from their livelihoods,
homes, land, property, freedoms of movement and residence, and are
caged into small territorial enclaves with marginal land, while the
bulk of the best agricultural land is allocated to members of the
military, government officials and supporters," a statement from the
organisation said. It said that in 1998 and 1999, more than 700,000
people in the northwestern Rwandan prefectures of Gisenyi and
Ruhengeri were "forcibly" moved from their homes by the military as a
"counter-insurgency measure" and "caged" in "NAZI-style" internment
camps, where harsh detention conditions resulted in the deaths of
"thousands of people".
It noted that when the government declared that security had been
restored in the area, displaced people were "not allowed" to return
to their original homes. "Instead, with no consultation at all nor
any say in the plan, displaced people were settled in government
villages along or near the roads in order to administratively control
them easily under the guise of modernisation and improvement of
access to social services," it said. RDR said that the programme was
being extended to the Kibungo, Gitarama and Cyangugu prefectures,
which "are not affected by the fighting between the army and the
rebels". It called on the international community to condemn this
programme.
RWANDA: Food security fears worst in Bugesera
Most crops in the high-altitude areas of western and northern Rwanda
were developing normally, after year 2000/2001 'Season A' started on
time in mid-September, USAID's Famine Early Warning System (FEWS)
reported on Sunday. However, in the Bugesera region of Kigali Rural
Prefecture, rains had been too intermittent to allow planting, and
people faced food shortages and increased poverty, it said. This was
also the case in neighbouring Gitarama, Butare and Kibungo
prefectures, the FEWS food security update stated. "The slow start of
this important season for nearly half of the country does not bode
well for agricultural production," it added.
The situation was particularly worrying because the combination of
inadequate rain, a seed shortage and low farm incomes reduced the
likelihood of the main seasonal crops (beans, maize and groundnuts)
being planted by 20 October as they should be, the report stated.
"The situation in and around Bugesera region is becoming very
disturbing, and requires close monitoring throughout the season," it
said. The government had distributed about 20 mt of seeds so far, but
needed to extend urgent support to the NGO, Caritas, which was ready
to distribute sweet potato vines, cassava cuttings and 100 mt of
various types of seeds, according to FEWS. [further details at:
http://www.fews.net/]
BURUNDI: Refugees streaming into Tanzania
Thousands of Burundian refugees from villages in the eastern province
of Cankuzo have been streaming across the Tanzanian border near Ngara
since early last week, following clashes between Burundian rebels and
government troops. A statement from UNHCR said that the number of
arrivals was increasing daily, reaching 2,700 on 12 October. Another
2,000 were waiting at the border to be transferred to Mbuba transit
camp. The first arrivals said rebels who recently attacked military
targets in the east had ordered civilians in the area to leave
Burundi. On 11 October, UNHCR staff in Tanzania saw villages burning
across the border in Burundi and some refugees arriving that day
identified the burning homes as their own.
The agency said that four people were treated for gunshot wounds, but
the majority were in "good health". It said that many of them were
indicating that they hoped to return to Burundi as "soon as possible"
as they expected to be able to harvest their crops in the coming
weeks. Through the night of 12 October, UNHCR staff had transferred
almost 5,000 refugees from the border zone 100 km south of Ngara to a
transit centre, and then to Lukole camp. "Refugees are over a wide
area, with the main crossings located in a steep-sided valley, which
makes movement more difficult," the agency said. Recent arrivals in
Kigoma Region also reported an increase in internal displacement in
Burundi, especially in Cankuzo Province.
BURUNDI: SCF warns of declining child health
Save the Children (UK) has warned of clear indications that Burundi's
already dismal record on child health was worsening. Malaria, in
particular, was on the increase as a result of lowered immunity due
to poor nutritional status, cramped living conditions in regroupment
camps, the breakdown of health systems, and restricted availability
of anti-malarial drugs, it said in an emergency update for October,
released last week. Serious outbreaks of measles and dysentery this
year also indicated that the health status of children was
deteriorating, the report said. While HIV/AIDS was the main public
health concern (21 percent of adults in urban areas are reported to
be HIV-positive), malaria, respiratory infections such as bronchitis,
and dysentery were the most common causes of child death, Save the
Children (SCF) added.
The agency also expressed concern over the growing number of
child-headed households, street children, and children living in
conditions of extreme poverty in slum areas. "Many of these children
enter into sexual relations at a young age and are therefore at risk
of contracting HIV," it said. These children also suffered from
emotional traumas due to conflict, low literacy levels, exploitation
by adults and inability to access health and social services, SCF
added. Some families who did not have enough to eat abandoned their
children at camp feeding centres and, "more generally, economic
decline was undermining the ability of poor families to care for
their children, leading to voluntary separation", it said.
Nairobi, 17 October 2000, 12:00 gmt

Original_Search_Title: Huambo Refugees to Be Self-sufficient in Food
Headline: Angola: Huambo Refugees to Be Self-sufficient in Food
Body_Text:
Luanda, Angola — Plans are underway to make some 300,000
Angolans at Huambo district, self-sufficient in food for the remaining
period of 2000 and 2001, a local administrator said.
Huambo district governor Paulo Cassoma said the necessary material
and technical requirements would be provided the refugees to engage in
substantial agricultural production in the area.
"This would be a way to allow the disaster victims, estimated to be
more than 300,000 people not to depend on international food aid," he
added. The refugees fled war-torn areas of Angola.
According to him, 1.15 million US dollars has been released to the
Cassoma governorship to carry out this mission, as part of the National
Humanitarian Aid Programme recently approved by the Angolan government.
He said 700,000 dollars of the amount would be allocated to boost
agriculture in the areas where the war refugees will be settled, while
the rest of the money would go towards the provision of seeds, mainly
sweet potatoes, maize, soya beans and groundnuts.
Some of the seeds, particularly beans, groundnuts and soya beans
are said to be already available in Huambo, the administrative capital of
the district.
The governor Thursday visited refugee camps being built at
Colomanda, Cassoco, Canata, Cantao-Paula and Cruzeiro.

Original_Search_Title: How Languages Were Monitored in Old Days
Headline: Kenya: How Languages Were Monitored in Old Days
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Once a Damiano or a teacher, always a Damiano. That is why
the main headmaster in this country, who was once a Damiano, will
