always be a teacher. That is why a week ago, the blood of the teacher
in him made him decide there is something wrong with a radio that
broadcasts in the language called Ki-Slopes.
After looking at coconut trees and the beaches in Mombasa during the
agricultural show, he announced he will give something called a
monitor to that radio station if its tongue does not start speaking
only in English and Kiswahili. A monitor was something that he used
to give little boys and girls on the slopes of Sacho where he was a
Damiano if they spoke the language of Sacho instead of English.
Teacher Dan, as he was known then, must have known and used that
thing called a monitor because in those days if you were a
no-nonsense teacher, one of the business that you had was to stop
small natives from speaking what were called shenzi (stupid)
languages. Shenzi languages were the languages in which those with
such words as "Atiriri - Koro? Ichiewo nade?-Omundu khu mundu- Murume
wa kale. Walamka kaze - Wiasya ata - Chamgei mising -" In other
words, they were languages that the natives of Kenya spoke.
They were called native tongues or shenzi languages because the Queen
of England was the boss here. That is why all the government vehicles
had number plates reading "OHMS or On Her Majesty's Service. She
decided that those languages were shenzi because everything that the
native owned was called shenzi. Those native cows with a hump were
called shenzi cows and the Queen's white agents held their noses as
if they smelt something evil when they saw one. Then they persuaded
the natives to own what were called mzungu or grade cows.
When the native got a hair cut in the style that his grandfathers and
grandmothers were doing, the agents of the Queen of England shook
their heads and said, That is a shenzi hair cut. You must learn to be
shaven in the civilised English way." The people of the Slopes
started speaking about being shaven githungu or the English style.
In her wisdom, the Queen of England decided that the tongues of
natives must be made civilised and one of the ways of doing so was to
make them stop speaking the shenzi languages. So she gave
instructions to the education officers who were riding in the OHMS
vehicles. "Any teacher who wants get an improved payslip must make
sure that the native totos don't speak shenzi languages. They must
speak the language of the Queen."
Teacher Damiano and Teacher Dan thought about their payslips and told
themselves that they were ready to improve them by obeying the Queen
and her agents who were riding in the OHMS vehicles. That is why they
came up with the item called the monitor. Since our tongues found it
very difficult to pronounce that word, we called the thing the monto.
The monto came in various shapes and sizes. It all depended on the
mood of the class teacher. When Teacher Damiano came to school in a
bad mood, perhaps after his Thatcher had given him sugarless tea, he
went for a monto that resembled what the donkeys in Limuru wear on
their necks as they pull carts. It is that wooden thing called a
yoke. Damiano made a monto that resembled this for the small native
girls and boys who spoke the shenzi languages.
The monto was a block of wood with a hole for the head to go through.
It also had a major statement on it. The statement said, "I am a
fool." Once you were caught speaking the shenzi language in class,
you were made to wear that wooden thing on your neck. If you heard
another small native speaking a shenzi language, you quickly handed
it to him or her like the cross that Jesus carried to where he was
nailed. Whoever ended up having the monto at the end of the day was
massacred with a cane by Teacher Damiano just to prove to them that
speaking shenzi languages was a major crime.
In my class there was a daughter of the soil called Chiru who was
neither too ugly nor too beautiful to look at. It happened that
whenever I saw her, certain insects got into my stomach and my blood
temperature rose as if I was suffering from malaria combined with
typhoid. My heart did a Kipketer and I suddenly felt as if my knees
were running out of fluids. In other words, the god of love started
playing games with me.
There was something else about Chiru. Her tongue was very fond of
refusing to speak the language of the Queen of England. She was a
real ka-native so the Queen's language avoided her tongue. In short,
she ended up carrying the monto at the end of many a day. This meant
that she risked being massacred by Damiano every evening.
Whenever I saw her carrying that monto, I felt like going to Damiano
to break his old neck but since my hands were too small for that job,
I did not do so. Instead, I ended up suffering on behalf of my
heartthrob Chiru. For that reason, I went to her and took the yoke
from her neck and put it on my own. So everybody read that I was
fool.
Then Damiano worked on me with his cane as if I had stolen from his
farm. I agreed to suffer all that much because of the god of love - a
fellow called Cupid. Then what happened to Chiru when time came for
me to declare that she should be my Thatcher at some date in the
future? She left our school. Later, I heard that she got married to a
bus driver because such characters as bus drivers were thought to be
very tough men in those days.
Teacher Dan must have made what were called native totos carry the
monto on the Slopes of Sacho for saying such things as "chamgei
mising", and I guess that is why he is saying that any radio station
which dares to speak the native languages will carry a monitor. It is
for that reason that I fear that one of these days, he will wake up
and say: "Gutoga leo, gila Mgenya lazima gusema Gizungu ama
Giswahili. Gutoka leo, sitagi gusigia eti aterere, sijui eti Ichiewo
nade ooo, sijui Achiewo maber, chamgei, wiasya ata, ama mambo gama
hayo. Na hiyo ni maendeleo." The former teacher might even make the
police carry monitors to put on the necks of people who dare speak
those languages. This is to say that I fear that Kenyans might be
forced to wear things stating"Mimi mjinga!" for speaking in the
shenzi languages.
The man who was born and brought up in Sacho must have worked under
those characters who used to drive around in those OHMS vehicles. The
vehicles were of a tribe called Austin because the Landrover was
still too civilised to step on the Kenyan soil and the fellows in the
Education Ministry who used to ride in them were called AEOs or
Assistant Education Officers. The tongues of the natives were too
heavy to pronounce AEO so they called them IO's.
Those of us who suffered in the hands of such terrorists as Teacher
Damiano loved those IOs. We held a party when they visited our school
because they terrorised the other terrorists such as Damiano. They
were known to make a man like Teacher Dan regret the day he became a
man of chalk. They were like guerrilla fighters and struck when they
were least expected.
The AEOs wore short pants and woollen stockings. They stuck biros of
three colours on the stocking of the left leg. One was blue, the
other red and the third green. They stuck similar biros in their
pockets. We heard that people who had chewed enough books were
supposed to dress like that anywhere in the world. We also heard that
each biro had a purpose. The blue biro was used for writing letters
to recommend the improvement of the payslip of a teacher who made the
native totos speak English through the nose as they used to say.
We heard that the green biro was used by the AEO to write letters to
a man called the Governor of Kenya who was the Queen's personal
representative in Kenya. The red had a very special purpose. It was
used to write letters of dismissal to the Damianos who did not
manufacture enough monitors to put sense into native totos by making
them speak English through the nose. We believed all those things
because there was no other way that we could understand why a man
needed to carry six pens.
When an AEO went to a school it was not to say jambo to Teacher Dan
or Teacher Damiano. It was to check how well he was doing his job. So
the AEO did not drive into the school compound. He parked it very far
from the school and then walked through the bush. When he got to the
school compound, he crawled on his belly like a trained soldier and
then popped up in the class just when Teacher Damiano was busy doing
things that a teacher was not supposed to be doing.
A few minutes before the arrival of the AEO, Teacher Damiano will
have said, "Look here children. Open page ten of your Highway
Mathematics and do your sums in silence. If I hear a word, I will sew
up the mouth where it has come from with a needle meant to sew up a
gunia." We knew that Teacher Damiano - God bless his soul - was
capable of carrying out his threat so we'd open the page and start
doing the sums in total silence.
Damiano would sit on a chair in front with a table before him. As
soon as he uttered the threat to sew up our mouths, his eyelids met
and his nose started making certain noises suggesting that he had a
tribe of frogs inside his stomach. A small stream of saliva started
doing a tour down his chin. In other words, he went into very serious
sleep, with his tongue hanging out.
It was precisely at that moment when the frogs in Teacher Damiano's
stomach were singing like a mad choir that the AEO arrived and walked
like a ghost to the window of the classroom. The long hairs inside
his nostrils started doing a dance when he saw Teacher Damiano
looking as if he was in the land of the dead. His nose became redder
and redder as he saw the stream of saliva doing a tour down the
teacher's chin.
Finally, he could not hold his tongue any more. It just exploded and
he, in what was called Ki-settler Kiswahili, roared: (It was a
Kiswahili that was neither here or there.He exploded"Damiano! Kwe
nini you blerry lala lala like a - like a - blerry Kukuyu kondoo
baada ya kula sweet potatoes. Nakwenda college kusoma kulala ama
somecha?" His voice sounded like a submarine vomiting torpedoes and
was loud enough to wake up even the devil in his sleep in hell.
Certainly that was enough to wake Teacher Damiano up.
He shot up as if he had been shot from a cannon. His eyes blinked as
he saw the AEO at the window. He saluted five times while at the same
time saying: "I swear, Sir, that I was not sleeping! I was meditating
on how to deal with algebra." Naturally, the whole classroom burst
into laughter. With his knees knocking like those of a chicken
suffering from a bad case of malaria, Teacher Damiano walked out of
the classroom to meet his fate in the hands of the AEO.
I don't know whether Teacher Dan might have suffered similar fate at
the hands of the AEO. However, if the good teacher from Sacho is
reading, I can only say: "Chamgei". I hope I won't be handed the
monto for that. I am sure that he understands that since he was born
and brought up where people say that in the morning instead of
opening their mouths to spread greetings in English.

Original_Search_Title: Shut Up And Eat Your GM Soup, Africans Are Told
Headline: Kenya: Shut Up And Eat Your GM Soup, Africans Are Told
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Kenya Agricultural Research Institute, Kari, teaming up
with the international agribusiness giant Monsanto, achieved a coup
of sorts when they went public early last month with their
genetically modified sweet potato, described as highly resistant to
viral diseases.
It was a clever strategy that presented a fait accompli and pre-
empted what critics would have said had these man-modified crops been
subjected to an open discussion.
In their moment of glory and back-patting, Kari forgot that Monsanto
is the agribusiness company that very nearly enslaved Third World
agriculture with their "terminator" seed technology, which would have
forced poor farmers into permanent dependence on Monsanto and similar
companies for their seed needs.
Before Kari's announcement, Dr Harold Salter of the British biotech
company, CropGen, had said casually on the BBC that Kari were proving
quite innovative with genetic engineering technology. He cited the
disease-resistant sweet potato. Dr Salter made me sit up and wonder
what else we are eating locally that has been modified with exotic
genes.
There is clearly no democracy in this matter; you eat what the
scientists deem good for you or for your agriculture. In the past, I
would shy away from American foods in supermarkets, but if Kari, with
the covert assistance of Monsanto, and other American or British
agribusiness giants, is bringing exotic genes into African nutrition
with the simplistic assurance that it is all safe, then we need to
worry.
Genetic engineering is the artificial insertion of the genetic
material of one organism into another type of organism in order to
effect a presumed advantage in productivity, disease resistance,
taste and other qualities. When one Dr Chakrabarty, an Indian born
American scientist, succeeded in patenting a man-made bacterium able
to lap up oil spills in the ocean, it was hailed at the time as a
major breakthrough.
I remember wondering aloud in the Sunday Nation at the time what
would happen if Dr Chakrabarty's oil-eating bacterium extended its
culinary preference to include all forms of plastics. Now, 20 years
down the road, truly exotic things are being done with similar
technology to give us what Martin Walker, Chairman of Iceland Foods
in the UK, has called "Frankenstein Foods." Genes responsible for the
manufacture of deadly scorpion poison (in scorpions) have been
transferred by man from where God put them, into some crops to
produce toxins that kill crop-eating insects.
Scientists have also transferred genes from animals with an ability
to withstand extreme cold into commercial crops to impart to them the
ability to withstand frost. Proponents claim that GM is merely an
extension of hybridisation, which has given us so many of our crop
varieties, such as Katumani maize. But hybridisation is a breeding
process within related species. You don't force genes of fish into
tomatoes and claim it is the same thing as breeding Katumani maize.
As for our dwarf Ruiru II coffee variety, it was never a GM crop but
a hybrid involving planting materials from coffee varieties obtained
from Colombia. The way Dr Salter presented his comments on the BBC
was typical of many scientists in this field, who assume they are
doing us a favour by stuffing all manner of combinations of genes
down our throats.
The commonest argument one hears in reference to Africa is that the
technology will ensure greater food supplies for a hungry continent.
Sadly, in a manner reminiscent of the intolerance that prevails in
African politics, not a whiff of dissent is allowed. Western
scientists who have ventured to warn their African colleagues to be
cautious of this newfangled science have been branded "racists" by
some African scientists - as happened to Dr Hans Herren, director of
the Nairobi- based International Centre of Insect Physiology and
Ecology, at a recent conference organised by the African
Biotechnology Stakeholders Forum.
According to The People of March 4, Dr Herren was accused of racism
and related diseases by, among others, Kari director Dr Cyrus
Ndiritu, and scientists Dr Florence Wambugu, Professor Norah Olembo
and Prof J.O. Ochanda, who, in classic Kanu witch-hunt style, "poured
vitriol" on the hapless (and absent) Dr Herren with the support of
(surprise, surprise) two officials from the biotechnology giant
Novartis, Klaus Leisinger and Timothy Reeves.
Don't fool yourself that the scientists who are doing these things to
us are remotely conversant with the repercussions. As an American
media commentator, Myra Rosenblum has observed "the bottom line is
money, money, money..." And as for those simplistic assurances of
safety, "No long term safety studies have been carried out,"
according to Dr John Fagan, a top British molecular biologist and
author of Genetic Engineering - the Hazards.
Charles Thomas of the British environment watchdog, Greenpeace, calls
GM "living pollution" while the head of the British Food Standards
Agency, Prof Phillip James, believes that those that see this
technology as an advance in science "are being totally naive." A case
has been reported in South Africa by Dr H Steinman of the Allergies
Society of South Africa, who noted the death of a person allergic to
Brazil nuts, who ate soya genetically engineered with a Brazil nut
gene.
The New England Journal of Medicine recently commented that the US
Food and Drug Administration actually favours the genetic engineering
industry. Should this be surprising, given that the deputy head of
FDA recently joined Monsanto as senior vice president?
Whom do we in East Africa hold accountable once a genetically
engineered food crop goes crazy leading to mass poisoning, sterility
or fatal asthma attacks? If food crops act as their own pesticides,
what is to stop us being poisoned by them? What is to stop bees and
birds being decimated by them? Furthermore, there is nothing to stop
these genes spreading to species. "Designer crops" with an ability to
withstand greater concentrations of herbicide than the common weeds
of agriculture may spread their genes to the very same weeds.
Once the weeds themselves become herbicide resistant and reach areas
of the world where subsistence farming is still practised, they could
spell the end of age-old farming traditions in the same way as the
water hyacinth in Lake Victoria nearly wiped out artisanal fishing.
It is precisely to protect American agriculture and food industries
from worldwide protests and bans that the Clinton administration has
made it illegal to indicate on food package labels that a specific
food item is not genetically modified.
This will, in time, turn out to be one of the most reckless legacies
of the Clinton era. Beggars cannot be choosers. It is only a matter
of time before we begin hearing reports that Africa's million-plus
refugee population are living on GM foods Research agencies working
on food productivity in Africa should be reined in and made
accountable to parliaments and consumers.
The world has to go on long after the children of these scientists
are old men walking with the aid of sticks.

Original_Search_Title: Price Hikes Lift Inflation
Headline: Uganda: Price Hikes Lift Inflation
Body_Text:
Kampala — The annual headline inflation (change in prices for all
goods and services) for the year ending August,2000 rose from 2.8%
for the year ended July,2000 to 3.1%.
The Uganda Bureau of Statistics deputy executive director, James
Mubiru released the consumer price index for August yesterday. He
said the rise was mainly due to higher price levels of staple foods,
fruits, vegetables, bread, cereals, beverages, tobacco, transport and
communication as compared to those of August1999.
Annual underlying inflation (change in prices for all goods and
services excluding food crops) however, dropped to 3.1% in
August,2000 from 3.8% registered in July 2000.
Mubiru said lower prices for charcoal and a drop in taxi fares in
Kampala as well as lower rental charges in Mbarara led to the
decline.
He said the main causes of the changes in the inflation rates were
due to increases in prices of milk in the centres of Masaka, Jinja,
Mbarara and Kampala.
"Prices of sweet potatoes in all centres, fruits and vegetables in
all centres except Mbale went up. Increases in prices of local beers
in Masaka, Jinja and Gulu were also responsible for the changes,"he
said. He said the changes also came as a result of decrease in prices
of cigarettes in Kampala.

Original_Search_Title: Kenya Launches Genetically Modified Potato
Headline: Kenya Launches Genetically Modified Potato
Body_Text:
NAIROBI — Kenya marked its entry into the era of genetically modified foods by launching a genetically engineered disease-resistant sweet potato at the weekend.
The Kenya Agricultural Research Institute developed the potato, in
conjunction with Musanto, an American chemical multinational.
According to the institute, the sweet potato variety - is
genetically engineered to make it disease resistant and increase its
yield by up to 60 percent. No pesticide is required.
Sweet potatoes are widely grown in Western, Nyanza, Central and
Eastern provinces. Currently, half the crop is destroyed by a virus which
issues black marks on the tubers.
The institution's acting director, Richard Kiome, said
agriculture, Kenya 's fourth largest income-earner, needed to be improved
by using more research.
"Agriculture is the backbone of the country's economy as well as
human development. That is why KARI, with US support, researched on a
sweet potato variety that is disease resistant," he added.
Present at the launch was the founder of the Goodworks
International, former US Secretary of State Andrew Young, who is now a
goodwill Ambassador promoting American science and technology, especially
bio-technology.
"Africa is on the verge of a tremendous revolution. With
bio-technology, we are going to make a green revolution in Africa,
not through pharmaceuticals, but through nutroceuticals," Young
remarked.
On his part, the permanent secretary in the ministry of
agriculture, Shem Migot-Adhola, said bio-technology was important in
poverty eradication and sustainable development.
"Bio-technology is not our problem in Kenya right now, poverty is
the problem. The quest to increase agricultural produce is our
challenge", Migot-Adhola said.
Genetically modified organisms have become a great environmental
concern and there are fears that some multinationals are using the
so-called "green revolution" to "colonise" Africa by controlling food
production and marketing in the world.
However, Musanto defended genetic modification of
organisms, saying that the technology has been declared safe by the
World Health Organisation, the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation, as
well as the International Life-science Institute and the European Union.
Some scientists also are in agreement that genetically modified
organisms will improve food security in Africa and thus help alleviate
poverty.
"The technology is useful. Several African countries are looking
into ways of using bio-technology to increase their crop production,"
Musanto's government and public affairs manager for Africa, Kinyua
Mbijjiwe said.
The Kenya Bio-safety Council approved the Sweet potato project
seven months ago and test trials have been going on in Western Kenya's
Kakamega and Kisii areas; in the central Muguga and Embu and Mtwapa in
Coast province.
Thanks to GM technology, farmers in these zones can now produce up
to 56 tons of sweet potatoes per hectare.

Original_Search_Title: Genetically Modified Sweet Potato Launched in Kenya
Headline: Kenya: Genetically Modified Sweet Potato Launched in Kenya
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Genetically modified foods are finally here. The Kenya
Agricultural Research Institute yesterday launched the first such
sweet potato variety in the country. It is genetically engineered to
make it disease- resistant and is expected to increase yields by up
to 60 per cent. No pesticide is required.
Sweet potatoes are mostly grown in the densely-populated Western
Province, as well as Nyanza, Central and Eastern provinces.
Unfortunately, more than half of the crop is destroyed by a virus
which causes black marks on the tubers.
Kari and Monsanto, an American chemical transnational, initiated the
efforts to apply genetic engineering nine years ago.
The launch, at Kari's Nairobi headquarters, was attended by the
founder of Goodworks International, Ambassador Andrew Young of the
US. Mr. Young, whom became the first Afro-American representative to
the UN in Mr. Jimmy Carter's presidency, is now a goodwill ambassador
promoting American science and technology, especially biotechnology.
"Africa is on the verge of a tremendous revolution," said he,
quipping: "With biotechnology, we are going to make a green
revolution in Africa, not through pharmaceuticals but through
nutroceuticals."
The ambassador, Ministry of Agriculture officials and Monsanto
representatives also paid a courtesy call on President Moi.
The sweet potato project was approved by the Kenya Biosafety Council
seven months ago and mock-trials have been going on in Kakamega,
Kisii, Muguga, Mtwapa and Embu, where farmers are now able to produce
up to 56 tons an hectare.
Scientists believe genetically modified organisms will improve food
security in Africa and thus help alleviate poverty.
Kari's acting director, Dr Richard Kiome, says agriculture is the
fourth largest income-earner and that more research needs to be
carried out.
"Agriculture is the backbone of this country's economy as well as of
human development," he said. "That is why Kari, with the US support,
researched on a sweet potato variety that is disease-resistant."
Chipping in, Agriculture Permanent Secretary Shem Migot-Adhola said
biotechnology was important in poverty eradication and sustainable
development. "Biotechnology is not our problem in Kenya right now,
poverty is the problem. The quest to increase agricultural produce is
our challenge," said Prof Migot- Adhola.
Genetically modified organisms have become of great environmental
concern. There are fears that some multinationals are using the
so-called "Green Revolution" to "colonise" Africa by controlling food
production and marketing in the world.
However, Monsanto's government and public affairs manager-Africa, Mr.
Kinyua M'Mbijjewe, defended genetic modification of organisms, saying
that the technology has been declared safe by the World Health
Organisation, the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation, the
International Lifescience Institute and, lately, the European Union.
"The technology is useful. Several African countries are looking into
ways of using biotechnology to increase their crop production," said
Mr. M'Mbijjewe.
Other African countries in support of biotechnology include Nigeria,
Ghana, Zambia, South Africa and Egypt. Cote d'Ivoire and Uganda have
also showed some interest. South Africa has genetically modified
maize and cotton while trials are being carried out in Zambia.

Original_Search_Title: Donkey Transport Boosts Community Pride
Headline: Uganda: Donkeys Boon to East
Body_Text:
Kapchorwa — Donkey transport has of late become a household name and
pride for the people of Kapchorwa and Teso.
"The donkeys here in Kapchorwa are easy to rear and cheap in terms of
feeding.
They go grazing on grass just like cattle," Mzee Chepsekut, a farmer
told Business Vision in an interview at his home recently.
Chepsekut said the rural folk here have greatly benefited from the
use of donkeys for transporting food from the hilly terrain of the
district and thick forests with rocky meandering contours.
He also said the donkeys have enhanced agriculture.
"On market days, you can witness a fleet of donkeys with their mules
carrying luggage and food-stuffs like maize, Irish and sweet potatoes
for selling," Chepsekut said.
Chepsekut says it takes about six years for a full grown donkey to
begin to carry loads.
Business Vision talked to Dr. Christopher Kyeswa, the Livestock
Development Officer based in Soroti under the Soroti Catholic
Integrated Development Organisation (SOCADIDO) a development arm of
Soroti Catholic diocese.
The organisation was created for improving the well-being and living
standards of the people of Soroti and Kumi.
Dr. Kyeswa said that seven donkeys have so far been bought from
Kapchorwa and they are being reared at Aminit demonstration dairy
farm, which is under SOCADIDO management.
He said that these donkeys are good and strong enough to carry heavy
loads, transporting farm-equipment, cattle feeds and transporting
milk to milk centres.
They also help carry elephant grass for the cows and other
commodities on return journey. "Another interesting point is the
selling of the young donkeys (mules) to the farmers who are the
beneficiaries of SOCADIDO at subsidised prices," he said.
An adult donkey costs between sh250,000-sh300,000. A donkey can carry
loads of up to half a tonne. A donkey weighs up to 275 kilogramms.
Kyeswa said besides donkey rearing, SOCADIDO also has nine cattle
paddocks.
Kyeswa also revealed that Aminit farm is also a cross-breeding centre
for multiplication of exotic goats. These include, he said, the
Anglo-Nubian type from the Sudan, the Boer goat type from
South-Africa and Saanen and Togennburg imported from France and
Holland respectively. He said that Saanen and Togennburg are bred for
milk production.SOCADIDO Development Co-ordinator Rev.
Fr. Atanaseus Mubiru said the group's objective is to improve
well-being and living standards of Soroti and Kumi.
He said women from Anyar, Lwala, Bukedea and Kachumbala have
benefited from a cattle restocking programme.

Original_Search_Title: Goma: A World Apart From Congo
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Sackfuls of Money But Little Food in Goma
Body_Text:
Kigali — When Rwanda Patriotic Front (RPF) fighters invaded Rwanda in
the early 1990s, threatening the regime of former President Juvenal
Habyarimana, President Mobutu Sese Seko of Zaire, now the Democratic
Republic of Congo, rushed to Kigali.
A frantic Habyarimana, insiders recount, listened attentively as
Mobutu whispered matter-of-factly: "I warned you not to construct
tarmac roads all over the country. See, the RPF are now driving in
from all sides." Lack of roads was for him a sure way to keep off
rebels.
Then history proved him wrong. For he, too, was kicked out of power,
and not by a mechanised army.
For a first-time visitor to Congo, Goma town tells it all. Goma is a
port located on Lake Kivu, a few minutes' drive from the Rwandan town
of Gisenyi.
Along with other invited Tanzanian newsmen, we visited the town twice
this year on guided tours organised by the government of Rwanda,
first in February and then in June. On the first visit, we didn't
need any visa to enter Congo; we were simply waved in.
In contrast to what Mobutu had told Habyarimana, Goma is a beautiful
city with clean, wide streets. Standing at the town's central
roundabout, Point d'Independence, it becomes clear that life in Goma
is no longer what it used to be.
There are few vehicles on the road, partly due to scarcity of fuel,
or perhaps because the automobiles themselves are worn out. But,
certainly, the wide thoroughfares could not have been constructed for
so few vehicles.
The streets are deserted, with the few people one sees wearing gloomy
faces. On the pavements, a few beggars ply their trade.
For them, it is business as usual, in times of peace as well as in
times of war. There is little economic activity in Goma, as in other
parts of Congo.
Goma is the capital of North Kivu Province, which, along with South
Kivu, Pweto, Manyema and the disputed province of Katanga are under
the control of the Congolese Rally for Democracy (RCD-Goma), led by
Dr Emile Ilunga. The rest of the country is controlled by the forces
of President Laurent Desire Kabila, the Ugandan-backed Prof Wamba Dia
Wamba and the Movement for the Liberation of Congo (MLC) led by Mr.
Jean Pierre Bemba. The central market in Goma is however abuzz with
all the activities of a typical African market.
The locals easily spotted us as foreigners. The children called out
"Vieux (old man), give us some money," a call that was echoed by some
market women.
Some in our team reached for their wallets to donate the few dollars
or Rwandan currency they had. Otherwise, the money in use is still
the Mobutu currency, against which our guide had cautioned us: "You
need a full sack to buy anything with it." The currency suffered
runaway inflation in Mobutu's last years, with Zaire's central bank
minting multi-million-zaire banknotes to provide an illusion of
wealth.
As we moved around the market, vans carrying plantains, sweet
potatoes and other foodstuffs arrived. Yet, most of the people we
spoke to said they faced food shortages, with many women selling
cassava leaves - a sure sign of a bad crop in most African
communities.
"Why is there a food shortage with all the rain?" I asked one of the
women selling cassava leaves. She looked at us, then turned her eyes
to our stern-looking Rwandan guides and answered haltingly: "I do not
know." Perhaps.
But it was clear she felt intimidated by the presence of the guides.
On our way to lunch at the Hotel Karibu, a few kilometres out of Goma
along Lake Kivu, we marvelled at one of Mobutu's private chateaux, a
beach complex which he would visit from time to time for a short
rest.
It is said he had such complexes not only all over Congo, but also
throughout Europe. We were strictly forbidden to photograph the
closely-guarded premises.
To our dismay, the hotel did not have any food and we had to go
elsewhere. "We no longer get regular visitors here," said the
waiters.
According to our guides, Hotel Karibu was one of the best in the
former Zaire. While we did not get a proper feel of RCD-Goma
officialdom during the first visit, we were accorded that on the
second leg.
This time round, and despite being accompanied by Rwandan guides, we
had to obtain visas from the Goma/Gisenyi border post. We then drove
in a convoy for a session with Dr Ilunga at his residence, a short
distance from the Rwanda-Congo border.
At the border, there is lively interaction between the people of the
two countries. Some were heading to Gisenyi, while others were going
to Goma.
From Rwanda, the Congolese returned carrying heavy loads on their
backs. Those returning from Goma did not carry heavy loads,
suggesting Congo is in dire straits, getting almost everything from
its neighbours.
As we approached Dr Ilunga's palace, we passed many armed soldiers in
combat fatigues, a seemingly well-drilled lot. We found Dr Ilunga
waiting for us in his fortress close to Lake Kivu.
Soldiers were everywhere, obviously taking no chances even with
journalists. An eloquent Kiswahili speaker, Dr Ilunga said he was not
confident of his "Katangese Kiswahili." As we sat down for the
interview, his assistants were busy setting up a satellite dish,
suggesting that they had either moved in recently or the
state-of-the- art equipment had been just been acquired.
Dr Ilunga, a self-professed Lumumbist, presents an impression of a
leader with a clear vision as to what the RCD-Goma stands for. RCD,
he says, is an alliance against what he calls the opportunism of
President Laurent Kabila, with whom they had in 1997 allied to oust
Mobutu.
He claims Kabila cannot lead Congo successfully because he is
"shortsighted, inexperienced and a nepotist. . . He has appointed one
of his untrained sons chief of Congo's defence forces, as well as
appointing other top dignitaries from his own village." In spite of
the divisions within the RCD, one faction of which is headed by Prof
Wamba dia Wamba (RCD-Kisangani), Dr Ilunga believes the ideals of the
RCD remain inseparable "until Congo is united." As we talked, a large
noisy plane passed overhead, headed for Goma airport. Such planes, he
told us, provided most transport between different places in the vast
country.
RCD-Goma also carries out trade in vital aeronautical minerals with
Europe. Out of the proceeds, Ilunga said, his government was
repairing roads and providing water and electricity to the people.

Original_Search_Title: Infected Plants Threaten Research On Crop Diseases
Headline: East Africa: Infected Plants Threaten Research On Crop Diseases
Body_Text:
Kisumu — Infected cassava plants from Uganda are threatening
a three- year research into cassava diseases by the Kenya
Agricultural Research Institute KARI in western Kenya.
The infection of cassava by the cassava mosaic virus (CMV) and the
Cassava Green Mite (CGM) has created a desperate situation among
farmers who have been forced to criss-cross into Uganda to obtain the
seeds for planting.
Since the diseases were first reported in Kenya in 1993 about 83
percent of the crop has been wiped out. Disappointed farmers are now
going into sweet potato farming whose acreage has increased by 185
percent.
KARI researchers fear that the infected cassava crops obtained from
Uganda could make the new varieties to develop a more potent immunity
or create a new strain which may be difficult to eradicate.
According to KARI's cassava project co-ordinator, Hannigton Obiero,
the initiative started in 1997 after they introduced CMV and CGM
resistant varieties SS4 and Migyera from Uganda which was the first
country in East Africa to be infested by the diseases.
Farmers said that the disease has created a huge food deficit in the
area which cassava had enabled to escape the pangs of hunger.
"Cassava is the crop that does well here because it can withstand the
harsh climatic conditions in the region," says Jared Matsetekhe, a
resident of Busia district in Kenya's western province whose one-acre
farm was wiped out in 1997 by CMV.
Since 1995, cassava production in Busia has dwindled to 25,966 tonnes
from 96,800 tonnes in 1998.
"Cassava is the crop we have relied on for decades and its absence
has created a situation of hopelessness as people have been rendered
poorer. Because of this, we have been forced to rely on sweet
potatoes and maize which are considered as supplementary crops," says
Julius Oshikuku from Teso district.
Farmers frustrations are justified as cassava prices have soared. For
example a two-kilogramme tin of cassava flour from Uganda now costs
KShs 45 (about KShs 76 to the US dollar) while it used to fetch KShs
17 per kg before the disease broke out. The extinct traditional
varieties are Bumba, Kipanda meno and Serere.
KARI obtained the new resistant varieties from Uganda and tested on
one acre in 1997. Work started at Alupe KARI research centre near the
Kenya-Uganda border in collaboration with the Kenya Plant Health
Inspectorate Services KEPHIS.
Since then an elaborate propagation exercise has been proceeding at a
brisk pace where 17 districts are currently involved.
Obiero says that in 1998, some 32 acres were planted for
multiplication, but only 16.5 acres survived as drought affected the
effort. The following year, KARI planted 113 acres - 68 percent SS4
and 32 percent Migyera.
So far a host of multiplication centres have been established at
farmers training centres in Busia, Bungoma, Bukura, Siaya and KARI
centres at Kibos, Alupe and Kakamega. 850 acres have been planted so
far.
To counter possible mutation, the researchers have introduced 600
different cassava clones and selected fourteen high fielding ones to
be supplied to farmers. Obiero estimates that by the year 2001,
farmers will have at least five disease resistant varieties to choose
from since by last year 189 clones had been identified.
The researchers argue that the two varieties are high yielding with
SS4 producing 35 tonnes per hectare while Migyera yields 25 tonnes.
This compares unfavourably with the traditional varieties whose best
performance were 7 tonnes per hectare.
"We can predict that within six months, a single district alone will
have planted more than 500 acres for multiplication and in three
years every home will have adopted them," says Obiero.
Uganda's initial breakthrough in obtaining the new cassava plant was
spearheaded by the Kampala-based East Africa Root Crops Research
Network EARNET with technical support from the Nigerian based
International Institute of Tropical Agriculture.
ITA is co-ordinating agricultural research project in Kenya Uganda,
Tanzania and Madagascar. US organisations such as the Rockefeller
Foundation, Office of Foreign Development Assistance OFDA and the
British Gatsby Charitable Trust were instrumental in funding of these
research initiatives.
Farmers however complained that it was taking too long for the
propagation process to be complete describing it as a "circuitous"
way of reaching them.

Original_Search_Title: How Have The Resettled Farmers Performed?
Headline: Zimbabwe: How Have The Resettled Farmers Performed?
Body_Text:
Murewa — After decades of trying to eke out a subsistence living from a
tired and barren two-acre field in the communal areas of Murewa, Mairos Reza
has now settled on more fertile land but his yields are still low.
Reza is puzzled at this turnout of events because, he says, he has done
everything the agricultural extension officers told him. But, he adds after a
long sigh, that he has had to stretch the eight bags of fertiliser he bought to
cover his six acres under maize.
Dressed in tattered blue overalls, the peasant farmer points at a crop of
wilting and yellowing maize and says that the only thing that has changed for
him is that his 10 head of cattle is now assured of adequate grazing land.
Reza is one of about 40 land-hungry peasants resettled on Mount Pleasant
Farm, about 80 km northeast of Harare, by the government during the so-called
inception phase of farm reforms which followed a meeting between the government
and donors in Harare in 1998.
Traditional crops
Reza said this week he and his colleagues on the farm had each been given 12
acres of land on which most are growing maize and groundnuts. "We are happy
that we now have more land but most of us still have the same problems of
sourcing adequate inputs, which is why our yields will not be as high as we
would want them to be," he told the Financial Gazette during a tour of the
resettlement scheme.
"This is also the reason why we have stuck to the traditional crops we used
to grow on our old pieces of land."
Between Zimbabwe's independence in 1980 and 1988, the ruling ZANU PF party
embarked on the first phase of its land redistribution scheme that was marked
by slow implementation, partly because of opposition from the intended
beneficiaries who were being lumped together without any infrastructure. This
programme was abandoned when donor funding dried up. More than 3,5 million
hectares of land were acquired then on which 70 000 families were settled, but
a further five million hectares to settle 100 000 more families is still
needed, according to the government.
Thorny issue
Land ownership in Zimbabwe has been a thorny issue since British colonists
settled in then Rhodesia in the 1890s. Since independence, the government has
set out to reverse a situation where 70 percent of the country's rich arable
land is owned by whites, who constitute less than one percent of the
population.
Down the dusty and winding road, Reza's neighbour Maria Marecha and her
husband are not faring any better. Part of their five-acre maize crop however
looks healthier but Marecha says she expects to get about 90 bags of maize when
harvesting begins shortly. She says that this is more than the 40 bags she used
to harvest from their old one-acre piece of land, also near here.
"Before we were resettled, we used to scrounge around for money to buy
fertiliser and seed and we are still doing that today. Now it's even more
difficult because the land is bigger," she said.
"My husband and five adult sons worked hard to collect every piece of cow
dung we could lay our hands on and use it as compost to enrich the soil. We
could only afford to buy 10 bags of the fertilisers that was needed," Marecha
said she had also grown half an acre of cowpeas and one-and-a-half acres of
groundnuts. A small patch of land close to the family kitchen is taken up by
three metre-long rows of sweet potatoes.
Inadequate inputs
And several kilometres away from Mount Pleasant Farm, about 140 km northeast
of Harare in Mutoko, lies the Hoyuyu Resettlement Scheme where Mudzingwa
Madzimbamuto says although he still has problems of inadequate inputs, he now
has more land to parcel out to his sons.
"I have 12 acres which I have distributed equally between groundnuts, maize
and cotton. The cotton crop has not done very well because I was unable to buy
sufficient pesticides to control disease," he said. Madzimbamuto noted that
although his maize crop was showing signs of distress, he expected to get 200
bags of maize compared to last year's 140 bags.
Madzimbamuto, who moved from his old three-acre home in Murewa to be
resettled in Hoyuyu in 1983, said the government had promised them 40 more
acres. He said if they got more land they would work harder to get more money
for inputs. As we walk around his maize field, Madzimbamuto explains that he is
luckier than some of his neighbours because he has an ox-drawn plough and 20
cows which he uses to till the land.
Farm invasions
In 1998, donors committed themselves to help the government with mostly
infrastructural development and technical assistance provided the government
carried out a transparent and successful inception phase of the land reforms.
The head of the delegation of the European Commission in Harare, Asgar
Pilegaard, told the Financial Gazette this week: "As the European Commission,
we are fully behind a land reform for humanitarian, social and political
reasons and we are also convinced that if such a programme is put in place to
benefit the common man we can support it.
"If it's implemented within the bounds of the laws, we will continue to
fully support it. At the moment, we are concerned with the farm invasions by
the war veterans."
Pilegaard said the com-mision, the influential arm of the 15-nation European
Union (EU), would continue to engage the government in dialogue on the
principles agreed during the land conference.
Limited funds
Although he could not quantify in financial terms the infra-structural
assistance the EU had contributed to the inception phase, he said his group
would, through its micro project progra-mme, continue to assess the needs of
the resettled people and provide aid.
According to a report released in March by the Ministry of Rural Resources
and Water Development on financial requirements of the programme, beneficiaries
of the inception phase were forced to move onto the land before the completion
of basic infrastructure such as roads and boreholes because of limited funds.
German parliamentarians, on a recent six-day tour of Zimbabwe, have queried
the government's decision to incorporate into the present constitution a
provision that will empower the authorities to confiscate land without
compensation.
Roundly rejected
In a statement at the end of their two-week tour, the parliamentarians
questioned why the government was going ahead to lift a core provision of a
draft constitution that was roundly rejected by Zimbabweans last month.
"Germany is willing to support the land reform programme on the basis of the
consensus reached between Zimbabwe and donors at the September 1998 land
conference. With this in view, we urge the government to intensify its efforts
in implementing land reform in a transparent manner," the MPs said.
Germany is part of the EU. A spokeswoman for the British High Commission in
Harare said the British government had not given any assistance to the
inception phase programme because: "We are not yet convinced that the Zimbabwe
government led inception phase is being handled in ways that will meet the
principles agreed at the land conference.
"We continue to discuss our views on land reform with the government of
Zimbabwe and to look for ways in which we can make progress on this important
issue," she told the Financial Gazette.
She said they were extremely concerned about the occupation of farms and the
potential for violent confrontation. This, she added, would neither help land
reform nor investor confidence which was essential for economy recovery.
"We and other donors have urged the government to ensure that the rights of
farmers are respected and the rule of law upheld," said the spokeswoman.
The report by the Water Development Ministry said all levels of
infrastructural development during the inception phase of the reforms would
require about $408 million.
This now seems to hang in the balance as the government needs assistance
from the donors, who have already suspended their aid over failed economic
reforms and are insisting that the present land redistribution is not being
carried out transparently.

Original_Search_Title: Vitamin A winners were great
Headline: Zambia: Vitamin A winners were great
Body_Text:
Lusaka — When we started out on the second phase of the Better Health
campaign a month ago, our main aim was not only to try and reach as many
people as possible. It was also to get them to involve them in radio and
television programmes and indeed this column.
With messages in local languages aired everyday, in English four times a
week (Monday, Wednesday, Friday and Saturday) and on television twice a week
(Monday and Friday) we had reason to believe we were reaching a reasonable
number of our people. We also hoped that people were getting the messages
that we were relaying to them. Put simply, that by taking simple to follow
actions everyday, you could control up to 80 per cent of your health
problems.
Prevention is of course the key to all this. Prevention is far much
cheaper and comes with little pain compared to cure.
But you must get into the habit of practicing all the things that enable
you to prevent disease before it takes root. Where prevention has failed,
then you must make every effort to detect the disease early. This makes the
management and cure of the disease that much easier.
And finally when the disease has been detected, you must take the
treatment correctly and completely according to your health care providers
instructions. The first topic we tackled as promised was child health,
specifically the importance of vitamin A for children. We looked at why this
critical vitamin was important for our children, where and when you could get
vitamin A supplementation and which foods were rich in vitamin A.
Just to ensure that you took another look at the messages, we asked two
easy to answer questions at the end of each topic. We then sat back and held
our breath, hoping you would participate in the programme by writing to us
with your answers and comments.
Being the ambitious people that we are, we were hoping that perhaps we
might get 10 letters per topic, maybe 20 if all went well. So we were more
than a little surprised.
No, absolutely bowled over when the letters averaged some 250 per
programme. At the time of the draw, we had more than 700 letters from you.
Now if someone had suggested just 50 letters per topic per week at the
beginning, I have a feeling that most of us would have prescribed an urgent
meeting with the shrink. The response has been terrific and each and every
one of you, not just those who wrote to us, even though they do get our very
special "thank you", has been marvelous. Initial reports are that the turn-
out at clinics and other medical centres during the vitamin A campaign was
impressive. That makes every parent or guardian who took their child to
receive the vitamin A supplement a winner! Let us just remind you what the
main messages in the three topics were: * Vitamin A is important to children
because it ensures normal growth, development and protects against disease.
* Vitamin A supplements ensure that children get enough of this critical
chemical. Ensure that all children from birth up to six years-old receive
vitamin A supplements every six months.
* That most foods which contain vitamin A such as chibwabwa (pumpkin
leaves) and other dark green vegetables, yellow fruits such as mangoes and
pawpaws and oil sources such as groundnuts are affordable. Most of these can
be grown in our backyards and need not always be bought from other people.
And for remembering these messages and writing to us about them, you stand
the chance to win a fabulous Your Health Matters and To Your Health T-shirt
and qualified for the grand prize of ONE YEAR'S SUBSCRIPTION TO A GOVERNMENT
HOSPITAL NEAR YOU FOR A FAMILY OF SIX. And now, the winners are: QUIZ ONE:
Question One - Why is vitamin A important for our children? Answer - Vitamin
A is important for our children because it ensures they have good eyesight
and protects them from disease.
Name one serious condition which can be caused by lack of vitamin A? Night
blindness, poor growth and development and in extremecases, death. QUIZ TWO:
Question One - At what ages and how often should children receive vitamin A?
From birth to six years. Where can you get vitamin A supplements for
children? From any clinic. QUIZ THREE Name five foods that are rich in
vitamin A? Dark green leafy vegetables (chibwabwa, ibondwe et cetera) liver,
kidneys, kapenta, yellow sweet potatoes, fortified sugar, milk, mangoes and
eggs.
How can you tell that sugar is fortified with vitamin A? The packet is
clearly labelled with the vitamin A logo and the words "fortified with
vitamin A." T-Shirt winners 1. Situmbeko Nalishebo Lusaka 2.
Beatrice Mutale - Kalulushi 3. Maggie Manda - Kitwe 4.
Sam A. K.
Llungu - Ndola 5. Pardon Mmukulusa - Kitwe 6.
Richard Chellah- Lusaka. Grand prize winner - Subscription to a
Government hospital near you for a family of six: Sam A K. Lungu.
Hearty congratulations to all. Please write to us at the address below
clearly indicating whether you want us to post your prize or whether you can
collect it from our offices.
To Your Health is brought to you by the Central Board of Health. In
addition, please tune in to ZNBC's radio Two (Monday, Wednesday, Friday and
Saturday) and One (daily) and television's news hour.
If you want more information on this and any health topic, please write
to: The Producer To Your Health Central Board of Health P.O. Box 32588
Lusaka.

Original_Search_Title: IRC lauds Caritas in Guinea
Headline: Guinea: IRC lauds Caritas in Guinea
Body_Text:
Freetown — The international NGO, International Rescue Committee,
Guinea Mission has quite recently commended CARITAS Makeni "for the
fine work they have done on behalf of the refugee community".
In a statement issued during the official launching ceremony of
CARITAS-Makeni, Basic Skills Training Programmes for refugees, IRC
Delegate Jereme Egerton to Guinea said "there is no other organization
working right now in the camps of Forecariah that has a native
understanding of the problems faced by the refugees as does CARITAS-
Makeni".
Some 250 unaccompanied and separated children are receiving foster-
care, household hygiene packages, trauma healing, and peace education
in the three refugee camps of Kaliah, Farmoriah and Dakhagbe. An
additional 1444 disabled and vulnerable youths in these camps also
benefit from the newly introduced skills training programme.
"This new initiative will help to provide activity for those
children who have little to do and are in danger of becoming victims of
whatever kind, in the frequently tumultuous world of the refugee camp.
It is very important for children to have something to concentrate on,
to direct their youthful energy to a positive end. This is a step in
that direction," Delegate Egerton added.
On food security, CARITAS supports 500 farm families targeting
CARITAS foster parents, animators, and other vulnerable refugee groups
in the cultivation of sweet potatoes, rice and maize.
Talking to Concord Times, CARITAS Makeni head of mission in Guinea,
Mr. Joseph A. Turay said "our skills training, food security, peace
education and reconciliation are all measures geared towards preparing
refugees for a meaningful life when once they return home".
CARITAS - Makeni has been operating in refugee camps in Guinea since
February 1999. It receives funding from the Bishop of the Diocese of
Makeni George Biguzzi and from the British base donor agency CAFOD.
CARITAS is the only Sierra Leonean NGO working in camps in Guinea.
It collaborates with UNHCR, IRC and two Guinean NGOs - ADRIP and CECI.

Original_Search_Title: Refugees: What's life in the Guinea camps?
Headline: Sierra Leone: Refugees: What's life in the Guinea camps?
Body_Text:
Freetown — The hope to return home someday is almost always constant
as the Northern Star among refugees and perhaps even among exiles
wherever they may be. Not surprising therefore, when Sierra Leonean
refugees in Kaliah, Farmoriah, and Dakhagbe camps received the news of
Foday Sankoh's unannounced trip to South Africa, they perceived the
event as a spanner in the spooks of the peace process.
Frustration and despair eclipsed the faces of refugees out there in
Guinea.
But if returning home is like a pendulum that swings between
certainty (continuing to live as a refugee) and uncertainty (coming to
live in the Sierra Leone peace-fear) then it is worth resigning life to
the fate of being a perpetual refugee whose survival is basically on
UNHCR bulgur, oil, beans and flour to keep bone and flesh together.
Those who have spent a year or so at refugee camps know that to
depend solely on UNCHR ration is a question of choice. Mr. George L.
Massaquoi a refugee at Kaliah camp chose to register his family with
the agricultural division of the Catholic NGO CARITAS-Makeni operating
in Guinea. Others register with Guinea NGO -ADRIP.
In my trip to the camp I saw huge quantum of hectares of land with
sweet potatoes, maize, rice and cassava.
The scheme known, as food security is an assured means through which
refugees complement their food kit from UNHCR. On February 24 and 25
refuges stoutly participated in the UNHCR sponsored agricultural show
held at Farmoraih camp. WFP, CARITAS and ADRIP were in attendance.
Refugees too have educational programmes. And so the over 80,000
UNHCR registered refugees in Forecariah region are engaged in some
learning activities pioneered by NGOs operating in the camps. The
international rescue committee NGO runs both primary and secondary
level schooling in all the camps. It has just introduced a computer and
business studies centre in Forecariah town.
The unlettered refugees, they too acquire skills training for now
provided only by CARITAS-Makeni pictured here is an exibition of
CARITAS pottery project at Kaliah camp.
Refugee life is nothing very extra-ordinary. Football and volleyball
matches, video, cultural and disco shows are common undertakings at the
camps. In fact, the frequency of marriages is recorded- one every two
months per camp. The proliferation of different Church denominations
has become characteristic in the three camps of Kaliah, Forecariah and
Dakhagebe.
They say every man is a political animal of some sort.
Even the refugees of committees are not apolitical.
But Kaliah is most politicised. Well the last committee there was
booted out in March by UNHCR.
One time deposed camp Chairman, Mr. A. Jalloh (alias "we all nar
wan") has been re-appointed by UNHCR.
Reasons for his come back are inseparably tied to camp politics.
Notwithstanding camp politics, refugees in Guinea hold the view that
the game of politics should not be played on the Sierra Leone pitch at
least before complete disarmament. This is the paramount message given
to Pa Kabbah through the paramount chiefs during their visit to the
refugee camps in Guinea last week.
Perhaps it is a desperate appeal to the incumbent leadership of the
country not to risk the lives and limbs of Sierra Leoneans any more by
conducting elections in such a volatile country where armed combatants
still refuse to disarm but wait for elections to come. But if elections
do not come, some day, somehow the refugees will have to return home.
This is a hope that has to come to pass.

Original_Search_Title: The science of the queue
Headline: Africa: The science of the queue
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — You're standing in the queue at the post office waiting for a
stamp, or at the Department of Home Affairs because you've lost your ID book.
And you know how long you can wait! Or even at the Department of Justice, and
that's world record-breaking in waiting. There's a TV set above the queue
running ads, infomercials and music videos. It comes from Twilight Advertising
and Entertainment Channel.
It's a new concept in narrow casting. It harnesses the three main qualities
of an audience standing in a queue:
It's captive, which means the advertisers are assured of attentive
viewership,
It's bored, which means it will be satisfied with anything that is
entertaining, especially the ads, and
It tends to get agitated - the video content reduces the agitation which
suits the host of the venue.
In short, the concept and its execution is magic.
A queue audience standing on its legs is far more proactive than the audience
buried in the comfort of the sofa at home, says Twilight's MD Harm Fourie. They
are heavily receptive to marketing messages, and are in a position to act on the
messages as soon as they get out of the queue. You can contrast this to the
couch potato who can only react the day after, by which time the motivation has
wound down.
Four legs
The Twilight formula has four legs: franchising, technology, content and
marketing of advertising. All four make use of glaring gaps in the traditional
broadcast audiences.
Twilight has divided the country and the outlets into various regions defined
by the movement of people around their contracted outlets. These areas are sold
as franchises, and for a remarkably small investment, the franchisee simply has
to sell local advertising, and has to ensure that the tapes are played and the
equipment remains in good order.
The KISS principle
The technology is simple. VHS tapes are compiled in the studio at Twilight's
head office in Sandton. There is a separate master for each region as this
venture harnesses the regional advertising pool that the advertising agencies
and the Independent Broadcasting Authority say does not exist. That is why
regional TV is so long in coming. Fourie should be very grateful for these
perceptions. The tapes are then dispatched by courier, and the franchisee
ensures the tapes are played in VHS machines that are set to a loop function.
The technology is no more than a VHS player, a TV monitor and a set of
speakers - one monitor for each area of the queue. That explains why queues are
now made to snake around in straight lines: it's not to squeeze more people in,
it's to give them more efficient viewing comfort.
The content follows a format and pattern that has been found by experience to
optimally suit the queue audience. The fundamental rule is that the content has
to be as entertaining as possible, says Fourie. We recommend that advertisers
make amusing ads, play down the hard sell and play up the content. But it's not
wall-to-wall ads. They are interspersed with sports highlights, short comedy
sequences, sports funnies, cartoons, public service announcements and
infomercials.
Short and sweet
No one sequence of content runs longer than a minute. You're trying to
alleviate stress and boredom. There's only one way to do that: with
entertainment and variety.
The compilation of the tape is customised to each region. It is a mixture of
national and local ads, and some queues move faster than others. So the design
takes into account that the tape must be longer than the longest time a person
will spend in the queue.
Regardless of that, Fourie will not allow advertisers to buy more than one
spot every fifteen minutes. We can't afford to irritate viewers.
Marketing the advertising is highly sophisticated and extensive research has
determined the number of people who will make use of the queue over any given
period. Ad rates are decided on the simple and conventional permutations of
frequency and reach.
Regions and franchises
There are about 80 regions, and 66 have been sold off as franchises. The
minimum return on investment (ROI) for a franchisee is 35% for doing absolute
nothing except to guarantee the tapes roll, as we guarantee turnover due to the
national advertising, says Fourie. With hard work, an ROI of 1000% is very
achievable.
Twilight will be making big changes within the next three months. Part of the
UAM group, Twilight has access to the technologies of AirMedia which satellite
content to the Hyperama Checkout Channel. Delivering the content to the 80
regions by satellite is very attractive. Currently mastering, duplicating and
delivery by courier costs some R100 000 a month.
This can be drastically reduced by playing out directly to the satellite from
the editing system all the 80 different versions by means of a playlist. The
signal is delivered direct by satellite to servers in the outlets, and played
out through AirMedia's tried and tested store and forward technology.
Can the local advertisers afford to make their own ads? Sure. The local
franchisee takes brief, sends it to the professionals in the Twilight studio,
and at the high rate of volume at which they make ads, we can turn out an
effective ad for about R3000.
The Twilight formula has proven successful by the expansion from where it
started in South African post offices, to similar queues in Zimbabwe and
Namibia. It's not going to stop there, but Fourie is understandably mum about
his future plans.
However, Twilight has just moved to even grander offices and studios, so it's
not hot air.

Original_Search_Title: Traders Irked By Arrests
Headline: Uganda: Traders Irked By Arrests
Body_Text:
Kampala — Anger and frustration amongst traders against the Uganda Revenue
Authority (URA), eased slightly over the weekend following the release of
several colleagues held at Jinja Road Police Station.
This followed a stormy meeting between traders and top government officials
at the City Hall on Friday. The visibly annoyed traders, complained of their
goods being kept at various unofficial places like the Police Training School
and CID headquarters without receipts.
About 300 traders, who overflowed City Hall, also threatened to boycott the
import trade. The reason being the unexplained arrests of some members of the
Uganda Import and Export Traders Association (UGIETA) over allegations of
smuggling.
Manzi Tumubweine the Minister of State in the Finance ministry, told them
that the Ministry of Internal Affairs should check up on how people are
arrested as the law assumes they are innocent until proved guilty.
He also told them to tell Parliament to decrease taxes if they thought they
were too high.
"It is Parliament, which sets the tax levels," he said.
Tumubweine had been told by the irate traders that "home boys" allegedly
driving UC registered vehicles, were harassing them despite paying the relevant
taxes.
"These arrogant boys do not seem to care what is written on the papers.
They are so arrogant and do not appear to follow the law of Uganda," said
Godfrey Mukasa who had only lately left prison himself.
"I had paid taxes of sh45m, but immediately after leaving Customs, I was
arrested by a boy called Musinguzi, allegedly for not paying taxes! "I was put
in a cell with 70 others, about 25 of whom were also supposedly there for
smuggling offences and had not made statements. I was told I had been using
forged documents when those were computed and stamped by URA."
Mukasa said.
The traders ridiculed Government for being a 'circus', because it had set up
a another agency (Special Revenue Protection Services) to watch over URA's own
Revenue Protection Service (RPS).
The Commissioner General of URA Elly Rwakakooko told the traders they were
not there to blame other agencies.
"That may be done after the formation of a tripartite committee composed of
Representatives of the President's Office, Internal Affairs and Trade, which
will look into this matter.
"You have a right to complain, if you are harassed or humiliated by even
this other agency you mention. However you should be policing yourselves to
report the smugglers thereby removing move the distortion they cause in the
market, " he said.
One trader claimed that he was slapped by people from the SPRS in Iganga the
previous week, when it turned out that he had only sweet potatoes in his car
boot.
Tumubweine told the traders to approach the relevant authorities. "I expect
anybody with similar problems to tell us, otherwise we might not know.
Human beings behave differently. So now we think there was a mistake made,
how do we ensure that it does not happen again?" He however caused some
laughter when he told the traders never to give URA officials any money, but he
said that if they really have too, they should inform him confidentially
Kampala Mayor, Ssebaana Kizito also encouraged the traders to stand up for what
is right after hearing how they had been mistreated by some security people.

Original_Search_Title: Institute Develops Seed Bank
Headline: Africa: Institute Develops Seed Bank
Body_Text:
Niamey, Niger — The Niger-based Sahelian Center of
the International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid
Tropics (ICRISAT), has developed a bank of genes for thousands of
seed varieties.
State-owned weekly "Sahel Dimanche" reported that the
programme by the institute located at Sadore, some 30-km south of
Niamey, would help to stem the disappearance of many plants due
to lack of seeds.
The genes bank stores selected seeds for future use.
Bissala Yahaya, an ICRISAT official said Niger is the only
African country with such a facility, which boasts some 5,540
varieties of groundnuts and 1,500 varieties of millet.
The African seeds can be preserved for 10 to 15 years.
Countries like Congo Brazzaville, Burundi and Rwanda, which
have experienced conflicts and destruction of their seeds
are supplied by the seed Bank in Niger.
According to the Director of the Institute, Anand Kumar,
ICRISAT is a reference point in the field of agricultural
research in Sub-Saharan Africa, with emphasis on millet, sorghum
and chickpea.
The institute is also interested in the reactivation of
peanut production in Niger.
In its 19 years of existence, ICRISAT has produced many
varieties of millet and sorghum, with research activities carried
out in well-equipped laboratories before being tested by farmers.
A project on the introduction of the date palms was
initiated recently in six countries in the sub-region --
Niger, Chad, Mauritania, Senegal, Cameroon and Burkina.
ICRISAT works jointly with many other institutes and
Universities World-wide.
It has trained  more than 300 Niger nationals in
agricultural research, and contributes in the building of
capacities of national and sub-regional production and seed
conservation programmes.

Original_Search_Title: Needy Regions Get Preferential Food Sales in Niger
Headline: Niger: Needy Regions Get Preferential Food Sales in Niger
Body_Text:
Niamey, Niger — Niger started Saturday morning
selling foodstuffs to regions which suffered a chronic cereal
deficit in the last agricultural campaign.
Under the scheme, a total of 5,000 tonnes of food will be
sold at preferential prices in the most vulnerable regions of the
country, according to the operation launched by health minister,
Assane Adamou.
The operation is expected to cover about 800 villages
populated by some 500,000 people, covering the districts of
Diffa, Agadez and Tillabery, areas most seriously hit by cereal
deficits.
The foodstuffs on sale comprise millet, a staple in Niger,
sold at 8,000 CFA francs a bag, as compared to 13,000 per bag at
open market price.
According to Boukari Saidou, co-ordinator of the
government-run crisis unit, the operation is aimed at
strengthening national capacity to supply food to the regions hit
by cereal deficit.
Figures from the ministry of rural development show that
famine threatens about 39 percent of the Niger's 4 million
populations.
Already a total of 4,523 villages have been identified to
have food deficits ranging 10 to 20 percent.
The output from rain-fed agriculture and prospects for
irrigated crops led to a deficit forecast of 163,000 tonnes,
while overall needs in the country's eight regions, all hit by a
cereal deficit, are estimated at 2.48 million tonnes.
Total cereal production in Niger is estimated at 2.32
tonnes, of which 1.82 tonnes of millet, 407,787 tonnes of
sorghum, 60,386 tonnes of rice, 18,384 tonnes of maize and 10,750
tonnes of wheat.
The agricultural campaign also had negative repercussions
on the livestock sector, as there was not enough pastures for
cattle grazing.
To prevent famine, the government is planning for
alternatives such as encouraging irrigated crops over 54,864
hectares in 220,000 farms.
Expected production is 168,000 tonnes of cereal, valued at
1.6 million francs.
The government is also planning to set up cereal banks and
initiated labour-intensive projects.

Original_Search_Title: National Sorghum Breweries Reports Profit For The First Time In Years
Headline: South Africa: National Sorghum Breweries Reports Profit For The First Time In Years
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — National Sorghum Breweries (NSB) has registered a
positive cashflow, reporting a profit of R504m for the first time in
years.
It expects to cut its long-term liabilities, according to the
company's latest annual report.
The company, which reports its results for the 12 months to December
31 1999 a year late because of past reporting problems, says
long-term liabilities will be cut by another R17,5m during the
current year.
The group had reduced its long- and short-term liabilities to R94m by
December 31 last year from R171m in 1998. The resultant reduction of
finance charges was expected to be about R4m.
The company's largest shareholder, India's United Breweries, has
committed itself to investing an additional sum of up to $5m in NSB
by way of share capital as and when required.
However, NSB is making efforts to raise capital from empowerment
groups in SA before accepting the investment from United. The
directors are confident that its profit will increase.
The sorghum-beer business is commercially and financially viable.
Last year's performance was significantly better than the year
before. Operating profit rose R13,6m to R27,6m from a previous R14m.
The improved performance of the industry together with the profit
generated from the Traditional Breweries Investments transaction and
the intended share issues, should increase shareholder funds.
Meanwhile, NSB shareholders have approved a change to the name of the
company to United National Breweries (SA) Ltd.

Original_Search_Title: United Breweries Buys Into Cape Hotel
Headline: South Africa: United Breweries Buys Into Cape Hotel
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — United Breweries Resorts South Africa, a subsidiary of
India's UB Group that holds a 30% stake in National Sorghum
Breweries, has added a Cape Town hotel to its portfolio of tourist
attractions.
UB Resorts CEO Jaishankar Ramchandran said yesterday the group had
joined with a prominent hotel management company to launch The Cape
Milner previously known as The Mijlof Manor which will be valued at
several million rands once the current upgrading exercise is
complete.
The Cape Milner, scheduled to open in March next year, is 65% owned
by UB Resorts. An empowerment partner, Sechaba, has acquired a 10%
stake with an option to buy the balance of 25% currently being held
by the Industrial Development Corporation.
Ramchandran said UB Resorts had formed a strategic alliance with the
Three Cities group, which has a portfolio of 21 tourist products. He
said UB's vision was to become a "sizeable multiproduct player" in
the tourism industry.

Original_Search_Title: Ngwenya: Medical Genius Or Charlatan?
Headline: Zimbabwe: Ngwenya: Medical Genius Or Charlatan?
Body_Text:
Harare — Forty-Year-Old marketing executive Sue Gorett swears by his
name. Were it not for Dr Richard Ngwenya, she said this week, her
life would still be miserable and characterised by bouts of
excruciating pain from an inflamed ulcer and severe stress.
Gorett says three months of Ngwenya's unconventional treatment of
wheatbran porridge, vegetables and deep stomach cleansing have done
the trick. The treatment has even improved her eyesight.
Thirty-nine-year-old Zimbabwe National Army soldier Muntu Josamu -
this is not his real name - has been confined to a wheel chair by
what started as a mild bout of athletes' foot.
Josamu says the small sores on his feet could not respond to
treatment and gradually multiplied until they spread to the groin.
His feet started swelling and developed scabs that emitted a watery
fluid.
Soon his head became swollen and he started experiencing difficulties
even to go to the toilet, said Josamu this week at Ngwenya's surgery
in the leafy Avenues area of Harare.
Doctors at Murewa Hospital told him he was suffering from Karposi
Sarcoma, a debilitating form of cancer that is said to attack mostly
people with AIDS. He was referred to Parirenyatwa Hospital for
radiotherapy.
It was while he was at Parirenyatwa that some people told him to try
Ngwenya's treatment.
"I have been under Dr Ngwenya's treatment and you can see my head is
now almost back to its normal size and the scars on my legs are
starting to heal," Josamu told the Financial Gazette.
Josamu now only feeds on traditional Zimbabwean foods such as sadza
prepared from sorghum, okra, roundnuts and pumpkin leaves. He says
his condition has greatly improved since taking Ngwenya's medication.
So is Protacius Richard Ngwenya a medical genius or a charlatan
making money out of the misery of Zimbabweans desperate to find a
cure for AIDS and other opportunistic illnesses? Hundreds of
Zimbabweans, both black and white, are flocking to Ngwenya's
controversial James Mobb Immune Enhancement Centre in the suburbs and
vouch that the retired former high-ranking Zimbabwe National Army
soldier is their saviour.
Ngwenya claims that most of the chronic diseases affecting
Zimbabweans today, including HIV/AIDS, diabetes, arthritis, cancers,
constipation, hepatitis, stress, meningitis and pneumonia are caused
by fungal infections and that people are consuming too much yeast and
sugars.
He says many people have been mistakenly diagnosed as positive of the
virus that causes AIDS and have died because even the new anti-
retroviral drugs will not help.
Anti-retrovirals such as Combivir are now being celebrated as the new
generation of drugs that can prolong the lives of HIV-positive people
but their costs have been found to be too prohibitive for patients in
developing countries.
"The medical community was looking for the so-called HIV and started
to blame other viruses which unfortunately were not there, saying
there was now Tuberculosis 1 and Tuberculosis 2. I am not seeing
HIV," Ngwenya said this week from his James Chinamano Avenue surgery.
Ngwenya, who admits that he has clashed with most of the local
medical fraternity and Health Minister Timothy Stamps over his views
on AIDS, this week trashed the HIV virus as a "hoax virus".
He blamed the emergence of many different diseases in Zimbabwe after
1980 to poverty and the biological warfare unleashed by retreating
Rhodesian forces at the height of the independence war which he said
had created the fungi.
Ngwenya, a member of the late Joshua Nkomo's ZIPRA in Zambia, claimed
that he was trained by ZAPU during the war to find ways to counter
the Rhodesians' biological weapons.
Some local AIDS experts this week however called Ngwenya a fake out
to make money from AIDS sufferers.
"We are talking of someone who just wants to make money from HIV,"
said Professor Norman Nyazema, director of the University of Zimbabwe
medical school's continuing health education department.
Nyazema reminded Zimbabweans that Ngwe-nya surfaced about three years
ago with claims that were supported by then Deputy Health Minister
Tsungirirai Hungwe that he had discovered a drug called Mocrea that
could treat AIDS.
"If three years ago this man was saying he was treating AIDS and now
he says there is no AIDS, then what was he treating?" Nyazema asked.
Nyazema said the fungal infections that Ngwenya talked about were the
same that caused opportunistic infections such as skin cancers but
that these infections had worsened among Zimbabweans because of the
AIDS pandemic.

Original_Search_Title: Research Institute Develops Small Scale Roller Milling Machine
