Headline: Nigeria: Research Institute Develops Small Scale Roller Milling Machine
Body_Text:
Lagos — In a bid to make it possible for the small scale indigenous
millers to use roller mills to produce different grains such as
cowpea, maize, sorghum, rice and wheat, among others, the Federal
Institute of Industrial Research, Oshodi (FIIRO)designed and
fabricated a low-cost locally produced equipment known as general
purpose small scale roller machine.
A300kg per hour all-purpose grains milling plants, the milling,
sifting and recycling units of the plant functioned satisfactorily
under preliminary tests.
According to FIIRO sources, the three units have been assembled for
overall performance testing and characterisation. The special feature
of the design is in using only one prime mover for four rolls.
The first three rolls are placed approximately on the vertices of an
equilateral triangle, while the fourth roll is placed such that on
adjustment, it makes contact with the third roll.
The plant, therefore, can produce various grades of flour by simple
adjustment of the rollers. A petrol engine can be substituted for an
electric motor, thereby making it possible to site and use the roller
milling machine in rural and urban areas.
Hitherto, grains milling machines commonly used in Nigeria,
especially by small -scale operators, were restricted to disc mill
and hammer mill types. These mills pulverise the grain before it is
sifted into flour this result in low yield of flour fraction.
How ever the Roller mill is better, but its high technology
discourages indigenous small millers from using it, thereby making
its use the monopoly of large-scale industries.

Original_Search_Title: News Briefs
Headline: Southern Africa: SOUTHERN AFRICA: IRIN News Briefs
Body_Text:
ZAMBIA-DRC: Zambia asks Zimbabwe to repatriate fuugitive soldiers
Zambia has asked Zimbabwe to repatriate its soldiers who reportedly
crossed into its territory from the Democratic Republic of Congo
(DRC) following the fall of the southeastern town of Pweto to rebel
forces, agencies reported on Friday. Zimbabwean troops are supporting
President Laurent-Desire Kabila against rebels backed by Rwanda and
Uganda. The number of Congolese, Zimbabwean and Hutu militia that
crossed into Zambia is unclear, some agencies put the figure as high
as 10,000.
Zambia's foreign minister Keli Walubita confirmed to journalists on
Thursday that President Robert Mugabe's government had been asked to
repatriate its troops. The Rally for Congolese Democracy announced
that it had captured Pweto two days ago. According to Walubita,
Zambia lacks the capacity to handle such a huge influx of soldiers.
Consequently, Lusaka has appealed to the United Nations, and the
International Committee of the Red Cross for assistance.
Walubita said that Zambian troops had managed to disarm the Congolese
and Zimbabwean soldiers who have congregated in the Zambian border
town of Chiengi. They have also reportedly been separated from the
thousands of civilian refugees who have also fled the fighting.
Thousands more soldiers may have crossed the border but remain camped
in the bush. Walubita said that arrangements were being made to
repatriate the Congolese soldiers to the DRC.
ZIMBABWE-DRC: MiG jet shot down in DRC
Rwanda shot down a Zimbabwean MiG jet-fighter during fighting for
control of Pweto town in Katanga Province in the Democratic Republic
of the Congo (DRC), Rwandan state radio reported on Thursday. But
Zimbabwean military officials, while confirming the attack on Pweto
by Congolese rebel backed by Rwanda, denied their jet was shot down.
Vast quantities of equipment, including several tanks, armoured
personnel carriers, machine-guns, howitzers and ammunition were
reportedly captured after the town fell to the rebels last Sunday.
On Wednesday, General Vitalis Zvinavashe, commander of the Zimbabwe
Defence Forces, confirmed the attack on Pweto. "Yes, Pweto was
attacked but I do not have details about the results of the attack."
Colonel Chancellor Diye, the army spokesman, denied the incident.
"That is not correct," he said. "Nothing of that sort happened." The
capture of Pweto on Lake Mweru near the Zambian border, is considered
one of the most significant blows to a fragile peace deal signed in
Lusaka last year.
SOUTH AFRICA: Albright kicks-off off three nation African tour
Secretary of State Madeleine Albright on Friday praised the role
South African women have played in transforming the country since
apartheid ended, but she said more work needed to be done to empower
women, the Associated Press (AP) reported. Albright arrived in South
Africa on Thursday, the beginning of a visit to three African
countries meant to highlight some of the democracies and economies
flourishing on the continent.

"Like so many other parts of the world, women remain
disproportionately poor, undereducated and underemployed, while
suffering too much hardship, too much violence and now the agony of
HIV/AIDS," she told a breakfast meeting of South African female
business and political leaders. Later on Friday, Albright planned to
meet with South African President Thabo Mbeki in Pretoria. They were
expected to discuss the AIDS pandemic ravaging Africa and what can be
done to combat it.

Mbeki has suffered withering international condemnation for
entertaining the views of fringe theorists who doubt the link between
HIV and AIDS and, in some cases, doubt the existence of the disease
at all. South Africa, with an estimated 4.2 million HIV-positive
people, has the highest population of infected people in the world.
ANGOLA: Angola's revenue from diamond production grows
Angola's income from diamond production has increased by 50 percent
this year as compared with that of last year, the official newspaper
'Jornal de Angola' reported on Thursday. Angola's Vice Minister of
Geology and Mines Antonio Carlos Sumbula was quoted as saying that
the Angolan government hopes in this year to make over US $600
million from the production of diamonds.

The record revenue is primarily because the Angolan Armed Forces
(FAA) have guaranteed security in the diamond extracting zones and
recovered almost all the regions formerly held by UNITA rebels,
Sumbula said. He added that in 1999 diamond production in the zones
controlled by the government was less than US$ 200 million.

ANGOLA: More than 300,000 people benefit from ICRC aid
The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) said in a press
release on Friday that it had distributed seeds to more than 71,000
displaced and resident families living in Angola's central highlands.
In total, this year about 300,000 people received maize, bean,
sorghum and groundnut seed, along with fertilizer. They were also
given enough food to ensure that the seed would not be consumed.
It was a major operation, which had to be completed before the rainy
season, the press releases said. The ICRC chartered B-727 aircraft
which made up to eight flights per day to bring food, seed and
farming tools to people in and around the central highland cities of
Huambo and Kuito. The seed was immediately planted by the families,
who should be able to harvest their crops between April and July
2001.
Since economic self-sufficiency has now been restored to most
villagers living around Kuito and Huambo, the ICRC plans significant
cuts in its emergency food aid next year. However, it will increase
its support for agricultural production, the release said.
MOZAMBIQUE: Capital hit by new cholera upsurge
A new outbreak of cholera has killed at least two people in Maputo,
capital of Mozambique, with 182 cases admitted to hospital, the daily
newspaper 'Noticias' reported on Thursday. The new outbreak was
caused by a failure to observe basic hygiene principles and a
shortage of clean drinking water and proper sanitation, Maputo city
health director Olivia Ferreira was quoted as saying.
According to Ferreira, about 80 patients a day are being admitted to
the cholera wards of Mavalane General Hospital. "This is a situation
that worries us, because the number of cases has been increasing
alarmingly," she said. She said there is a plan to increase the
number of beds in the Mavalane wards from 100 to 120 to respond to
the growing demand. In another development, the health authorities in
the northern province of Nampula decided recently to ban the sale of
fish caught in Lake Niassa, for fear that it may be contaminated with
cholera, following an outbreak of the disease in Niassa Province.
MOZAMBIQUE: Police arrested for prison deaths
Eleven policemen have been arrested in connection with the deaths of
82 inmates in a prison in northern Mozambique, state radio reported
on Friday. Most of the dead in the prison in the town of Montepuez
were supporters of the opposition RENAMO party rounded up by police
after riots last month in which over 40 people had died. Radio
Mozambique said the policemen had been taken to the coastal city of
Pemba, the capital of northeastern Cabo Delgado province, where the
clashes occurred.
The report did not say what charges the group faced. A preliminary
report by government doctors concluded that the inmates probably died
of asphyxiation after being squeezed into a single prison cell. Radio
Mozambique reported last Saturday that two senior police officials in
Cabo Delgado had been suspended and a government commission would
investigate the incident which took placed on 21 November.
RENAMO leader Afonso Dhlakama has blamed President Joaquim Chissano's
ruling FRELIMO party for orchestrating the deaths, but Frelimo has
denied the allegation. Dhlakama's party has refused to recognise
Chissano's government following last December's elections which they
accuse Frelimo of rigging.
NAMIBIA: Meningitis Still Spreading
A meningitis outbreak in northern Namibia has continued to claim
victims, even though vaccines have finally arrived after stocks were
depleted within two days last month, the 'Namibian' reported on
Friday. The number of cases detected has risen to 65 in Ohangwena,
Oshana and Omusati and Oshikoto. Nine people are known to have died
of the disease in northern and northeastern Namibia since October.
Top health official in the North, Dr Naftali Hamata, said from his
office at Oshakati on Friday that 17,000 meningitis vaccine doses
have arrived but that syringes and needles have not yet been
delivered from the capital Windhoek.
ZIMBABWE: Workers at Zimbabwe's state-run papers go on strike
Employees at Zimbabwe's state-run newspapers in Harare went on strike
on Thursday over non-payment of their annual bonuses, the Associated
Press (AP) reported. Workers and management were still talking, but
workers said they would not return to their jobs until they received
a written promise that their full bonuses would be paid.

"The workers have since January been foregoing their overtime and
allowances in order to save the company from collapsing, yet
management has done nothing at all," the workers said in a letter,
according to the official ZIANA news agency. The strike affects 'The
Herald' and 'The Sunday Mail', both based in Harare. Workers at 'The
Chronicle', based in Zimbabwe's second city of Bulawayo, went on
strike last Friday. 'The Herald' has faced stiff competition for
circulation and public opinion since an independent daily newspaper,
the 'Daily News', began printing last year.
ZIMBABWE: Succession issue suppressed at ZANU-PF congress
ZANU-PF secretary for administration Didymus Mutasa and senior party
functionaries are manipulating the ruling party's forthcoming
congress agenda to exclude "the succession issue", the 'Zimbabwe
Independent' reported on Thursday. The move is designed to protect
President Robert Mugabe from internal challenges, party sources told
the newspaper.
Mutasa and the congress secretariat were suppressing the succession
issue which was stirring within the ranks of the party.
Pronouncements by senior officials suggest a determination to
suffocate the issue, insiders said. The newspaper reported that party
luminaries were working flat out to ensure that Mugabe emerges
without challenge after the December 13-16 gathering.

In an interview on Thursday, Mutasa insisted the succession issue
would not be discussed "whatever the views of other party members".
He said the agenda had already been formulated and there was no room
for the issue to be included. "Let me say it again that the
succession issue will not be discussed," Mutasa said.

Original_Search_Title: Cereal Production Is 17.5% Down
Headline: Mali Records 170,000 Ton Cereal Deficit
Body_Text:
Bamako, Mali — Mali had an estimated
2,386,296 tons of various cereals by 30 September,
according to a survey commissioned by the departments
of statistics and agriculture.
The survey revealed a deficit of 17.5 percent in
production compared with the previous crop season when
2,893,000 tons of cereals were harvested.
It found that while an increase of 2.5 percent was
registered in rice and 35.4 percent of wheat/barley
production. There was a 2-percent shortfall for millet,
14.4 percent for sorghum, 64.1 percent for maize and 55.3
percent for fonio.
The survey established that there was a drop in
production in all the Malian regions, except Kayes in
the west, Sikasso, south, and Segou in the east.
The two departments' provisional assessment was that
the country faces a gross deficit of 172,470 tons in
cereals in 2000.
If imports and exports are taken into account, the
net global deficit is around 147,470 tons, including
142,370 tons of dry cereals and 5,360 tons of rice and a
wheat and barley surplus of 260 tons.
However, Mali's production is comparatively better
that in other countries in the Inter-State Committee for
Drought control in the Sahel, whose summit ended in
Bamako over the weekend.
A food situation report presented at that summit by
the Network for the prevention of food crisis in the
Sahel, member countries are expected to experience a
cereal deficit of 930,230 tons during 2000-2001 season.
The report showed that Burkina Faso, Cap Verde,
Niger and Chad will be most affected by the cereal
deficit.
During their Bamako summit, the member states --
Burkina Faso, Cape Verde, Gambia, Guinea Bissau, Mali,
Mauritania, Niger, Senegal and Chad -- made an urgent appeal
for food assistance from external partners.

Original_Search_Title: Poverty Link in Lethal Brew Deaths
Headline: Kenya: KENYA: Poverty Link in Lethal Brew Deaths
Body_Text:
Nairobi — In a remarkable about-turn, Kenyan
political leaders and a clergyman are now calling on the government
to take a lead from its East African neighbours and legalise the
production and distribution of traditional brews as part of its
poverty eradication policy.
Their call comes in the wake of a highly publicised incident in
Nairobi's sprawling Mukuru slums last week in which an adulterated
concoction left 140 people dead, tens blinded and more than 500
hospitalised with complications.
Parliament on Wednesday said the lives of poor Kenyans could be saved
and alcoholism reduced if traditional brews were legalised, as is the
case in Uganda and Tanzania. Most African countries have legalised
traditional brews such as chang'aa.
Bishop Otieno Wasonga of the Anglican Church of Kenya argued that two
traditional brews, busaa and chang'aa, were the only ones affordable
by poor Kenyans as well as a source of income for some of them.
Noting that the church does not condone alcoholism, the Bishop
however argued that if the government believed that alcohol was good
for the people, then it should not be selective but should legalise
these traditional brews.
The legislature concurred with Bishop Wasonga, saying that if the
drinks were legalised, hygienic standards could be assured, while the
government could at the same time earn revenue.
Teetotaller President Daniel arap Moi, however, disagreed. Speaking
during a visit to the city's extensive Korogocho slums where chang'aa
brewing is commonplace, he reiterated that "factories manufacturing
illicit brews will be closed down" to rid the country of "doomsday"
drinks. He blamed the brew deaths on opposition MPs and the clergy
who campaigned for the repeal of the notorious 'Chiefs Act', which
empowered local chiefs to raid chang'aa and busaa distilleries and
drinking houses.
The victims of adulterated alcohol are almost always the poor because
there is no cheap alternative beer on the market that compares with
"kumi kumi" (literally 10-10 in Swahili), which is sold at 10 Kenya
shillings (roughly $US 0.12) a glass. Typically, the victims in last
week's incident comprised the poor, single parents, the unemployed
and the under-employed whose pastime is drinking chang'aa, a potent
brew made from fermented maize or sorghum flour which is distilled.
The killer brew was laced with ingredients such as methanol and
formaldehyde, an embalming fluid. For most of these people, chang'aa
is the only affordable drink.
The basic national minimum salary of Kshs.5,000 a month is a drop in
the ocean in so far as goods and services are concerned. At most,
such an amount lasts two weeks for a family of two.
"We are in the business, not because we like it," one civil servant
said. "We have to supplement our lean pay packets." Luckily this
dealer did not get his supplies from the Mukuru "distiller".
The legislators argued much in the same vein, saying that the
legislation against traditional brews in 1998, ostensibly to rid
society of drunks and to save lives, was a futile exercise because
"our people are poor and have to eke a decent or indecent living at
whatever cost".
At that time, Parliament blamed several cheap locally distilled
liquors following the death of 80 people believed to have drunk an
illicit brew laced with methylated spirit. The following year, 19
people were killed after taking an adulterated brew. The alcohol
content of some of the brews has been found to be as high as 90
percent. There are more chang'aa dens in the country's poor
neighbourhoods than officially acknowledged.
Even the cheaper brews by Kenya Breweries Ltd and other large
distillers, which retail at about Kshs.36 a bottle (about US $0.45)
are still way beyond the means of the vast majority of Kenyans. The
country's per capita income is estimated at US $280, with about half
the population living in absolute poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Ministry Warns Of Grain Deficit
Headline: Botswana Faces Grain Deficit
Body_Text:
Gaborone, Botswana — Botswana faces a grain deficit of 207,
278 metric tonnes after its harvest was affected by pests and excessive
rains during the growing period, the latest food and security bulletin
from the agriculture ministry says.
It adds that the country intends to meet the shortfall through
imports.
Apart from this, the state-owned Botswana Agricultural Marketing
Board (BAMB) has been denied supplies by local producers and will
therefore have to import grain to boost its stocks.
The marketing board miscalculated and slightly reduced its retail
price of cereals in anticipation of a bumper harvest.
But since the country has suffered a grain deficit this has
aggravated the long-time problem of farmers withholding their produce
instead of selling to the marketing board.
The board has therefore been forced to source some of its grain
supplies from outside the country.
So far Botswana has received 51,380 tonnes or 34 percent of the
total planned maize imports of 145,000 tonnes.
The bulletin notes that because the received imports in the second
half of the year is less than 50 percent of the required figure, this
means that the country is consuming less maize than predicted.
At the end of September 2000, maize stocks in Botswana stood at
27,967 tonnes, while sorghum stocks were 11,874 tonnes.
Despite the fact that national cost of living rose by 0.3 percent
during the period under review, the prices of cereals have remained
stable in Botswana.
At the same time the miscalculation by the marketing board in
reducing its retain prices did not affect the overall price of cereals.
Meanwhile, the bulletin predicts that the country expects normal
and above normal rainfall, and urges farmers to take advantage and plant
early.
Though the heavy rains and the resultant floods early this year
proved quite disastrous to crops, it has been a boon to livestock.
The report says that though surface water had dried up, underground
water sources were providing enough water for livestock.
However, the availability of many water sources affected the
government's efforts at disease control because it was not easy to round-
up livestock at the usual watering places, the report points out.
As a result, the turnout in the government's drive to control
outbreaks of Nagana and lumpy skin disease was low, it says, but adds
that the animal diseases have been put under control through vaccination.
But a major source of worry has been the frequent outbreak of veld
fires, some of which are caused by cattle owners so that green cover can
sprout up.

Original_Search_Title: Women Step Up War Against Illegal Brews
Headline: Kenya: Women Step Up War Against Illegal Brews
Body_Text:
Nairobi, Kenya — Still grieving the loss of over 140
people who died after consuming a contaminated illegal
brew, a group of Kenyan women has launched a strong crusade
against the social menace.
Leaders of various women's organisations Tuesday mobilised
their lot in Kangemi, a low-income estate on the western
outskirts of Nairobi, and flushed out those suspected to be
behind the sale of the deadly "chang'aa" and "busaa" drinks.
Chang'aa is unrefined gin, while busaa is brewed from
millet or sorghum.
The 300 protesters, all members of the Kangemi Women's
Group, destroyed the shebeens and impounded the brewing and
distilling equipment, which they ferried to the local provincial
administration office.
At one of the busaa dens, the women were shocked to find
dead rats and women's underpants and sanitary cotton wool in
busaa and raw chang'aa containers.
During the march, the women arrested a man with 10 litres
of chang'aa and handed him over to police.
Efforts by the provincial administration officials to calm
the women and plead with them to let police do their work, were
fruitless.
The group's chairperson, Grace Njoroge, said they had
been forced to take this action because their husbands had been
"married" to the illicit drinks and abandoned their homes.
"Most of them (men) have been made impotent by the drinks
and cannot perform their night duties," she complained.
The move follows an incident at the Nairobi slum
residential area of Mukuru, on the southern suburbs of the
capital, in which over 140 consumers of the illegal drink have
died and 400 others hospitalised in the past 10 days.
Scores of other drinkers have gone blind.
The spirit, dubbed "kumu kumi," or "ten ten" on account of
its price of 10 Kenyan Shillings (about 12 US cents) per measure,
is suspected to have been laced with methanol, a highly poisonous
chemical.
To enhance its potency, the dealers usually mix the gin
with ethanol, which is less toxic. But, out of greed and lack of
concern for human life, some dealers occasionally lace the drink
with small doses of methanol.
Occasionally the plan goes awry, resulting in the death of
the consumers who cannot afford the legal drinks because of
endemic poverty.

Original_Search_Title: 4 Million People At Risk of Famine in Niger
Headline: Niger: 4 Million People At Risk of Famine in Niger
Body_Text:
Niamey, Niger — Some four million people in Niger,
with a total population of 10 million, are threatened by famine
this year due to poor harvests, Rural Development Minister
Wassalke Boukari, has revealed.
The Minister told a meeting of agriculture experts in
Niamey Tuesday that while Niger's total foodcrop needs are
estimated at 2,480,000 tonnes, all the eight regions of the
Sahelian country have recorded harvest deficit this year.
The total foodcrop productions are estimated at 2,319,206
tonnes, comprising millet, 1,821,899 tonnes, sorghum, 407,787
tonnes, rice, 60,386 tonnes, corn, 18,384 tonnes and wheat,
10,750 tonnes.
Boukari said the harvest projections take into account
yields from irrigated farming and the production prospects in the
hydro-agricultural sub-sector.
On livestock, he said production has also been poor all
over the country, with a further deficit of 1,800,000 tonnes of
dry feed.
To combat the famine, the Minister said the government is
planning alternative measures such as expanding irrigated farming
over 54,864 hectares for 220,000 farmers.
This is expected to yield additional 168,000 tonnes of
foodcrop at an estimated cost of more than one billion CFA francs
(750 CFA francs=1USD).
The government also plans to create cereal banks, set up
security stocks and implement manpower-building projects in the
country.
Tuesday's meeting on the mechanism for the prevention of
food crisis in Niger examined the poor results of the 2000
farming season with a view to finding solutions to food deficit
of 163,000 tonnes recorded this year.
The meeting of specialists from various departments of the
agriculture sector, non-governmental organisations and donors
operating in rural areas, focused on the results of the farming
campaign and proposals to avoid the looming food crisis in the
country.

Original_Search_Title: Saving Kenyan Addicts From Killer Drinks
Headline: Kenya: Saving Kenyan Addicts From Killer Drinks
Body_Text:
Nairobi, Kenya — A Kenyan Police report Saturday of the arrest of a man identified as
Samwel Njoroge Karanja, suspected to be the owner of a chemical industry in
the Kariobangi slum, has rekindled national curiosity over a killer local
brew.
Police said the factory is suspected to be manufacturing the infamous
kumi-kumi (ten-ten) illicit brew that has claimed over 120 lives and blinded
more than 100 persons over the last two years.
More than 400 of the victims are still in hospital, and their ages range
between 17 and 60 years.
The death toll is expected to rise, as the methanol-laced drink is
believed to be widely distributed in the city and its environs.
Authorities say 29 people, including 12 women, are under arrest.
Methanol is a chemical compound which when consumed can cause liver and
kidney problems and blindness.
Officials of the health ministry say methyl alcohol and ethanol are
highly poisonous to humans.
The recent case has claimed victims in the wider outskirts of Nairobi,
Kiambu, Kajiado, Thika, Dandora, Kiserian and Uthiru.
Chief government pathologist, Kirasi Odumbe told PANA the victims of the
demon drink died of "severe liver cirrhosis, a condition that is caused by
excessive abuse of alcohol."
Despite the outrage expressed by women, who bear the brunt of a drunk
society, and the heavy death toll witnessed in the recent past, Kenyans
continue to drink the potent brews with much abandon.
By Saturday, as a way of relaxing over the weekend after a hectic week
at the industrial plants, villagers were still patronising the Mukuru slums
drinking dens, despite a government warning to city residents against
venturing in such spots.
The daredevil drinkers told PANA the calamity could be treated as an
accident because they had been drinking the brew for many years.
"This is a calamity just like the August 7, 1998 terrorist bombing of
the US embassy in Nairobi, a road or plane accident," John Kinyanjui, 31,
said as he sipped a glass of Chang'aa gin.
According to University of Nairobi sociology lecturer, Odegi Awuondo,
Kenyans are consuming cheaply available illegal brews in order to sink their
frustrations.
While Kenyans want to get drunk and forget about their problems, a
majority of them cannot afford the ordinary beer, and thus resort to the
cheap local and unlicensed brews, he said.
"Since I was idle I decided to try out the brew, which is normally sold
in the open," 17-year old John Kabiru said in agreement with Awuondo's
explanation.
The cheap brews are prevalent in slum areas because of widespread
poverty.
Although they are poor, says Awuondo, they also want to get drunk like
other slightly well off Kenyans.
He says brewing and consumption of local drinks can be eradicated if the
law enforcement officers take up the matter seriously and apprehend
violators.
However, a group of legislators tend to disagree. National Development
party (NDP) MP, Otieno Kajwang, says the poor also have a right to drink
alcohol, especially in an environment of economic gloom, unemployment and
stress.
Kajwang has called on the government to legalise the traditional brews,
arguing that neighbouring Uganda and Tanzania produce local brews Waragi and
Konyagi under legal tender.
He blames the government for the tragedy, saying if the brews were
legalised, nobody could drink or sell an unlicensed and unlabelled
drink.
Another MP, Norman Nyagah, says illicit brews have been abused because
of the restrictions the government has put on it.
But while some villagers are still taking the brews, there are those who
have promised never to touch it after a close shave with death.
Many recall the August 1998 episode involving one John Mwangi and other
victims.
"Hata mkizima taa, tutaendelea kunyua tuu, (We will continue drinking
even though you want us out by putting off the lights)," Mwangi, 41, was
reported as having vowed.
The sun was still up and nobody had switched off the lights at the
Changa'aa (local gin) drinking den patronised by Mwangi and his fellow
drunks, who apparently did not know they had gone blind until two days later
when they became sober and found themselves in hospital.
Despite losing his sight, Mwangi was at least lucky to be alive.
Some 85 people died in that August 1998 Mai Mahiu drinking tragedy that
also left 40 others totally sightless and 21 partially blind.
Also in September 1999, another set of 17 people died after consuming an
illicit brew in Embu, about 65 miles east of Nairobi.
The victims, police later said, had consumed Sorghum, one of the
recently outlawed beers, at a bar and restaurant in the sprawling Dallas
slums in the city.
In fruitless campaign that year, more than 200 women had marched to the
police station in Nairobi's Kangemi slum village claiming that their men had
become impotent as a result of drinking poisonous beer.
There was also an April drama this year when scores of women from
Kangema village of Kandara division, about 50 miles north of Nairobi,
outraged the public when they walked to a police station demanding sex from
police officers whom they accused of failing to deter their "perpetually
absent men" from taking the brews.
Yet, not even death, ill-health or the women's protest has deterred the
local gin addicts.

Original_Search_Title: Women Parade Their Basketry Skills
Headline: Botswana: Women Parade Their Basketry Skills
Body_Text:
Gaborone — In one of his articles, Tom Ketlogetswe, Motswana visual
artist and art educator, highlighted the need for Botswana art
students to preserve or at least make an effort to promote the
country's indigenous arts. Is this happening? Not much of it is
taking place at school or college level in the country but there is
one commercial enterprise that has done a lot to promote Botswana's
culture and to a certain extent Africa's original visual arts and
crafts.
Botswanacraft is one such enterprise. The Botswanacraft showroom and
gallery premises are host to a recently opened basketry show
featuring the work of established weavers Mokhwahepo Muronga,
Thimporeni Muronga, Mahurero Twapika, Kapangi Mpunda and Tukaraneo
Pithatho among others. All these Batswana ladies earn a living from
their God-given skill of basket-weaving.
That being the case, most if not all of their products are for sale
and this is where Botswanacraft comes in. They are responsible for
the marketing of these crafts, not just in Botswana but also
worldwide.
"Through the internet, Botswanacraft has widened the market base for
African crafts, particularly Botswana's baskets", said Oliver Groth,
a representative of the company.
A facility for packaging and delivery is available for the foreign
market. And the Etsha crafts people can wear broader smiles with news
that their marketing agent intends to open another centre in the
North West District.
Looking at the aesthetic qualities of the craftwork, a very
appreciable use of simplified lines and shapes makes up the major
identifying factor of the origin of such visual products.
With the availability of other forms of basketry in the Botswanacraft
showroom, it is the earthy shades of brown, which separate the Etsha
ones from Lesotho and the surrounding parts of South Africa. An
encouraging factor is the way Botswana's weavers have managed to
establish their craft to gain international recognition as much as
other arts such as ceramics in India and China have done.
On the baskets, various images appear, most of which are inspired by
the sun, diamond shapes and other natural features which can be seen
or imagined by the weavers.
Lack of exposure to modern technology does not set back the ever
forward- moving Batswana weavers. They never sit back and complain of
lack of modern dyes or machinery.
All their weaving stages are natural from preparation to finishing.
Through the use of the Mokolwane tree leaves the masterpieces are
patiently woven. But these threads need to be dyed before the weaving
process and it is through the use of the fluids obtained from various
roots, barks of trees and aloes, that such dyeing is done.
When sorghum seeds rot, they turn into a red/brown shade. They are
then taken and boiled to produce a similarly coloured pigment, which
is commonly found on most of these baskets.
The current exhibition at the Botswanacraft showroom and gallery
according to Oliver Groth, has basketry available for tender which
were woven by the highly respected and leading, artists from Etsha.
These are more expensive than the other lot done by the other less-
experienced weavers. Until the end of the exhibition, visitors can
also take advantage of the weaving on site and seeing basket
production done by the ladies in one corner of the gallery.
But how far can the level of appreciation go on the part of Africans,
for our own type of art? Can some of those few who are lucky to
afford it, consider investing in art? We have heard of Matisse,
Kandinsky or Klee's work being sold for large sums of money in
European and American markets.
Will this happen in Africa one day judging from the level of
mistrust, disrespect and to a certain extent mere hatred for each
other among fellow Africans? In most cases, African art has only
successfully been promoted by Europeans and Americans.
Where are African collectors? Success can only be thought of if
Africans diversify their level of thinking and stop tribalistic and
individualistic perception.
In many parts of Africa globalization is still a theory and until
this ceases, we continue to see more El Negroes undergoing "burials"
in museums than in graves.

Original_Search_Title: Poverty Is Behind the Increase in Illicit Brews
Headline: Kenya: Poverty Is Behind the Increase in Illicit Brews
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Death and injury from consumption of illicit brews is an
almost daily occurrence in the poorer sections of the Kenyan
community.
Unable to afford conventional drinks, the number of poor Kenyans
submitting to the vice has grown with the worsening state of the
country's economy. Half-hearted and haphazard attempts by the police
and the provincial administration to curb the practice have largely
failed due to growing poverty.
The fact that many of the poorly-paid police officers and chiefs
comprise a good portion of patrons who frequent these illicit
outlets, speaks volumes for the failure of the crackdown.
It is also common practice for law enforcers to operate protection
rings that ensure dealers in illicit brews are not arrested.
Save for the occasional raid by police officers out to punish traders
for failing to pay the "retainer", the effort has largely been
abandoned to women's groups who, out of concern for their menfolk,
have attacked dealers.
The practice has of late gained currency in Kirinyaga, Nyeri,
Murang'a, Kiambu districts and parts of Nairobi among them Kangemi
and Kawangware.
Nyeri MP, Mr. Wanyiri Kihoro has also mounted a one-man campaign
against the trade, and like the women, has had to contend with a
hostile police and Provincial Administration.
A highly-publicised government ban of some of the better known brews
and spirits in 1998, was largely ignored by the manufacturers who
abound in Nairobi and most of the country's urban centres.
The brews included Kulta, Sorghum-Sake, Kibuku Ram, International
Beer, Rumulika, Medusa and Simba Wine, Cantata, Kamata, Kulta,
Sorghum, Medusa and Teachers.
These, and many new brands can be readily obtained in many of the
dingy outlets dotted in Nairobi slums and trading centres in Central
and Rift Valley provinces.
The ban followed a parliamentary motion by Kangema MP, Mr. John
Michuki whose area has been badly afflicted by the vice. In his
constituency alone, at least 120 people had died after consuming the
brews, he told Parliament.
Mathira MP Matu Wamae said 200 people had died in his constituency
from the brews. Kabete MP, Mr. Paul Muite said 205 of his
constituents had also died.
Faced with a plethora of life-sapping problems, the Kenyan society
has generally relegated to the periphery the vice which only comes to
public attention when people die.
Yesterday's is a case in point.
The catalogue of deaths arising from consumption of illicit brews and
spirits is frightening.
The worst single incident, and perhaps not the last, occurred in
August, 1998 when nearly 100 people died, and many others went blind
after consuming a brew laced with power alcohol.
The Maai Mahiu incident was - Kenyan style - quickly forgotten as
many similar tragedies have followed, the difference being the brands
of brew consumed.
Among those who died in Narok in 1998 was a policeman and a public
health officer said to have been a regular consumer of the brew.
The brew was siphoned from tankers destined for neighbouring
countries and sold cheaply in Nakuru and Narok districts.
The 33 suspects who were subsequently arrested and charged with
causing the death of 36 of the victims, were later released as most
of the witnesses either died, were too ill to testify or could not be
traced altogether.
In 1996, 24 people died while six others ran blind in 1996 after
taking an illicit brew in Murang'a.

Original_Search_Title: Irin Update
Headline: East Africa: HORN OF AFRICA: IRIN Update
Body_Text:
SOMALIA: President to meet Ethiopian prime minister
Somali President Abdiqasim Salad Hasan was expected to arrive in the
Ethiopian capital Addis Ababa on Wednesday, 15 November, to meet
Ethiopian Prime Minister Meles Zenawi and senior officials to discuss
the relationship between the two countries, sources close to the
interim government told IRIN. The trip was related to an invitation
extended by Meles when he attended Hasan's inauguration in Djibouti
after the Arta peace conference on Somalia, the source added. The
trip was later postponed by the Ethiopian government.
Somali government sources said Hasan was expected to request
Ethiopia's help in establishing stability in Somalia, and to seek
assurances that Ethiopia would not support Somali factions opposed to
the new government. Diplomatic sources said Ethiopia would want a
commitment from the Somali government that Somalia would not be used
for attacks on Ethiopia by armed groups opposed to the Ethiopian
government. Relations between the new Somali government and the
Ethiopian government have been strained by the presence of Ethiopian
troops in Gedo region, southwestern Somalia, and in the south-central
regions of Bay and Bakool, diplomatic sources told IRIN. Ethiopia has
also been accused of supporting faction leaders opposed to the new
government.
SOMALIA: Yemen invites faction leaders
The President of Yemen, Ali Abdalla Saleh, has invited Mogadishu
faction leaders to travel to the Yemeni capital Sanaa for talks,
according to the south Mogadishu daily 'Qaran' newspaper. Husayn
Aydid, Osman Ali Ato, Muse Sudi Yalahow, Husayn Haji Bod, and Mowlid
Ma'ane Muhammad have been invited, and though no details of an agenda
have been released, Somali political sources say they will involve
mediation between the interim government and the faction leaders. The
Yemeni president has attempted such mediation since the election of a
Somali interim government in Djibouti in August. According to the
'Qaran' report, Osman Ato had also visited Somaliland in an effort to
persuade its president, Mohamed Ibrahim Egal, to attend the Yemeni
talks.
President Saleh hard earlier met Somali President Hasan in Doha,
Qatar, where both leaders were attending a summit meeting of the
Organisation of Islamic States.
SOMALIA: Assassinated MP buried
A funeral was held on Monday for the Somali parliamentarian Hasan
Ahmed Elmi, assassinated on Sunday night. The funeral was attended by
Prime Minister Ali Khalif Galayr, members of the cabinet, Speaker of
the Assembly Abdalla Deerow Issak, and other members of parliament.
The MP was killed in front of his family, by unidentified gunmen, in
Madina district of southwestern Mogadishu. No one has claimed
responsibility for the murder of Hasan Ahmed Elmi, known as Hasan
Jaale. Faction leader Muse Sudi Yalahow, who controls the area where
the killing took place, has denied any responsibility for the
shooting. Yalahow, one of the main faction leaders in Mogadishu, is
openly opposed to the transitional government. "I don't think people
in our area were responsible for the killing of Elmi", he told Agence
France Presse (AFP).
Abdalla Deerow Issak told reporters that the "killing was a
pre-planned plot to undermine the interim government." He said it
would not undermine the determination of the new government to
establish itself, the report added.
SOMALIA: UN develops plans for demobilisation
A UN inter-agency workshop on the demobilisation of combatants in
Somalia began on Monday in Nairobi. The workshop intended to create
"a common approach, tailored to Somalia's particular situation and
problems", according to a UN press release. "It is the first time we,
as the international community, have sat down to listen to concrete
experiences of the people themselves," said Jeremy Brickhill, the
workshop organiser.
The findings would "form the basis of a new common approach for all
actors involved in demobilisation and reintegration in Somalia",
according to the press release. Some work was already being done in
some areas in Somalia in the field of demobilisation and
reintegration, but the workshop was aimed at creating a common
approach, the statement added. There are several different types of
militias operating in Somalia, including those who work for the
business community, the Islamic courts, faction leaders and
sub-clans, as well as freelance gunmen who "often engage in criminal
activities like robbery, kidnapping and setting up illegal
roadblocks," the statement said. While there were no reliable
figures, estimates of the number of militiamen across Somalia "range
into the tens of thousands," it added.
SOMALIA: Banknotes flown into Puntland
A consignment of new Somali shilling banknotes arrived in Bosasso, in
the self-declared autonomous region of Puntland, northeastern
Somalia, on Saturday, according to the local newspaper 'Yool'. Some
30 billion Somali shillings (about $3 million) was flown in from
printers in Indonesia and Malaysia by two cargo aircraft, the report
said. "This is the fourth consignment of illegal banknotes to arrive
in Bosasso in the past two weeks," and the arrival of the banknotes
had caused a sharp rise in the prices of essential goods, the paper
added.
SOMALIA: Two reported killed in Somaliland protest
Two people died on Saturday following protests in Hargeisa, the
capital of the self-declared state of Somaliland in northwest
Somalia, the BBC reported on Monday. A child and a policemen were
killed in the incident, which took place when a crowd of people
protesting the arrest of Sultan Mohamed Abdulkadir of the Idagale
sub-clan of the Issakwere blocked the main road to the airport, the
BBC report said. More than 60 people were arrested and Somaliland
president Mohamed Ibrahim Egal, returning from an official visit to
Ethiopia, was forced to take a circuitous route, it said. Sultan
Mohamed Abdulkadir was arrested on his return from Djibouti, where he
attended the Somali peace conference. He is an elected member of
Somalia's transitional assembly. The Somaliland authorities strongly
opposed the Djibouti process and have arrested people who attended
the conference.
SOMALIA-ETHIOPIA: Somaliland signs trade accord
The administration of Somaliland, northwest Somalia, announced on
Sunday that it had signed a trade accord with Ethiopia aimed at
boosting trade and communication, Reuters news agency reported. The
agreement was signed following a three-day official visit by
Somaliland's Mohammad Ibrahim Egal to Ethiopia, where he and Prime
Minister Meles Zenawi held discussions covering bilateral issues and
the general situation in the Horn of Africa, the report said.
Somaliland presidential spokesman Abdi Idiris said that the core of
the agreement was to increase the use of the Somaliland port of
Berbera, Reuters said. According to the Somaliland administration,
Ethiopia would also install a microwave communication link between
Burao, Hargeisa and Berbera. In addition, the accord would see the
Ethiopian and Somaliland central banks facilitate trade by providing
links to the outside world, according to Idiris.
ETHIOPIA-ERITREA: UN envoy meets Meles
UN Secretary-General Kofi Annan's Special Representative for Ethiopia
and Eritrea, Legwaila Joseph Legwaila, met Ethiopian Prime Minister
Meles Zenawi in Addis Ababa on Tuesday to discuss the UN peacekeeping
mission for Ethiopia and Eritrea (UNMEE). A UN statement described
the meeting as "cordial and productive". Legwaila also met OAU
Secretary-General Salim Ahmed Salim in Addis Ababa on Friday to thank
him for the part the organisation played in the Ethiopian-Eritrean
peace process.
Legwaila said he hoped that there would a be a peaceful resolution to
the dispute between the two countries "so that they can devote all
their energies to development", according to a statement released by
UNMEE. A cessation of hostilities was established between the two
countries on 18 June, but no negotiated settlement has been achieved.
Legwaila noted in his conversation with Salim Ahmed Salim that there
was still an issue of mistrust on both sides, and that it was
essential to implement the agreement signed. He said he hoped that
the two sides would soon agree on the delineation and demarcation of
the disputed border, UN sources said.
ETHIOPIA: Food aid tops one million tonnes - WFP
Ethiopia has received a total of 1,231,233 mt of food aid since
January, according to the latest WFP emergency report, issued on
Friday. Meanwhile, an emergency needs assessment is expected to start
in Tigray, northern Ethiopia, and Borena, southern Ethiopia,
involving 22 teams with over 100 team members. An aerial livestock
assessment of the Somali Region was also being considered in order to
obtain better information on actual livestock populations in the
region, WFP stated.
In the Somali Region, which was hit hardest by the regional drought,
the number of new admissions to supplementary feeding centres in
Korahe Zone has decreased, said the report. "This is attributed to
the fact that nomadic populations are moving towards improved grazing
areas, following a much higher rainfall during the 'deyr' rains than
in previous years," it said. However, the rainfall was also causing
problems with the storage of recently-harvested sorghum, the WFP
report stated. Excessive rainfall in Warder Zone, Ogaden region, had
led to floods and, in the Shinile Zone of Hararghe, there were
reports of new internally displaced people (IDPs), it added. There
has also been an increase in confrontations between the Ethiopian
Army and the Ogaden National Liberation Front (ONLF) in the Somali
Region, and clan problems along the Ethiopia-Kenya border have caused
displacement of Garre people, the report said.
SUDAN: 'Lost boys' to be resettled in US
About 300 Sudanese 'lost boys' were expected to be resettled in the
US by the end of the year through a State Department programme, the
Associated Press (AP) reported on Monday. Some 3,800 Sudanese, mostly
boys and young men, became known as the "lost boys" after they walked
together out of the southern Sudan war zone in the early 1990s, and
were received by international agencies in refugee camps. Many of the
young boys were unable or unwilling to say where they had come from
but humanitarian sources said some had escaped from rebel training
camps, the report said.
A resettled boy interviewed in Philadelphia, USA, by Associated Press
said he had been forced to leave his village in southern Sudan in
1987 during heavy fighting. He left with his uncle and made a 15-day
trek to Ethiopia by foot, and he was later expelled from a refugee
camp in 1991 when the Ethiopian regime was overthrown, he said. The
'lost boy', Thon, said his uncle had been killed as the two made
their way back into Sudan, and eventually on to Kakuma refugee camp
in Kenya. Like many of the 'lost boys', Thon told journalists he
believed his mother and his siblings could still be alive. However, a
representative of the Lutheran Family Services refugee resettlement
group told AP that all attempts to find the boys' real parents had
failed. The resettled boys are staying with foster parents.
SUDAN: ICRC reports vaccination success in Yirol
The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) has carried out
successful vaccination campaigns against tetanus and yellow fever in
Yirol in the Western Equatoria region of southern Sudan, according to
a news release from the agency. The campaign successfully "reached
most villages", and loudspeaker announcements several hours ahead of
time helped bring people in for the vaccinations, it said. Other
programmes being pursued included special care for pregnant women to
ensure that they give birth in the best possible hygiene conditions
and to reduce the neonatal mortality rate, the ICRC said. A clinic
had been built with a dozen beds at the site of Yirol hospital, which
was partially destroyed in a bombing raid, it added. The Committee's
October newsletter said that humanitarian organisations were
struggling to curb high mortality rates in southern Sudan, and that
"conditions remain difficult" despite improvement through the course
of this year.
HORN OF AFRICA: Pastoralist meeting highlights aid weaknesses
The recent drought crisis "highlights weaknesses in the way that aid
agencies interact with pastoral communities and brings urgently
needed attention to the sector," according to a UNDP press release on
a meeting between pastoralists, UN agencies and donor representatives
in Isiolo town, northern Kenya, from 31 October to 2 November.
Livestock herders, marketers, managers and local leaders met with UN
agency and donor officials as part of strategy to give pastoralists
from Somalia, Kenya and Ethiopia an opportunity to tell aid agencies
what was needed in terms of international assistance, the press
release said. Problems in communicating, negotiating and cooperating
with pastoralists (typically marginalised from the political and
institutional mainstream) had tended to result in only short-term
relief, it added.
The problems of pastoralists were a "truly regional issue, as these
migratory communities cross national boundaries, and the
[pastoralist] system itself can only work in the larger regional
context," the UNDP statement said. The results of the meeting will be
written up by the Institute of Development Studies in the UK, with
the United Nations, and will form the basis for action plans at
regional, country and agency level, it added. The workshop was
organised by the UN Coordination Unit for Somalia, the Institute for
Development Studies and UNICEF Regional Office.

Original_Search_Title: Famine Threatening Millions
Headline: Niger: Famine Threatens Four Million People In Niger
Body_Text:
Niamey, Niger — Famine threatens at least for
million people, or 39 percent of the population in Niger due to a
failed crop season, the country's rural development minister,
Wassalke Boukari, revealed in Niamey Thursday
The minister told parliament that some 4,523 villages were
experiencing food shortages ranging from 10 percent to 20
percent.
An assessment tour had revealed that all the country's
eight regions were faced with a deficit in cereal harvests and
that the total deficit stood at 163,000 tonnes of cereals.
This crop season, the country expects to harvest 1,821,899
tonnes of millet, sorghum (407,787 tonnes), rice (60,386 tonnes),
maize (18,384 tonnes) and 10,750 tonnes of wheat.
Boukari said that the livestock sector had also faired
badly due to poor rainfall.
He said the government envisages a switch from rain-fed to
irrigated agriculture on 54,864 hectares of land, in which
220,000 peasant farmers would be involved to produce an estimated
168,000 tonnes of cereals. The project is estimated to cost 1.6
billion CFA francs.

Original_Search_Title: Irin News Briefs
Headline: Southern Africa: Irin News Briefs
Body_Text:
MALAWI: Three axed cabinet ministers to be prosecuted
Three influential Malawi cabinet ministers who were axed from
President Bakili Muluzi's executive last week are being probed for US
$2.5 million fraud, authorities confirmed on Monday. Malawi director
of public prosecutions Fahad Assani said the country's
Anti-Corruption Bureau (ACB) had begun investigating former education
minister Dr Cassim Chilumpha, former labour minister Peter Chupa and
former public works minister Brown Mpinganjira following indications
that they allegedly masterminded an elaborate fraud scam.
Assani said the three disgraced politicians would face multiple fraud
charges for allegedly awarding large government tenders totalling US
$2.5 million for new schools, roads and boreholes to non-existent
companies or corporations owned by friends and relatives. The three
were among the 33 cabinet ministers sacked by Muluzi last Thursday,
but were not reappointed to the reshuffled and enlarged cabinet at
the weekend.
The tenders were never completed, or were inadequately built, but
full payment was made by the government, news reports said. The scam
was originally exposed by Malawi's parliamentary public accounts
committee earlier this year and confirmed by an in-depth ACB
investigation. The confidential investigation report was handed to
Muluzi just days before he axed his entire cabinet and is scheduled
for review by parliament on 13 November.
ANGOLA-DRC: FAA commander visits
The commander of the Angolan armed forces (FAA) said in Kinshasa on
Tuesday that the Angolan military presence in the Democratic Republic
of Congo (DRC) was now only symbolic, news agencies reported.
General Joao de Matos, in Congo for a two-day visit, said: "We
considerably reduced our forces here, our representation today is
symbolic. That does not mean Angola has removed itself from the
Congolese conflict, we follow in detail what happens here." De Matos
met DRC President Laurent-Desire Kabila during the trip, which
follows a meeting he had in Angola last week with the head of the
Ugandan military, Jeje Odongo. An earlier visit by de Matos to Uganda
in September sparked widespread speculation that Angola might be
preparing to withdraw its support from Kabila. De Matos told
reporters on Tuesday that the meeting between Ugandan and Angolan
officials had been to discuss bilateral relations between the two
countries.
Meanwhile, Angolan Minister of Defence Kundi Paihama arrived in
Zimbabwe on Monday for a visit aimed at reinforcing cooperation
between the two countries, state television monitored by the BBC
said. His visit followed a message sent to Zimbabwean President
Robert Mugabe on 3 November by his Angolan counterpart Jose Eduardo
dos Santos. The message was carried by the Angolan deputy minister of
foreign affairs, George Chicoty, the official news agency Angop
reported. Chicoty, who did not disclose the content of the message,
had just visited Namibia where he presented another message from the
Angolan president to Namibia's Sam Nujoma, Angop said. Angolan,
Zimbabwean and Namibian troops are supporting Kabila in the war
against Ugandan and Rwandan-backed rebels.
ZIMBABWE: Whites not targeted in land grab

Zimbabwe's deputy attorney general said on Tuesday the government's
drive to seize white-owned farms for landless blacks was legal and
did not discriminate against whites, Reuters reported. Deputy
Attorney-General Bharat Patel told a Supreme Court hearing that the
government distanced itself from violence that followed the invasion
of white-owned farms by war veterans, landless peasants and
supporters of the ruling ZANU-PF party.
"The identification and acquisition of farms under the land reform
process is not wholly or mainly attributable to the race or colour of
the applicant's members and therefore there has been no contravention
of the constitution," Patel said. The mainly white Commercial Farmers
Union (CFU), is challenging the constitutionality of President Robert
Mugabe's power to seize their land with no obligation to pay
compensation.
Mugabe's government stood by as hundreds of farms were invaded from
February and ignored High Court orders to drive the squatters out.
Earlier this year Mugabe amended the constitution to give him the
power to seize white-owned farms for blacks with no obligation to pay
for the land. He said former colonial ruler Britain must compensate
the farmers.
ZIMBABWE: Former Rhodesian leader Ian Smith returns home unhindered
Despite threats by Zimbabwe's President Robert Mugabe to have him
arrested, Ian Smith, the leader of the former white-minority regime
was allowed back into the country unhindered on Tuesday, news
agencies reported. Mugabe told supporters last month that Smith and
other whites who fought against black guerrillas two decades ago
would be tried for war crimes. "I was hoping there would be a
problem," Smith said after returning from a speaking engagement in
Britain. "I am disappointed that there wasn't."
Smith had dismissed Mugabe's threat to revoke his amnesty and
prosecute him for the deaths of 30,000 people in Zimbabwe's
independence war. "We have a person who is mentally unstable who
makes statements which have no bearing on reality. I don't think we
should take that seriously," Smith said.
MOZAMBIQUE: Opposition lawmakers return to parliament
Mozambique's opposition coalition, led by former RENAMO rebels, said
on Tuesday it had ended its boycott of parliament after seven months.
"We will never boycott again, it's over," Maximo Dias, a prominent
lawyer from one of ten smaller parties allied to the Mozambique
National Resistance (RENAMO) told AFP.
The RENAMO-led coalition began the boycott to protest against the
December 1999 general elections, which they insist were rigged
despite a Supreme Court ruling that the polls were free and fair. "We
achieved our objective, to show that the government of President
Joaquim Chissano is not a legitimate government," Dias said of the
decision to retake the coalition's 117 seats. Since parliament opened
in January, all RENAMO-Electoral Union deputies walked out of the
chamber whenever a government-sponsored bill was brought to debate.
Although the boycott has ended, RENAMO still plans to carry out
nationwide demonstrations to protest against what it calls an
illegitimate government. RENAMO and the ruling FRELIMO party fought a
16-year civil war, until a 1992 peace accord that led to the
country's first multi-party elections two years later.
ZAMBIA: Real GDP forecast to grow by five percent
Zambia's finance ministry on Tuesday forecast that real gross
domestic product would rise by five percent in 2001, increasing to
5.5 percent in 2002, up from a projected 4.0 percent in 2000. The
ministry also predicted that overall inflation would drop to a
single-digit figure next year. Zambia's annual inflation rate is
currently running at around 27 percent, news reports said.
The forecasts were included in the ministry's interim Poverty
Reduction Strategy (PRSP) report prepared by the government for
foreign aid donors. "The attainment of these broad macroeconomic
objectives is to be aided by institutional and structural reforms in
various sectors of the economy," the finance ministry said.
The ministry referred to tighter fiscal and monetary discipline, and
reform of the state-owned Zambia National Commercial Bank. It also
planned to initiate the process of privatising the state power
utility Zesco and is preparing to accept bids for parts of Zambia
Railways. Zambia privatised its debt-laden mining industry earlier
this year, and copper production is expected to rebound sharply in
2001 as the sector benefits from improved management and capital
investment.
ZAMBIA: IMF recommends debt relief
An International Monetary Fund document has recommended early debt
relief under a Heavily Indebted Poor Countries (HIPC)initiative for
Zambia, Reuters reported. It is estimated that relief under HIPC
would amount to about US $2.5 billion and the IMF's share of this, US
$600 million, would be delivered within five years, shorter than in
any other HIPC case so far.
Zambia's external debt stands at US $6.5 billion. Without debt relief
it would be forced to repay the IMF around $230 million, plus
bridging loans made in the early 1990s that are now falling due. The
IMF board will meet on 22 November to decide Zambia's fate and the
IMF staff in Zambia have suggested that the relief should be
approved.
Decision point is reached when the IMF decides a country is meeting
agreed economic reform benchmarks on schedule. Ten countries have so
far qualified for debt relief, which is being offered under the
international community's HIPC initiative.

ZAMBIA: Konkola copper production on target
Primary copper production at Zambia's Nchanga and Konkola mines rose
to 60,133 mt in the second quarter, slightly above a target of 60,000
mt, Reuters reported on Tuesday. Nchanga and Konkola, which operate
under the name Konkola Copper Mines Plc (KCM), are owned by a unit of
London-listed Anglo American Plc.
KCM said production for Nchanga Open Pit and Nchanga Underground
mines in the July-September quarter reached 50,376 mt, which was 850
mt above target. Production at Konkola mine was 9,757 mt. KCM
repeated a commitment to break even by the end of its current
financial year. It said improved copper production at its operations
had been achieved despite problems posed by the delayed arrival of
spares required to refurbish mining equipment.
SOUTH AFRICA: Government says land restitution programme on track
The return to black South Africans of land taken from them under
apartheid is speeding ahead, with more than 10 percent of claims
settled, AFP quoted the government as saying on Tuesday. The
government is buying up farmland at agreed prices. Some 80 percent of
farm land is still owned by whites, who make up only 13 percent of
the population, for distribution.
President Thabo Mbeki has stressed that he would not tolerate illegal
land occupations, like those in Zimbabwe where landless blacks moved
onto 1,500 white-owned farms in the first part of this year. Land
Claims Commissioner Wallace Mgoqi told a parliamentary committee that
more than 6,530 claims had been settled out of 63,455 lodged by the
cut-off date, the end of 1998.
By June last year, only 41 claims had been settled, he said. "By all
standards, this is a phenomenal increase," Mgoqi said, admitting that
the land restitution process started after the apartheid government
was voted out of power in 1994 had initially been very slow. "The
process has picked up speed and the momentum is picking up all the
time," he added. The commission was also considering expropriating,
at a set price, land from white farmers unwilling to accept the
government's offer for their land, he said.
NAMIBIA: Government view of Caprivi emergency rejected
The government was wrong when it said the State of Emergency
proclaimed in the Caprivi strip last year could be applied more
widely, 'The Namibian' newspaper reported on Tuesday. In a High Court
ruling, Acting Judge Peter Shivute held that the State of Emergency
and its regulations, which granted the security forces wide powers of
arrest and detention without trial, could only be exercised in the
Caprivi.
His judgement followed the hearing of the first three of more than
100 claims against the government over alleged unlawful arrests in
the wake of a security crackdown that followed a separatist attack at
Katima Mulilo in August last year.
Tens of millions of dollars are also being claimed from the
government in some 120 civil suits by people who claim they were
unlawfully detained and in several cases badly tortured by the
security forces in Caprivi.
SOUTHERN AFRICA: Regional maize crop variable

Zimbabwean farmers have predicted a drop in maize plantings due to
financial and political worries, while South African and Zambian
summer crop sowings have begun with good rain, Reuters reported on
Tuesday. The Zimbabwe Farmers Union, representing communal farmers
who produce about 60 percent of the country's maize, predicted a
sharp fall in output due to lack of financial resources. The
Commercial Farmers Union, whose members account for about 38 percent
of maize output, said plantings were expected to drop sharply due to
concerns over viability, coupled with a resurgence of farm invasions
by war veterans since February.
In South Africa, farmers indicated they may switch from maize to
sunflowers, ground nuts and soy after last season's bumper maize
harvest of 10.14 million mt depressed prices. Rains have arrived in
Zambia and spread over the key growing areas last week. Farmers said
land preparation had begun, with planting already underway in some
areas. Zambia produced 14 million 50-kg bags of maize in the
1999/2000 (April/March) season compared with annual national
consumption needs of 12 million 50 kg bags.
The Malawi government is to distribute free farm inputs to over 1.5
million poor family households next week, which is slightly late for
early planters, farmers said. Farm minister Leonard Mangulama said
the exercise, which should have started on 1 November, had been
delayed because the government had problems transporting soybean seed
and fertilizer for the scheme from Zimbabwe and Mozambique's port of
Beira. Under the US $12 million scheme farmers will be given 20 kg
input packs, comprising 10 kg of fertilizer and 10 kg of improved
maize, sorghum and legume seed, enough for 0.1 hectare.

Original_Search_Title: Township Dwellers Weigh Up Poll Options
Headline: South Africa: Township Dwellers Weigh Up Poll Options
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Driving down the Soweto highway past Diepkloof and
Orlando East, pictures of a smiling Democratic Alliance (DA) leader
Tony Leon proclaim in Sotho: "Ya batho ka moka" (for all people).
Ironically, the African National Congress (ANC) poster's message is
in English: "Speeding up change and fighting poverty."
The absence of posters of other traditionally black parties like the
Pan Africanist Congress, the Azanian People's Organisation (Azapo)
and the Socialist Party of Azania is glaring. If the posters are
anything to go by, next month's local government elections in the
area will be a contest between the DA and the ANC.
Where Noordgesicht, the coloured township next to Orlando East, meets
Soweto there is a busy shopping centre.
Ephraim Khuzwayo, who works in the bottlestore for R100 a week, says:
"I did not register to vote because I cannot think of any party to
support. I have been let down by the ANC."
Raymond Norman and Henry Koerratwo, from Noordgesicht, who had
crossed the "artificial" racial divide of a busy highway to venture
into the township of Orlando to buy liquor, join the conversation and
say: "Tony Leon, he is our man."
Outside the building, taxi owner and driver Vusi Manana says he lives
in Meadowlands township and up to now there has been no delivery of
services promised by local councillors, who he describes as
"useless". "Let us not even blame them for unemployment, but let us
look at what our townships look like. The water and electricity gets
cut, there are no proper streets, no sewerage. What do we have to
vote for?" he asks.
Orlando East, one of Soweto's oldest townships, has a politicised
population made up largely of older people, as the younger generation
has moved out. Residents still speak of James "Sofasonke" Mpanza, who
led the homeless to build shacks in Orlando West. This led to
government having to build houses for the thousands living on the
edge of Orlando township.
Further down the road, two women say they not only voted for the ANC
in the past, but also campaigned in the township for it. Yet they
also say the party has let them down.
"We applied for houses in 1991 and again in 1995 and nothing has come
out of our applications. Instead foreigners, because they have the
money, have been allocated houses near New Canada. That area was
ours. It was earmarked for Orlando residents. We have electricity and
water cut off, there are no jobs and we are suffering. Why should we
support the ANC? Let those who have benefited from them vote for
them," the women say.
Orlando West in Soweto has always been an ANC stronghold. This is
where veteran ANC leaders such as Nelson Mandela and Walter Sisulu
lived before being imprisoned on Robben Island.
It is also the township which experienced bloody turf battles between
ANC-affiliated Soweto youth organisations and those backing Azapo. It
is where the Mandela United Football Club, which terrorised Soweto
townships, was born and destroyed.
In the 1980s, Xorile Street in Orlando West was known to be the turf
of ANC youth. Today, where the ANC youth once carried AK-47 rifles in
broad daylight, it is now a forlorn area. Except for the unemployed
who loiter aimlessly, there is nothing extraordinary about the
street.
It is midday and pensioner Bobby Zwane, who recently built a one-
roomed structure, sells sorghum beer cartons and is enjoying a drink
with his friends, whose ages range between 25 and 60. They share many
things. They are unemployed and bitter. They also voted for the ANC
in the past elections.
"We gave the ANC two chances and they failed us. For now, we are
going to give Tony Leon a chance. If he also fails us we will give it
to someone else who will live up to his promises," says Zwane. His
friends join in the conversation; a chorus of damnation of the ANC.
They are not moved by criticism that by voting for the DA they will
be bringing back a white government. "What has a black government
done for us? Our electricity and water is being cut, pensioners have
to queue for long hours to get their pensions. Who cares for us and
our children who died to get the ANC into power?"
One of the men says: "During the apartheid government, life was
difficult, but I always had money in my back pocket."
Their anger, it seems, has been fuelled by the recent actions of
municipal officials who removed containers where people had set up
telephone services. They are of the opinion the ANC government should
have known better than to remove the containers, because as a black
government they know black people's problems. They say they feel
betrayed.
ANC provincial spokesman Mike Ramagoma says his party is confident
many people will vote for the ANC because they have seen what the
party has been able to achieve in only six years.
He says it was not an easy six years, as government had to turn many
things around to make towns and cities work better, to include black
townships left out under apartheid. "That process has taken time and
plans have now been put in place. We are on track towards achieving a
better life for all the people, not just a few," he says.
Ramagoma says those planning on abstaining from voting need to be
reminded that the vote was achieved through blood and the loss of
loved ones. He says those planning on abstaining from voting need to
be reminded that the vote was achieved through blood and the loss of
loved ones.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Emergency Report No. 44 - Africa
Headline: Africa: WFP Emergency Report No. 44 - Africa
Body_Text:
Rome — This report includes: (A) Great Lakes: (1) Uganda, (2)
Democratic Republic of Congo (B) Horn of Africa: (1) Kenya, (2)
Eritrea (C) Sierra Leone (D) Angola
From Francesco Strippoli, Senior Humanitarian Adviser. Available on
the Internet on the WFP Home Page (www.wfp.org), or by e-mail from
Zlatan.Milisic@wfp.org. For information on resources, donors are
requested to contact Valerie.Sequeira@wfp.org at WFP Rome, telephone
39 06 6513 2009. Media queries should be directed to
Trevor.Rowe@wfp.org, telephone 39 06 6513 2602. The address of WFP is
Via Cesare Giulio Viola 68, Parco dei Medici, 00148 Rome, Italy.
(A) Great lakes
(1) Uganda
(a) WFP expanded its relief operation for the most vulnerable
population in the drought-affected Karamoja region in the north-east,
for a period of six months, and appealed for USD 7.1 million in
additional funding. The operation is expected to provide 12,000 tons
of emergency food to some 190,000 semi-nomadic people living in
Moroto and Kotido districts, who are suffering consequences of a
widespread crop failure caused by the same drought which is currently
affecting the Horn of Africa.
(b) WFP has been feeding drought victims in Karamoja since April,
with the beneficiary number peaking at 250,000 during the hunger gap
period in July. WFP interventions are believed to have helped avert
famine earlier this year, but the crisis in Karamoja is continuing
and continued provision of food aid is essential to maintain the
livelihood of thousands of needy people. The first phase of the
emergency operation received USD 3.9 million (70 percent of the
requirement) for a feeding operation benefiting an average of 160,000
people.
(c) The expanded operation will provide maize, beans, vegetable oil
and blended fortified food. WFP food will be distributed by the
Adventist Development and Relief Agency (ADRA). The operation also
includes expanded school feeding and food for work programs.
(d) The Government has started sustained efforts to address chronic
problems plaguing Karamoja, such as recurrent drought-induced food
shortages, cattle rustling, ethnic clashes and widespread insecurity.
(2) Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC)
(a) WFP interventions in the country are focused on food security of
displaced persons, nutritional status of vulnerable groups and their
access to basic services, security of minority groups and
reintegration of war-affected communities. Currently it is estimated
that there are around 1.8 million IDPs all over the country. WFP
project is targeting 350,000 persons of whom 200,000 are internally
displaced and 150,000 vulnerable groups. Despite the ongoing war,
security and access-related problems, WFP has been able to reach up
to 300,000 beneficiaries.
(b) The project has so far mobilised more than 90 percent of the
required resources, but the problem has been in the late arrival of
food because of late donor contributions. At the end of September the
total quantity distributed since January was 14,984 tons. The
constraints related to the pipeline obliged WFP to suspend general
distribution in the East, where only vulnerable groups in nutritional
centres were assisted.
(c) WFP is also assisting 51,300 refugees from Angola and Republic of
Congo and 10,000 persons within the host community. The host
communities benefit from WFP assistance in support of small-scale
rehabilitation projects. WFP has been continuing with the
distribution of half rations through its partners. In September, the
project assisted 61,739 beneficiaries and a total of 5,838 tons of
food was distributed from January to September. With the resumption
of political unrest in Angola, an increasing number of refugees into
the southern provinces of DRC has been noted. It is envisaged that
the beneficiary caseload will continue to increase and reach 80,000
by April. WFP is currently preparing a budget revision of the project
to accommodate beneficiary increases.
(B) Horn of Africa
(1) Kenya
(a) Following the rapid needs assessments carried out in September,
the number of WFP beneficiaries in 19 districts of Kenya, assisted
through the current EMOP, has increased by 17%, from 2.46 to 2.89
million people. Some 659,000 people in 11 districts, the most
vulnerable among those assisted will also benefit from supplementary
food. In addition, over 1.04 million children are targeted through
emergency school feeding under the Expanded School Feeding Programme.
(b) Funding status of the WFP EMOP remains unchanged, though there
are indications that a new contribution providing additional 3,000
tons of pulses might be forthcoming. This would improve the current
funding situation to 77 percent of the total requirements being
covered.
(c) General distribution for the month of October commenced in most
districts. It was delayed by a prolonged customs clearance procedures
regarding the food aid being imported.
(d) An influx of pastoralists from Tana River district caused tension
in Garissa district, particularly on the banks of the Tana, where
pastoralists and agriculturists are competing for land space. This
influx comes at a difficult time of worsening drought conditions
throughout the area and suggests that the numbers of vulnerable
people are set to rise.
(e) In Wajir District, the issue of beneficiary figures continues to
be an issue with the lead implementing agency advocating a higher
figure than that established by WFP and the Government. WFP will
continue to monitor the situation and review beneficiary statistics.
(f) In Kitui district, WFP met local leaders and district officials
to discuss the community based targeting system, after the community
in one division threatened to disrupt the food distribution process
if blanket distributions did not occur. The outcome of the meeting
was positive and the leaders understood the importance of targeting
the available limited resources, which was jointly agreed by WFP and
the Government and supported by the NGOs and provincial
administration.
(g) The supplementary feeding programme is ongoing in ten districts
of Kenya, under the coordination of UNICEF. The following population
groups are targeted: children under five, women pregnant beyond six
months and nursing mothers with babies up to six months old. All
those targeted for supplementary feeding are also receiving general
rations for their households. Supplementary feeding programme is
currently targeting the highest number of beneficiaries in Turkana,
Mandera, Wajir and West Pokot districts. During October, WFP has
supplied 2,410 tons of CSB and 434 tons of Unimix and UNICEF 450 tons
of Unimix (3,294 tons in total). In addition to the ten districts
prioritised for supplementary food under the WFP EMOP, 33,726 people
in Baringo district will supplementary food from World Vision.
(h) Tensions have been rising between religious leaders in Garissa
town and similar tensions were reported in Tana River district. In
Wajir, inter-tribal tensions have been reported. Banditry continues
to be a problem throughout the northern and eastern districts.
(2) Eritrea
(a) A total of 9,882 tons of WFP food was distributed to 511,700
beneficiaries in the first 20 days of October. The distributions
