included 7,567 tons for 466,058 war-affected persons in Debub, Gash
Barka, and Northern Red Sea regions and 2,315 tons for 45,636
drought-affected persons in Anseba and Northern Red Sea regions. Some
1,840 tons of food was distributed during the week, to 107,164
beneficiaries in Debub, Gash Barka and Northern Red Sea.
(b) A second HFEA training was completed in Keren in the Anseba
region. A total of 21 trainees who participated have been
short-listed to conduct the Annual Needs Assessment.
(c) The preliminary findings of the post distribution monitoring in
Aibaba and Gheleb distribution centres in Anseba region indicate that
the beneficiaries have limited access to clean drinking water which
led to incidents of water born diseases such as diarrhoea. Malaria,
tuberculosis and pneumonia are also reported as common diseases in
the area. According to the local administration, malnutrition cases
have been decreasing.
(d) A post distribution monitoring in Molki in Gash Barka region
indicated that at least 80 percent of the community are
agro-pastoralists, while the rest are involved in small trading and
provision of services, including labour. The main activity currently
taking place in the area is harvesting of sorghum. Food aid
distributions have been carried out by elected village relief
committees and the targeting was well understood by beneficiaries,
especially women who confirmed their awareness of the entitlements.
The team did not observe malnutrition in the area, but a
supplementary feeding for lactating mothers and children was reported
to be starting for the first time in Molki with Unimix from Oxfam
Belgium and milk from Lutheran World Federation (LWF).
(e) There was ample rainfall in Debub region with highest rainfall in
Adi Quala (63 mm). The winter rain season seems to have started early
in the Northern Red Sea, though limited in its spatial distribution.
(C) Sierra Leone
(a) WFP distributed 1,191 tons of food during the week, to 75,868
beneficiaries in Freetown, Kenema, Bo and Moyamba. Shortages of
cereals continue and are increasingly affecting WFP operations.
Further reductions in vulnerable groups and emergency school feeding
programmes were made in November, to ensure minimum stocks for
December.
(b) RUF forces reportedly attacked at least three villages in the
north, causing a new wave of displacement into Kabala. According to
CRS that operates there, 9,000 new IDPs arrived to the town during
the week. UNAMSIL reported that there are indications that RUF forces
have started to mobilise their troops towards the Guinea border.
(c) WFP, in collaboration with its partners, conducted a verification
exercise in seven IDPs camps in the Western Area. Preliminary
findings indicate that the beneficiary figures will be reduced by
10-15 percent. WFP is currently assisting 60,000 IDPs residing in the
camps there. WFP also participated in an interagency mission to
Lokomasama chiefdom (Port Loko) to assess the needs of IDPs from
Kambia district. Some 5,100 IDPs who fled ongoing fighting along the
border with Guinea were identified in Kakum Island and Yurika
environs.
(d) Due to a significant number of WFP activities in the Lungi
peninsula, including assistance to IDPs, returnees, malnourished and
school children, WFP is planning to open a field office in Masoila in
early November. This is expected to improve the efficiency of
programme implementation and increase monitoring capacities in the
area. A second temporary field office is planned for the town of
Daru, where WFP has commenced school feeding and vulnerable groups
feeding programmes.
(D) Angola
(a) WFP special operation (SO) providing passenger air service in the
country has been fully funded, following the confirmation of an ECHO
contribution of USD 620,017.
(b) The confirmation of the Spanish contribution of USD 629,330 for
the WFP PRRO in Angola has slightly improved the funding of the
operation. So far, a total of USD 117.77 million have been received,
representing 56.7 percent of the total requirement. Further
contributions are still urgently needed, to pre-empt food shortages
expected as early as November. WFP is currently exploring
possibilities for local and regional purchases.
(c) Malnutrition is reported to be on an increase in Kuito, as the
lean season sets in. The increase is also attributed to a high influx
of IDPs into the town. The number of children attending supplementary
feeding centres in Kuito rose again this week, bringing the total
number of beneficiaries to over 5,000. Medecins sans Frontiers (MSF)
Belgium, one of the main WFP partners for nutritional projects, is
planning to conduct a nutritional survey during November.
(d) A total of 909 new IDPs were verified in Kuito during the week,
bringing the total number of new IDPs verified in October to 8,740.
As a result of tense security situation in Cangumbe (Moxico
province), Kuemba Munhango (Bie) and Sautar (Malange) the influx of
IDPs into Luena has increased. New IDPs were also reported in
Malange, Lunda Sul and Kuando Kubango.
(e) The overall security situation in Angola is characterised by
small-scale ambushes and attacks in most parts of the country. During
the week, security incidents were reported in Bie, Moxico, Lunda Sul,
Uige, Malange, Huila, including several mine incidents. Some security
incidents involved human casualties and caused further population
displacements.
(f) Due to the current rehabilitation of Luena airport, restriction
has been placed on aircraft landing and take-off times until further
notice - allowed only between 5:00 am and 11:30 am.
(g) WFP airlifted a total of 3,218 tons of food and 748 tons of
non-food items (3,966 tons in total) from Catumbela, Lobito and
Luanda, to various provinces during the week.
Note: All tonnage figures in this report refer to metric tons
(End WFP Emergency Report No 44)

Original_Search_Title: Famine Threatens Kelafo
Headline: Ethiopia: ETHIOPIA: Famine Threatens Kelafo
Body_Text:
Kelafo — As people struggle to recover
from drought and food shortages in Kelafo, southeastern Ogaden, an
outbreak of crop pests is threatening residents with more shortages
for months to come.
Recent rains have left Kelafo green, but the community of Somalis who
farm along the banks of the huge Shebelle river say they have no
harvest to look forward to. "The green is beautiful, but useless
unless you have animals to put on it, or crops that come out of it,"
one old man said.
At a feeding centre run by the Italian NGO, Commitato Collaborazion
Medica (CCM), 79 severely malnourished children receive theraputic
feeding, together with 106 accompanying mothers and family members.
At the peak of the food crisis in the Ethiopian Somali State this
year, the agency registered about 110 children in the Kelafo feeding
centre. "Things have improved, but there's still a problem here," the
supervisor of the centre, Farah Ali Madobe, told IRIN.
He said malnourished children could still be found in the outlying
villages. The agency sometimes had to send out a vehicle to collect
people unable to walk to the centre, said the staff. Some had walked
as far as 45 km to reach Kefafo in the hope of help.
On the outskirts of this town, which is situated in a fertile area,
camps for internally displaced people (IDPs) have mushroomed this
year, and these still depend on hand-outs. They join an already
established camp for refugees from Somalia, most of whom arrived two
years ago from the southern Bakool area, and have depended on food
aid from CCM and the local NGO, Guardian. The expanse of makeshift
straw-domed huts known as 'aqal'is a dominant feature of the town.
About 200 km south by road lies the Somali border, and the Somali
shilling is the most common currency in Kelafo, along with the
Ethiopian bitt.
Predominantly a farming community, crops have been blighted with what
is locally known as "gududeye", which means "making red". It affects
maize plants, damaging their roots and turning the stems red before
killing the plants. Ga'alow Dinle Doli, a local farmer, showed IRIN
his maize crop ruined by the disease. Young fruit trees had also been
affected. "Only the onions are safe," he said.
According to CCM and local farmers, a government team came to take
crop and soil samples. Barkat Sirad Umar, the secretary of the Kelafo
district committee, told IRIN that the team had come three months
after the alarm was sounded - "but we have not heard anything since".
In Kelafo, although the community mainly relies on farming, livestock
is also a part of life. Many people complained that they had lost
part or even all of their herds during the peak of the regional
drought, which hit the Ogaden area of the Somali State hard from
April onwards. But with the recently imposed Rift Valley fever ban on
livestock exports to the Gulf States, even those still with herds in
their possession find the market frozen.
"In Kelafo people must invest either in livestock or farming, but now
they have no options," Barkat Sirad Umar told IRIN. He said the
economic effects of the ban had already hit the town. Before the ban,
a 50 kg bag of sugar cost 160,000 Somali shillings (US $16); since
the ban, the same bag costs 245,000 Somali shillings (US $25). Local
trade in sorghum and livestock across the border to Somalia has also
diminished.
"This should be the recovery stage," local humanitarian sources told
IRIN, "but food aid will have to continue for longer". International
agencies say a crisis was effectively averted in the area, but local
people are already talking about a famine. In Karanka, 45 km west of
Kelafo, 70 year-old Umar Duale said this year's hunger was locally
called "Fa'ka weyn" - which means "greater than age". He said it
meant nobody had experienced such a prolonged disaster in living
memory. Last year he owned 300 sheep and goats, but now he had only
20 to support his wife and nine children, he said.

Original_Search_Title: Researchers Develop High-yield Potato, Sorghum Varieties
Headline: Ethiopia: Researchers Develop High-yield Potato, Sorghum Varieties
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa, Ethiopia — Researchers at Alemeya
University in eastern Ethiopia have developed two high-yield and
disease-resistant varieties of potato and sorghum.
The government-owned Ethiopian Herald newspaper reported
Thursday that the two potato varieties yielded up to 40 tons per
hectare while those of sorghum produced 8.3 tons per hectare.
The potato varieties were discovered by a team headed by
Tackling Tsega, head of the potato research project, while Temam
Hussein headed the team on sorghum.

Original_Search_Title: Muluzi Arrives for Visit
Headline: Kenya: Muluzi Arrives for Visit
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Malawi President Bakili Muluzi arrived in the country
yesterday for a three-day State visit.
The plane carrying him and his entourage landed at the Jomo Kenyatta
International Airport at 12.30pm.
His host, President Moi, accompanied by members of the Cabinet, had
arrived at the airport at 11.50am to welcome the visiting leader, who
became President in 1994 after defeating Hastings Kamuzu Banda in the
Malawi's first multi-party polls.
Pomp and pageantry greeted Mr. Muluzi and his 10-member team as they
disembarked from an Air Malawi aircraft onto the red carpet, to
hearty handshakes from President Moi, ministers, diplomats and top
military officers. Mr. Muluzi then inspected a guard of honour
mounted by the Kenya Air Force and was thereafter entertained by an
array of traditional dancers.
The visiting Head of State was then driven to State House, Nairobi,
where he held talks with President Moi.
Later, he toured two smallhold farms in Limuru and the DolliCraft
Women's Project at Kangemi, Nairobi.
Mr. Muluzi is today scheduled to fly to Mombasa to tour, among
others, the Kenya Ports Authority headquarters and its installations.
Malawi's economy is based on agriculture, but only about a third of
the land is suitable for farming. The most important export crop is
tea, which is grown on estates in the highlands that are owned by
Europeans.
Important crops grown by Africans include sorghum, sugar cane,
tobacco, cotton, corn, and peanuts. Many farmers raise livestock.
Fishing in Lake Nyasa has also become an important industry.
Malawi's few manufacturing industries produce such goods as bricks,
cement, cotton goods, and processed foods. Valuable hardwood forests
cover the northwest part of the country, but they are too difficult
for trucks to reach.

Original_Search_Title: IRIN Focus On Food Security
Headline: Southern Africa: SOUTHERN AFRICA: IRIN Focus On Food Security
Body_Text:
JOHANNESBURG, 24 October (IRIN) - Agricultural unions in southern
Africa have warned that output had generally deteriorated over the
past 12 months, affecting regional food security.
In a statement released at the end of their two day annual general
meeting in Namibia last week, representatives said the deterioration
was due to a number of factors, including the issue of a slow pace in
land reform. "The issue of land reform, complicated by the breakdown
in the rule of law, criminality and personal security, has
exacerbated the already fragile agricultural economies in the regions
and has been highlighted by the volatile situation in Zimbabwe," the
statement said.
In its latest update on food security in the region, the Famine Early
Warning System said food security and agricultural production was a
cause for concern in a number of countries within the Southern
African Development Community (SADC).
Zimbabwe
FEWS said that the current economic environment combined with the
socio-economic factors prevailing in the country made the 2000/01
production prospects gloomy, weakening the country's food security
position. "Grain production could drop 300,000 to 400,000 mt below
average and the wheat harvest is likely to slip 65,000 to 95,000 mt
below the good harvest of 320,000 mt in 1999/2000," FEWS said.
It said that although the Zimbabwean government had down-played
public fears of bread shortages, "the possibility of shortages was
real". "Zimbabwe is expected to harvest about 250,000 mt of wheat
starting in late October, not enough to cover annual consumption
needs," the update warned. It added that several recent developments
had pushed up the prices of basic commodities, threatening the food
security of urban consumers and those rural consumers who relied on
market purchases to meet part of their food requirements. These
developments included the devaluation of the Zimbabwe Dollar,
increases in fuel prices and shortages of fuel supplies.
According to FEWS, there were an estimated 22,000 highly
food-insecure people in areas in the southern and eastern parts of
the country that required food aid until the next harvest in March
next year.
The update noted that prices of all agricultural inputs had increased
by over 50 percent from last year. Fewer credit facilities were
available to farmers, maize marketing in most farming sectors had
been sluggish, and low producer prices of tobacco, maize and cotton
had made it difficult for average farmers to raise enough money to
purchase inputs for the coming cropping season. "Various experts in
the industry forecast that the agricultural sector will have a
negative growth rate. This poor performance will impact negatively on
food security for the rest of the 2000/01 consumption year ending in
March, and beyond," the update said.
Mozambique
FEWS said that although the food security situation was good on a
national level due to the abundant availability of food, not all the
population had access to enough nutritious food to meet their dietary
needs. It said of the 135 districts in the country 165,000 people in
37 districts were identified as food-insecure and did not have enough
food reserves or means to cover their food needs until March next
year.
The update said that projected maize supplies would be sufficient to
meet needs from September to almost the very end of the 2000/01
marketing year, however, after this newly harvested maize would be
needed to replenish supplies.
It said that good progress had been made in supplying seeds to an
estimated 186,000 small farmers in central and southern Mozambique
who, due to poverty and poorly developed rural markets, did not have
adequate seeds for the season. "The Ministry of Agriculture and Rural
Development (MADER) reports that 657 mt of rice, 1,236 mt of maize,
586 mt of cowpeas, 261 mt of sorghum, 130 mt of millet, and 833 mt of
groundnuts were distributed as seeds to 100,000 farmers to date." It
added that markets were "working reasonably well" including the
formerly flooded provinces of Maputo, Gaza, Inhambane, Sofala and
Manica.
FEWS said that reports from the National Institute of Meteorology
(INAM) showed very low precipitation for this time of year,
especially in the northern and central regions of the country. In
contrast, Maputo, parts of Gaza and Inhambane provinces experienced
above-normal rainfall during the latter part of September. It said
that probabilities of above-normal or normal rainfalls in the
southern and central regions would heighten the risk of repeat floods
in some low-lying areas where the soil remained saturated from the
massive flooding earlier this year. It said there was a 45-50 percent
probability of normal rainfall from October to December.
"These normal rains could contribute to good crop performance in the
northern maize-growing region of the country, particularly in Mecula
and Erati Districts (Cabo Delgado Province) where erratic rainfall
negatively affected crop performance during the 1999/2000-production
year," the update noted. "The enhanced probabilities of above-normal
and normal rainfall in the southern and central regions could slow
post-flood recovery and reconstruction activities or lead to
outbreaks of malaria, cholera or livestock diseases."
Malawi
FEWS said that food availability in Malawi was "very satisfactory"
with the majority of households still having food crops from their
own production. It added that both Agricultural Development and
Marketing Corporation (ADMARC) and local markets had maize readily
available for people to buy.
"Malawi expects to export 60,000 mt of maize to Kenya through the
newly created National Food Reserve Agency (NFRA)," the update said.
It added Malawi was expected to experience normal rainfall in the
2000/01 rainfall season from October to March next year. "Farmers are
now busy preparing their fields, while others have already finished,
in readiness for planting with the first rains normally expected
towards the end of October or early November."
It added that since Malawi expected normal rains this season the
country may have another good crop production season. FEWS said that
although the average local market maize prices were beginning to
rise, they still remained very low compared to prices in the past two
seasons.
Zambia
The supply of maize, maize meal and other staple foods on the Zambian
market continued to be adequate, FEWS said. It added that reports
from the Food Reserve Agency (FRA) suggested that millers were buying
most of their maize from small-scale farmers because commercial
farmers were stockpiling their produce to release on the market when
prices were more favourable. Maize and maize meal prices remained
relatively stable in August compared to prices in July. The price of
maize was "significantly below" that of millet and sorghum.
The update warned that concerns were mounting about the growing
prevalence of east coast fever and foot-and-mouth cattle diseases in
parts of Western, Northern, Central Eastern and Southern Provinces. A
campaign to vaccinate cattle against foot-and-mouth disease was
expected to get underway in late September. "Complete eradication of
east coast fever could take years," it added. Based on the seasonal
forecast, the northern parts of the country were expected to receive
mostly above-normal rainfall while the southern half was likely
receive normal rainfall.
The FEWS update can be found at: http://www.fews.net/

Original_Search_Title: Irin Regional Update
Headline: East Africa: HORN OF AFRICA: IRIN Update
Body_Text:
SOMALIA: Delegate to Djibouti process killed in Mogadishu
A delegate to the Djibouti peace process and supporter of the new
government, General Yusuf Talan - who represented the Gadabursi
sub-clan of the Dir group - was killed at "Kilometer 4" square, near
the Sahafi hotel, where a number of the recently-arrived MPs are
staying. According to sources in Mogadishu, Talan was sitting at a
popular tea shop when men in a white Toyota Cressida drove by and
opened fire. Talan died on the spot. No one has claimed
responsibility, but a Mogadishu businessman told IRIN there was an
assumption that the killers were allied to one of the opposition
faction leaders. The government has made no statement regarding the
attack. Talan attended the Djibouti-hosted peace talks and was a
member of the committee on disarmament.
SOMALIA: Faction leader withdraws support
Demonstrations were staged in parts of Bay and Bakol, southern
Somalia, following the announcement by Colonel Mohamed Hasan Nur
'Shatigadud', chairman of the Rahanwein Resistance Army (RRA, that he
was withdrawing his support from the new government. According to the
BBC, demonstrators chanted anti-Shatigadud slogans. In the regional
capital, Baidoa, militiamen loyal to Shatigudud, opened fire with
heavy weapons, but no casualties were reported. Shatigudud's position
has caused split in the RRA, which is based in the Bay and Bakool
regions and supported by Ethiopia.
Shatigudud said he no longer recognised the authority of the
transitional assembly in which he was allotted a seat. He accused the
clan of the interim president of "illegal land occupation" - a
reference to the Hawiye invasion during the civil war - and said the
government had not taken into account the wishes of the other regions
and leaders, AFP reported.
Shatigudud said earlier this week that he had no problem with the new
president, Abdiqasim Salad Hasan, but rejected the election as
Speaker of his fellow RRA leader, Abdullahi Deroow. According to the
BBC, he also complained he was not given any of the 20 extra seats
that Djibouti President Ismail Omar Guelleh was allotted for
distribution during the Djibouti-hosted talks.
SOMALIA: Chiefs disown Shatigudud
Sheikh Malaq Osman, a highly regarded traditional leader in the Bakol
area, told AFP that "Nur (Shatigudud) can no longer represent the
Rahanwein people". A letter was signed by 26 Malaqs - traditional
chiefs - which opposed Shatigadud's stance. In the letter, the chiefs
said they were prepared to defend the new government "with their
blood", the BBC reported. Many of the demonstrators blamed the
Ethiopian government - a chief supporter of Shatigadud - for the
military leader's statement.
SUDAN: British government "must respond"
Little attention has been paid to the development needs of the people
of southern Sudan, where chronic conflict has "systematically
destroyed the social fabric of institutions sustaining food security,
education and health care". In a joint statement, Christian Aid and
Oxfam said despite humanitarian efforts in the war-affected south,
underdevelopment had become institutionalised. "Southern Sudan now
represents one of the most glaring examples of development failure in
the whole of sub-Saharan Africa", the statement said. It criticised
British government policy for "reducing development assistance while
war continued", saying it had done little to further the pursuit of
peace.
It said successive generations of children had grown up without
adequate primary education, and that approximately 90 percent of
girls did not attend school. With agricultural production below
subsistence level, many people remained dependent on emergency food
aid. Forced population movements were increasing pressure on the
environment "deepening already extensive poverty for most families".
According to the statement, health services are almost non-existent
with people dying of preventable or curable diseases. "Little is
being done to counter the AIDS pandemic which will have devastating
consequences for future development in southern Sudan," the statement
said. Chronic underdevelopment also meant almost no electricity or
telecommunication system, and little commerce and trade.
British non-governmental organisations working in southern Sudan
called on the British government to step up efforts to secure a
negotiated settlement to the conflict, sustain the provision of
humanitarian assistance and respond urgently "to the development
disaster in the Sudan".
ERITREA: Poor harvest predicted
Below average rainfall and the disruption of farming activities in
war-affected areas will produce a poor harvest this year in Eritrea.
An October USAID's Famine Early Warning System (FEWS) warned that
"agriculturally important areas" were expected to produce cereals,
pulses and oilseeds far below previous harvests. Apart from poor
rainfall because of regional drought, important agriculture-producing
areas in western Eritrea had suffered because of "occupation of prime
farmland in war-affected areas that led to the disruption of farming
activities and displacement of farm households".
The FEWS report said the Eritrean government had released grain from
Eritrean Grain Board (EGB) outlets in September at a fixed price,
which had the effect of driving down soaring market sorghum prices.
It warned that without continuous releases of grain "it is not clear
how long sorghum prices will stay at this low level in view of poor
crop production".
The weekly repatriation of Eritrean refugees from Sudan has
continued, with more than 24,000 to date returned to mainly western
Eritrea under the UNHCR voluntary repatriation programme. Some
217,223 internally displaced people (IDPs) remain in 25 camps, mostly
in Gash Barka and Debub (western Eritrea) and North Red Sea Zobas. An
estimated additional 100,000 IDPs are staying among host communities,
the FEWS report added.
Nairobi, 18 October 2000, 14:45 gmt

Original_Search_Title: Drought Displaced Start to Flock Towards Khartoum
Headline: Sudan: Drought Displaced Start to Flock Towards Khartoum
Body_Text:
Khartoum, Sudan — The first wave of people
displaced due to the current drought have started to trek
towards Khartoum, an official of the Khartoum State has
announced.
Sharfaddin Bannaga, the state's housing minister,
said "a limited number" of the displaced have already
settled on the outskirts of Omdurman, Khartoum's twin
city, just across the White Nile.
Bannaga, who has been warning of a possible mass
exodus towards the capital and other major cities for
sometime now, reiterated the call that due measure be
taken to face the problem.
"All indications show that the number of the
displaced would escalate because of drought and low
rainfall," he warned in a press statement Monday.
The minister's warning came just a day after an
official report said the areas cultivated with sorghum,
the staple food of rural Sudanese, were less by 24 percent
this year.
According to the report, the areas most affected by
the low rainfall are Northern Kordufan and Nahr el Neel
States (around Khartoum) and North Darfur State in the far
west.
Compiled by representatives of the agriculture
ministry, FAO, CARE International and UNICEF, the report
has urged the government to buy a reserve of sorghum to be
used when the need arises.
The report, according to Bannaga, has proposed for
the reserve to be "within half a million tons."
Bannaga last week urged the government to seek
international aid "to control the situation before it is
too late."
He had also indicated a deficit in animal fodder in
the State of Khartoum as a result of low rainfall. He put
the deficit in fodder at 20 percent of the overall demand.

Original_Search_Title: Irregular Rainfall Causes Concern in Burkina
Headline: Africa: Irregular Rainfall Causes Concern in Burkina
Body_Text:
Ouagadougou, Burkina — The rainy season, which
generated hope in Burkina in July, has now given rise to
concern, due to its irregularity and uneven distribution
pattern in the country, experts have said.
August which is considered the country's wettest month,
looked rather like the beginning of the rainy season, this
year.
According to the Department of Agricultural Meteorology,
this season has been marked by poor, unevenly distributed
rainfall, blamed on "pockets of severe drought" in the
agricultural regions of the North, the Sahel, the Central
North, the Mouhoun Loop, the Gnagna and Kompienga provinces.
"Only the agricultural regions of the South-East, Comoe,
Haut Bassins, East, Central-South and Central-West have had a
rainy season ranging from average to satisfactory," the
September issue of the agro-meteorology bulletin revealed.
"The total accumulated rainfall recorded in August in the
Central North is around 60mm of water, which fell in 9 days,
against 220mm in 16 days in 1999 during the same period,"
according to the provincial director of agriculture, Alexis
Komboudri.
At the hydrological level, the filling of dams and the
flow of watercourses are still insufficient compared to what
they were during the same period in 1999.
"This situation jeopardises the hydro-agricultural
development", said an Agriculture Ministry official, Nicolas
Kabore.
According to Kabore, the last 10 days of August were
characterised by a drought gap in three-fourth of the
agricultural regions, which resulted in the slowing down of
plant development, particularly cereals.
Corn that was at 50-percent flowering stage is the most
affected.
Parasites like the whitebottle and Spanish fly have
started appearing in the Mohoun Loop where cotton is mostly
grown.
The expected yield will decline substantially compared to
what it was last season for most of the regions in the northern
part of the country.
Agriculture Minister, Issa Martin Bikienga, after a tour
of the regions experiencing a rainfall deficit, has called on
his colleagues in other ministries to help avert the danger.
In Kabore's view, there's all hope is not lost if the
rains can resume soon.
However, the agricultural season appears, to be running
smoothly in the East and South-West of the country.
Rain monitoring stations in Sindou and Banfora recorded
respectively 975mm and 1025mm in 54 days, against 862 and 900mm
in 42 days in 1999.
Cotton is at the capsuling stage, millet, corn and
sorghum at the earring stage, with cereals sold in the market
for prices that are as low as during the same period last year.

Original_Search_Title: Irin Humanitarian Briefs
Headline: East Africa: Irin Humanitarian Briefs On Horn Of Africa
Body_Text:
CONTENTS:
HORN OF AFRICA: Drought threatens 20 million people
HORN OF AFRICA: Christian aid food crisis report
ETHIOPIA: Save the Children drought emergency update
ETHIOPIA: WHO food report for Somali region
SUDAN: USAID complex emergency situation report for September
SOMALIA: USAID complex emergency/drought report for September
HORN OF AFRICA: Drought threatens 20 million people
According to CARE's 14 September drought report, the cloudless skies
over the Horn of Africa were threatening the health and wellbeing of up to
20 million people. Severe drought conditions have continued to plague Kenya,
Eritrea, Ethiopia, Somalia and Sudan, in all of which the weather was a life
and death issue. Widespread crop failure and politcal instability had
disrupted trade, and families had been robbed of their primary source of
food and income, CARE added. Many people had been reduced to eating one meal
a day and malnutrition had reached dangerous proportions, especially among
children, the elderly, and pregnant and nursing women.
CARE's response in each of the five countries was as follows: In Kenya,
CARE, was feeding the most vulnerable, repairing and constructing water
systems, and training men and women in disaster prevention activities, such
as erosion and control. In Eritrea, CARE was helping people recover from the
dual crisis of war and drought, distributing food and seeds, conducting
land-mine awareness training and demining, as well as providing care to
those displaced by the war. In Ethiopia, where 40 percent of households lack
the means to grow, buy or barter for food, CARE was working with
international partners and the Ethiopian government in providing food, seeds
and logistical assistance in the areas of greatest need. In Somalia, where
floods, droughts and poor quality seeds had limited crop yields for the past
five years, CARE was working to improve food production by distributing
high-quality, locally grown sorghum and maize seed.  In Sudan, CARE was
working extensively with the UNHCR in providing food, shelter, camp
logistics, clean water and sanitation for Eritrean refugees who had
previously fled to Sudan to escape war.
HORN OF AFRICA: Christian aid food crisis report
Christian Aid on 8 September released its food crisis report for the
Horn and East Africa. The report examined the overall current situation in
Ethiopia, Eritrea, and Kenya. In addition, it briefly discussed the peace
efforts made by the United Nations in bringing to a halt the two-year border
war between Ethiopia and Eritrea. UN Secretary-Kofi Annan recently informed
the Security Council that 19 member states from around the world were ready
to contribute troops to the United Nations Mission in Ethiopia and
Eritrea. The report also offered partners news of food aid reaching Bale
Zone in the Oromiya State, a 12-hour drive from Addis Ababa.
[To read a complete copy of this report see:
http://www.reliefweb.int/w/rwb.nsf/480fa8736b88bbc3c12564f6004c8ad5/e
db76c2a eac865e9852569580070ec71?OpenDocument]
ETHIOPIA: Save the Children drought emergency update
Given a severe drought and three consecutive crop failures, the current
crisis has caused hundreds of deaths and massive displacement in
Ethiopia. Save the Children's September report ranked Ethiopia as the
world's third poorest country, with a per capita income of US $100 in a
country of over 60 million. According to the UN, an estimated 10 million
people, including 5 million children were at risk of serious food
shortages. The report gave an overview of Save the Children's response with
regard to relief, nutrition and health, education, nutrition survey and
monitoring, water tankering and surveys, and veterinary care.>
[To read a complet copy of this report see:
http://www.reliefweb.int/w/rwb.nsf/480fa8736b88bbc3c12564f6004c8ad5/b
11de01d 85ef5f1b8525695900589928?OpenDocument]
ETHIOPIA: WHO food report for Somali State
This recent report by the WHO examined the food and nutrition situation
in Gode, Fik, Korahe, Jijiga, and Shinile zones of the Somali State in
Ethiopia. The objective of the report was to give the reader an overview of
the nutritional situation in the Somali State and the humanitarian response
made thus far. It went on to outline the major problems hindering an
effective response, and finally provided recommendations for
action. Although the Somali State, and many of the issues and recommendation
noted here were typical for this state, WHO anticipated that some would also
be relevant to programmes in other drought-affected regions such as the
SNNPR and the Amhara State. The assessment process consisted firstly of the
collation of various survey reports and assessment reports of NGOs, the
Disaster Preparedness and Prevention Committee (DPPC) and UN agencies. This
was followed by interviews with key informants such as agency directors,
medical coordinators and nutritionists, as well as field visits to Gode
Zone, Jijiga Zone and Shinile Zone.
[To read a complete copy see:
http://www.reliefweb.int/w/rwb.nsf/480fa8736b88bbc3c12564f6004c8ad5/e
db76c2a eac865e9852569580070ec71?OpenDocument]
SUDAN: USAID complex emergency situation report for September
Given the current phase of Sudan's 17 year-old civil war, USAID has in
its analysis released its 11 September Sudan complex emergency
report. According to the UN Humanitarian Coordination Unit (UNHCU), there
were an estimated 4 million IDPs in Sudan, including 2 million in greater
Khartoum and 1.2 million in the transitional zone and southern areas. The
report added that the UNHCR had broken down the amount of Sudanese refugees
according to region, including 175,000 in Uganda, 80,000 in the Democratic
Republic of Congo, 58,507 in Ethiopia, 32,000 in Kenya, 35,500 in the
Central African Republic, and 20,000 in Chad. In the meantime, Sudan hosted
refugees from other countries, including 147,302 Eritreans registered in
Sudan as of January 2000. With the flare-up of fighting between Ethiopia and
Eritrea in May, 2000, another group of Eritrean refugees, estimated to
number up to 90,000, fled to eastern Sudan: of this group, an estimated
21,000 have returned to Eritrea.
The USAID report examines the background of the conflict, the numbers
affected and the current situation. In addition, it discusses the issues of
food security, health and nutrition, the inter-ethnic peace process,
refugees returning, as well as USAID/OFDA's humanitarian assistance
totalling almost US $ 22 million.
[To read a complete copy see:
http://www.usaid.gov/hum_response/ofda/sudan_ib01_fy00.html]
SOMALIA: USAID complex emergency/drought report for September
USAID's 11 September complex/drought emergency report, examines the
complex emergency this Horn of Africa nation has been facing consecutively
for the past nine years. Since the fall of Siyad Barreh's dictatorship in
1991, Somalia has lacked a functioning and internationally recognised
national government. Kidnappings, looting, killing and continual inter-clan
warfare, as well as abnormally low rainfall for the past three years, has
made Somalia's position in terms of humanitarian aid, a difficult
one. According to the UN Coordination Unit, 750,000 people are still
vulnerable as a result of the drought in Somalia, in addition to 450,000 who
have been displaced by war. This amounts to 1,200,000 vulnerable people in
need of food assistance and other humanitarian relief. This month's report
examines the background, the numbers affected, the overall humanitarian
situation and food aid, as well as discussing USAID/OFDA's relief efforts
and assistance. >
[To read a complete copy see:

Original_Search_Title: Farming Seeds For Flood Victims
Headline: Mozambique: Farming Seeds For Flood Victims
Body_Text:
Maputo — Agricultural authorities in Mozambique are to formally start handing out
seed kits to 97,478 families affected by the February and March floods, for
the coming planting season.
Joaquim Cuna, co-ordinator of the Emergency Operations Group in the
Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development, told reporters Friday that
dozens of trucks loaded with seeds imported from South Africa and Zimbabwe
have reached the affected Mozambican provinces.
These are Maputo, Gaza, and Inhambane in the south, Sofala and Manica in
the centre, as well as the western province of Tete.
The kits would include maize, sorghum, millet, rice and ground-nut seeds
for distribution to the families.
Maputo province, where the Incomati, Umbeluzi, Movene and Maputo rivers
devastated vast areas of farmland, is to receive the largest share, 39,400
kits, while Gaza families will receive 24,474 kits, and Sofala, 12,100
kits.
Manica, Inhambane and Tete will receive 12,000, 7,900 and 700 kits,
respectively.
The river that did the most damage in Gaza was the Limpopo, while in
Sofala it was the Buzi that wreaked havoc. The Save river, that marks the
boundary between southern and central Mozambique, caused floods in four
provinces - Gaza, Inhambane, Sofala and Manica.
Apart from the seeds, the families are to receive agricultural hand
tools.
The import of seeds and tools was carried out under a contract signed by
the Mozambican government and the UN food agency, FAO, which is funding the
project.
The post-flood reconstruction needs of the agriculture sector are
estimated at about 63.3 million US dollars, including the rehabilitation of
irrigation channels and seed distribution.
About 7.9 million dollars will be needed for the purchase of seeds as
the ministry has set a target of 1.8 million tonnes of grain for the coming
agricultural years.
It is hoped that this will guarantee self-sufficiency in maize. However,
there will still be a deficit in rice.
Meanwhile, the multi-national seed giant Monsanto has offered about 267
tonnes of hybrid maize seed expected to be sent to the provinces as soon as
the necessary import documents are concluded.
According to the ministry, about 26 percent of crop land, estimated at
about 215,400 hectares, was seriously affected at the height of the
floods.
The animal husbandry sector lost about 167,000 heads of cattle, or 70
percent of the total figure, which also involved chickens and other poultry,
the ministry added.

Original_Search_Title: UN Appeals for $2m to End Food Scarcity
Headline: Uganda: UN Appeals for $2m to End Food Scarcity
Body_Text:
The United Nations (UN) is appealing for US$2,115,000 from the
international community to support efforts to end the current food
insecurity in the country.
"The UN appeal for Uganda includes an agricultural component prepared
by FAO comprising of five project proposals amounting to US$2,115,
000. The project addresses the most urgent needs of the food insecure
population in Uganda," says a FAO report from Rome.
The project will enhance the food security of 80,800 affected
families in Kasese and Kabarole districts in western Uganda through
distribution and support to the rapid multiplication of cassava
cuttings.
Ms Anne Bauer, chief of Special Relief Operations Service in Rome,
said the Swedish government had given US$230,000 to help the western
districts of Kabarole and Kasese.
"FAO, in response to the appeal for assistance to Uganda, has
received US$230,000 from the Swedish Government for the provision of
essential agricultural inputs to 6,000 drought-affected and
strife-torn farming households in Kasese and Kabarole. "Input
distribution under this project (sorghum, maize, bean and finger
millet seeds, hoes and pangas) is currently underway," Bauer said.
Officials said the provision of high yielding, mosaic free cassava
would cost about US$1,080,000.

Original_Search_Title: Botswana's Food Security Under Threat
Headline: Botswana's Food Security Under Threat
Body_Text:
Gaborone — Botswana faces a food shortfall after a poor harvest coupled with the
fact that livestock owners have shown a notable reluctance to sell their
animals to the country's abattoirs.
The low harvest has been caused by the floods, which ravaged the
southern Africa region early in the as well as quelea birds, which ate up
farmers' crops in Botswana.
So far, the troubled Botswana Agricultural Marketing Board has not been
able to purchase grain locally because of low harvests. Already indications
are that grain imports are going up.
According to the current Botswana Food Security Bulletin, compiled when
most harvesting has been completed, a nation-wide shortage of the major
cereals, sorghum, maize, and millet, has started being felt in the
country. Of these sorghum has the highest deficit.
The bulletin is based on reports from the early warning team of the
agriculture ministry, which is composed of experts from the department of
meteorological services, the nutrition unit of the family health division
and the board.
It indicates that by the end of June monitored millers in Botswana had
shipped in over 20,000 metric tonnes of maize or about 14 percent of their
import plan for the year.
Sorghum and wheat dealers had imported 0.7 percent and 19 percent
respectively of their import plans during the same month.
The country's maize stocks stood at 5,403 metric tonnes of which 4,802
tonnes were commercial stock while the board held 1,683 tonnes. Sorghum
stocks were 8,495 tonnes with the board holding 2,092 tonnes, the rest being
commercial stocks.
The ministry recently said that the vegetable harvest for the year would
only meet 20-30 percent of national requirements.
Meanwhile, the precarious food situation in Botswana is not helped by
the current crisis facing the country's beef industry.
Analysts say that the crisis, which has resulted in massive losses for
the state-owned Botswana Meat Commission threatens the country's balance of
payment security.
Statistics indicate that the number of cattle slaughtered at the
country's two major abattoirs in Francistown and Lobatse has been on a
continuous decline from June 1999 to June 2000. The figures are still
falling.
Between May and June, the Lobatse and Francistown abattoirs recorded 13
percent and 34 percent declines respectively in the number of cattle
slaughtered.
The overall effect was a 23-percent reduction in the total number of
cattle slaughtered by the meat commission.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Emergency Report No. 35 - Africa
Headline: Central Africa: WFP Emergency Report No. 35 - Africa
Body_Text:
Rome — This report includes: A) Horn of Africa: Kenya, Eritrea and
Ethiopia; B) Great Lakes Region: Tanzania, Rwanda, Uganda and
Burundi.
From Francesco Strippoli, Senior Humanitarian Adviser. Available on
the Internet on the WFP Home, Page or by e-mail from
Zlatan.Milisic@wfp.org. For information on resources, donors are
requested to contact Aleesa.Blum@wfp.org or
Marius.deGaayFortman@wfp.org at WFP Rome, telephone 39 06 6513 2004
or 06 6513 2250. Media queries should be directed to
Trevor.Rowe@wfp.org telephone 39 06 6513 2602. The address of WFP is
Via Cesare Giulio Viola 68, Parco dei Medici, 00148 Rome, Italy.
PART I - HIGHLIGHTS (Details below in Part II)
A) Horn of Africa
1) Kenya: malnutrition increasing; 25-30 percent of children in five
worst hit districts malnourished; food stocks rapidly decreasing; WFP
rations reduced due to lack of adequate funding; rations not
decreased in six worst affected districts; total WFP food shortfall
until end of year 35,000 tons; WFP beneficiary number increased by
210,000 people, to 3.5 million; over one million are children
targeted through emergency school feeding; no rains anticipated in
pastoral districts until October; increasing livestock mortality;
harvest reductions by 20-30 percent expected for beans and maize;
5,000 Turkana people drove their cattle to Uganda.
2) Eritrea: 2,098 tons of WFP food distributed to 103,000 war and
drought- affected; monthly total for August - 9,357 tons distributed
to 388,737 war affected in Debub, Gash Barka and Northern Red Sea and
50,780 drought affected in Anseba and Northern Red Sea; crop
performance assessment and post distribution monitoring carried out
in Afabet, Kamchewa, Felket and Northern Red Sea; late rainfall
delayed cultivation and planting; majority of farmers had no seeds;
ten percent of cultivable land not cultivated; good rains recorded in
many parts of country; emergency food aid, kinship support and
minimal trade in livestock products main food sources.
3) Ethiopia: UN released appeal for assistance to IDPs, seeking USD
30.5 million for period September to January; USD 15.1 million for
food and USD 15.4 for non-food; Government-led Meher assessment in
Tigray, Amhara, Oromiya and SNNPR regions, WFP participating; WFP/IOM
population movement tracking and food security monitoring underway;
data being completed and analysed for Somali region; Government
requested assistance for 63,000 Ethiopians returning from Eritrea;
budget revision of WFP EMOP under preparation; beneficiary increase
from 272,000 to 352,500 expected; WFP, UNHCR and Government
discussing food assistance to Eritrean refugees in Northern Tigray;
three month rations allocated for immediate needs; 80,752 tons of WFP
food to be dispatched from Djibouti during August, daily average of
2,600 tons; 140 short-haul trucks being imported by WFP for food
deliveries in Ethiopia; WFP sub office to be established in Gode to
strengthen logistics and food monitoring.
B) Great Lakes
1) Tanzania: assessment in June indicated likely food shortages in 32
districts of ten regions; drought prone regions experiencing crop
failures for fourth year; traditional coping mechanisms almost
exhausted; most affected Arusha, Shinyanga, Dodoma, Singida,
Morogoro, Kilimanjaro, Tanga, Mwanza, Mara and Coast; Government
conducting rapid assessment to determine number of households in need
and most appropriate intervention modality; WFP expecting Government
appeal after mission.
2) Rwanda: WFP operations for drought affected continue mainly in
Kibungo, Gitarama and Kigali Rural; 5,892 tons of food aid to be
distributed to 74,869 drought-affected households, 374,345 persons,
between January and August.
3) Uganda: Karamoja region continues facing drought related food
shortages, compounded by insecurity; WFP assisting 160,000 people
since March; 7,065 tons of food distributed so far, against target of
9,069 tons; new donor contributions needed; WFP/Government/NGO rapid
assessment found increased number of 270,000 people in need of
relief; incoming harvest in September expected to be poor; relief
likely to be needed until March; WFP preparing expansion of drought
relief operation.
4) Burundi: more than 110,000 people benefited from 623 tons of WFP
food, mainly drought affected in Muyinga and Kirundo and regrouped in
Bujumbura Rural; household food economy assessments conducted in
Kayanza and Karusi and three sites in Muhuta (Bujumbura Rural), where
all five regroupment sites were dismantled; assessments established
food aid needs; insecurity continued in Cankuzo, Ruyigi, Bubanza,
Bujumbura Rural, Makamba, Rutana and Karusi provinces and Bujumbura
town
PART II - DETAILS
A) Horn of Africa
1) Kenya - information as of 27 August
a) Malnutrition among children under five is increasing at an
alarming rate. Nutritional survey conducted by UNICEF in five
districts worst hit by the drought revealed that 25 to 30 percent of
children are suffering from malnutrition. This comes at the time when
food stocks across the country are rapidly decreasing and WFP relief
rations have been reduced, as funds have not been secured to meet all
planned requirements. The food aid rations have been generally
reduced by 30 percent, so some districts that previously received 50
percent rations are now receiving 35 percent only. The rations have
not been decreased in the six worst affected districts of Turkana,
Marsabit, Moyale, Mandera, Wajir and Samburu. Total food shortfall
for the WFP drought relief operation, until the end of the year, is
35,000 tons.
b) Beneficiary caseload for WFP emergency operation in nineteen
districts of Kenya has risen by 210,000 people, to 3.5 million, due
to the worsening drought. Around 1,074,300 of the beneficiaries are
children, targeted through emergency school feeding programme.
c) The Arid Lands Resource Management Project has reported that
several divisions of Turkana, Wajir, Marsabit and Moyale have entered
the emergency warning phase. A vulnerability update from the Famine
Early Warning System Network (USAID) indicates that no further rains
are anticipated in the pastoral districts until October. Increasing
livestock mortality has been reported across pastoral districts. It
is estimated that the harvest will be reduced by 30 percent for maize
and 20 percent for beans.
d) Reports were received that about 5,000 Turkana people have been
forced to drive their cattle into Uganda as a result of the drought.
2) Eritrea - information as of 30 August
a) A total of 2,098 tons of WFP food was distributed during the week
to 95,221 war-affected persons in Debub and Gash Barka regions and
7,768 drought- affected persons in Anseba Region. The cumulative
figure for the month stands at 9,357 tons for 388,737 war affected in
Debub, Gash Barka and Mekete camp in Northern Red Sea Region and
50,780 drought affected persons in Anseba and Northern Red Sea
Regions.
b) WFP monitors in Keren carried out crop performance assessment and
post distribution monitoring in Afabet, Kamchewa, Felket and Mekete
camp in Northern Red Sea Regions. The information obtained indicates
that late rainfall this year delayed cultivation and planting. The
team also reported that majority of the farmers had no seeds stocks
and relied on supplies from the Government and FAO. Sorghum, pearl
millet, barley, groundnuts, maize and wheat are main crops grown this
season. It is estimated that about ten percent of cultivable land has
not been cultivated.
c) Wet weather conditions were recorded in many parts of the country.
The spatial and temporal distribution of rainfall was fairly good.
d) Post distribution monitoring reports indicate that emergency food
aid, kinship support and minimal trade in livestock products are main
sources of coping mechanism in Anseba and the Northern Red Sea
Regions.
3) Ethiopia - information as of 31 August
a) On 24 August, the UN released an appeal for "Rehabilitation and
Recovery Programmes for Internally Displaced Persons in Ethiopia".
Donor contributions are being sought for USD 30.5 million, for the
period September to January. Some USD 15.1 million represents food
needs and related costs and USD 15.4 represents interventions in
agriculture, health, nutrition, water and sanitation, emergency
education, humanitarian mine action, and HIV/AIDS sectors.
b) A Government (DPPC) led mid-season Meher assessment started on 27
August. WFP is participating in all four missions travelling to
specific areas of Tigray, Amhara, Oromiya and SNNPR regions. An
assessment is usually not undertaken at this time of the year, but
given the failure of the belg rains and reported irregular rainfall
during the Meher season, it was considered appropriate to carry out
the assessment at this time.
c) Joint WFP/IOM "Population Movement Tracking and Food Security
Monitoring" initiative is underway. Around 20 WFP monitors and NGO
staff have been trained on methodologies of data collection.
Questionnaires are currently being completed in Somali region and the
collected data analysed. This initiative might be extended to other
parts of the Horn of Africa Region.
d) The Government (DPPC) sent a request on 18 August to donors and UN
Agencies, for assistance to over 63,000 Ethiopians returning from
Eritrea. There is general agreement that this figure is an accurate
estimate of those who have already returned and those expected to
return in the coming weeks. A budget revision of the WFP EMOP 6080.01
is under preparation with an expected increase in the number of
beneficiaries from 272,000 to 352,500.
e) Discussions are underway between WFP, UNHCR and relevant
government agencies to provide food assistance to Eritrean refugees
in Northern Tigray. WFP has reserved a three month ration to meet the
immediate needs. Transport of food commodities will start as soon as
the partners agree on the modalities of the operation.
f) Around 80,752 tons of WFP commodities will be dispatched from
Djibouti Port for the Ethiopia operations during August, at a daily
average rate of over 2,600 tons.
g) In mid August, 140 short-haul trucks imported by WFP arrived at
Djibouti Port and are awaiting finalisation of customs procedures.
The trucks will be used for food deliveries within Ethiopia. Seven
moveable storage units have been delivered to Gode town and will be
erected in early September to be used for intermediate storage of
food A WFP sub office will be established in this area to strengthen
logistics and food monitoring activities.
D) Great Lakes- information as of 30 August
1) Tanzania
a) Rapid Food and Crop Assessment carried out in June, indicated that
32 districts in ten regions are likely to face food shortages during
the later part of the year and up to the next harvests. Drought prone
regions in Tanzania are experiencing crop failures for the fourth
consecutive year and the traditional coping mechanisms have been
almost completely exhausted. The most affected regions are Arusha,
Shinyanga, Dodoma, Singida, Morogoro, Kilimanjaro, Tanga, Mwanza,
Mara and Coast.
b) The Government is currently conducting a Rapid Needs Assessment in
the affected areas to determine the number of households in need of
food aid and the most appropriate modality of relief intervention.
The mission will also look at logistics and management issues. WFP is
expecting a Government appeal after this mission.
2) Rwanda
a) WFP relief operations in support of vulnerable families affected
by drought continue, mainly in Kibungo, Gitarama and Kigali Rural
prefectures, in central and eastern parts of the country.
b) WFP has scheduled food distributions of 2,518 tons of food in
Kigali and Gitarama prefectures during the month, to benefit 160,000
persons. Once these distributions are completed, some 5,892 tons of
food aid will have been distributed by WFP to 74,869 drought-affected
households, 374,345 persons, since the beginning of the year.
3) Uganda
a) Large parts of Karamoja region in north-eastern Uganda continue to
face food shortages as a result of adverse climatic conditions,
compounded by insecurity. WFP has been providing food aid to drought
affected people in Kotido and Moroto districts since March, targeting
160,000 vulnerable persons. So far, a total of 7,065 tons of food
have been provided by WFP, against the estimated need until September
of 9,069 tons. Additional donor contributions are needed to cover the
shortfall.
b) A recent joint WFP/Government/NGO rapid food assessment indicated
that the number of people affected and in need of relief has
increased to 270,000. Incoming harvest in September is expected to be
poor and relief likely to be needed until March next year. WFP is
currently preparing an expansion of the current drought relief
operation to meet food aid needs until that period.
4) Burundi
a) During the past two weeks more than 110,000 people benefited from
over 623 tons of food aid provided by WFP, including victims of
drought in Muyinga and Kirundo provinces and regrouped populations in
Bujumbura Rural Province.
b) Household food economy assessments were conducted in two sites
hosting more than 2,600 people in Kayanza and Karusi provinces.
Assessments were also carried out in three sites in Muhuta Commune
(Bujumbura Rural), following the dismantling of all five regroupment
sites in the commune. Both assessments established a need for food
aid distributions. Some of the assessment missions planned were
cancelled due to security reasons.
c) Insecurity continued in Cankuzo, Ruyigi, Bubanza and Bujumbura
Rural provinces with many human casualties. Rebel incursions and
attacks were also reported in Makamba, Rutana and Karusi provinces
and Bujumbura town
Note: All tonnage figures in this report refer to metric tons
(End WFP Emergency Report No. 33 / 2000 of 18 August 2000)

Original_Search_Title: Agricultural Production to Drop By 43 Percent
Headline: Zimbabwe: Agricultural Production to Drop By 43 Percent
Body_Text:
Harare — The compulsory acquisition of 3 270 farms over the next
three to four years could see commercial agricultural production
decline by 43 percent from $45 billion to $25.3 billion by 2003.
The Commercial Farmers Union says production of tobacco will drop by
36 percent, horticulture by half and beef by 28 percent. Sugar and
tea production will not be affected but coffee production will more
than halve.
But there will also be some gains. Cotton production will rise by
almost 75 percent.
There will also be huge increases in sorghum, sunflowers and
groundnuts, because these are not produced by commercial farmers.
Analysts say the decline in agricultural production, which accounts
for 12 percent of Gross Domestic Product will knock of 3 percent of
GDP. The CFU says farm exports will drop by 25 percent while exports
of tobacco will decline by 37 percent.
Some 150 000 farm workers are also likely to lose their jobs.

Original_Search_Title: "Neem - a Credible Village Industry!"
Headline: Ghana: "Neem - a Credible Village Industry!"
Body_Text:
Accra — Azadirachta indica (neem tree) belongs to the mahogany
family, the meliaceae. The exact origin is not known but is believed
to have been introduced to Ghana between 1919 and 1927 from India.
Known as "kingtsho", "dua gyare" or "liliti" by the Ga, Ashanti and
Ewe respectively, it is now widespread in the warm climates of
Africa, Asia, the Caribbean and Americas.
It is an evergreen, drought-resistant tree which has been
successfully used as a shade-tree in the re-afforestation efforts in
the northern sector of Ghana to arrest the advance of the Sahara.
In other parts of West and East Africa such as Mauritania and
Somalia, the plant is considered the number one candidate for
checking the rapid southward advancement of the Sahara. It grows well
in any part f the country, irrespective of climate or soil type and
does well in very dry regions like the Sahara desert, where it is
highly valuable for its shade and as a source of fuel.
Extracts from various parts of the tree have been found to exhibit
medicinal values and potencies in relieving pains, fevers and other
infections. Neem products are widely used in Asia and Africa for
treatment of various ailments especially malaria, and as
anti-bacterial preparations, fungicides and insecticides. These
valuable properties earned it the envious name, "the Village
Pharmacy".
The leaves are very rich in potash and phosphate hence when used as
manure, help to reduce acidity of the soil as well as supply it with
very useful nutrients. The dry leaves serve as excellent mulch
materials while the green leaves are perfect nutritious cattle-feed.
Even though it sometimes warps and splits easily, the timber is
insect- resistant and sometimes used for furniture, building and
constructing farm implements like ploughs, carts and wheel barrow
handles. An indispensable source of fuel wood in rural Ghana, where
it grows wild, the small twigs are used by small-scale bakers to
maintain a consistent temperature in the traditional ovens especially
after the first lot of bread has been baked and the oven is being
prepared for the introduction of the next batch. The young stems of
the plant are used for making 'heavy-duty' baskets for transporting
processed fish and farm products to the market.
Widely used as a chewing stick for oral hygiene and defense against
malaria by rural folks, it is very bitter but leaves a sweet after-
taste and a feeling of freshness in the mouth. Birds eat the sweet
fruit-pulp as their main diet and are largely responsible for the
rapid spread of the plant especially in the Accra plains. Because of
this spread, some environmentalists regard neem as a 'dangerous pest'
that is likely to take-over large tracts of farmlands.
One important use of the plant is as a pesticide for both pre and
post harvest crop protection. Entomologists have found that neem
products are effective against more than 200 insect species as well
as some mites, fungi, bacteria, nematodes and even a few viruses
including plant viruses. In Ghana and Togo, it is used against
cabbage pest and most pests that attack vegetables and fruit crops.
Past generations effectively used it extensively in protecting cocoa
beans from pests and diseases including Ephestia cautella, which is
now being controlled with expensive synthetic fungicides and
pesticides. The seed extract, which contains about 42% oil, is active
even against locusts. The leaves or seeds, crushed and steeped in
water, alcohol or other solvents are astonishingly effective against
plant pests. The concentration is believed to contain four major and
twenty minor active ingredients. In The Gambia, trials have shown
that it is as active as malathion, and in Nigeria, it is comparable
in effectiveness to Dieldrin and DDT. The seeds are ground, one
calabashful mixed with a bucket of water and simply sprinkled on
vegetable, maize and sorghum farms, where it is said to be very
effective in preventing insects and other pests from attacking the
crops. The use of seed oil for the treatment of hair lice
demonstrates both insecticidal and medicinal properties.
In his book, "African Herbalism, a Mine of Wealth" published in 1934,
J.A. Kwesi Aaba reported that the bark is used as a cathartic,
vermifuge, anthelmintic and anti-bilous. As an enema, the decoction
makes a good clyster for waist and bowel complaints. The leaves are
again used for the treatment of boils, ringworm, in steam baths for
fever and even used to treat wounds that do not seem to respond
readily to more conventional treatment. In Northern Ghana and Burkina
Faso, the seed oil is used in manufacturing soaps and cosmetics which
act as anticeptic to the skin and scalp. It is interesting to note
that in the U.S.A., aqueous neem leaf extracts have shown a degree of
inhibition of the viral DNA polymerase of the hepatitis B virus,
which is one of the major causes of jaundice in Ghana.
Virtually every child who has lived in urban or rural Ghana can
easily recollect the days when grandma boiled a large bundle of neem
leaves and made the malaria patient "sweat-out" the fever under a
collection of heavy blankets or clothes over the steam-bath. No
wonder, researchers have established credible scientific basis for
its use in treating malaria. However while research into neem is
intensified in most parts of Asia, the same cannot be said of the
West African sub- region where the tree occupies virtually all known
ecological zones.
As Ghana's economy continues to go through its worst recession, the
Ghanaian youth must be armed with the fact that, "the battles of life
are fought on scientific lines, so that the race that is able to
produce the highest, sustainable and most beneficial scientific
development, is the race that shall ultimately enjoy eternal glory!"

Original_Search_Title: Poverty Targets May Not Be Met
Headline: Africa: Poverty Targets May Not Be Met
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Sub-Saharan African countries have little chance of meeting
the international target of halving the numbers of people living in
absolute poverty by 2015.
A new report by the Overseas Development Institution (ODI) - a
London-based research organisation specialising in the developing
world - said that with pro- poor, high growth rate policies, most
other regions of the world should reach the target.
But Africa would need to substantially increase its current average
per capita growth rate, although some individual countries including
Uganda could yet reach the target.
ODI said countries with substantial income differences between the
richest five per cent and the poorest 20 are least likely to achieve
the target, and stressed that "poverty reduction depends on the
economic conditions that generate pro-poor growth."
Key issues remain the performance of the agricultural sectors, open
economies and increasing investment levels.
Of equal importance is providing credit to the poor, together with
the development of Green Revolution-type higher yields for crops such
as maize, sorghum and cassava.
Land reform is also a vital part of enabling poorer people coupled
with improving basic education, health and skills of poorer people.
More girls need to be educated in school in order to enable them both
to increase their productivity through access to better levels of
technology and also to reduce fertility rates among the young.
The state should also lessen its "bureaucratic interference" with
small-scale activities in the informal sector, the report says.

Original_Search_Title: Awassa And the Road to Moyale Part II
Headline: Ethiopia: Awassa And the Road to Moyale Part II
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — Last week we went from Awassa to the Shabe Rock
Carvings at Dilla. This week we continue on Tuesday, further south.
Tuttufalla and Tootitti:
Past Dilla there are two large monument fields. The first one is
Tuttufalla, 25 km south of Dilla on the left hand side down a gravel
road. The locals tell you where it is. You go down the gravel road
about 2 km, then take a dirt road about 1 km up a hill to the
monuments.
Tuffufalla is a nice field. It has been worked on by the same French
archaeological team, which reconstructed the Tiya field south of
Addis. Around 1994, they set up the 70 or so stelae in a loose rock
foundation.
The stelae are really interesting - with a greater variety of designs
than either the Tiya or the Tootitti fields. They have a spider-like
carving, which is the only carving they have at Tootitti, and a
pillow stand design like in Tiya. Tuttufalla is therefore a link
between the other two sites. The stelae are smaller and shorter than
in the other two fields.
The unique feature of Tuttufalla is the faces carved on some of the
stones. These are fascinating. There is one stone with a long face
with an aquiline nose, very nicely done.
The stories of the locals about the stones are interesting. They
associate them with Gran Mohammed, the Muslim invader from Harar who
conquered the area in the early 1500s. One local informant said he
didn't know if Gran had them carved, but he had taken the pillars
with his powerful left hand and driven them into the ground. He'd
then used them to tether his horses.
Although the locals dated the stelae at around' 3000 years old or
so', they seem to be associated with Tiya which has been dated to
around 700 years ago. Who did them is a mystery. They are called the
' Sidamo monuments in the Southern People s' Region, but the local
Sidamo people have very little knowledge about them. With the number
of invasions which have gone though there over the centuries, it's
very had to know who was there back then.
Another 10 km south of the turnoff to Tuttufalla is the twin stelae
field of Tootitti. The turnoff to the left is at a little place
called Chelba. It is easy to miss the little dirt road up to the
monoliths - my first time I drove straight past. On the second try I
found it, and we drove the rough 3 kilometers up to the top of the
hill to Tootitti.
The stones appear suddenly on the left. Some are standing but most
have fallen over. The tallest ones tower over the sorghum in the
field. They stand 2- 4 meters high. At first we counted 56 in the
bunch in front of the local administration office - I thought that
was all of them. But the field went on and on, and I lost track of
the number after almost 200. The monoliths vary in size and shape,
but most are cylindrical. Most of the ones lying on he ground are
broken. The ones standing seem to indicate regular shapes and walls.
A-tell tale ridge worn in the top of one of the stallest standing
monoliths seems to indicate that it was a pillar with a roof and/or
walls attached to it.
All of this is speculation of course. Again, I wasn't able to find
out from anyone local the age or reason or builders of the monoliths.
The only marking on the Tootitti monoliths I could see were carved
spiders with long legs - which don't resemble the swords, trees and
circles of the Tiya monuments, but were also in evidence a
Tuttufalla.
Borena and the Road to Kenya:
About 150 km south of Awassa you begin the long descent from the lush
highlands to the barren lowlands of Borena. One of the first signs is
the appearance of camels. Camels simply cannot survive forlon in
cooler climates, they get sick and die, so they area a very good
indicator of lowlands.
Banana trees are replaced by low acacias, the dryland tree of Africa.
The landscape turns to classical Africa Savannah, with the flat
topped accia standing out above a vast grassland. The soil is iron
red, and formed into phallic ant hills in an astonishing variety of
shapes.
The people change too. The highland Sidamo give way to the lowland
Borena Oromo. While highland people have generally adopted casual
western dress, the Borena women retain a different style. They wear
colorful shawls and dresses, falling fetchingly off their shoulders.
The men wear western-style clothes, but they carry spears. My clever
brother- in law-Dennis, who visited us, observed that men in the
north seemed to be born with sticks in their hands, and men in the
south with plastic bags. These guys must have been genetically
disposed to spears.
There is an interesting tale about the Borena and their spears. It is
said that a Borena woman, even a married one, can have a roll in the
hay with any man she chooses. The man drives his spear into the
ground in front of the hut before he goes in, and even the husband is
not allowed to intrude so long as the spear is there.
The Borena are also famous for their strong caste system based on age
groups. Groups move though stages of life and responsibility
together, moving from one stage to another as they age. The Borna and
Guji Oromo are the least Westernized and have the least contact with
Amhara people, so they retain traditions which may have been general
with Oromos but have been discontinued with most.
There are very few towns on the road. For miles there is nothing but
trees and antihills. Occasionally you see livestock and people, but
must of the time is seems like empty wilderness.
Next week the series concludes with the trip to Kenya.
Send your comments or questions to John Graham at
jgraham@telecom.net.et

Original_Search_Title: Quelea Birds Threaten Harvest
Headline: Kenya: Quelea Birds Threaten Harvest
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A flock of quelea birds, which can cause extensive damage
to wheat fields, has been sighted in Nakuru. About 50,000 birds were
sighted in Bahati division of Nakuru District, and farmers said
Ministry of Agriculture personnel were helping to control the birds.
Bahati is one of the few parts of the district that have received
ample rainfall and farmers are expecting reasonable maize and wheat
yields.
The wheat crop is now at a tender stage, where it is mainly attacked
by the birds.
A single quelea bird eats between three to four grammes of wheat a
day and causes the loss of another six grammes which fall down during
feeding.
The quelea move in large flocks, sometimes comprising a million
birds, causing great damage to wheat, barley, millet and sorghum
plantations.
The biggest invasion of quelea birds on wheat plantations in the
province was in 1998 when 25 million birds attacked the wheat crop.
Last year, a population of about six million quelea birds attacked
the crop in various parts of the Nakuru. This was, however, a smaller
flock compared with 1998 when more than 11 million birds migrated to
the district.
Farmers in Nakuru District produced 172,000 bags of wheat last year
compared to 384,500 bags in the previous year and 554,201 in 1997.
The quelea birds breed near Lake Magadi and the Tsavo National Park
and migrate to the growing areas when wheat starts to mature.
Many parts of Nakuru district including Njoro, Rongai, Naivasha and
Lower Subukia have not yet received adequate rainfall and some
farmers are still replanting maize on a trial basis.
Maize planting in Nakuru District starts in March and ends in May but
most of the crop planted between March and May wilted due to lack of
adequate moisture.
A farmer in Rongai said that the area was witnessing the worst
drought since 1944 when the area received only 20 inches of rainfall.
Rongai and its neighbourhood has received only eight inches of
rainfall this year.
The maize crop in areas which received adequate rainfall during the
germination state is tassling at a very low height and yields are
expected to be far below the normal yields.
Farmers in Nakuru district harvested about 11 bags of maize per
hectare last year compared to 25 bags per hectare in 1998.

Original_Search_Title: South African Investment Sought By Nigerian States
Headline: South Africa: Nigerian States Seek South African Investment
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Nigeria's northwestern states are calling for SA
investment and joint ventures in agriculture, mining, tourism and
construction sectors in a bid to attract billions of dollars worth of
foreign direct investment into the country each year.
At a briefing organised by the Johannesburg Metropolitan Chamber of
Commerce and Industry yesterday, Kebbi state executive governor
Muhammed Adamu said the three states Sokoto, Kebbi and Zamfara were
battling with economic development despite being the most peaceful
regions in Nigeria.
He said despite the abundance of mineral resources in the three
states, there was very little mining done in the region. Minerals
found in the region include gold, limestone, kaolin and gypsum.
Adamu said the three states relied heavily on agriculture as a source
of employment and revenue. Agricultural products found include maize,
sugar cane, sorghum, rice, wheat, onion, tomatoes, cotton and garlic.
Livestock found in this region are sheep, cattle, goats, horses and
camels.
Adamu said there were also few educational institutions and these
would need to be further developed with the help of foreign investors
and educators.

Original_Search_Title: "There Are Not Enough Crop, Food And Income"
Headline: Rwanda: "There Are Not Enough Crop, Food And Income"
Body_Text:
Geneva — A pastoralist is leading his cattle through the country in
search of pasture and water. Enoch Katabarwa's two boys help him keep
the small herd together. The animals with their fearsome horns look
emaciated. The land doesn't yield enough grass for the cattle,
because of a long lasting drought.
The northeast is the hottest part of Rwanda. The sun parches the land
in Umutara prefecture. The distance between the few waterholes is
very far.
"The draught started in May and there will be no rain till October",
Enoch Katabarwa explains. Every day the 49 year old man makes his way
from Bihinga-village to a well near Kabarore, 30 kilometers back and
forth, that means some 12 hours walking with the cattle.
"Some of the cattle herders already shifted to other areas", Enoch
says. "Kids even leave school to look after the cattle."
Travelling through the Umutara region one meets pastoralists with
their browsing herds all over the area. The Northeast is cattle land.
"The huge number of cattle eats off all the plants transforming the
savanna step by step into a desert", a relief worker from the
Lutheran World Federation (LWF) says. The LWF, a member of Action by
Churches Together (ACT), has constructed 65 wells in the area. This
still doesn't meet the needs of the people. LWF has also distributed
drought resistent seeds such as sorghum and helped the villagers in
constructing trenches around their fields to protect them against
wild boar eating the crop.
The drought is the main problem people are facing in the region
bordering Tanzania In Umutara prefecture the civil war started in the
Nineties. Many people fled the fighting. After the rebels took over
government in 1994, the refugees returned from camps in Tanzania and
Uganda. Enoch Katabarwa came back in 1996.
When the refugees arrived in Umutara they scattered all over the
area. The government encoureged them to live in villages because then
its easier to provide schools and especially water for the people.
LWF in partnership with UNHCR build five resettlements, each
comprising between 100 and 440 houses.
83,000 families or around 500,000 people live in Umutara prefecture,
30,000 of them are returnees. LWF also constructed 11 schools and
rehabilitated 3, many families were supplied with seeds and were
supported to construct rain water tanks. But these tanks have now
been empty for a long time.
In the resettlement villages the children show the swollen bellies
caused by malnutrition related to the general lack of food. Although
there are irrigation facilities the soil is dry because the water is
too scarce for irrigating the land. "Only 20% of the trees grow when
we plant them", two elder villagers say.
The area is generally very dry. But this year the prolonged drought
has brought hunger close to the village. There are not enough crop,
food and subsequently not enough income Â», a young men in one of
another settlements points out.
