The urgent need of water has changed the life of the villagers. They
have to walk 10 kilometers to the river to get water. We don't have
time to do any other work than fetching water Â», the young man says.
"Still they are trying to survive on their own now", the LWF relief
worker says. A further difficulty is that much of their lifestock
died during the war and they have no money to restock it. The two
elder villagers, returnees from Uganda, express their cocerns about
the situation. They fear the lack of rain and the wild animals eating
the crop will force the villagers to depend on relief.
The situation could turn as bad as in some southern areas of the
country. Famine there has claimed the lives of over 400 people since
last September, according to official sources in the area. During the
last three years there have been no harvests in the Bugesera region
and as a result many families have left the area for other parts of
Rwanda, the German news agency dpa reports.
Drought and famine has gripped most of the countries in the region.
Some 115 people in Karamoja in the northeastern region of Uganda have
reportedly died of hunger since June. An estimated 600,000 to700,000
people are believed to be affected by drought in Burundi, according
to the UN Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO). Some parts of
Tanzania are facing acute food shortages due to the drought
experienced during the last 1999/2000 rainy season and Kenya is
suffering severly from drought.
For further information please contact: Nils Carstensen (mobile ++ 41
79 358 3171). ACT Web Site address: http://www.act-intl.org
ACT is a worldwide network of churches and related agencies meeting
human need through coordinated emergency response. The ACT
Coordinating Office is based with the World Council of Churches (WCC)
and the Lutheran World Federation (LWF) in Switzerland.

Original_Search_Title: World Food Program Report
Headline: East Africa: WFP Emergency Report No. 31 - Africa
Body_Text:
Rome — This report includes: A) UN CAP/Mid Term Review; B) Horn of
Africa: Ethiopia-Eritrea Relations, Ethiopia, Djibouti, Kenya,
Somalia; C) Great Lakes: Burundi, Tanzania.
From Francesco Strippoli, Senior Humanitarian Adviser. Available on
the Internet on the WFP Home, Page or by e-mail from
Zlatan.Milisic@wfp.org. For information on resources, donors are
requested to contact Aleesa.Blum@wfp.org or
Marius.deGaayFortman@wfp.org at WFP Rome, telephone 39 06 6513 2004
or 06 6513 2250. Media queries should be directed to
Trevor.Rowe@wfp.org telephone 39 06 6513 2602. The address of WFP is
Via Cesare Giulio Viola 68, Parco dei Medici, 00148 Rome, Italy.
PART I - HIGHLIGHTS (Details below in Part II)
A) UN CAP Mid Term Review - information as of 2 August
a) WFP and FAO held in Rome joint meeting and consultation with
representatives of donor and recipient countries on CAP MTR; OCHA
also attended the consultation. b) WFP/FAO collaboration successful
and increasing; CAP important as humanitarian strategy and
inter-agency coordination mechanism, not only for fund-raising;
urgency of additional funding for ongoing operations emphasised. c)
Drought operations in Horn of Africa and western and central Asia not
included in MTR; additional funding needed there as well.
B) Horn of Africa: Ethiopia-Eritrea, Ethiopia, Djibouti, Kenya,
Somalia
1) Ethiopia-Eritrea Relations - information as of 3 August
a) UN Security Council approved establishment of 100 member strong UN
observer mission; liaison officers assigned to Addis Ababa and
Asmara.
2. Ethiopia - information as of 3 August
a) Pledge for 85 percent of cereals requirement and 67 percent of oil
requirements under the recent budget revision received (total of
179,100 tons of food). b) Displacement in border areas still evident,
with reported increased population movements. c) Deteriorating
situation reported in primarily pastoral region of Afar, due to
drought. d) Nutritional surveys conducted in Sudanese and Somali
refugee camps; situation generally improving; deterioration reported
in some camps; e) Nutritional surveys conducted in Borena, Bale
(Oromiya) and KAT Zone (SNNPR); Situation improving in Bale, but
deteriorating in the others; WFP discussing with NGOs distribution of
supplementary rations f) Helicopter missions started in Somali
region;
3) Djibouti - information as of 3 August
a) Port upgrading operation not affecting WFP logistics; 103,000 tons
o food moved during July.
4) Kenya - information as of 1 August
a) Power and water cuts affecting increasing area; some rains
reported. b) Food aid requirement 30,000 tons per month from August
to December; food pipeline problems persist. c) Special operation
aimed at road repairs in support of the EMOP is in final stages.
5) Somalia - information as of 1 August
a) Assessment missions to Awdal, Togdheer and Sanaag regions of
Somaliland carried out; b) Drought affecting 26,000 people in Awdal;
water access reduced; livestock conditions poor, with reported
mortality among small animals. c) In Togdheer, 10-12,000 people
affected by drought; Gu floods reported in Sanaag. d) Good harvest
expected in southern Somalia e) Some 7,250 tons of food aid
distributed in country during 2000, most in the south; greater
emphasis given to FFW.
C) Great Lakes: Burundi and Tanzania
1) Burundi - information as of 1 August
a) Joint FAO/WFP Crop and Food Supply Assessment Mission report
released; harvest expected at 3 percent below the last year's
production; substantial deficits expected. b) Food economy
assessments starting in Bugesera region, reportedly affected by
drought. Comparative findings for Rwanda and Burundi expected.
2) Tanzania - information as of 1 August
a) First distribution with reduced food ration (by 40 percent)
implemented in refugee camps; no problems reported; market prices
increased; b) Rapid assessment mission identifies 32 districts
affected by drought; predicts critical food shortages for some areas
until next harvest. c) Government requested WFP assistance in
launching a needs assessment mission.
PART II - DETAILS
A) UN CAP Mid Term Review - information as of 2 August
a) Following the UN Consolidated Appeal Process (CAP) Mid Term Review
(MTR) Meeting in Geneva on 26 July, WFP and FAO hosted in Rome
jointly a consultation meeting on the same subject with the
representatives of donor and recipient governments, on 1 August. The
meeting was chaired by the WFP Executive Director and the FAO Head of
Emergency Operations. The meeting was also attended by the OCHA
Director of the Geneva Office, who chaired the MTR Meeting in Geneva.
b) WFP and FAO informed the government representatives on their
ongoing projects that have been reviewed within the CAP MTR and
presented operational and funding updates for these projects. The
increasing collaboration between WFP and FAO in a number of
countries, especially through joint assessment missions and
implementation of projects aimed at agricultural recovery and
increased food production, was particularly emphasised. The
importance of the CAP for the development of humanitarian strategy
and as the inter-agency coordination mechanism, in addition to its
fund-raising role, was also stressed.
c) The government representatives present were informed that the
response of the donors to the WFP and FAO parts of the UN CAPs has
been under expectation in almost all countries and that several
current operations are facing major food aid supply problems if new
contributions are not received promptly. WFP operations in Angola,
Republic of Congo and Tanzania require urgent donor response.
d) The CAP MTR process did not include some of the largest WFP
operations implemented at present, such as the drought relief
projects in Horn of Africa and western and central Asia, which
started later in the year and were not reviewed at this stage. WFP
informed the donors that the funding needs for these operations need
to be addressed as well.
B) Horn of Africa: Ethiopia-Eritrea, Ethiopia, Djibouti, Kenya,
Somalia
1) Ethiopia-Eritrea Relations - information as of 3 August
a) The UN Security Council approved establishment of a 100 member UN
observer mission, which is expected to prepare grounds for a possible
peacekeeping operation along the border between Ethiopia and Eritrea.
It is expected that the eventual peacekeeping force will consist of
2000 to 4000 troops. Two liaison officers for the mission have been
assigned to Addis Ababa and two to Asmara.
2. Ethiopia - information as of 3 August
a) Following the approval of the budget revision for the emergency
operation for the drought affected population, on 21 July, a pledge
of 175,000 tons of cereals and 4,100 tons of vegetable oil has been
received from USA. This represents 85 percent of the cereals and 67
percent of the vegetable oil requirements under the budget revision.
Discussions are currently underway with the EU and Italy regarding
possible additional contributions.
b) Following the cease-fire agreement between Ethiopia and Eritrea,
population movements have increased around the border area, with many
IDPs returning to their places of origin and Ethiopian nationals
residing in Eritrea moving to transit camps in Ethiopia. Presence of
refugee groups and internally displaced, who were the result of the
conflict, is still apparent. WFP will join the planned UN/Government
mission to border areas, to review reports of increasing numbers of
refugees.
c) The failure of two short rainy seasons in Afar region, followed by
a delay in the commencement of the long rainy season by at least 3
weeks, has led to concerns about the deteriorating situation in the
primarily pastoral region of Afar. There are reports of decreased
availability of potable water and cattle deaths due to shortage of
water and pasture. WFP is working closely with DPPC to closely
monitor the situation and ensure that food distributions in areas
most in need take place.
d) A joint WFP/UNHCR/ARRA nutrition survey to review the blanket
feeding project was conducted in July in two Sudanese refugee camps
and five Somali refugee camps. Preliminary analysis indicates that
the health status has improved in most camps, compared to November
1999. This is attributed to the introduction of the blanket feeding
project for under-fives and pregnant / lactating women. However, one
Sudanese refugee camp and two Somali refugee camps showed a
deterioration in nutrition status. With continuing food distributions
for population affected by the drought and on-going blanket feeding
of the most vulnerable in the refugee camps, it is expected that the
situation will improve over the coming months.
e) A nutrition survey of 1,898 children under 5 was carried out by
GOAL in Yabelo and Taltele weredas (Borena Zone, Oromiya) in July.
Using WFH (Weight for Height) measurements, preliminary results
indicate global malnutrition of 11.7 percent, which is an improvement
compared to the results of the surveys undertaken in March/April. A
worrisome decline in nutritional status has, however, been reported
in Meda Welabu wereda (Bale zone), with 11 percent of children in
some areas severely . malnourished. Mekaneyesus/NCA is distributing
general rations to those identified as requiring assistance, as well
as supplementary food to children under five who are less than 80
percent WFL. A nutritional survey by World Vision Ethiopia indicates
a level of wasting in Omo Sheleko wereda of 23.3 percent and 17.4
percent in Katcha Bira wereda. Discussions are underway with NGOs to
implement distributions of supplementary rations for the most
vulnerable in priority weredas.
3) Djibouti - information as of 3 August
a) Upgrading operation at the Djibouti port is continuing with
minimal disruption to the port activities and the WFP logistics
operations. Assurances have also been received from the authorities
that this will continue to be the case in the future, as well. A
total of 103,000 tons of food have been dispatched by WFP from
Djibouti port during the period 1 July to 1 August, a daily average
of 3,400 tons.
4) Kenya - information as of 1 August
a) The situation in Kenya remains difficult, with power and water
cuts affecting increasingly large areas of the country. Rain was
reported in some northern areas during the past week, resulting in
flash floods in Samburu and West Pokot.
b) From August until December the planned requirement for WFP general
distributions is around 30,000 tons per month to feed the 3.3 million
drought-affected people in the country. The pipeline is generally
poor for all commodities. For July the requirement is actually around
50,000 tons, which includes the pre-positioning of 19,700 tons.
c) Special Operation aimed at repairing and rehabilitating Kenyan
roads to support the implementation of the current emergency
operation is in its final stages. The operation will focus on spot
repairs of road and river crossings to ensure accessibility of all
affected areas. Problems with trucks getting stuck due to poor road
conditions in case the works are not done are very possible,
especially during the rainy period.
d) WFP has recruited a total of 16 logistics assistants to support
the lead NGOs, WFP implementing partners, with logistics planning,
warehousing, handling and transport of commodities to the final
distribution points (FDPs). The staff will also be responsible for
commodity tracking.
5) Somalia - information as of 1 August
a) Following field reports on the drought in the north-west, Food
Security Analysis Unit (FSAU) joined government and UN agency
missions to Awdal, Togdheer and Sanaag regions in Somaliland, to
assess the situation.
b) Some 26,000 people in Awdal (Zeilac and Lughaye districts) are
particularly affected by the drought. The failure of the Hais rains
in most areas of the region, has greatly reduced water access.
Livestock conditions are reportedly poor and milk and ghee production
is down by over 60 percent. About 80-90 percent of camel and cattle
have moved out of the region in search of water. Mortality of sheep
of 60-70 percent and goats of 20-25 percent has been reported, as
these smaller stocks could not migrate due to adaptation problems.
c) Around 10-12,000 people are food insecure in Togdheer region
(Burao, Odweyne and Buuhodle districts) where below normal to poor
successive rains have led to a similar situation to that of Awdal.
Water points are overcrowded and poor pasture has been reported in
these areas. In the Sanaag region, immediate concern arises from the
effects of the Gu floods which has caused damage to agricultural
infrastructure and led to gully and soil erosion. WFP Hargeisa is
currently undertaking an assessment in these areas to determine exact
needs for food assistance during the upcoming month.
d) A good prospect for sorghum and maize production is expected for
the Gu 2000 harvest in southern Somalia. In the best case scenario, a
total of 214,000 tons of maize and sorghum are expected. This is 17
percent higher than the post war average cereal production.
e) From January to June 2000, WFP distributed 7,250 tons of food aid
in Somalia. Around 80 percent of this total was distributed in the
south, mainly to hardest hit communities in Bay, Bakool and Gedo
regions, 15 percent in the north-west and 5 percent in the
north-east. With the shift in strategy from emergency distributions
to FFW, a greater emphasis is being placed on the development of FFW
programmes that target vulnerable population in the south. A total of
1,548 tons of food aid were distributed in southern Somalia through
various FFW activities in July.
C) Great Lakes: Burundi and Tanzania
1) Burundi - information as of 1 August
a) WFP and FAO released the report of the food supply situation in
Burundi following the joint FAO/WFP Crop and Food Supply Assessment
Mission undertaken in July. The report indicates a yield decrease of
3 percent for the season 2000B compared to the same period of last
year. This decrease was due to low rainfall, premature departure of
precipitation and insecurity The food production is considered to be
below the country average of both, the past five years and the
pre-crisis period before 1993. Season B is the main agricultural
season in the country. The mission provisionally estimated total food
production for the year 2000 to be around 3,472,000 tons and the
unmet deficit (after production, expected imports and food aid have
been considered) at 21,000 tons of cereals, 99,000 tons of pulses and
58,000 tons of other food (cereal-equivalents).
b) WFP and SCF started a food security assessment involving food
economy teams from Rwanda, Burundi and DR Congo in the Bugesera
region, which is covering the north of Burundi and the south of
Rwanda. Almost all population living in that region are affected by
the drought. The assessment will allow the teams to make comparison
and establish similarities between the situation in Rwanda and
Burundi and determine the food aid requirements. The assessment will
make an analysis of cross-border food movements.
2) Tanzania - information as of 1 August
a) The first general bi-weekly food aid distribution with rations
reduced by 40 percent was implemented last week in all refugee camps
in Kigoma and Ngara. The reduction is due to very low availability of
food commodities for this refugee feeding project. Dissatisfaction
among the refugees was noticed during the distributions, but no
violence or incidents were recorded. A significant impact of the
ration reduction was noted in the local markets, where prices of
relief commodities increased.
b) According to the Rapid Assessment Mission done in July, 32
districts in Tanzania are affected by the drought and are likely to
face critical food shortages during the later part of the year, until
the next harvest. The most affected regions are Arusha, Shinyanga,
Dodoma, Singida, Morogoro, Kilimanjaro, Tanga, Mwanza, Mara and
Coast. These areas are facing crop failure for the fourth consecutive
season and the traditional coping mechanisms have been almost
exhausted. The report further revealed a very low household carryover
stocks with minimum added stocks from the ongoing harvest.
c) The government subsequently requested WFP to assist with the
fielding of a food needs assessment to identify the number of people
who will not be able to meet their food needs in the coming months
and will need relief, to determine the most appropriate type of
intervention for the vulnerable and to collect information useful for
planning logistics such as storage facilities and transportation,
available NGOs for the management of relief projects. WFP is
currently planning with the partner agencies for the implementation
of these assessments, once the funding is confirmed..
Note: all tonnage figures in this report refer to metric tons
(End WFP Emergency Report No. 31 / 2000 of 4 August, 2000)

Original_Search_Title: Zamfara State Under the Shroud of Sharia
Headline: Nigeria: Zamfara State Under the Shroud of Sharia
Body_Text:
Lagos — Zamfara State is a paradox. It is a land of sorghum, millet,
corns and onions. It is also a land of gold and Sharia.
Zamfara on the extreme is a land of poverty, beggars and destitutes.
It has some of the best roads available in the country; it also has a
digital telephone facility that works uninterrupted.
Zamfara has by the same token very few businesses. The only
institution is government offices and alkali courts. It has only one
decent hotel, with few vehicles on the road, and most of them are on
inter-state operations, or government-owned cars. But it does have
lots of motorcycles and donkeys. At night it is very quiet with
little social activity. The radio station operates when there is
public power supply, it does not have a private electricity facility.
As the rest of the country, Zamfara receives very poor electricity
supply.
There are not many amputees on the streets as a result of violation
of Sharia laws. Women still ride motorcycles, though by August 1st,
the edict of Gusau local government will come into effect banning
such co-mingling with men. Women are to dress in conservative
fashion, covered from head to toe; those who dare put on anything
skimpy or remotely skimpy, which is a rarity anyway are either
visiting or in transit. Yet, for the elite who can afford cable
television, they are permanently tuned to channels that show a great
deal of Asian women, sensuous-looking, putting on dresses that barely
conceal, but reveals a lot.
This state of Sharia is small in population and probably has one of
the largest green areas ever seen The pride of the people and the
state is farming and Sharia; all around the entrance to the capital,
Gusau and beyond are rows and rows of farmland, in neat order, broken
occasionally by a huge rock, resuming thereafter. The huge farmland
is pockmarked with small mud house settlements of people, who indulge
their vocation of farming. Zamfara State is a cross between a huge
farmland and an expedition to the lost ancient city.
The people are warm and friendly, but language is a major hindrance.
A small number of ordinary people speak English. Traversing the
state, communication barrier is the biggest headache. To get around,
is to engage in a dialogue of the deaf : a great deal of
gesticulation, finger pointing and frustration. Most of the young are
barely able to communicate in English, which a youth corper on
primary national assignment describes as very widespread, only
comparable in opposite proportion by the fanaticism for their
religion. With zeal for Sharia a social order, which many people
hitherto not familiar is being firmly entrenched with mixed results,
the outcome of which is not yet clear, but with some disturbing
signs. Pro-Sharia advocates (and there are many; it is difficult to
come across any Muslim who does not profess his belief in this law)
say that the social system had gone to the dogs before the governor
took office last year. The governor says that recognizing this
reality he promised his people last year a transformation of their
socio-economic existence through the instrumentality of Sharia.Or as
he puts it" part of my campaign promise was to reorganize and reform
our religious ways of worship to ensure that Muslims are worshipping
Allah the way he directed them. Because I believe that if that was
achieved, then the enabling environment would have been created, and
the resources available to government would be applied more
meaningfully."

Original_Search_Title: Mpumalanga Gets New Sorghum Brewery
Headline: South Africa: Mpumalanga Gets New Sorghum Brewery
Body_Text:
SECUNDA — Mpumalanga's new Economic Empowerment Corporation (MEEC) is
scheduled to break ground on a R250 000 sorghum beer brewery in
eMbalenhle near Secunda on Friday.
The brewery, which is expected to produce between 4 800 and 8 000
litres of sorghum beer per day, is the corporation's first large
black empowerment project since it was restructured in July.
MEEC spokesman Klaas Skosana said the project would eventually be
fully owned and managed by Danham Bottling cc after the initial R250
000 loan had been paid back over a five-year period.
The MEEC will provide managerial and other technical support during
the repayment period.
Skosana said that the brewery would immediately create six permanent
jobs and would also have a ripple effect in the local economy,
creating new business opportunities for other entrepreneurs.
The MEEC is one of four new parastatals established last month after
the scandal ridden Mpumalanga Development Corporation (MDC) was
finally dismantled.
MEEC chairman Ernest Khosa is also scheduled to open the
corporation's second satellite office in Secunda to serve emerging
businesses in eMbalenhle, Kinross, Evander, Trichardt and Bethal.
The first satellite office was opened in Hazyview on Wednesday.
Khosa said in a statement on Thursday that the satellite offices
would provide free expert advice on business plans, financing
proposals and viability analysis for both new and existing business
ventures.
The MEEC would also help broker joint ventures between existing
businesses and start-ups, and would also offer loan or equity deals
to help kickstart rural economic development.
Khosa stressed that the MEEC was obliged to provide aftercare,
counselling, training and performance monitoring of all companies it
made financial commitments to.
Some businesses targeted for assistance will be offered cheap rentals
in the MEEC's existing industrial and commercial properties.
"The MEEC's primary aim is to advance black economic empowerment
through sound financial packages in order to fast-track entrepreneurs
from disadvantaged communities into the mainstream," said Khosa.
He stressed, however, that preference would be given to applicants
who targeted the manufacturing, tourism, mining and financial service
sectors.
"[All] these sectors have been identified as areas in which
Mpumalanga has a comparative advantage over competing regions," he
said.
The three other new parastatals are the Mpumalanga Agricultural
Development Corporation, the Mpumalanga Regional Training Trust and
the Mpumalanga Housing Finance Corporation.

Original_Search_Title: Road Rehabilitation Underway
Headline: Mozambique: Road Rehabilitation Underway
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — The World Food programme (WFP) this week offered
tenders for the rehabilitation of three roads in Mozambique to enable
the delivery of food aid to flood-affected areas of the country.
The tertiary roads, two in Maputo Province and one in Gaza, would
provide easier access for WFP relief trucks to Xai Xai and the
islands in the Limpopo river, as well as Sabi 100 km northwest of the
capital, Maputo. "We're going ahead with the tendering and will
probably be awarding the contracts in the next two or three weeks," a
WFP official told IRIN.
The rehabilitation has been made possible by a recent US $1.4 million
donation by the Italian government to a WFP appeal for road and
bridge repairs following the devastating floods of February and
March. WFP has identified more than 500 km of tertiary roads that
need overhauling at a cost of US $3.2 million.
"Repairing destroyed infrastructure such as roads and bridges is not
only essential for relief food delivery, but for economic revival
overall," Georgia Shaver, WFP's Regional Manager for Southern Africa
said in a statement this month.
Meanwhile, the Italian government is to disburse approximately US $4
million to help boost agriculture in the central provinces of Manica
and Sofala. The funds would enable the distribution of maize, beans,
sorghum, millet and groundnut seeds, as well as farming tools, to
77,400 families. The project is due to start in September, news
reports said.
However, an official of the National Agriculture and Rural
Development Directorate was quoted as saying that at least US $11
million would be needed to support 187,000 families affected by the
floods in Sofala, as well as the southern provinces of Gaza and
Inhambane.
This item is delivered by the UN's IRIN humanitarian information unit
(e- mail: irin@ocha.unon.org; fax: +254 2 622129; Web:
http://www.reliefweb.int/IRIN), but may not necessarily reflect the
views of the United Nations. If you re-print, copy, archive or
re-post this item, please retain this credit and disclaimer.

Original_Search_Title: National Sorghum Breweries Turns Profit
Headline: South Africa: National Sorghum Breweries Turns Profit
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — National Sorghum Breweries (NSB), South Africa's first
black-owned company, has turned a profit for the first time in many
years.
The company is on track for a brighter future and plans to reveal the
details at its annual meeting in September, said Jaishankar
Ramchandran, a representative of Indian industrial group United
Breweries (UB), which is NSB's largest shareholder and has management
control.
Ramchandran said the UB management team had succeeded in reducing
overheads and increasing productivity and capacity utilisation at
NSB's breweries.
The company's prospects have also improved after the competition
commission recently ruled in favour of NSB buying SA Breweries'
Traditional Beer Investments. The commission gave the takeover the
goahead in April and the deal was finally concluded in June.
UB bought a 30% interest in NSB for R70m in November 1996, with an
option to increase its shareholding to 50% in due course.
Ramchandran said UB was sticking to its agreement with NSB to rope in
an empowerment partner.
He said there were still plans to list NSB once it has established a
sound profit record. NSB commands a market share of approximately 70%
in the sorghum beer segment in SA.
Ramchandran said the deal between NSB and UB represented the largest
Indian investment commitment in SA and a close co-operation between
Indians and black SA.
He said: "UB is committed to build shareholder value so that the
value of NSB's black ownership grows. The UB group believes that this
investment will be seen as a test case of how serious the SA
government is in efforts to make SA more attractive for international
investment." Ramchandran said NSB was UB's largest investment outside
of India and certainly the biggest Indian investment in SA.
With worldwide revenues of $1bn UB is one of India's fastest- growing
industrial houses with more than 60 companies around the world.
UB's core competencies are in brewing and distilling its own brand of
rum, brandy, spirits and beer such as Kingfisher. New lines of
business are trading, media, ecotourism and resorts, including the
Mabula Game Lodge. Ramchandran said UB's main focus in the future was
to strengthen the group's position in existing profitable operations.

Original_Search_Title: North Gondar Attracts More Investors
Headline: Ethiopia: North Gondar Attracts More Investors
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — Some 70 investors with an aggregate capital of 75.5
million birr are engaged in the production of sesame, cotton, sorghum
and other crops on 11,000 hectares of land in North Gondar zone,
Amhara region.
The agricultural activities in the area have created job
opportunities for 498,000 people, it was learnt. Meanwhile, the
construction of 11 of the 16 schools being built in the area at a
cost of over 11 million birr is completed, according to the zonal
education department.
The school would enroll over 4,400 students, thus raising the
education coverage in the area from 40 to 43 percent.

Original_Search_Title: Italian Funds for Agriculture Re-Launch
Headline: Mozambique: Italian Funds for Agriculture Re-Launch
Body_Text:
TETE — The Italian government is to disburse about
four million US dollars for the re-launching of agriculture
activities in the central Mozambican provinces of Manica and Sofala.
The February and March flooding affected these provinces.
Alberto Chidiamassamba of the National Agriculture and Rural
Development Directorate told the national news agency, AIM, that,
however, at least 11 million US dollars is needed to support about
187,000 families affected in Sofala as well as the southern provinces
of Gaza and Inhambane.
The Italian funds will cover 77,400 families, he said, adding that
this will involve the distribution of maize, bean, sorghum, millet,
groundnut seeds, apart from agriculture tools.
The distribution of the inputs will start in September when the
2000/2001 season starts.
For its part, the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation has disbursed
270,000 dollars for the purchase of seeds, pesticides and
fertilisers. Its aid will benefit 30,000 families.
Chidiamassamba said that 1.8 tonnes of seeds donated by the Egyptian
government were unloaded at the central Beira port this week,
intended to re- launch agriculture activities in Sofala.
The European Union has also expressed interest in supporting 40,000
families in Gaza, Inhumane, and Sofala, he added.
For the agriculture emergency programme, the government needs about
10,650 tonnes of seeds, 429,900 tools.

Original_Search_Title: Agro Processing: the Giant Sleeping Industry in Tanzania
Headline: Tanzania: Agro Processing: the Giant Sleeping Industry in Tanzania
Body_Text:
Dar Es Salaam — Despite that the Agro processing industry have played
a significant role in the industrialization of Tanzania, expert say
much remain to be done for Tanzania to make use the giant sleeping
sub- sector.
In Tanzania experienced an unprecedented industrial growth in the
late 1960s and 1970s.
The country's Second Five Year Plan (1969-1994) and the Basic
Industrial Strategy (BIS) of 1975-1995, which were formulated in the
context of pursuing socialism principles and public ownership of
industry, succeeded, for a while, in promoting industrial development
and growth. However, because of poorly designed public investments in
industry, inadequate technological capabilities and skilled
industrial human resources, the performance of the industrial sector
was short lived.
Others factor included inappropriate industrial policies, inadequate
infrastructural and institutional support services which also
contributed to the economic crisis of the country in the 1980s. There
are observations saying that among the giant sleeping industry in
Tanzania is the agro-processing.
They include leather, textiles and the food industries.
In the leather industry for example, the collection of hides and
skins for 1997 was 1, 500,000 hides, 1,200,000 goatskins and 700,000
sheepskins. In the absence of a fully integrated leather industry,
the country's production of hide and skins currently outstrips the
requirements of the local tanneries.
Various studies indicate that the raw stock is still exported mainly
to India, Pakistan and Iran. A ready-made market for wet blue tanned
hides has been established in Switzerland, Italy and Hong-Kong.
Tanzania had three parastatal tanneries that were privatized, but
until recently their performance was doubtful. The shortage of
finished leather has, to a large extent, impeded further development
and product improvement of the footwear and leather goods industry.
The textile industry, ranging from spinning to garment making was an
important industrial sector in the 1980s, employing over 37,000
people and representing 36 percent of the total employment of the
manufacturing sector. Experts say that there were over 385,000
spindles with a total spinning capacity of approximately 49,000 tons
of yarn per annum and 6,000 looms capable of weaving 270 million
square meters of woven cloth per annum.
The total installed capacity was approximately 190 million linear
meters of fabric. However, since 1990 the textile industry has been
in the serious decline as a result of global competition, including
competition from second hand clothing.
Recent data reveal that there are 16 mills operating with an average
capacity utilization of 15 to 20 percent. Recently, however, with the
restructuring and reengineering of some textiles, cloth and yarn
production have increased from 33, 436,000 square meters in 1996 to
42,874,000 in 1998.
Due to poor performance or closure of other textiles, the
contribution of the industry to the GDP has remained low. However,
the completion divestiture of some of these, their contribution to
the national income would increase.
Tanzania is also an important producer of cotton lint with an average
annual output of around 62,000 tones during the last seven years. 84
percent of cotton produced is exported.
The food industry reflects the status of the agricultural sector in
the country, which stakeholders divide into three major components.
They include processing of subsistence crops like maize, sorghum,
millet, cassava, rice, plantains, and wheat among others.
The second is processing of agricultural commodities oriented to the
domestic market and this covers brewery, distillery, soft drink,
sugar and edible oil industries. Lastly is the industrialization of
key agricultural commodities including fisheries traded in the
international markets like coffee, tea, cashew nuts, and fresh water
fish.
Data from 1995 shows that the food industry including beverage and
tobacco industry accounted for approximately 30 percent of the
manufacturing sector in Tanzania. The utilization of the installed
capacity for the food industry ranges between 50 to 70 percent with
some exception as low as 10 percent.
In addition to high production costs due to low capacity utilization,
the food-manufacturing sector faces serious problems such as access
to modern technology, quality product and management and environment.
They also include lack and costly energy, high costs of key inputs
and lack of appropriate policies.
The good news is that of January 1997, when the government of
Tanzania launched its Sustainable Industrial Development Policy
(SIDP) for 1996-2020. With outsiders support, they policy aims at
contributing towards the overall national long-term development goals
and national vision as well as enhancing sustainable development of
the industrial sector.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Surplus Forecast
Headline: Southern Africa: Maize Surplus Forecast
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Southern Africa is forecast to have a maize surplus of
1.33 million mt for the 2000/2001 marketing year, according to the
latest bulletin by the regional food security unit.
The Southern Africa Development Community (SADC) Regional Early
Warning Unit said the surplus is mostly due to the marked increase in
maize output in South Africa, which recorded an exportable maize
surplus of 2.27 million mt, and a surplus in Malawi hitting 725,000
mt.
The bulletin said that regional maize production is estimated to have
increased by 14 percent to 19.48 million mt from the 1998/99 harvest
of 17.15 million mt - attributed to significantly increased output in
South Africa, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Botswana and Namibia.
The region also faces a reduced overall cereal deficit of 679,000 mt
compared to 2.19 million mt last year. The cereals are maize, wheat,
rice, sorghum and millet. The unit said the total SADC projected
cereal availability of 27.04 million mt is insufficient to cover the
total estimated requirements of 27.72 million mt.
"This is despite the devastation caused by flooding in southern and
central Mozambique and parts of South Africa, Swaziland, Botswana and
Zimbabwe," said the bulletin. It added that the increase is due
mainly to increased plantings and the prevalence of above average
seasonal rainfall.
The bulletin said that regional deficits assessed in wheat and rice
will need to be covered through imports. Some 1.21 million mt of
wheat imports and 540,000 mt of rice will be necessary to cover the
projected deficit, said the bulletin. It added that although sorghum
and millet deficit is also projected, this is likely to be covered
through cross substitution with maize.
This item is delivered by the UN's IRIN humanitarian information unit
(e-mail: irin@ocha.unon.org; fax: +254 2 622129; Web:
http://www.reliefweb.int/IRIN), but may not necessarily reflect the
views of the United Nations. If you re-print, copy, archive or
re-post this item, please retain this credit and disclaimer.

Original_Search_Title: The Firewood For Conflict in Sierra Leone
Headline: Sierra Leone: The Firewood For Conflict in Sierra Leone
Body_Text:
Freetown — Whenever we talk about the cause of conflict especially
that of the RUF carnage, our minds are usually drawn to minerals
(particularly diamonds) as the only resources fuelling wars in
Africa. Except for other reasons like thirst for political power and
the economic well-being of man, hardly do people think of other
resources - gold, cocoa, coffee - as the firewood for war and
conflict.
In a broader look at Africa, the gems are indeed playing a major role
in exacerbating wars and conflicts especially in the very recent past
decades.
But for Sierra Leone, a little more adds to the reasons for the
successes in the RUF campaign against the people of this country.
When the RUF launched their rebellion earlier in 1991 they were quick
to take over the economic mainstay of the country. The diamond mines
in Kono and Tongo fields, the gold fields in Mbanman and the cocoa
and coffee regions of the South East.
Before the war, there was no doubt that the cocoa and coffee
industries were playing a strong role in the economic development of
Sierra Leone. Expert agro- economists will agree with me that since
the war started some nine years ago, there has been an adverse
discrepancy in government generated export figures in cocoa and
coffee. What then is happening to our cocoa and coffee products?
Little or no attention has been given to this question. You read
through the newspapers, listen to radio and watch the television, all
what people are concerned about are diamonds - discussions on how to
stop the illegal trade in diamonds.
What about gold, cocoa and coffee, are they not strategic resources?
Unlike diamonds being trafficked through Liberia, our cocoa and
coffee products are on a full scale being traded in Guinea.
Guinea- Conakry has been in this illegal trade quietly and
successfully because of the lack of state concern and involvement on
the part of our government. The RUF has been making use of this trade
to get food, medicines, clothes and other logistics.
Now that these things are coming to light and the international
community is fully behind the government of Sierra Leone, officials
in the Ministry of Trade and State Enterprises must work hand in
gloves with their counterparts in the Ministry of Mines and the
Government Gold and Diamond Office who are bent in putting an end to
these illegal trading.
If the United Nations ban on the sale of blood diamonds cannot be
extended to these other commodities like gold and cocoa, then
authorities in Conakry must be informed to put an end to these
illegal trafficking. Notable trafficking are being carried out
through routes linking Kono to Kanyandera and from Koindu to
Geigeidu.
For our cocoa and coffee farmers in the South Eastern part of the
country, the state of their activities is a famished economy. So far,
for them, economic projection for now is in adversity. What is worse
is that they have been turned into slaves in their non-farms being
exploited by criminal gangs - the RUF.
The questions which most people are asking now are when will the UN
deliver our people Up-country from the hands of the oppressors
calling themselves revolutionaries, and when will Freetown start to
see fresh fruits and vegetables from the North? When will the Eastern
Farmers Union be revived? Have the authorities decided to forget
about harnessing Sierra Leonean products for bulgur, beans sorghum?
Does it mean Sierra Leoneans will ever remain in begging?

Original_Search_Title: HORN OF AFRICA: IRIN Update
Headline: East Africa: HORN OF AFRICA: IRIN Update
Body_Text:
Nairobi — SOMALIA: Interim Charter adopted - The Djibouti-hosted
Somali National Peace Conference adopted a Transitional National
Charter on Sunday, conference sources told IRIN on Monday. They said
the charter was adopted by an overwhelming majority of participants.
It provides for a three-year transitional government based on a
federal system, with a transitional national assembly (TNA) elected
on the basis of clan. It also provides for the establishment of a
transitional judiciary system, validating the 1960 Somali
constitution and "other laws not in contradiction with the charter",
a source said.
After the new charter was presented, delegates broke into applause
and sang the national anthem, an observer told IRIN. [For details,
see separate IRIN story headlined "Peace conference passes new
charter", which should be dated 17 July].
SOMALIA: Aideed still awaited in Djibouti
Mogadishu-based faction-leader Hussein Mohamed Aideed, reported to
have come to Djibouti on Friday 14 July, was expected to arrive soon
by plane from Mogadishu with other Mogadishu-based leaders.
[Subscribers please note the IRIN story of Friday 14 July headlined
"Aideed arrives at Djibouti talks" is incorrect and is withdrawn].
SOMALIA: Concern for arrested journalist
Reporters Sans Frontieres (RSP) said it had sent a letter to leader
of the Puntland administration, Colonel Abdullahi Yusuf Ahmed, to
protest against the arrest of Mohamed Abdulkadir Ahmed,
editor-in-chief of the private weekly 'Sahan'.
According to information collected by the Paris-based organisation,
Mohamed Abdulkadir Ahmed was arrested by police in Bossaso 11 July,
and was "beaten, then taken to the port of the town and put inside a
container". He had been writing articles in support of the ongoing
Somali peace talks in Djibouti and criticising the Puntland
administration for boycotting the process. The Puntland
administration, led by Colonel Abdullahi Yusuf, has refused to attend
the talks, saying they would lead to further conflict, and banned
people from the autonomous northeastern region from attending.
"As far as we know he did no more than exercise his right to inform
as guaranteed in the Article 6.2 of the Charter for Puntland State",
said Robert Menard, RSF General Secretary, in the statement.
SOMALIA: Pirates free French ship and crew
Pirates in Somalia's autonomous state of Puntland have abandoned a
French- registered ship on which they were holding 10 Lebanese,
Pakistani and Syrian crew captive, AFP reported at the weekend. In a
brief dispatch quoting local sources, it said the gunmen freed the
crew and left the 'Net Express' on Saturday night after negotiations
and threats of military action by Puntland authorities.
The pirates boarded the ship from heavily armed speedboats last
Monday, looted the cargo, and took the crew hostage. "The gunmen left
the ship and escaped in speed boats. The crew were not harmed," said
the report.
ETHIOPIA: Clashes leaves 40 dead
Clashes between Oromos and ethnic Somalis in Western Haraghe, eastern
Ethiopia, last week left 39 Oromos and a government security agent
dead, Ethiopian media reports said. Hundreds of livestock were
stolen. According to reports in a regional newspaper 'Tobya' - also
carried on radio stations monitored by the BBC - the Isa Somalis
surrounded the Oromos and massacred them. The reports said
"unidentified groups" took advantage of the clashes and attacked six
electric transmitters, leaving the towns of Meison, Asebe Teferi,
Debesa, Gelemso Irna among others without power for a number of days.
No independent sources have confirmed the killings.
Humanitarian sources in Ethiopia told IRIN that drought had
exacerbated traditional hostilities between the Oromos and Somalis,
both pastoral communities. The northernmost zone of the Somali region
has suffered a prolonged dry season without rains for two months,
said the source, causing movement of livestock into the more fertile
Haraghe hills, Dire Dawa and Harar. Although the seasonal migration
is not an unusual movement, the number of people and cattle involved
this year is unusually large, and has put stress on local
relationships and available pasture. Severe drought in the northern
Somali zone has also recently caused the displacement of Ethiopian
ethnic Somalis into southern Djibouti.
ETHIOPIA: Chief prosecutor jailed
The Federal High Court has sentenced the Special Public Chief
Prosecutor, Girma Wakjira, to one month's imprisonment for contempt
of court, said a report posted on the pro-government Walta
Information web site 14 July. Chief prosecutor Girma was head of the
special prosecutor's office, which is prosecuting former Ethiopian
officials accused of genocide. According to government reports, the
verdict was passed on Girma after he was found guilty of "obstruction
of justice" and "jeopardising the court's neutrality and
independence". Girma had distributed a statement to the media on 12
July saying he would not attend the court until the presiding judge
was removed. He had claimed the judge, Tafese Yirga Gebre-Medhin, was
a member of the former ruling Workers Party of Ethiopia (WPE). The
Federal High Court said Girma's statement not only defamed a judge,
but was also an attack on the whole judiciary system.
Established under deposed Soviet-supported dictator Mengistu Haile
Mariam, WPE was a crucial political and organising tool used for some
of the worst excesses of the regime. It was banned by the Ethiopian
Peoples Revolutionary Democratic Front government, formed in 1991.
Many of the former officials charged with genocide and crimes against
humanity belonged to the WPE.
SUDAN: Government accuses rebels of breaking ceasefire
The government of Sudan and southern rebels at the weekend blamed
each other for a series of ceasefire violations in the southwestern
Bahr el Ghazal district where a cessation of hostilities had been
called to enable the delivery of humanitarian relief.
The Sudanese External Relations Ministry said it reserved the right
to "repulse aggression". It recalled that the government had
repeatedly warned the international community that the rebel
movement, the Sudan Peoples' Liberation Army (SPLA), "was not
serious" about the ceasefire. The ceasefire was due to expire on
Saturday after its extension by both sides in April.
The government said it had agreed to extend the ceasefire for three
months on 27 April, in compliance with requests by the European
Union, the United States and other international donors. Listing a
series of violations, it said the attacks should be condemned by the
international community. "The government of Sudan affirms again that
it remains committed to the ceasefire and reiterates its call for a
comprehensive ceasefire and permanent peace," said the statement
carried by state radio and monitored by the BBC. "The government at
the same time affirms its right to repulse aggression and protect the
life and property of citizens. It also affirms that it will not sit
idly by and watch the rebel movement capture the town of Bahr el
Ghazal, one after another."
SUDAN: The rebels counter-charge
In a counter-charge, Samson Kwaje, the official SPLM/A spokesman said
rebel forces had killed 92 government soldiers, captured weapons and
munitions: "The SPLM/A deplores and condemns the government of Sudan
for the use of tribal militias to raid for slaves in Bahr el Ghazal
and Western Upper Nile as part of its war strategy to depopulate
these areas in its futile attempt the secure the oil wells". The
statement also condemned aerial bombardment in the south, saying the
government was targetting the civil population and non-military
targets.
SUDAN: External Relations Minister visits Europe
External Relations Minister Mustafa Uthman Ismail was scheduled on
Monday to visit Britain, Italy and the Vatican to discuss the crisis
and bilateral issues, reported Sudan state media.
The newspaper 'Al-Ra'y al-Amm' in a dispatch monitored by the BBC,
said the government was considering withdrawing from the east African
Inter- Governmental Authority on Development (IGAD) peace initiative
after IGAD delegates meeting in Oslo, Norway, had rejected a merger
of IGAD proposals with a new joint Libyan-Egyptian reconciliation
initiative. But he said the government would study all options before
deciding on such a move.
Mohamed al-Atta, a member of Khartoum's negotiating team, was quoted
by AFP as saying IGAD had been asked to take a "stern" position
towards the SPLA and "pressure it to abide by the ceasefire. He told
reporters that the fighting in Bahr el Ghazal "will cause a grave
humanitarian crisis".
SUDAN: Civilians killed in Nuba mountains
Five people collecting relief items were killed in an ambush by
government forces, in Heiban County, Nuba Mountains, at the end of
June, humanitarian sources told IRIN. Fear of ambush has since
prevented displaced civilians from freely collecting vital relief
items, provided by church groups and international NGOs. Since the
ambush, displaced civilians fear the long trek to collect vital
supplies of tools, seeds, plastic sheeting, and cooking implements,
said the source. The Western Jebels (west of Kadugli) have been
effectively cut off from the eastern Nuba mountain range, the only
area with an available air strip. Pockets of displaced civilians have
been surviving in the mountains since government attacks in March on
the SPLA-held areas of Tabanya and Toro, Burum County, Nuba
Mountains.
The harvesting of sorghum was halted and preparation of land
disrupted. This has "serious implications" for both the displaced
people and for other villages, said the source, as Buram County is
traditionally a surplus area for grain.
DJIBOUTI: Cholera outbreak claim five lives
Five people have died and more than 200 admitted to hospital in a
cholera outbreak on the outskirts of Djibouti city, AFP reported. A
hospital official told AFP that more than half the hospital cases
were children. A special facility has been set up in a tent near the
Peltier hospital, and French doctors working in Djibouti have been
asked for assistance in tackling the outbreak. Most of the victims
come from the impoverished district of Balbala and use poorly stored
water, reported the agency. Unsanitary conditions and malnutrition
have contributed to the outbreak of cholera among Balbala's
residents, who are mostly refugees from Somalia and Ethiopia, and
destitute nomads, said AFP.
ERITREA: UNICEF humanitarian airlifts
UNICEF's Eritrea Country Office announced at the weekend that it had
airlifted humanitarian supplies worth US $2.25 million to vulnerable
populations between 24 May and 9 July 2000.
In a statement, the agency said the support had been allocated
towards health, nutrition, water and sanitation, and education.
"While the humanitarian needs remain enormous, UNICEF is convinced
that this support will go a long way in meeting the emergency
requirements of a sizeable number of the estimated 1.5 million people
currently displaced by the war and affected by the drought," the
statement said. "Those affected constitute about 70 per cent children
and 20 percent women."
This item is delivered by the UN's IRIN humanitarian information unit
(e- mail: irin@ocha.unon.org; fax: +254 2 622129; Web:
http://www.reliefweb.int/IRIN), but may not necessarily reflect the
views of the United Nations. If you re-print, copy, archive or
re-post this item, please retain this credit and disclaimer.

Original_Search_Title: Fake Yoghurt Flood Nigerian Markets
Headline: Nigeria: Fake Yoghurt Flood Nigerian Markets
Body_Text:
Lagos — Fan yoghurt, chocolate etc. should watch it as some of the
dairy foods have been discovered to be fake in most markets in town.
Consumer Watch investigation revealed that following public
acceptance of all kinds of yoghurt products, some over-zealous and
greedy marketers have started faking the products to deceive the
general public. A survey at Oshodi Market showed all kinds of
adulterated yoghurt products currently on sale and ignorantly
perceived by consumers as genuine.
The fake producers of yoghurt products have introduced a of methods
which makes their kind of yoghurt similar to the popular and familiar
fan dairy product. Prices of the adulterated yoghurt products are
sold for N10,00 and has no large size as in fan yoghurt, sellers
market the fake ones alongside the genuine products to further
confuse innocent buyers especially school children who mostly
patronise them.
Yoghurt is a by-product of milk and could also be refined to produce
cheese also a by product of milk, but the fake ones did not come from
milk but from plants which bear fruits like sorghum. Consumers are
further deceived in the packaging of this products. There are places
they sale empty wrappers which could be used as yoghurt pack in
Oshodi, Iddo and Idomota market.
What this fake producers usually do was to purchase the empty yoghurt
wrappers, fill the fake diary product and freeze before selling to
unsuspecting public as genuine.
The danger in it is that the fake diary producers package their wares
in unhealthy environment without checks by relevant government
agencies.
Besides, products which are supposed to be disposed are re-injected
into circulation for consumers who are later infected with all kinds
diseases after consumption.
Also in Oshodi, marketers of the products convey a series of
freshness on the product, telling consumers that fan had introduced a
new product which consumers were not aware of to enable them sell.
But, one feels this is where fan, as a corporate body, should
intervene. Those who act as spoilers are supposed to be fished out to
enable the diary industry breath fresh air of proper sanitation. It
is absurd to see companies that should invest in healthy enterprise
destroy people who patronise them by condoning activities of pirates.
Fan should not only help the consumers in making public the fake
producers, it should also go a step further in liaising with relevant
government agencies to properly stop the activities of these
unregistered outlets from their nefarious operations before it
becomes endemic.
Custodians of public health organisation and relevant NGO's should
spare no effort in saving consumers from the activities of
manufacturers of fake diary products by intensifying the search for
solutions in care of casualties.
Other areas of concern is the fruit the fake diary manufactures use
for their products which till date, had not be proven to be good for
consumption.
Sources have it that other materials are mixed with the fruits to
achieve the end result of fake yoghurt. It was still a misery how the
products are distilled and pushed to the public, this claim has to be
broken. The fake products are distinguishable from the genuine ones,
firstly, the prizes are low, it also has rough edges which a times
slit open and content spilled.
It has no fan labels and logo which made it look smaller than in size
than the original, it has not chocolate brand but only yoghurt cream,
therefore, consumers beware.

Original_Search_Title: FIIRO Develops Grains, Slicer Machines
Headline: Nigeria: FIIRO Develops Grains, Slicer Machines
Body_Text:
Lagos — A 300kg per hour all purpose grains mill, has been developed
through the machinery design and development programme effort of the
Federal Institute of Industrial Research Oshodi (FIIRO).
According to FIIRO, the mill is designed and fabricated to aid Small
Scale indigenous millers to use roller mills to process different
grains such as cowpea, Maize, Sorghum, rice, wheat etc; with
low-cost.
FIIRO in its recently published annual report, expounded that the
milling, sifting and recyling units of the plant functioned
satisfactorily under preliminary tests.
It's three units is said to have been assembled for overall
performance testing and characterization, while its special feature
is in using only one prime mover for the four rolls.
The first three rolls are placed approximately on the vertices of an
equilateral triangle while the fourth roll is placed in position
where on adjustment, it makes contact with the third roll.
The plant according to FIIRO can therefore produce various grades of
flour by simple adjustment of the rollers. A petrol engine can be
substituted for the electric motor, thus increasing the versatility
of the plant installation locations.
Beside the 300 kg grains mill, FIIRO through the effort of its
research and development activities has also designed and fabricated
Grain Destoner, Okro Slicer, Ginger Slicer, Automatic liquid filling
machine and a Solar Dryer for fruits and vegetables.
The Grain Destoner is designed as a solution to the presence of sand,
grit and other unwanted impurities in indigenous milled grains which
is due to lack of pre-cleaning facilities such as grains destoners
and blowers.
In order to correct this, FIIRO has successfully developed and
demonstrated a general purpose grains destoner for Maize Soyabean and
beans.
The Okro Slicing equipment which has been already designed and
fabricated is said to be undergoing rigorous testing to ensure that
it solves the problem inherent in manually slicing dried Okro.
The hinger slicer which has been tested is reported to be capable of
producing 300kg of sliced ginger per hour. The slicer according to
FIIRO is to play a vital role in a ginger processing plant as sliced
ginger is said to dry faster than whole ginger.
Regarding the Automatic Liquid filling machine, the prototype
equipment developed was successful in filling liquid automatically
into a series of bottles, but there remained the problem of uneven
filling now being tackled.
The Solar Dryer equipment fruits and vegetables is capable of drying
different types of harvested fruits and vegetables, it is now being
characterized using different fruits and vegetables.
FIIRO in the annual report explained that the activities of the
institute were concentrated in laboratory research, machine and
equipment development; and on extension services, to transfer the
developed technologies.
It noted that some of the research and extension activities were
funded through the World Bank-sponsored National Agricultural
Research Project (NARP).
FIIRO added that grains milling machines commonly used in Nigeria,
especially by Small Scale operators, are restricted to disc mill and
hammer mill types which usually pulurise the grain before it is
sifted into flour; noting that this resulted in low yield of flour
fraction.
It further informed that roller milling is better, but regretted that
its high technology drives indigenous small millers away from using
it thereby making its use a monopoly of large-scale industries.
However, FIIRO emphasized that its goal is to encourage both large
and small- scale indigenous millers.

Original_Search_Title: Critical Crop And Food Supply Situation in Kenya
Headline: Kenya: Critical Crop And Food Supply Situation in Kenya
Body_Text:
Rome — OVERVIEW: Kenya's current food supply situation and outlook
give cause for serious concern. The long rains season (March-May),
which normally accounts for 80 percent of total annual food
production, has failed due to a severe drought.
With the exception of parts of Western Province and Nyanza Province
which received significant amounts of rainfall, the rest of the
country, including the "bread basket" Rift Valley Province and
Central Province which is normally close to self-sufficiency in food,
have received little or no rainfall, leading to widespread crop
failures as well as large livestock losses in the pastoral areas of
the north, northeast and northwest. The food situation is
particularly dire for pastoralists because this is the fourth
consecutive rain failure in their areas.
The current drought has, therefore, aggravated an already severe
scarcity of water and pastures for livestock. The current food crisis
in the pastoral areas is the result of a combination of cumulative
livestock losses, falling livestock prices and sharply rising cereal
prices.
Starvation-related deaths, particularly among children, are being
reported. The Government has appealed to the international community
for assistance to cope with the emergency.
Maize is the main staple food of Kenya, averaging over 80 percent of
total cereals (rice, wheat, millet and sorghum).
Current official forecasts put the 2000 long rains maize crop at only
1.4 million tonnes, 36 percent lower than the long rains average of
2.21 million tonnes and 22 percent less than the drought-reduced 1999
long rains crop of 1.8 million tonnes. Assuming the 2000 short rains
(October-December) harvest at the same level as in 1999, estimated at
450 000 tonnes, total domestic production available for consumption
in 2000/2001 amounts to 1.85 million tonnes.
Maize stocks are estimated to be depleted at all levels throughout
the country. With a national maize utilization requirement (including
food, feed, seed, losses) estimated at 3.21 million tonnes, Kenya
will need to import some 1.4 million tonnes until the next main
harvest in September 2001 (see maize balance sheet below).
Prices of maize are very high and rising, progressively diminishing
access to food for the poorer sections of the population. However,
with the removal of the high import tariff on maize in mid June, it
is hoped that the private sector will cover most of the deficit.
Nevertheless, vulnerable people, particularly pastoralists, will not
have access to maize on the market and will therefore need food
assistance.
2. MAIZE PRODUCTION IN 1999 AND 2000
2.1 Production in 1999
The long rains of 1999 (March-May) were very variable, both spatially
and temporally.
Western and Nyanza provinces and the major maize producing areas of
Rift Valley Province (the districts of Kericho, Nandi, Trans Nzoia
and Uasin Gishu) received normal rains and obtained normal harvests.
In the rest of the country, however, including Central Province,
Eastern Province, North Eastern Province, northern parts of Rift
Valley Province and Coast Province, rainfall was below normal,
resulting in a significantly reduced harvest.
Total production of maize and beans was estimated at 1.8 million
tonnes and 270 000 tonnes respectively. While the bean harvest was
close normal, the maize harvest was more than 20 percent below the
long rains average.
The short rains season (October-December), which accounts for around
20 percent of annual cereal output, is the main season for the arid
and semi-arid pastoral and agro-pastoral areas of the north,
northwest and northeast, as well as for the marginal agricultural
areas in the east and south.
The 1999 short rains started late and were insufficient in many areas
for crop production and pasture growth and for adequate replenishment
of water supplies, particularly for livestock. The worst affected
districts included Turkana, West Pokot, Baringo, Samburu, Marsabit,
Isiolo, Garissa, Wajir, Moyale and Mandera (arid and semi- arid
pastoral areas), as well as Tharaka, Mbeere, Mwingi, Machakos,
Makueni and Tana River (marginal agricultural areas).
Total maize production in 1999 was estimated at 2.25 million tonnes
compared to 2.44 million tonnes in 1998 (-8%) and 2.7 million tonnes
average of the previous 5 years (-17%).
2.2 Long Rains Production in 2000
The main season of 2000 - the long rains (March-May) - has largely
failed.
There was a false start in mid-April (late by a whole month) with
sporadic showers in many areas but they petered out by the end of the
month, except in the west and along the Indian Ocean coast where
precipitation has been significant but patchy. Area planted to maize
with the April showers is estimated at 79 percent of long rains
average, but the crop has dried up in most areas, including in
Kenya's bread basket, the Rift Valley Province, and the
high-potential Central Province which is normally close to
self-sufficiency in food.
As a result, maize production from the current season is
provisionally estimated at 1.4 million tonnes, around 64 percent of
the normal long rains harvest, but the final outcome could be
significantly lower.
3. OVERALL FOOD SUPPLY AND DEMAND SITUATION
3.1 Food Prices and Access to Food
Maize prices throughout the country have been rising since January
1999, reflecting growing maize shortages.
Between January and July 1999, retail maize prices in three major
consuming centres, Nairobi, Mombasa and Kisumu, rose by 91 percent,
98 percent and 75 percent, respectively, before easing slightly in
September/October with the arrival of the new maize crop on the
market. However, they have resumed their upward trend (Figure 1).
By May 2000 they were close to the high levels of July 1999 and still
rising. This is seriously hurting the poorer sections of the Kenyan
population whose access to food is increasingly being curtailed.
The situation is most alarming in the pastoral areas. Livestock
losses, plummeting livestock prices due to the poor state of the
animals, and the high and rising grain prices have combined to
precipitate a grave food crisis for the pastoral households.
With four successive droughts, household economies have collapsed and
coping mechanisms have been exhausted, leading to destitution and
reported starvation-related deaths, particularly among children.
There are also reports of increasing inter-ethnic armed conflicts
over scarce water and pasture resources.
3.2 Maize Supply/Demand Balance, 2000/2001 (October-September)
Estimation of Kenya's maize supply and demand for marketing year
2000/2001 (October-September) is based on the following parameters
and assumptions:
total population of 30.85 million as at March 2001; total maize
production of 1.85 million tonnes (long rains 2000: 1.4m; short rains
2000: 0.45m tonnes); per caput maize consumption of 97 kg/year;
negligible maize stocks at all levels; feed use at 2 percent of
production other uses including seed and losses at 10 percent
Commercial imports have not flowed in as would have been expected
largely because of a high tariff rate of 75 percent on imported
maize. However, with the removal of the tariff in mid June, it is
expected that the private sector will import maize to bridge much of
the supply/demand gap.
Kenya has a sophisticated private sector with the necessary
experience and resources. As Figure 2 shows, the private sector has
imported over 1 million tonnes before (1996, 1997) when tariffs were
reduced by the Government.
However, vulnerable groups, particularly pastoralists, will need food
assistance as they do not have enough purchasing power to buy maize
from the market.
This report is prepared on the responsibility of the FAO Secretariat
with information from official and unofficial sources.
Since conditions may change rapidly, please contact Mr. Abdur Rashid,
Chief, ESCG, FAO, (Fax: 0039-06-5705-4495, E-Mail (INTERNET):
GIEWS1@FAO.ORG) for further information if required. Please note that
this Special Report is available on the Internet as part of the FAO
World Wide Web at the following URL address:
http://www.fao.org/giews/ .
The Special Alerts/Reports can also be received automatically by E-
mail as soon as these are published, subscribing to the GIEWS/Alerts
report ListServ. To do so, please send an E-mail to the
FAO-Mail-Server at the following address: mailserv@mailserv.fao.org,
leaving the subject blank, with the following message: Subscribe
GIEWSAlerts-L To be deleted from the list, send the message:
Unsubscribe GIEWSAlerts-L

Original_Search_Title: South Africa's Awethu Breweries Poised For A Turnaround'
Headline: South Africa's Awethu Breweries Poised For A Turnaround'
Body_Text:
Durban — Traditional beverage and food products group Awethu
Breweries says it expects to stage a turnaround in its current
financial year, on the back of greater market penetration and cost-
cutting initiatives.
Newly appointed MD Tony Ford said last week the group expects to post
only a marginal loss (1999: R8,9m loss) for its full year to end-
June and is on track for a profitable interim period.
Ford, already the major shareholder in Awethu, subscribed earlier
last week for 21,67-million shares in the company at the current
price of 6c. The move will raise R1,3m for Awethu.
The issue, necessitated by "a severe cash flow shortage", means Ford
will own 42,4-million shares, or 50,09% of the increased issued share
capital.
He was appointed MD several weeks ago with the previous MD Mike
Kennedy assuming the position of strategic and commercial director.
Ford said the historic cashflow problems stemmed from Awethu
acquiring several "overpriced assets". Awethu, although trading
profitably, has been forced to commit resources to paying for these
assets. Ford says the capital injection will ensure the company
overcomes these problems.
He says Awethu is trading profitably and expects to either break even
or report no more than a R500000 loss for the year to end-June.
Ford forecasts an interim profit of between R2m and R2,5m, as cost-
cutting exercises of R150000 a month pay further dividends.
Awethu distributes traditional beverage and food products to the mass
informal market, mainly through its own retail network of more than
700 franchised outlets in the Free State, Gauteng and North West
Province.
The company also manufactures sorghum beer, mageu (an alcohol-free
maize drink), milled maize products, milled wheat products and bread.
The business is highly integrated with full control of raw material
inputs, manufacture and distribution.
Ford said the group has four years left of its contract with National
Sorghum Breweries. Increased market penetration should push sales up
20% to 25% over that time. The contract can be renewed.

Original_Search_Title: Kenyan Pastoralists Face Severe Food Shortage
Headline: Kenyan Pastoralists Face Severe Food Shortage
Body_Text:
ROME, Italy (PANA) — Kenya is suffering severely from drought and the
food situation gives cause for serious concern, the UN Food and
Agriculture Organisation (FAO) says in a special report published
Monday.
"The food situation is especially dire for pastoralists because this
is the fourth consecutive rain failure in their areas," FAO says.
Starvation-related deaths, particularly among children, are being
reported.
The long rains season (March-May), which normally accounts for 80
percent of total annual food production, has failed due to severe
drought, the report says.
Most of the country, including the "bread basket" Rift Valley
Province and the normally nearly food self-sufficient Central
Province, has received little or no rainfall, leading to widespread
crop failures as well as large livestock losses.
Maize prices are very high and continue to rise, FAO observes in the
report.
"This is seriously hurting the poor whose access to food is
increasingly being curtailed."
FAO has voiced special alarm about the food situation of
pastoralists. "Livestock losses, plummeting livestock prices due to
the poor state of the animals, and the high and rising grain prices
have combined to precipitate a grave food crisis for the pastoral
households.
"Household economies have collapsed leading to destitution and
starvation- related deaths, especially among children. There are also
reports of increasing inter-ethnic armed conflicts over scare water
and pasture resources."
Maize is the main staple food of Kenya, averaging over 80 percent of
total cereals (rice, wheat, millet and sorghum). Current forecasts
put the 2000 long rains maize crop at only 1.4 million tonnes, 22
percent less than the drought- reduced 1999 long rains crop of 1.8
million tonnes.
Assuming the 2000 short rains (October-December) harvest comes in at
the same level as in 1999, estimated at 450 tonnes, total domestic
production available for consumption in 2000/2001 will amount to only
1.85 million tonnes.
Maize stocks are estimated to be depleted throughout the country with
a national maize requirement (including food, feed, seed, and losses)
estimated at 3.21 million tonnes.
Kenya will need to import some 1.4 million tonnes until the next main
harvest in September 2001, the FAO global information and early
warning system said.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Emergency Report No. 27 - Africa
Headline: Africa: WFP Emergency Report No. 27 - Africa
Body_Text:
Rome — This report includes: A) Horn of Africa: Ethiopia, Eritrea and
Kenya B) Mozambique, C) Angola, D) Sierra Leone.
From Francesco Strippoli, Senior Humanitarian Advisor. Available on
the Internet on the WFP Home, Page or by e-mail from Zlatan
Milisic@wfp.org . For information on resources, donors are requested
to contact Aleesa.Blum@wfp.org or Marius.deGaayFortman@wfp.org at WFP
Rome, telephone 39 06 6513 2004 or 06 6513 2250. Media queries should
be directed to Trevor.Rowe@wfp.org telephone 39 06 6513 2602. The
address of WFP is Via Cesare Giulio Viola 68, Parco dei Medici, 00148
Rome, Italy.
PART I - HIGHLIGHTS (Details below in Part II)
A. HORN OF AFRICA
1. Ethiopia - information as of 6 July
a) Initial findings of the belg and pastoral assessment mission
indicate increased food insecurity and growing relief needs in the
country.
b) Budget expansion for the WFP EMOP is currently being prepared.
2. Eritrea - information as of 6 July
a) Some 750,000 people will continue to receive WFP food aid until
next April (April 2001).
3. Ethiopia - Eritrea relations
a) Department of Peacekeeping Operation (DPKO) mission arrived to
Addis Ababa to look into the preparation of the possible peacekeeping
mission.
4. Kenya - information as of 5 July
a) Emergency Programme in Kenya extended for another six months, with
increased beneficiary caseload and a total requirement of USD 88
million.
B. MOZAMBIQUE
1. Update - information as of 5 July
a) Some USD 2 million urgently needed for emergency road and bridge
repairs which will enable delivery of relief commodities.
C. ANGOLA
1. Update - information as of 6 July
a) To date WFP received only 42 percent of total requirements for the
ongoing operation for war-affected populations.
b) Findings of nutritional survey indicate stable nutritional
situation in Kuito, but mortality rates remain high.
c) New internally displaced persons (IDPs) in Moxico province to be
registered and assisted.
d) July plans are to deliver 15,377 tons of food aid in the country.
D. SIERRA LEONE
1. Update - information as of 6 July
a) Security situation still tense, with confrontations between
various armed parties; several attacks on UN peacekeeping forces
reported.
b) Some IDPs return to their homes, but others become uprooted.
c) Food aid distributed to 41,900 beneficiaries during the week.
PART II - DETAILS
A. HORN OF AFRICA
1. Ethiopia
a) The initial findings of the just concluded belg and pastoral
assessment conducted jointly by WFP and the Government indicate
noticeable increases in relief food needs. The initial findings for
the northern parts of the country are that there was a delay in the
planting of belg crops and the harvest will be much reduced. There
has been a deterioration of assets at the household level, which will
affect food security in the longer term. There was also a delay in
planting of belg crops in the south, where cessation of rains before
schedule would significantly diminish the harvest.
b) A budget revision for the current WFP emergency operation (EMOP
6218) providing food aid to drought affected populations is currently
under way. The revision is due to the increased relief requirements
among the drought affected populations, which result from continued
poor rains and the need to increase the proportion of the overall
relief needs covered by WFP.
c) A field visit by WFP and USAID to Chereti in Afder zone took place
on 29 June. The situation there appears to be improving. Sorghum and
maize harvest is expected in August, but its volume will still depend
on the rainfall in the highlands. The situation of the internally
displaced in the area seems precarious though.
d) The Disaster Prevention and Preparedness Commission (DPPC) and the
National Transport Coordination Committee (NTCC) continue to pre-
position food to key areas before the onset of the rainy season. Up
to 3 July, a total of 29,502 tons had been positioned, mainly in the
Amhara region
e) The Ethiopian Emergency Food Security Reserve (EFSR) stocks amount
to 156,204 tons (as at 4 July).
f) Large quantities of relief food are expected to arrive in the Port
of Djibouti in July and there is a need to increase the trucking
capacity there from the current 750 long haul trucks to over 900.
g) An interagency mission involving WFP, EUE, WHO, FAO, IOM, UNHCR
and UNICEF finished an assessment in the northern areas of Tigray.
The Mission identified new groups of war affected population and
found that the land mine and unexploded ordnance threat is greater
than previously anticipated. Relocation of some relief distribution
sites and opening of new ones is being considered, to avoid that IDPs
travel long distances through unknown terrain. WFP will try to
identify possible food for work and similar projects to support
social infrastructure rehabilitation.
2. Eritrea
