USAID/Addis Ababa In-kind Contributions Somali $340,291 WFP Airlift Somali
$81,000 WFP Port Improvements Djibouti $600,000 WFP Road Rehabilitation
Djibouti $1,750,000 World Vision Agriculture Amhara $330,447 Administrative
Costs N/A $141,438 Total USAID/OFDA USAID/FFP and USDA N/A Food Assistance All
affected regions $241,272,586* Total USG $251,456,180 * This dollar figure
represents emergency food assistance designated for both drought and war-
affected/displaced victims, but does not include additional food aid planned
for this year to support development programs (valued at $36,551,500).
Public Donation Information Â· In the interest of effective coordination of
public response, we encourage concerned citizens to provide monetary donations
to appropriate organizations. USAID encourages the public to contact directly
those private voluntary organizations (PVOs) currently working in the region to
provide monetary donations. A list of relevant PVOs may be obtained from the
USAID web site (). The list is composed of PVOs that are registered with USAID
and/or listed by InterAction, a coalition of voluntary humanitarian and
development organizations that work overseas. Information can also be obtained
via InterAction's web site at . Those interested in providing specific
technical services or commodities should contact Volunteers in Technical
Assistance's (VITA) Disaster Information Center for information and guidelines
at (703) 276-1914.
For more information on the USG's response to the drought in Ethiopia and
the Horn of Africa, please refer to USAID's "Horn of Africa - Drought"
information bulletins. USAID/OFDA fact sheets and information bulletins can be
obtained from the USAID web site at http://www.info.usaid.gov/hum-response/ofda
Â· The online version of this document contains a map showing the affected areas
Â· Past Factsheets can be obtained from the USAID web site at:
http://www.info.usaid.gov/hum-response/ofda/situation.html

Original_Search_Title: LRA Kill 3 In Bloody Ambush
Headline: Uganda: LRA Kill 3 In Bloody Ambush
Body_Text:
Kampala — Three people were killed and another seriously injured in an
ambush by rebels of Joseph Kony's Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) at Barogal, 15km
south of Gulu town and 1km from a UPDF barracks on May 15. The UPDF 4 Division
Public Relations Officer, Lt. Khelil Magara, confirmed that LRA rebels set
ablaze a commuter taxi Reg. No. 543 UDE near Opoka Prisons farm.
"We have advised travellers not to move at night since these rebels started
roaming around. The vehicle was hit at 10:00 p.m," Magara told The Monitor on
phone yesterday.
Those shot dead were identified as Alfred Ochola, headmaster of Agweno
Primary School; Isaac Onekalit, headmaster of Awere Primary School; and Mrs
Ojera, wife of a UPDF soldier.
Magara said two people found lying unconscious in the bush with gaping bullet
wounds were rushed to St Mary's Hospital, Lacor.
Sources at the ambush scene told The Monitor that some corpses could still be
lying in the bushes. Locals said they had heard many people crying for help deep
in the bushes late in the night. The rebels also reportedly abducted some
children from Lela bar and Barogal in Koro.
A Toyota pick-up which was travelling to Gulu through the Iceme-Aboke short
cut from Lira's popular market, Awere, was intercepted by rebels and later set
ablaze after heavy looting. The driver of the ill-fated taxi identified as
Oryang Silver escaped unhurt. The ambush scene was visited by Koro military
officers among others.
Crates of soda, sorghum and simsim were burnt in the vehicle. Clothes,
sandals and shoes could be seen scattered at the scene of the bloody ambush.
Magara said the vehicle had defied UPDF orders to halt movements before roads
are cleared and decided to dodge army roadblocks for fear of delay.
For the last one week traffic mostly commuter buses and taxis plying through
Karuma to Lira and Gulu roads have been subjected to long hours of waiting till
the roads are declared safe of LRA rebels.
Magara said the rebels were pursued by a combination of UPDF and the LDU
across Opoka Forest. He however said the roads were back to normal after the
rebels were flushed out.
A civilian at Baragol, one Oceng Wonpaco, whose watch was grabbed by the
rebels estimated rebel numbers to be at over 100.
It's reported they have retreated to Lujjo in Lakwana sub-county.

Original_Search_Title: Hungry For An End To War
Headline: Sudan: Hungry For An End To War
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Julie Flint reports from Sudan's frontline where hunger is
now used as a weapon of war against the ancient Nuba tribe
In a few weeks, barring miracles, the children in this region of southern
Sudan will begin to die - if not from hunger, then from disease.
Most families are living in the open, without clothes, blankets or clean
water. The mud hut that passes for a hospital contains nothing but flies. The
young man in charge, a farmer-turned-health worker, says 200 children and 50 old
people are already in urgent need of medical care. He is treating them with tree
leaves and bark, but he needs anti-malarials and antibiotics. "I am doing my
best," he say, "but I know it's nothing."
Less than a year after international pressure forced Sudan's Islamist
government to allow United Nations relief into the Nuba mountains for the first
time in more than a decade, Khartoum is attempting to put the Nuba beyond the
reach of relief by cutting off all access to the mountains and starving
civilians out of areas controlled by the rebels of the Sudan People's Liberation
Army (SPLA).
In the past two months, moving in whenever United Nations teams move out,
government forces have captured two of the three rough airstrips that make
relief operations possible and have mounted a surprise assault on some of the
Nuba's most productive land around the village of Tabanya.
The UN's silence has been almost as shocking to the Nuba as the attack on
Tabanya that displaced 15 000 people and propelled them towards Kadoro - a
mountain village of 150 families that has shared its meagre supplies for more
than a month and now has nothing left to offer.
"The UN's own first mission to the mountains was heavily shelled - but it has
never said a word of criticism of the government," said Walid Hamed, assistant
to a rebel commander, Yusuf Kuwa.
"When it comes to the government, the UN is on its knees. All we are asking
is equal treatment so people can decide freely whether they stay in SPLA areas
or go to the government."
The Nuba are in their 13th year of struggle for a democratic, secular state
in which the country's African south would be the equal of its Arab north. Since
the fundamentalist generals of the National Islamic Front came to power in 1989,
an attempt to defeat the Nuba rebellion has grown into a scorched-earth holy war
of annihilation against a people whose tradition of political and religious
tolerance threatens the Front's whole project of a conformist Islamic extremism.
Today hundreds of thousands of Nuba starved out of the mountains are
imprisoned in "peace villages" where men are armed and compelled to fight
against fellow Nuba; where children are separated from their parents and
conscripted into Islamic militias; where women are raped to dilute Nuba
ethnicity.
Nuba who refuse to leave SPLA-controlled areas are being driven off the
fertile plains and into the mountains where survival is a struggle. Even the UN
acknowledges that Nuba women venturing down to the government-controlled plains
to fetch water and mangoes are subjected to rape "often of the most horrendous
kind".
As evidence of human-rights abuses piles up, the government promises relief
but fights with the oldest, and cruellest, weapon in its arsenal - hunger.
Rahila Kuku, a mother of five, was put to work gathering in the sorghum that
was thick in the fields surrounding Tabanya at the time of the attack on the
village on March 17. She says hundreds of prisoners, working at gunpoint,
stripped the sorghum fields bare and loaded every grain on to trucks bound for
government garrisons in other parts of the mountains.
Kuku escaped to Kadoro after being raped repeatedly over a month, but at the
hospital has found only words of comfort - not the painkillers she needs. She
says her body still hurts from the beatings she received. She suspects she is
pregnant.
Phoebe Tutu fled to Kadoro with nine children and a jerrycan when the first
government troops attacked Tabanya. Forced off Kadoro mountain by hunger, she
joined a group of 30 women foraging for food on the edge of government-
controlled areas a four- hour walk away. The women were ambushed and four died.
Phoebe was shot and wounded. Reduced to one small plate of sorghum a day, all
her children are suffering from diarrhoea.
The government's capture of Toro airstrip near Tabanya has put the displaced
almost beyond the reach of help. The nearest airstrip is now 120km away - a
three-day trek up granite mountains and down valleys where water holes are
drying up and government troops lay ambushes for defenceless civilians.
"At one stroke, the attack on Tabanya has cut people off from their harvest
and also from easy access from outside," said Paul Savage of the British charity
Christian Aid.
"It now takes a huge logistical effort across insecure and dangerous areas to
bring the smallest amount of help to the displaced. This is a planned and
purposeful undermining of the Nuba's resilience and capacity to cope and exist."
Zakariah Suleiman, an elderly farmer, was among those captured in the Tabanya
offensive. For three days he was forced to work for the government forces. "They
burned all the far sorghum and collected all the near sorghum," he said. "They
sent us out every day in large groups protected by soldiers. This is their
weapon against us: hunger. They have taken everything from Tabanya - sorghum,
beans and maize. Nothing is left."
Like Kuku, Suleiman succeeded in escaping from Tabanya and fled to Kadoro -
fully aware that he was running from hunger to hunger.
"I'm happy to be here whether I find food or not," he said. "I have left
everything behind, but I don't care if I'm naked or clothed. Here I can go
wherever I want to without asking for permission . I may die of hunger here, but
I'll die free - not penned in like an animal."

Original_Search_Title: Latest Update on the Drought in Ethiopia
Headline: Ethiopia: Latest Update on the Drought in Ethiopia
Publisher: United States Agency for International Development (Washington, DC)
Body_Text:
Washington DC — Currently, the Government of the Federal Democratic
Republic of Ethiopia (GFDRE) estimates that 8 million people are
affected by the drought, primarily in the southern and southeastern
portions of the country. Due to the recent failure of the secondary
harvest, or belg season, this number may increase to as many as 10
million people to include northern drought-affected regions (the
highlands) of the country.
Current Situation
* The UN World Food Programme (WFP) and other relief organizations
state that although the drought situation is serious, it is not yet at
the level of a famine. Should sufficient assistance reach drought
victims in a timely manner, these organizations predict that further
deterioration of the situation can be averted.
* According to USAID's Famine Early Warning System (FEWS), the short
belg rains, which normally begin in mid-February in the northern regions
of Ethiopia, did not arrive until the latter part of April. As a result,
the GFDRE's Ministry of Agriculture estimates that less than 1% of the
expected belg crops were actually planted, signaling total crop failure.
However, some farmers in these areas have already begun planting maize
and sorghum in preparation for the meher harvest that begins in October.
These farmers are reported to be in need of seeds for cereals.
* FEWS reports that the situation in the pastoralist areas remains
extremely fragile. Recent rains in Borena (Oromiya region) resulted in
additional losses to weakened cattle herds due to water-borne diseases.
Intermittent rain has recently been falling in the Somali region, with
certain parts reportedly receiving moderate to heavy rain over the past
few days.
* The Somali region (including Gode, Korahai, and Fik zones) remains
the worst affected area, however, the International Committee of the Red
Cross (ICRC) reports that the situation is very serious, but not
unmanageable in the four districts where the organization is working in
Gode zone. Rough estimates indicate that the mortality rate in Gode is
around 3.5/10,000 people per day. Normally, a mortality rate above
1/10,000 people per day is indicative of an emergency situation.
* According to Action Contre la Faim (ACF), the situation in Korahai
zone, population 242,000, has recently deteriorated, with an estimated
malnutrition rate of 7%. ACF also reports that Korahai received heavy
rain on May 3.
* Various sources report that people are migrating in increasing
numbers from drought-affected areas in search of food and water. These
victims are often in a weakened state prior to beginning their long
journeys in search of assistance.
U.S. Government Response * Emergency Food Aid: USAID's Office of Food
For Peace (FFP) and the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) currently
plan to contribute approximately 436,600 tons of food assistance valued
at almost $160 million in response to the emergency in Ethiopia this
calendar year. (This does not include an additional 61,000 tons that are
planned to support ongoing development programs in the country this
year.) * As of April 24, a total of 131,346 tons of U.S. food aid was
reported to have arrived at the port of Djibouti for distribution.
Approximately 269,000 tons are planned for delivery during the period
April - June, with an additional 201,800 tons scheduled for July -
December.
* A U.S. vessel carrying 86,000 tons of U.S. food commodities arrived
at the port of Djibouti on April 22. This food is currently being
offloaded in stages for transport via truck to distribution points in
Ethiopia. A second shipment of 30,000 tons of U.S. food aid arrived in
Djibouti on May 3.
* Non-Food Relief Activities: In FY 2000 to date, USAID's Office of
U.S. Foreign Disaster Assistance (OFDA) has committed an estimated $7.6
million to fund emergency water/sanitation, health, nutrition, and
logistical support programs in Ethiopia. This amount does not include
USAID/OFDA's in-kind contributions of $340,000 for an airlift and
funding of $600,000 to WFP to improve capacity at the port of Djibouti.
The majority of the work to the port will be finished by late May.
* A number of USAID/OFDA-funded emergency programs are already
operational in Ethiopia. ICRC is providing supplementary feeding to
approximately 188,000 people in Somali region, and Concern is providing
similar nutrition assistance to approximately 6,000 children and
pregnant or lactating women in North Omo zone. SCF/US has established
two supplementary feeding centers in Gode to benefit 2,000 children, and
one therapeutic feeding center to treat an estimated 170 malnourished
children. Save the Children Fund/US (SCF/US) is also providing clean
water to affected populations through the establishment and
rehabilitation of water sources.
* CARE is implementing an emergency water and food program in Borena
zone to benefit an estimated 146,000 people. In Liban zone (Somali
region), COOPI is benefiting approximately 20,000 people through water
and agriculture initiatives. SCF/UK is working in Fik zone to establish
feeding centers and water points for affected populations. The nutrition
initiatives will treat an estimated 6,900 children. SCF/UK reports that
large numbers of migrants have been moving into Fik (an insecure area)
from Gode, stressing the relief capacity in Fik.
* In April, USAID/OFDA deployed a logistics team to the region to
assess infrastructural capacity and access to Ethiopia for distribution
of food and non-food commodities. The team determined that, given
planned improvements to the port of Djibouti, the port and long-haul
truck capacity in country is sufficient to handle transport of
humanitarian food and non-food aid to main distribution points.
* The logistics team also stated that logistical coordination will be
key since this capacity will be utilized to its fullest limits during
the coming months. Based on the team's recommendations, USAID/OFDA is
currently reviewing several proposals to support projects to improve
infrastructure vital to relief operations.
* The Director of USAID/OFDA and the head of USAID/FFP/ER (Emergency
Response) are currently en route to Ethiopia to assess the current
situation and determine future USG response activities. In addition,
USAID/OFDA is deploying a team in mid-May to coordinate and monitor USG
programs.
Relief Efforts * To date, donor pledges of food aid received total
690,033 tons in response to the GFDRE's current appeal for 836,000 tons.
Food assistance to Ethiopia for this calendar year will approach one
million tons including repayments by donors of 250,000 tons to the
Ethiopian Food Security Reserves.
* Planned regional purchases of food include 100,000 tons by the
GFDRE, 10,000 tons by WFP from Sudan, and 32,000 tons by the EU and
EURONAID.
* Between January and March, food distribution throughout Ethiopia
totaled 75,503 tons. The Somali region is reported to have received
22,194 tons, representing only 34% of its estimated quarterly
requirement.
* WFP reports that the GFDRE's Disaster Prevention and Preparedness
Commission (DPPC) has established 105 distribution points in the Somali
region. WFP plans to work with DPPC in Somali region to augment their
ability to track relief commodities distribution and report results.
* On May 1, the UN Secretary-General's office announced the
appointment of Manuel Aranda da Silva, senior member of WFP, as the UN
Regional Humanitarian Coordinator for the drought in the Horn of Africa.
* Some of the major constraints identified by numerous relief
organizations include lack of adequate water, fuel, and veterinary
services and irregular or insufficient food distribution. Insecurity in
various parts of the country is also a serious issue hindering
distribution and implementation of relief programs.
U.S. Government Assistance * The following table summarizes USG
funding to date in response to the drought in Ethiopia. See attached map
for geographic references. USG Agency Implementing Partner (if
applicable) Project/Sector Region (if applicable) Dollar Amount
USAID/OFDA WFP Airlift Somali $81,000 ICRC Nutrition Somali $1,000,000
SCF/UK Water/Nutrition Somali $858,109 CARE Water/Livestock Oromiya
$879,887 SCF/UK Nutrition Amhara $452,267 Concern Water/Nutrition
Oromiya $456,370 CISP Water Oromiya $332,589 COOPI Water Oromiya
$346,004 World Vision Agriculture Amhara $330,447 American Red Cross
Nutrition Amhara $500,000 USAID/Addis Ababa Airlift Somali $47,600 IRC
Water Somali $590,933 UNDP-EUE Monitoring/Coordination Addis Ababa
$150,000 ACF Water/Nutrition Somali $664,103 SCF/US Water Somali
$592,577 CRS Logistical Support N/A $48,832 WFP Logistical Support N/A
$600,000 USAID/Addis Ababa In-kind Contributions Somali $340,291 Total
USAID/OFDA $8,271,009  USAID/FFP and USDA N/A Food Assistance All
affected regions $158,397,586*  Total USG Assistance (to date)
$166,668,595 * This dollar figure represents emergency food assistance
designated for both drought and war-affected/displaced victims, but does
not include additional food aid planned for this year to support
development programs (valued at $36,551,500).
Background * As a result of three years of drought conditions, an
estimated 8 million people are currently at risk of food insecurity in
Ethiopia. The situation is exacerbated by the ongoing border conflict
with Eritrea, which has created vulnerable refugee and internally
displaced populations.
* The drought is currently affecting most countries in the Horn of
Africa, primarily Ethiopia, Eritrea, Somalia, Kenya, and Djibouti.
However, Ethiopia is by far the worst affected country in the region.
* Relief efforts have been continuously hampered by lack of adequate
infrastructure, poor coordination amongst governmental and relief
organizations at the field level, and insecurity in various parts of the
country.
* Because Ethiopia is land-locked, food and relief commodities must
be trucked from the ports of Djibouti and Berbera (Somalia) to the
Ethiopian border. The capacity of these ports will be stretched to their
limits to handle the influx of food and relief commidities.
Additionally, the roads from Djibouti and Somalia to the Ethiopian
border are in poor condition, causing significant delays in deliveries.
The situation could become worse if certain roads become impassable
during the upcoming rainy season in June.
Public Donation Information * In the interest of effective
coordination of public response, we encourage concerned citizens to
provide monetary donations to appropriate organizations. USAID
encourages the public to contact directly those private voluntary
organizations (PVOs) currently working in the region to provide monetary
donations. A list of relevant PVOs may be obtained from the USAID web
site (). The list is composed of PVOs that are registered with USAID
and/or listed by InterAction, a coalition of voluntary humanitarian and
development organizations that work overseas. Information can also be
obtained via InterAction's web site at . Those interested in providing
specific technical services or commodities should contact Volunteers in
Technical Assistance's (VITA) Disaster Information Center for
information and guidelines at (703) 276-1914.
For more information on the USG's response to the drought in Ethiopia
and the Horn of Africa, please refer to USAID's "Horn of Africa -
Drought" information bulletins. USAID/OFDA fact sheets and information
bulletins can be obtained from the USAID web site at
http://www.info.usaid.gov/hum-response/ofda.  * The online version of
this document contains a map showing the affected areas * Past
Factsheets can be obtained from the USAID web site at:
http://www.info.usaid.gov/hum-response/ofda/situation.html

Original_Search_Title: Survival As A Nation
Headline: Ethiopia: Survival As A Nation
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — The issue of Ethiopia's survival as an agricultural nation was
picked up by the media this past week.
Proud and poor
The private thrice-weekly Monitor (April 27) wrote that Prime Minister Meles
Zenawi said that the agricultural sector "remains Ethiopia's Achilles' heel and
the source of the country's instability". The current drought-induced crisis
Ethiopia is faced with symbolized "in a very painful manner the enormous
challenges we face to ensure the survival of our country and a dignified
existence for our people," Ato Meles said. Speaking at the opening of an
international symposium to review and "critically assess" Ethiopia's socio-
economic performance between 1991-1999, Meles stated that the country knows
that it continues to face "an uphill struggle" in agricultural development due
to cyclical drought. He said despite much endeavor to enhance the productivity
of peasant farmers, what had been achieved during the last eight years had not
been commensurate with the scale of the challenge the country faces in the
agricultural sector. He noted that a stable political climate and sound macro-
economic system were in place in Ethiopia in the first six years of the end of
the civil war before the border conflict surfaced with Eritrea in May 1998.
The four-day symposium was organized by the Inter-Africa Group (IAG). It
brought together close to 100 participants, including government officials,
international experts of diverse backgrounds from such organizations as the
World Bank, the International Monetary Fund (IMF), the African Development
Bank, Ethiopian professionals abroad, as well as the private sector, civil
society and academicians from Ethiopia, Ghana and Nigeria.
No privatization of land
Radio Ethiopia (April 28) disclosed that Prime Minister Meles Zenawi said on
April 26 that the position of the government on land ownership would not change
in the foreseeable future. "We have rejected the concept of changing land into
a commodity because we feel that this choice is not economically rational in
our context," Meles said.
The Prime Minister was answering questions raised by a group of panelists
and participants of the international symposium on the Ethiopian economy. He
stated that if there were to be consolidation of land ownership through
privatization, a good number of those who are working on the land now would
clearly have to be shifted from agriculture to other sectors. "But we do not
believe that we have the type of growth that would allow us to make
consolidation possible without a major social distribution and social
explosion," Ato Meles remarked. Concerning reform, Ato Meles said that capacity
building and institutional reform would be at the center of the government's
five-year development program. "We are convinced that reform is vital for our
development, and we will take the reform whatever the international environment
is," he said. He added that the government undertakes reform not to please any
international financial institution but to safeguard the interest of the
people.
Through rose-tinted glasses
According to Radio Ethiopia (April 25), the Vice Minister of Agriculture,
Ato Belay Ijigu, has stated that the agricultural extension program designed to
ensure food security in the country has been fully successful in the past five
years. The Vice Minister told journalists that the extension program has proved
that the country can ensure food security and self-sufficiency in food grain
production. Crop production during the reported period has grown by up to four
percent through the application of the extension program since 1995, Ato Belay
said. The annual food grain production that was 67 million quintals during the
first few years of the extension program now stands at 85 million quintals. In
fact, Ato Belay added, during the 1997/98 Ethiopian crop season, it was
possible to raise the annual food crop production to 104 million quintals.
Meanwhile, ETV (April 24) informed its viewers that the Ethiopian Peoples'
Revolutionary Democratic Front (EPRDF) has drawn up various agricultural
development strategies to boost the country's production output. According to
the Front's programs, the party has formulated a strategy that would help
peasant farmers in areas with abundant rainfall get adequate agricultural
inputs through extension services. This would in turn help produce raw
materials needed for the manufacturing of food items, the program indicates.
The second strategy focuses on the development of irrigation dams and livestock
resources in lowland areas. Preparing select seeds suitable to soils in the
lowland areas is another priority included in the strategy. The program also
includes the supply of fast growing seeds to the estimated seven million
peasant farmers living in drought-prone areas.
In related news (ENA, April 23), the Ethiopian Select Seeds Organization has
announced that it has prepared 215 quintals of select seeds of various
varieties for the next production season. According to the general manager, Ato
Ethan Alamo, select seeds for wheat, maize, tiff, barley and sorghum would be
made available.
Seven Days Update, a weekly summary of the Ethiopian press , is available by
subscription from Waag Communications tafari.wossen@telecom.net.et.

Original_Search_Title: Food Is A Strategic Product
Headline: Ethiopia: Food Is A Strategic Product
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — The current drought and the issues raised regarding this
drought should teach us a lesson that should not be forgotten. It is a drought
which is unforgettable than all the more disastrous previous droughts that
occurred in our country.
The story should be told and retold to our children. In 1991, Eritrea one of
the former provinces of Ethiopia got her independence.
She became independent and was accepted as a member of the United Nations.
At the end of the Eritrean saga people hoped peace will for ever reign in the
country. But peace did not reign in our country. It became worse.
The Eritrean leaders had a hidden design. It was the design of our
traditional enemies.
It was the design of the enemies with whom we were fighting for hundreds and
hundreds of years. It was the design of the enemies who did not and do not want
us to be developed and self-reliant.
The Eritrean regime immediately started implementing the hidden design
worked out in collaboration with our arch enemies. The regime started
frustrating the economic development as well as the political stability of
Ethiopia.
The Eritrean leader once boasted of giving the Ethiopians a homework for one
hundred years. But he was wrong.
During this period Ethiopia is supposed to be stripped off all her wealth,
pride and sovereignty. Soon after Eritrea became a major coffee exporter,
although she does not own one coffee plantation.
She became an important sesame seed exporter, even though her production is
very negligible. Some Eritreans imported goods through Kenya claiming that the
goods are on transit to Eritrea.
But the goods were somehow unloaded and sold in Addis Ababa. The profits
earned from such illegal trade was converted into American dollars in the black
markets in Addis Ababa.
What is more, they used forged money to buy cereals from poor farmers in
Gojam and in the Kara Kore area on the Addis - Dessie road. What have they not
done in so short period of time? The crimes committed by Asmara regime and its
proponents cannot be exhausted in such a short article.
After committing such damaging crimes, Asmara became over-confident,
arrogant and aggressive. Soon enough Asmara leaders adopted the posture of
their colonial masters.
They started behaving like their former fascist rulers. Their mentors should
have advised them otherwise.
With their new artificial and pretentious colonial posture, they invaded, in
1998 Badme, Egala, Zalambessa, Alitenna and some areas around Bure, on the
Awash- Assab road. With the secession of Eritrea from Ethiopia, our country
became a land locked nation which means importing and exporting vital products
could at times be difficuLt. For instance, Eritrea took 100,000 tons of food
aid destined for Ethiopia and 300 million US dollars worth of Ethiopian imports
from Assab and Massawa.
The current drought and famine occurred when Ethiopia is in such a
predicament. Although 16 million people, in the horn of Africa, are affected
with drought and endangered with famine 8 million are in Ethiopia.
It is to be recalled that there were much worse droughts in Ethiopia in the
past. In fact, the 1984/85 drought was much disastrous.
But at that time, the rich countries did not ask Ethiopia to exchange her
freedom and sovereignty for food aid. They did not even ask the Dergue to
abandon communism.
They rushed to save lives and Ethiopians were grateful for that. But during
the current drought their behaviour was tantamount to asking Ethiopia, to give
up Egala, Zalambessa, Alitenna, and the Bure areas for food aid.
It is tantamount to asking Ethiopia to give up what she had been upholding
and defending from time immemorial. However, the Ethiopian government did not
hesitate to reject such a humiliating suggestion and started to handle the
famine on its own and with those who did not want to put their hands in such an
ignomineous act.
Now the price that Ethiopia is asked to pay for food aid is freedom. That is
why we cannot and should not forget the current drought.
That is why we should take an effective and decisive step now. Ethiopia
should consider food as a strategic product just like other developed countries
do.
The agricultural production potential of Ethiopia has been told and retold
many times. Now is the time for taking steps, not for talking.
The need for importing wheat, maize and sorghum from foreign countries
should not arise again. So let us establish without much ado a task force
consisting of the Ministry of Agriculture, Investment Authority, Grain
Corporation, Investment Bank and other relevant government ministries and
organizations to encourage the establishment of modern private, capital and
knowledge intensive commercial farms in Metemma, Humera, Metekel, Assosa area
and in other places with adequate annual rainfall.
Food as a strategic product would have to be domestically available. The
need to use sea ports for the importation of wheat, maize and sorghum should
not at all arise.
The establishment of new commercial agricultural enterprises and the
rehabilitation and re-vitalization of the old ones should now be given top
priority. Yes, in view of the fact that Ethiopia is now a land locked nation,
that there are many drought prone regions in the country, and that there are
also vast areas with adequate annual rainfall suitable for the establishment of
modern private commercial farms the local production of food should be
encouraged now on a federal level because it is a strategic product.
To encourage modern, capital and know-how intensive private commercial
farms, the right marketing, price and fiscal policy mix would have to be
designed. The task force will have to look into the transportation facilities,
land allotment and rents, storage and marketing facilities.
In order to avoid wild price fluctuations for important food products such
as wheat, maize and sorghum a price stabilization mechanism, perhaps a minimum
purchase price may have to be announced before the agricultural season.

Original_Search_Title: Northern Ethiopia Waits In Vain For Rain
Headline: Ethiopia: Northern Ethiopia Waits In Vain For Rain
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — April, 2000, North Welo, Ethiopia It's the wrong color.
At this time of year, the highlands around the village of Gubalaftu
should be green. Yet after two years with little rain, today everything
here is brown.
And yet people plow the land, sow a few seeds, and watch the skies.
These days, hope is all they have to live on. There's not much food.
Eneinat Amara eats just once a day. The 57-year old woman tends a
small fire in the round stone and thatch structure that is the
traditional home to families here in the stark northern highlands of
Ethiopia. She prefers to eat her only meal in the evening. "It gives me
something to look forward to during the day," she declares, coughing.
Along with her children and many others in this village who are weakened
by an insufficient diet, she suffers from chronic respiratory problems.
Eneinat cooks the little bit of wheat she received from the Mekane
Yesus Ethiopian Evangelical Church, a member of Action by Churches
Together (ACT). It's not enough to go around, but the church's grain
storage warehouse down the road is empty, awaiting a promised new
shipment. Eneinat mixes in some moss and leaves, what are known here as
"famine foods." Their use is a sign that life in the highlands has grown
critical.
Much of the agriculture here is dependent on the shorter of two rainy
seasons. Although there are variations in different regions of the
country, the shorter "belg" rainy season usually runs from March to
June, and the longer "meher" season (also called the "kiremt" in some
parts) from July onward. Many highlanders are more dependent on the belg
because at such high elevations, the torrential rains, frost and strong
winds of the meher can destroy much of the crop.
The region suffered a bad belg season in 1998, a total failure of the
belg in 1999, and this year any belg rains that fell were too little,
too late. When the rain comes late, rather than planting their normal
crops of sorghum, maize and barley, farmers plant more rapidly-maturing
crops such as teff and chick pea, which are less productive. Yet even
the later crops can wither before harvest if the rains are too erratic.
The drought hasn't generated the dramatic suffering here in the
northern highlands that it has caused in the east of the country, where
in recent weeks foreign television crews have had no trouble finding
starving children. Yet according to workers from the United Nations and
several nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) operating here, a number of
"stress indicators" warn the situation here is already desperate, and
may get worse.
Migration from rural areas to the cities or coffee-producing areas,
for example, has increased in recent weeks, aid workers report, as
traditional "coping mechanisms" have proved inadequate. "For some it
comes down to a choice between migrating or dying," said Tesfaye Ejesu,
director of the Lutheran World Federation (LWF) office in Waldyia.
"If help doesn't arrive soon, those who can will leave their village,
looking for alternatives, and many of them will die on the road," said
Dereje Jemberu, the Dese-based coordinator of relief operations in
northern Ethiopia for the Mekane Yesus Ethiopian Evangelical Church.
"People who are too weak to walk will stay at home. If we want to
help people, now is the time. If we don't respond soon, we'll be facing
a situation like 1984," when a massive famine killed as many as one
million Ethiopians.
In some hard-hit areas, where farmers have no seeds left to sow and
no animals left alive to sell, people have begun dismantling their
houses, selling the wooden timbers or exchanging the straw from their
roofs for food. Prices for firewood and charcoal, gleaned from an
already heavily deforested landscape, have dropped as residents take
whatever they can to the market. Animal dung is being burned for cooking
rather than wood, meaning soil fertility will be reduced. School
attendance has dropped in many communities, as families migrate in
search of work or children stay at home because there isn't sufficient
food to give them strength to attend classes. Villages where school-
related feeding programs operate appear to be an exception to that
phenomenon.
Some relief specialists in the region suggest that a delay in
providing food aid to North Welo farm families has exacerbated the
situation. By waiting until traditional coping mechanisms have been
exhausted and people have depleted their entire asset base in order to
purchase food, tardy relief aid has insured that the eventual process of
recovery will be much longer and more painful than otherwise.
Ethiopia's churches are doing what they can to head off the incipient
famine brewing here. At Kombolcha, just four hours to the south of
Gubalaftu's brown fields, hundreds of tons of cooking oil and a high-
energy corn-soy mixture were loaded onto trucks last week. A shipment of
wheat is expected in a few days, and Tesfaye said churches are
discussing with government officials the possible need to truck drinking
water into some communities.
Catholic Relief Services (CRS) trucked the food to Kombolcha from the
seaport in neighboring Djibouti. CRS then turned the food over to the
Ethiopian Orthodox Church, the Mekane Yesus Evangelical Church, and the
Ethiopian Catholic Secretariat for distribution in communities
throughout the North Welo region. The groups are all members of the ACT-
supported Joint Relief Partnership, an ecumenical alliance formed during
the 1980s famine to coordinate emergency response among faith-based
relief organizations here.
The manager of the CRS warehouse in Kombolcha, Negase Jemaanih, said
such ecumenical cooperation is crucial. "We're working hand in hand with
other churches throughout the area," Negase said. "By cooperating
together we can better respond to those who need food to survive."
More food shipments are on the way. DanChurchAid has 5,000 metric
tons of wheat in transit as part of more than 67,000 metric tons to be
provided under the current ACT appeal for Ethiopia. Issued in early
April, that appeal requests more than $32 million in food aid, seeds,
fertilizer, hand tools, and animal fodder.
This isn't the first time that members of ACT and the JRP have
provided food assistance in the region. Indeed, giant warehouses in
Kombolcha and Dese, as well as smaller ones located in rural
communities, all built during the famine of the 1980s, remain empty and
waiting today, ready to be pressed into service once food arrives.
Aid officials always worry that such repeated assistance could create
dependency among farmers, yet workers on the ground here believe the
opposite is true. "People aren't expecting food, and only get it when
they really need it," said Ken Soerensen, a relief consultant for
DanChurchAid. "Far from producing dependency, after farmers have lost
their crop for yet another year, food aid helps provide the strength and
hope for them to go out and plow the fields and sow the seeds one more
time. Rather than dependency, food aid here creates hope."
According to Kidane Mariam Gebray, former general secretary of the
Ethiopian Catholic Secretariat, the religious faith of rural farmers
keeps them from losing hope. "Farmers in this country are all believers,
be they Christian or Muslim," Kidane said. "So they never lose hope.
They believe that next year will be better. That's their faith and their
tradition. No matter what has happened, when the rains come they forget
about the past and sow their fields."
Seeking alternatives to vulnerability When not scrambling to provide
emergency assistance to hungry highland families, ACT member
organizations here are helping people develop alternatives to complete
dependance on rain-fed agriculture.
In Cherety, for example, LWF helped local residents build a small-
scale irrigation system alongside the Humo River in 1998. Today, 886
families benefit from the project, which provides water for 172
hectares. Rather than getting one harvest a year at best from rain-fed
agriculture, today farmers in Cherety get as many as three crops. Trees
have been planted and flourish alongside irrigation ditches.
"People were ready to migrate from the area because of repeated
droughts," said LWF soil technician Terefe Seife. "Now they're not just
stable here, but the quality of their lives is improving. And we don't
have to worry about providing them with emergency food aid this year."
According to Yasin Noriey, one of the farmers who has benefitted from
the irrigation project, besides basic grains, he and his neighbors are
also growing vegetables and fruits which provide needed nutrition for
their families and fetch attractive prices in local markets.
"Before we planted our fields and if the rains came, we got a crop
and we'd have enough food to eat. But today, thanks to Allah, we are
getting three or four crops a year, and there's enough food for both us
and our animals," said Yasin Noriey.
In Lasta, an area just west of Gubalaftu, a survey conducted by
development workers from Christian Aid and the Ethiopian Orthodox Church
showed that only 2.5 percent of the population had access to safe water
supplies, and only 10 percent access to latrines. Those two factors
caused over 60 percent of the health problems in the communities, the
survey indicated. After a small scale irrigation system was built by the
community with help from the two ACT member organizations, residents
also enjoyed new access to water for drinking, cooking, and washing. As
a result, the quality of life (especially for women, who in rural
Ethiopia are the ones who fetch the water and tend the sick) has shown
dramatic improvement.
Paul Jeffrey is an ACT Press Officer. He has just returned from
travel in Ethiopia. Pictures to go with this story will be available via
the ACT web site shortly.
For further information please contact: Nils Carstensen (mobile ++ 41
79 358 3171). ACT Web Site address: http://www.act-intl.org
ACT is a worldwide network of churches and related agencies meeting
human need through coordinated emergency response. The ACT Coordinating
Office is based with the World Council of Churches (WCC) and the
Lutheran World Federation (LWF) in Switzerland.

Original_Search_Title: Mozambique Chronology 01-29/02/2000
Headline: Mozambique Chronology 01-29/02/2000
Publisher: Southern African Research and Documentation Centre (Harare)
Body_Text:
Harare — Item No. 00/02/01 - Mz Mozambique - Political Parties -
Government Afonso Dhlakama, leader of Mozambique's main opposition
party, Renamo, has threatened to proclaim a Renamo government month to
rule the six provinces where Renamo took a majority of the votes in the
December general elections.
Speaking in the northern province of Cabo Delgado, Dhlakama once
again demanded a recount of the votes from the December elections. If
there could be no recount, he said, then fresh elections should be held
after the rainy season, in April or May. He threatened that, if the
ruling Frelimo Party refused these demands, then Renamo would go ahead
and set up its own government in those parts of the country where it is
the majority party. Neither of Dhlakama's two demands - for a recount,
and for fresh elections - has any basis in Mozambican law. As for fresh
presidential elections, the constitution makes it clear that these
could only be held if President Joaquim Chissano were to die, resign or
become permanently incapacitated. From: Dhlakama threatens to declare a
Renamo government / AIM / 1 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/01 - Mz Mozambique - Natural Disasters - Floods About
113,000 people have been affected one way or another by the floods in
the southern Mozambican provinces of Maputo and Gaza since the onset of
the present rainy season. Of that total, 93,000 are in Gaza, affected
by flooding in the Limpopo valley, while the remaining 20,000 are in
Maputo province, where floods have also claimed 18 lives. In Gaza,
isolated areas, surrounded by water, have been identified in the
districts of Xai-Xai, Chibuto, Guija, Mabalane, Massingir and
Chicualacuala. The provincial government, after an extraordinary
session to assess the flood situation, issued a press release saying
that it has taken measures to prevent further social and economic
damage. The provincial government is urging all people dwelling in
flood-prone areas, particularly in the Limpopo and Incoluane valleys,
to seek safer places on higher ground, pointing out that the rainy
season is continuing, and that there remains a risk of further
flooding. The government also called upon the owners of boats to help
rescue people stranded in isolated areas. From: Floods Affect 113,000
in Maputo and Gaza / AIM / 1 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/02 - Mz Mozambique - History - Culture The Mozambican
Minister of Youth and Sport, Joel Libombo, called for the annual
celebrations of the 1895 battle of Marracuene to be a moment of
refection "on history, culture, identity and our Mozambican nature".
The celebrations, known as "Gwaza Muthini", commemorate a defeat. The
battle marked a decisive moment in the colonisation of southern
Mozambique, when Mozambican warriors made a stand at Marracuene, 30
kilometres north of Maputo, but were defeated by the invading
Portuguese army. The battle marked the beginning of the end for the
independent states in this part of the country. Libombo called on
Mozambicans to understand and value their past. "We cannot speak of our
Mozambican identity with any clarity, if we do not take into account
the trajectory followed by our ancestors", he said. More than a century
after the battle of Marracuene, he continued, the actions of those,
from the north, the centre, or the south of the country, who had the
courage to resist foreign occupation despite their military
inferiority, live on the memory of Mozambicans. From: Celebrations of
"Gwaza Muthini" / AIM / 2 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/03 - Mz Mozambique - Political Parties - Government
The plans of Mozambique's former rebel movement Renamo to establish a
government of its own in the six provinces where it won a majority of
votes in the December general elections are causing some dissent within
its coalition partners in the "Electoral Union". Thus Maximo Dias,
leader of MONAMO (Mozambican Nationalist Movement), one of the ten
minor parties allied to Renamo, has made it clear that he is not
prepared to serve in any parallel government that Renamo leader Afonso
Dhlakama may set up. Dias said he regarded any such government as
illegal, and in any case "I am not available to take part in any
government in the provinces where Dhlakama won, because I have my home
and my office in Maputo". Dias was elected to the Mozambican
parliament, on the Renamo-Electoral Union list for the central province
of Zambezia. A prominent lawyer, Dias is one of the few credible
intellectuals in the Renamo-led coalition. Without men such as Dias,
any "government" announced by Dhlakama will look a very sorry affair.
From: Maximo Dias will not join any Renamo "Government" / AIM / 3
February 2000
Item No. 00/02/04 - Mz Mozambique - Agriculture - Natural Disasters
- Floods The Mozambican government has decided to prioritize the
distribution of seeds for the second planting season among farmers who
have lost their crops as a result of the January floods in the Limpopo,
Incomati, Umbeluzi and Maputo river valleys, in the south of the
country. According to Prime Minister Pascoal Mocumbi, this decision was
taken after a survey where it was found that most farmers affected by
the floods would have no seeds for the next season. Preliminary
estimates are that at least 20,000 hectares of crops have been lost to
the floods in Maputo province. A similar calculation has not yet been
made for Gaza. The seeds will be distributed after the rainy season,
when definite figures will have been drawn up. Anything done now is
only preliminary, because there is still the risk of more floods
striking the river valleys. At the beginning of the 1999/2000
agricultural campaign, the authorities had estimated the needs in terms
of seeds at 2,275 tones of various grains, such as maize, rice, millet
and sorghum, but this figure must now be reviewed, because of the
floods. From: Seed distribution after floods is Priority / AIM / 4
February 2000
Item No. 00/02/07 - Mz Mozambique - Natural Disasters - Floods
Mozambican Prime Minister Pascoal Mocumbi said that the government's
immediate priority, in its response to the current flooding in Maputo
and the neighboring city of Matola, is to provide shelter for those
driven from their homes by the waters. Mocumbi was speaking to
reporters after a meeting of the Natural Disasters Coordinating
Council, held to discuss the crisis caused by the near incessant rains
that have been falling on the Mozambican capital. The rains have
severely disrupted the transport system, particularly by cutting the
main road between Maputo and Matola. "Our task is to re-establish safe
road links and identify the critical points", said Mocumbi. He added
that. Although the situation was dramatic, there are so far no reports
of any deaths in the Maputo floods. From: Shelter for flood victims top
priority / AIM / 7 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/07 - Mz Mozambique - International Cooperation The
Prime Minister of Norway, Kjell Magne Bondevik, is due an official
visit to Mozambique, at the invitation of President Joaquim Chissano.
According to a communique from the Mozambican Foreign Ministry, the
Norwegian premier will discuss with Chissano the national and
international political situation, bilateral cooperation, as well as
the conflicts in the southern African region, particularly in Angola
and in the Democratic Republic of the Congo. His wife, Bojorg Bondevik
will accompany Bondevik, senior members of the Norwegian government,
and a group of Norwegian businessmen interested in investing in
Mozambique. The main areas of cooperation between Mozambique and Norway
include health care, energy, the environment and the maritime sector.
From: Norwegian Premier to visit / AIM / 7 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/08 - Mz Mozambique - Natural Disasters - Industry The
construction site of the MOZAL aluminum smelter on the outskirts of
Maputo has not suffered damage from the torrential rains that hit
southern Mozambique. But the MOZAL offices, which are several
kilometres from the site, were severely flooded. The MOZAL Manager for
Mozambique Affairs, Peter Cowie, said, "The site is undamaged, because
it was designed to withstand such rains. But the problem is getting
people there". Work on the site is largely at a standstill, since the
disruption to transport has meant that the construction workers have
been unable to reach it. The MOZAL offices are in a complex near the
break in the road. Water and mud swept through this complex: Cowie said
that in his office the water was a metre deep. Valuable equipment and
records have been lost "because our computers have been underwater", he
added. From: Flood impact on MOZAL / AIM / 8 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/08 - Mz Mozambique - Social Development - Community
The MOZAL aluminium smelter, under construction at Beluluane, 17
kilometres outside Maputo, plans to set up a Foundation, to sponsor
projects to the benefit of nearby communities. Peter Cowie, the MOZAL
Manager for Mozambique Affairs, said that the MOZAL board of directors
has decided to approve the creation of a separate body, to be known as
the MOZAL Development Foundation, that will deal with community
development programmes. Cowie said that initially the board approved a
grant of 1.68 million US dollars for the new body - of this amount,
468,000 dollars is earmarked for a malaria eradication campaign. The
shareholders in the smelter, a MOZAL document says, "believe that the
measure of a successful project should go beyond world class
construction and operation performance, to include world class
environmental, social and ethical performance". From: MOZAL plans
development foundation / AIM / 8 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/09 - Mz Mozambique - Natural Disasters - Floods The
Buzi River in the central Mozambican province of Sofala has burst its
banks, cutting the road between Buzi town and the port of Beira.
Further upstream, in Chibabava district, the Buzi swamped the St.
Francis of Assisi catholic mission in Mangunde. The water on the
mission's premises reached a depth of two meters, and covered a
generator and a small flourmill. The Sofala delegate of the Natural
Disasters Management Institute, Antonio Chicumbe, said that people took
to higher ground as soon as they realised that the river was rising.
Flooding is also under way on the Save River, which marks the boundary
between southern and central Mozambique. The good news from Sofala is
that the break in the main north-south highway in Chibabava district
has been repaired. Traffic on this stretch of the road was interrupted
for five days when the Muari and Ripembe rivers, which normally pose no
threat at all, burst their banks. From: Flooding in Central Mozambique
/ AIM / 9 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/10 - Mz Mozambiqiue - Natural Disasters - Emergency
The Mozambican government launched an emergency appeal for 2.7 million
dollars required for rescue operations and immediate relief for the
victims of catastrophic flooding in the south and centre of the
country. Presenting the appeal at a meeting with donor representatives,
Foreign Minister Leonardo Simao said that the immediate priority was to
save the lives of people trapped by the floods. The top item in the
appeal is therefore a request for 100 inflatable rubber dinghies that
can be used to navigate along the swollen rivers, rescuing people at
risk of drowning. The government is also requesting 1,000 life buoys,
and 2,000 life jackets. Other items in the appeal include tents and
tarpaulin to provide shelter for the tens of thousands of people made
homeless, and generators to provide electricity for the accommodation
centres. Speaker in the name of the donors present, the resident
representative of the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP),
Emmanuel Casterle, expressed "deepest sympathy" for the Mozambican
government and people, and said he was "very impressed by the way the
population is coping with the tragedy". From: Mozambican government
launches emergency flood appeal / AIM / 10 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/10 - Mz Mozambique - Government - New Ministry
Mozambican President Joaquim Chissano has created a new Ministry to
deal with problems of veterans of the national liberation struggle
against Portuguese colonial rule. A presidential decree announces that
the new Ministry of Veterans' Affairs will take over from the former
State Secretariat for Veterans and will inherit all its staff and
assets. This upgrading of the state body dealing with veterans to the
status of a Ministry clearly responds to the concerns expressed by
former guerrilla fighters that the Mozambican State is neglecting them.
So far, however, the new Ministry is leaderless. Chissano has not yet
appointed anyone to head it. From: New Ministry For Veterans / AIM /
February 2000
Item No. 00/02/10 - Mz Mozambique - International Cooperation -
Energy Norwegian Prime Minister Kjell Magne Bondevik expressed hopes
that companies from his country will make further contributions to the
use of solar energy in Mozambique. Bondevik said that this contribution
should in particular target the Maluana Maternity, in the district of
Manhica, north of Maputo, where since 1999 there has been an on-going
solar energy pilot-experience. Speaking at the end of his visit to the
health centre he added that Mozambique has a great potential for the
use of solar energy as opposed to his own country, given the different
amounts of sunlight that fall on Mozambique and on Norway. Two systems,
each with four panels, supply power to two houses belonging to the
centre's staff, and two other systems with nine panels provide current
to a freezer used to keep syringes, vaccines and other medical utensils
as well as to provide illumination. From: Norwegian Prime Minister on
solar energy / AIM / 10 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/11 - Mz Mozambique - Natural Disasters - Floods About
110,000 people have been seriously affected by floods in the southern
Mozambican province of Maputo, with massive loss of crops, and the
situation seems to be worsening. According to the governor of Maputo
province, Soares Nhaca, about 18,000 people in Xinavane, in the
district of Manhica, had sought refuge from the raging waters of the
Incomati river, many of them on top of their houses. The greater part
of Xinavane town was under water. The navy made available two boats to
save the lives of people trapped by the floodwaters. Nhaca said that
the plantation of the Xinavane Sugar Company was submerged. Meanwhile,
the district of Boane is still isolated from the rest of the province,
and the Magude district capital cannot communicate with other
localities in the district. As for Moamba district, the capital, Moamba
town, is only in communication with Ressano Garcia, on the border with
South Africa. From: Flood situation worsens in Maputo province / AIM /
11 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/11 - Mz Mozambique - Political Parties - Givernment
The former rebel movement Renamo, through its leader Afonso Dhlakama,
gave the Mozambican government 10 days to recount the votes of the
December general elections or agree to new elections, failing which
Renamo will unilaterally declare a parallel government in the central
and northern regions of the country. This is the second time Dhlakama
has given the government such an ultimatum. Nothing happened when the
first 10 day ultimatum, given in January, expired. Speaking at a Maputo
press conference, Dhlakama said that "there will be fighting if the
constituted government tries to hinder Renamo's governance" of the six
northern and central provinces where it won a majority of votes. He
argued that a parallel government is what people in those regions
demanded from him when he made a tour there in late January, to thank
his electorate for voting for him and for Renamo. He also said that the
parallel government would collect taxes and use them in the provinces
that voted for Renamo. It would also prevent the existing central
government from collecting taxes. From: Renamo ultimatum to the
Government / AIM / 11 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/12 - Mz Mozambique - Regional Cooperation -
Agriculture Malawian and Zimbabwean farmers have expressed interest in
reactivating an agricultural complex in the district of Gurue, in the
central Mozambican province of Zambezia. Gabriel Paposseco, the
Agriculture and Rural Development provincial director, said that the
farmers recently visited the 10,000 hectares Agricultural Development
Unit (UDARL) of Lioma in Gurue, paralysed during the war of
destabilisation. The complex infrastructure consists of 73 office
buildings, warehouses, and houses for the workers, seed processing
factories, and a sawmill, all in an abandoned state. It also has an
agriculture training centre, about 50 tractors, a number of vehicles
and various equipments. UDARL land was mainly used for the production
of grain, such as maize and rice, but it has also an area for farming
and forest activities. From: Malawian and Zimbabwean farmers eye
agricultural complex / AIM / 12 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/15 - Mz Mozambique - Government Mozambican President
Joaquim Chissano appointed two more ministers and 15 deputy ministers
to the new government formed following his victory in the December
general elections. For the new job of Minister of Fisheries, Chissano
appointed Cadmiel Muthemba, a former governor of the western province
of Tete. As Minister of Veterans' Affairs, he appointed Antonio Hama
Thai, who was deputy defence minister in the last government. Among the
deputy ministers, several have simply been re-appointed to the jobs
they held in the previous government. This is the case with the two
deputy foreign ministers, Frances Rodrigues and Hipolito Patricio, the
deputy labour minister, Adelaide Amurrane, and the deputy minister of
transport and communications, Antonio Fernando. Thus Manuel Chang,
former director of the treasury, becomes deputy planning and finances
minister. Aiuba Cureneia, formerly national director for the civil
service, becomes deputy minister of state administration. Francisco
Mabjaia is new deputy minister of environmental coordination, and the
new deputy health minister, Aida Libombo, was formerly national
director of health. Joao Carrilho appointed as deputy minister of
agriculture and rural development and Historian Luis Covane, as deputy
minister of culture. The other new appointments are Henrique Banze as
deputy defence minister, Esperanca Bias as deputy minister of mineral
resources and energy, Alfredo Massinga as deputy minister of fisheries,
Eduardo Munete as deputy minister of justice, and Henrique Cossa as
deputy minister of public works and housing. Chissano may well appoint
other deputy ministers later: so far, for instance, there is no deputy
ministers for education, or for industry. From: Further cabinet
appointments / AIM / 15 February 2000 Item No. 00/02/15 - Mz Mozambique
- Natural Disasters - Floods - Gender Mozambique's Minister for Women's
Affairs and Social Welfare, Virgilia Matabele, said in Maputo that her
institution will do its best to ensure that women, children and other
vulnerable groups who are affected by the current floods receive
adequate assistance. Speaking during a press conference, Matabele said
that her institution's main concern at the moment is to reunite members
of families who were separated during the floods and rescue operations.
Matabele announced that a nucleus was set up to deal with the social
consequences of the floods, identifying the people in need and
channeling the necessary support to them. The nucleus is made up of
various national organizations, including the Mozambican Women's
Organization (OMM), Muleide (Women, Law and Development), the
Association of Women in the Legal Professions (AMCJ), and the umbrella
body for NGOs dealing with gender issues, the Women's Forum. She said
that her institution is to carry out the programmes drawn up by the
former Social Welfare ministry, dealing particularly with women,
children and the elderly. From: Women's Ministry supports flood victims
/ AIM / 15 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/15 - Mz Mozambique - Energy - Economic Policies The
Mozambican Ministry of Mineral Resources and Energy announced rises in
the prices of liquid fuel of up to 20 per cent, taking effect
immediately. The Ministry release decreed a 14.5 per cent rise in the
price of petrol, which increases from 6,620 to 7,580 meticais a litre
(at current exchange rates, there are 13,700 meticais to the US
dollar). A litre of kerosene now costs 2,780 meticais, compared with
the previous price of 2,350 meticais, an increase of 18.3 percent. Jet
fuel rises by 19 per cent, from 3,231.5 to 3,844.1 meticais a litre.
The highest increase fell on diesel, which rose by 20 percent, from
4,340 to 5,210 meticais a litre. The Ministry explained that, since the
last price adjustment, last October, there had been a general increase
in oil prices on the world market. Furthermore, the metical had
devalued by 2.3 per cent against the dollar. From: Rise in fuel prices
/ AIM / 15 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/16 - Mz Mozambique - Human Rights Mozambique's
Minister of Justice, Jose Abudo called for the involvement of all
sectors of society in the promotion and defence of human rights.
Speaking at a Maputo meeting between his ministry and Mozambican NGOs
that work on human rights, Abudo stressed that the government wishes to
request assistance from all of society in seeking practical solutions
to solve the problems that prevent Mozambicans from fully enjoying
their rights. He said that the Justice Ministry intends to enter into
dialogue, not only with the human rights groups, but with the other
official entities involved, such as the courts, the prison service, and
the Ministry of the Interior. For her part, Alice Mabota, chairperson
of the Mozambican Human Rights League (LDH) expressed concern at the
serious abuses of human rights that take place in the country's
prisons. She said that such abuses take place in those prisons run by
the Ministry of the Interior, despite constant appeals by the LDH to
the minister, Almerino Manhenje, for an end to this situation. The
collaboration between human rights bodies and the Justice Ministry
should be extended to other institutions, including the interior
Ministry and the courts, she added. From: Minister meets with human
rights groups / AIM / 16 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/17 - Mz Mozambique - Political Parties - Government
Anonymous leaflets are circulating in the central Mozambican city of
Beira, calling for the country to be divided at the river Save, which
conventionally marks the boundary between southern and central
Mozambique. According to Radio Mozambique the leaflets also declare war
on the police, and insult President Joaquim Chissano. Thus one of them
declares "Get out Chissano. We didn't vote for you, you thief, or for
your Frelimo". The anonymous authors go so far as to claim that they
are imposing a blockade on the Save. They say that they will prohibit
the sending of produce such as prawns, cashew nuts, timber, cotton,
fish and beans from the northern and central provinces to the south of
the country. Local officials of the ruling Frelimo Party believe the
leaflets are the work of the former rebel movement Renamo - but Renamo
denies the charge, and claims the leaflets were produced by Frelimo
with the aim of damaging Renamo's image. The Frelimo first secretary in
Sofala province, Filipe Paunde, told the radio he had "no doubt" that
Renamo wrote the leaflets. From: Anonymous leaflets call for splitting
the Country / AIM / 17 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/20 - Mz Mozambique - Natural Disasters - Floods
Mozambican President Joaquim Chissano visited the flood zones in the
southern province of Gaza, over flying the Limpopo Valley, and
observing the continuing work on strengthening the defensive dike in
the provincial capital, Xai-Xai. According to the provincial officials
told the President that so far 70,000 hectares of crops and pastures
have been lost to the floodwaters. Severe damage has been done to
irrigated agriculture at Chokwe, on the middle reaches of the Limpopo.
Of the 10,000 hectares of the Chokwe irrigation scheme that were
recently rehabilitated, the flooding has destroyed 7,000. Chissano took
an upbeat approach. He told reporters that this year's harvest in the
Limpopo Valley is not necessarily a write-off - everything would depend
on an efficient supply of seeds to farmers for second sowings. From:
Chissano visits Gaza flood zones / AIM / February 2000
Item No. 00/02/20 - Mz Mozambique - Economic Condiotions - Trade And
Development Mozambican Prime Minister Pascoal Mocumbi said that
cooperation in southern Africa is a priority in the construction and
promotion of the peace and stability that are necessary for sustainable
economic and social development. Speaking at the tenth United Nations
Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD), Mocumbi said that peace,
stability and development are the goals that the region has been
pursuing ever since the days of the national liberation struggles, and
which led to the creation of the SADC. Speaking in the name of SADC,
which Mozambique is currently chairing, Mocumbi stressed that it is
only with peace, stability and political will that one can carry
forward strategies for the globalisation and liberalisation of the
world economy. On the economic front SADC had also chalked up
noteworthy successes, Mocumbi said, mentioning in particular the trade
protocol which aims to setup a southern African free trade area. Among
the main challenges facing the region, Mocumbi mentioned the
eradication of poverty, the combat against unemployment, and the fight
against killer diseases notably malaria and AIDS. From: Mocumbi
addresses UNCTAD / AIM / 20 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/21- Mz Mozambique - Journalism - Obituary Mozambican
journalist Ian Christie, founder of the AIM English service, died in a
Johannesburg clinic. He was suffering from complications that arose
after an operation for cancer of the Esophagus. Iain Christie was born
in Edinburgh in 1943. After working as a reporter on various British
newspapers, he moved to Tanzania in 1970. In Dar Es Salaam, Christie
met leaders of Frelimo, and helped edit the Frelimo English language
magazine "Mozambique Revolution". In 1972, he visited the liberated
areas of the northern province of Cabo Delgado, in the company of
Frelimo President Samora Machel. In 1976, Christie was asked to train
Zimbabwean reporters on "The Voice of Zimbabwe", the radio programme
beamed into Zimbabwe using Radio Mozambique equipment. With the
independence of Zimbabwe in 1980, "The Voice of Zimbabwe" ceased
broadcasting, and in its place Radio Mozambique established its own
English language External Service, with Christie as its director.
Christie is also the author of "Machel of Mozambique", the only full-
length biography of Mozambique's first president. He leaves a widow,
and two children. From: Obituary: Iain Christie / AIM / 21 February
2000
Item No. 00/02/21 - Mz Mozambique - Energy - Floods The Mozambican
Electricity Company (EDM) will need about 4.5 million US dollars to
reinstate power lines destroyed by the floods in Maputo province. The
paper cites sources present at a meeting between EDM and a team from
the Mozambican ministry of Mineral Resources and Energy, to draw up a
balance sheet of the damage caused by the floods and discuss strategies
for its repair. "A preliminary estimate shows that to repair the
(transmission) lines 4.5 million US dollars will be needed", said the
source, adding that "now we will have to see how we can mobilise those
resources to repair the damage in the whole of Maputo city and
province". Several pylons and transmission lines were knocked down by
the floods, leaving entire suburbs and neighborhoods in the dark for
days, and affecting a large number of economic and social units. From:
Flood damage: EDM needs 4.5 Million US dollars / AIM / 21 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/22 - Mz Mozambique - International Cooperation -
Peace Agreement Mozambican President Joaquim Chissano encouraged the
warring parties in Burundi to continue dialogue as the only way to
attain a lasting peace in that country. Speaking at a summit on the
pacification of Burundi, held in the Tanzanian town of Arusha, Chissano
stressed that continuous dialogue and compromise are important
instruments in any peace negotiation, "particularly during the crucial
stage of implementing the final agreement". Opening his speech,
Chissano paid tribute to the late Julius Nyerere, former President of
Tanzania, for his role in the mediation of the conflict in Burundi.
Nyerere has been replaced in this role by former South African
President Nelson Mandela. To give an example of how peace can be
attained through dialogue, Chissano recalled the war of destabilisation
in Mozambique, and how it ended through dialogue, involving religious
leaders and heads of state from other countries in the region. He noted
that, despite all this, and through dialogue, reconciliation, and a
spirit of tolerance, "we are strengthening the culture of peace in our
country". From: Dialogue alone will bring peace to Burundi / AIM / 22
February 2000
Item No. 00/02/23 - Mz Mozambique - Political Parties A group of
seven minor parties, none of them represented in parliament, have
formed the grandiloquently named "United Front for the Salvation of
Democracy" (FUSADE), whose principal aim seem indistinguishable from
that of the former rebel movement Renamo - namely, the cancellation of
the results of the December general elections, won by President Joaquim
Chissano and the Frelimo Party. The coordinator of FUSADE is Yaqub
Sibindy, leader of PIMO (Independent Party of Mozambique). At a FUSADE
press conference, Sibindy alleged that in reality Renamo and its leader
Afonso Dhlakama had won the elections. This was a distortion of what
the court had said. Those votes were from polling stations whose
results could not be included in the final count, because of
insuperable errors in the polling station notices. "Chissano and
Frelimo were elected fraudulently, and so they have to give up power",
declared Sibindy. If they declined to leave office, then FUSADE would
take unspecified "other measures". All those FUSADE parties which stood
in the December did extremely badly. They have few members and no
credibility. From: Minor Parties echo Renamo / AIM / 23 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/24 - Mz Mozambique - International Cooperation -
Natural Disasters The Mozambican government appealed to the
international community for over 65.5 million US dollars to help
rebuild the areas that have been shattered by catastrophic flooding.
Introducing the appeal, Foreign Minister Leonardo Simao said that the
flooding has caused at least 70 deaths, and affected at least 300,000
people in seven of the country's 11 provinces. These are people who
have lost their homes or livelihoods, and are in need of immediate
assistance. Not surprisingly, the largest single item in the appeal is
for the repair of roads and bridges, costed at 30.5 million dollars.
The repair of railway lines will cost over 5.9 million dollars, and
rehabilitation of the damaged electricity grid requires a further 4.5
million dollars. Food aid is costed at 7.1 million dollars, for the
period up to mid-August. This is to provide flood victims with a basic
ration of maize, beans, oil and sugar. It is also planned to provide
high-energy biscuits to children and to adults suffering from
malnutrition. A further 2.4 million dollars is requested for health
care, particularly for disease control and prevention. The appeal also
has components of seven million dollars for the supply of clean water,
1.5 million dollars for shelter and resettlement, and three million
dollars for agriculture. From: Government appeals for over 65 million
dollars / AIM / 24 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/24 - Mz Mozambique - Economic Conditions - Debt The
London-based coalition of NGOs, Jubilee 2000, has called for the urgent
cancellation of all of Mozambique's foreign debts, in the wake of the
disastrous flooding in the south and centre of he country. In a press
release, Jubilee 2000 denounced the fact that, even as Mozambique
appeals for emergency relief, it is forced to pay out 1.4 million US
dollars a week in debt servicing. Jubilee 2000 noted that the British
government has so far promised 1.2 million US dollars in response to
the flood appeal. This happens to be much the same as the amount
Mozambique is forced to pay every week in debt service to its
international creditors, it said. The release urged creditors "to stop
taking payments now", saying Mozambique desperately needs to keep the
dollars it currently pays out. From: NGO Coalition calls for scrapping
Mozambican debt / AIM / 24 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/25 - Mz Mozambique - Transports And Communications
Mozambique's state-owned airline, LAM, and its Portuguese counterpart,
TAP, are to start a new operational and commercial arrangement, whereby
they are to share the flights between Maputo and Lisbon. To this
effect, the chairpersons of the respective management boards, Jose
Viegas for LAM, and Norberto Pilar for TAP, signed an agreement in
Maputo. The growing demand for flights between Mozambique and Portugal,
both for tourism and for business, is one of the reasons that dictated
this step. This will allow an increase in the number of flights between
Mozambique and Portugal, from the current two a week to three a week by
the end of March, and increasing to four a week as from 4 June. One of
the Maputo-Lisbon flights will also call at Beira. As for the tariffs,
they will be the same for both companies. Promotional tariffs for
economic class will be fixed at 6.5 million meticais (about 450 US
dollars at current exchange rates) for a return ticket between Maputo
or Beira and Lisbon. This new agreement between LAM and TAP was enter
into force on 26 March 2000. From: Agreement between LAM and TAP / AIM
/ 25 February 2000
Item No. 00/02/28 - Mz Mozambique - Education - Regional Cooperation
Teachers from the Portuguese Speaking African Countries (PALOP) are
facing similar problems that include undervaluing by their respective
governments, poor working conditions and low wages, said participants
to a conference of PALOP teachers' trade unions, in Maputo. In the
opening speech, Mozambican Education Minister Alcido Nguenha said that
teachers are the main factor for the development of education in the
country. He acknowledged that working conditions for teachers are very
poor, inasmuch that some of them do not even have buildings for the
classes they are to teach. Emanuel Fantoma, representative of Education
International, said, however, that low wages are not sufficient reason
for teachers to quit public education. Fantoma added that teachers also
face classes that contain far too many pupils, and that the training
period for teachers is too short. The result is poor lessons for the
pupils. "When education is not successful, teachers are blamed", said
Fantoma, while stressing the need to work out strategies to overcome
these problems. The meeting, aims to give the participants an
opportunity to exchange experiences on how to organize trade unions,
and how each of them has been dealing with the problems they come
across. From: PALOP teachers facing common problems / AIM / 28 February
2000
