Original_Search_Title: An Ounce of Prevention is Better than a Pound of Cure
Headline: Eritrea: An Ounce of Prevention is Better than a Pound of Cure
Body_Text:
Asmara — As famine threatens Ethiopia and images of starving children are
flashed on television screens, neighboring Eritrea is also feeling the effects
of prolonged drought, but in contrast to Ethiopia, is managing to avoid a
similar human tragedy.
One might well ask: "How is Eritrea avoiding the humanitarian catastrophe
being played out in Ethiopia?" After all, more than a quarter of a million
people have been internally displaced by the war with Ethiopia, losing their
economic means of survival. Of these, almost 150,000 are living in makeshift
temporary camps and are dependent on aid for their survival. Another 67,000
people of Eritrean origin have been illegally expelled from Ethiopia with
little more than the clothes on their back while their property and assets,
estimated at $800 million, were confiscated. In addition to the above, more
than 200,000 people in 11 sub-regions in the coastal areas have been severely
impacted by drought conditions. If you add up the numbers, you get nearly
750,000 people in need of assistance - more than 20% of Eritrea's population.
Having just returned from a two-week assessment visit to Eritrea where I
toured the camps in Debub and Gash Barka, I saw no signs of famine and
starvation, no epidemics, even in the camps with the harshest circumstances. It
is critical, however, to underscore that while Eritrea has managed to stave off
a crisis so far, internal resources are stretched to the maximum and the needs
are quickly outstripping the available resources to meet them. In the present
situation of "no war, no peace" that has prevailed since the heavy fighting of
1998, the people in the camps are living on the edge -- their position is
precarious and any small interruption of aid could spell disaster.
The needs are very basic. Available food in the camps consists mostly of
sorghum made into 'engira'. Cooking oil and pulses such as lentils are rare.
With a complete absence of fruits and vegetables, diets are of poor quality and
signs of malnutrition among the younger children are evident.
Women and girls, who must carry 20-litre jerry cans strapped to their backs,
walk an average 5-6 kilometers to fetch water for drinking, cooking and
washing. Sanitation and health remain grave concerns at the camps. NGOs are
working with ERREC to provide basic sanitary facilities and education on
hygiene.
Basic health care continues to be a challenge with diarrhea and upper
respiratory infections (especially among the very young) accounting for the
main health problems. As the rainy season approaches, however, concerns about
outbreaks of malaria grow.
Education, a sector getting very little emergency assistance, the needs are
immense: shelter from the sun is urgently needed, as are basic educational
supplies, such are pencils and exercise books. So too are simple recreational
materials, like balls and nets, which will provide the camp's children with
some normalcy.
I visited Jejah camp in Zoba Gash Barka, with almost 10,000 inhabitants
expelled from 43 different villages in Ethiopia. The camp is now a year old.
The needs in Jejah Camp exemplify the needs elsewhere: shelter continues to be
a problem - the tents have not held up very well in the wind, are too small and
have no ventilation making them stiflingly hot. While there is enough food, the
rations are small and there is no variety, no tea and no sugar. There is enough
clean water for everyone, but with no storage capacity at the household level,
long lines form at the pumps during their operational times. The camp's school
age children are being educated, but the youngest ones have little or no shade
to sit under in the camp 'schools', and the older ones must walk between 3 to
10 kilometers each way to attend schools elsewhere.
Nevertheless, the lesson is clear: while drought is a natural event, famine
is not! Famine is the result of poor government planning and distribution of
relief aid. International bodies and NGOs have praised Eritrea's government for
its efficient handling of the camps. That praise is well earned: despite little
assistance from the international community, the Eritrean population in the
temporary camps is fairing better than one would dare imagine. Much of the
credit goes to the Eritrean Relief and Refugee Commission (ERREC), which has
done a remarkable job in averting a humanitarian disaster by organizing the
camps well and assuring that what little assistance there is, gets to the
people quickly and effectively. Upon completing his tour of drought affected
areas in the Horn of Africa, USAID's Assistant Administrator Hugh Parmer was
quoted as saying that he was "highly impressed by the excellent job that the
Government of Eritrea has accomplished with meager resources to alleviate the
humanitarian needs of those affected by war."
It is, however, difficult to escape the irony of the situation. Precisely
because Eritrea does not have starving children listlessly waiting for death to
come; precisely because Eritrea does not have naked babies with extended
bellies, stick limbs and flies in their faces, the humanitarian crisis there is
being nearly ignored. Yes there has been assistance to Eritrea that was crucial
in helping ERREC avert a disaster, but in 1998, only 34% of requested emergency
assistance was actually received. In 1999, the figure was 40% of the total
amount of food aid requested. In the education sector, only 6% of the requested
aid has been pledged or received. At a coordinating meeting for international
donors in Asmara, ERREC representatives called the trend "worrying". It is easy
to be convinced that Eritrea is being penalized for its rational practices and
its responsible use of what small amounts of aid are received.
It may be cynical to conclude that the international community needs to see
pictures of starving African children before a situation is considered serious
enough to merit the kind of generous outpouring we saw in Kosovo. The
international community seems accustomed to thinking of Africa as a "basket
case," and so, when things are not as bad as the stereotypical image (now being
reinforced in Ethiopia), then it is easier to imagine that things will get much
worse and thus they must not be too bad after all.
Perhaps too the international community is accustomed to thinking of Africa
as a continent where corrupt regimes siphon resources from the people that
should have them. Thus, when an African government acts responsibly toward its
citizens, it is misunderstood, ignored, or deemed ungrateful. War or not, the
government of Eritrea has shown its commitment to achieving food security for
all and it shows.
An ounce of prevention is better than a pound of cure. Eritrea has shown
that with a serious commitment to the well-being of the people, good planning,
and a careful assessment of internal capacities, famine can be avoided and a
little bit of aid not only gets to where it is intended, but can be a catalyst
for positive change.
Eritrea needs the assistance of the international community to continue to
meet the needs of those who are dependent on aid. ERREC has shown that it is
committed and able to deliver aid quickly to those who need it.
*Nuhad Jamal is Executive Director of the Eritrean Development Foundation
(EDF), based in Washington, DC. She has recently returned from a working visit
to Eritrea, which involved visits to camps in Gash Barka and Debub, meetings
with Eritrean organizations, Eritrean line ministries, and international
organizations and NGOs in Eritrea.
The Eritrean Development Foundation (EDF) is a 501(c)(3) independent
organization whose mission includes supporting relief, rehabilitation, and
development efforts in Eritrea. All contributions to EDF are tax deductible and
can be sent to: Eritrean Development Foundation, 1111 14th Street, Suite 1000,
Washington, DC 20005. Tel,202-408-6995 Fax 202-408-6997.

Original_Search_Title: The Skeleton On The Screen
Headline: Ethiopia: The Skeleton On The Screen
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — The BBC's recent TV coverage of the drought in Gode,
southeastern Ethiopia, has brought to life the scale of the famine. Finally,
the international community is swinging into action.
We told you so!
VOA (April 15) reported that Ethiopia has declared that relief assistance
coming from the international community for the drought-hit Ethiopians is far
too inadequate. In a speech he made at the European-African conference in
Cairo, Ato Seyoum Mesfin, Ethiopia's Foreign Minister, said the international
community is not responding sufficiently to Ethiopia's appeal for emergency
food aid. Foreign Minister Seyoum dismissed as untrue allegations made at the
conference that the relief assistance to Ethiopia is being hampered by the
Ethio-Eritrean war.
The thrice-weekly private paper Monitor (April 6) quoted the minister as
saying, "This is Africa and the situation in Africa always gets a response from
Europe or the international community when people start to see skeletons on
screens." Seyoum Mesfin told reporters at the EU-Africa summit in Cairo, "All
in all, close to eight million people are threatened in Ethiopia alone. But
Somalia, parts of Kenya and Eritrea are also affected."
Meanwhile, according to Radio Ethiopia (April 5), Ethiopian Ambassador to
the United Kingdom, Dr. Beyene Negewo, said the drought can be averted if the
international community takes action instead of politicizing the issue. He said
that the drought was a natural disaster affecting the whole region.
Sir Geldof to the rescue
According to Monitor (April 6), Sir Bob Geldof has warned the world it has
four weeks to avert another famine disaster in Ethiopia. The musician, who
launched a Live Aid appeal for Ethiopian famine relief in 1985, urged the
international community to react rapidly. "Everyone has been alerted as to what
is going on; everyone feels a natural state of repugnance," Sir Geldof told BBC
Radio 4's "Today" program. He said he believed Ethiopia's war with Eritrea was
being used as an excuse not to deliver emergency aid. From what I understand,
there is no fighting in the area," he said.
"If they keep talking about that, it's a red herring." But international
politicians appear to be unable to agree on this point. International
Development Secretary Clare Short said the Ethiopian Government must end its
war with Eritrea as it was "wasting valuable resources". The director of the UN
World Food Program, Judith Lewis, said it was an overstatement to suggest that
the West was dragging its feet but admitted there were difficulties in getting
food aid to some of the worst affected areas. In related news, VOA (April 6)
informed its listeners that a USAID official has called on Ethiopia and Eritrea
to stop fighting and to allow food assistance to reach the needy in the Horn.
USAID deputy administrator, Mr. Hugh Parmer, said that during his recent visit
to Ethiopia and Eritrea, he had requested officials of both countries to
facilitate conditions for providing immediate food assistance to drought
victims. He said that this effort had later proved to be unacceptable. Mr.
Parmer said Ethiopia's Foreign Ministry had rejected an offer by Eritrea to put
Assab port at the disposal of the victims.
Gode: the eye of the storm
The Somali state disaster prevention and preparedness bureau has said that
the Federal and the state governments are actively engaged in providing
emergency relief food to people affected by food shortages in the state. The
head of the bureau, Ato Abubaker Abdi, told ENA (April 4) that 260,308 quintals
of grain and 8,847 quintals of nutritious food dispatched by the Federal
Government have been distributed among nearly one million people. A number of
needy people in the nine zones of the state have also been provided with
drinking water, he said. Ato Abubaker also added that the agriculture and
health bureaux were also supplying medicines, medical equipment and personnel
in the affected areas to preempt the outbreak of epidemics. According to the
private paper Reporter (April 3), the Ogaden Welfare Association has disclosed
that 75% of the population of Gode and its surroundings, which had suffered
rain shortages for the past three years, stand in need of emergency food
assistance and that at least two children die weekly. At present, the
association is running two feeding centers for children under five with USAID
and is feeding 2000 children.
Looking to the skies
Radio Ethiopia (April 6) disclosed that the National Meteorological Service
(NMS) has announced that in the next ten days, there would be rainfall in
Amhara, western part of Tigrai, Benishangul Gumuz, Western Oromiya, Gambella
and in most parts of the Southern Peoples' state. In a release to ENA, NMS said
that these almost regular rains will create favorable conditions for grains
such as sorghum and maize and to plant seeds. NMS added that the less than
average rain in central eastern Oromiya, most parts of Borena, Somali, Amhara,
eastern Tigrai and Afar will aggravate the problem in these areas and will
especially affect pastoralists.
Writing on the matter in further detail, the private paper Reporter (April
5) said that in addition to starting late, the Belg rains this year have shown
a decline in intensity as compared to the "good" seasons, an expert at the
Ethiopian Meteorology Institute said. He stated that the main factor for this
was the "La Nina" incident which occurred in Eastern and Central Pacific Ocean.
The dry weather over the Indian Ocean is diverting the moist air and clouds
coming to Ethiopia as a result of which, he said, the Tropical Convergence Zone
could not go to the north. This has created unusual rainfall in Mozambique
having sucked the moist air which was supposed to have gone to Ethiopia. This
problem might continue for some time, the expert explained.
Count us in
- The European Union said it has decided to send 8 million quintals of food
grain for people affected by food shortages in Ethiopia. An EU spokesman said
that the food aid would be sent to Ethiopia through Djibouti (VOA, April 4).
- UN Secretary General Kofi Annan has called on the international community
to provide immediate food assistance for the drought-stricken Horn of Africa
people. Mr. Annan urged the world community to continue providing food
assistance without any slackening and sense of fatigue (VOA, April 6) .
- Ethiopia and France have signed a grant agreement providing for 6,000
metric tons of soft wheat. The agreement was signed by Vice Minister of
Economic Development and Cooperation (MEDaC), Dr. Mulatu Teshome, and Charge d'
Affaires of the French Embassy, M. Francois Dall'Orso. The grant aims at
generating counterpart funds to finance development and food security projects
in road building, water supply infrastructure as well as veterinary services
(Radio Ethiopia, April 6) .
- Canada has announced a 34.3-million birr assistance as an initial
contribution for emergency food aid to Ethiopia. According to a press release
issued by the Canadian Embassy, the contribution was announced by Maria Minna,
Minister for International Cooperation. The minister said that Canada would be
taking further action in the near future adding that acting quickly was
important in order to aid children and their families who are at risk. The
release noted that Canada had a long-term commitment to Ethiopia, and that CIDA
had contributed close to 45 million dollars over the last three years in
support of food security in Ethiopia (ENA, April 6).
- Staff members of the Ethiopian Embassy in Stockholm, Sweden, have raised
25,000 Swedish Krone in support of drought-affected Ethiopians. The embassy
said the fund-raising committees established by the Ethiopian community in
Scandinavian countries were carrying out activities to increase their support
for the same cause (Radio Ethiopia, April 5).
Seven Days Update, a weekly summary of the Ethiopian press , is available by
subscription from Waag Communications tafari.wossen@telecom.net.et.

Original_Search_Title: Nutrient needs and the well-being of older adults
Headline: Ethiopia: Nutrient needs and the well-being of older adults
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — It is essential and helpful to first recognize some of
the physiological changes that can occur throughout the aging process.
The most noticeable changes are the physical ones. The loss of
teeth, for example, can limit chewing, which could require the choice
of foods for the texture rather than for the nutrients or flavor.
Many older adults are healthy, energetic and enjoy life; but some
are frail due to illnesses, social changes due to loss of loved ones or
lack of financial security. These unfortunate incidents can have
negative effects on their well-being, which would in turn interfere
with their food intake, physical activities and social life.
Therefore, older adults should be encouraged to pay due attention to
eating sensible diets and to engage in physical activities as well as
having good social life (spending time with people they like being
with). There should be no compromise in making sure that older adults
know how to take care of their health at this wonderful stage of the
life cycle. They have worked hard to serve their country and raised
their children as best as they could.
This is the time for them to take life a little bit easier, relax
and enjoy the rest of their years. About 4% of the total population in
this country (Ethiopia) are 60 years and older.
In developed countries, such as the United States of America, where
the life span is substantially longer, the older population (age 65
benchmark for older population) is over 13% of the population. Older
adults, due to physical disability, polypharmacy, the presence of
disease, poverty, and inability to chew properly are at increased risk
of eating inadequate meals that could eventually lead to malnutrition
and related health problems.
They (older adults) need to be informed of drug-nutrient
relationships. This is the stage of life when many of them use
prescription drugs needed for chronic diseases associated with aging.
They are more vulnerable to drug-drug/ drug-nutrient interactions
than they had ever been. Medication can interfere with nutrients at
several points in digestion.
Many prescription drugs depress appetite, reduce the secretion of
enzymes, can cause nausea, alter the pH in some areas of the
gastrointestinal tract and compete for binding sites. All these
interfere with nutrient absorption.
A reduction of gastric acid secretion affects adequate gastric
motility, which in turn could reduce the absorption of crucial
nutrients like protein-bound vitamin B12 (Cobalamin), calcium and
vitamin D (Calciferol) and folate. Many older adults are metabolically
deficient in vitamin B12 and its absorption is also impaired in some
older adults.
An insufficiency of vitamin B12 can lead to pernicious
(megaloblastic) anemia; calcium and vitamin D deficiency can cause
osteoporosis, poor nerve transmission, risk of colon cancer etc. A
decrease in folic acid can lead to mental confusion, nerve damage and
decreased white blood cells (WBC) formation.
Foods/nutrients influence the pharmacokinetics of drugs and alter
potential pharmacological effects to cause additional health problems.
For example, minerals in dairy products, such as calcium and magnesium,
chelate with tetracycline and thereby decrease the absorption and the
potency of the drug.
Phenobarbital and phenytoin can alter vitamin D metabolism, thereby
impairing its absorption for the prevention of osteoporosis. Isoniazid,
an antimicrobial drug, impairs metabolism of pyridoxine (vitamin B6),
found in meats, legumes, whole grain cereals, wheat germ, potatoes and
bananas.
Also health problems and prescription drugs can contribute to a low
vitamin B6 status. This vitamin is critical to protein synthesis,
needed in hemoglobin synthesis (microcytic hypochromic anemia) and
synthesis of neurotransmitters GABA, serotonin, participation in
carbohydrates (CHO) and fat metabolism. Diagnoses of symptoms
attributable to drug-nutrient interactions necessitate the
establishment of temporal (timing) relationship between relevant diet
and medication prescriptions.
Any deviation in nutritional status should be checked out thoroughly
after an evaluation of the association between a particular food intake
and the adverse reactions is completed. Older adults should seek
counsel and be educated to recognize potential risk factors of their
health.
They must try to visit clinics or hospitals in their Kebeles or
Weredas to seek medical care and advice. It is also helpful to obtain
all medications from the same pharmacy, if possible, and ask the MD or
pharmacist about timing of doses in relation to meals, snacks, drinks,
various drugs compatibility, alcohol intake, foods to avoid,
intolerances, allergies and potential side effects.
Older adults in developed countries such as in the United Kingdom or
United States of America need caution in the use of over-the-counter
(OTC) drugs (MD's prescription is not needed). Older adults here in
Ethiopia should exercise extra caution in self-medication, because both
prescription and OTC drugs are accessible to the public.
OTC drugs are perceived to be with no risk of any kind, but this is
not true at all: a common analgesic such as aspirin, for example, when
excessively used leads to blood loss and anemia. Antacids that contain
aluminum-hydroxide bind phosphate and accelerate bone loss, a non-
curable, but preventable disease.
Many older adults are frequently cautioned against taking
potentially toxic amounts of vitamin-mineral supplements. Another
common OTC drug problem, besides the overuse of aspirin and vitamin is
the intake of laxatives.
Saline or stimulant laxatives reduce the absorption of electrolytes,
thereby jeopardizing the maintenance of homeostasis and the intake of
mineral oil decreases the assimilation of fat-soluble vitamins. Another
concern of this stage of the life cycle is depression, it could surface
when concentration and memory are affected through cognitive
impairment.
Depression can cause a reduced food intake that might progress into
malnutrition. Depressed individuals usually lack motivation and have
low self-esteem.
It is rather difficult to quantify changes in the level of cognitive
functions that are normally age related, but studies suggest that
short-term memory declines as one ages. The ability to use the
application of problem solving strategies decline in some older adults
after age seventy; the individuals in this group, therefore, tend to
achieve better results on tests of recognition than recall.
Dementia, on the other hand, is presented through a gradual decline
of multiple cognitive functions and mental disorders may prevent
adequate food consumption. Excessive drinking should be discouraged.
Alcohol can negatively interact with nutrients and medication.
It is strongly suggested that older adults inform their doctors the
type of alcohol, duration, frequency and how much alcohol is consumed.
Prompt medical intervention could prevent falls and accidents, which
are common with this age group, cirrhosis of the liver, potential
depression and psychiatric or personality disorders.
Nevertheless, studies have shown that one or two drinks per day have
health benefits for this group. A glass of t'ejj, gojjam t'ella or
shamit, nech't'ella or beer (or any favorite choice), with meals or
before meals will be okay, but potential interactions with medication
must be cleared medically first. One of the major nutrition challenges
older people must face is prevention of nutrient deficiencies.
Scientific studies have proven, over the years that caloric needs
decrease with aging, but requirements for nutrients, such as
carbohydrates, proteins, and especially some of the critical minerals
and vitamins discussed on the first part of this article (iron,
calcium, vitamin D, vitamin B12, vitamin B6, vitamin D and folate) must
be met for optimum health. Older adults, therefore, are encouraged to
eat a variety of foods that are rich in minerals, vitamins and fiber,
but eat less of high caloric foods.
The Ethiopian diet is adequate when planned properly. The Dagussa
Injera (Millet Injera), for example, contains 3.8gm protein and 169mg
calcium in 100gm serving.
The Nech' Mashyilla Injera (White Sorghum Injera) has 7.1gm protein
and only 10mg calcium, while the T'yef Injera (Tef Injera) and the
Beqqollo Injera (Corn Injera) are 4.2gm protein, 64mg calcium and 5gm
protein, 27mg calcium respectively. Injera made with a combination of
grains definitely will be more nutritious.
The incorporation of steamed fresh vegetables like kosta (leafy
green vegetables) as a side dish to accompany a meal of injera and
yesiga wet' (meat wet') will supply more nutrients. An orange for
dessert can add fiber and other nutrients to enhance the nutritional
quality of the meal.
Religious and cultural norms must be preserved and respected:
Fasting foods are vegetarian in nature, thus they also can be planned
to supply most nutrients the body needs. Vitamin B12 is found only in
foods of animal origin such as meats, fish and dairy products, so it is
appropriate for nutritionists or physicians to recommend synthetic
Vitamin B12.
Studies have shown that 10 to 30 percent of older adults lose the
ability to assimilate protein-bound Vitamin B12, therefore, this re-
emphasizes why older adults must work on meeting their nutritional
requirements for a robust life. In conclusion, this age group like
other age groups should explore the areas discussed in this paper and
make good healthy food choices.
They should get involve in regular daily activities, such as
walking, stretching and swimming, if possible, in order for them to
strengthen bones and joints as well as to maintain a desirable weight.
A high percentage of older adults do have varying degrees of
disability, it is imperative that they seek medical attention regarding
their functional capacity to determine the appropriate type of exercise
to undertake.
* Dr. Awipi is a professor of Nutrition Science from the U.S.A.
currently serving as an IFESH Volunteer at Jimma University (Health
Sciences), Jimma.

Original_Search_Title: Mapping of rice gene is imminent'
Headline: South Africa: Mapping of rice gene is imminent'
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — US-BASED agricultural group Monsanto Company has
announced that it is close to mapping the genetic structure of rice - a
development it says could lead to unlocking the genes of other plants.
Monsanto SA CEO Peter Turner said in one example of genetically
modified plants being tested, farmers in KwaZulu-Natal had seen a 40%
increase in crop yields when they used insect-resistant cotton.
"The benefits are quantifiable, but you've got to remember that once
the genetic codes have been worked out, you are still at the mercy of
factors like the weather and soil nutrition," said Turner.
The research on the mapping of the rice genome will be given to the
International Rice Genome Sequencing Project, an initiative led by the
Japanese agricultural, forestry and fisheries ministry.
Monsanto Company is a subsidiary of Pharmacia Corporation which was
borne out of a merger between pharmaceutical multinational Pharmacia &
Upjohn and Monsanto Company on April 3 this year.
The mapping of plant genes enables scientists to breed
characteristics such as drought-resistance and improved fertiliser
uptake into crops.
Monsanto said yesterday rice had the smallest genome of all the
cereals, and knowing the whereabouts of specific traits within rice
would assist them to understand more complex plants such as corn,
wheat, sugar cane and sorghum.
Monsanto said the data would be shared with international rice
researchers and was expected to reduce the time it would take to
sequence the entire rice genome by several years.
The company's motivation behind disseminating the information is to
increase the flow of information on biotechnology.
Turner said certain groups had reacted negatively to genetically
modified foods and the company hoped to demonstrate the benefits.
In SA an initiative called AfricaBio was launched in October last
year to attempt to openly debate and to inform people locally of the
benefits the researchers believe genetically modified organisms have to
offer.
"The benefit for Africa as a whole is to share in technology to
facilitate sustainable food development," said Turner. But he said
biotechnology was not the only answer to the world's food crisis; it
had to be part of a broader organic effort.

Original_Search_Title: UACN Reaps South African Partnership *Provides 35% of demand Research
Headline: Nigeria: UACN Reaps South African Partnership *Provides 35% of demand Research
Body_Text:
Lagos — The decision, few years ago, by the UAC of Nigeria Plc to go
into a collaborative effort with pannar Pty Limited, a South African
Agricultural Research outfit is paying off, sources said in Lagos.
Mr. Emmanuel Obiyan, Managing Director, UAC-Agro Industries Limited
disclosed this in a chat with THISDAY recently.
The gains in the relationship, Obiyan revealed is to be found in the
access of the Nigerian conglomerate to research expertise of Pannar in
developing improved seeds.
According to him, the South African firm saves UAC the trouble of
committing untold resources to research and development of hybrid seeds.
He explained that through the working relationship, UAC seeds now
tests about 150 seeds varieties throughout the nation's agro-ecological
regions. Eventually, the most beneficial of the maize, sorghum, rice and
vegetable seeds are mass produced for sales to farmers.
Another interesting aspect of the working relationship is the time
gained as it takes about 15 years to develop improved seeds.
However much as the improved seeds technology helps raise the
production volumes of crops on farm fields, critics have branded it "a
technology that enslaves the farmer."
But Obiyan argued that with the efforts committed to the development
of the seeds, the farmers are availed the best of the seeds in terms of
yield and resonance to pests and diseases.
The gains from these positive attributes of hybrid maize suggest that
the improved seeds are the better of the options, he submitted.
Undoubtedly, the pannar-UAC relationship may have quite influenced
the present premium position of UAC seeds in the nation's seeds market.
At present, UAC seeds provides about 700 tonnes of the estimate 2,000
tonnes of hybrid seeds sold annually nationwide.
Other competitors are the premier seeds, UTC seeds, Alhaeri and
Savannah seeds-all strategically located in the country's grains belt-
Zaria and Jos. Premier seeds produces about 600 tonnes but the others
produce about 200 tonnes each. There is still much need to expand the
hybrid seeds market, Obiyan would contend. He expects the country to
learn from East and South African nations where awareness about hybrid
seeds technologies and the use of improved seeds are quite rife.

Original_Search_Title: Red Cross to provide vital link in Ethiopia
Headline: Ethiopia: Red Cross to provide vital link in Ethiopia
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — In a race against time, the International Federation of Red
Cross and Red Crescent Societies is working to help to avert a famine in
Ethiopia that is threatening more than 8 million people across the country.
An appeal for 5.2 million Swiss francs has been launched to help nearly
80,000 people in Ethiopia's northern highlands where the belg rains, which
normally fall from February to April, have failed for the fourth successive
year.
The Federation, in conjunction with the Ethiopian Red Cross Society, will
initially be focusing on two districts in South Wollo - Ambassel and Kutaber,
where a six month supplementary feeding programme was put into operation last
year for 40,000 people. The Red Cross is the only international aid organisation
to work in these two areas, scene of some of the worst famine in 1974 and 1984.
The Ethiopian Red Cross has been working in Ambassel and Kutaber for more
than ten years. Its role and that of the Federation's has been vital in
supporting the poorest sections of the community and it will continue to be so
during the next year.
The Federation and the Ethiopian Red Cross will also be assessing the
possibility of aid interventions in other areas of the country.
In addition, the Federation will be coordinating with the International
Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) which will be conducting relief operations in
various districts in the Somali National Regional State.
"The Red Cross provides an essential and reliable link between those who want
to give and those in need. Our presence on the ground ensures that," says
Richard May, the Federation's head of delegation in Addis Ababa.
Two million people in South Wollo are dependent on the belg rains for the
production of short cycle crops and for planting the high yield, long maturing
crops such as corn and sorghum in April.
Successive failures of the rains have left dwindling stocks of food and
rising levels of malnutrition among the farmers who've had to sell off livestock
and other belongings to buy what food they can.
Many have deserted the land in search of other sources of income and food.
Nearly 140,000 people in Ambassel and Kutaber are now badly affected by the
drought. The Red Cross will be helping the most vulnerable amongst them - the
farmers. Speed is of the essence if their lives are to be saved.
Funds raised for the appeal will help provide food rations for up to 15,500
farmers for eight months, supplementary feeding for 23,250 at-risk individuals
including children under five, pregnant women and mothers with new born babies.
The Red Cross will also initiate an employment generation scheme that will
provide work for approximately 12,400 households. The money earned will enable
the people to buy food on the open market.
These informal notes are meant to contain practical information for a limited
number of broadcasters -- detailing coverage opportunities, press facilities
(including space on RC relief flights), forthcoming major appeals, and giving
advice on covering Red Cross/Crescent field operations. To be added on to or
taken off the list please e-mail the Head of the Media Service Jon Valfells at
valfells@ifrc.org
2/00 Intvu nos for Red Cross delegates in Ethiopia: Caroline Hurford,
