and he was armed with a gun and was leading the interahamwe from Ngoma.
It was that attack which had driven us up on that hill, and more than
three people in our group had been killed in it. So I advised them not
to go to Mugonero as I could see it was a ploy to gather us all
together to be killed en masse.
But the educated people would not believe me, and went to Mugonero,
accompanied by another group of Tutsi refugees from Mugembi. They went
thinking they would be safer at the hospital under the control of
Elizaphan the minister.13
Innocent Nkusi had been at Mugonero, looking after a sick child in
the hospital, when the genocide began. On 7 April, he had gone out to
look for food and saw a group led by the bourgmestre and the
councillor, Mika Muhimana, murder two Tutsis outside the commune
office. So he decided to flee to Bisesero. He was there when
Ntakirutimana's promise of safety at Mugonero was received by the staff
of the church and hospital.
I had my suspicions about Elizaphan's sudden concern, and stayed in
the Bisesero area. We almost owe our lives to the fact that we were not
ministers!
Ntakirutimana also remarked upon the absence of Isacar Kajonge, the
treasurer of the hospital. His sister, Jael Kankindi, who also worked
at the hospital, described how Elizaphan used her and Isacar's wife to
lure him to his death.
On 15 April I saw the minister Ntakirutimana come in to the hospital
and go round talking to the refugees. He promised to send us some
soldiers to back up the three gendarmes who were there already. He went
up to my sister-in-law, Julie Kangabe, and asked her: "Where is your
husband, Isacar Kajonge? I haven't seen him here with the others so
far, or our director Nkuranga. The people here will be protected. I
can't be so sure about the ones outside."
My brother was the hospital treasurer. He hid in his house. After
Elizaphan's reassuring speech, Julie and I went to find her husband. We
had no idea that the servant of God was lying to us. We really believed
in him. We could not imagine that he had any evil intentions.
We persuaded Isacar to leave his house. Meanwhile, Elizaphan had
sent a message to Nkuranga, urging him to come back.14
As Innocent Nkusi points out, all of the people who returned to
Mugonero were killed in the massacre on the 16th. But the insincerity
of Ntakirutimana's promise must have been apparent to them soon after
they reached Mugonero. The pastor's son, Dr Gerard, had been left in
charge of the hospital when the American director was evacuated at the
beginning of the genocide. Dr Gerard showed no sympathy for the
refugees' plight. Although the hospital's stock rooms were bursting
with food supplies, the doctor would only sell them food at exorbitant
prices. Nor did Elizaphan Ntakirutimana make any effort to provide for
the needs of the refugees. On the contrary, Elie Muhayimana told of how
the father and son tricked the refugees into handing over their money.
He was 15 at the time and lived in Ngoma, but is now an apprentice
mechanic in Kigali.
Between the 10th and the 13th, we received a huge number of people
at the hospital, coming from the bush and the hills of Murambi who
hoped to find security at the hospital and church. On the 12th, pastor
Elizaphan and his son, Dr Gerard, came to the hospital to check up on
our health and the overall situation. They spoke to the Tutsi pastors
who were with us. They told them that we had to collect money so that
they could go to the market and fetch food for us. Everyone tried to
give what they had. But unfortunately we never got anything. The same
day, people whose identity we didn't know cut off the electricity and
turned off the water pipes.15
Pascal Sibomana, 23, is a farmer from sector Bisesero who is now
living in Gitarama. He recalled the same incident.
One day, Elizaphan and Dr Gerard Ntakirutimana came to the hospital
and suggested that people give money so that they could bring us
something to eat. Those who had money gave money; those who did not
gave cows instead. But they didn't bring us anything. 16
Augustin Nsengimana also mentioned that the water and electricity
were cut off on 14 April which, he said, "alarmed us, especially as no-
one had warned us in advance." A few hours later, their suspicions
increased.
That evening, Elizaphan and Dr Gerard came to the hospital and
church, and told us we could no longer come and go as we had been
doing, but should stay where we were because there were interahamwe in
every sector of the commune. They said that if we went outside, we
might run into them and our lives would then be at risk. When we heard
this, we began to suspect that Elizaphan and his son were going to
betray us.
The refugees, he said, had become "like prisoners", who had no
choice but to believe the word of Ntakirutimana and his son.
The minister Elizaphan and Dr Gerard asked us all for money to pay
for our food and water. They told us they were going to Kibuye in their
own cars to buy supplies for us. Those who had money gave them some;
others borrowed money, promising to pay it back when the crisis was
over. Even though we handed over the money, we spent the whole day
without food or water.
The crisis they hoped to avoid was about to unfold.
A Cry for Help From Fellow-Pastors, 15 April
A large number of Adventist pastors and their families had joined
the refugees at the church. As the rumours of an impending massacre
intensified, the pastors, fearful for their flock, for their families
and for themselves, decided to appeal directly to their president.
Their fears grew on the 15th when one the gendarmes explained that an
attack was imminent and they could not save the refugees. Seth Sebihe,
the pastor in charge of religious activities at the college of nursing,
withheld the information from the refugees but organised a small
meeting of the college's administrative council to discuss the security
situation. He informed the participants that the gendarmes were to be
withdrawn. Manasse Bimenyimana took part in the meeting.
When it was over, Seth wrote to Elizaphan, to the bourgmestre of
Gishyita, Charles Sikubwabo and to Charles' father, the [retired]
minister, Ferdinand Seburikoko, asking them to safeguard the lives of
the refugees at the hospital. He urgently requested Elizaphan to
protect his fellow ministers and the congregation which had taken
refuge at the hospital and at the church.
As the refugees included so many fellow Adventists, they hoped that
Seburikoko would be willing and able to dissuade his son from
organising the murder of the refugees. Josue Rubambana, whose father
was an Adventist pastor, knew Ntakirutimana well. He explained why the
pastors felt that Ntakirutimana and Seburikoko could intercede on their
behalf. "Ferdinand was the father of a prominent killer and
Ntakirutimana was the friend of the killers."17 The letter to Elizaphan
was entrusted to a policeman. Manasse recalled Elizaphan's response.
Elizaphan replied in writing, and gave the letter to Aminadabu
Murara, his driver's assistant, who lived at his place. All the letter
said was: "There's nothing I can do for you. All you can do is die."
Seth called us together again and read us that reply. We decided to
defend ourselves.
Josue commented:
Instead of responding positively, Elizaphan brought the militia and
the soldiers to massacre us. All the pastors who prepared the letter
are dead.
Samuel Ndagijimana, a nurse at Mugonero hospital, added:
Elizaphan had received a letter from his fellow ministers asking him
to protect them and alert the local authorities. Instead, he advised
the ministers to be ready to die, and brought in the murderers himself.
Once the refugees became aware of Ntakirutimana's response, they
knew their death was only a matter of time. Anticipating violence,
Sebihe took the women and children into the church, where they could
best be shielded from the attack. But Ntakirutimana and his
collaborators were also taking steps to ensure that they were well-
organised. Isacar Sebayesu remembered a visit from the pastor and Obed
Ruzindana on the 15th.
They came into the hospital saying they were looking for Antoine
Nzamurambaho. He hid so well they couldn't find him, but they took his
van.
Jean-Damascene Uhoraningoga added:
His wife had the keys and refused to hand them over until they
pointed a gun in her face; then she gave in. They took that vehicle and
the one from the hospital and the college of nursing and left.
The van would be used in the massacre that unfolded the following
day. Jael Kankindi recalled the parting words of the gendarmes.
On the evening of Friday the 15th, the three gendarmes who had been
guarding us told us: "Now the war is coming, and you won't escape it.
We can't do anything to help you. So you'd better die bravely."
Slaughter on the Sabbath Day: Mugonero Adventist Church and
Hospital, 16 April
At about 6:00 a.m. on Saturday 16 April, the refugees saw Obed
Ruzindana's vehicle, a Pajero, on its way to the home of Elizaphan
Ntakirutimana. The first attack took place at about 8:00 a.m. The
refugees fought back effectively, and managed to hold off the
militiamen. Augustin Nsengimana said that the refugees "kept nothing
back."
We tried to defend ourselves despite the fact that they were armed
and had come in such large numbers. We all took part, even the women
and children. We came out of the church and hospital premises and
hurled stones and pieces of wood at the genocidaires. We used
everything we could find and kept nothing back, using all our strength
to stop that onslaught.
Jason Nshimyumukiza described Ntakirutimana's response.
I saw Elizaphan's vehicle heading towards the commune of Gishyita.
Reinforcements arrived shortly afterwards.
He added: "We realised he was going to bring back some soldiers to
kill us and we were right." Ntakirutimana returned at about 9:00 a.m.
with soldiers and gendarmes armed with guns, and interahamwe and
civilians carrying traditional weapons. In the words of one survivor,
"We had been surrounded without being aware of it." The killing began
in earnest shortly afterwards. The massacre, the hunt for survivors and
the looting of the dead and the wounded lasted until 11:00 p.m. The men
who took charge of the slaughter were Elizaphan Ntakirutimana; a close
relative, Innocent Kagaba, a lecturer at the college of nursing; his
son, Dr Gerard Ntakirutimana; the businessman, Obed Ruzindana; the
bourgmestre, Charles Sikubwabo; Mika Muhimana, the councillor of
Gishyita; Mathias Ngirinshuti, a nurse at Mugonero hospital and two
former soldiers, Usia Karibana and Augustin Kanyabongo.
The assault was well organised. The assailants were divided into
several groups which together ensured that there would be no escape
route from the massacre site. The first group came straight up from the
cemetery; the second group came from the commercial centre of Ngoma;
the third group went round the hospital and appeared outside the
surgical wing and the last group came up from the church, past the
college of nursing, in order to stop anyone leaving the school or the
chapel.
Jason was among the refugees at Mugonero and gave this account of
the events of the 16th.
They kept shooting at us until we scattered; some went into the
church, some into the college of nursing, some into the hospital and
the rest into the bush.
Edison Kayihura lost his wife and three children, aged one, three
and five, in the massacre. He holds the pastor responsible for their
deaths. "They and thousands with them all died because of Elizaphan
Ntakirutimana. Ntakirutimana used to preach the word of God in the
churches, but after 6 April, the man of God turned into a brute."
Edison noted that the leading genocidaires of Kibuye had all left the
country after the genocide and that none had yet returned, but he said:
"I'll never forget their faces." He spoke of the terrible moment when
Ntakirutimana returned with reinforcements and the fate of the majority
of the refugees was sealed.
The attackers returned in force and with more determination, as they
now had some five vehicles, including Ntakirutimana's. A lot of
interahamwe from different communes turned up on the 16th, Sabbath day.
He had left his car with the others, on the other side of the cemetery,
outside the entrance of the hospital. We could see Elizaphan
Ntakirutimana at the head of the mob. Then we heard two gunshots fired
in the air as a signal to attack.
After these shots, the interahamwe charged at us and surrounded the
hospital. We tried to resist, but in vain. We had to retreat because of
the bullets. I fled up to the first floor of the hospital, while others
hid in the surgical wing on the ground floor and in the hospital
chapel. The interahamwe then invaded the hospital from the first floor.
I saw Elizaphan in the courtyard. He was wearing his usual suit and
tie.
Seth Sebihe had put the women and children in the hospital chapel so
that they could pray and die close to Christ. The interahamwe found
them in there and massacred them, while others were chasing us from one
room to another. The massacres continued all over the hospital, but I
hid under the dead bodies. I made sure that I was adequately covered by
dead bodies and soaked with the blood of others. When they thought that
everyone was dead, they started the looting. The fact that there are
any survivors is because a few of us were able to sneak out during the
night.
Edison left the hospital around 9:00 p.m. and he headed for
Bisesero, spending the night in Murambi.
Augustin Nsengimana said that the reinforcements "came in the form
of trained soldiers."
They began shooting at us as soon as they arrived, and that gave new
courage to the interahamwe after their earlier defeat. The soldiers'
use of firearms enabled the interahamwe to fight their way in, as the
danger of being shot forced us back into the church and the hospital.
The interahamwe first began massacring the people outside. Nearly
all the ministers were in the church. Gerard led an attack on the
church to kill them all. I saw the minister Elizaphan walking around
outside the church watching the killing. One of the ministers, Sebihe,
tried to escape; he had only moved about 50 metres when Dr Gerard shot
him.
After the church, it was the turn of the hospital.
I was in the hospital, on the second floor, in a room the staff
didn't use. There were some other young people in there with me. The
killers drew near our room. Before breaking down our door, they found
another room where the door was not locked. Several women and children
were hiding in there; they went in and killed them. After that, they
forgot about our room. Once they made sure they had finished the
massacre, they set about looting the church and hospital. Total silence
reigned in the hospital at midnight. Early on the morning of 17 April,
we left our room as calmly as possible, making sure there were no
genocidaires about.
Vincent Usabyimfura also survived the massacre by covering himself
"with corpses." The refugees, he said, "had been betrayed and abandoned
by everyone, even by churchmen."
Just before the attack, I saw Obed Ruzindana. He was going to the
home of Elizaphan Ntakirutimana. A few minutes later, I saw Obed coming
back with the pastor, but each one was in his own car. I was in the
courtyard and I saw these cars.
When they came back, their cars were full of interahamwe. They were
accompanied by other cars full of soldiers. The local population had
come armed with machetes. We were surrounded. I saw Elizaphan and his
son, Gerard, and Obed Ruzindana. The soldiers began to lob grenades. We
defended ourselves with stones. Those who were in the courtyard were
the first to be killed.
Vincent crawled out from under the corpses at midnight and, under
the cover of darkness, made his way to the hill of Gitwe in Bisesero.
Manasse Bimenyimana described how God's house became "the house of
the dead."
Around 9:00 a.m. Elizaphan returned with some soldiers to reinforce
the men attacking us, and they returned to the offensive. A lot of
people were shot dead or killed with machetes as they ran back up to
the hospital. Elizaphan was there, in the midst of the mob that was
attacking us. He had driven up quietly in his van, as far as the
cemetery, and walked the rest of the way. It was sad seeing him
standing there with the mob, on the Sabbath. I saw him with my own
eyes. He was wearing a suit, a white shirt and a tie.
When we ran into the hospital, he got back into his Toyota van and
drove it right onto the premises, stopping outside the laundry. They
chased the refugees through all the rooms in the hospital. Some escaped
through the windows, but others were subjected to horrible atrocities.
There was, said Elie Gashi, a farmer of 36 from Ngoma, "a lot of
shooting and a lot of dying." He added:
There were no survivors among the women and children in the church.
The man who is responsible for their death is Elizaphan Ntakirutimana.
He is the one who mobilised the other pastors and the population which
was scared to go to the church.18
Those who had managed to evade the crescendo of gunfire and grenade
explosions sought a way out. But they were trapped; the church and
hospital were encircled by thousands of civilians armed with machetes.
I ran into the surgical wing with a lot of other people, but they
threw a hand grenade at us. Shrapnel from that grenade removed the tip
of this finger, [the third finger of his left hand]. They threw in
teargas to blind us, and then fired into the crowd. I let myself fall
so that the bodies covered me.
That 16 April was like the last day described in the apocalypse. So
many people died. Those four large coffins you see over there are
filled with the victims who died in this room on that day. This room
used to be the chapel for the hospital and the college of nursing.
Since the massacre of 16 April, when people were killed in here as they
prayed, we have been keeping this room as a memorial to them. The
stains you can see on the floor are dried blood.
By 11:00 p.m. that night, complete silence reigned in the house of
the dead. That silence terrified me. I managed to get out from under
the dead bodies covering me, and escaped out of one of the half open
windows. Outside, the murderers were sharing out the loot: our cattle
and various valuables the refugees had brought with them. While they
were intent on this, I slipped away into the bush and reached Gitwe
forest.
Ismael Mbonimpaye, who had also seen the pastor in the company of
Obed Ruzindana at 8:00 a.m., said that "my whole family was massacred
in the church and I found myself all alone."
We saw soldiers and interahamwe arriving. They parked their vehicles
and approached us on foot. We fought them, and pushed them back to the
place where they had left their vehicles. Then they forced us back up
to the hospital. They spent the whole day shooting us. The cries of
people is indescribable. Some died in the church, others in the
courtyard, others in the hospital and out in the bush.
I was inside the church to begin with. I saw Mika and Sikubwabo take
up positions by the lower door of the church, while Elizaphan and his
son, Gerard, guarded the top door. Elizaphan called the minister, Seth
Sebihe, over and asked him to give him all the keys. He told Sebihe
they were finished and there was nothing he could do to protect them.
Then Elizaphan and his son went and joined Ruzindana.
Meanwhile, Mika and Sikubwabo were telling Hutu women married to
Tutsis to leave the church and abandon their Inyenzi children in there.
I saw Ntakirutimana and Ruzindana go up to the militia men and tell
them to make sure no-one got out while the doctor told them to be
careful not to damage the hospital when using their guns, because they
would need it afterwards. A bullet hit me in my left arm; you can see
the scar. I fell down among the dead bodies.
Gerard told his father that Nkuranga had just been killed, and that
he had got the hospital safe keys from Karoli. Elizaphan told the
interahamwe: "Keep this place under surveillance. Don't let anyone
escape."
It was about 7:00 p.m. by then, and already dark. They went on
killing people until 11:00 p.m. or thereabouts. Then they got
distracted by the idea of breaking open the safe. That was when I left
along with Bitahurugamba and Assiel Nzamutuma's children.19
Augustin Nkubana had only reached the hospital compound the previous
evening; he found his route blocked when he tried to leave on the
morning of the 16th to fetch his wife and children.
We were stopped at the hospital entrance by Dr Gerard, who was with
Ngarambe and a certain "Captain." They would not let us leave the
hospital. The soldiers and militiamen burst in shortly afterwards, at
about 9:00 a.m. I saw Elizaphan Ntakirutimana and Obed Ruzindana in
their vehicles, with soldiers and interahamwe marching behind them. The
minister had a white Hilux van.
They immediately started firing indiscriminately. We fought back
with stones to stop them getting onto the hospital premises. But there
were too many of them, and they overpowered us. They shot people dead,
and also killed them with traditional weapons. The wounded were
groaning and gasping and the women and children were crying. The
attackers were in a frenzy. People were falling to the ground all over
the place like ripe fruit. The hospital courtyard and the church were
soon full of dead bodies; there were bodies everywhere.  I remember the
minister, Sebihe, asking Elizaphan: "Have you brought us here to kill
us?" Elizaphan replied: "You've fallen into our hands, and you're
finished!" Sebihe took up a Bible and told him: "You will be
responsible for this blood." It was a miracle that anyone got out alive
that day. They went on killing all day, and continued until late at
night. I feigned death and lay among the dead bodies. By 11:00 p.m.
they were exhausted. They went to have a rest and eat the cattle they
had slaughtered and had got the young interahamwe to cook for them.
I seized my chance to escape them and went up to the hills to Gitwe;
I arrived there at about 3:00 a.m. I found a lot of Tutsi refugees up
there, including some of my relatives.
They went on killing people at Mugonero hospital on the Sunday, but
more survivors kept getting through to us. They were very tired and
dehydrated. We were in the Catholic centre in Gitwe, but decided to go
to the Adventist church in Murambi.
The refugees had put up a brave fight at first, as Elie Muhayimana
recalls, but the killers were well-armed. Dr Gerard commanded the
gendarmes, while his father surveyed the corpses, checking for signs of
life.
The interahamwe, who had surrounded the church and hospital, the
gendarmes and soldiers began the attack immediately after the gunshots
given as a signal. As we were there in large numbers, we tried to
defend ourselves, throwing stones, broken bottles and bits of iron
bars. We fought back for over an hour, but were defeated by the
soldiers and gendarmes brought from Kibuye as reinforcements. The
reinforcements from Kibuye played a crucial role because they enabled
the interahamwe to get inside the church and the hospital. As nearly
everyone was outside fighting off the interahamwe, once we were
defeated, we all desperately tried to get back inside. Only a few
managed it, and the rest remained outside. The killers started on those
outside and then came into the hospital. They found it hard to get at
those of us inside, because we barred the doors as best we could, so
that no-one could get in. Dr Gerard ordered the gendarmes to break down
the doors with their rifles or use grenades on them.
I was in the operating theatre. As I looked out of the window, I saw
the minister, Elizaphan Ntakirutimana, walking around the bodies and
checking to see if they were dead. While I was watching Elizaphan, my
hiding place came under siege. They broke down the door; then they
poured in and began killing people at once. There were 15 of us in that
room, including my father, Thomas Rukara, and my mother, Emerthe
Mukagatere. They all died at about 3:00 p.m. that afternoon. I
recognised someone I knew among the killers, Captain Uwimbabazi. I
begged him for mercy; instead, he shot me in the thigh and cut me about
the head with a machete. I fell down and lost consciousness. I came
around at midnight and found no-one there, neither the killers nor the
survivors. I was badly wounded, and weak from loss of blood, but I
managed to climb out from under the bodies and make my way to Bisesero.
Nathan Gatashya, now working as a tailor in Kigali, is from Wingabo
in Ngoma. He accuses Dr Gerard of shooting at the refugees.
On Saturday 16 April, we saw lots of soldiers and militiamen with
machetes, grenades, spears, swords...etc. Dr Gerard and Obed Ruzindana
were in front of them with rifles. The women and children were in the
church, while the men were outside in the hospital yard. Gerard shot at
the entrance door to the church. The militia went in, and they killed
these women and children with machetes. Gerard then came back to kill
the men using his rifle. I escaped and joined the people in Bisesero.20
Jael Kankindi, a medical assistant at the hospital, realised
something was wrong when Dr Ntakirutimana closed the surgical wing a
few days after wounded Tutsi refugees began to arrive en masse at the
hospital; "He said he had no medication to use on Tutsis." Her anxiety
had increased when Dr Ntakirutimana sent all the Hutu patients home and
gave them medication to take home.
On 16 April, at around 9:30 a.m., we saw three vehicles arrive: Dr
Gerard's Toyota, the hospital's vehicle and another vehicle
requisitioned from a rich Tutsi businessman named Antoine. These
vehicles came from the home of Elizaphan Ntakirutimana and were full of
soldiers. Behind them came a mob of peasants armed with traditional
weapons and chanting: "We'll massacre them!" They began by firing a
hail of bullets at us. We tried to hide wherever we could, but it was
hard to escape that hail of bullets. There were people packed tightly
into every room in the hospital, and in the church and the courtyard as
well.
While the shooting was going on, I took refuge in the hospital
cellar at about 2:30 p.m. Five other women and girls joined me there.
But the interahamwe discovered us a few hours later. They began hitting
us, and robbed us of everything we had. One of them killed four of the
women with his spear. When he came to the fifth, the spear broke. He
went out to find another spear. It was already dark so I told poor
Evelyne to leave. She told me she couldn't do so, as she was badly
wounded. I heard her cry out once, and then she passed away. So I was
left on my own.
I felt dizzy, my petticoat was covered in blood, my hair was matted
with blood and there were wounds all over my body. I felt very weak and
all alone in the world. I didn't know what to do. I got out of there at
about 2:00 a.m. I had to walk over the dead bodies, as there were so
many of them all over the courtyard. I decided to head for Gisovu, and
took a footpath leading off behind the hospital. I heard a voice coming
from near Elizaphan's place, saying, "Block up all the pathways so that
no-one can get out."
I lay down in the bush and hid there all day. I returned to the
hospital in the evening in the vain hope of finding someone from the
Red Cross. But all I found there were dead bodies. It was a horrifying
sight.
Jael hid in a cupboard in an abandoned house. The following morning,
an armed man approached; she said provoked him in the hope that he
would kill her, but he refused and left. She went to the home of a Hutu
friend who welcomed her and took care of her. But she could not remain
with her because her friend feared that her brothers would kill Jael.
She returned to the bush and then made her way home to find her family
had been wiped out and the house destroyed. Isacar Sabayesu left the
corpses of all his brothers at Mugonero.
On the 16th, I saw with my own eyes Elizaphan Ntakirutimana; Obed
Ruzindana; Gerard Ntakirutimana and Kagaba, Elizaphan's close relative.
They surrounded us and brutally massacred us. The murderers' war cries
died down by about 1:00 p.m., and then the groans of the dying could be
heard. I hid among the dead bodies. When they thought they had killed
us all that Sabbath afternoon, I took a look at what was going on
outside that house of death. I saw the lecturer Kagaba get into the van
that the college director had left outside. It was a large white van
with black lines on the sides. The college director, Nkuranga, was dead
by then.
I remained inside, in the midst of the dead bodies of my brothers,
until 1:00 a.m., because the murderers were still outside celebrating
and sharing out their loot.
Isacar took the familiar route towards Murambi on his way to
Bisesero.
Josue Rubambana is the younger brother of Jean Nkuranga, one of the
men who was lured to the hospital by Ntakirutimana's promise of
protection. He works in a factory in Kigali. He accompanied his brother
when he agreed to return to the hospital. Nkuranga was killed in the
massacre of 16 April as detailed above, along with many other members
of Josue's family, including his wife, Vera Mushimiyimana, and his
daughter of six months, Sylvie Kamaliza. Josue named the men who
carried out the massacre.
On 16 April, in the morning, I saw Elizaphan Ntakirutimana in his
car with policemen. He had parked his car in front of the church of
Ngoma. The policemen got out of the car and they began to surround us.
A lot of cars filled up with soldiers and militiamen also came. I saw
the prefet of Kibuye, Clement Kayishema, councillor Mika, Obed
Ruzindana and Gerard Ntakirutimana. During the massacre, Elizaphan was
in front of the other militiamen. They encircled us, threw grenades and
use their machetes, guns and swords. Many Tutsis died immediately.
There were too many of us. They killed from 9:00 a.m. until 11:00 p.m.
I was covered with corpses and with blood. The militiamen didn't
think that I was still alive. At about 1:00 a.m., I went to Bisesero
with some other survivors.
At least 50 Adventist pastors died in the massacre. They include
Seth Sebihe; Ezechiel Semugeshi; Isaac Rucondo; Jerome Gakwaya; Eliezer
Seromba; Seth Rwanyabuyo; Samuel Muhayimana; Esias Nzamwita; Ezechias
Ngirinshuti and Semafaranga. Why, Josue asked, did Ntakirutimana do
nothing to help his fellow-pastors.
What did Ntakirutimana lack to save at leas the Tutsi pastors who
had worked with him? He had the means to look for a pirogue [small
boat] to take them to Idjwi Island in Zaire?
The Foreman of the Killers: Destroying Lives and Looting at the
Adventist Church in Murambi
Murambi lies between sector Ngoma and sector Bisesero in Gishyita.
Refugees, many of them badly wounded in Mugonero and elsewhere, sought
safety and shelter from the rain and cold in the Adventist Church in
Murambi. There were also many Tutsis hiding in the thick forest of
Gitwe near the church. This part of Gishyita was populated principally
by Tutsis, making it an obvious target for the genocidaires. The women,
children and the wounded would spend the night in Murambi church then
return to Gitwe in the morning. For the survivors of Mugonero, there
was no respite. Just one day after they arrived, they were again the
prey of the same group of killers who orchestrated the massacre at
Mugonero. And Ntakirutimana not only organised the killing, but he
ordered his men to loot from the church. Jean Uhoraningoga identified
the men he saw in Murambi.
Elizaphan, Ruzindana, Dr Gerard, Sikubwabo and Mika drove here to
Murambi with the militia and soldiers who were fully armed. They opened
fire on us as they had done in the hospital. Elizaphan took all the
tiles off the church roof; wounded Tutsis had been hiding in there, but
he had them all killed first. We were not far away from the church. I
heard him say: "If they all die, we'll see about building some more
churches."
Augustin Nkubana explained why the pastor ordered the destruction of
the church.
Elizaphan, the bourgmestre and Ruzindana turned up at the church to
finish off the wounded. They took all tiles off the roof so the Tutsis
would have nowhere to shelter. It was the pastor, Elizaphan, who had
the church demolished. He had all the tiles loaded into his white Hilux
van.
At around 8:00 a.m. that day we saw some vehicles arriving,
including Dr Gerard's white Toyota, and a green lorry. As soon as we
saw them, we got out of the church and hid behind it, but there were
wounded people who couldn't get away. They killed them first before
stripping the roof.
They went on firing at us until 5:00 p.m., and then left. We tried
to fight back with stones, but we mainly hid in the bush. They were
hunting us down like the deer.
Didas Hitimana used to frequent the church in Murambi. He went there
after his family perished in Mugonero.
On Sunday, 17 April, they killed the Tutsis in the Adventist chapel
at the bottom of the hill. Then the chapel was demolished and
Ntakirutimana drove off with the roof tiles in his van. I was close to
the church. He came back and the attackers turned in our direction.
There were a lot of Tutsis on that hill, and we tried to repel them by
throwing stones and by hiding people in the forest to ambush them.
Vincent Usabyimfura and his companions ran into the bush as soon as
they saw vehicles approaching.
Very early in the morning, I saw Elizaphan Ntakirutimana, with his
car full of militiamen. He was driving. They went to the church of
Murambi. They killed all the wounded people, all the women and
children. Afterwards, they destroyed the church and transported the
roof tiles in Ntakirutimana's car. I was with Vianney Ntaganira, but
had to leave him behind because he couldn't run. He died with the other
wounded people in the church in Murambi.
After his wife, three young children, parents, brother and three
sisters died in Mugonero, Edison Kayihura left the church and sought
refuge in Murambi, only to be caught up in more killings. Again
Ntakirutimana was present.
On 17 April I saw the Ntakirutimanas, father and son, at Murambi.
The father was still wearing a suit and tie; Dr Gerard was in a white
T-shirt and white shorts, dressed as a sportsman and armed with a gun.
I saw them with other interahamwe, killing the people in that chapel. I
was some distance away in an abandoned, roofless house. After the
massacre, I headed for Bisesero with the others who had managed to
scramble out of the chapel and hide in the bush.
Elidade Kagabo, 14 at the time and now a student in Kigali,
described Elizaphan Ntakirutimana as "our pastor", saying that "he and
his family were born into, lived and grew up in the Adventist Church of
which Ntakirutimana was the president." Dr Gerard had looked after the
family's health problems. While some relatives went to Mugonero,
Elidade, his brothers and sisters opted for Murambi. The refugees were,
he said, "taken by complete surprise."
No one was expecting such an attack. As soon as they arrived, they
surrounded the church. Then Dr Gerard told his militiamen to start
work. They killed people in the main body of the church and in the
rooms and annexes where church documents were kept. Dr Gerard then told
the interahamwe at least five times to make sure everyone was dead, and
that no one was left alive under the dead bodies. He found five
children and two women survivors where the women had been hiding. He
got them out, and ordered Karibana to kill them. Karibana carried out
the orders at once.
Some of us tried to escape from the interahamwe. Dr Gerard ordered
them to run after us and catch us. From where we were hiding, we heard
the minister, Elizaphan, telling the interahamwe that if they had
finished killing, they could destroy the church. After they destroyed
the church, he ordered them to stack the roof tiles in his van, so he
could take them home. I was with my elder brother, Jean Butera, and my
younger brother Niyongira.21
Jael Kankindi joined the survivors in Murambi before proceeding to
Bisesero.
Dr Gerard, Mathias Ngirinshuti and others used to come on killing
raids to Murambi. The pastor Elizaphan had all the tiles taken off the
church roof, and they massacred all the wounded Tutsis who had been
hiding inside. The interahamwe were shouting that God had abandoned us
and delivered us to them.
Ismael Mbonimpaye spoke of the days "we were hunted down like
animals."
I saw Elizaphan drive up in his Hilux, and strip the tiles off the
church roof so that the Tutsis would have nowhere to shelter. We fought
back as best we could. Those who survived fled up to Bisesero.
When he heard the sound of a vehicle, Manasse Bimenyimana
immediately jumped out of the window.
We hid in the forest near Gitwe, where I could see what was going
on. The vehicle I could hear belonged to Elizaphan. He had come in
person with his son, Dr Gerard and some other young interahamwe, to
kill and loot. Elizaphan was still wearing a suit and tie. His son was
wearing white shorts and a white T-shirt. Gerard had a high-powered
rifle, and was firing at people running away, while the interahamwe
were inside the chapel, killing the refugees. The massacre did not take
very long.
I then heard Elizaphan ordering the young people to take the tiles
off the chapel roof. I saw them doing so, and loading the tiles into
Elizaphan's van. Then, as I watched, they left for home.
Manasse left for Bisesero where he was wounded by gunshots in the
left thigh.
Alexandre Rwihimba, 39, was living in sector Muramba when his home
came under siege. He went to Murambi church and witnessed the massacre.
Soon afterwards he arrived in Bisesero.
Very early one morning, Dr Gerard Ntakirutimana, his father,
Elizaphan, the councillor of Ngoma, Abel Bahunde, and various cronies,
accompanied by the militia, launched a large-scale attack on the church
in Murambi where the survivors from the Mugonero massacre were hiding,
along with some other Tutsis, including myself. Elizaphan ordered the
militia to demolish the church and remove its roof tiles. After they
had taken all the tiles off the roof, he made them stack them in his
van. He added: "Once we've settled the Tutsi problem, we'll repair the
church."
Despite Ntakirutimana's entreaties, John Ndahimana, who lived in
Ngoma, did not join the other Tutsis in Mugonero. Anticipating the need
for self-defence, he decided to go to Murambi because of its proximity
to Bisesero where "there were enough Tutsis for us to fight back."
John, 38, is a farmer.
I saw Ntakirutimana during the battle of Murambi. He had come with
the interahamwe who had killed the refugees in the church there. I saw
Ntakirutimana and his son, the doctor, during that attack. I escaped
through the window and went into the forest. Gerard was there, shooting
the refugees who tried to run away. He had a rifle. He was wearing a
white T-shirt over white shorts. He had come in his white car, while
his father had brought his van. After his men finished killing people
in the chapel, Elizaphan ordered it to be demolished. He was standing
there like a foreman as the interahamwe loaded his van with the roof
tiles, then he got into the van and drove off with the booty. The
interahamwe then chased the survivors from the chapel up into the
hills, and killed them together with the other Tutsis from Murambi.22
Many of the Tutsis who escaped the massacre of 17 April left
immediately for Bisesero. Those who remained, like Didas Hitimana, said
the refugees concentrated on self-defence; three days later,
Ntakirutimana and his collaborators returned.
They gave us a break of three days. We used the time to organise
more effectively to resist any future attacks, but they too were
getting ready with more men and weapons to exterminate us. They came on
20 April, with the residents of the four nearby communes-Gitesi,
Gishyita, Gisovu and Rwamatamu. There were so many of them that they
could have exterminated us, but fortunately they began by attacking our
herd of cattle. They slaughtered some right there, and went off with
the rest. The herdsmen who tried to defend their cattle were massacred
by the mob, but the rest of the Tutsis were spared that day.
John Ndahimana had also remained in Murambi.
Elizaphan returned shortly afterwards, along with Murakaza's son,
Obed Ruzindana. As time went by, the number of interahamwe increased.
Some of them brought their dogs to hunt down those who had fled into
the forest, and others hired Batwa, who are specialists in hunting.
As I was joining our people to fight Ntakirutimana's men, a bullet
hit me in the right shoulder, and stopped me from throwing any more
stones. I moved back into the forest.
Innocent Mazimpaka, an employee of the college of nursing aged 27,
was one of the young men who tried to keep the killers at bay.
They withdrew in the evening, after killing some people, including
Jean and Alexandre Munyankundo. We killed two interahamwe. Mika told
them to retreat, because he saw it would be hard to defeat us. He was
leading the attack that day, along with Sikubwabo and Gerard.
They came back the next day and surrounded us on higher ground. We
scattered. I saw Elizaphan in his Hilux that day, heading for the
church. They set fire to the bush, but our hiding place was untouched.
Hoping to find safety in numbers, Innocent went to Bisesero and
joined the refugees' defence team.23 There was no way to escape the
pastor and his companion in arms, said Vincent Mutwewingabo.
I saw Dr Gerard; his father, Elizaphan; Mika; Sikubwabo and Karibana
when they came to Murambi. They killed our people and forced us to flee
to Bisesero. But even there they still came after us.
Bisesero: The Last Battle
The story of the struggle for survival of the Tutsis who gathered at
Bisesero illustrates just how relentless the genocide was. Some 50,000
Tutsis had gathered on these hills, hoping to hide in the many bushes
and rocks on the steep slopes. They worked together to form a strong
fighting force which was at first successful in holding back the
interahamwe.24 Women and children collected stones to throw at them
while the men used traditional weapons and managed to win arms from
their attackers. They congregated on Muyira hill to coordinate their
defence, battling not only against repeated onslaughts from the
militia, but against the cold, rain and hunger. Many of them were
wounded, but had no access to treatment. Women, children and the old
were most vulnerable as they could not run as fast as the men. With
each new day they fought off further attacks and they became
progressively weaker.
Despite their brave defence, the number of corpses increased
steadily, but the refugees knew there was nowhere else they could go.
Then, in early May, there was a lull in the fighting. The killers were
gathering reinforcements in preparation for a massive offensive. On 13
May, the forces of the genocide arrived in Bisesero in strength. All
the genocidaires and militiamen from Kibuye were backed up by notorious
killers from other parts of the country who had completed the genocide
in their regions. The massacre which took place that day claimed the
lives of tens of thousands in the most brutal killings. This marked the
end of the refugees' hopes. It was followed by a second attack on 14
May. Any refugees who did survive these two days were invariably the
only members of their family to do so. They have the most horrific
memories of searching for their loved ones among the corpses strewn all
over the hills. By the time French troops, who came as part of
Operation Turquoise, passed through the area on 26 June, there were
only around 2,000 refugees still alive. At least half of them were
killed when the French went to look for reinforcements, leaving the
refugees alone and exposed for four days.
Elizaphan Ntakirutimana made his contribution to the success of the
genocide in Bisesero. Jason Nshimyumukiza, 21, a nurse at Mugonero
hospital, had survived the massacre of the 16th and like so many
others, first went to Murambi and then with difficulty made his way to
Bisesero as a last hope. He overheard a conversation between
Ntakirutimana and the militiamen he was sending to take part in the
killing on the hills.
One day, my companions and I were so desperate and at the end of our
tether that we thought of drowning ourselves in Lake Kivu. Then we came
across Elizaphan and his gang of interahamwe, including Serinda's son,
Samuel. The minister was inciting his militia to kill, saying: "Don't
be afraid to exterminate every last one of the Inyenzi, because God has
delivered them into our hands." I was only a few metres away from them,
and could hear what he was saying. We were lying on the ground in the
forest of Bisesero. There was no man of God there anymore. Just the
hunters and their prey, that's all. They went on tracking us down until
the French soldiers arrived for Operation Turquoise on 30 June. But how
many of us were left?
Innocent Nkusi accuses Ntakirutimana of coming regularly to Bisesero
in his car, transporting militiamen to join the killers.
We saw them coming from all over the commune of Gishyita, namely the
sectors of Mubuga, Ngoma, Muramba, Rubazo, Gitesi, Rwankuba and
Kagabiro. All these groups converged at Muyira. Most of those from
Ngoma came in vehicles along with Obed Ruzindana and Mika. I could
usually see Elizaphan's vehicle among the ones from Ngoma. They all
gave one another the signal to start, except for the group from Gisovu.
They did not wait for the signal because they were coming from a
different direction. We fought back as best we could. When they got
tired of killing, they withdrew and came back two days later.
Jael Kankindi also saw Ntakirutimana delivering killers to the
battleground.
Ntakirutimana used to drive to Bisesero in his Hilux to lead the
militia attacks along with the others. I saw him.
Edison Kayihura, who had struggled against Ntakirutimana's militia
in Mugonero and Murambi, said that "the fighting went on in Bisesero."
The same murderers from Gishyita came after us up there. We fought
them ferociously, helped by our more extensive knowledge of the hills
and our easy access to stones there. We had divided ourselves into
several groups to fight off attackers coming from different directions;
from Gisovu to the north, or from Ngoma and Gishyita.
But the scale and preparation of the violence in mid-May overwhelmed
the refugees. "The attacks of 13 and 14 May were large-scale, intended
to exterminate us", Edison concluded.
Elizaphan Ntakirutimana also came to kill us at Bisesero. I saw him
on 13 May in Muyira together with his son, Gerard. Elizaphan came in
his car, a white Hilux, while Gerard was in the car that belonged to
Mugonero hospital. I passed Elizaphan while running. Obed Ruzindana and
the prefet, Kayishema, were all there that day; they killed a lot of
Tutsis, especially women and children. These genocidaires put leaves on
their head. They came back on the 14th to comb the area. The attacks of
13 and 14 May were large-scale, intended to exterminate us. I hid in a
bush and by chance escaped by taking refuge in a cave.
Saying the refugees had decided to "die fighting", Vincent
Usabyimfura, who lost his father at Bisesero, said the killings of 13
May crippled the refugees' chances of resistance and survival.
A lot of militiamen came on 13 May with many cars. They killed a
huge number of people. Self-defence was impossible. Everywhere, there
were corpses; everyone looked for a hideout. I saw Elizaphan
Ntakirutimana's car, a white Hilux. These two days, 13 and 14 May, they
killed in a special manner. They cut off the legs and the arms and then
left the victims. Before, when they hit with machetes, they did not aim
at a specific part of the body. The crows and dogs came to devour the
corpses.
Elizaphan used to hold his Adventist services on Saturdays, and come
up to Bisesero with his whole congregation afterwards, to kill us.
People like Mika, Kanyabungo, Ezechias, Gerard, Nyiringango, Ndayisaba
and Sikubwabo used to meet up with Elizaphan.
Aaron Kabogora said he "decided not to run away, but to defy them,
and that is what we did in Bisesero." The defiance cost Aaron dearly;
his ten children and wife were killed during the genocide and he was
injured. Aged 50, Aaron is a cattle breeder from cellule Gitwa in
Bisesero who was working for a Swiss firm in April 1994.
After wiping out the Tutsis in Ngoma, Ruzindana, Kayishema,
Sikubwabo, Mika, Elizaphan Ntakirutimana, Dr Gerard, Eliezer
Niyitegeka, Alfred Musema and the councillors came up here to Bisesero.
We used to see them arriving in their vehicles, at the head of a column
of interahamwe, soldiers and gendarmes. They supervised the operations.
They had firearms; all we had to defend ourselves with were clubs and
spears. With such odds against us, we were facing annihilation.
On 13 and 14 May we fled to Muyira and Rwankuba. Then buses full of
interahamwe, soldiers and gendarmes arrived there. They began their
"work" and went home exhausted in the evening. I was hit in the right
leg by a hand grenade and fell down. As I was lying on the ground, I
saw their vehicles go by. Ruzindana's vehicle joined those from Gisovu,
while Elizaphan's vehicle stayed at the cross-roads. He was telling the
interahamwe to surround the whole area and to be ready for him. I saw
him and I knew him. He was rather dark, plumb and had a lot of white
hair.25
Ismael Mbonimpaye had seen Ntakirutimana at both Mugonero and
Murambi and knew that his presence was a sign of trouble.
I saw Elizaphan with Ruzindana, Mika and Sikubwabo in Bisesero.
First they exterminated us, then they turned up with the loudspeaker
blaring out: "Peace has returned." Ruzindana asked us to take the
wounded down to the road and gather together to receive food supplies.
Some of us were suspicious, but others came out of hiding and were
completely massacred by the interahamwe brought in from Bugarama that
afternoon by Yusufu. I was injured by a hand grenade that day; you can
see I have scars everywhere. I hid in a hole. Not far from me, the
interahamwe stripped old Tamari naked and killed her with machetes. I
came out of that hole when the French arrived.
Azarias Munyampama, a farmer and cattle breeder, is 47 and comes
from Bisesero. An Adventist, he said he "knew the minister Elizaphan
Ntakirutimana very well before the genocide because he used to preach
in the Seventh Day Adventist churches." He added: "Even if you brought
him here today, I could still point him out to you, and I would accuse
him of everything I saw him do." He described an encounter with
Ntakirutimana towards the end of the genocide, in mid-June.
Our numbers had decreased so much by then that I decided to stay in
hiding as long as possible. It was Elizaphan and Ruzindana who got me
out of hiding. This is what happened.
Because so many of us had died, a lot of survivors remained in
hiding in caves, in the bush, or in the sorghum fields. I hid in a
sorghum field above the road. Ruzindana's white vehicle drove up, with
Ruzindana and Elizaphan inside. They stopped because they had seen a
terrified young girl. They told the girl not to run away, saying they
wouldn't harm her, but if she did run away, they would shoot her. The
girl obeyed and went up to them. I heard their conversation: "If we
send you to call the people over, will you do it?"
"Yes, I can tell them."
Ruzindana said: "If she leaves, she won't come back!"
While the men were talking, Eliezer Niyitegeka drove up in a jeep.
They told the girl to go and fetch an adult. Azarias decided to come
out of hiding. He asked the men if he could "approach them without
being killed" and they told him not to be afraid. Ntakirutimana asked
Azarias if he recognised him and, although he did, he denied it. Then
Ruzindana explained why they had come.
"We are genuinely here on a peace mission. Do you see that box?" He
showed it to me. "It's full of medical supplies for your wounded. Go
and call your people, especially the wounded. We want to help you."
I agreed and called over Charles Seromba who was hiding in the same
field. Charles had a swollen eye injured during the fighting. They had
a long conversation with him while I was standing a few feet away.
Eliezer told him: "Don't worry, the war is over now. Go and call
your people over."
When Charles had only gone a short distance he shouted to Azarias to
join him, then he warned him of his fear that the group's intention was
to kill them. Charles suggested that they both run away. Azarias
managed to make his escape easily.
About 20 minutes later, I told the men: "As Charles Seromba is
taking a long time, I'll go and call him, and see if there are any
other people who need carrying and transport." They said: "All right,
but don't be long."
I ran off and found Charles with some men he had just given the news
to. After some discussion, we decided to split into two groups. The
first group would go round the vehicles and ambush the murderers from
behind if they tried to get away. The second group would charge down at
them with spears and machetes and, if they didn't fire at us, we would
capture them alive. More than 50 of us attacked them.
When they saw us charging down the hillside towards them, with our
weapons, they scrambled back into their vehicles and drove off.
Azarias remembers that Ntakirutimana was dressed very smartly in a
black suit, a shirt and tie, and brown shoes, while Obed Ruzindana was
wearing brown trousers, a short sleeved, white pullover, and boots and
was driving. Although they were forced to leave Bisesero on this
occasion, the events of the following day proved that they had, in the
words of Azarias, "achieved their objective."
They had come to see whether there were any survivors left. The
following day they came back, bringing a huge horde of killers into
Bisesero from both directions; from Gishyita as well as Gisovu. They
made a horrible noise; screaming and shouting and banging things, so
that we thought we were finished. We were forced to fight back. I was
in the group that fought the men from Gisovu. A stone hit me in the
ribs and my head was slashed with a machete. You can still see the scar
[on the left side of his head]. That was my last battle. I had to take
cover in the bush because of the state I was in. A lot of survivors
fell that day, fighting our attackers.
I stayed in the bush. The younger survivors who knew where I was
would come and change my position or put me in the sun and take me in
again in the evening.26
Justice at Last
Perhaps the reason it has taken so long for Elizaphan Ntakirutimana
to be extradited to Arusha is that he was able to employ a strong
defence team. Ramsey Clark, a former attorney-general, was his lawyer,
before he died, and was a well-known critic of the legality of
international penal jurisdiction. When the case caught the attention of
the press, Clark told Joshua Hammar and Marcus Mabry of Newsweek that
Ntakirutimana was the victim of a plot.
Ramsey Clark... won't rebut specific allegations against his client,
but he calls the depositions unreliable and says witnesses were
enlisted in a Rwandan-government plot to take revenge against prominent
Hutus. "He has time after time stood up for Tutsis," says Clark. "In
times of danger, he opened the church as a sanctuary."
But the investigation carried out by Newsweek in Kibuye reached a
different conclusion.
Yet interviews conducted by Newsweek in January with 20 survivors in
the Mugonero region...describe a man who changed overnight from
protector to predator, who delivered thousands to their deaths and who
may have actually committed murder himself.27
Those who have been prepared to travel to Mugonero, like Newsweek,
have found strong evidence against the pastor. Yet the authorities of
the Seventh Day Adventist Church have not seen fit to make their own
investigation into the case. They have offered no comfort to the
Adventist survivors living there. Indeed the comments made by L.T.
Daniel, president of the Africa-Indian Ocean Division, on the subject
of the allegations against Ntakirutimana, showed an astonishing lack of
concern.
He did not act officially, if he acted at all. There was no
committee meeting, as we know of, which he called and [which] passed
any action. So the church cannot defend him officially because there
was no official action... If he acted at all he acted on his own... so
the church does not have an official position on him.
Mr. Daniel's response to the genocide in Rwanda was equally
dismissive.
Our approach is to forget the past and begin afresh. It is not easy
to preach to the deeply aggrieved people in Rwanda after hearing of
such atrocities. But we must forgive those who hurt us. We must forgive
anyway.
His predecessor and head of the Africa division during the genocide,
J.J. Nortey, echoed his opinion.
There should be a point where we just say what is gone is gone.
Let's begin afresh.28
Such views, however, are not shared by all Adventists in the US or
worldwide. Roy Branson, the editor of Spectrum, The Journal of the
Association of Adventist Forum published in the US, has argued that the
genocide in Rwanda represented "a horror unprecedented" in the history
of Adventists, and that "to forget these crimes is tantamount to
condoning them." His message has particular relevance at this time,
when the ICTR may finally have its chance to present the evidence
against Elizaphan Ntakirutimana.
How can we remember and help stop the cycle of criminal violence
against humanity and the body of Christ?
* Cooperate fully with international tribunals attempting to
establish the guilt or innocence or Rwandans-including Adventists-in
the slaughter of innocents.
* Simultaneously establish a commission chosen by the world church
that reports to the denomination concerning the nature of Adventist
involvement in the slaughter in Rwanda, as well as acts of heroic
rescue...
Adventists hope that the international tribunal at The Hague never
announces indictments of Seventh-day Adventists. Instinctively, the
world church wants to quietly walk away from what happened in Rwanda.
But if the leadership of the Seventh-day Adventist Church is truly
committed to the importance of unity, it will act vigorously to make
certain that Adventists do not forget, but remember Rwanda.
For us to try to forget Rwanda as we move into the next millennium
would be to remain frozen in horror at what they did. We must remember
in order to honor our slain fellow Adventists, and to realize our
identity with our fellow believers who murdered them. We must remember
Rwanda because only then can we truly repent. We must remember because
only by remembering can we remind ourselves and our children that each
man, woman and child is precious in the sight of God. We must remember
in order to remind ourselves, and the relatives of the victims, and the
murderers still worshipping from Sabbath to Sabbath within the
Adventist Church, that God most assuredly remembers.29
Ntakirutimana's case should be a landmark in acknowledging the depth
of the crimes committed by churchpeople during the genocide. It should
finally put an end to the silence and complacency which have
characterized the response of the Christian Churches to allegations
against certain of their members. It is to be hoped that this will be
the first of several cases against members of the Rwandese clergy which
the ICTR will pursue.
