deep cuts on them. ODU said that the students union president, Mr.
Olasupo Kazeem and one female student sat on the students' union bus,
chanting war songs while other colleagues raged.
The release explained that the crisis got out of hand when the Essa
went to the campus to promise the students that the vigilante group in
the town would not retaliate. On hearing a rumour that the entire
vigilante group was approaching the school, the students went for the
head of the Essa and the rector of the institution had to hide him in
her toilet for five hours. The inability of the students to apprehend
the Essa was said to have enraged them into setting his vehicle ablaze.
"The vigilante group then moved into the campus to rescue Chief Essa
and they succeeded in doing so. They also provided vehicles and
security for moving the rector, the registrar and some other top
officers out of the danger zone", the release asserted. It added that
in the midst of the confusion, some hoodlums entered the polytechnic
and unleashed terror.
The students would, however, have none of the ODU's explanation.
"They are lying", Amusan Johnson, the National Association of Nigerian
Students (NANS), mobilisation officer, Offa chapter, blurted out in a
voice laden with anger. In the words of the President of NANS, Offa
Chapter, Olasupo Kazeem, who the students claim has been declared
wanted by the ODU, a female student was assaulted by a taxi
driver/apprentice mechanic who is an indigene of Offa. The lady
reported the incident to the students union's director of sports and
welfare officer respectively, who immediately visited the scene of the
incident. To the students' surprise, they were beaten black and blue by
vigilante leaders. "They came back to the campus to report the matter
to the union and from our campus, we proceeded to the police station to
lodge the case with the DPO. We then visited the Olofa's Palace but he
was absent,'' Olasupo explained.
The students explained that on getting to the campus, the union
leaders called the students together to address them. The meeting was
on when a male student was rushed into the campus clinic badly battered
by the indigenes. Olasupo said that it was at this point the Essa (the
second in command to the Olofa), Chief Yinusa Bukoye drove into the
campus and requested to meet with the students union leaders. "He told
us he came into the campus because of the danger he perceived could
erupt from the actions of his people.'' The meeting, which held in the
office of the Dean of Students' Affairs, Mr. Okonta was attended by the
DPO, Mr. Rufus Amusan, the Chief Security Officer of the institution,
Mr. Umelo and some union leaders was still on when Umelo received a
radio message that the vigilante group had invaded the campus. Not even
the DPO's presence or the security officer's strength was enough to
hinder the vigilante group from forcing their way into the campus.
"Then they started shooting at students from the main road and
entered the school shooting and machetting whoever they saw. Our
wounded colleagues were rushed to the school clinic but the vigilante
members went into the clinic and shot students. This was where we
suffered our fatal casualties", Oyeneye Hakeem, the financial secretary
told this magazine. The vigilante group went ahead to "set buildings
and vehicles ablaze, vandalised school property, went into the female
hostels, stripped the students naked and raped over 40 of them in broad
day light".
The students realised they had no option than to run for cover to a
nearby town called Erin-Ile. The Elerin of Erin, according to the
students, received them and camped them in a primary school. "Over
10,000 of us were camped. We were fed and maximum security was provided
us by the Elerin", Oyeneye explained. Erin-Ile, The News gathered,
became an emergency refugee camp, with a  hospital where students'
wounds were treated and the bullets lodged in their bodies, removed
traditionally. "In all, 29 students had bullets removed from their
bodies in Erin-Ile", the students union president said. The News met
one of the students, Olalekan Adetayo, an HND I Marketing student, from
whose body six bullets were extracted. He simply said: "I thank God I
am alive. I escaped by the whiskers. My cousin, Adeyemo Okunola of 28,
Shitta Street, Surulere, Lagos wasn't so lucky. He died a few days ago
at home as a result of gun shot and machete injuries". As he made to
open the plaster covering one of his wounds, blood oozed out.
The 8 May incident was the last straw in the cat and mouse
relationship that had existed between the students and their Offa
hosts.  The students complained there has always been some
discrimination against them in the provision of services. The amount an
indigene pays for accommodation, for instance, is always cheaper than
what a student is charged by the landlords. Ditto for other services
and goods. "The segregation got to the point where indigenes insist
they must fetch water before students because that is their fatherland
and you are a stranger", the students' union treasurer said. The
discrimination was based on the erroneous belief that the students are
from Lagos where money flows like water and as such, they should be
made to spend all their money in Offa, the treasurer added. By this
tragedy, the cold relationship between the hosts and their guests has
been brought to a head.
The vigilante group was established about 15 years ago by the Offa
community to combat hoodlums who usually overpowered the conventional
security bodies in the town. Their operations were between the hours of
11 pm and 5 am. The vigilante carried out their duties judiciously as
they were able to stem the activities of men of the under world. They
won the hearts of the people.
The relationship between the vigilante group and the Offa
Polytechnic was sealed when the rector of the institution, Engineer
Lady Taiwo Olatoun Osemekhain employed their services to curb the
juvenile activities of students on the campus. In return, the school
authorities assisted the vigilante group in some other ways. The school
made a donation of N100,000 to the group last year. This had never
augured well with the students who saw the activities of the security
group as a hindrance. Beyond that, the punishment meted to their
colleagues off campus by the group irked the students, said the
president of the students' union. "If any student in town had a
misunderstanding with an indigene of the town, the vigilante would deal
with him in the night. It was this same vigilante group that
mysteriously killed one of our students, Femi Oni in 1996 while another
colleague of ours lost his eye to their activities".
Coupled with the aforementioned is the fact that members of the
Union of Road Transport Workers who are also members of the vigilante
group were accused of exploiting the students whenever the latter
travelled to Lagos. The students' alternative arrangement to this
exploitation angered the union. "They conveyed us to Lagos at N250 and
this was exploitative. We then decided to help ourselves by putting our
union buses on the road to convey ourselves to Lagos at N150 and they
said we spoilt business for them", Johnson explained.
At a press conference organised by the students of Offa Polytechnic
in Lagos, a video cassette was played. The cassette showed the
destruction unleashed on the school. There was hardly any building that
was not torched or vandalised. Burnt vehicles littered the road and the
rector, whose eyes were red with weeping captured the story in the
following words: "I have been here in Offa doing my job to the best of
my knowledge and I have been nice to the indigenes who I love dearly.
But this is what they have repaid me with. I saw my students running
for safety while my girls were raped and broken bottles were used to
make marks across their breasts. They came at me and would have stabbed
me. They burnt my car and everything. What have we done to deserve all
these? My students did nothing, they were overpowered".
ODU maintained that no life was lost in the clash because the
students could not  produce the identities of the slain students. But
the students insisted that their colleagues were felled. "We saw many
dead bodies while we were running away but after the crisis, we could
not find them again. We found only two. Reports reaching us say that
the vigilante group dumped some bodies in the nearby Afelele river and
a nearby bush", Kazeem said. He gave the identities of the slain
students, whose bodies were recovered as Emmanuel Toyin Oluwasanmi,
(Computer Studies OND I, from Ikere-Ekiti), Nurudeen Oyedeji (Business
Administration Department, OND I) and one Adeyemo Okunoke of 28, Shitta
Street, Surulere who died at home as a result of injuries he sustained.
Although the ODU which is the umbrella body of the vigilante group
has asked the students to report back to school, the students say they
will only return to a relocated polytechnic. "We want the school to be
relocated to either Erin-Ile or another town that deserves our
presence. We are no longer safe in that town. Offa is hostile to
strangers", Johnson, the NANS mobilisation officer said. Secondly, they
want the perpetrators of this inhuman act to be brought to book. They
gave this magazine the aliases of those that participated in the
carnage as 'Idi Amin, 'Asori re', Landcruiser', 'Bournnvita and
'Sherebe'. Others are Ojo Adeyemo, an HND II Accountancy student of
Offa extraction and one Fatai, a mechanic.   They fingered business
mogul, Chief Emmanuel Adesoye, the ODU chairman, as the one who
equipped the vigilante group with sophisticated weapons and even bought
them a jeep. Although this magazine called on Adesoye's Offa office,
his secretary said he had travelled out of the country.
The Governor of Kwara State, Alhaji Mohammed Lawal has banned the
activities of the vigilante group. Said he: "The vigilante operation,
all over the world, is based on point defence and it is strictly
location based. Its operations are also always restricted specifically
to night times. However, in the case of Offa, the vigilante groups
appear to have constituted themselves into a parallel police force.
Accordingly, vigilante operations as presently constituted in Offa area
are hereby abolished". He also cancelled the curfew imposed
unilaterally by the community as it impinges on people's fundamental
human rights.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Says Renewed Fighting Disrupting Farming In Sierra Leone
Headline: Sierra Leone: WFP Says Renewed Fighting Disrupting Farming In Sierra Leone
Body_Text:
DAKAR, Senegal (PANA) — The UN World Food Programme warned Friday that the
recent upsurge of fighting and insecurity in Sierra Leone is seriously
disrupting the work of thousands of farmers, especially in areas where clashes
are still going on.
The worst affected areas are the major rice producing centres of Kambia and
Bombali districts in northern Sierra Leone.
"Due to the renewed fighting, WFP was forced to postpone food aid
distributions destined to 34,000 farm families in the districts of Bomali,
Tonkolili, Kailahun and Port-Loko," the agency said in a news release.
The food was meant to help farmers to feed themselves during the planting
season and until the next harvest.
"With the recent upsurge of hostilities, many farmers have been displaced and
many others lack the seeds and tools which would enable them to start
cultivating before the heavy seasonal rains set in soon," Patrick Buckley, WFP
representative in Sierra Leone, said.
"With the high insecurity prevailing, particularly in rural areas, delivery
of services and agricultural inputs by government and humanitarian agencies has
been seriously disrupted," he added.
Since the start of the civil war in Sierra Leone in the early 1990s, the
production of rice, the staple food, has declined steadily to reach in 1999 less
than half of its pre-war level.
Due to the lack of seed rice and to help minimise the risk of food being
looted by combatants, farmers have often turned to cassava and sweet potatoes.
"In the coming weeks, farming families will quickly exhaust the small food
stocks they spared and they might resort to the consumption of their seed
supplies," Buckley said. "If the security situation improves, we could
immediately resume our food for agriculture activities."
However, in the southern district of Bo, WFP has started distributing food to
some 3,000 farmers and their families.
The security situation permitting, the agency will also begin food
distribution to farmers in Kenema where it is planning to help 18,000 families.
During the last 10 months, WFP provided emergency food aid to 1.2 million
people in need of international assistance throughout Sierra Leone.

Original_Search_Title: Letter to a Kampala Friend: African Curse And Kaguta's, Kagame's Congo
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Letter to a Kampala Friend: African Curse And Kaguta's, Kagame's Congo
Body_Text:
Kampala — Dear Tingasiga: I am between tears and laughter, as the Chinese
philosopher Lin Yu Tang would say. War between Uganda and Sudan, yes. Between
Uganda and Kenya in years past, perhaps. But war between Uganda and Rwanda?
Is this a joke of some kind? What, pray tell, would our two countries gain
from a war that would result in mutual destruction?
Is it not enough that Uganda already has a war on its northern and western
borders?
Is it not enough that Rwanda is at war with itself and with Laurent Kabila's
Congo?
Perhaps it is the ghosts of King Leopold II and Mobutu Sese Seko, brutal
monarchs of the Congo Free State, which are haunting the state houses in Kampala
and Kigali.
As we never tire of warning those with ears, WEKUUME CONGO! (Beware of
Congo!) All who have ventured into that haunted land have come to grief.
Of course we shall never know why the relations between the Sabagabbe of
Uganda Yoweri Museveni and the Umwami of Rwanda Paul Kagame have reached such a
low point that they have amassed their battle-hardened troops along our common
border, ready for a war that neither of them will win.
I say we shall never know because truth is the first casualty of war, and
truth from rulers is a very rare commodity.
That is why the spokesmen from Kampala and Kigali have been issuing the usual
claims of innocence in the bloody conflict in Kisangani and beyond, heaping all
blame on their opponents, convinced that the rest of us are so gullible as to
buy their stories.
"They shot first", Rwandan government spokesman Joseph Bideri tells us from
Kigali.
"They shot first," Ugandan National Political Commissar James Wapakhabulo
tells us from Kampala.
OK, I believe them both. But who cares who shot first?
I am reminded of young herds boys in my youth. The lads from one family would
take their parents' goats and cows to the pastures, where they would meet other
herds boys on a similar mission.
Overcome by greed and temptation, the herds boys would soon forget the task
at hand and turn to helping themselves to fruit, corn or sweet potatoes from a
farm that did not belong to them.
At first they would work together to pick the crops, make a fire, roast the
goodies and watch out for the owners.
But soon the comrades would be at each other's throats, fighting over the
stolen food.
"It is mine!", one boy would declare. "No, it is mine! Let go!", the other
fellow would declare. Fists, clubs and sticks would swing. Blood would flow.
The other herds boys would take sides and the small melee over stolen food
would evolve into a full-fledged battle, with some taking the opportunity to
settle old scores and to demonstrate their "manhood and bravery."
Should the owner of the farm or the boys' parents come upon them, the lads
would claim innocence and blame "the other boys" for starting the fight,
oblivious to the fact that they were all stealing somebody's crops in the first
place.
I thought Uganda and Rwanda went into Congo to protect their borders from the
ADF and Interahamwe bandits?
Perhaps only Museveni and Kagame know what really happened in the pastures of
Eastern Congo and the reasons why Uganda and Rwanda are on the brink of a war
neither will win.
However, it does not take a magician to know that central to the conflict is
the struggle for control of Congo's natural resources.
In his classic novel Heart of Darkness, which described the extreme brutality
and greed that defined the Congo Free State under Belgium's King Leopold II,
Joseph Conrad talked about "the vilest scramble for loot that ever disfigured
the history of human conscience."
That was 1899.
One would think he was writing in 1999, for Conrad's novel describes the
Congo Free State under Kabila-Kagame-Kaguta.
The vilest scramble for loot in Kisangani and other parts of Congo is at the
heart of the darkness that now threatens the lives of the innocent peasants
along the Uganda-Rwanda border, and the economic and social stability of the
rest of their countrymen
Conrad's Heart of Darkness extends from Kabila-Kaguta-Kagame's Congo Free
State to Jonas Savimbi's Angola, to Foday Sankoh's Sierra Leone, to Meles Zenawi
and Issayas Afeworki's Horn of Africa.
While the leaders, rebels and other potentates of Africa cause mayhem over
diamonds and other gifts of mother earth, Africa's people totter under the
burden of extreme poverty and lack of basic necessities of modern life.
Indeed one of Africa's greatest paradoxes is that its enormous wealth is the
major cause of its people's poverty and perpetual suffering.
From the slave trade to Cecil Rhodes and South African apartheid; from
European colonialism to Bill Clinton's "new breed of African leaders," greed for
Africa's natural resources has inflicted great pain and death on Africans.
Take diamonds for example, those shiny crystalline versions of carbon, whose
global production is worth between $6 billion and $ 8 billion a year.
Although more than 50 per cent of the world's new diamonds come from Angola,
Botswana, Congo Free State, Sierra Leone and South Africa, very few Africans,
including those from Kisangani, Lobito, Freetown or Kimberley have ever seen a
single diamond.
Yet these diamonds affect the lives of Africans in a very profound way.
The insane Foday Sankoh of Sierra Leone's Revolutionary United Front murders
and mutilates his people in his struggle to maintain control of the diamond
mines in his country, then exports them to the West via his fellow thug Charles
Taylor, President of Liberia, earning the two of them hundreds of millions of
dollars every year.
Old Jonas Savimbi's UNITA has reportedly earned $3.37 billion from diamonds
in the last decade alone.
Yet Angolans have become poorer and sicker during the same period. The lucky
ones have died.
The same is true of the black gold (oil) which flows with abundance all over
Africa.
The Niger Delta of Nigeria, the marshlands of Southern Sudan, the coasts of
Gabon, Cameroon and Angola are soaked in vast quantities of oil that could have
provided the engine for modernizing those countries' economies just like the
Arab countries of the Middle East.
Yet Africa's oil producers remain stuck among the world's most backward
nations, thanks to the kleptocrats and war-lords who control the national armies
and governments but care little about the lot of their fellow Africans.
While Canadian oil drilling companies siphon wealth from war-torn Southern
Sudan, international charity organizations like World Vision send back to Canada
video images of starving Southern Sudanese children and women with shrivelled
breasts, and beg Canadians to send money for food and medicines.
One almost despairs. Africa, blest with abundant wealth, a largely agreeable
climate and bright and energetic people, seems to take one step forward and four
steps backward.
Perhaps Africa's wealth is a curse to her people.

Original_Search_Title: Preventing Childhood Blindness In Africa With Sweet Potatoes
Headline: Africa: Preventing Childhood Blindness In Africa With Sweet Potatoes
Body_Text:
Mary Land — After more than a decade of research, scientists are deploying
sweet potatoes in remote areas of Africa in the war against blindness caused
by vitamin A deficiency.
The new sweet potatoes, which are suitable to African tastes and climates,
offer hope to women and children who are currently beyond the reach of
vitamin supplement programs.
In Sub-Saharan Africa, some 3 million children under age five suffer
blindness caused by lack of vitamin A. Two-thirds of the children who do not
meet their requirements for vitamin A die from increased vulnerability to
infection.
The problem also affects their mothers. The World Health Organization
reports that women who suffer from vitamin A deficiency face a significantly
higher risk of death during pregnancy than those who have access to the small
amount of vitamin A required for good health.
Developing countries are working hard to reach the United Nation's goal of
eliminating disease related to insufficient vitamin A. Programs to distribute
vitamin A capsules and fortify food have reduced the number of children
suffering blindness related to vitamin A deficiency by two thirds over the
past 20 years. In 1997, an estimated 12 million children received vitamin A
supplements.
Reducing the incidence of blindness not only increases the quality of
people's lives; it also benefits their countries' economies. The World Bank
estimates that for every dollar invested in capsule supplements, more than
$100 is returned in the form of increased productivity associated with
healthier workers and lower public health costs.
Millions of women and children, however, still require help in meeting
their daily vitamin A requirements. Many rural areas lack the all-weather
roads and stable transportation systems that are necessary for successful
supplement programs.
Researchers believe they can assist those beyond the reach of supplement
programs by helping them produce their own vitamin A. One of the best ways to
do this is by incorporating orange-fleshed sweet potatoes into their diet.
Sweet potatoes, a crop native to South America, are easy to grow and have
become a staple food in many African countries. Orange-fleshed sweet potatoes
are rich in beta-carotene, a nutrient that the body uses to produce vitamin
A. In much of Sub-Saharan Africa, however, the most common type of sweet
potato is the white-fleshed type, which contains little or no beta-carotene.
For many years, development experts believed that African consumers would
not accept the beta-carotene-rich sweet potatoes, which they considered too
moist and sweet for African tastes. Results from recent research are
challenging this view.
Scientists have spent more than ten years selecting orange-fleshed sweet
potatoes that could pass African consumer taste tests and not lose their
beta-carotene content when subjected to processing. The research, conducted
by the Peru-based International Potato Center, the Kenyan Agricultural
Research Institute, the Uganda National Agricultural Research Organization,
and the International Center for Research on Women, shows that the addition
of small amounts of the new sweet potatoes to a family's diet can eliminate
vitamin A deficiencies in both children and adults.
"One of our principal findings was that African mothers are motivated to
accept the new varieties once they understand their nutritional value," said
Dr. Wanda Collins, deputy director general for research at the International
Potato Center." This dispels the notion that African taste preferences rule
out the use of varieties that contain beta carotene, the presence of which is
linked genetically to the sweeter, moister orange flesh."
Based on the findings, researchers now know that the tools are available
to expand the fight against vitamin A blindness and other vitamin A-related
diseases. But Collins is hesitant to declare victory. "The heart of our
project," she says "is to make the new varieties available in rural areas.
But we also have to help develop better plant production and distribution
systems, educate national health and agriculture workers, and stimulate
microenterprises that will generate cash. A few good sweet potatoes--no
matter how good they are-won't do it."
Now support is being organized for a research and development campaign--
appropriately called the VITA project--that will expand the use of the new
varieties. "The goal is to extend the high beta-carotene sweet potatoes to
those currently outside the reach of conventional nutrition programs and to
East and Southern Africa where the problem is most severe," said Collins. In
West Africa, red palm oil, used widely for cooking, provides sufficient
vitamin A-producing beta-carotene to meet nutritional needs.
"Our most important task will be to work with local women's groups, many
of which are located in places that are difficult to reach," says Collins.
"We'll be putting a lot of emphasis on working with local communities and
nongovernmental organizations."
"We have been hearing a lot about high-tech agriculture lately. This
project demonstrates that research geared to promote grass-roots changes on
the farm and in the family kitchen can provide a straightforward solution to
a serious problem," said Collins. "Sometimes it's the simple solutions that
work the best."
For additional information, contact Christine Graves at the International
Potato Center at c.graves@cgiar.org or see
http://www.cipotato.org/projects/sweetpotato.htm.

Original_Search_Title: IT Centre Brings The World To Sleepy Nabweru
Headline: Uganda: IT Centre Brings The World To Sleepy Nabweru
Body_Text:
Kampala — The incessant buzzing of a diesel-powered engine from a building a
few metres away reinforced the impression of a flourmill in a typical rural
area in Uganda.
However, unlike the normal flourmill, whose outside is awash with white
flour, this particular building was clean. As we came closer, the diesel engine
turned out to be a generator running a local information technology centre.
"We are training them how to use computers," said one young man. In the
other corner a woman in her mid-30s was waiting for her telephone call to go
through.
Nabweru Community Telecentre (NCT), was opened in 1998. It is situated seven
kilometres south of Kampala. The centre has given the lives of the people of
Nabweru a fresh meaning. Having telephone and photocopier services a stone's
throw away from their homes and at affordable rates is no longer a dream.
"This centre is revolutionising our lives," Ms Dorcus Kabusomba, an exited
and regular user of the centre, said.
A pilot project set up by the Uganda National Council of Science and
Technology (UNCST), Nabweru Telecentre is one of the information and
communication technology sites (ICTs) funded under the Acacia initiative of the
International Development Research Centre (IDRC).
The Acacia initiative aims at promoting community based applications for
rural development and empowerment, in ways that build upon local goals,
cultures, strengths and processes.
"UNCST and IDRC are looking at ICTs and how they can help rural communities,
especially women. UNCST has developed a framework within which ICTs can be
applied and women as a target group can eventually benefit from them," says Dr
Zevubabel Nyiira, executive secretary of UNCST and Acacia project national
programme co-ordinator.
Through the telecentre, it is hoped that accessibility to ICTs, which has
hitherto eluded rural communities especially women, will be addressed.
The Nabweru Telecentre has five computers, a fax machine, a telephone line a
photocopier, a television set, a video recorder and a generator. One of the
computers is connected to the Internet while the rest are used for training.
Ms Norah Madaya, the project officer for Acacia Uganda national secretariat
says that the Internet will be used to address maternal health and child
nutrition issues in the area.
Mr. Daniel Semakula, an information officer at the telecentre, says already
the Internet has become very popular on matters of agriculture.
"Since the economy of the community is agriculture-oriented, most of the
information we download is on agriculture," says Semakula.
The telecentre, according to Semakula, has established a working
relationship with agricultural research centres in Uganda. He and other
employees at the centre go around the community, collecting information from
farmers.
The information is e-mailed to research centres and the research centres e-
mail back their solutions to the farmers concerned. Once at the institution,
says Semakula, the information is downloaded and repackaged into the local
language and given to the farmers who need it.
"I remember a farmer came to us desperately looking for a a away to save his
rotting passion fruit. We e-mailed the agricultural research centre, which gave
us all the solutions. After following the advice, the farmer came back to tell
us that he was doing well," says Semakula.
To reinforce its strategy of using the e-mail to make agricultural
information available to farmers, the telecentre also organises video shows for
the farmers.
"The farmers tell us subjects they would like the video show to be based on.
So we go to the British Council library and pick the one they want," says
Semakula.
Video shows are screened at two o'clock every Thursday, when Nabweru Women
in Development Association (NWDA) members meet. It has proved effective in
reaching a large number of women, who make up a substantial proportion of
Nabweru's farmers.
One farmer benefit from the centre is Ms Kabusomba.
Ms Kabusomba, a small-scale farmer, says the information from the centre has
been beneficial to her. "Through the video shows I have learnt new methods of
cultivating maize, fodder crops and sweet potatoes in a productive way and how
to carry-out zero grazing and poultry keeping."
At the moment, she says, she has 200 broilers, which sell at Ush3,500
($2.36) each and one Friesian dairy cow that gives 20 litres of milk per day
that goes for Ush600 ($0.4) per litre.
"Before I started getting information from the telecentre, I was getting
less than ten litres from my cow. But after being taught new ways of improving
production, the number of litres increased."
At the Telecentre, one is charged a flat rate of Ush100 a minute (US cents
7) compared to service providers in town who charge Ush1,000 ($0.68) for the
first four minutes, and between Ush150 ($ 0.10) and Ush200 ($0.13) per minute
thereafter depending on the service provider, according to Tamale.
Apart from the Internet, charges for other services, telephone, fax and
photocopying are equally cheap. A three-minute telephone call at the telecentre
costs Ush500 ($0.34), for which other service providers would charge between
Ush900 ($0.61) and Ush1,000 ($0.68).
Students at Nabweru Secondary school are also beneficiaries. A Form Three
student and chairman of the writers' club, Vincent Kasozi, talks excitedly
about the Telecentre.
"The Straight Talk newsletters we get from the telecentre, enrich us with
information on how to protect ourselves from Aids, from being deceived, and
generally about our lives."
Seventeen-year-old Kasozi says that, during the vacations, he is going to
take advantage of the low rates charged by the telecentre to learn how to use a
computer.
"Demand for computer training has been increasing each day," says Jjuuko.
Currently, the centre is training 30 computer students.

Original_Search_Title: Ups And Downs Of Life In Kampala
Headline: Uganda: Ups And Downs Of Life In Kampala
Body_Text:
Nairobi — One of my greatest regrets in life is that I did not graduate from
Makerere University. Field Marshal Idi Amin Dada, Conqueror of the British
Empire, took care of that. But I still hold fond memories of Makerere, the
illustrious university which has schooled a huge crop of political, industrial
and intellectual giants in the eastern Africa region, and which was still the
place to be as late as the 1970s.
Uganda is a beautiful country. Most of it is ever green; it has adequate
rainfall throughout the year, and the peasants don't have to work very hard to
feed themselves. I don't know how it is now but in those days, coffee literally
grew on trees - very tall, untended trees. I remember wondering how the Baganda
ever picked the berries.
They also had a way of cultivating small patches of land for planting sweet
potatoes, one of the staples of Baganda diet, in triangular very artistic
patterns - from which they could expect to dig up enormous tubers. But the most
amazing thing was the profusion of plantains, whose stems strived to reach the
skies, supporting huge bunches of matoke which kept the nation- at least the
capital city - going, even during the worst ravages of Amin's misrule.
Makerere itself was the very definition of a university campus, a self-
contained settlement atop one of the many picturesque hills that dot the city.
It contained everything you ever wanted, and since there were not too many
things available to buy those days, there was no real need for students to go
downtown for anything.
In short, Makerere was a miniature city within Kampala, complete with two
celebrated slums at the foot of the hill, Wandegeya and Katanga. That is where
the students went to look for those goodies not found on the hill, specifically
hookers and refined waragi which was not allowed on the campus.
But we Kenyans, on the whole, stuck to the campus and rarely ventured out
after dark, not for fear of thugs, not for fear of policemen (they were then a
quaking, cowering, mainly ineffectual lot), but because that is when unruly
army men and the dreaded, murderous State Research Bureau boys were on the
prowl, looking everywhere for Amin's enemies whom they "disappeared" at will.
But this is not about Amin or his Uganda. It is about what happened to a
sizeable number of Kenyans who were in Makerere in an exchange programme in
which students from each East African country would study in Makerere, Nairobi
or Dar-es-Salaam universities. That, incidentally, was a noble experiment which
worked very well because it exposed the few lucky students to the conditions in
fellow African states. A reciprocal programme of that nature should be
reconsidered.
Of course, Amin should not bear all the credit for breaking up the
programme. It would have happened sooner or later any way because the Kenyan
authorities were convinced the students sent to Dar came back hardened marxist
revolutionaries.
Three things happened in 1976 which hastened the departure of Kenyan
students from Makerere. First, in mid-July, an Israeli passenger plane was
hijacked by terrorists of the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
(PFLP) and forced to land in Uganda. President Amin was implicated in aiding
and abetting the terrorists. On July 14, Israeli commandos freed most of the
hostages in a most dramatic and spectacular fashion, in the process destroying
at least half of Amin's fighter jets at Entebbe Airport. Kenya helped in the
rescue by giving the Israelis landing rights and logistical support. It was a
top-secret operation and very few Kenyans knew what had happened or Kenya's
role in it.
Towards the end of the year, the small community of Kenyans received a most
severe jolt. We had almost completed our end-year examinations when we learnt
that two of our mates, Esther Chesire and Sally Githere, had, somehow,
disappeared. Frantic efforts to involve the authorities in their search did not
bear fruit.
The reason it had taken us a number of days to discover the two friends had
disappeared was because they used to travel home frequently, especially over
the weekends, and come back on time for classes on Mondays or Tuesdays. The two
young girls came from wealthy families. Sometimes they would even fly home.
My recollection of Esther was that of a beautiful girl with chubby cheeks,
and a brown complexion. She was a very friendly soul who was popular with her
mates. We attended the same lectures, but generally, she was rarely available
for social outings for there was no place to go.
Sally, on the other hand was a vivacious, slim lady who had gone to Makerere
to study law. I understand she is practising law right now, but she won't
recount any harrowing experiences.
Rumours abound about Chesire's disappearance. If she was killed, her body
has never been discovered. It was not unusual for people to disappear
mysteriously, especially in a country without law and order and only the brutal
force of the gun. It was whispered, long after we had returned to Kenya, that
because of her frequent travels, she had been mistaken for a spy. It was also
rumoured that a number of Amin's brutes wanted to marry her and started
fighting over her, and she could have been caught in the crossfire.
None of those rumours have ever been anything but that, and we may not know
anything until our intelligence sources declassify whatever information they
may have on her demise.
Personally, I did not even participate in those rumours because something
happened to me one evening after I had completed my exams. A few of us decided
to go to Katanga to seek a good time. Whatever happened there has remained a
mystery to me up to this day, because none of my erstwhile friends has ever
volunteered any information. I don't even have any idea who they were. Any
little information I have, I gathered from Mulago Hospital where I had been
taken in the night.
This is what I gathered. In the course of our perambulations in the slum, I
turned cold feet and left my friends. I decided to go back to the campus. Along
the way, when I was very close to the gate leading to University Hall where I
resided, I must have seen a fourth-year medical student who had just finished
his final exams being pumped with bullets by Amin's soldiers. They had mistaken
him for someone else. Certainly, the student's body was discoverd barely 200
yards from where I lay, having been clubbed senseless by the same soldiers.
What I do know is that I must have been pretty thick-headed because it was
soon discovered at the hospital that my skull was intact though I had become a
temporary amnesiac. Someone must have informed the university authorities where
I was because the the following morning, a delegation of fellow Kenyans and a
sprinkling of Ugandan friends came to see me and discovered that the bed next
to mine was occupied by a rather healthy Research Bureau character who,
ostensibly, wanted to know excatly what I knew about the attack on me.
My colleagues quickly decided to have me transferred to the university
sickbay where a doctor, whose name I cannot remember now, treated me. The one
thing I can remember about him was that he had lost his entire family - his
children and parents - to Amin's soldiers. Nevertheless, he was a good doctor.
He had me discharged as soon as my fellow Kenyans asked him to, the moment they
were ready to send me home. All my property was brought to the sickbay, and I
was soon on an Akamba bus bound for Kenya. The students had got wind that the
State Research Bureau wanted to question me. I never even knew who paid for my
ticket.
The journey was uneventful except that I was very weak and a young Ugandan
girl in a Brownie uniform who was travelling to Nairobi for a convention had to
help me with my luggage at the border. She must also have provided cover for me
because no customs official on the ugandan side of the border looked at me
twice.
I duly arrived in Eldoret and decided to alight in that strange town to look
for my brother who was a forest officer 100 kilometres away, somewhere in
Keiyo. I had no idea where it was, but I was young, adventurous and in a mood
to escape all the questioning I knew I would face. But that is another tale for
my memoirs.
What is important is that on the day I hit Eldoret, legislator Philomena
Chelagat Mutai had just been jailed and the mood was very ugly in that town.
Two days later, the then Voice of Kenya (VoK) started announcing my demise,
saying they would not reveal the name until the next-of-kin had been informed.
The Daily Nation too got into the act, but it was a Kenya New Agency (KNA) chap
who traced my whereabouts after about four days of hiding. I refused to speak
to him.
However, a day later, Esther Chesire's relatives, two young men driving a
Range Rover, came to see me. I could not help them; they must have thought me
pretty daft in their sorrow, but I really knew nothing about the fate of
Esther. It is everyone's hope that the mystery of her disappearance will be
solved now that the story is out.
That is how we never graduated from Makerere. The exchange programme was
dead.

Original_Search_Title: Biotechnology: New danger or solution to Africa's food deficit?
Headline: Africa: Biotechnology: New danger or solution to Africa's food deficit?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — An entire country faces famine. The government calls out
for food relief to help stem the death and starvation across. The
relief food that is donated is genetically-modified. The government
knows this. Should it accept the food or not? Or is this really a
rhetorical question?
This scenario goes to the heart of the issue of genetically
engineered food for Africa. It was painted by a University of Zimbabwe
biochemist at an interview during a recent conference on bio-diversity
in Mombasa.
The scenario brought together the science and economics of genetic
engineering and the uncertainty surrounding the safety of genetically-
engineered food.
Genetic engineering is not yet widespread on the continent. The
Executive Director of Nairobi's African Centre for Technology Studies
(Acts), Dr John Mugabe, reports that only three countries are now
involved in genetic engineering: South Africa, Zimbabwe and Kenya. In
Dr Mugabe's assessment, Kenya is really in between tissue culture
technology and genetic engineering.
Tissue culture technology and genetic engineering are part of a
broader field of biotech, a technique using living organisms to make or
modify a product, to improve plants or animals or to develop micro-
organisms for specific uses.
Right now, Kenya has only one genetic engineering project. The Kenya
Agricultural Research Institute (Kari) and Monsanto, the besieged
multinational, are working on a transgenic sweet potato.
Aventis, another multinational, formed by merging Rhone-Poulenc with
AgrEvo, may be working with the International Livestock Research
Institute (ILRI) on another genetic engineering project in Kenya.
In the African and Kenyan context, genetic engineering is mainly a
Western import. It follows that the debates in the West are important
to Africans as they generate their own debates," says Dr John Nderitu
of the Association for Better Land Husbandry (ABLH).
"The effects of genetically modified crops and products on the human
body are not known. There is no conclusive evidence of whether or not
they can be harmful."
The Association for Better Land Husbandry is an organisation that
promotes organic farming as a sustainable form of agriculture. Dr Idah
Sithole-Niang, a senior lecturer at the University of Zimbabwe's
Department of Biochemistry, agrees. She "absolutely" supports the
development of agriculture biotechnology.
"There are no data on the long-term impact or effects on human
health that a genetically modified organism may have. Nobody has such
data because nobody has eaten them long enough for the data to be
collected".
In the United States, genetically modified organisms have been in
use for close to a decade. So, ideally, that is where data should be
available on the long-term impact of such food on human health.
The US Food and Drug Administration, the body officially charged
with regulating new food and drug products in the US, declared in 1992
that there was no major difference between conventional and genetically
engineered foods.
This meant that the rigorous testing to which new products are
normally subjected in the US has not been applied to genetically
engineered foods. The result is that no labelling is required for
genetically engineered foods in the US and no special safety tests are
carried out.
Therefore, data that would help clear the air about genetically-
modified foods have not been collected in a country where genetically
modified seeds are used by many farmers.
Sithole-Niang observes that such foods can be tested for toxicity
and allergy in humans. She says this is standard and that such effects
are not necessarily because the food is genetically engineered. For
instance, an individual's allergic reaction to such food is similar to
somebody else's allergy to eggs or meat.
In 1992, countries gathered for the Earth Summit in Rio de Janerio
to discuss the environment and what needed to be done to reverse any
damage done on it. The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD), which
resulted from those talks, has been ratified by several African
countries, including Kenya. Significantly, the US has not ratified it
to date.
Article 19.3 of the convention provides for Parties (countries that
have ratified it) to consider the need for and modalities of a protocol
on the safe transfer, handling and use of living modified organisms, or
what in this article are called genetically-modified foods, that may
have an adverse effect on biodiversity.
The negotiations around such a protocol began in 1996 and have been
extended several times. But a final text was eventually agreed on in
Montreal in the wee hours of last January 29. That text is known as the
Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety.
It is named after the Colombian city where it was to have been
adopted by the negotiating countries in February, 1999. The protocol
will be open for signing during a conference in Nairobi next month.
One significant aspect of this protocol is that it allows countries
to stop the importation of genetically-modified foods even when there
is not enough scientific evidence of the risks presented. It also
allows countries to stop the import of such foods if new scientific
evidence shows negative effects of the foods.
Once 50 countries ratify the protocol, present it to their
parliaments for ratification and then inform the UN, those with any
worries about genetically-modified foods, especially as their long-term
effects are not known as yet, can still stop importing such foods.
Article 18.2 of the Protocol also requires that such products be
labelled. However, labelling states merely that product A "may contain"
modified foods not intended for introduction into the environment. It
also specifies a contact point to obtain further information.
Against genuine safety concerns about genetically-modified foods are
the genuine food needs of many people in the world.
"This technology will help fight hunger in Kenya," states
agriculture economist Gem Argwings-Kodhek. A senior research fellow at
Egerton University's Tegemeo Institute, he says: "The cost of
production of commodities will decrease and help protect plants against
pests." He sums it up as "High production for little seed input".
Nderitu sees the food needs issue in a different light. "Food-for-
the-whole-world proponents argue that with modified foods there will be
increased availability of seeds and it will be easy to feed the world,"
he observes. He sees this argument as, basically, a marketing strategy.
Generally the whole debate revolves around seeds that, for example,
produce an insecticide-resistant crop that allows for indiscriminate
spraying of the crop's area, killing harmful insects at one go while
sparing the crop. Therefore, whoever controls the sale of that seed or
other genetically engineered seeds will in effect control agriculture
and its profits.
The Canada-based Rural Advancement Foundation International (Rafi)
has been monitoring the agriculture biotechnology business for more
than 20 years. In 1979 it carried out an assessment of who markets seed
around the world.
They came up with a list of 7,000 public and private institutions.
At that time not one company controlled a significant percentage of the
global commercial seed market. Today, 10 companies control in excess of
one-third of the global commercial seed market.
Another trend is seed companies merging to form what are now called
"life sciences companies". These seed companies usually already have a
division that develops and markets crop chemicals. In fact, according
to Rafi, nine companies today control 91 per cent of the global market
for insecticides, herbicides and nemacticides.
All are based in the West. And the monopolistic hold they have on
seed and crop chemicals allows them to dictate the prices of their
products, usually on the higher side to make more profits, because
there is little competition to force their prices down.
The pitch for genetically engineered seeds today is part of a global
marketing strategy. But that does not mean there is no serious food
problem in Africa, in general, and in Kenya, in particular.
In a November 1999 paper, Oxfam admits that 790 million people in
the world are undernourished. However, in their view, food insecurity
is primarily caused by low incomes and unequal access to land, water,
credit and markets. In short, poverty is the cause of the food crisis
in many parts of the world, and not low food production.
The obvious gains in planting modified crops include the lower costs
of production owing to their herbicide and insect resistance
properties.
In the words of Novartis' Dr Florence Wambugu: "This frees women and
children's weeding time considerably, allowing them to engage in other
activities."
If the whole debate is really just about more people having more
food to eat in Africa, in general, and Kenya, in particular, has
anybody asked the farmer why she (for, in most cases, it is a she) or
he generally produce less and less each year? Why is she or he
experiencing famine? The answers to these questions may be surprising.

Original_Search_Title: Blick's Speedy Bug
Headline: Uganda: Blick's Speedy Bug
Body_Text:
Kampala — An odd 25 years ago, many will recall his fiercely competitive
nature, that made him a feared opponent in both rallying and speedbike racing.
Arthur Blick, now 52 and a brother to the executive director of the Uganda
Investment Authority (UIA) Dr. Margie Kigozi, always seemed to have an
obsession for speed.
But he had to quit motorsports in 1982 after a near fatal accident which
left him with a broken spine. The obsession is still there though. Only that
the satisfaction is not experienced by exciting bouts of acceleration and
braking, but watching the steady growth of his orchard farm on Kisinsi
Peninsula in Mukono district. It faces Ggaba Landing site.
Not surprisingly he named it 'Speedway Orchards'. Everything Blick does on
this impressive 80 acre piece of land, covered by pineapples, vines, mangoes,
improved orange varieties, coconuts, eucalyptus trees and clonal coffee is
based on speed. Getting things done as fast as possible.
For example in the growing of oranges of which he has over 2,000 trees, he
uses vegetative reproduction to speed up the growth and on top of that, applies
coffee husk manure twice on one tree in a space of six months so that they are
heavily laden in a year's time.
For other farmers who use local seeds it takes a minimum of five to six
years to start harvesting..
"You see that orange tree over there?" he points. "It's withering because of
water. Water is our biggest problem here. Government should put in place
systems to ease irrigation farming, because you cannot talk of modernization in
the agricultural sector when the farmers are entirely dependent on natural
factors," Blick says.
"That means if it does not rain farmers are going to suffer and some of them
will loose market contracts because they will be unable to meet market quotas
agreed upon with their buyers and in the process be sued. And you know what it
means to be sued in terms of money or loosing contracts," he adds.
Blick has plans to develop Speedway Orchards, first as a fruit producing
centre for both local and export markets and as a tourist centre.
The intriguing thing about Arthur Blick and people like him, is their never-
say-die attitude. He seeks nor entertains any sympathy based on the inability
to use his lower limbs, but he does a full-days job and perhaps even more.
Blick says that what mainly takes the tourists to Mombasa in Kenya, is none
other than the beautiful coconuts and beaches. "So I am going to provide the
same here in Uganda, so that tourists begin flowing in like hell. As an
organised businessman I will have a beach divided into classes to cater for
price discrimination. One for bazungu, paying dollars and the other for locals
who will be paying in local currency," he said.
However he is not very forthcoming about exact details of his capital.
Asked where he got the initial money to start off Blick said he bought the
land from his Uncle Kasule in 1991. He said he also has buildings which he
rents out in Kampala.
Rent from these buildings including the former Muyenga Speedway Club, now
'Africa Sana' and a mechanical workshop in Lubaga on Musajjalumbwa Road is what
he uses to invest. He dislikes bank loans.
"They make you a slave to the giving bank at the expense of your life and I
have never got any though I fall in the category of disabled persons who might
easily get access to this credit," he said. This coupled with other personal
savings currently enables him to run this farm employing over 20, both casual
and permanent staff plus giving peasants in the area land to grow their food
like sweet potatoes.
Although Blick detests bank loans, he considers himself a progressive
farmer. When his oranges are infected with the greening disease currently
terrorising the district like the coffee wilt disease he injects them with
tetracycline to quicken the healing process though the disease eventually comes
back.
He does the same for mangoes and coconuts which he says are his trademark
product. He plans to transform the place into a resort beach in the next one or
two years. And he does not wait for electricity to come to him. Instead Blick
has built a localised windmill made out of drums to harness energy for home and
farm use in terms of pumping water for semi irrigation uphill.
Asked how a motor rally and a cycling ace of the 1970s turned to farming yet
he would have concentrated on many other things to improve the two sports in
the country he hurriedly answers, "I wanted something to show of my
profession."
Born in a family of five children with four daughters and a son, Blick is an
agricultural graduate from Bukalasa .He worked with the tea out-growers in
Mukono in the 1970 under the then Uganda Tea grower's Corporation, before
quitting public service to start his own business of motor vehicle and cycle
mechanics.
He learnt on the job as they were his main equipments during his illustrious
sporting career spanning over 30 years.
Asked about the problems facing farmers, Blick appeals to Government to
remove taxes on agricultural equipment, especially those for irrigation
purpose, because some farmers can afford them at cheaper prices and also lack
of marketing agencies to sell local items.

Original_Search_Title: Mozambique Farmers Need 13 Million Dollars To Rebuild Homes
Headline: Mozambique Farmers Need 13 Million Dollars To Rebuild Homes
Body_Text:
Dakar, Senegal (PANA) — Reconstruction of agriculture, forestry and fisheries
in Mozambique will require at least 13 million US dollars for the next six
months to address the needs of rural people returning to their villages while
flood waters are receding, the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation said Monday
in a new appeal.
The organisation said in a release that its appeal reflects a better
understanding of the agriculture-related damage in Mozambique since the floods
have started to recede.
"There are more than 120,000 farmers and their families in desperate need of
assistance in the southern and central provinces of Mozambique," Anne M. Bauer,
chief of FAO's special relief Operations service, said.
"They need emergency support so that they can rebuild their homes and fields
to start planting immediately and prepare for the main agricultural season,
which begins next September," she added.
Roads, irrigation systems, and farm equipment were severely damaged by the
recent flooding, the worst in 40 years. Many farm animals were drowned and
animal disease control facilities were destroyed, Bauer said.
Almost 140,000 hectares of crops such as maize, beans, rice, sweet potatoes,
groundnuts and vegetables have been destroyed, or seriously affected.
Food and seed stock were also seriously damaged and in many cases washed
away. Major irrigation systems have also been destroyed.
FAO estimates that 350,000 cattle, goats and sheep, between 70percent and 80
percent of the livestock in the affected areas, have died or have been seriously
injured. The threat of animal diseases could increase, it warned.
Many major markets and local food supply systems have been completely
disrupted and agro-industry and food-processing activities have been halted.
Almost 6,000 small-scale fishers have lost 50 percent of their boats, fishing
nets and fishing gear.
Emergency assistance for the agricultural sector will include seed packs and
basic tools, the release said.
It added that providing vegetable seeds could improve the nutritional
situation of the flood-stricken households. Veterinary drugs will need to be
supplied and diagnostic facilities rehabilitated.
Emergency activities will also support small-scale fishers, helping restore
their main source of food.
Marketing infrastructure will need to be rebuilt as quickly as possible to
help revive the rural economy.
FAO's appeal is part of the United Nations Inter-agency appeal launched
together with the government of Mozambique. It had previously appealed for 2.5
million dollars.

Original_Search_Title: SOUTHERN AFRICA: Floods report
Headline: Southern Africa: SOUTHERN AFRICA: Floods report
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — As flood relief operations in Mozambique move into the
reconstruction phase, UNICEF this week raised new concerns about the plight of
children separated from their parents. It also cited a lack of safe drinking
water for many of the country's displaced people.
In Madagascar, humanitarian officials said rescue operations were continuing
to identify and assist displaced people in areas isolated by the tropical
storms Eline and Gloria. Although floodwaters have been receding in the Indian
Ocean island 400 km off the Mozambique coast, landslides have reportedly
prevented many roads from reopening.
In Botswana, where 73,000 people were left homeless by the floods, the
government has already repaired damaged water systems and provided water
sources in most of the flood-hit areas in the northeast of the country.
Meanwhile, reports reached IRIN on Friday from Swaziland, where it was
reported that much of the staple maize crop had been wiped out. The Kingdom
which shares borders with Mozambique and South Africa will now have to make up
some of the shortfall from donors.
MOZAMBIQUE
UNICEF, in its latest report, said some key roads in Mozambique have been
reopened and aid was now being distributed by road. It said, however, that many
main roads in rural areas remained submerged in floodwaters.
Death toll mounts
The numbers of people killed in the flooding has now reached 640 following
the discovery of more bodies in the southern town of Chokwe and surrounding
areas in the Gaza Province, a government spokesman said.
Antonio Macheve said on Friday that searches will continue as water levels
recede and more bodies are found. "A further 100 people were still reported
missing in the province," Macheve said.
Food aid
To help revive Mozambique's economy, WFP said on Friday it was buying as
much food as possible on the domestic market. Since the beginning of the
floods, WFP has purchased 900 mt of maize locally. The agency said it planned
to buy an additional US $1 million worth of maize this week.
It added that it has delivered more than 3,876 mt of food aid nationwide
since the beginning of its emergency operations in February. WFP said it would
use the food it buys to pay workers to help reconstruct the highways connecting
Save to Beira and Vilankulos - both vital lifelines for the movement of aid in
central Mozambique.
Shelter
UNICEF said 30,000 flood victims are accommodated in 12 shelters in Maputo
province, while in the capital, Maputo about 4,000 displaced people have sought
shelter in four schools and a factory. It added that the largest refugee camp
is in Chaquelane, about 160 km northeast of the capital in the Gaza province,
where an estimated 57,000 people are accommodated.
It said most displaced people's camps are in "desperate" need of necessities
including food and water.
Women and children's plight
UNICEF estimates that 30 to 40 percent of children in parts of Inhambane
province have been separated from their parents. It was working with the
Mozambican government in registering all unaccompanied children. It added,
however, that 58 out of 110 unaccompanied children in the Chaquelane camp have
been reunited with their families.
The UN's World Health Organisation (WHO), meanwhile, estimates that 15,300
displaced women are pregnant and that 4,600 of these women are expected to
deliver within the next three months and require special medical attention to
ensure safe delivery.
Health
UNICEF said since the beginning of the floods, most of the affected
population has had little food or safe drinking water. "Cases of severe and
moderate malnutrition among children in the affected areas are increasing,"
UNICEF said.
WHO has warned that 800,000 people in the Southern African region are at
risk of cholera and malaria because of the floods. It added that in one
hospital in Maputo province, at least 500 people have malaria symptoms, while
in Chaquelane, at least 300 malaria cases are diagnosed daily, added UNICEF.
Water and sanitation
The lack of clean and safe drinking water has been made worse by the serious
damage caused to wells and sanitation facilities in many parts of the country,
said UNICEF. Cases of communicable diseases and outbreaks of malaria and
dysentery have increased as a result.
UNICEF said it is providing support for hygiene education campaigns and
communication awareness initiatives to prevent further outbreaks of water-borne
diseases. It said it had begun distributing hygiene packs to 50,000 families in
Chibuto and Macia. Each pack, designed for use by individual families, contains
soap, a jerrycan, a bucket and a cholera awareness leaflet.
It also said it would help provide shovels, buckets and protective clothing
to facilitate proper sanitation practices and waste disposal in temporary
shelters. It added that it would help disinfect and chlorinate household wells.
Agriculture
Mozambican authorities estimate that over 311,000 planted acres have been
destroyed in the provinces of Maputo, Gaza and Inhambane. Further estimates
indicate that 62,600 farming households have lost their farming lands and that
the most affected crops include maize, beans, rice, sweet potatoes, peanuts and
vegetables.
In addition, the authorities estimate that more than 50,000 head of cattle
have died.
UNICEF said it is preparing to support rehabilitation efforts in agriculture
by providing seeds, basic farming tools, equipment, as well as emergency
veterinary assistance for surviving cattle.
Education
UNICEF said efforts to reconstruct and repair destroyed and damaged school
buildings are underway. It said 10 mt of emergency education materials have
been airlifted from the capital, Maputo to Chibuto in the Gaza province.
The supplies include exercise books, pencils, pens, black boards, sets of
school maps and soccer balls, which will benefit at least 79 schools, UNICEF
added.
Nutrition
UNICEF and WFP are helping to provide more than 129 mt of high-protein
biscuits to children and adults suffering from malnutrition. The two UN
agencies are also setting up therapeutic feeding centres in various camps,
including the hard-hit Maciene town.
BOTSWANA
Floodwaters have diminished throughout the country following torrential
rains that displaced 73,000 people mainly in the northeast of Botswana, USAID
said in its latest report.
Damage
The government said that the bulk of the damage occurred in heavily
populated areas in the eastern half of the country, where a majority of
Botswana's 1.5 million people live. It said 17,000 houses were damaged or
destroyed, the majority of which were informal or squatter settlements.
USAID said recent reports indicated that 400 homes were damaged in the
northeast and that about 1,300 people were left homeless in areas around the
western town of Ghanzi.
Shelter
Botswana's National Disaster Management Unit has identified a need for more
tents and planned to purchase 3,800 tents in the near future. USAID said it had
observed that tents provided by the government have been set up in many flood-
affected areas.
Health
USAID said that no significant outbreaks of water-borne or vector-borne
diseases resulting from the floods have been reported. It said many health
posts were open and appeared to have sufficient medicines for basic needs. It
added that the prevalence of diarrhoea cases appeared to be declining.
Water and sanitation
The government, according to USAID, has already repaired damaged water
systems and provided water sources in most affected areas. However, a joint UN,
USAID and government team will visit Maun, located in the northeast, to assess
damage to water and sanitation systems, roads, agriculture and clinics.
SWAZILAND
Heavy rains and flooding in Swaziland have destroyed at least 10 percent of
the country's cotton crop resulting a loss of revenue earnings estimated at
over US $9 million, according to the agriculture ministry.
The tiny Kingdom bordering Mozambique and South Africa, also expects to see
yields of the national staple, maize, reduced by 37 percent.
According to the country's National Disaster Relief Unit, Swaziland will
have to import 66,000 mt of maize, and will seek assistance from donors to make
up the remainder of the country's annual 138,000 mt requirements.
The Swazi authorities also reported that bean and sweet potato crops had
virtually been wiped out, and that farmers could not plant new crops because
fields along the swollen Umbuluzi and Komati rivers which flow into southern
Mozambique were flooded.
This item is delivered by the UN's IRIN humanitarian information unit (e-
mail: irin@ocha.unon.org; fax: +254 2 622129; Web:
http://www.reliefweb.int/IRIN), but may not necessarily reflect the views of
the United Nations. If you re-print, copy, archive or re-post this item, please
retain this credit and disclaimer.

Original_Search_Title: DOs face Moi cut-back
Headline: Kenya: DOs face Moi cut-back
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The number of District Officers is to be reduced, the President
announced yesterday. Those who keep their jobs will have to cover larger areas.
This will toughen them up and help them to be ready for bigger challenges -
including possible promotion to become tomorrow's District Commissioners, the
President said.
His decision comes hard on the heels of his about-turn on the planned
sacking of 6,000 assistant chiefs as part of an attempt to streamline the
provincial administration.
The chiefs and their assistants will stay, the President repeated, but they
must be at least 30 years old and be sufficiently mature to resolve both
domestic and community disputes.
District Commissioners will be required to teach assistant chiefs about
their roles in modern Kenya, the President told a meeting of district leaders
in Kabarnet.
The President once again told chiefs and their assistants never to involve
themselves in the collection of harambee money or any other funds. He reminded
the provincial administration that its main role was to interpret government
policies to wananchi.
The exact number of DOs countrywide - said to be in the hundreds - could not
be immediately established. There are however about 70 District Commissioners
each of whom is responsible for several District Officers.
At the same time the President announced a one-month amnesty during which
people with illegal firearm scan surrender them to the authorities without fear
of prosecution.
The arms could be surrendered at District Commissioners' offices or the
nearest police station.
The Head of State said the amnesty follows the Africa conference on the
proliferation of small arms and light weapons, held in Nairobi recently, where
leaders expressed concern over the dangers arms posed in the region.
He called on leaders irrelevant of their political affiliation to put the
welfare of wananchi foremost.
A good leader should assist and guide wananchi to help people achieve their
development goals.
It was pointless for them to be seen only during election time.
The President told leaders from Baringo to ensure they attended district
development committee meetings, saying they were important forums. They should
ensure they prioritised developments according to demand and the availability
of funds. The President asked agricultural extension officers and the
provincial administration in Baringo to spearhead campaigns aimed at making
wananchi aware of the need to curb soil erosion.
Saying he was unhappy with the agricultural officers, President Moi told
them to make themselves visible and teach wananchi about soil conservation and
the need to plant drought resistant crops like sweet potatoes.
He also asked the provincial administration to tell people of the dangers
posed by the destruction of forests.
On education, he said leaders and parents should be meeting every year to
review the performance of schools. This would help in finding the reasons for
any poor performances.
On AIDs, President Moi emphasised there was no cure yet, adding the Kenyan
population was threatened with decimation.
Each District Commissioner should hold four meetings a month and District
Officers to hold a meeting every week.
He said chiefs would also be involved in these meetings for effective
dissemination of information regarding this killer disease and efforts to stop
its spread.
He said it would be prudent to form district committees which would include
religious leaders and teachers in this regard. He emphasized that cattle
rustling was an outdated practice which should be discarded by communities
still practising it adding that the practice deprived families of their
livelihood and brought immense suffering.
President Moi observed that it was unfortunate that one of the main causes
of cattle rustling was young men looking for cattle for bride price.
He advised elders in affected communities to relax such outdated cultural
requirement so that prides were easily affordable.
He said young men should be given longer time to pay bride price as they
accumulate wealth.

Original_Search_Title: Relief Supplies Arrive At Mombasa Port Kenya
Headline: Kenya: Relief Supplies Arrive At Mombasa Port Kenya
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Thousands of tonnes of relief food has arrived at Mombasa.
This is part of international relief agencies' supplies to help
alleviate famine in Eastern Africa.
Some 29,000 tonnes of wheat, sorghum, wheat and yellow maize are
expected to be discharged this week. More than 25,000 metric tonnes
of yellow maize arrived on board the mv Otello Mombasa yesterday in
transit to Uganda.
The food, from the World Food Programme's office in Rome, is said to
be part of a large consignment of relief supplies at the port.
Another vessel, the mv Hope Well, is expected at the port with yellow
maize, sorghum and wheat for Somalia and Uganda.
According to information from the Kenya Ports Authority, the vessel
will discharge 1,002 tonnes of wheat, 2,505 tonnes of sorghum and 913
tonnes of yellow maize for Samalia and 1,780 bags of yellow maize for
Uganda.
At the same time, local millers and commodity dealers have imported
more than 28,000 tonnes of wheat.
Atta Kenya Ltd has imported 20,000 tonnes, Bakex Millers of Thika
4,000 tones, Suntania 3,500 tonnes and Cemco Uganda 550 tonnes.
Last week, 15,000 tonnes of maize imported by Unga from South Africa
arrived at the port.
The World Food Programme has launched an urgent appeal for
assistance, saying it needs Sh6.9 billion to feed an estimated 3.3
million hungry Kenyans. President Moi recently announced a duty
waiver on maize imports and directed the Minister for Finance, Mr.
Chris Okemo, to effect it as part of the government's efforts to
address the hunger.
More maize imports are expected following reports that the
government's famine relief programme stocks dwindled to less than
60,000 at the beginning of June. A further 1.3 million bags were held
as commercial stocks by the National Cereals and Produce Board.
It is estimated that more than three million Kenyans are in desperate
need of food assistance compared with 961,000 in 1992, the last time
the country experienced a severe drought.
According to the Office of the President, maize purchases made during
the last season totalled 868,680 bags, while, since July, last year,
more than 811,600 bags have been distributed to drought- and
famine-hit areas.
The government has set aside Sh500 million to buy303,000 bags of
maize for distribution to food deficient districts.
Apart from the anticipated government imports, local millers and
commodity dealers are expected to bring in thousands of tonnes of
maize to cash in on the shortage and the duty waiver.
Shipping and KPA sources said large quantities of maize imports were
expected at Mombasa this month.

Original_Search_Title: Increasing Refugee Exodus Exhaust Relief Supplies
Headline: Sudan: Increasing Refugee Exodus Exhaust Relief Supplies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Almost two decades of civil war has left Sudan with many overseen
emergencies. Blue Nile, where Action By Churches Together ACT has an ongoing
relief project, is just one of the areas where needs are growing but funds for
relief are in very short supply. Jane Connolly visited the area and recounts
the situation in one of Africa's most volatile situation.
A hard, intermittent rain is falling as we land at a remote airstrip in
Sudan's Blue Nile area. It's the beginning of the rainy season - great for
planting but water on the black cottonseed earth makes it sticky and
treacherous.
As we drive from the airstrip three tall women saunter past in formation
with huge piles of straw on their heads, like supermodels in outrageous hats.
They are working in the rain to rebuild and extend the mud-and straw church.
The people already living in the settlements, called Belatuma, don't have
much. They are mainly Maban who came last year and settled here. They were
reaching devastation point even before other displaced groups started arriving.
Now, 7,000 people have come in to the area in the last few days. People are
coming to Belatuma from Mayak and Wadega, both of which have been affected by
drought. Kali and Karen-Karen, too.
Also, an estimated 2,000 Nuer and Bunj have been displaced from Adan, an oil
area. They will be making their way here, or somewhere equally unequipped to
host these desperate guests.
The Blue Nile area is in the eastern parts of Sudan, close to the border
with Ethiopia. Here, as in most of Sudan, it is not culturally acceptable to
send away anyone.
As long as you can give them water it's up to the visitor to decide to stay
or go. The local leaders are encouraging the new arrivals to disperse and to
cultivate the land for themselves. But what will they eat until their food is
grown?
In a "normal" year Blue Nile Province is self-sufficient in terms of food
security. But while the eastern part of southern Blue Nile continues to
produce, crops had failed in the western part due to floods in November and
December of last year.
Since January, thousands of people have been chased from their homes near
some oilfields and have arrived at Wadega, Maringi and Belatuma displaced
camps. Eyewitnesses report the Government of Sudan (GoS) army and their related
militia carrying out systematic depopulation of this highly strategic area
around the oilfields.
Yet more people are coming east because of drought. In addition, the food
crisis in Ethiopia has meant that many Sudanese who had gone into exile there
are now making their way home.
An ACT supported Church Consortium ACT-CC is the only relief agency working
in Blue Nile and it's staff has already had to cut food distributions by half,
due to lack of funding.
"We don't know how to distribute," says a staff member who for security
reasons cannot be named. "There are so many more people in need than we were
able to anticipate. Even in January people were relying on roots and wild
foods".
The newly-arrived displaced tell that they left their homes in Girop (near
Bunj in northern Upper Nile) after fierce GoS offensives in the area. It had
taken them four days walking to reach Belatuma.
Rebel fighters with Sudan People's Liberation Army SPLA had had a camp in
Girop which served as some protection for the local population. That was until
GoS pursued SPLA and sacrificed any local citizen who happened to be in their
way, using heavy artillery against them.
The final straw for many came when a young boy and his sister were snatched
by the government forces, the boy arbitrarily beaten and the girl taken away.
So the population fled, leaving their carefully tended fields for the
possibility of scavenging rock-hard palm fruit (the only "wild food" available
here) and safety.
Now they are selling their beads for sorghum. Some will continue on their
way to Wadega and Kurmuk. If they are lucky they can make "homes" under plastic
sheeting, given to them by existing residents, to whom they had originally been
donated by ACT-CC.
Walle Dwagi is in her sixties, thin and somewhat fragile-looking, but still
standing tall. She and her family have moved into an abandoned and half-
collapsed tukul (mud hut). Given what she has been through in the last week,
she must be stronger than she looks.
"My son, daughters-in-law, grandchildren and I ran here from Girop because
of fighting and came here to look for food. But when we arrived we found there
was none. For four days we have been living on palm fruit and "lalop" leaves.
An official from the Sudan Relief and Rehabilitation Association (the relief
wing of the SPLA), told us that 15 to 17 thousand were on the move from Girop,
Midir and Tebreb. SRRA officials are notorious for gross overestimation in such
circumstances.
Peter Luic, a local Maban has just come back to Blue Nile from the Bonga
refugee camp in Ethiopia, where he works as a camp leader and medical
assistant. Many in the camp suffer from malaria and dysentery.
His people are starting to come home to Blue Nile. They want to cultivate,
which they are not allowed to do in the refugee camp. But should they stay or
go? In Bonga there is no famine, but they have no freedom. They are not even
allowed to collect firewood.
Each family has an area 15 X15m and they live in a state of total dependence
on the United Nations High Commission for Refugees UNHCR. In 1987, Peter left
Anyile and went to Bonga with his family. Now they remain in Bonga.
Accompanied by the Programme Manager of ACT-CC, I take a walk around the
village where their compound is situated. Past the village, we spot two new
tukuls, far away from everything else and each other. This is not how the
Sudanese live.
Puzzled, we approach the nearest tukul. A family are there, regarding us
warily. The Programme Manager greets them in Arabic. They greet him in return,
but they are still nervous as they enter into a conversation with him.
