for everybody to win.
After four exciting months of fortnightly wins in different regions,
Coca- Cola brought 18 finalists to Lagos to contest for the grand
prize of N500,000. At the end of the day, Mr. George Onuguwa from
Benue State clinched the big prize in addition to the N100,000 he won
in the earlier plant draws.
Onuguwa, a 24-year-old patent medicine seller, emerged from 18
national qualifiers who converged from the North, South, West, East
and Central Zones of the promotion for the national draw held at
MUSON Centre, Onikan Lagos.
By the end of the promotion 90 people won N100,000 each, 800 others
won N10,000 each, while thousands won N1000, N100 across the country,
including thousands of free drinks as instant prizes.
Speaking shortly after receiving his cheque for the N500,000 the
excited star prize winner said the money was a big break that is sure
to turn his life around. According to him, "God has used Coca-Cola to
give me a chance to be somebody in life".
Head of Lagos State Lotteries and Pools Betting Board Hajia Kabir who
was present at the draw also applauded the promotion for its
transparency and the impact on the lives of the winners.
She commended the Coca-Cola initiative for giving so much money to
its consumers in the promotion adding that "this promotion is a
praiseworthy consumer friendly move by Coca-Cola which has really
served to identify with its consumers."
Lucozade Boost Registration for the Second Phase of the Lucozade
Boost Saturday Workout continues at the University of Lagos Sports
Ground. Dr. Ojo, a Physical and Health lecturer at the university who
also doubles as the co- ordinator said the extension became
necessary, so as to afford the public the opportunity of
participating in the two hour workout.
The Lucozade Boost workout is an initiative of SmithKline Beecham,
makers of Lucozade Boost, Ribena and a host of pharmaceutical and
consumer healthcare products, in conjunction with the University of
Lagos.
The programme which was designed to help reduce tension and stress
among the citizenry, has to a large extent recorded an appreciable
degree, a position hi nged on the yearnings of participants of the
first phase who asked for the extension.
For those wishing to be part of the workout, participation fee
attracts N200, while the sum of N150 is for the old members, and
would cover the 13 weeks duration of the programme.
Publication date: October 23, 2000 (20001023)

Original_Search_Title: Miss Nigeria: Vien Bemigbo Tetsola
Headline: Nigeria: Miss Nigeria: Vien Bemigbo Tetsola
Body_Text:
Lagos — People say that beauty is in the eye of the beholder. But in
the case of Vien Bemigbo Tetsola, the DTN-sponsored Miss Nigeria, her
beholders did see beyond mere beauty, brains too. And, watch it! At
first sight, you could get smitten, by this born again Christian,
with a simple and nice disposition.
As a person and a professional, who are you?
I am simple and easy-going and as a professional, I hope to get to
the peak in modelling and broadcasting.
To whom do you owe whatever inputs you've made to fashion?
I owe most of it to my fashion designer Larry K, owner of 'Mon Ami',
best designer, 1999. Most of the designs he makes for me are done in
African fabrics to promote Nigerian culture.
Where do you go from here?
After my reign as queen, I hope to set up my own Non-governmental
Organisation as a follow up to the Aids campaign, after which I will
face my profession in Mass Communication which I am presently
studying in the Delta State University, Lagos campus.
What is fashion to you?
To me, fashion is about putting on good clothes. How you model
outfits, is what makes you fashionable. Timeliness counts, but it's
more about wearing what suits you, not just what is in vogue.
How long have you been in this business?
Seven months to be precise, but largely I have been in the business
since 1996, freelancing with people like Frank Osodi, Ben
Murray-Bruce and a host of others, not really attached to any agent.
Do people's opinion of you count much?
I am a simple person. I try not to pretend. My friends know that I am
not a proud person. And I don't ever dress to impress anyone.
What's leisure like for you?
I read the bible a lot and I read novels. I like writing and watching
TV.
At what point in life did you become interested in what you are doing
now? Were you amazed at discovering it?
I have always had this dream that some day I will be a beauty queen.
This started in 1994, and people kept coming to me, telling me that I
have a good chance of making it to the top.
Who is your favourite designer?
Larry K of 'Mon Ami'.
Who or what inspires your choice?
In Nigeria, Nike Oshinowo, abroad, Whitney Houston.
What is your look and what kind of outfit do you go for?
All outfits depending on the occasion.
Accessories ?
I wear them simple, as I try not to dress too loud.
Any make-up or closet bias?
I use MAC powder. It makes me look natural, no pimples or
dis-colouration; Fashion Fair lipsticks leave no stains and last
longer.
My closet is fearfully and wonderfully made up of choice clothes.
What are your favourite colours ?
I love blue, it reminds me of love and peace.
What's the first thing on your mind when getting dressed?
I want to look good. I see myself as a role model people look up to,
and as such, I need to dress up to look smart and presentable.
What clothes turn you off?
Uncomfortable outfits that are untidy and ill-fitting. I won't be
caught dead in them nor in baggy clothes.
What are your favourite perfumes?
Organzar, Kenzo Jungle, Romance, Beverly Hills etc.
Where do you shop?
'Everywoman' for corporate wears, 'Collectibles' for casuals but most
designs I wear are from 'Mon ami'.
What is the most expensive thing you have ever bought for yourself?
A Rado wrist watch.
Any perculiar beauty tips?
I use less make-up minimally. A bit of exercise, and try not to eat
late night in order to keep a flat tummy. I take fruits and
vegetables, to maintain a fresh skin.
What would you always keep in your make-up bag?
My perfume.
Where is your favourite hangout ?
Church.
What is a typical day like for you?
Wake up in the morning, thank God, go to school. I normally go to
functions especially as part of my official schedule, but I always
want God to direct me for the day.
Who are your role models?
Whitney Houston and Pastor Chris Okotie.
Any advice for dressing up or down?
Be yourself, try to create a style for yourself, just be creative.
If you are to go on a pleasure trip for three days, what would you
take with you?
My bible, materials to study for my project and my handouts for
school work.
If you had to take three things out of a burning house, what would
they be?
My certificates, pictures and cell phone.
How do you relax?
I read, watch movies, and listen to music. I particularly like
Whitney Houston's songs, and back home, the 'Plantation Boyz'.
Favourite authors?
Danielle Steel, Jeffrey Archer
Favourite food or drink?
I love rice, carrot soup with plantain, and I drink Sans cream soda.
Any favourite travel or tour spots?
Paris, the centre of fashion. I'd love to travel around the world to
see what's happening on all sides of the globe.
Do you work-out, if not how do you keep trim?
I try has much as possible to get my tummy trimmed down and I jog to
keep fit.
What is the craziest thing you've ever done in your life?
None really.
Which were your greatest moments in life?
The day I accepted Jesus Christ as my Lord and personal saviour, and
the day I was crowned Miss Nigeria.
Your favourite magazines?
Ebony, Ovation, Hello, Cosmopolitan and Vogue.

Original_Search_Title: Irin Update 1,020
Headline: Uganda: GREAT LAKES: IRIN Update 1,020
Body_Text:
UGANDA: Return of displaced "slow"
The permanent return of internally displaced people (IDPs) to their
homes of origin is going ahead "very slowly", OCHA said. However, in
Nyahuka in Bundibugyo district, western Uganda, there appeared to be
an increase in the daily movement of IDPs to their fields. The report
said that planting was occurring and the rains had been good. It also
noted that food was scarce in the markets at this time, with some
significant price increases witnessed for beans, cassava and matoke
[plantain].
The report further noted that due to inflated population figures in
IDP camps, ICRC suspended distribution of non-food items to camps
until the IDP numbers were verified and reflected the "true picture".
WFP also suspended food distributions for a month to all but the most
needy camps due to the sensitive issue of selling relief food.
DRC: UNHCR assessment mission for Central Africa
UNHCR announced on Tuesday that it was sending a mission to the two
Congos and Central African Republic (CAR) to assess the impact of a
fresh arrival of refugees from the DRC who are settled along the
Congo and Oubangui rivers, north of the neighbouring Republic of
Congo (ROC). The agency's spokesman in Geneva, Kris Janowski, told
journalists that the team would visit several areas affected by the
influx and look at ways of helping refugees trapped in unreachable
parts of the river. It will also consider cross-border aid deliveries
from the Central African Republic (CAR) where about 98,000 refugees
have arrived recently after fleeing fighting between DRC's government
forces and those of the MLC in Equateur province. Most of the
refugees, Janowski noted, are presently unreachable because of
tension along the river.
A recent UNHCR mission to Betou prefecture, in northern Republic of
Congo (ROC), confirmed the presence of some 20,000 refugees, most of
whom arrived in July. "They are putting a tremendous strain on the
local population, which stood at 7,000 before their arrival," he
said. He noted that fish and cassava had become very scarce, adding
that the price of cassava had doubled since the refugees' arrival.
"Although many of them have been accommodated with local families,
they sleep in the open and lack basic necessities," Janowski said.
UNHCR, which is currently planning to provide medical, educational,
agricultural and food aid to the population, is negotiating with the
authorities to grant the refugees plots of arable land. It also plans
to provide plastic sheeting, wooden rafters, kitchen sets and
blankets. Vaccinations against measles have been administered to some
4,000 persons, including both refugees and the local population.
RWANDA: Lobby group rejects 'gacaca' courts in Rwanda
An exiled lobby group, the Rassemblement pour le Retour des Refugies
et la Democratie au Rwanda (RDR), on Wednesday appealed to all
"democratic governments", human rights organisations and
freedom-loving peoples, to denounce, condemn and not fund the
traditional court system, known as 'gacaca', proposed by the Rwandan
government. It said gacaca was proposed as a solution to the
overcrowding of state prisons "created by massive arbitrary arrests
and prolonged illegal detentions". Furthermore, gacaca "violates the
principle five of the basic principles on the independence of the
judiciary", the RDR said. It added that gacaca jurisdictions also
"grossly violate" the right of the accused to have a fair trial
before a competent, independent and impartial tribunal as recognised
by the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and the International
Covenant on Civil and Political Rights.

Original_Search_Title: Murders Affect Business
Headline: Ghana: Murders Affect Business
Body_Text:
Accra — The murders that rocked the country recently are taking their
toll on Accra's trading environment. Women traders appear to have
abandoned their dawn and late night commuting, the period that the
perpetrators found convenient to strike.
A survey by the Ghana News Agency in Accra, reveals that business
undertaken particularly by women has slackened to a late start and
early closure. This guarantees their safety but also means a slash in
earnings, as some market women and business people around the Tema
lorry station and Makola market said.
They said for fear of their lives, they now get to the market and
other trading points around 8am instead of 5am giving them shorter
periods for sales.
"This has made it necessary for some of us to increase the prices of
foodstuff to make up for the loss," one plantain seller said.
Ama Frema, a bread seller, said she misses the patronage of early
travellers who bought her bread at the lorry station. Adjoa, a shoe
seller, said traders are sometimes forced to sleep at the markets
when they have to sell late into the night. According to her, the
recent shortage of diesel, which caused a dislocation in
transportation, also compelled a number of market women and traders
to sleep in groups by their wares overnight.
A few daring women however, said they would not be bogged down by
fear. "When you are too anxious, that is when you get killed," a
young lady working with a communications centre said.
Two women shop assistants said although they had heard about the
killings, they have not taken any specific steps to ensure their
safety since, as they put it, "we pray every day and believe in God".
The police are yet to solve the mystery surrounding the murders and
with the uneasy calm after the stormy protests mainly by women's
groups, hopes of finding the perpetrators appear to be dwindling.
Publication date: September 25, 2000

Original_Search_Title: Save Ghana Now! a Genesis of the Economic Crisis Under the PNDC
Headline: Ghana: Save Ghana Now! a Genesis of the Economic Crisis Under the PNDC
Body_Text:
Accra — In 1983 the PNDC launched the Structural Adjustment
Programme, otherwise known as the Economic Recovery Programme (ERP),
under the supervision of the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and
the World Bank (WB) who promised that the programme will help promote
growth, stabilize the external accounts, and reduce poverty.
The policy actions that were implemented to achieve these objectives
included trade liberalization; abolition of foreign exchange controls
and import restrictions; devaluation of the exchange rate; monetary
and fiscal anti-inflationary measures including control of bank
lending; higher interest rates; reduced spending; increases in taxes;
withdrawal of subsidy for example on education, health, petrol,
agriculture etc.; control of wage increase; open-door policy on
foreign investment and multinational corporations including the free
repatriation of their profits; reduction of spending on social
services: such as education, health, housing, etc.; privatization or
sale of public enterprises. In other words the IMF insisted on the
widest possible latitude for market forces in the overall management
of the economy.
It is evident that only a tyrannical dictatorship could implement
such an anti-people programme. This is the sense in which the IMF has
often been accused of propping up repressive authoritarian regimes
because no democratically elected government, compelled by a
democratic constitution to act in the interest of the people, and
return to them periodically to renew its mandate can successfully
implement the IMF conditionalities and survive. In other words, to
carry out the IMF- sponsored SAP, the government in question must
possess the capacity to suppress resistance to IMF austerity
programmes. The bloody suppression of the Kume Preko demonstration in
1995 is a case in point. It is therefore no coincidence that the
Rawlings regime has been the most rigorous adherent to the IMF/WB
programmes in Africa, and at the same time has proven to be the most
repressive the history of the country.
It is important to stress here that the real and unstated objectives
of SAP are to ensure that we repay our debts to the Western
creditors, which are the major sponsors of the IMF and WB. In 1999
alone Ghana was made to cough out three trillion cedis
(C3,O0O,OOO,OOO,OOO.OO) as interest payments to the Western
creditors. The other unstated objective is to intensify the foreign
domination and exploitation of our country through unequal exchange
falsely labeled as free trade; expand the accumulation of profits by
private capital through the weakening of the public sector by
privatization and through the imposition of market forces; undermine
and weaken the working class through retrenchment of labour, wage
freeze and the reduction of real wages. In all these SAP intends to
integrate us into the international capitalist system, not as equal
partners but as neocolonial appendages which they hope to perpetually
consign to the role of producers of raw materials and consumers of
western products.
The main agents in this process of recolonizing Ghana are the
multinational corporations such as Unilever, Nestle, Kaiser, and
recently, mining companies which have all been given the widest
latitudes of freedom to choose their modes of operation and location.
As a result of this they have concentrated their investment in
consumer goods manufacturing in overcrowded urban centres like Accra,
Kumasi and Tema, in order to secure the quickest and maximum levels
of profits that these markets offer. That is why Kpando, Sunyani,
Asamankese, Tamale, or Bolga are not suitable candidates for
multinational investments.
Some of the harmful consequences of this include a disproportionate
attention accorded the manufacturing of superficial and semi-luxury
products like cigarettes beer, etc. and the importation of luxury
cars, liquor and other consumer goods.
Even more shortsighted is Ghana's policy towards agriculture. While
food production has stagnated and the country has become a net
importer of food some peasants are encouraged, for example, to grow
cotton, cashew or tobacco for export, rather than much-needed food
crops for the Ghanaian masses. It is a complete indictment on the
government that we now have to import plantain, cooking oil and other
staples. The Ministry of Finance confirms that the country spends
$100 million annually on rice imports when a fraction of that can
transform Northern Ghana into the granary of the country. Equally
very serious is SAP's insensitive exposure of our agriculture and
especially our rural peasants to the so-called competition of the
world market. Incidentally in the Western countries that constitute
this market, governments protect their farmers by a barrage of
tariffs, price supports and subsidies.
The circumstances of SAP in Ghana are such that they have had adverse
and traumatic effects on the masses of the Ghanaian people. The major
negative consequences are both internal and external. Internally, SAP
has worsened, and will continue to do so, unemployment of all
categories of workers through its massive retrenchment and freeze on
employment in the public and private sectors, privatization, subsidy
withdrawal, drastic spending cuts, destruction of small and
indigenous capital, and import dumping. Real wages have also
plummeted through wage freeze and inflation unleashed by the
continuous decline in the value of the cedi especially this year when
the currency has depreciated by more that 100 per cent.
In the external sector, the SAP's open door policy on Multinational
Corporations and foreign investment has tightened the grip of
foreigners over the economy who have taken advantage of these
structural distortions to transfer abroad huge amounts of profits in
foreign exchange. In effect the net beneficiaries of SAP and the
foreign loans supposedly pouring into Ghana are the MNCs, foreign
consultants and their home countries.
It has been estimated that about 80% of American assistance funds
flow back immediately to the US through exports tied to that aid. For
example every dollar that American taxpayers pay into the World Bank
generates about $10 in procurement contracts for US companies for the
supply of equipment, vehicles, and management contracts for technical
skills and training.
When Japan grants a loan to Ghana for a specified project, part of
the condition is that Ghana has to import the needed vehicles and
machinery from Japanese companies irrespective of the fact that we
could have got the same machines cheaper elsewhere on the
international market. The Japanese government will also normally
insist that Japanese companies be granted the contracts for the
execution of Japanese government-funded projects. And so the money
goes back to where it came from, and Ghanaians are then saddled with
a loan they have to pay back with interest.
The destructive conditions attached to the SAP are so pervasive that
they directly impinge on, or even insult our sovereignty. IMF and
World Bank staff supervise the Bank of Ghana and the Ministries of
Finance and Trade. Indeed our annual budgets have to be approved or
endorsed by these agencies.
From the above it is obvious that the deepening crisis of our
underdevelopment is the result of the logic of world imperialism.
This however does not absolve us, Ghanaians in general, from
responsibility for our predicament. There is an African saying that
when the witches enter the house then someone has opened the door. It
was the Rawlings regime which opened the floodgates of our economy to
these predatory plunderers. Those who have benefited from SAP are the
Rawlings regime and its cronies, while the broad masses of workers,
peasants, students, petty artisans, market women and even the
so-called middle-class salaried workers are the losers. In
particular, both domestic and foreign exporters gain through higher
profits from devaluation while the local financial institutions
especially the banks reap higher profits from higher interest rates
among others. In other words, the bulk of SAP's adverse effects fall
on the poor working people.
We of the Socialist Forum are of the conviction that the interests of
Ghana, and indeed those of the Third World are marginal to the
functioning of the IMF and World Bank. What Ghana needs is
development, the ability to produce what we consume, and this
includes not only food and other consumer items, but also capital
goods such as machines. We certainly cannot achieve this by following
IMF prescriptions which insist that we should concentrate on the
production of the goods for which we have a comparative advantage. In
other words we should leave the production of machines and other
electronic gadgets to Europe, America and Japan, and concentrate our
attention on the production of primary products such as cocoa, gold
and timber, because we have a comparative advantage in those areas.
This is inspite of the cyclical price crises initiated largely by the
exploitative capitalist speculators, and which are largely
responsible for our inability to liquidate underdevelopment. Of
course this is without prejudice to the systematic looting of our
national wealth by the kleptocratic regime.
We of the Socialist Forum therefore reject the IMF and SAP and
identify ourselves with the vision of Kwame Nkrumah which emphasizes
national development through the mobilization of the Ghanaian people
around a
collective national self-reliance programme. We have identified the
priorities of the majority of Ghanaians to include food, security,
functional shelter, health care, education and transport. Alcoholic
beverages, liquor, luxury cars and presidential jets are certainly
not among these priorities.
We equally advocate the drastic curtailment of the exaggerated role
of external loans and foreign aid in our economy. More important for
us is the urgent need to pursue indigenous industrialization at two
levels: First, an industrialization strategy that matches
manufacturing of basic needs items with local resources, based on
national needs. The second is the manufacturing of capital goods
preferably through regional and sub-regional cooperation. Ultimately
however our economic future lies in the integration of African
economies. In this regard we Africans need to urgently organize our
national, political and economic strengths and link them up with
collective continental effort.
Our strategies and priorities must be clearly defined around these
objectives. This is the way in which we can structurally disengage
ourselves from the exploitative orbit of the international market as
it is today. We have also to make it unambiguously clear that the IMF
and World Bank and their Western creditors are part of the problem
and cannot therefore be the solution, and that the adjustment
programmes they are sponsoring will only catapult us from one crisis
to the other.
Finally we would like to stress that it is only those who suffer from
the destructive consequences of SAP who can change their situation.
We are therefore appealing to the working people of Ghana to extend
their battle lines beyond the economics of their struggles and
mobilize themselves around the issues we have raised here. The
problems confronting Ghana today are generally economic, but their
solution is political. In other words which political leadership has
the requisite economic and political programme to deal with the
current crisis, and as such deserves to be voted into power ?. This
should be the issue in this year's elections.

Original_Search_Title: Nigerians Counselled On Overseas Study
Headline: Nigerians Counselled On Overseas Study
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigerian students have been advised to study science-based
courses when studying abroad to hasten the country's development.
Mr. John D.S. Williams, an educational consultant and founder of the
British Education Training and Technology (BETTA), England, gave this
advice at his Lagos office in an interview recently.
"I think what is needed out here is specialist skills, it's much
difficult to acquire through a Nigerian University basically, because
to do an Engineering course, the capital cost of keeping that
equipment up-to-date is enormous," he said.
Using the University of Nottingham in England as an example of heavy
investors in science-based courses, Williams said: "They invest
something like a $100 million a year on updating their equipment in
the engineering or medical laboratories etc."
"That is a huge investment, therefore it's much better to go, abroad
for courses that require that kind of investment rather than law that
doesn't require it."
Such students on their return to Nigeria would help in the
technological advancement of the country and soon Nigeria would be
able to take its place amongst the developed countries, Williams
said.
He stressed that "there are too many students who travel to the UK
and America and other places doing art-based courses like law,
business administration."
Williams founded BETTA in 1985 to assist students from around the
world to study at British Colleges and Universities.
Every year, BETTA counsels thousands of students, including
Nigerians, to study at anyone of the over seventy British
institutions they represent. The body places students on University
entry courses at Colleges that specialise in the needs of overseas
students.
According to him: "we send first timers to UK, to Tutorial Colleges
that have the experience with dealing with African kids which means
they get food that they like and so on, we make sure, for example,
that the young Nigerian ladies get their plantain because they can't
live without it!"
Describing Nigerian students as very good students, Williams said
'we've had a lot of first class degrees from a lot of Nigerian
students we have sent to British Universities over the past 10, 12
years."
He said Nigerian students do not only study hard but also take active
part in University life in England. He disclosed that in the last two
years at the University of Nottingham, the President of the student
union has been a Nigerian, that is because they become involved and
that is appreciated".

Original_Search_Title: Crawling Through The Niger Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Crawling Through The Niger Delta
Body_Text:
Lagos — Benin City is the gateway to the oil soaked Niger Delta. It
was the largest empire in that region. About six centuries ago, Benin
City exchanged ambassadors with some Europeans like the Portuguese.
Today, the city gets some prominent place in the Nigerian map due to
the grace of God; it is under developed and past governments that ran
the area were allergic to road construction.
On this Thursday night, June 29, the rains visited for some seventy
minutes and the historical city was completely under floods. Not even
the Government Reservation Area (GRA) was spared as the waters took
over the streets and sometimes houses. The Benin kingdom that was
famous for the moat built round the city hundreds of years ago,
virtually as no drainage's in the 21st century. Dirt and sand blocked
the few available.
On this wet night, former governor John Adage Oyegun told me that
with Sharia, issues like derivation are no longer relevant, people
must decide what they want; in this case, Nigerians every where
should control their own resources.
I picked Jesse, the scene of the October 17, 1998 oil pipeline fire
disaster. After that tragedy, the first count of those roasted alive
was over a thousand. The town is composed of thirty-six villages with
three oil wells including those at Okogho and Buboroku.
At the Jesse junction, women sat cooking with firewood. A short
distance away was where the fires of hell consumed the pipeline fire
victims. There was a rectangular cemetery purposely built for the
victims. It has six slabs containing 950 names of the victims given
mass burial at the spot. The first name was that of Isuku Inije and
the last H.Idiodemese. There was a big cross at the head of the
cemetery. A few metres away, there is a signboard which reads
"Danger. NPPC High Pressure Pipeline. No Digging. No bushfire. Keep
off".
One of the villagers told me "Our president (General Abdulsalam
Abubakar) came here. He landed at this very spot we are standing in
an helicopter, he shook his head and made lots of promises including
the construction of a hospital in the area and that was all. He
didn't fulfil any of his promises."
I visited the only medical facility in the town, the Primary
Healthcare Centre. After the tragedy occurred, hundreds of people
were brought there but the place had only six beds, a nurse and an
attendant. Today, nothing has changed except that a new nurse has
come in to replace the old one.
The orphans produced by the disaster were all over the town, some of
the victims remain sick needing medical attention.
My guide round the town contacted a lady victim who is still trying
to cope with life. Immediately I approached, she took to her heels
with her three kids. I increased my pace, then she hid in the
surrounding farm, keeping her head and those of her kids low; so I
lost them. My guide explained that many of the victims remain
paranoid, fearing that government may still come and haul them before
the courts en route to jail as the then governor, Walter Fegbabor had
vowed.
Sapele remains an economically dead town, people seem to have lost
hope. At the Okpe junction was a giant bill board of a church which
told the people "Don't Fear, don't Fret. Don't Despair. You can have
all you want" I made my way to the Koko Garage in the city near the
police headquarters where people square up to traditional food like
starch, black plantain and banga soup. I joined the crowd before
setting out again. I passed Ekpan then to Okpara Waterside which has
a single road and has been abandoned by successive governments in its
state of nature. Nearby Eku had electric poles which meant that
unlike nearby towns like Igun, Orerokpe, Oha and Okurvwhe, it has
electricity.
Waffi (Warri) has clearly regained its complexion lost to years of
inter-ethnic communal clashes; the traffic was back as was commerce.
A friend advised that I should not be taken in by all these; it takes
a few minutes for the city to become a ghost town.
The last point in Delta State where there is electricity is Ughelli,
from its outskirts on to Yenagoa, capital of Oil soaked Bayelsa state
there is no electricity. The main change I found in Yenagoa is that
there are now five fuel stations although only one had fuel to sell.
As is now customary, the East West road linking Delta state to
Bayelsa and Rivers states had lots of failed portions. Just after the
Odi junction, motorists waited an average of ninety minutes to pass
as a lorry had fallen into a big pit on the road and the road had
become narrowed to one very bad lane for vehicles coming from
opposite directions.
Rivers state still has scars of inter communal clashes especially
between Eleme and Okrika while not far from Choba, anti-riot
policemen camped; the Ogbakiri bloody clashes over which the state
government has set up a judicial panel, was still claiming lives.
Uyo, capital of Akwa Ibom now has a far larger population than when I
last visited a few years ago. But the night was still quite dark as
NEPA had thrown the city including its GRA into darkness.
I found the state the most abandoned in the Niger Delta outside
Bayelsa state. Its governor, Obong Victor Attah told me the state
does not even feel the impact of MOBIL the dominant fuel exploiter in
the area. He said "If we have our own OPC, APC or Egbesu imagine what
would happen to Mobil. But we have deliberately discouraged the
formation of such groups."
I came away from the Niger Delta feeling that unless something
positive is done to develop the area, it will be the fire next time.

Original_Search_Title: Task Force Harasses Kanu
Headline: Kenya: Task Force Harasses Kanu
Body_Text:
Lagos — The men of the Remo Local Government Task Force in Ogun State
last week Tuesday along Lagos-Ibadan express road harassed the convoy
of the African Footballer of the Year Nwankwo Kanu,as he visited the
the University College Hospital (UCH) Ibadan in continuation of his
crusade against heart diseases in the country .
Kanu who travel to the ancient city in a Toyota Hiace Bus with
registration number EE759KJ,accompanied by members of his "Heart
Foundation" suddenly encountered the men of the Local Government Task
Force from Remo stopping his vehicle at the second toll gate, asking
the occupants to purchase a rubber waste basket compulsorily a move
apparently meant to generate funds from all commercial buses plying
the express road.
All entreaties to the men of the Task Force fell on deaf ears as they
threatened to puncture the vehicles' tyre if the driver failed to
park and obey their order.
The men of the Remo Local Government Task Force however met serious
resistant in Kanu's Manager,Paul Okoye and the Media Director of his
"Heart Foundation" Onyebuchi Abia who barked at them to clear -off
the road, but they refused to adhere to the order.
However, when THISDAYSports called on men of the Task Force and
explained that the African Footballer of the Year was on an
humanitarian mission to Ibadan, their leader quickly shouted : "Bros
I don't know you are the one inside the vehicle please carry on ."
It was on this note that Kanu was allowed to continue the journey to
Ibadan, but the lanky player who refused to say a word throughout the
shouting session did not fail to call for plantain chips to cool off
as he resumed sleeping .
Meanwhile, the player who did not want to be identified by the crowd
as he covered his face with cap met his waterloo at Ibadan, around
Adam- asingba area when an handkerchief hawker sighted him and
screamed: Kanu ! But the undaunting young man would not succumb to
intimidation thereby resisting the efforts made to calm him down by
the Super Eagles' attacker, as he abandoned his ware to catch further
glimpse of the soccer idol.

Original_Search_Title: Owodunni Declares Bada An Impostor in Nigeria's Cele Church Crisis
Headline: Nigeria: Owodunni Declares Bada An Impostor in Nigeria's Cele Church Crisis
Body_Text:
Lagos — The dust stirred up by the Supreme Court judgement nullifying
the appointment of Pastor Abiodun Bada as Supreme Head of Celestial
Church of Christ is gradually gathering momentum as the man who took
him to court, Pastor Josiah Kayode Owodunni of the CCC Holy Mary
Cathedral, Ijeshatedo revealed how he was appointed by the Pastor
Founder S. B. J. Oshoffa to lead the church.
Said Owodunni at a conference last weekend: "In 1984, at a meeting of
the Board of Trustees of the church held at the instance of Pastor
Oshoffa, he told us, five of us, present, that he would dine with us
today. We were served with fried plantain in a large bowl. The
plantain was prepared by Oshoffa's trusted cook. After eating, Pastor
Oshoffa passed round a bowl of water for us to drink. He served the
others and came to me and said it all ends with you. Thou art the
Joseph."
Pastor Owodunni cited several instances to buttress his claim to the
leadership of the church, saying Pastor Oshoffa invited him once to
his bedroom and told him (Owodunni) not to prostrate before him
again. He also mentioned several instances when late Pastor Oshoffa
declared him as the anointed choice of the leadership of the church.
According to him, the Pastor /Founder had already declared him as his
successor before he died.
"The founder of the church, Samuel Bilewu Oshoffa had declared me his
successor before he died in accordance with Section III, Page 37 of
the church constitution," he stated.
Pastor Owodunni revealed that the Pastor/Founder certainly did not
appoint Pastor Abiodun Bada to head the CCC after his demise.
He accused some members of the Board of Trustees of distorting the
constitution of the church by appointing Bada. He said what was
contained in the CCC Constitution as regards the election of a
successor to the founder was that the holy spirit will appoint the
person who will succeed him (Oshoffa). He explained that Pastor
Oshoffa made it clear during his lifetime that the person to be
appointed may not be the person next to him or closest to him, but
that at the appropriate time, the holy spirit will guide him
(Oshoffa) to pick the successor.
Pastor Owodunni said this has been done by his appointment as
successor to Oshoffa.
He quoted Oshoffa as telling the church members in Imeko: "Look at
me, I am giving you a message." He said Pastor Oshoffa was leaning on
him in front of the congregation when he made this declaration.
"If they are spiritual, they will know that he has appointed well, he
said. Pastor Owodunni said he was now assuming the headship of the
church, adding: I have been the Pastor of the church since 1985."
He further said that the Supreme Court judgement has rendered Pastor
Bada as an impostor to the leadership of the church, saying that all
appointments made by Bada have therefore been rendered null and void.
He said he will reverse everything done by Bada which are against the
church constitution, but that he will retain those that are
constitutional.
Pastor Owodunni said his parish at Ijeshatedo will be the new
headquarters of the CCC.
"This place is going to be the new headquarters of the church.
Anywhere the king resides is known as the palace. Whenever the king
moves, the palace moves," he declared.

Original_Search_Title: Poverty Persists In Accra Ghettoes Dispite Politicians' Promises
Headline: Ghana: Sahara: The Architecture Of Poverty
Body_Text:
Accra — A wooden, improvised bridge links the Sahara settlement with
the rest of Accra. The area itself is tucked between the industrial
area and Adabraka. This is home to many families to whom politicians
often go to seek support to execute their campaign strategies. It is
not unusual for some of the promises to take the form of the usual
ones like: 'We will make things better for you when we come to power
or when you return us to power.'
Residents in this part of the city could easily pass for Frantz
Fanon's ' Wretched of the Earth'. The architecture here is the type
one finds at places where people, for fear of the recurrence of an
earthquake, do not want to risk relying on the bricks for
construction. It also represents the authority's carelessness for
some of our residents. Sahara offers an ideal skulking place for many
social outcasts -prostitutes, robbers and so on.
A large open space serves the needs of kids who live here, and there
are many of them. To them the main entertainment is football and the
open space offers such a facility, baring a downpour. Sahara is the
part of Accra where the rainy season is one period that sends fear
down the spine of residents.
Sikiru is one of the residents in Sahara who migrated to the place
from Tudu. He has a herd of cattle, which graze from nearby pastures.
According to him, when the rainy season comes, residents are on their
toes at night.
Many women eke out a living selling meals to the residents which
range from beans and gari to rice and roasted plantain and
groundnuts. It is surprising how fitting shops are common to slums of
the city. Maybe it is as a result of the ease with which land can be
acquired for such purposes at these places. There are a number of
workshops dotted in various parts of Sahara. Landlords who charge the
tenants for the use of the facility own the wooden structures that
serve as dwelling places.
The Odaw River which meanders its way towards the Atlantic Ocean
passes through the side of Sahara close to the walls of the
industrial area, serving as a border between the two. But for
pollutants, which make the colour of the stream murky, that part of
the area could present a beautiful spectacle for seekers of nature.
Spent oil, floating human fecal matter, polythene sachets all spoil
the beauty of the river.
The place has one public toilet, which a businessman, as part of the
AMA's privatizing of such facilities, has put up for the use of the
residents. This is not within the reach of everyone. Maybe the coins
needed to pay for passage into the toilet could buy one or two more
necessities. After all, the banks of the Odaw are there for free.
Residents in places like Sahara have the tendency to disregard the
advantages of family planning and the number of kids here attests to
this. Education? Quality medical care? To them not much can be done
about these. 'Fama Nyame' or give it to God, they seem to say.

Original_Search_Title: Hungry Uganda Army Completes Pull-Out from Kisangani
Headline: East Africa: Kisangani Pull-Out Completed
Body_Text:
Banalia — Ugandan soldiers in Kisangani on Monday beat the 100km-
mark set by the UN Security Council demanding the withdrawal of
Rwandan and Ugandan troops from the war-torn city.
The UPDF's 65th battalion camped at Bubedi in Bafabwooli, 107km north
of Kisangani, a week ago. The last Ugandan soldier in Kisangani
arrived at Banalia, 128kms north of the war-torn city, on midday on
Monday.
"This is the last unit of the 9th battalion. There is now no Ugandan
soldier in Kisangani. Thank God, we are out of there," said Col. Sula
Semakula, the UPDF commander in the Kisangani sector.
Maj. Paul Lokech, the Ugandan officer in the Joint Command Centre,
the body comprising two Rwandan soldiers and the UN observer mission
in Congo (MONUC), said, "With the bad roads and break-down of our
equipment, we were worried that we would not make it."
A Romanian officer, Col. Leonida Grecu, the MONUC officer overseeing
the withdrawal, said he was happy with the response by the Ugandans
who withdrew along with troops of Jean Pierre Bemba, leader of the
Congolese Liberation Movement (MLC).
"The roads are bad but the Ugandans are now far from the 100km mark.
They are now crossing the river to Buta. This is a victory for the UN
mission here." He said Rwandan soldiers had also withdrawn from
Kisangani but "a few were still at 75km on Kisangani-Lubutu road.
They are moving with another MONUC team following them."
He, however, blamed the Rwandan backed RCD-Goma rebels who are still
refusing to leave the city. There are reportedly two brigades of the
Goma rebels in the city.
"They have assured us that they will leave the city but they are not
moving," said Grecu, flanked by officers from Russia and Libya.
Ugandan soldiers from the 3rd, 5th, 9th, 67th and 75th battalions who
fought in Kisangani II battle (May 5 and 8) and Kisangani III (June
5-10) battles are pulling out of Congo.
At Banalia, the soldiers cross River Aruwimi to walk the 222km to
Buta where they are expected to be flown to Entebbe. Wounded soldiers
and those with malaria are a sorry sight.
Fatigued, hungry and angry, the soldiers longing to return home after
over two years of the Congo mission, are faced with a bigger hurdle
at Banalia. The 35-tonne ferry that plies the 200-metre breadth of
River Aruwimi, got stuck two months ago on a rock in the river.
Eight long canoes joined by timber and powered by two boat engines
are the UPDF invention to transport across about 4,000 soldiers,
trucks and over a hundred tonnes of arms.
The army on Monday pulled out the ferry and repaired it. A Russian
made T55 tank weighs about 36 tonnes. Fuel is also in short supply.
The officers are worried of how they will transport the over 100
tonnes of equipment. Most trucks are in terrible mechanical
condition.
On the dirty, potholed and bushy road to Buta, the soldiers march in
stunned exhaustion. Lack of food and non-payment of salary were the
main complaints.
"It is now six months since I last received my salary. We rely on
begging to survive," said a soldier.
"I am happy to be heading home. I have not tasted a meal of matooke
(banana plantains) and cow meat for over two years," said another
soldier at Kole, 90km from Buta.
The soldiers are bitter with the memory of the six days battle at
Tchopo bridge which they term as "the battle of shame."
"For six days and nights, we fought. We drunk dirty water out of the
river.
That was the battle of my life. Cooked cassava reached us at the
frontline after three days. Hunger was killing us. I just want to see
my wife and mother," said a soldier.
A small portion of the soldiers on the weekend reached Buta. They are
accompanied by hundreds of Congolese girls many of them pregnant or
carrying babies. These are wives of the Ugandan soldiers.
"No body will stop me from returning home with my wife. This is the
only woman I have ever loved. I will board the plane but, somehow,
she will reach me," a soldier said.

Original_Search_Title: I Have No Regrets About Executions - Rawlings
Headline: Ghana: I Have No Regrets About Executions - Rawlings
Body_Text:
Accra — Twenty-one years ago today, on June 26, 1979, six senior army
officers, including two former military Heads of State, were executed in public
by firing squad at the Teshie Shooting Range in Accra. 10 days earlier, on June
16, 1979, another military Head of State and a senior military officer were also
executed at the Teshie Military range.
Those executed on June 26 were General Fred William Kwasi Akuffo and Lt-
General Okatakyire Kwasi Amankwa Afrifa (the two Heads of State); Rear Admiral
Joy Amedume (former Navy Commander); Air Vice-Marshall George Yaw Boakye (former
Air Force Commander); Major-General Robert Ebenezer Abosey Kotei (former Chief
of Defence Staff) and Col. Roger Felli (former Commissioner for Foreign
Affairs).
A former Head of State, Gen. (later Mr.) Ignatius Kutu Acheampong and the
former Border Guard Commander, Major-General Edward Kwaku Utuka, were executed
on June 16.
The Daily Graphic variously reported that the executed officers were tried
and found guilty by the Armed Forces Revolutionary Council (AFRC) Special
Courts, on charges of using their positions to amass wealth to the detriment of
the nation.
Their offences included the acquisition or obtaining of bank loans,
properties, material promises, favours or advantages whatsoever by abuses or
exploits by virtue of their official position in the public service. Illegal or
dishonest acquisition of property and intentional or reckless misapplication of
cause or damage to public property were also sighted. Additionally, they were
found guilty of gross negligence or dishonestly applying public property and
intentional or reckless dissipation of public property. All these offences were
specified in Section Three of the AFRC Special Courts Decree.
We reproduce below, the published assets of Air Vice-Marshal Boakye, for
which he was found guilty and executed.
"HOUSES:
1. No. 55 East Cantonments (Â¢250,400) built through Armed Forces Housing Loan
Scheme, 1973.
2. No. 6 North Labone (uncompleted) being held in trust for daughter, Barbara
Boakye. Mortgaged. Loan from SSB (Â¢85,000).
3. Housing at Aduman, (uncompleted). Mortgage Loan from NSCB. Estimated Cost,
Â¢50,000.
4. Plot No. 3B, Third Circular Road. Loan approved by GCB but contractor had
not commenced work yet.
CARS: 1. Mercedes Benz car paid for from proceeds from sale of cars GZA 1512
and GS 5598.
1 VW Bus (2nd Hand) bought from Armed Forces.
1 Benz Bus (2nd Hand) bought in 1977. Vehicles still under repairs.
BANK ACCOUNTS:
SAVINGS ACCOUNT:
G.C.B. About CURRENT: Â¢3,500
(1) SSB - About 1,300
(2) GCB B/Camp 4,000
(3) NSCB IN RED
INSURANCE:
(a) Life Policy - Crusader Ins. Â¢4,000 due to mature in December 1979.
(b) SIC - Â¢2,000 - Due to mature in December 1979.
FARM:
15 acre plantain farm at Apinamang, near Akwatia, started in1975."
One of the unresolved issues concerning the 1979 executions were the
rationales, and the basis behind those selected. During the celebration of the
17th anniversary of the 31st December Revolution on in 1998, President Jerry
Rawlings, (who as Flt-Lt. Rawlings was the head of the AFRC) made a comment
about the 1979 executions.
He said even though some senior officers were executed, "I have no regrets
about it. We had to take responsibility for it. The point is that the bigger
criminals were around and are still around. You can go and quote me."
In his book, Ghana: Peace and Stability, Mr. Kofi Quantson, a senior member
of the intelligence community quoted Rawlings as explaining why there had to be
those executions, "I know why you are here. I had no choice. Either that or you
and I would not be talking this morning. All of us, plus other innocent people
would have been dead by now."
In his June 3, 2000 address, marking the 21st anniversary of the June 4
Uprising, President Rawlings threatened that, "if there should be another
upheaval - and God forbid - the civilian front will pay a heavy price" because
the soldiers paid a price with their lives in both the June 4, 1979 and the
December 31, 1981 upheavals. The irony of the President's threat was that he was
the leader of both upheavals. The question one might want to ask him is, "what
have you done in the over 18 years you have been in power to ensure that the
injustices which led to the upheavals do not exist?"
The basis for the selection of the officers to be executed has never been
clear-cut. The National Organiser of the National Democratic Congress (NDC)
stated last year on the GTV programme, Public Concern that the execution of Gen.
Afrifa was unfortunate. He said he found it extremely difficult to rationalize
Afrifa's execution since he had long retired from the Ghana Armed Forces when
the Uprising took place.
The NDC Organiser openly expressed his misgivings about the fact that, "the
inclusion of Gen. Afrifa in the execution up till today baffles my mind. That
was one of the most unfortunate events."
The former spokesman and number two man of the AFRC, Major (rtd) Kojo Boakye
Djan, has on a number of occasions, explained the basis of the executions.
We reproduce below, excerpts of a response the retired Major sent to the
media last year, in response to the President's assertion that he had no regrets
about the executions.
"I have read with dismay the reported remarks by Rawlings, the then Chairman
of the AFRC that he has no regrets for the executions during the 1979 June 4
armed uprising in Ghana. For the past years, I have had numerous occasions to
talk and write about the AFRC affairs and in particular about those executions.
On behalf of the AFRC members, functionaries and particularly those voiceless
participants who are still serving in the Ghana Armed Forces, I have
consistently reported that those executions were regrettable but unavoidable,
given the circumstances in which we had to operate and both the moral and legal
principles and procedures for the executions that we had to use.
For the avoidance of any doubts those officers were executed for their
ultimate responsibilities for illegal acts (high treason) of removing from
office constitutionally elected civilian governments in 1966 of Kwame Nkrumah
and in 1972 of Kofi Busia; sustaining successor military regimes in office which
were unaccountable to Ghanaians in their corrupt and/or murderous conduct of
national affairs; and finally allowing themselves to personally profit from
those regimes or allowing their agents and cronies to do so.
It is instructive to note that today these crimes are enforceable under
international and most national laws. The current constitution has entrenched
it. In South Korea, the Generals who found themselves in a similar position to
those we executed in Ghana have recently been sentenced to death. The British
House Of Lords has offered a similar ruling against General Pinochet of Chile
for overthrowing the elected government of Allende.
Rawlings and for that matter informed Ghanaians cannot claim to be unaware of
our legal and moral approach to these executions which have both local and
international force of law and irreversible implications and lessons for civil
plural democratic rule in Ghana. There was nothing personal about the
executions. We all shared then as we must now share both the collective
responsibility and deep regret for the loss of those lives, however necessary
and unavoidable they were.
We find it so very curious that after turning his back on the principles of
June 4 and launching his 31st December 1981 military takeover of the civilian
administration of Limann almost seventeen years ago, Rawlings even had the need
and the courage to clothe himself in the glory and protection that he hopes only
June 4 can give him. For the principles behind the June 4 Uprising and the 31st
December 1981 coup are mutually distinct and exclusive."
A book, When The Rules by Col. Kofi Jackson (rtd), has given a few insights
into military rule between 1975 and 1979. The 249-page book revealed where the
graves of the eight executed senior military officers are located.
Col. Jackson stated that, "by 10am (on June 16, 1979), we heard the shocking
news that General Acheampong and General Utuka had been executed by firing squad
that morning at the Teshie firing range. By lunch time, we heard that the bodies
of the two Generals had been brought to the Nsawam Prison Condemned Block for
burial at the Adoagyiri Cemetery."
Regarding the second batch of officers executed on June 26, he said, "the
bodies of the five Generals and the Colonel were brought to the Nsawam Prison
Condemned Block for burial. The inmate carpenters quickly cut and nailed wawa
boards into make-shift coffins for the bodies. ...
A burial party made up of convicted prisoners under the watchful eyes of
revolutionary soldiers buried the Generals and the Colonel at the Nsawam Prisons
Cemetery at Adoagyiri. The simple burial was in sharp contrast to the way the
Military bury their dead, especially Generals. In normal times, during the final
march into the cemetery, the captivating sound of drums dominate the air. At the
graveside, soldiers fire volleys of farewell shots across the grave and the
buglers dutifully deliver the 'last post' in sombre notes. It is a whole solemn
ritual treasured by all soldiers."

Original_Search_Title: Hawkers Cleared Off First Light
Headline: Ghana: Hawkers Cleared Off First Light
Body_Text:
Accra — It is a common sight to see hawkers and itinerant vendors at
various traffic intersections in the national capital. These people
sell all sorts of wares from handkerchiefs to plantain chips and
sugarcane. A good number of them are children of school-going age, who
should be seeking academic knowledge in the classroom.
The dangers these people expose themselves to in a city where
drivers run the streets as if the devil is after them is very
frightening, but the economic situation lends itself to the survival of
the fittest. The most notorious of these traffic intersections is the
one popularly called First Light, just after the Kaneshie market
towards the direction of Odorkor.
About two years ago, a tipper truck ran into the crowd of vendors
and other motorists, killing five people in the process. No sooner had
the debris of mangled bodies been cleared than they were back to ply
their trade.
Among the honest ones who genuinely engage in economic activities
are the rogues and thieves who make life extremely uncomfortable for
motorists. Their modus operandi is to wait until the red light shows
for vehicles to come to a stop. After a couple of seconds, they close
in on the vehicles ostensibly to offer one item or the other for sale.
Just as the light shows green for the vehicles to move, these boys
snatch wristwatches from passengers who dare put their arms on the
windows of the cars. Some go to the extent of even snatching mobile
phones from motorists.
As a result of complaints from victims of these rogues, the Kaneshie
police have cleared the First Light area of street vendors. According
to the commander of the Kaneshie Division of the Ghana Police Service,
Superintendent E. M. Amamoo, they had to take the action to make the
area safe for motorists. Supt. Amamoo told The Accra Mail that the
First Light area has become a den for miscreants who take advantage of
motorists and passengers who should have no reason to be afraid of
driving in the city.
Mr. Amamoo told The Accra Mail that his outfit has identified areas
within its jurisdiction where such nefarious activities take place and
has waged a relentless war on such deviant behaviour. He expressed the
hope that all other traffic joints will be cleared of rogues.

Original_Search_Title: The Day Accra Stood Still
Headline: Ghana: The Day Accra Stood Still
Body_Text:
Accra — History has repeated itself. Five years after the members of the
Ghana Union Traders Association (GUTA) shut their shops in protest against the
introduction of the Value Added Tax (VAT), they have again stared down the
government and last Monday called a one week protest, this time against the
rapid depreciation of the cedi and crippling tax regimes.
The Public Relations officer of GUTA, Paa Kofi Ansong said until government
stops the free fall of the cedi against major foreign currencies and halts its
excessive taxation policy, the traders would not also budge in their stance.
GUTA argues that since the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) and the Customs,
Excise and Preventive Service (CEPS), have always exceeded their targets there
is no justification for tax increases and the introduction of new ones which
will invariably affect the purchasing power of the consumer.
GUTA is of the view that with the introduction of the VAT certain special
taxes would have been scrapped, instead the government has gone ahead with an
increase in the rate of VAT which is going to raise price levels and hamper
their businesses.
GUTA is an association of importers, exporters, wholesalers and retailers
who are responsible for importing almost all industrial and consumable items in
the country as well as distributing various items produced in the country.
Their strike action therefore is potentially very hazardous to the economy and
could bring the country to its knees if no meeting ground is found between it
and the government.
GUTA's misfortune is the unstable prices that have hit the market since the
government liberalised the exchange market. For instance, in December last
year, a bag of cement was sold for Â¢12,500. It shot to Â¢13, 500 in March this
year and has hit an all time high of Â¢20,500 at the time of filing this report.
At press time last Monday the dollar was selling between Â¢4800 and Â¢5000 and it
is unlikely the government will intervene to arrest the situation. GUTA feels
the depreciation of the cedi is not the result of excessive importation as held
by the Minister of Trade, Dan Abodakpi.
When the protest took effect last Monday, during which all shops in the
business centre of Accra were shut, many people, though not taken off guard,
were nevertheless "pleasantly" surprised because they had initially waved the
GUTA action off as another bark without bite.
But the reality of a major industrial strife stared many people; including
government officials in the face when business ground to a halt on Monday. The
protest was initially scheduled to last one month, but was scaled down to one
week by GUTA leadership in order to minimise its impact on the economy. They
will however go the whole distance if at the end of the one week the government
does not heed their demands.
Ansong has dismissed claims by the government that the opposition were
behind the GUTA action. "Nobody should see our action as anti government. We
are matured enough to make rational decisions. We are not happy about
increasing prices every day. We are taking this action because we pity workers.
The fight is a consumer fight and not a political one", he explained to The
Accra Mail.
Ansong in a reaction to a threat by the Greater Accra Regional Police
Commander that the police would not spare traders who disturbed the peace of
the city said rather than threatening them the police should guard their stores
for the duration of the protest. "It is the hope of GUTA and in the interest of
the government, the business community, and the international community to
ensure that this protest will not degenerate into something nasty", he
cautioned the peace officers.
It was discovered during our rounds that GUTA's action was not a fluke. It
had started taking its toll on the economy as many people who journeyed from
Eastern, Central and Volta Regions to purchase various items returned home
disappointed. The loss to the economy cannot be immediately quantified, but it
is obvious that it would hurt painfully if it continues to bite into the ailing
economy. Business at the city centre was generally very dull. But GUTA insists
that even though they are worse affected because of loss of revenue, they will
prefer shutting their shops to doing business in an unpredictable economic
atmosphere.
At Okaishie where pedestrians and vehicles jostle for the right of way
especially the Granville Avenue portion, the area looked desolate with only the
activities of small time traders who sell cooked food like rice, waakye, kenkey
and fried plantains and groundnuts. There were also a few others who sold
toothpaste in wheelbarrows. The kayayee (women porters) were not spared the
effect of the strike action. One of them who mentioned her name as Hawa said
she had not carried anything since morning when we met her at about 1p.m as she
wandered through Okaishie in search of non-existent wares to carry.
Another woman who sells iced water complained that because of the action she
was unable to make good sales. Some of them could be seen sleeping in front of
the shut shops due to inactivity. One Taawa who sells on a table said although
she does not own a shop she and her colleagues were supportive of the action as
there is the need to inform the authorities that all is not well.
A woman who sells at Tudu said she went to the market in vain as she could
not buy what she wanted and besides times are hard. She asked: ' Do they want
us to die?' The place called 31st December Market was also affected as it was
deserted. One political activist who resides at Tudu told us that the action is
in order as things are just too hard.
The GUTA told The Accra Mail that the regional offices of the union are
asking to be allowed to join in the action but the executives want it limited
to Accra so that workers and the generality of Ghanaians did not suffer unduly.
GUTA is one of the few existing pressure groups left in the country which can
bite. In 1995 GUTA played a leading role in compelling government to withdraw
the VAT.
Whether this report is overtaken by events or not, GUTA would have shown the
spirit of 1948 when Nii Kwabena Bonney organized the mass boycott of British
goods in protest against foreign goods and fall in cocoa prices.

Original_Search_Title: 'Ghanaians Are Not Helpless Victims' - Delle
Headline: Ghana: 'Ghanaians Are Not Helpless Victims' - Delle
Body_Text:
Accra — The Director of Rabito Clinic and renowned dermatologist,
Dr. Edmund Delle, has said the greatest damage the NDC government has
done to Ghana is making the people believe that they cannot do anything
for themselves with out foreign assistance.
He said most Ghanaians perceive themselves as hapless, forlorn
people who must always depend on foreign aid and grants for survival.
Delle said Ghanaians have been made to lose so much confidence in
themselves that they have to depend on Malaysians, who come here and
spit on themGhanaians.
"We are not helpless victims" Dr. Delle declared.
Delle, a chairmanship aspirant of the Convention People's Party
(CPP) made these observations when he interacted with CPP members in
the Kwahu South district at Nkawkaw recently.
Dr. Delle said Osagyefo Dr. Kwame Nkrumah was able to achieve so
much for Ghana within the short span of nine years mainly because of
his unwavering confidence in the ability of the black man to be master
of his own destiny.
He said it is the absence of the kind of leadership which can
inspire the people to aspire towards greater heights which has plunged
our nation into the crisis facing us now.
"The vision of our founding fathers was that of a nation of proud
and dignified people who would work hard and prove to the world that
the black man is somebody", Delle said.
He said the current economic crisis facing the nation stems from the
fact that we have to depend on foreigners for aid and grants and
consumer foods like groundnuts, rice, poultry products and even
plantains.
The Nandom-born dermatologist assured Ghanaians that the CPP when
voted into power under his leadership will re-introduce the work and
happiness programme which saw the people of Ghana putting in much
energy to build and develop their own country.
He said the re-emergence of the CPP at his crucial time of national
crisis is a good omen, for the party is the only one that can provide
the leadership that will inspire the people to work hard and do things
for themselves.
"The industries that will provide jobs for the youths have all been
sold out or collapsed, and the youth have to depend on gambling for
survival", Delle lamented, adding that until the nation paid serious
attention to the agricultural sector and established agro-based
industries to process the farmers' produce, we cannot attain the goals
envisioned in the Vision 2020 document.
He urged party activists and supporters in the Kwahu District to be
evangelists and spread the CPP message to the people.
'You are all centres of organisation", he told them adding that soon
after congress his administration will mobilise massive resources to
assist them in their campaigning.
The Vice-chairman of the Nkawkaw constituency branch of the CPP,
Alhaji Haruna said the party has already started a massive campaign to
win fresh supporters and attract all those who left to join other
parties to come back home.
Alhaji Haruna said the re-gaining of the CPP name has brought hope
to many Nkrumahists, adding that manyof those who defected to other
parties have started coming back to their real home.

Original_Search_Title: Land Of Our Death, Heroes And Villains
Headline: Ghana: Land Of Our Death, Heroes And Villains
Body_Text:
Accra — In one of my pieces last year I established the fact that
most military adventurers in our sub-region who want to make dawn
broadcast after strike from mid-May towards June or July. And this is
for very good reasons.
The month of May begins with hungry (and therefore angry) season. By
May the planting season is over, and food becomes scarce and expensive
on the markets. Housewives daily pull the ties of their husbands, who,
in turn, blame the occupants of the Castles for making life difficult
for the descendants of good old Kwaku Adam.
Do we now understand why May 1 was chosen as the day on which
workers must carry placards and show how hungry and angry they are?
'Why are you home so early?' a father would ask his schoolboy son.
'School fees Â our Madam says Â'
'Is she crazy? Go and tell her I'll pay, but not today.'
Therefore, if you are in a position of authority and you issue
orders by heart around the month of May, the result could be big wahala
for you.
If a Regional Security Council remotely domiciled in Cape wants to
stop Winneba Aboakyer they must know they are in for disobedience. And
a police commissioner who thinks he can successfully order his
subordinate to either seize a deer from the sweat-soaked shoulders of
asafo machomen or arrest a possessed fetish priestess lies in this
world but does not know the world.
And as the prophets of old spake standing in the holy places, around
May the hunger and anger of many shall increase. The sizes of plantain
chips sachets will shrink along the Adenta-Legon road. Wives will hold
the ties or necks of husbands, while children will rebell at home.
But this would be just the beginning of the tribulations. Consumers
will refuse to pay their utility bills, and the Electricity Company
will have to lure debtors with chacha to pay their bills.
And ye shall hear of strikes and rumours of strikes, especially on
the aluta-prone campuses of our universities.
Many keen observers were, therefore not surprised when members of
the University Teachers Association of Ghana (UTAG) decided to do 'we
no go sit down' on May 15, the same day on which twenty-one years ago,
a hungry and angry airman rebelled against the establishment.
The announcement of the intention of the lecturers to go on strike
elicited a funny reaction from the umbrella organisation of schoolboys
and girls - the National Union of Ghana Students (NUGS).
They told their lecturers: please don't go on strike to back your
demand for better pay, for that will further disrupt the academic
calendar, which suffered much jeopardy when we went on a long strike to
protest against the increase in academic facility user fees.
Funny, isn't it? But that is life for you. When people at the tree
tops chop and become bellyfull and use our scarce foreign cash to buy
or lease or buy and lease presidential jets and workers demand better
pay, they turn round to tell them: any increase in pay will further
worsen our foreign exchange problems. Therefore, continue to suffer in
the interest of the nation. And may God be with you.
On May 15, which was last Monday, I was travelling early morning
from Nkawkaw to Accra. When I stopped at a news vendor's to buy the
papers, there was katakata around the place. The angry season is here,
I said to myself.
'I won't buy it koraa, give me my money. Whaaat Â this man our hero?
That was a five-star brokeman protesting violently.
'But you've bought and paid for it,?
'Yes, but I also say I can't buy it with this nonsense.'
I became curious and preened my neck to see what was the bone of
contention.
And I saw the Son of Man, Efo Kokuvi, sitting in his majesty and
glory on the front page of a Graphic Supplement with the accompanying
captions: "President Rawlings, Hero of our time." It was this that had
sparked off the brokeman's anger and subsequent protests.
The protest of the Nkawkaw man against the coronation of the Son of
Man as the hero of our time should not be seen as an isolated incident:
he was a representative figure protesting on behalf of millions of
Ghanaians home and abroad.
One of the reasons given by the Joe Bradford Nyina, the august
author of the write-up, for the proclamation of the Son of Man as the
hero of our time was that he had launched two (?) revolutions.
Yes, the Son of Man has the singular 'honour' of scoring a hat-trick
in pulling the gun to overthrow governments. But that in itself does
not mean much to the people. What matters is the effect of his
'revolution' on the people.
Comparing the state of the nation and Ghanaians at the time the Son
of Man launched his so-called revolutions and what we are going through
now, many are those Ghanaians who would wish he had never appeared on
the national political landscape. The result of his coming is now very
clear, and at the twilight of his two decades of trial and error
governance, which saw the formulation of policies based more on
instinct than on a pragmatic appraisal of our peculiar circumstances,
our dear nation ghana is teetering at the brink of economic collapse,
if it hasn't collapsed already. And they say he is the hero of our
time?
When the Son of Man overthrew the PNP government of Dr. Hilla
Limann, he said that was the most disgraceful government of our time.
but after subjecting his ministers and top officials to strict
inquisition, he could not prefer any criminal charges against any of
them.
Under the administration of the Son of Man, however, we have
ministers and top government officials being cited for hyper-
corruption, rape, and even murder. And they say he is the hero of our
time?
We know who heroes are. Moreover, we are not novices in the forest
for the Joe Bradford Nyinas of our time to present asokwaa to us as the
akyenkyena bird.

Original_Search_Title: Curbing Our Reliance On Imported Goods (Editorial)
Headline: Ghana: Curbing Our Reliance On Imported Goods (Editorial)
Body_Text:
Accra — One OF the reasons that have been cited for our present
economic predicament is our over-reliance on imported goods. One does
not have to look far for indicators of our insatiable appetite for
imported consumer goods: the shelves in our shops and supermarkets are
virtually filled with imported consumables.
As Dr. P. Kwesi Nduom pointed out in his piece on Ghana's economic
crisis in the Daily Graphic, "we now import everything, from
groundnuts, chicken, pork, beef, rice, corn, plantain, used appliances
of all types with the little foreign currency we earn from our
exporters."
Aside of that, the majority of the few industries that are still in
production depend almost entirely on imported raw materials.
If our cedi is daily depreciating against currencies like the dollar
and the pound sterling, this is one of the reasons.
The result of our current economic crisis, especially the rapid
depreciation of the cedi and the scarcity of foreign exchange which
have necessitated the regression into the rationing method we abandoned
long ago, has been the realisation that we need to depend less on
imported items and as much as possible aim at attaining national self-
reliance in several sectors of the economy.
It is against this background that the GHANAIAN CHRONICLE deems as
pertinent and timely the call by the Northern Seed Growers Association
(NOSGA) on the government to drastically reduce the importation of rice
into the country and instead encourage and support local production.
Ghanaians would recall that in the mid-seventies when the government
of late General Acheampong launched the Operation Feed Yourself and
Operation Feed Your Industries programmes the nation attained near-
sufficiency in food production and even managed to export large
quantities of maize.
The irrigation schemes in Dawhenya and Afife and some parts of the
north have been producing large quantities of local rice.
But the efforts of these hard-working farmers have been frustrated
every year by the importation of foreign rice into the country.
The nation's rice farmers, like the producers of other crops, depend
on bank loans to purchase seeds and other inputs or hire labour and
diverse equipment.
Unfortunately, their produce remain piled up in warehouses because
the markets are choked by imported rice. The result is that most of
them have been plunged into debt, thus reducing their capacity to
increase production.
As the association rightly said, the nation has the capacity to
produce its entire need of rice, and it would be advisable to encourage
our rice farmers to do so by limiting rice imports into the country.
As has been discovered lately, the concept of globalisation has been
found to be detrimental in many respects to especially developing
countries.
Though it is a desirable concept, we need to embrace it selectively
and with circumspection.

Original_Search_Title: Pipeline Vandalism: Feasting on Forbidden Spots
Headline: Nigeria: Pipeline Vandalism: Feasting on Forbidden Spots
Body_Text:
Lagos — Determined to safeguard oil pipelines, the Federal Government is
intensifying security surveilance in the Niger Delta. But the vandals are also
finetuning their tactics, reports Vincent Nwanma who visited Abia State
recently.
The sign post, "Warning: Petroleum Pipeline," is conspicuous enough to deter
any would-be adventurer. That is, in ordinary times and in normal
circumstances. But in today's Nigeria, the times are not just different, people
also think and act differently and things previously thought unlikely have
become everyday events.
Which explains why the warning above failed to deter both nocturnal and
daytime adventurers, at Ibeku, a village some 10 km north of Aba, in Abia
state. This spot, which marks the intersection between the major road in the
village and Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation's oil pipeline, is one of
the latest sites of pipeline vandalism.
And the attraction grew, so much that "everyone became involved. There is no
family (in Ibeku village) that was not involved," Chinwe, a 32-year old man,
told this writer at the site recently.
He blamed the rising incidence of vandalisation on poverty. Government's
estimates say that about 70 percent of Nigeria's 120 million people live below
the poverty level. Vandalising pipelines is now a way of poverty alleviation!
"If things can improve so everyone can earn a decent living, then those who
engage in this vandalisation will stop," said Chinwe, who sells footwear.
He saw the pipeline through the eyes of a child, while growing up in this
village, at the time the pipes were being laid. At that time, he thought that
these big pipes could not break by themselves. "It is human beings that cause
the breakage."
The times have changed, and vandalisation of oil pipelines has become such a
lucrative venture here and other villages around Aba, the Enyimba city, that
there is yet no imminent sign of an end to it.
And it has changed the scene of petroleum products marketing in this part of
the country, as many young men have become "independent" marketers, eagerly
beckoning motorists to buy petrol or diesel from them, in some cases just a few
meters away from fuel stations.
At first, says Chinwe, residents of Ibeku were reluctant to cash in on the
golden opportunity to supplement their meagre earnings. "We who are close to
the site were not getting it; rather it was people from afar off," he said.
The reason for their initial reluctance, he said, was incessant police
harassment. "They (the police) move from house to house. If they see the
products in your place, they seize them, and also take money from you," he
said.
That attitude soon changed, and the residents now convey the products-
including petrol-in jerry cans, on motorcycles, bicycles, etc.
And for fear of fire explosion at home, they keep the products in nearby
bushes, under plantain and banana trees. Indeed, every available space behind
the house is converted into a strategic storage place.
"You know you don't store fuel at home. Anyone who stores it at home is
seeking for trouble," says Chinwe. The best place to keep it, he said, is in
the bush, "or you bury it underground. It's only kerosene you can keep in the
house."
But Chief Joseph Chikezie Egbufor, of Amasator Autonomous Community, says
what happened at Amuzu, his village, that borders Ibeku, on March 3, 2000,
