cannot be described as vandalisation.
On that day, he said, his subjects reported to him that there was air
leakage somewhere on the pipeline. Upon the receipt of this complaint, he
reported the case at NNPC ( at its depot, in Osisioma, near Aba). And when
officials from NNPC inspected the site, they gave the incident a term called
"spillage," according to him.
"As they explained," he said, "the English word, spillage, means something
that burst by itself."
He's not happy with the way NNPC officials repaired the leakage. Instead of
welding the pipe to close the hole, he said they merely covered it (the hole)
with a carved stick, "rather like plugging a punctured calabash."
The Nigerian government has approved the setting up of a Special Squad to
monitor the country's pipeline network of more than 5,000 km, but the frequency
with which the pipelines are being broken and products intercepted, bears no
correlation with the activity of this squad, yet.
At the time this writer visited the site Tuesday, May 2, 2000, the hole
around the pipeline had just been refilled with earth.
But the debris of materials left by the scavengers, the polluted water pool
by the roadside and the scourged farmland, were some of the evidences of the
flow of petroleum products around the site.
"We just woke up from sleep one morning and saw petrol flowing from it,"
said Chinwe. It started about two months ago, and had been flowing up to
Sunday, two days prior to the visit, he explained.
He could not say who broke the pipes, but later explained that those who do
it-usually at night-"come with tankers."
Those who spoke to this writer gave the names of some independent
productsmarketers, who they say carry out the illegal acts, or sponsor the
vandals.
Sources here also say the vandals first pour acid on the pipes, which
corrode the pipes, thereby making holes from which they draw the products.
The same sources allege that the vandals carry out their nightly operations
under the protective cover of armed security men.
The last time they came to Ibeku, Chinwe said, "they were arrested, and
their tankers seized by the police, who also took them way."
That probably sent the signals to the residents. Chinwe later told this
writer that on sighting this writer's vehicle enter his father's compound, he
got ready to take to the bush, before he saw a familiar face that was sent to
contact him.
And, in Asa-Umungbede, a village in Osisioma Local Government Area, the site
of the pipeline vandalism there is now a quiet spot, after a fire outbreak
there last month claimed more than 50 lives.
The dead have since been buried. But the charred remains of motorcycles,
plastic containers, and palm trees by both sides of the bush track, tell the
story of the blaze that occurred there.
As in Ibeku, most people in Asa-Umungbede refuse to speak with a stranger on
the incident, for fear of being arrested by the police and other security
agencies who now swoop on the residents of these areas since the fire outbreak.
The vandalised pipeline supplies products to all the states in the eastern
part of the country, as well as the north-east and the central states, referred
to as the Middle Belt, from the Port Harcourt refinery.
And this year, the vandalisation of this pipeline has led to disruption in
the distribution of petroleum products, as the NNPC first suspended pumping
products through the pipeline.
Although NNPC says it resumed pumping products through the pipeline some
weeks ago, the scarcity caused by the problems persists here.
As a result, the selling price of fuel is now up by about 50 percent, going
at about 30 naira per litre, instead of the pump price of 20 naira.
NNPC says that such incidents rose to 497 in 1999, up from just 58 in 1998.
This year may set yet another record.
An official of the NNPC told Dow Jones Newswires recently that the Special
Squad was charged with the responsibility to monitor the country's pipeline
network "by land, air and sea."
The squad also has the power to shoot on sight people seen engaging in
illegal activities along the network system.
Now, many residents in Ibeku have products to sell-petrol, diesel and
kerosene. And they see visitors-especially those who come in vehicles-as
potential customers.
Some, says Chinwe, have made a lot of money from the products, enough, for
instance, "to buy a motorcycle." In a village such as this, the possession of a
motorcycle can easily elevate one above the claws of abject poverty.
Imagine how much it can generate in a day, if employed for commercial
transportation, the popular 'Okada'.
Others have not been so lucky, as they have been objects of constant raids
by the police.
"The police search from house to house. They seize whatever products they
see, and resell them," he alleged.
There is still a long way to go. President Olusegun Obasanjo has just
announced a new monthly minimum wage of 7,500 naira, for federal civil
servants, and 5,500 for state employees.
This is part of government's efforts to reduce poverty in a country with a
per capital income of about $300.

Original_Search_Title: Mariam on the move
Headline: Ghana: Mariam on the move
Body_Text:
Acrra — A weekly insight into the life, stresses and strains of Ghana's
ambassador to beauty - Miss Ghana '99
I have always worried a lot and frankly, I am good at it. I worry about a
snake biting me from the water closet and I worry about scientists
discovering that plantain is actually fattening but these days I worry about
the family - about its survival in a world that changes daily. I worry about
the other aspect of children for which I profess complete ignorance, e.g.
"who is I don't know?"
Ever since I remember, every family harbours a child by that name - "I
don't know".
Just you ask.
Who left the front door open? And it's the "I don't know".
Who left the soap to dissolve in the dish?
"I don't know."
Frankly, "I don't know" is driving me insane. He's put mud on the carpet
and broken a mug and...
Once I told my little nephew if he misbehaves (as I have been told he does
on the stroke of 12 midday) I would not give him a present I promised him.
The reply? "I don't care". Now, that can only mean one thing. I don't know
has a brother "I don't care".
Try to understand children and you are in big trouble. I have never
understood for example how a child can climb up the roof and rescue a cat and
yet cannot walk down the hallway without grabbing both walls with his grubby
hands for balance. Or how a child can chew gum that he found on the floor or
ash tray and put his mouth over the muddy garden hose, yet refuse to drink
from a glass that his brother or sister has just used. Just watch them. They
drink from where the handle is.
Do you also realise that "I don't know" and "I don't care" have a cousin
called "Why"? You must be very familiar with that one, the least said about
him or is it her, the better. I worry about understanding children, but do
children really want our understanding? Sometimes I believe they want our
trust and blinding love.

Original_Search_Title: Tribute To A Father
Headline: Nigeria: Tribute To A Father
Body_Text:
Lagos — Bolanle will be getting married on 29April . Although she's
looking forward to the day, the only thing that would make her not to
be happy as she would have wished to be is the fact that she lost her
father in the month of April.
She is writing about her life experiences this month just to pay a
tribute to her late dad whom she describes as a unique man who made a
lot of good things happen to her.
She's so happy recounting her experience and is thanking God for a
life well spent thus far by her father whom she also described as a man
of the people.
Her Story:
Bolanle Johnson is my name. I'm from the cradle town of Ile-Ife in
Osun State. The month of April will mark the tenth year that my father
left this sinful world which is my fundamental reason for sending in my
own experience.
Well, I thank God for his life because since his departure life for
me has taken a new shape for both good and bad. The bad aspect of it is
that he left us the children behind. When a man dies, you either hear
the announcement on the television or radio.
It is a two-way thing: it is either they say "with gratitude to God
for a life well spent'' or ''with deep regret'', this I believe is
dependent on what the dead man left behind. If he leaves worthy things
behind for his children, they will be grateful to God and if otherwise
they announce his passage with regret. This is simply the truth. My
father was somebody I loved and admired so much and I never thought of
losing him until it happened because he was a man full of life and
energy. I could not remember a day he was hospitalised. He was a lovely
man to the core, a generous man, he took care of other people's needs
at his own expense. Talk of anything a man should possess in life, he
had them. He was very hardworking indeed. He worked tirelessly to make
sure he realised his goals but unfortunately he was unable to achieve
all his aims. But he was a father who never indulged his children at
the expense of other people's children. This does not mean that he
could not go to any length to please his children and pilot them in a
positive way. Daddy was a cute dresser and people often say that I took
after him which I believe is the truth. My mother can never forget him
for all he represented in her life and she held on to him till the very
end.
''Although you built a house, which you never completed and also
left your beloved Sade, Kunle, Kemi and Bola, we will never hesitate to
do our best to take after you in everything''.
The day dad died was the day we least expected that he would die
because he went to work that day and came back very early. He ate
normally and performed all his daily routine, he even cracked jokes
with us till it was time for us to have our dinner. It was mummy that
prepared the meal and when it was around 7p.m. I remember that we took
rice and plantain.
That evening, at about 9p.m. he excused us that he wanted to go and
sleep while my mother also left us (the children) in order to attend to
some other things in the house. I had an examination during that period
which I was reading for so I read late into the night with some of my
cousins that were staying with us. At about 1a.m. he came out of his
room and greeted us as we studied together and went back to his room.
About ten minutes later he came out of his room (coughing) and demanded
for hot water to drink which we couldn't readily get. We thought it was
all a joke. The cough was getting too serious and so I ran to wake my
mum up. She proceeded to give him hot water which was reserved in a hot
water flask. He took it but it did not change the situation as he was
later rushed to a nearby hospital. It was one of my cousins that drove
the car. I was there (in the car) as my mother sat behind him in the
car.
When we got to the hospital, he was attended to immediately, and
after about an hour we were told by the doctor to go home and return
later in the day. I went back home with mum with the expectation of
preparing something for Daddy to eat. At this time, some of my Dad's
friends had been informed, including his Pastor friend and they were
all with him during those trying times till about 7a.m. of the
following day.
In the morning I took his breakfast to him in the hospital but was
not allowed to see him because he was already in the theatre where the
doctors were battling to save his dear life. Again I was told to go
back home and that all was well. I went back home.
At exactly 9a.m. my Pastor (Obi) came around and demanded that we
should pray. As he prayed, the last thing he said was:''let's pray for
the soul of late Abiola Johnson." Not one of us could bear this as we
all wept profusely, mum, my brothers and sisters. We cried until we
were unable to cry any longer. We could not stomach the reality that
the man was dead.
Meanwhile, I discovered that while I was in the hospital, he had
died except that they refused to tell us. I could have run mad that day
because it was too much for me to bear. I tell you, this shattered my
dream. As for the examination I wanted to do, I could not read anything
any longer, although, I was encouraged to write the papers. But as soon
as I entered the examination hall all what I read disappeared. Here was
a man who opened a separate account for all his children. He kept a
good sum of money in the bank accounts, both for our education and
other things. He also kept N150,000 each for us in case any of us
wanted to get married in future. That man really planned ahead for us.
I cherish him not because he was okay but due to the fact that many
lessons could be learnt from him.
Many people had money but could not take care of their children but
his own case was different. He was loved by his workers because he did
his best to satisfy them. The only thing that would have pained us was
if he had died without Christ. He accepted the Lord Jesus Christ as his
Lord and personal saviour before he died of cardiac arrest.
''Daddy, sleep well, I remember all dinners we shared together,
those picnics, your sweet words of encouragement; in fact you will
remain evergreen in my memory. Dad, you cannot hurt a fly although you
died before your time. Thank God you are resting in the bosom of your
Lord. All the advice you used to give me I still keep to them till this
day. Mum talks about you almost everyday. Your pictures hung on the
wall of our home have been removed because we could not but cry each
day we looked at them.
Your little baby asks of you everyday, but we keep telling her you
travelled, although we do know that you travelled on a journey you can
never return from. We also keep praying for her to be able to bear the
pain on the day we will eventually tell her you are no more.
Meanwhile, we are planning to do that when she gets to JSS 3 . Thank
God for a life well spent. I love you Dad. We will meet one day to part
no more''.

Original_Search_Title: University At New Juabeng: Still On The Drawing Board?
Headline: Ghana: University At New Juabeng: Still On The Drawing Board?
Body_Text:
Accra — The hope of the New Juaben municipality getting a university
is now a far cry, as little has been done by the All Nations
International Development Agency (ANIDA), who promised the municipality
an All Nations University.
The Canada-based ANIDA last September announced its intention at
Koforidua amidst pomp and pageantry. That very day, seven young
seedlings were planted as the 'souls' of the university, and the ANIDA
president, Asokore-born Dr. Samuel Donkor, promised that some buildings
would be put up early for the first batch of students to be admitted in
September, this year. He made the promise at the sod-cutting ceremony
attended by Council of State member, Osabarima Awuah Kotoko, New
Juabeng Paramount Chief, Daasebre Oti Boateng, some ministers and
directors from the Ghana Education Service (GES).
Five months later, the trees planted for posterity have withered
away. A few weeks after the September ceremony, cocoyams took over the
1,000 acres of land, which had been cleared for the project. Soon, the
cocoyams gave way to 'Acheampong' shrubs, which are now maturing into a
secondary forest. Not a single concrete block has been laid in
fulfillment of the promise.
By December, last year, the expectation of the people of New Juabeng
had heightened to the point of anxiety.
Chronicle, therefore, inquired from a representative of the board of
the planned university as to what was happening. At that time, the
gentleman 'disclosed' that prefabricated materials for the building had
been imported and were being kept at Tema, from where they would "soon"
be sent down to the project site at Afidwase.
The All Nations University mission statement is to provide quality
higher education that is applied to promote development. The institute
is also to raise leaders with Christian values and ethics for good
governance to serve the people. In addition to the obvious benefits
from such a noble mission, the people of New Juaben expected that some
10,000 skilled and unskilled jobs would spring up from the university
for them,
What is more, the university had been assigned the rights to
approximately 1,000 acres of land by the government. By February, last
year, only 160 acres had been "paid" for. Even so, those whose parcels
of land were ceded to the government complained that they had been
cheated, as their parcels of land and crops were not fairly assessed
before being paid.
"It was with heavy hearts and great expectations that we let go our
parcels of land" one chief said, pointing out that New Juaben, being
the smallest district in the whole country, is one of the places where
land is very scarce. "Now, they bulldozed our cocoa, orange, plantain,
cassava and oil palm farms only to plant useless 'Acheampong' weeds in
their stead,' the chief concluded.
Efforts to obtain an explanation from the Ontario, Canada office of
the ANIDA for the stalling of the project has so far failed. But,
sources opined that the land acquisition issue is at the heart of the
problem.
According to a New Juabeng palace source, certain aggrieved
individuals had petitioned against the free takeover of their parcels
of land, making it impossible for ANIDA to start work. The source,
however, claimed that the "against people's efforts will come to
nought" as in New Juaben the whole land belongs to the state.
He explained that when the Juabens deserted the old Asante Kingdom
and settled at their new place in the early years of the last century,
it was the colonial government that bought the whole land, which is now
the New Juaben Municipal Area, for them.

Original_Search_Title: Three Expatriate Scientists Among Crash Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Three Expatriate Scientists Among Crash Victims
Body_Text:
Lagos, Nigeria (PANA) — The Nigeria-based International Institute of Tropical
Agriculture said Thursday that three of its expatriate staff died in Sunday's
Kenyan Airways Airbus that crashed off the coast of Cote d'Ivoire.
Dirk Vuyisteke, a Belgian, Paul Speijer, Dutch and John Hartman, an
American, all working at the institute's Eastern and Southern Africa Regional
Centre in Uganda, were returning to the Ibadan headquarters of the institute,
aboard the ill-fated KQ 431 flight that crashed in Abidjan with 179 passengers
and crew on board.
A statement by the institute described Vujisteke as leader of the
institute's "plantain and banana improvement project."
Speijer was a nematologist, while Hartman was a banana breeder.
"Individually and collectively, the three eminent scientists played major
roles in the development of improved plantains and bananas for the African
farmers during heir lifetimes," the institute said in a tribute.
Flags at the institute's stations in Nigeria and elsewhere are also being
flown at half mast, it said, as a sign of respect to the memory of the
scientists.
The Nairobi-Lagos flight was diverted to Abidjan because of bad weather in
Lagos. The victims are from more than 10 nationalities, including some 100
Nigerians.
One of them is Mohammed Lai Adegboye, chairman of the Nigerian Bar
Association in the central state of Kwara branch. He was also the state's former
rural development and water resources commissioner.

Original_Search_Title: Blessing In Disguise For Mabvuku-Epworth Commuters
Headline: Zimbabwe: Blessing In Disguise For Mabvuku-Epworth Commuters
Body_Text:
Harare — Following the free-for-all illegal cultivation in Harare, the
city's environment has been destroyed but, for commuters travelling between
Mabvuku-Tafara and Epworth, the lawlessness has been a blessing in disguise.
The thick bush between Mutare Road and the boundary with Epworth that was
left intact for decades is no more. As there is no public transport between
Epworth and Mabvuku, residents from both suburbs walk the long distance for
various business.
Because the bush is no more, so are the countless muggings and rapes of
yesteryear. Hundreds were mugged along the path to and from Epworth, many a
time in broad daylight and the police in the area were not very helpful.
Said Tobani Chizhanue of Mabvuku: "Nowadays it is very safe.
The land has been cleared and one can actually detect a prospective mugger
from a distance. It's always advisable to keep a weapon like the catapult.
If you suspect somebody is trying to be funny, just release the missile."
Only a few months ago the area was impassable and "terribly risky"for
Epworth residents, some of whom do their shopping in Mabvuku or Tafara. The
thieves had established themselves in the area secluded from the effective
intervention of the district police office.
Soldiers from the adjacent Zimbabwe National Army station are said to have
fallen victim to muggers and retaliated in a big way-they beat up the culprits
"the soldier way" and caused grievous bodily harm to thieves who could not
report their 'misfortune' to the police.
"They beat up some of them with sticks until they wailed.
They even allowed onlookers to take turns in beating the robbers and you can
imagine how people can be excited by that kind of invitation," said one Mabvuku
resident who refused to be named.
Due to the destruction of the indigenous Msasa tree which was dominant in
the area, the landscape is now almost barren. The scenery now clearly exposes
the brown and grey rocks which the people of yesteryear named "Mabvuku" as they
travelled from Seke communal lands to Chishawasha-now occupied by the Roman
Catholic Church.
Illegal cultivation became a popular pastime in Mabvuku and Tafara in the
1990s, when the economic climate saw many people failing to make ends meet.
As the "land question" became topical, local authorities adopted the central
government's political philosophy-let transgressors go on as long as political
mileage is guaranteed.
The local politicians, or councillors, some of whom are themselves
participating in illegal cultivation, have encouraged hordes of cultivators to
violate council bye-laws by invading Cleveland Dam, which is a catchment area.
Although some compromise was reached, allowing limited cultivation on
selected plots for co-operatives and disallowing steam bank cultivation, the
streams and the dam have been silted with impunity. Nobody has been warned or
reprimanded for flouting the regulations.
Many such cultivators have a genuine grievance-they have suddenly become
poor as a result of retrenchments and the general high cost of living, but
other well-to-do residents have joined the fray for commercial purposes.
"It's easy. . .you just hire people to do it for you. I can even hire a
tractor, like the ones that the 'comrades' (ex-combatants) now have," said Mr.
Masaka, who lives in the adjacent Greendale.
Many indigenous fruits like hute, maroro, tsambatsi,hacha, mazhanje and
matamba have either disappeared or are nearly extinct.The only wildlife species
existing in the cultivated area are mice and snakes. The popular hare is slowly
disappearing from the scene due to lack of breeding space.The most popular
crops are maize, sweet potatoes and pumpkins.
The volume to traffic between Epworth and Mabvuku, along the the dust road
created long ago for the nearby farmer, has increased dramatically.
School children, shoppers-some of whom will be carrying large paraffin cans
from Tafara where it is available-young lovers taking a walk away from their
elders, those courting and others now freely tread the once feared pathway.

Original_Search_Title: U.S., SADC Expected to Sign Agreements
Headline: Southern Africa: U.S., SADC Expected to Sign Agreements
Publisher: United States Agency for International Development (Washington, DC)
Body_Text:
Washington — The U.S. Agency for International Development (USAID)
and the Southern African Development Community (SADC) are expected to
sign several agreements June 13 to fund work in regional market
integration, agriculture, natural resources management, and democracy-
building, totaling $18.4 million, according to a USAID press release.
Dr. Prega Ramsamy, the acting executive secretary of SADC, and
Edward J. Spriggs, director of USAID's Regional Center for Southern
Africa, are expected to sign the agreements at the SADC Secretariat in
Gaborone, Botswana, in the presence of Ambassador John E. Lange, U.S.
ambassador to Botswana and special representative to SADC.
The U.S. government is expected to grant $8.85 million for regional
market integration activities that will promote free trade within the
region and with global trading partners, remove trade barriers, and
improve the movement of goods and services throughout the SADC region.
The United States, according to the release, will also fund $3.6
million worth of agricultural projects to provide improved seeds for
sorghum, millet, cassava, and sweet potato and to develop cattle
disease control technologies for farmers in the region.
USAID is expected to contribute $5.8 million as part of its ongoing
support for regional wildlife management programs, including assistance
to the recent initiatives for the creation of transboundary wildlife
protection areas such as the Kgalagadi Transfrontier Park in Botswana
and South Africa. The balance of the $18 million, according to the
USAID release, will support democracy-related endeavors carried out by
civil society associations in the SADC region.
In remarks prepared for the signing ceremony, Ambassador Lange said
the U.S. government places a high priority on the U.S.-SADC
relationship and noted the successful outcome of the second US-SADC
Forum held in Maputo last month.
In his prepared remarks, Spriggs, representing USAID, emphasized
that the agreements to be signed demonstrate continued U.S. government
support for the social and economic development of the SADC region.
He added that USAID remains committed to SADC's goals of promoting
regional market integration, strengthening democratic practices, and
adopting successful agricultural and environmental management practices
that protect the region's valuable natural resources and provide food
security.
USAID assistance in the region, Spriggs noted, has already achieved
significant results in the sustainable management of natural resources
and the development of a SADC free trade area, thereby benefiting the
people of southern Africa.
The goal of USAID's Regional Center for Southern Africa, located in
Gaborone, is to support SADC's regional integration efforts while
promoting equitable, sustainable economic growth in a democratic
southern Africa.
The U.S. Agency for International Development is the U.S. government
agency that provides development and humanitarian assistance worldwide.
(Distributed by the Office of International Information Programs,
U.S. Department of State. Web site: http://usinfo.state.gov)

Original_Search_Title: Agricultural Enterprise to be Privatized
Headline: Ethiopia: Agricultural Enterprise to be Privatized
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — The Oromia Investment Bureau has announced that ten
agricultural enterprises in East Wollega zone will be privatized "some time this
year."
The enterprise, said to have been idle for nine years as the result of
vandalism following the downfall of the previous regime, covers an area of over
40,000 hectares and used to produce about one million quintals of maize and
sorghum every year.
Among the enterprises to be privatized are Bello, Anger, Ekko, and the  Horro
Eleltu projects funded by Yugoslavia.
According to the investment bureau, last year three enterprise in Bale,
covering 13,000 hectares, were privatized.

Original_Search_Title: "The Southern Lady of Means"
Headline: Nigeria: "The Southern Lady of Means"
Body_Text:
Lagos — The situation in Nigeria today is like a marriage and
threatened divorce. The British Government took over the territory of
Niger Coast Protectorate and the Royal Niger Company in 1900. In
1914, the entity known as the Colony and Protectorate of Nigeria was
created by the British Government.
The Colony of Lagos came into being in 1861 when Dosumu ceded Lagos
to the British Government as Colony. The difference between "Colony"
and Protectorate" is that in the case of Colony the title to the
territory was vested in the British Crown, whereas in the case of
Protectorate the title to the territory remained vested in the Chiefs
and people of the territory.
The legal and factual situation were explained in a letter written by
the British Consul in Calabar in September 1884 to Jaja of Opobo.
It was further explained in a Cabinet Paper prepared for the British
Cabinet by the Lord Chancellor -the highest law officer in the
British system.
The Sardauma of Sokoto, during the political crisis of 1953, in the
confrontation between the North and the South, made the following
historic statement in the House of Representatives in Lagos: "The
mistake of 1914 has come to light." This turned attention immediately
to the serious implications of the amalgamation of 1914. See the
autobiography of the Sardauna of Sokoto, My Life, 1962, at p 133; and
at p 135 of the same book he said "Lord Lugard and his amalgamation
were far from popular amongst us at that time (1953 Constitutional
crisis) and there were suggestions in favour of secession."
There was also evidence of divorce at the Ibadan Constitutional
Conference in 1950 to review the Richard's Constitution when the
Emirs of Katsina and Zaria said that unless the Northern Provinces
were granted 50 per cent of the seats in the proposed Central House
of Representatives, they would "ask for separation from the rest of
Nigeria on the arrangements exiting before 1914" See p 218 of the
"Proceedings of the General Conference on Review of the Constitution
held at Ibadan, January 1950."
The actual Amalgamation Report's full title is "Report by Sir F. D.
Lugard on the Amalgamation of Northern and Southern Nigeria, and
Administration, 1912 - 1919 CMD 468 XXXVI 609." But Lugard's entire
Report was never published. It was the edited version "in a suitable
form" that was published.
When Lugard returned to London from Nigeria in 1918, Lugard wrote a
letter to the Colonial Office as follows: "I was informed in 1913
that it was Lord Harcourt's wish that my report submitting proposals
for the amalgamation of Northern and Southern Nigeria should be
published in a suitable form. Some delay took place in the
preparation of the papers and it was eventually decided to abandon
the idea.
There is no record in Nigeria accessible to the public of the reasons
for the change which were introduced, and the alternative proposals
which were discussed. . ."
Lord Scarbrough, Chairman of the Niger Company declared that "the
coast ought to pay for the development of the interior and that any
policy would be a suicide policy." The central kernel was that
amalgamation was to the economic advantage of the North and to the
British Treasury.
The South must finance the North. Section 10 of the 1999 Constitution
provides that the Government of the Federation or of a state shall
not adopt any religion as state religion.
The section 10 provides: "The Government of the Federation or of a
state shall not adopt any religion as state religion."
This was lifted from the 1979 Constitution. So it was not new in
1999.
Section 5(3) of the 1999 Constitution also provides: "The executive
powers vested in a State under subsection (2) of this section shall
be so exercised as not to- (a) impede or prejudice the exercise of
the executive powers of the Federation; (b) endanger any asset or
investment of the Government of the Federation in that State; or (c)
endanger the continuance of a federal government in Nigeria."
The Sharia provision in the 1979 Constitution was a compromise worked
out before the Northern members of the Constituent Assembly called
off their walk- out.
No group has the right to do anything inconsistent with that
compromise. That will be as bad as destroying the Constitution.
But why are we in the mess we are today?
To answer this, I must quote copiously from the seminar work of
Professor Omo Omoruyi: The Tale of June 12 published by the Press
Alliance Network Ltd: Origin Of Nigeria "There is no question that
there were relatively independent units, call them states/empires, in
the territory that currently makes up Nigeria before the advent of
the white man and the colonial order.
Anthropologists and the Ibadan History Series provide us with the
history and form of political organisation of different groups in
Nigeria. Following the Berlin Conference of 1884, Lugard, then a
captain, paid his first visit to Nigeria to organise on behalf of the
Royal Niger Company, troops of subjugation to be used in extracting
treaties from the chiefs of the North.
The various groups in Nigeria had no inkling that Britain was trying
to amalgamate them.
Brief Of Review Of The Process "Britain's implementation of the
Berlin Treaty was done through independent administrators namely: 1.
The Oil Rivers Protectorate, renamed Niger Coast Protectorate in
1895.
The Niger Coast Protectorate was ruled by Consul with headquarters in
Calabar and responsible to the Foreign Office, not the Colonial
Office, because the relationship with these states was by virtue of
treaties of protection and by cession.
2. The Colony of Lagos ceded to the British Crown in 1861 was ruled
by Governors responsible to the Colonial Office based in Sierra Leone
(1866-18740 and Gold Coast (1874-1886). In 1886, Lagos had its own
Governor, Cession of Lagos was through a highly fraudulent treaty.
There was a treaty of Cession, nevertheless.
3. The Niger territories of the Royal Niger Company rule, 1886 to
1899 by the agents of the private company, was responsible to the
Board of Directors in London. This corresponds to the present day
Northern Nigeria. Sir Frederick D Lugard had, earlier as a captain,
worked for this company in negotiating a series of treaties with
traditional rulers in 1897. He was, as a Brigadier- General,
appointed the first High Commissioner of the Niger Territories of the
Royal Niger Company to be called Northern Nigeria with effect from
January 1, 1900. This was the day the term Nigeria was first used in
official communication. It would appear that the term was meant to
refer to the North of Nigeria and later the other two governments
were renamed Southern Nigeria and Lagos. They were merged to form
Southern Nigeria in 1906.
From 1906, Northern Nigeria and Southern Nigeria were technically two
autonomous entities responsible separately to the colonial office.
"This was the situation when the design of Nigeria was conceived
between 1900 and 1912. It should be noted that Lugard brought the
various parts of the North together and produced an administrative
entity called first, Nigeria Territories and later Northern Nigeria.
It should be further noted that Lugard was privy to the thinking in
London about amalgamation. This was why he campaigned to be given the
assignment of incorporating the various units in the South
constituting Southern Nigeria into Northern Nigeria. He did not do in
the South what he did in the North as the High Commissioner.
Lugard did not hide his intention in his new assignment in 1912. His
plan was meant to incorporate the various units and people of the
Southern territory into a unified North. By 1912 when Lugard emerged
on the Southern scene, there was something called Northern Nigeria
(which was over 14 years old) in the minds of the traditional rulers
and of the traditional elite in the North.
On the other hand, there was nothing called Southern Nigeria in the
minds of the traditional rulers and of the educated elites in various
parts of the South.
The various groups in the South were treaty-based states who related
to Britain independently. The North knew what they were getting into;
the South did not. I came to this conclusion after examining how
Lugard went about his assignment between October 1912 and 31 December
1913 in both sectors before amalgamation was proclaimed on January 1,
1914.
I also examined the terms of amalgamation and how it was conceived to
use Southern Nigeria to develop the North."
Lugard Lied To Trick Southern Leaders "Lugard toured the Southern
states from October to 31 December 1912 and went back to Lagos to
reflect on what he saw in Benin, Yoruba land (Ibadan, Oyo and
Abeokuta), Igbo land (Onitsha) in Calabar and in the Niger Delta. He
framed how he was going to get around the problems that he might face
with the amalgamation plan.
Throughout the visits to these places in the South, Lugard never
raised with them what was going to befall them in a few months. The
people of Abeokuta, Benin, Calabar, Ibadan, Lagos Okrika, Onitsha and
Oyo did not see themselves at the time as belonging to one political
entity called Southern Nigeria. Nor do they to this day."
"If the people of the Southern Nigeria did not see themselves as one
entity, on what basis would one be talking of an amalgamation of two
parts others than an incorporation of the various parts of the South
into the North.
Most significant was that the different treaty-based obligations
between Britain and the various communities in the South would have
warranted consequential renegotiations of the various treaties with
these communities before embarking on the two forms of
amalgamation-within the South amalgamation, and the North-South
amalgamation. But at this time it should be emphasised that Northern
Nigeria had been functioning as one country for about 20 years.
Shouldn't this have been allowed to continue? Why was it not allowed
to continue? It was uneconomical to administer Northern Nigeria as an
autonomous colonial entity, hence the need to embark on the
amalgamation plan with Southern Nigeria."
Lugard Saw The North As One Entity And The South In Pieces "Lugard's
visit to the North beyond Zungeru was well planned because it was
obvious he was visiting a country. He did not have to visit the
various parts as he did in the South.
This was why a special Durbar at Kano was arranged for this visit by
all the Emirs and Chiefs in the North to honour him on 1 January
1913. The Dubar was graced with well over 15,000 horsemen and
countless number of footmen, each grouped around their chief.
There were 800 from the Royal West African Frontier Force with some
100 mounted infantry which was created by Lugard in 1898. Lugard's
account of the Durbar is fascinating."
"All the Emirs were seated together - a marvelous sight when one
recalls the bitter jealousies which formerly possess them. Even the
Chinese have little or nothing to teach these Emirs in the way of
courtesy and good manners but the Pagan dances were simply wonderful.
There were tumblers and contortionists from Ilorin, and wrestlers and
also pugilists. These latter impressed me more than anything.
Round their right hands they had matting bond with rough native sting
making a most formidable "boxing glove," much more severe than a
naked fist. With this they dealt each other blows in the face or body
with their whole strength, sparring with the left hand.
The blows were so heavy that I wondered why serious damaged was not
done, but the way they were taken, and the genial way in which when a
man was knocked down and vanquished, he accepted defeat."
"And so also among all these vast crowds of horsemen and footmen
during all these three days belonging to rival Emirs who had
grievances against each other, and each trying to aggrandise their
own lord-not a single quarrel or even altercation did I see or hear
of! Everyone kept saying to each other that it was a really marvelous
sight which they would not for the world have missed. And so it was!"
"Contrary to what obtained in the South whose leaders were seeing
Lugard for the first time, the Emirs in the North were familiar with
him and were in communication with him.
As soon as he landed in Lagos he dispatched gifts to them and they in
turn acknowledged the customary formal gifts they received. From
Lugard's papers, were letters in Hausa showing the warm relationship
between the Emirs and Lugard.
Examples demonstrate the difference between the North and South. Some
of these letters are: Shehu Of Borno: "Letter from the slave of God.
. . to our good helper, our prop, the solver of our difficulties, the
one who carries our heavy burdens, Governor Lugard with salutations
more scented than the must perfume and sweeter than honey.
May God prolong your life, the keeper of those who keep others."
Emir Of Bida: "Sent salutations and good will shining bright as do
the planets and constant as their light as the return of morn and
eve, Oh! My Chief, my Leader, we ask God to prolong your life in
prosperity and health.
My present to you is a sword in a silver scabbard and two gowns."
Emir Of Yola: "To the Deputy of the King of England, the Governor,
who holds all our country in his hand and rules it all. A thousand
salutations and fealty and homage repeated."
Emir of Jama: "I and my people, all of us, will not refrain from
praying for you morning and evening. I told my people to rejoice at
your safe return.
We rode our horses and raced. I read your letter morning and evening
and it makes me very happy."
Emir Of Nupe: "Many beautiful salutations, a clean love my present to
you is two turkeys, I package of plantains and 1 of limes."
Amalgamation - Unified Administration "According to Perham, Lugard's
task was to unify administrations not peoples.
Lugard was bent on keeping the North as one entity. He rejected
Morel's plan of 1912 which would have separated Ilorin and Borgu from
the North and merged them with Yoruba land.
Lugard also rejected the second plan submitted by Governor Temple
based on his experience which would have divided the territory into
seven large provinces: four in the South and three in the North
(Hausa States of the North West, Chad territory covering Borno,
Bauchi and Yola and Benue Province containing the remaining Southern
and Western districts along the Niger.) Lugard rejected it and stuck
to the North as one entity."
"In the two plans rejected by Lugard, the people or their accredited
representatives were also not consulted. In all the cases the treaty
obligations of Britain were ignored.
Specifically, when Britain captured Benin in 1897 after a bitter war,
the rulers of Benin were made to sign a letter of protection but they
were not told at the time Lugard emerged on the scene in 1913, that
their land was going to be ruled along with some artificial territory
called Southern Protectorate and later Nigeria. Secrecy engulfed the
British plan in Southern Nigeria because Lugard was concerned with
the anxiety within what Lugard called "educated native." Even the
Legislative Council which at this time had been extended to the whole
of Southern Protectorate never discussed this matter.
The first time Southerners knew of what was to befall them was after
Lord Harcourt's announcement in London which was followed by the
statement of the relationship between the North and the South."
Harcourt's Plan Meant Incorporation Of Southern Parts Into Unified
North "Lord Harcourt went to the House of Commons on June 27, 1913
after receiving Lugard's report on his tour of the North and the
South and announced the new British Government policy of two
Nigerias. According to Lord Harcourt, unification of Nigeria demanded
both "a method" and "a man".
The man was to be Lord Lugard and the method was to be the marriage
of the two entities.
According to Lord Harcourt.
Happily the man is at hand with a tried and proved capacity to supply
the method. Though I have been convinced ever since I came to the
Colonial Office that this amalgamation was desirable.
I frankly admit that I should not have thought the moment opportune
unless I had happened to know and to command the services of the one
man marked out for this great work. Sir Frederick Lugard. I have been
able, greatly to the regret of Hong Kong, to induce him to leave that
post and to take up what will shortly become the Governorship of the
combined Nigerias. Northern Nigeria is in the truest sense the
product of his foresight and genius.
He reclaimed it from the unknown; he gave it a legal code, differing
only in its civilisation from the essential lines of native custom;
he established a land system which, combining altruism with revenue,
may well be a model and inspiration to other Protectorates. . . On my
earnest solicitations he returns now to the field of his early and
brilliant Labour, to complete and consolidate what was proved, I
think, to be the greatest tropical province of the Crown."
On another occasion Lord Harcourt laid down the kind of relationship
that should exist between North and the South as a marriage with the
North as the "husband" and the South as the "wife." According to Lord
Harcourt: "We have released Northern Nigeria from the leading strings
of the Treasury. The promising and well conducted youth is now on an
allowance on his own and is about to effect an alliance with a
Southern lady of means.
I have issued the special license and Sir Frederick Lugard will
perform the ceremony. May the union be fruitful and the couple
constant (italics mine)."
Founding Fathers More Concerned For Northerners Than Southerners "Who
were the Founding Fathers and what did they have in mind? Chief
Obafemi Awolowo did not think that he and other Nigeria political
leaders of his generation were the founding fathers.
He identified Sir Frederick D Lugard (later Lord Lugard) as the
founding father when he stated of Lugard in his book. The People's
Republic that: "to him, more than anyone else, belongs the credit or
discredit for setting Nigeria on a course which Nigerian nationalists
and patriots feel obliged to pursue albeit with mixed feelings till
the present day."
"What Chief Awolowo did not appreciate was that Lord Lugard was
simply implementing the British model of a Colonial order which was
on the drawing board with the setting up of the Selbourne Committee
in 1898 and later personified by Lord Lewis Harcourt who was then the
colonial secretary.
It is sad to say that in answer to the question, who were the
founding fathers of Nigeria, no Nigerian Leader (Azikiwe, Sardauna,
Ironsi, Gowon, Murtala, Obasanjo, Shagari, Buhari, Babangida, Abacha)
could claim to be a founding father. Rather, they were all part of
the problem of nationhood which continue till today.
I would say the founding fathers were Lord Harcourt, Lord Lugard and
Sir James Robertson. What was the credit? What was the discredit? Why
did the so- called Nigeria nationalists feel obliged to abide by the
colonialist plan despite the mixed feelings? At what stage did the
mixed feelings turn into a mistakes for some and a source of despair
for others? Why was the matter raised in 1950,1953, and 1966 as "the
mistake of 1914" by the North? What was the true feeling of the
Southern leaders in 1950, 1953 and 1966? Was it a blessing for the
South then? At what stage did this mistake turn into the blessing of
1914 for some? What is the true feeling of Nigerians today from the
North and the South?" The situation is that to the Southerners today,
1914 is a mistake; to the Northerners, it is a blessing.
What steps can we take to make it a blessing for all? Democracy in
Nigeria must be found in the solution to this question.
The original design for Nigeria in 1914 was conceived as a marriage
between the North and South with the North as the "husband" and the
South, "the wife." The June 12 election would have amounted to a
"status reversal" as it would have put a Southerner in charge of
Nigeria contrary to the model introduced into Nigeria in 1959 by Sir
James Robertson. This was why the June 12 Presidential election was
annulled on June 23, 1993."
Publication Date: September 28, 2000

Original_Search_Title: Awassa to Moyale- Awassa And the Shabe Carvings Part I
Headline: Ethiopia: Awassa to Moyale- Awassa And the Shabe Carvings Part I
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — Recent road-building work has made the 500km road from
the Southern Region capital of Awassa to the border of Kenya at
Moyale an easy trip. The road is marked with very few potholes, it is
often straight, it has little traffic as you head further south, and
it becomes increasingly straight and fast.
The road also has a few interesting sites along the way, and passes
by some very interesting peoples.
Awassa
Awassa is nice. It rests on Awassa Lake, where you can gaze at the
wonderful lakefront from either the Wabe Shebelle 1 or 2 Hotels. I
prefer number 2. Tables and chairs are perched a bit precariously on
the dyke that holds the water from the lake back. During the wet
season the grounds of both the Shebelles are semi- flooded, but the
dyke is still dry.
From the front of the hotel you can be entertained by the birds on
the lake. Small kingfishers perch on branches overhanging the lake,
occasionally diving abruptly in after little fish. Big pelicans,
ducks, egrets, and geese float gently by. There is a rowboat service
on the lake, but it looks a bit desultory - paddling about in the
reeds. There are brave swimmers in the lake, but it is a bilharzia
area, you wouldn't catch me in there.
Another attraction of the Wabe Shebelle 2 is the colobus monkeys, or
gurezas, with their wonderful monklike black caps around their white
faces, and their long white tails with the bushy ends. Vervet monkeys
also frequent the grounds, and occasionally do unequal battle with
the larger gurezas for scraps from the hotels. My kids love the
monkeys, but they can be a bit aggressive around food. One time the
peanuts were stolen from the kids' hands, and the vervets have been
known to snatch bread from your outside table as you eat. I think
it's great.
There is a tennis court at the hotel, which looks permanently out of
commission. A swimming pool which was also in a state of disrepair
was recently rehabilitated. Despite the water coming directly from
the lake, I decided to chance it and had a nice swim. I ended up
giving impromptu swimming lessons too, though I wouldn't make a
career of it. I was worried about bilharzia, although the snails
which carry it were not in the pool, and I haven't died yet.
Awassa is nice to drive around in, with wide tree lined streets. It
has recently added several traffic lights, which seem to be more
about status than efficiency. There are plenty of shops, although
there is nothing special to buy except perhaps the special southern
coffee beans.
A recent addition to Awassa is the nearly completed Beta Gabriel
church, a large and impressive structure in the best traditions of
the Ethiopia Orthodox Church. No doubt the church is asserting
itself. The Southern Region is mostly Protestant, based on active
evangelization amongst the 'heathen' by missionaries for over 60
years.
As the capital of the Southern National, Nationalities Peoples Region
(SNNPR) Awassa is benefiting from the de-centralization policy of the
government, and there is quite a lot of building and activity. It is
a lively little place.
There is even a good restaurant in Awassa now. The Pina has a nice
pastry shop on the main floor, and a comfortable restaurant up the
stairs on the second. It is on the main drag, not hard to find.
South of Awassa the scenery is gorgeous. Green hills line the
tropical route, with plantain and thick woods dotting the scenery.
The best sites to see are near the road south of Awassa. Dilla is the
first stop - 90 kilometers south of Awassa. The tourism office at the
Wabe Shebelle 1 on the road into Awassa is very helpful and provides
directions to the sites.
Unfortunately the tourism office in Dilla is not helpful. Even though
the Awassa tourism office had directed us to the sites and advised us
to check in with the Dilla office, initially the Dilla office refused
to help unless they had a letter from the Awassa office. After some
persuasion they reluctantly agreed, but instead of giving directions
they offered a guide who would show up in an hour or so and would
take us to the monuments. We preferred to go ourselves, to avoid
having to return the guide 30 kilometers to Dilla, while we were
planning to continue south. They couldn't help us with the rock
carvings of Shabe, because even though they were only 10 kilometers
to the west, they are in Oromo Region, not SNNPR. We contemplated
changing the sign outside the office from 'Tourism Promotion' to
'Tourism Prevention'.
We got much more help the normal way. We went to lunch and asked the
other folks there. They were very helpful. They didn't ask for a
letter from Nations, Nationalities and officials. They told us where
we could find the Shabe rock carving. We went.
The Shabe Rock Carvings are considered to be one of the foremost
archaeological sites in Ethiopia, a country rich in archaeology. They
can be found by following the sign to Dilla University on the right
hand side as you drive into Dilla from the north. In front of the
university we took on a boy playing table tennis as our guide, and
headed off on a track past the University that could be called a road
only with the greatest exaggeration. We were fortunate that it was
completely dry, and we had a four- wheel drive with a high clearance,
or we probably couldn't have made it.
Our initial guide we picked up at the university lost his way, but
another young fellow found us and took us down the right pathetic
track. Just as I was beginning to despair about the state of the road
and the distance we had gone, we arrived.
You couldn't see anything special as we got out of the car, with the
exception of three guys with sharp spears that followed us with
interest. There was a bunch of bushes, and our guide directed us to
crawl in through a space, which we did with a bit of trepidation.
There was a short path, then a fairly deep crevice with a stream on
the bottom. The carvings were on the rock just across the narrow
chasm.
The carvings are all represent the same thing - an oddly shaped cow
with oversized udder, and a tiny head with long odd shaped horns. At
the main point there are 14 of these cows. There are 5 or 6 further
down the chasm which are a bit harder to see, and a few more on a
rock by the short path down. Most of them are about 8-10 inches high,
with some variation and a few very small.
The locals don't have a meaning for the place 'Shabe'. They call the
vicinity 'Ragoo'. The carvings themselves they call 'Haroo Photo' -
Haroo means deep gorge with water in it and Photo is self-explanatory
and obviously of recent vintage. The three guys with spears, Desta,
Oude, and Danny explained that they didn't know who had done the
carvings, but they were very old - 'before the Italians'. One of them
thought the carvings were not man-made, that they were natural or
'made by God'. They said if the stone broke off that there would be
more cows beneath.
Although dating anything on rock or made of rock is usually pretty
difficult, the tourist information says these carvings are 3000 years
old. That would put them at about the same time as the Queen of Sheba
was visiting Solomon.
Our guide, Gezahegn Girma was good. He was pretty unkempt looking,
but turned out to be fine. The guys with the spears were also very
nice.
Next week the tour to Moyale continues.
